topics.title: Approaches to Software Design - Functional Oriented Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Approaches to Software Design - Functional Oriented Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Functional Oriented Design (FOD)**, also widely referred to as *Procedure Oriented Design* or *Function-Oriented Design*, is a classical software design paradigm in which a problem is decomposed into a set of self-contained **functions** (also called procedures, routines, subroutines, or modules). The primary emphasis is on **"What is being done"** (i.e., the sequence of operations / algorithms) rather than **"What is being affected"** (i.e., the data).

In FOD, the entire program is structured as a **hierarchy of functions**, where the `main()` function sits at the top and progressively invokes smaller, more specific sub-functions. Data is typically **global** and is passed between functions either as arguments or through shared global variables. Languages such as **C, Pascal, FORTRAN, and early versions of BASIC** are classic examples of functional/procedural-oriented languages.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1.1):**
> The 2024 scheme introduces this topic specifically to provide **historical context** for Object Oriented Programming. Examiners expect students to clearly articulate *why* Java was designed as an OOP language by first understanding the limitations of the older **Functional/Procedural approach**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Old-Style Restaurant Kitchen"

Imagine a traditional restaurant kitchen with no specialized stations:

- The **Head Chef** (`main()`) reads the customer's order.
- He calls upon the **Sous Chef** (`function prepareStarter()`).
- The Sous Chef calls the **Sauce Cook** (`function makeSauce()`).
- The Sauce Cook, when done, hands the sauce back up the chain.
- The **order details (data)** are pinned on a common board in the middle of the kitchen — **every chef can read or write to that board (global data)**.

| Real Kitchen Element | FOD Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Head Chef | `main()` function |
| Sous Chef, Sauce Cook | Sub-functions |
| Order ticket on the board | Global data / shared variables |
| Cooking recipe (steps) | Algorithm inside each function |
| Customer order changes | **Every function must be rewritten** |

**The pain point:** If the restaurant adds a "Vegan Menu," the Head Chef, Sous Chef, and Sauce Cook **all need new training** — but the kitchen itself (data structure) is unchanged. This rigidity is the central weakness that **Object Oriented Design** was invented to fix.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Glossary of essential KTU terms:**
> - **Function / Procedure:** A named, parameterized block of code that performs a specific task and may return a value.
> - **Top-Down Decomposition:** The systematic process of breaking a large problem into smaller sub-problems.
> - **Global Data:** Variables accessible to all functions in the program.
> - **Local Data:** Variables whose scope is limited to a single function.
> - **Coupling:** The degree of interdependence between functions.
> - **Cohesion:** The degree to which elements within a function belong together.
> - **Modularity:** The property of decomposing a program into independent, interchangeable modules.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Summary

## 2.1 The Operational Workflow of Functional Oriented Design

The FOD methodology follows a structured, step-by-step philosophy:

1. **Identify the Problem Domain** — Clearly state the input, processing, and output requirements of the system.
2. **Decompose into Functions** — Break the main task (`main()`) into a hierarchy of smaller, well-defined functions.
3. **Establish the Function Hierarchy** — Arrange functions in a tree structure (call tree / structure chart).
4. **Identify Shared Data** — Determine which data must be **global** (visible to multiple functions) and which data can be **local** to a function.
5. **Design the Algorithm for Each Function** — Specify the exact sequence of operations within each function.
6. **Define Interfaces** — Specify parameters and return types for inter-function communication.
7. **Code, Test, and Integrate** — Implement each function, unit-test it, and then integrate from the bottom of the hierarchy upward.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The "Why" behind FOD is **simplicity of thought** — humans naturally decompose complex tasks (e.g., cooking, travelling) into smaller steps. FOD mirrors this cognitive pattern. The "How" is a *divide-and-conquer* strategy applied recursively until each sub-task is trivial to code.

## 2.2 Core Characteristics of Functional Oriented Design

The defining features that examiners repeatedly test:

- **Function-Centric:** Functions are the *first-class citizens*. Data exists to serve the functions.
- **Top-Down Approach:** Always starts from the most abstract level and drills down to details.
- **Shared Global Data:** A large amount of data is usually declared globally so multiple functions can access and modify it.
- **Sequential Execution with Branching:** The program flow is largely linear, with conditional and loop constructs controlling the order of function calls.
- **High Reusability (within limits):** Well-designed functions can be reused across the same project, but **cross-project reuse is poor** because data and functions are tightly coupled.
- **Statelessness of Data:** Data is *passive* — it does not "know" which functions act upon it.
- **Focus on Algorithms:** The primary intellectual effort is spent on writing efficient logic, not on modeling real-world entities.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Summary Table

| **S. No.** | **Property** | **Description / Value in FOD** |
|:---:|---|---|
| 1 | Primary Building Block | **Function / Procedure** |
| 2 | Design Strategy | **Top-Down Decomposition** |
| 3 | Data Treatment | **Global, shared, passive** |
| 4 | Code Reusability | **Limited** (function-level only) |
| 5 | Real-World Mapping | **Weak** (does not mirror real entities) |
| 6 | Security | **Low** (global data is exposed to all functions) |
| 7 | Maintenance Cost | **High** for large systems |
| 8 | Typical Languages | **C, Pascal, FORTRAN, COBOL** |
| 9 | Coupling | **Often high** (functions depend on global state) |
| 10 | Cohesion | **Good** (each function does one task) |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Even in 2026, FOD is **not obsolete**. It is extensively used in:

- **Embedded Systems & Firmware:** Resource-constrained microcontrollers (8051, ARM Cortex-M) often run C-based procedural code.
- **System-Level Programming:** Operating system kernels (Linux scheduler, device drivers) contain large procedural subsystems.
- **Numerical & Scientific Computing:** Libraries like BLAS and LAPACK use highly optimized functional APIs.
- **HPC Pipelines:** Data-processing pipelines (ETL, signal processing) are naturally procedural.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Misconception:** "FOD is the same as Functional Programming (FP)." **They are NOT the same.** FOD is a synonym for *Procedural* programming. **Functional Programming (FP)** (Haskell, Lisp, modern Java Streams) is a *declarative, mathematical* paradigm emphasizing pure functions and immutability — a completely different philosophy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Illustrative Case Study: A Student Grade Processing System

To concretely demonstrate how FOD decomposes a problem, consider a system that reads student marks, computes the total, average, and grade, and prints a report.

### Step 1 — Identify the Problem

The system has three logical phases:
- **Input Phase:** Read marks of 5 subjects.
- **Processing Phase:** Compute total, average, and assign grade.
- **Output Phase:** Display the formatted report.

### Step 2 — Top-Down Decomposition

The single monolithic problem is split as follows:

```
StudentGradeSystem
 ├── inputMarks()
 ├── processMarks()
 │    ├── calculateTotal()
 │    ├── calculateAverage()
 │    └── assignGrade()
 └── printReport()
```

### Step 3 — Establish the Function Hierarchy

$$
\text{main} \rightarrow 
\begin{cases}
\text{inputMarks} \\[4pt]
\text{processMarks} \rightarrow 
\begin{cases}
\text{calculateTotal} \\[4pt]
\text{calculateAverage} \\[4pt]
\text{assignGrade}
\end{cases} \\[6pt]
\text{printReport}
\end{cases}
$$

### Step 4 — Identify Data

- **Local Data (inside `processMarks`):** `total`, `average`, `grade` — these are intermediate values.
- **Global Data (shared across all functions):** `marks[5]` — the array of subject marks must be accessible to input, processing, and output functions. In FOD, this is typically declared as a **global variable** in C-style.

### Step 5 — Algorithmic Logic for `assignGrade()`

The grade assignment rule can be expressed as a piecewise mathematical function:

$$
\text{grade} = 
\begin{cases}
\text{"A"} & \text{if } \text{average} \geq 90 \\[4pt]
\text{"B"} & \text{if } 75 \leq \text{average} < 90 \\[4pt]
\text{"C"} & \text{if } 60 \leq \text{average} < 75 \\[4pt]
\text{"D"} & \text{if } 50 \leq \text{average} < 60 \\[4pt]
\text{"F"} & \text{if } \text{average} < 50
\end{cases}
$$

**Conversion Logic Explanation:**
- Step (i): The function receives `average` as a parameter.
- Step (ii): A series of conditional checks evaluates the range in which `average` falls.
- Step (iii): The corresponding grade character is returned to the calling function `processMarks`.
- Step (iv): In FOD, this returned grade is then stored back into a **global variable** (or passed upward) so that `printReport` can access it.

### Step 3.6 — Pseudocode Sketch (Paradigm Illustration Only)

```
DECLARE marks[5] AS GLOBAL       // shared data

FUNCTION main():
    inputMarks()                 // reads into global 'marks'
    processMarks()               // computes & stores in globals
    printReport()                // reads from globals

FUNCTION inputMarks():
    FOR i = 0 TO 4:
        READ marks[i]

FUNCTION processMarks():
    total = calculateTotal()
    average = calculateAverage(total)
    grade = assignGrade(average)
    STORE grade, average, total INTO GLOBALS

FUNCTION calculateTotal():
    sum = 0
    FOR i = 0 TO 4:
        sum = sum + marks[i]
    RETURN sum
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** Every function in the above sketch either **reads from** or **writes to** the global `marks` array. This is the hallmark of FOD — *data is detached, floating, and accessible everywhere*. As the system grows, this becomes a maintenance nightmare, which directly motivates OOP.

## 3.2 Quantitative Comparison: Lines of Change

If a new requirement is added — *"Display the student's name alongside the report"* — we can quantify the impact:

$$
\text{Impact Factor} = \frac{\text{Number of functions modified}}{\text{Total number of functions}} \times 100
$$

For our example, adding a name to the report requires modifying `inputMarks`, `processMarks`, and `printReport`:

$$
\text{Impact Factor} = \frac{3}{6} \times 100 = 50\%
$$

> [!TIP]
> **In an OOP approach, the impact would be confined to a single `Student` class** — only **1 of, say, 3 entities** is touched, yielding an impact factor of $\frac{1}{3} \times 100 \approx 33.3\%$. This numerical comparison is a high-yield KTU answer point.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Decomposition Hierarchy (Structure Chart)

The following Mermaid diagram renders the classic **Structure Chart** used in structured design methodology to visualize FOD. The diagram shows the `main` function invoking its three top-level children, with `processMarks` further decomposed into three sub-functions. Dashed arrows represent **data coupling** (passing of global data), while solid arrows represent **control flow** (function calls).

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[main]
    nodeB[inputMarks]
    nodeC[processMarks]
    nodeD[printReport]
    nodeE[calculateTotal]
    nodeF[calculateAverage]
    nodeG[assignGrade]
    dataX[(Global Data: marks 5)]

    rootA -- CALL --> nodeB
    rootA -- CALL --> nodeC
    rootA -- CALL --> nodeD
    nodeC -- CALL --> nodeE
    nodeC -- CALL --> nodeF
    nodeC -- CALL --> nodeG
    nodeB -. READ WRITE .-> dataX
    nodeC -. READ WRITE .-> dataX
    nodeD -. READ .-> dataX
    nodeE -. READ .-> dataX
    nodeF -. READ .-> dataX
    nodeG -. READ .-> dataX
```

**How to read this chart:**
- **Solid arrows** denote *control flow* — the calling relationship between functions.
- **Dashed arrows** denote *data coupling* — every function reaching into the same global pool.
- The **cylindrical node** `dataX` represents the shared global data store, accessed by all six functions.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

This second diagram presents an alternative **data-flow perspective**, illustrating how information cascades through the FOD pipeline.

```mermaid
graph LR
    inA[User Input] --> fA1[inputMarks]
    fA1 --> dStore[(Central Data Store)]
    dStore --> fB1[calculateTotal]
    fB1 --> dStore
    dStore --> fB2[calculateAverage]
    fB2 --> dStore
    dStore --> fB3[assignGrade]
    fB3 --> dStore
    dStore --> fC1[printReport]
    fC1 --> outA[Formatted Output]
```

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- The **central data store** sits at the *heart* of the design — this is a defining weakness of FOD.
- All processing revolves around the data, but the data has **no identity, no validation, and no encapsulation**.
- In a real-world FOD implementation (e.g., legacy banking software), this single data store can contain tens of thousands of global variables, leading to "**spaghetti code**" where tracing a bug becomes virtually impossible.

## 4.3 FOD vs. OOP Conceptual Mapping

| **FOD Block** | **OOP Equivalent (Java)** |
|---|---|
| `main()` function | `main()` method that creates `Object`s |
| Sub-function | `Method` of a class |
| Global data array | `private` instance variable of a class |
| Structure Chart | **UML Class Diagram** |
| Calling a function | Invoking a method on an object |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Takeaway for KTU 2024:** When the examiner asks *"What is the fundamental difference between FOD and OOP?"*, the most concise, marks-fetching answer is:
> *"FOD treats **functions** as the primary unit and **data** as secondary; OOP treats **data** as the primary unit and **functions (methods)** as secondary services attached to that data."*

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper Pattern, Module 1]**

### Question 1
**Q: Define Functional Oriented Design. List any FOUR of its key characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):**
Functional Oriented Design (FOD) is a software design methodology in which a problem is decomposed into a set of functions or procedures, with a strong emphasis on the *sequence of operations* to be performed. The functions are arranged in a top-down hierarchy, and data is typically global and shared.

**Any Four Characteristics (4 × 0.375 = 1.5 Marks):**
1. Functions are the primary building blocks; data is secondary.
2. The approach is **top-down**, starting from the most abstract `main()` function.
3. Data is usually **global** and accessible across multiple functions.
4. Code reuse is limited to the function level within the same project.
5. Each function is highly cohesive but the system often suffers from high coupling due to shared data.
6. Implementation languages include **C, Pascal, and FORTRAN**.

---

### Question 2
**Q: Differentiate between Functional Oriented Design and Object Oriented Design in terms of (i) primary unit, (ii) data treatment, and (iii) reusability.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | FOD | OOD |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Unit** | Function / Procedure | Class / Object |
| **Data Treatment** | Global, passive, separated from functions | Encapsulated within objects, active |
| **Reusability** | Limited (function-level only) | High (inheritance, polymorphism, composition) |

**Concluding Statement (bonus credit):**
*"In FOD, the programmer asks 'What does the program do?' In OOD, the programmer asks 'What real-world entities does the program model?'"*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester Evaluation, Module 1 Pattern]**

### 📘 Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) Explain the Functional Oriented Design approach in detail with a neat diagram. Discuss its key features.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (2 Marks):**
Functional Oriented Design (FOD) is a classical, top-down, algorithm-centric software design methodology. It emerged in the 1970s alongside structured programming (Dijkstra, Wirth) and dominated software development until the 1990s OOP revolution.

**Step-by-Step Methodology (3 Marks):**
1. Start with the main problem; identify the top-level task.
2. Decompose recursively into smaller functions until each function performs a single, well-defined task.
3. Establish the **structure chart** showing call relationships.
4. Declare necessary data (mostly global) and pass information via parameters.
5. Write algorithms for each function using sequence, selection, and iteration constructs.

**Key Features (2 Marks):**
- Function-centric hierarchy
- Shared global data
- Top-down decomposition
- Passive data, active functions
- High cohesion within functions, but high coupling across the system

**Diagram:** The structure chart drawn in Section 4.1 of these notes may be reproduced.

**(b) Discuss the major advantages and limitations of Functional Oriented Design. Why did the software industry shift to Object Oriented Design?** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Advantages (3 Marks):**
1. **Simplicity:** Easy to learn, understand, and apply for small-to-medium projects.
2. **Efficiency:** Function calls are fast; compiled C/FORTRAN code is highly optimized.
3. **Modularity:** Functions can be developed and unit-tested independently.
4. **Mathematical rigor:** FOD supports formal verification using preconditions and postconditions.
5. **Suitable for embedded and system-level work** where OOP overhead is undesirable.

**Limitations (2.5 Marks):**
1. **Data is not secure** — any function can corrupt global data.
2. **Poor real-world mapping** — code structure does not resemble the problem domain.
3. **Difficult maintenance** — a small change can ripple through many functions.
4. **Limited reusability** — cannot easily extend functions for new data types.
5. **Scalability issues** — beyond ~10,000 LOC, the global data pool becomes unmanageable.

**Why the Industry Shifted (1.5 Marks):**
The explosive growth of GUI-based, event-driven, and large-scale enterprise software in the 1990s exposed FOD's weaknesses. The need for **secure, reusable, and maintainable** code led to the adoption of OOP (C++, Java), which binds *data and behavior together* into classes — directly solving the global-data and reusability problems of FOD.

---

### 📘 Question B (14 Marks) — *Internal Alternative Choice*

**(a) With a suitable example, illustrate how a real-world problem is decomposed using the Functional Oriented Design approach. Draw the corresponding structure chart.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Chosen Example:** *Library Book Management System.*

**Top-Level Functions (2 Marks):**
- `addBook()` — accepts book details and adds to the central database.
- `searchBook()` — locates a book by title, author, or ISBN.
- `issueBook()` — marks a book as issued to a member.
- `returnBook()` — marks a book as returned and updates availability.
- `generateReport()` — prints daily/weekly library statistics.

**Function Hierarchy (2 Marks):**
`main()` invokes `addBook`, `searchBook`, `issueBook`, `returnBook`, and `generateReport`. The `searchBook` function may further delegate to `searchByTitle`, `searchByAuthor`, and `searchByISBN`.

**Structure Chart (2 Marks):** The student should draw a tree-style structure chart with `main` at the root and the five functions as its children, optionally showing further sub-decomposition of `searchBook`. Shared data (`bookDatabase[]`, `memberDatabase[]`) should be indicated as a global block at the side.

**Identification of Global Data (1 Mark):**
`bookDatabase`, `memberDatabase`, `transactionLog` are declared globally and accessed by all functions.

**(b) Compare and contrast Functional Oriented Design and Object Oriented Design across AT LEAST SIX technical parameters.** **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A tabular comparison must be presented covering at least six of the following parameters:

| **Parameter** | **Functional Oriented Design** | **Object Oriented Design** |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Building Block | Function | Class / Object |
| Data-Function Relationship | Data is global & passive; functions operate on it | Data and functions are bundled inside objects (encapsulation) |
| Design Strategy | Top-down decomposition | Bottom-up design using real-world entities |
| Reusability | Function-level only, limited | High, via inheritance, polymorphism, composition |
| Security of Data | Low — global data is exposed to all functions | High — data is `private`/`protected`, accessible only via controlled interfaces |
| Real-World Mapping | Weak — focuses on algorithm | Strong — models real entities and their interactions |
| Maintenance Cost | High for large projects | Low — changes localized to specific classes |
| Code Size Efficiency | Compact, fast execution | Slightly higher memory overhead due to objects |
| Example Languages | C, Pascal, FORTRAN | Java, C++, Python, C# |
| Suitability | Small/medium systems, embedded, HPC | Large enterprise, GUI, web, mobile apps |

**Concluding Statement (1 Mark):**
*"FOD and OOD are not enemies — they are complementary. Modern Java code often contains procedural elements (e.g., `static` utility methods), and high-performance kernels are still written in C-style procedural code. The choice of paradigm must be driven by the problem, not by fashion."*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks on "Approaches to Software Design" Questions:**
> 1. **Conflating FOD with Functional Programming (FP).** These are *different* paradigms. FOD = procedural. FP = mathematical, declarative, pure functions. Writing "FOD means Haskell-style code" will fetch **zero marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting the diagram.** A 14-mark FOD question without a structure chart typically loses **2 to 3 marks**, even if the textual answer is perfect. Always draw the function hierarchy.
> 3. **Listing features without explanation.** Writing "Top-down approach" without explaining *what it means and why it matters* yields only partial credit.
> 4. **Ignoring global data.** Any FOD answer that does not mention *global shared data* as a defining (and problematic) feature is considered incomplete by KTU examiners.
> 5. **No transition to OOP.** Module 1 of this course is structured as a *bridge* to OOP. Concluding your FOD answer with a one-line bridge to OOP ("This limitation led to the development of Object Oriented Design…") is a **bonus mark magnet**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Functional Oriented Design:**

- ⭐ **Definition:** FOD is a *top-down, function-centric* software design methodology where the problem is decomposed into a hierarchy of functions operating on shared (mostly global) data.
- ⭐ **Primary Focus:** "What is being done?" — the algorithm and sequence of operations.
- ⭐ **Primary Building Block:** Function / Procedure (NOT data).
- ⭐ **Design Strategy:** Strict top-down decomposition; structure charts are the standard visualization tool.
- ⭐ **Data Treatment:** Global, passive, shared across functions — this is the **Achilles' heel** of FOD.
- ⭐ **Key Characteristics:** Top-down, function-centric, global data, limited reusability, weak real-world mapping, simple for small systems.
- ⭐ **Typical Languages:** C, Pascal, FORTRAN, COBOL.
- ⭐ **FOD ≠ FP:** FOD is *procedural*; FP (Haskell, Lisp) is *mathematical/declarative*. Never confuse the two in exams.
- ⭐ **Advantages:** Simplicity, efficiency, modularity, suitability for embedded/HPC.
- ⭐ **Limitations:** Data insecurity, poor scalability, high maintenance cost, limited reusability.
- ⭐ **Historical Significance:** FOD dominated software development from the 1970s to early 1990s. Its limitations directly motivated the rise of OOP in the 1990s.
- ⭐ **Bridge to OOP:** OOP binds *data + behavior* into classes, directly solving FOD's data-security and reusability problems — this is the central message of KTU Module 1.
- ⭐ **KTU Exam Mantra for this Topic:** *"Always draw a structure chart, always mention global data, always bridge to OOP in the conclusion."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object Oriented Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Object Oriented Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Object Oriented Design (OOD)** is a software design methodology that models a system as a collection of cooperating **objects**, where each object represents an instance of a **class**, and the system behavior emerges from the interaction between these objects through well-defined **interfaces** and **messages**.

In the KTU 2024 OOP-Java context, Object Oriented Design is the **intermediate phase** of the software development lifecycle that sits between **Object Oriented Analysis (OOA)** and **implementation**. It translates the requirements captured during analysis into a detailed **blueprint** consisting of classes, their attributes, methods, relationships, and collaboration patterns that can be directly implemented in Java.

OOD is grounded in four foundational principles (commonly remembered as the **PIE-A** acronym):
1. **P**olymorphism
2. **I**nheritance
3. **E**ncapsulation
4. **A**bstraction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 of PBEOT404 expects the learner to articulate *why* OOD supersedes classical procedural design, and to demonstrate the ability to identify objects, classes, and responsibilities from a real-world problem statement.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The House Blueprint Analogy:** Think of designing a house before constructing it. An architect does not physically lay bricks first — instead, they produce a **blueprint** showing every room, door, window, pipe, and wire. Two houses built from the *same blueprint* can have different paint colors or furniture, but their **structure** is identical.
>
> - The **Blueprint** = a *Class* (the design/template).
> - The **Actual House** = an *Object* (an instance of the class).
> - The **Rooms and Doors** = *Attributes and Methods* (data + behavior).
> - The **Wiring Plan** (how rooms interact) = *Messages and Interfaces* (collaboration between objects).

This analogy captures the essence of OOD — **design first, instantiate later, and reuse often**.

## 1.3 Why Move From Procedural to Object Oriented Design?

| Aspect | Procedural Design (C-style) | Object Oriented Design (Java-style) |
|---|---|---|
| **Unit of decomposition** | Functions / procedures | Objects / classes |
| **Data handling** | Separate from functions (global) | Bundled with behavior inside objects |
| **Reuse mechanism** | Function libraries | Inheritance and composition |
| **Extensibility** | Difficult (modifies existing code) | Easy (adds new classes/subclasses) |
| **Mapping to real world** | Weak | Strong — nouns → classes, verbs → methods |
| **Maintenance** | High coupling → costly | Low coupling → cheaper |

> [!WARNING]
> A frequent KTU valuation pitfall: students often confuse **Object Oriented Analysis (OOA)** with **Object Oriented Design (OOD)**. OOA answers *what* the system must do; OOD answers *how* the system will do it using software constructs.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Object Oriented Design

### 2.1.1 Encapsulation
**Definition:** The bundling of data (attributes) and the methods that operate on that data into a single unit (class), and the restriction of direct external access to some of the object's components.

**Why it matters:** Protects the **invariants** of an object. For example, a `BankAccount` class can guarantee that its `balance` is never negative because all mutations are funnelled through controlled `deposit()` and `withdraw()` methods.

**How it works in Java:** Achieved using `private` data members and `public` accessor (getter) and mutator (setter) methods.

### 2.1.2 Abstraction
**Definition:** The process of hiding complex implementation details and exposing only the essential features of an object.

**Why it matters:** Reduces complexity. The user of a `Car` object needs to know about `start()`, `accelerate()`, `brake()` — not about the internal combustion cycle.

**How it works in Java:** Achieved through `abstract` classes and `interface` constructs.

### 2.1.3 Inheritance
**Definition:** A mechanism by which a new class (subclass/child) is created from an existing class (superclass/parent), inheriting its attributes and methods, and optionally overriding or extending behavior.

**Why it matters:** Promotes **code reuse** and establishes a natural **"is-a"** hierarchy (e.g., `SavingsAccount` *is-a* `BankAccount`).

**How it works in Java:** Achieved using the `extends` keyword (for classes) and `implements` (for interfaces).

### 2.1.4 Polymorphism
**Definition:** The ability of a single interface to represent different underlying forms (data types). The same message can elicit different behaviors depending on the receiving object.

**Why it matters:** Enables **extensibility**. A `draw()` method on a `Shape` reference can correctly draw a `Circle`, `Square`, or `Triangle` without the caller knowing the exact subclass.

**How it works in Java:** Achieved through **method overloading** (compile-time polymorphism) and **method overriding** (run-time polymorphism via dynamic dispatch).

## 2.2 The Object Oriented Design Process (Step-by-Step)

1. **Identify the objects** from the problem domain (look for nouns).
2. **Identify the classes** by grouping similar objects.
3. **Define attributes** (data that describes the state of each object).
4. **Define methods** (verbs/operations that represent behavior).
5. **Establish relationships** between classes (association, aggregation, composition, inheritance, dependency).
6. **Assign responsibilities** using techniques like **CRC (Class-Responsibility-Collaborator) cards**.
7. **Refine the design** by applying **GRASP** principles (General Responsibility Assignment Software Patterns).

## 2.3 GRASP Principles (High-Yield for KTU)

| Principle | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Information Expert** | Assign responsibility to the class that has the information needed to fulfill it | `calculateArea()` goes to `Circle` (it has `radius`) |
| **Creator** | Class A creates B if A aggregates, contains, or closely uses B | `Order` creates `OrderLine` |
| **Low Coupling** | Minimize dependency between classes | Use interfaces instead of concrete classes |
| **High Cohesion** | Keep related responsibilities together in one class | Avoid "god classes" |
| **Controller** | A non-UI class coordinates system events | `LoginController` handles login flow |

## 2.4 Relationships Between Classes

| Relationship | Symbol (UML) | Meaning | Java Realization |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Association** | `────────` | "uses-a" — objects collaborate | One class holds reference to another |
| **Aggregation** | `◇────────` | "has-a" (weak ownership) | Car *has* a Driver (Driver exists independently) |
| **Composition** | `◆────────` | "owns-a" (strong ownership) | House *owns* Rooms (Rooms die with House) |
| **Inheritance** | `────────▷` | "is-a" | `extends` keyword |
| **Dependency** | `- - - - >` | Temporary use | Local variable, method parameter |
| **Realization** | `- - - - ▷` | Class implements interface | `implements` keyword |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Term | Notation | Constraint / Rule | Unit / Category |
|---|---|---|---|
| Class cardinality | $\vert C \vert$ | Must be $\geq 1$ in any valid OOD model | Count |
| Inheritance depth | $D_{inherit}$ | $D_{inherit} \geq 0$ (0 = no inheritance, uses interfaces) | Integer levels |
| Coupling between objects | $C_{oo}$ | Lower is better; ideally $C_{oo} \rightarrow 0$ | Metric |
| Cohesion of a class | $H_{class}$ | Higher is better; ideally $H_{class} \rightarrow 1$ (normalized) | Metric |
| LCOM (Lack of Cohesion) | $LCOM$ | $LCOM = P - Q$ if $P > Q$, else $0$ | Metric |
| Polymorphic dispatch | $M_{poly}$ | Resolved at runtime via vtable lookup | Mechanism |
| Encapsulation strength | $E_{enc}$ | $E_{enc} = \frac{\text{private members}}{\text{total members}}$ | Ratio $\in [0,1]$ |
| Design reuse ratio | $R_{reuse}$ | $R_{reuse} = \frac{\text{inherited lines}}{\text{total lines}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage |

> [!NOTE]
> For Part A conceptual questions, KTU typically asks the **definition** of these four pillars, *not* numerical computation. The metrics above appear in Part B design-evaluation questions.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Object Oriented Design is the foundation of nearly every modern software system:
- **Enterprise Java (Spring/Hibernate):** Heavily uses inheritance, polymorphism, and dependency injection (a form of Inversion of Control born from OOD).
- **Android Development:** Every UI component is a class; activities inherit from `AppCompatActivity`.
- **Embedded & IoT (Kerala's growing tech sector):** Device drivers modeled as classes with well-defined interfaces.
- **Banking Software (a classic KTU example):** `Account`, `Customer`, `Transaction` classes with strict encapsulation rules enforce business invariants.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation Walkthrough: Deriving Classes from a Problem Statement

This section demonstrates the **exhaustive analytical process** of arriving at an OOD model from a plain-text requirement — a frequently tested Part B (14 marks) skill in KTU exams.

### Problem Statement
> *"Design an object oriented model for a Library Management System that allows a librarian to add books, register members, and issue/return books. Each book has a title, author, ISBN, and a status (available or issued). Each member has a name, memberID, and a list of books currently borrowed."*

### Step 1: Identify the Nouns (Candidate Classes)

We scan the problem for **nouns** that represent potential classes:

$$
\text{Nouns} = \{\text{Library}, \text{Librarian}, \text{Book}, \text{Member}, \text{Status}, \text{Name}, \text{ISBN}, \text{Title}, \text{Author}, \text{List}, \text{MemberID}\}
$$

### Step 2: Filter Irrelevant or Redundant Nouns

We eliminate nouns that are either **attributes** of other nouns, **primitive values**, or **meta-entities** not part of the domain model:

$$
\text{Rejected} = \{\text{Name}, \text{ISBN}, \text{Title}, \text{Author}, \text{Status}, \text{List}, \text{MemberID}\}
$$

These are *attributes*, not classes. The reasoning: a `Name` by itself is not a meaningful software entity — it only exists as a property of a `Member`.

### Step 3: Identify Verbs (Candidate Methods)

We extract **verbs** that represent behaviors:

$$
\text{Verbs} = \{\text{addBook}, \text{registerMember}, \text{issueBook}, \text{returnBook}\}
$$

### Step 4: Refine the Class Set

After filtering, the final candidate class set is:

$$
C_{final} = \{\text{Library}, \text{Librarian}, \text{Book}, \text{Member}\}
$$

### Step 5: Assign Attributes to Each Class

Each class gets state information derived from the problem:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Book} &\rightarrow \{title, author, isbn, status\} \\
\text{Member} &\rightarrow \{name, memberID, borrowedBooks\} \\
\text{Librarian} &\rightarrow \{name, employeeID\} \\
\text{Library} &\rightarrow \{name, address, bookList, memberList\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6: Assign Methods (Responsibilities)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Book} &\rightarrow \{getTitle(), getStatus(), setStatus()\} \\
\text{Member} &\rightarrow \{getName(), getMemberID(), borrowBook(), returnBook()\} \\
\text{Librarian} &\rightarrow \{addBook(), registerMember(), issueBook(), returnBook()\} \\
\text{Library} &\rightarrow \{searchBook(), getAllBooks()\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 7: Identify Relationships

From the verb interactions we derive the relationship graph:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Librarian} &\;\text{manages}\; \rightarrow \text{Library} \\
\text{Library} &\;\text{contains}\; \rightarrow \text{Book} \quad \text{(composition)} \\
\text{Library} &\;\text{contains}\; \rightarrow \text{Member} \quad \text{(aggregation)} \\
\text{Member} &\;\text{borrows}\; \rightarrow \text{Book} \quad \text{(association, many-to-many)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 8: Apply GRASP Information Expert Principle

The responsibility of *checking availability* belongs to `Book` because it owns the `status` attribute:

$$
\text{Responsibility}(isAvailable) \rightarrow \text{Class}(Book) \quad \because \text{Book has } status
$$

The responsibility of *issuing* a book belongs to `Librarian` (or a `LibraryController`) because it coordinates both `Book` and `Member`:

$$
\text{Responsibility}(issueBook) \rightarrow \text{Class}(Librarian)
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Mapping OOD Concepts to Java Constructs

| OOD Concept | Java Construct | Syntax Pattern | Encapsulation Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Class | `class` keyword | `public class Car { }` | `private` fields + `public` methods |
| Object | `new` operator | `Car myCar = new Car();` | Reference in stack, instance in heap |
| Attribute | Field declaration | `private int speed;` | `private` access modifier |
| Method | Method declaration | `public void brake() { }` | `public` access modifier |
| Encapsulation | Access modifiers | `private`, `protected`, `public`, default | Getters/setters |
| Abstraction | `abstract class` / `interface` | `abstract class Shape { }` | Cannot instantiate |
| Inheritance | `extends` | `class Dog extends Animal { }` | `super` keyword for parent |
| Polymorphism | Method overriding | `@Override public void draw() { }` | Dynamic dispatch via vtable |
| Interface | `interface` keyword | `interface Drawable { void draw(); }` | All methods implicitly `public abstract` |
| Package | `package` keyword | `package com.ktu.oop;` | Namespace + access boundary |
| Composition | Field of object type | `private Engine engine;` | Strong ownership |
| Aggregation | Field of object type | `private List<Driver> drivers;` | Weak ownership |
| Association | Local variable / parameter | `void process(Book b) { }` | Temporary link |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation of the LCOM Metric (Lack of Cohesion of Methods)

The LCOM metric quantifies the lack of cohesion in a class, which KTU may include in a design-evaluation question.

**Step 1:** Let $M$ be the set of instance methods, and $F$ be the set of instance fields.

**Step 2:** Define $P$ as the number of method pairs $(m_i, m_j)$ that **do not share** any instance field access:

$$
P = \vert \{(m_i, m_j) \mid m_i, m_j \in M, \; i < j, \; \text{access}(m_i) \cap \text{access}(m_j) = \emptyset\} \vert
$$

**Step 3:** Define $Q$ as the number of method pairs that **share** at least one instance field access:

$$
Q = \vert \{(m_i, m_j) \mid m_i, m_j \in M, \; i < j, \; \text{access}(m_i) \cap \text{access}(m_j) \neq \emptyset\} \vert
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the LCOM value:

$$
LCOM = 
\begin{cases}
P - Q, & \text{if } P > Q \\
0, & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Interpretation:**
- $LCOM = 0 \Rightarrow$ **High cohesion** (good design).
- $LCOM > 0 \Rightarrow$ Consider splitting the class.

**Worked Example:**
A class `StudentReport` has methods `m_1$: calculateGPA()`, `m_2$: printTranscript()`, `m_3$: calculateAttendance()` and fields `{gpa, attendance}`.

- $m_1$ accesses `{gpa}$`, $m_2$ accesses `{gpa, attendance}$`, $m_3$ accesses `{attendance}$`.
- Pairs sharing fields: $(m_1, m_2)$ share `gpa`; $(m_2, m_3)$ share `attendance`. So $Q = 2$.
- Pairs not sharing: $(m_1, m_3)$ share nothing. So $P = 1$.

$$
LCOM = \max(P - Q, 0) = \max(1 - 2, 0) = 0
$$

Since $LCOM = 0$, the class has **high cohesion** — methods are well-coordinated around the same data.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Object Oriented Design Workflow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **sequential pipeline** followed when performing OOD on a problem statement. Each stage feeds the next, culminating in a Java-implementable design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[problemStatement] --> B[nounExtraction]
    B --> C[classIdentification]
    C --> D[attributeAssignment]
    D --> E[methodAssignment]
    E --> F[relationshipMapping]
    F --> G[responsibilityAllocation]
    G --> H[graspRefinement]
    H --> I[umlClassDiagram]
    I --> J[javaImplementation]
    B --> B1[rejectAttributes]
    B1 --> C
    F --> F1[association]
    F --> F2[aggregation]
    F --> F3[composition]
    F --> F4[inheritance]
```

**Reading the diagram:** A textual problem is first scanned for nouns. After filtering out attribute-nouns, candidate classes emerge. Each class receives its state (attributes) and behavior (methods). Relationships are then mapped, and responsibilities are allocated using GRASP. The refined model is captured as a UML class diagram and finally translated to Java source code.

## 4.2 UML Class Diagram of the Library System

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **UML class diagram** for the Library Management System derived in Section 3.

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Library {
        -String name
        -String address
        -List~Book~ bookList
        -List~Member~ memberList
        +searchBook(isbn String) Book
        +getAllBooks() List~Book~
    }
    class Book {
        -String title
        -String author
        -String isbn
        -boolean isAvailable
        +getTitle() String
        +getStatus() boolean
        +setStatus(status boolean) void
    }
    class Member {
        -String name
        -String memberID
        -List~Book~ borrowedBooks
        +getName() String
        +getMemberID() String
        +borrowBook(b Book) void
        +returnBook(b Book) void
    }
    class Librarian {
        -String name
        -String employeeID
        +addBook(b Book) void
        +registerMember(m Member) void
        +issueBook(m Member, b Book) void
        +returnBook(m Member, b Book) void
    }
    Library "1" --> "*" Book : contains
    Library "1" --> "*" Member : registers
    Member "1" --> "*" Book : borrows
    Librarian "1" --> "1" Library : manages
```

**Reading the diagram:** The `Library` class composes (strong ownership) many `Book` objects and aggregates many `Member` objects. A `Member` may borrow many `Book` objects. The `Librarian` manages one `Library`. All multiplicities are explicitly declared.

## 4.3 The Four Pillars — Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph oopPillars[Object Oriented Design Pillars]
        A1[Encapsulation] --> A2[Abstraction]
        A2 --> A3[Inheritance]
        A3 --> A4[Polymorphism]
    end
    A1 -. uses .-> B1[private fields and public methods]
    A2 -. uses .-> B2[abstract classes and interfaces]
    A3 -. uses .-> B3[extends keyword]
    A4 -. uses .-> B4[method overriding and overloading]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The four pillars form a learning progression — each builds on the previous. Encapsulation uses access modifiers, Abstraction uses `abstract`/`interface`, Inheritance uses `extends`, and Polymorphism uses method overriding/overloading.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]

> **Q1.** Define *Object Oriented Design*. List any four advantages of OOD over procedural design.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Object Oriented Design (OOD) is a software design methodology that organizes a system as a collection of cooperating objects, where each object is an instance of a class encapsulating both data (attributes) and behavior (methods), and the system is modeled around real-world entities and their interactions.

**Four Advantages (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Better modularity** through class-based decomposition.
2. **Code reuse** via inheritance and composition.
3. **Easier maintenance** due to low coupling and high cohesion.
4. **Improved extensibility** through polymorphism — new classes can be added without modifying existing code.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Supplementary]

> **Q2.** Differentiate between *abstraction* and *encapsulation* with suitable examples.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Abstraction | Encapsulation |
|---|---|---|
| **Goal** | Hide implementation complexity | Hide internal data |
| **Focus** | *What* an object does | *How* the data is protected |
| **Achieved by** | `abstract class`, `interface` | `private` fields + `public` getters/setters |
| **Example** | `Shape.draw()` interface — caller doesn't know how each shape draws | `BankAccount.balance` is `private`; accessed via `getBalance()` |
| **Mechanism** | Design-level decision | Implementation-level discipline |

*(0.5 mark per correct row; 3 rows = 1.5 marks; 1.5 marks for accurate examples and explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Model Paper]

> **Q.A.** (a) Explain the **Object Oriented Design process** in detail. Describe each step with a suitable example. **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) Design an **object oriented model** for an *Online Course Registration System*. Identify classes, attributes, methods, and relationships. Draw the **UML class diagram**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) OOD Process — 7 marks**

The Object Oriented Design process is a systematic methodology consisting of the following steps:

1. **Requirement Gathering and Noun Extraction (1 mark):** Read the problem statement carefully. Identify all nouns; these are *candidate classes*. Example: in a Library System, nouns include "Library", "Book", "Member", "Librarian".

2. **Class Identification and Filtering (1 mark):** Remove nouns that are attributes, primitive values, or meta-entities. The remaining nouns form the class set $C_{final}$.

3. **Attribute Identification (1 mark):** For each class, identify the data fields that describe its state. Example: `Book` has `title`, `author`, `isbn`, `status`.

4. **Method Identification (1 mark):** Extract verbs from the problem statement; these become methods. Example: `addBook`, `registerMember`, `issueBook`.

5. **Relationship Establishment (1 mark):** Identify associations, aggregations, compositions, and inheritance between classes. Apply multiplicities.

6. **Responsibility Allocation (1 mark):** Use **GRASP** principles (Information Expert, Creator, Controller, Low Coupling, High Cohesion) to assign methods to the most appropriate classes.

7. **Refinement and UML Modeling (1 mark):** Apply design patterns where needed, refine the model, and produce a **UML class diagram** that serves as the implementation blueprint.

**(b) Online Course Registration System — 7 marks**

**Nouns:** Online, System, Course, Student, Registration, Instructor, Schedule, Fee, Grade, Semester.

**Class Identification:**

$$
C_{final} = \{\text{Course}, \text{Student}, \text{Instructor}, \text{Registration}, \text{Semester}\}
$$

**Attributes and Methods:**

| Class | Attributes | Methods |
|---|---|---|
| `Course` | `courseCode`, `title`, `credits`, `instructor` | `getDetails()`, `assignInstructor()` |
| `Student` | `studentID`, `name`, `email`, `registeredCourses` | `registerForCourse()`, `dropCourse()`, `viewGrades()` |
| `Instructor` | `instructorID`, `name`, `department`, `coursesTaught` | `assignGrade()`, `viewCourses()` |
| `Registration` | `registrationID`, `date`, `student`, `course`, `status` | `confirmRegistration()`, `cancelRegistration()` |
| `Semester` | `semesterID`, `startDate`, `endDate`, `courseList` | `addCourse()`, `getActiveCourses()` |

**Relationships:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Student} &\;\text{has}\; \rightarrow \text{Registration} \quad \text{(composition, 1 to many)} \\
\text{Course} &\;\text{has}\; \rightarrow \text{Registration} \quad \text{(composition, 1 to many)} \\
\text{Instructor} &\;\text{teaches}\; \rightarrow \text{Course} \quad \text{(association, 1 to many)} \\
\text{Semester} &\;\text{contains}\; \rightarrow \text{Course} \quad \text{(aggregation, 1 to many)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**UML Class Diagram:**

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Course {
        -String courseCode
        -String title
        -int credits
        -Instructor instructor
        +getDetails() String
        +assignInstructor(i Instructor) void
    }
    class Student {
        -String studentID
        -String name
        -String email
        -List~Course~ registeredCourses
        +registerForCourse(c Course) void
        +dropCourse(c Course) void
        +viewGrades() void
    }
    class Instructor {
        -String instructorID
        -String name
        -String department
        -List~Course~ coursesTaught
        +assignGrade(s Student, c Course, g String) void
        +viewCourses() List~Course~
    }
    class Registration {
        -String registrationID
        -Date date
        -Student student
        -Course course
        -String status
        +confirmRegistration() void
        +cancelRegistration() void
    }
    class Semester {
        -String semesterID
        -Date startDate
        -Date endDate
        -List~Course~ courseList
        +addCourse(c Course) void
        +getActiveCourses() List~Course~
    }
    Student "1" --> "*" Registration : initiates
    Course "1" --> "*" Registration : receives
    Instructor "1" --> "*" Course : teaches
    Semester "1" --> "*" Course : offers
```

*(7 marks: 2 marks for class identification with attributes, 2 marks for methods, 2 marks for relationships with proper cardinalities, 1 mark for the UML diagram.)*

---

### Question B — Choice 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]

> **Q.B.** (a) Explain the **four pillars of Object Oriented Design** with Java-style examples for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> (b) Explain the **GRASP principles** of *Information Expert*, *Creator*, *Low Coupling*, and *High Cohesion*. For each, give a real-world analogy and identify the class that should hold the responsibility. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Four Pillars of OOD — 7 marks**

1. **Encapsulation (1.75 marks):**
   Bundling data and methods that operate on that data within a class, and restricting external access using access modifiers. In Java, declaring fields as `private` and exposing controlled access via `public` getter/setter methods achieves encapsulation. Example: a `BankAccount` class keeps `balance` private and allows modification only through `deposit()` and `withdraw()`, ensuring the balance invariant is preserved.

2. **Abstraction (1.75 marks):**
   Hiding implementation details and exposing only the essential interface. In Java, this is achieved using `abstract` classes (which may have both abstract and concrete methods) and `interface` types (which declare method signatures without implementation). Example: an `interface Shape` with method `double area()` allows each implementing class (`Circle`, `Rectangle`) to provide its own computation logic while presenting a uniform API to the caller.

3. **Inheritance (1.75 marks):**
   The mechanism by which a subclass inherits attributes and methods from a superclass, enabling code reuse and the "is-a" relationship. In Java, achieved using the `extends` keyword. Example: `class SavingsAccount extends BankAccount` inherits common fields and methods and may add interest-related functionality.

4. **Polymorphism (1.75 marks):**
   The ability of a single interface to invoke different behaviors depending on the actual object type at runtime. In Java, achieved via method overriding (run-time) and method overloading (compile-time). Example: a `Shape` reference variable can call `draw()`, and the JVM dynamically dispatches to `Circle.draw()` or `Square.draw()` based on the actual object.

**(b) GRASP Principles — 7 marks**

1. **Information Expert (1.75 marks):**
   *Principle:* Assign a responsibility to the class that has the information necessary to fulfill it.
   *Real-world analogy:* A doctor (not the receptionist) prescribes medicine because they hold the medical knowledge.
   *Example:* `Circle.calculateArea()` should reside in the `Circle` class, not in a separate `AreaCalculator`, because `Circle` already holds the `radius`.

2. **Creator (1.75 marks):**
   *Principle:* Class A should be responsible for creating instances of class B if A aggregates, contains, or closely uses B.
   *Real-world analogy:* A factory (creator) produces cars (created objects) because it has the raw materials and assembly knowledge.
   *Example:* The `Order` class should create `OrderLine` objects, because an `Order` aggregates many `OrderLine` items.

3. **Low Coupling (1.75 marks):**
   *Principle:* Minimize the dependencies between classes so that changes in one class do not ripple through the system.
   *Real-world analogy:* Two houses built far apart are less likely to be damaged by a single fire than two adjacent houses.
   *Example:* Instead of `ReportPrinter` directly depending on `MySQLDatabase`, depend on an interface `Database` so the implementation can be swapped without modifying the printer.

4. **High Cohesion (1.75 marks):**
   *Principle:* Keep related responsibilities concentrated in a single class; avoid classes that try to do too much.
   *Real-world analogy:* A surgeon specializes in surgery rather than performing all medical tasks — specialization leads to excellence.
   *Example:* A `User` class should manage user data and authentication, not handle email sending, report generation, and payment processing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do not confuse OOA with OOD.** OOA captures *what* the system does; OOD specifies *how* it is built using software constructs. Examiners deduct up to 1 mark for this confusion.
> 2. **Always justify the relationship type.** A common error is writing "associated with" for every relationship. You must distinguish between **association, aggregation, and composition** with proper reasoning (ownership, lifetime dependency).
> 3. **Include multiplicities in your UML diagrams** (e.g., `1` to `*`). Omitting them loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Do not list every noun as a class.** Attributes and primitive values are not classes. Examiners expect the *filtered* class set.
> 5. **For Part A**, definitions must be crisp (2–3 lines). Long paragraphs are penalized for not being exam-oriented.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Object Oriented Design (OOD)** translates problem requirements into a software model of cooperating **objects** grouped into **classes**.
- The **four pillars** of OOD are **Encapsulation, Abstraction, Inheritance, and Polymorphism** (acronym: **EAIP** or **PIE-A**).
- The **OOD process** follows a sequence: *Noun Extraction → Class Filtering → Attribute/Method Assignment → Relationship Mapping → Responsibility Allocation → UML Modeling → Implementation*.
- **Encapsulation** is achieved in Java using `private` fields with `public` getters/setters.
- **Abstraction** is achieved using `abstract` classes and `interface` constructs.
- **Inheritance** uses the `extends` keyword and models the **"is-a"** relationship.
- **Polymorphism** has two forms: **compile-time (overloading)** and **run-time (overriding)**.
- **GRASP principles** guide responsibility assignment: **Information Expert, Creator, Controller, Low Coupling, High Cohesion**.
- **Class relationships** include: *association* (uses-a), *aggregation* (has-a, weak), *composition* (owns-a, strong), *inheritance* (is-a), *dependency* (temporary), and *realization* (implements interface).
- **UML class diagrams** must include: class name, attributes (with visibility), methods (with visibility), relationships (with multiplicities).
- The **LCOM metric** quantifies lack of cohesion: $LCOM = \max(P - Q, 0)$; $LCOM = 0$ indicates high cohesion.
- For KTU exams, always **filter nouns carefully**, **justify relationship types**, and **include multiplicities** in UML diagrams.
- The **Information Expert** principle is the most frequently tested GRASP concept in KTU Part B questions.
- **CRC (Class-Responsibility-Collaborator) cards** are a useful brainstorming tool for identifying responsibilities before formal UML modeling.
- A good OOD exhibits **low coupling** and **high cohesion** — these are the two most important *qualitative* metrics for any design.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Case Study of Automated Fire Alarm System

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

An **Automated Fire Alarm System (AFAS)** is a software-controlled, real-time event-driven system that continuously monitors environmental parameters such as **smoke density**, **temperature**, and **gas concentration** to detect fire hazards in a building. In the context of **Object Oriented Programming (OOP)**, it serves as a canonical pedagogical case study used to model real-world entities as a network of interacting software objects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Highlight:** In OOP, the Fire Alarm System is decomposed into **collaborating classes** (e.g., `Sensor`, `Alarm`, `Building`, `ControlUnit`, `Sprinkler`) where each class encapsulates a specific responsibility. The case study illustrates the four foundational pillars of OOP: **Abstraction, Encapsulation, Modularity, and Hierarchy**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

Imagine a large shopping mall. There are **smoke detectors** on the ceiling, **sprinklers** above the aisles, a **fire alarm bell** near the exits, and a **control room** where a security guard sits. None of these devices are independent — they **communicate** with each other:

- A smoke detector *senses* danger → sends a signal to the control room.
- The control room *decides* what to do → triggers the alarm bell AND the sprinklers.
- The alarm bell *notifies* people, while sprinklers *suppress* the fire.

In OOP, each of these devices becomes a **class** (a blueprint), and each physical device in the mall becomes an **object** (an instance). The wiring that connects them becomes **message passing** (method calls). This is the essence of an Object Oriented case study.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world parallel:** Think of the system as a "team of specialists." Each specialist has one job, owns their own tools, and they coordinate by sending messages — not by knowing each other's internal brain. That is OOP in one sentence.

## 1.3 Why KTU Chose This Case Study for Module 1

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Familiar domain** | Every student has seen a fire alarm — no domain expertise required. |
| **Multiple interacting objects** | Naturally exercises classes, methods, and message passing. |
| **Event-driven nature** | Introduces the concept of *objects reacting to events*. |
| **Layered responsibilities** | Demonstrates *separation of concerns* (sensing vs. decision vs. action). |
| **Extensible design** | Easy to later add OOP features (inheritance for different sensor types). |

## 1.4 Core System Components (Vocabulary)

> [!NOTE]
> The following entities are the **canonical classes** typically identified in this KTU case study. Mastering them is essential for Part A short-answer questions.

- **Building** — the top-level container holding floors and rooms.
- **Floor** — a sub-container of rooms.
- **Room** — the smallest physical space where a fire may occur.
- **Sensor** (subtypes: `SmokeSensor`, `TemperatureSensor`) — detects abnormal conditions.
- **ControlUnit** — the "brain" that processes sensor input and triggers responses.
- **Alarm** — auditory/visual alert device.
- **Sprinkler** — fire suppression device.
- **Notifier** — sends SMS/email to fire department and building occupants.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

## 2.1 System Operational Logic (Step-by-Step)

The Automated Fire Alarm System operates as a continuous **sense → decide → act** loop. Below is the structured logical flow:

1. **Initialization Phase**
   - The `ControlUnit` powers up.
   - It registers all `Sensor` objects in the `Building` (one per `Room`).
   - It verifies connectivity (each sensor returns a `ping()` acknowledgment).
2. **Monitoring Phase (Idle Loop)**
   - Every `Sensor` continuously samples its environment at a fixed polling interval (typically **every 100 ms to 1 s** in real hardware).
   - Each sample is stored as a `reading` attribute.
3. **Detection Phase**
   - When a `Sensor.reading` exceeds its `threshold`:
     - It raises an internal `isTriggered = true` flag.
     - It sends a `notify(reading)` message to the registered `ControlUnit`.
4. **Decision Phase**
   - The `ControlUnit` validates the alert (reduces false positives using a **2-out-of-3 confirmation rule**).
   - It identifies the `Room` ID from the sensor's location.
   - It consults a **priority policy table**.
5. **Action Phase**
   - The `ControlUnit` invokes `alarm.activate()` for the relevant zone.
   - It invokes `sprinkler.activate()` for the relevant zone.
   - It invokes `notifier.sendAlert("FIRE at Room 304")`.
6. **Reset Phase**
   - Once `Sensor.reading` falls below the threshold for a sustained period, the `ControlUnit` resets all devices to standby.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" behind step 4:** The 2-out-of-3 confirmation rule is **not an OOP rule** — it is a real-world **reliability engineering** technique to avoid false alarms from cooking steam or cigarette smoke. In OOP modeling, this is implemented as a method of `ControlUnit` called `validateAlert(sensorList)`.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet

| Concept | OOP Realization in Fire Alarm System | KTU Exam Weight |
|---|---|---|
| **Class** | A blueprint like `Sensor`, `Alarm`, `ControlUnit` | Very High |
| **Object** | A specific instance, e.g., `smokeSensorRoom304` | Very High |
| **Attribute** | `int threshold; boolean isTriggered;` | Very High |
| **Method** | `sense()`, `activate()`, `notify()` | Very High |
| **Encapsulation** | Sensor's `reading` is private; accessed via `getReading()` | High |
| **Abstraction** | User interacts only with `ControlUnit.armSystem()` — internal details hidden | High |
| **Message Passing** | `controlUnit.notify(sensor)` is a method call between objects | High |
| **Modularity** | Each device is a separate `.java` file | Medium |
| **Association** | `Room` *has-a* `Sensor` (one-to-one) | High |
| **Aggregation** | `Building` *has-a* `Floor` *has-a* `Room` (whole-part, can exist independently) | High |
| **Event-Driven** | Sensor raises event → ControlUnit handles it | Medium |

## 2.3 Class Identification Methodology (CRITICAL for 14-mark questions)

KTU examiners expect students to demonstrate the **Grady Booch Object-Oriented Analysis technique** for this case study:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 1 — Noun Extraction:** Underline all nouns in the problem statement ("A building has floors. Each floor has rooms. Each room has smoke and temperature sensors..."). Nouns become **classes** or **attributes**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 2 — Verb Extraction:** Underline all verbs ("senses", "triggers", "notifies", "activates"). Verbs become **methods** or **events**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 3 — Filter:** Remove synonyms, eliminate nouns that represent values rather than entities, and discard implementation details.

### Sample Application

> **Nouns extracted:** building, floor, room, sensor, smoke, temperature, alarm, sprinkler, control unit, notifier.
> **Verbs extracted:** sense, detect, trigger, activate, notify, reset, arm, disarm.

**Resulting Class List:** `Building`, `Floor`, `Room`, `Sensor` (abstract), `SmokeSensor`, `TemperatureSensor`, `Alarm`, `Sprinkler`, `ControlUnit`, `Notifier`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

This case study maps directly to **embedded systems in IoT smart buildings**, **SCADA industrial control**, and **BIS 2189:2008** fire detection code (India). The OOP model allows hardware engineers and software engineers to work on parallel modules — exactly the architectural philosophy behind **ROS (Robot Operating System)** and **Industrial IoT frameworks**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Class Design Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section walks through the *complete* class-by-class design that KTU expects in a 14-mark question. Each class is shown with its **attributes**, **methods**, and **justification**.

## 3.1 Class 1: `Sensor` (Abstract Superclass)

**Justification:** Both smoke sensors and temperature sensors share common behavior (sensing, threshold checking, notifying) but differ in the *type* of value they read. This is the textbook setup for **inheritance and polymorphism** in later modules — Module 1 lays the groundwork.

$$
\text{Attributes} = \{\texttt{int sensorId}, \texttt{String location}, \texttt{float threshold}, \texttt{float currentReading}, \texttt{boolean isTriggered}\}
$$

$$
\text{Methods} = \{\texttt{void sense()}, \texttt{boolean isAboveThreshold()}, \texttt{void notify(ControlUnit c)}, \texttt{float getReading()}\}
$$

**Step-by-step reasoning for each method:**

- `sense()` → Reads the physical transducer. Returns void; updates `currentReading`.
- `isAboveThreshold()` → Compares `currentReading > threshold`. Returns boolean.
- `notify(ControlUnit c)` → Sends message to ControlUnit. This is the **message passing** concept in action.
- `getReading()` → Encapsulation: external code cannot read the raw field directly.

## 3.2 Class 2: `SmokeSensor` (Subclass)

In Module 1, this is often shown as a **concrete class** with a smoke-specific threshold (e.g., **obscuration > 5 %/m** as per NFPA 72).

$$
\text{Inherits from Sensor}, \quad \text{Adds: } \texttt{int smokeDensityPPM}
$$

## 3.3 Class 3: `TemperatureSensor` (Subclass)

$$
\text{Inherits from Sensor}, \quad \text{Adds: } \texttt{double temperatureCelsius}, \quad \text{Threshold default} = 60^\circ \text{C}
$$

## 3.4 Class 4: `Alarm`

$$
\text{Attributes} = \{\texttt{int alarmId}, \texttt{String zone}, \texttt{boolean isActive}, \texttt{int decibels}\}
$$

$$
\text{Methods} = \{\texttt{void activate()}, \texttt{void deactivate()}, \texttt{void ring()}\}
$$

**Logic walkthrough for `activate()`:**

1. Check if `isActive == false` (avoid double-activation).
2. Set `isActive = true`.
3. Call `ring()` which outputs a **75–120 dB** audio alert.
4. Log timestamp for incident report.

## 3.5 Class 5: `Sprinkler`

$$
\text{Attributes} = \{\texttt{int sprinklerId}, \texttt{String zone}, \texttt{float waterFlowLPM}, \texttt{boolean isActive}\}
$$

$$
\text{Methods} = \{\texttt{void activate()}, \texttt{void releaseWater()}, \texttt{void deactivate()}\}
$$

**Real-world standard:** Sprinklers typically discharge **60–80 L/min** per head until manually stopped or until the `ControlUnit` sends a `deactivate()` after sensor reset.

## 3.6 Class 6: `ControlUnit` (The "Brain")

$$
\text{Attributes} = \{\texttt{List<Sensor> sensors}, \texttt{List<Alarm> alarms}, \texttt{List<Sprinkler> sprinklers}, \texttt{Notifier notifier}, \texttt{boolean isArmed}\}
$$

$$
\text{Methods} = \{\texttt{void registerSensor(Sensor s)}, \texttt{void armSystem()}, \texttt{void disarmSystem()}, \texttt{void handleAlert(Sensor s)}, \texttt{void resetAll()}\}
$$

**Step-by-step logic of `handleAlert(Sensor s)`:**

1. Receive the sensor ID and reading from the message.
2. Look up the `zone` of the sensor.
3. Find all `Alarm` and `Sprinkler` objects whose `zone` matches.
4. Call `activate()` on each.
5. Construct a `Notification` object with timestamp, zone, sensor type, reading.
6. Call `notifier.send(notification)`.

## 3.7 Class 7: `Room`

$$
\text{Attributes} = \{\texttt{int roomNumber}, \texttt{String roomType}, \texttt{Sensor smokeSensor}, \texttt{Sensor tempSensor}\}
$$

This class shows the **"has-a" (association)** relationship: a Room *has-a* smoke sensor and a temperature sensor.

## 3.8 Class 8: `Floor` and `Building`

$$
\text{Floor} = \{\texttt{int floorNumber}, \texttt{List<Room> rooms}\}
$$

$$
\text{Building} = \{\texttt{String buildingName}, \texttt{String address}, \texttt{List<Floor> floors}, \texttt{ControlUnit controlUnit}\}
$$

This hierarchy demonstrates **aggregation** — a Floor can theoretically exist without a Building (e.g., a free-standing warehouse floor), and a Building can be empty of floors (demolished upper floors).

## 3.9 Master Analytical Matrix — Class Summary

| Class | Key Attribute | Key Method | Relationship |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Building` | `floors: List<Floor>` | `addFloor()` | *has-a* Floor (aggregation) |
| `Floor` | `rooms: List<Room>` | `addRoom()` | *has-a* Room (aggregation) |
| `Room` | `roomNumber, sensors` | `getSensor()` | *has-a* Sensor (association) |
| `Sensor` (abstract) | `threshold, currentReading` | `sense(), notify()` | *is-a* SmokeSensor / TempSensor (inheritance) |
| `Alarm` | `zone, isActive` | `activate(), ring()` | *used-by* ControlUnit |
| `Sprinkler` | `zone, waterFlowLPM` | `activate()` | *used-by* ControlUnit |
| `ControlUnit` | `sensors, alarms, sprinklers` | `handleAlert()` | *controls* all devices |
| `Notifier` | `phoneNumbers, emails` | `send()` | *used-by* ControlUnit |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matrix matters:** In a 14-mark question, KTU examiners award 7 marks for class design and 7 marks for relationships. This matrix is your single-page cheat sheet for both.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]`

**Identify the main classes in the "Automated Fire Alarm System" case study and explain any TWO with their key attributes and methods.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The main classes identified in the Automated Fire Alarm System are: `Building`, `Floor`, `Room`, `Sensor`, `SmokeSensor`, `TemperatureSensor`, `Alarm`, `Sprinkler`, `ControlUnit`, and `Notifier`.

**Class 1: `Sensor`** (1 mark)
- **Attributes:** `sensorId`, `location`, `threshold`, `currentReading`, `isTriggered`.
- **Methods:** `sense()`, `isAboveThreshold()`, `notify(ControlUnit c)`.

**Class 2: `ControlUnit`** (1 mark)
- **Attributes:** `List<Sensor> sensors`, `List<Alarm> alarms`, `List<Sprinkler> sprinklers`, `notifier`, `isArmed`.
- **Methods:** `registerSensor()`, `armSystem()`, `handleAlert()`, `resetAll()`.

**Significance:** (1 mark) The `Sensor` detects abnormal conditions and notifies the `ControlUnit`, which acts as the central decision-maker. This division follows the **Single Responsibility Principle** of OOP.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]`

**Explain the concept of message passing in the context of the Fire Alarm System. How does it differ from a procedure-oriented function call?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Message Passing in OOP (2 marks):**
Message passing is the process by which an object invokes a method of another object to request a service. In the Fire Alarm System, when a `SmokeSensor` detects smoke above threshold, it sends a message `controlUnit.notify(this, reading)` to the `ControlUnit`. The `ControlUnit` then sends messages `alarm.activate()` and `sprinkler.activate()` to respective objects.

**Difference from procedure-oriented call (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Procedure-Oriented Call | OOP Message Passing |
|---|---|---|
| Binding | Function name resolved at compile time | Method resolved at runtime (dynamic dispatch) |
| Data ownership | Global data is shared | Data is owned by the receiver object |
| Coupling | High (function knows global structure) | Low (sender only knows receiver's interface) |

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 regulations, every Part B question carries **14 marks** split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You must answer EITHER Question A OR Question B in full.
