# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Explain the role of the Java compiler (`javac`) in the Java platform. Why does it produce bytecode instead of native machine code?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Java compiler `javac` is a translator program supplied with the JDK that converts Java **source code (`.java` files)** into **Java bytecode (`.class` files)** — an intermediate, platform-independent instruction set defined by the JVM Specification.

It does **not** produce native machine code because Java follows a **two-stage execution model**: the compiler handles language semantics once, and the **JVM** (which differs per operating system) interprets or JIT-compiles that bytecode on each target platform. This separation is the technical foundation of Java's **"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)** principle. The bytecode is verified by the JVM's bytecode verifier for safety before execution, enabling Java's strong security and portability guarantees.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Distinguish between compile-time errors and runtime errors in Java. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Compile-Time Error | Runtime Error |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Detected by** | Java compiler (`javac`) | JVM during execution |
| **When** | Before the program runs | During program execution |
| **Cause** | Violation of syntax or static semantics | Invalid operation during execution |
| **Example** | `int x = "hello";` → `incompatible types` | `int a = 10/0;` → `ArithmeticException` |
| **Fix** | Correct the source code | Add validation / exception handling |

A **logical error** is a third category — the program runs to completion but produces wrong output; it is detected by neither compiler nor JVM.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. Students often confuse `javac` (compiler) with `java` (launcher) — **remember: `javac` → `.class`, `java` → execution**.
> 2. Do NOT write `.class` extension with `java Hello.class` — this will throw `ClassNotFoundException`.
> 3. Forgetting the entry point signature `public static void main(String[] args)` leads to: `Error: Main method not found`.
> 4. In Part B answers, students often **only list phases** without **explaining what each phase produces**. KTU examiners expect you to state the **input → output** of every phase.
> 5. Avoid generic statements like "compiler checks errors" — be specific: *"the lexical analyzer detects illegal characters"* vs *"the semantic analyzer detects type mismatches"*.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** Describe the various phases of the Java compilation process with a neat diagram. Explain the role of the lexical analyzer and the syntax analyzer in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to add two integers and explain the bytecode generated by `javac` for the addition statement. Use `javap -c` to demonstrate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

**Java Compilation Phases:**

The Java compiler (`javac`) executes the following six logical phases to transform `.java` source code into `.class` bytecode.

**Phase 1 — Lexical Analysis (1.5 marks)**

- The **lexer** (implemented as `JavaTokenizer` in `jdk.compiler`) scans the source character stream and groups characters into **tokens** — the smallest meaningful units such as keywords (`int`, `class`), identifiers (`x`, `main`), literals (`10`, `"hello"`), operators (`+`, `=`), and separators (`{`, `;`, `)`).
- Whitespace and comments are discarded.
- **Example:** The statement `int a = 10;` produces tokens: `int`, `a`, `=`, `10`, `;`.
- It detects errors such as **illegal characters** and **unterminated string literals**.

**Phase 2 — Syntax Analysis (1.5 marks)**

- The **parser** consumes the token stream and verifies that the sequence of tokens conforms to Java's **context-free grammar** (defined formally in the JLS).
- It constructs an **Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)** — a hierarchical tree where each node represents a syntactic construct (class declaration, method declaration, expression, etc.).
- It detects errors such as **missing semicolons**, **mismatched braces**, and **unexpected tokens**.
- The modern `javac` parser is generated by **JavaCC** (Java Compiler Compiler) from grammar files inside the JDK source.

**Phase 3 — Semantic Analysis (1 mark)**

- Walks the AST and performs **type checking**, **scope resolution**, **definite-assignment analysis**, and **method overload resolution**.
- Detects errors like `incompatible types`, `cannot find symbol`, `variable might not have been initialized`.
- Builds the **symbol table** mapping each identifier to its declared type, scope, and modifiers.

**Phase 4 — Intermediate Representation (1 mark)**

- The decorated AST is traversed by a **TreeTranslator visitor** (Visitor Pattern) that converts high-level tree nodes into a linear intermediate representation closer to bytecode.

**Phase 5 — Code Generation (1 mark)**

- The IR is consumed by the bytecode **emitter** (`Code` class in `javac`), which produces **JVM bytecode instructions** — each instruction is 1-byte opcode + optional operands.
- Output is written as a `.class` file starting with the magic number **`0xCAFEBABE`**.

**Phase 6 — Optimization (1 mark)**

- Limited compile-time optimizations are applied: **constant folding** (`3+5` → `8`), **compile-time inlining** of `static final` primitives, and removal of unreachable code.

**Diagram (0 marks — verbal credit acceptable but recommend):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[".java source"] --> B["Lexical Analyzer"] --> C["Parser"] --> D["Semantic Analyzer"] --> E["IR Generator"] --> F["Bytecode Emitter"] --> G[".class bytecode"]
```

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

**Java Program (2 marks):**

```java
public class AddNumbers {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int a = 5;
        int b = 7;
        int sum = a + b;
        System.out.println("Sum = " + sum);
    }
}
```

**Compilation and Disassembly (1 mark):**

```bash
javac AddNumbers.java
javap -c AddNumbers.class
```

**Generated Bytecode for the addition logic (2 marks):**

```text
   0: iconst_5          // push 5 onto operand stack
   1: istore_1          // store into local var 1 (a)
   2: bipush 7          // push 7
   4: istore_2          // store into local var 2 (b)
   5: iload_1           // push a
   6: iload_2           // push b
   7: iadd              // pop two ints, push their sum
   8: istore_3          // store into local var 3 (sum)
   ...
```

**Explanation (2 marks):**

- `iconst_5` is a one-byte opcode that pushes the integer literal `5` onto the **operand stack**.
- `istore_1` pops the top of stack and stores it in local variable slot `1` (named `a`).
- `bipush 7` pushes the byte `7` (extended to int).
- `iload_1` and `iload_2` reload `a` and `b` onto the operand stack.
- `iadd` is the JVM integer-addition opcode — it pops two ints and pushes their sum.
- `istore_3` stores the result into local variable `sum`.
- The expression `"Sum = " + sum` would compile to a `StringBuilder` chain of `append()` calls followed by `println` — this is **compile-time code-gen**, not a runtime library feature.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** What is bytecode? Explain the structure of a Java `.class` file. List any five important JVM bytecode instructions with their purpose. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between `javac` and `java` commands. Explain the role of JIT compiler and how it differs from `javac`. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks**

**Bytecode Definition (1 mark):**

Bytecode is the **platform-neutral intermediate instruction set** generated by `javac`. It is the **machine language of the JVM**, not of any physical processor. Each instruction is **1 byte (opcode) + optional operands**. The JVM interprets bytecode line-by-line and JIT-compiles "hot" methods to native code for performance.

**Structure of `.class` File (3 marks):**

The `.class` file follows a strict binary layout defined in the JVM Specification:

| Section | Size | Purpose |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Magic Number** | 4 bytes | `0xCAFEBABE` — file identifier |
| **Minor Version** | 2 bytes | Compiler's minor version |
| **Major Version** | 2 bytes | Compiler's major version (e.g., 61 = Java 17) |
| **Constant Pool** | Variable | Literals, type names, method/field refs |
| **Access Flags** | 2 bytes | Class-level modifiers (`public`, `final`, etc.) |
| **This Class** | 2 bytes | Index into constant pool |
| **Super Class** | 2 bytes | Index of parent class (`java/lang/Object` if none) |
| **Interfaces** | Variable | Implemented interfaces |
| **Fields** | Variable | Class-level variables |
| **Methods** | Variable | Method signatures + bytecode |
| **Attributes** | Variable | Debug info, source file name, line numbers |

The file can be inspected with `javap -v ClassName.class`.

**Five Important JVM Bytecode Instructions (3 marks):**

| Opcode | Operand | Purpose |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `iconst_0` … `iconst_5` | None | Push integer constant 0–5 onto operand stack |
| `bipush` | 1 byte | Push a byte (sign-extended to int) onto stack |
| `iload_n` | None | Load local variable `n` (int) onto operand stack |
| `istore_n` | None | Pop int from stack into local variable `n` |
| `iadd` | None | Pop two ints, push their sum |
| `getstatic` | 2 bytes (CP index) | Read a static field value |
| `invokevirtual` | 2 bytes (CP index) | Invoke an instance method |
| `return` | None | Return `void` from current method |

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks**

**`javac` vs `java` (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `javac` | `java` |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Full name** | Java Compiler | Java Application Launcher |
| **Function** | Compiles `.java` → `.class` | Launches JVM to execute `.class` |
| **Output** | Bytecode file | Program execution / output |
| **Part of** | JDK | JRE / JDK |
| **Input format** | `javac Hello.java` | `java Hello` (no extension) |
| **When it runs** | Before execution | At execution time |

**Role of JIT Compiler (2.5 marks):**

The **Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler** is a component of the **JVM** (not `javac`) that improves runtime performance by **dynamically compiling frequently executed ("hot") bytecode methods into native machine code** at runtime. Once compiled, the native code is cached and reused, eliminating the overhead of repeated interpretation.

Two famous JIT implementations:
- **HotSpot C2** — the default server-grade optimizing JIT in OpenJDK.
- **Graal JIT** — a modern JIT written in Java itself, used in GraalVM.

**Difference from `javac` (1.5 marks):**

- `javac` is **Ahead-of-Time (AOT)** at the source level — it runs **once**, before execution, and produces **platform-independent** bytecode.
- JIT is **Just-In-Time** — it runs **during execution**, on the **specific host platform**, producing **platform-specific** native code.
- `javac` performs minimal optimization; JIT performs aggressive optimization (inlining, escape analysis, loop unrolling) because it has runtime profiling data available.
- `javac` cannot know the target CPU; JIT can exploit features of the actual CPU (AVX, SSE instructions, branch prediction).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Java Compiler — Key Points to Remember (High-Density Summary)**

- [x] The Java compiler is invoked by the command **`javac`** (Java Compiler), shipped with the **JDK**.
- [x] `javac` translates **`.java` source code** → **`.class` bytecode** (NOT native machine code).
- [x] Bytecode is **platform-neutral** and runs on any OS that has a compatible JVM — this enables **WORA**.
- [x] The `.class` file begins with the magic number **`0xCAFEBABE`**.
- [x] Six logical phases: **Lexical → Syntax → Semantic → IR Generation → Code Generation → Optimization**.
- [x] **Lexical analyzer** produces tokens; **Parser** produces AST; **Semantic analyzer** produces a decorated AST with type info.
- [x] **String concatenation** (`+` on String + anything) is rewritten at **compile time** into `StringBuilder.append()` calls.
- [x] `javac` performs **limited optimizations** (constant folding, inlining of `static final`); heavy optimization is the JIT's job.
- [x] The **`java` command** launches the JVM to execute bytecode; **never add `.class` extension** to the `java` command.
- [x] **JIT (Just-In-Time)** compiler lives inside the JVM and converts **hot methods** of bytecode into **native machine code** at runtime.
- [x] `javac` = Ahead-of-Time, platform-independent; JIT = Just-In-Time, platform-specific.
- [x] Compile-time errors are caught by `javac`; runtime errors are caught by the JVM; logical errors are caught by no one.
- [x] Useful diagnostic flags: `-d`, `-classpath`/`-cp`, `-source`, `-target`, `-Xlint:all`, `-deprecation`, `-verbose`.
- [x] Use `javap -c -v ClassName.class` to **disassemble** bytecode for debugging and exam demonstrations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bytecode
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Bytecode # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Bytecode** in Java is the **platform-neutral, intermediate machine code** generated by the Java compiler (`javac`) after compiling a `.java` source file. It is a **low-level, instruction set** designed for the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** rather than for any physical hardware processor. Bytecode is stored in `.class` files and is the executable form of Java programs inside the JVM runtime environment. Each bytecode instruction is precisely **1 byte (8 bits) in length** for the opcode, hence the name **"Byte-code"**. Instructions may optionally be followed by **0 or more operand bytes** (operands/arguments). The complete bytecode instruction stream is **compact, stack-based, and type-safe**. | Property | Value / Description | |---|---| | Instruction Size | **1 byte (opcode) + optional operands** | | Storage File | `.class` file (binary format) | | Execution Engine | Java Virtual Machine (JVM) | | Platform Dependency | **Platform Independent** | | Generation Tool | `javac` (Java Compiler) | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Universal Translator" 🌍 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Think of Bytecode as a "Universal Travel Phrase-Book."** Imagine you are a speaker of English (your **Java Source Code**), but you need to travel to 10 different countries, each speaking a different native language (Windows, Linux, macOS, Android, etc.). - If you learned all 10 native languages, you would need 10 translators — exhausting and error-prone. - Instead, you write your message once in a **Universal Intermediate Language** (the **Bytecode**). - Each country provides its own **local interpreter** (the **JVM** for that OS) who translates the universal language into their native tongue at runtime. > **Result:** "Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA) — you write the source once, and the bytecode runs on any device that has a JVM. ``` Java Source (.java) --[javac compiler]--> Bytecode (.class) --[JVM]--> Native Machine Code (runs on hardware) HIGH-LEVEL TRANSLATION INTERMEDIATE INTERPRETATION / JIT HUMAN-READABLE PLATFORM-NEUTRAL HARDWARE-SPECIFIC ``` --- ## 1.3 Why Bytecode Matters in Java Engineering > [!NOTE] > **Java's three-step execution pipeline is unique among major languages:** > 1. **Compile-time:** `javac` translates `.java` → `.class` (bytecode) > 2. **Verification-time:** Bytecode **verifier** checks safety, type-correctness, stack overflows > 3. **Runtime:** JVM **interprets** or **JIT-compiles** bytecode → native machine code This design delivers the **security**, **portability**, and **dynamic class loading** features Java is famous for. --- ## 1.4 Properties of Bytecode — The "Big Six" > [!IMPORTANT] > **Six foundational properties every KTU student must memorize:** > 1. **Platform Independent** — Same `.class` file runs on Windows, Linux, macOS, embedded systems > 2. **Compact** — Average instruction ≈ 1–2 bytes, much smaller than native machine code > 3. **Stack-Based** — Operates on a **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) operand stack**, not registers > 4. **Type-Safe** — Each instruction carries type information (verified by class verifier) > 5. **Verified** — Bytecode verifier validates legality BEFORE execution > 6. **Dynamically Loadable** — Classes are fetched from network/disk only when needed # 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Journey: From Source to Execution > [!NOTE] > The complete Java execution pipeline involves **four major stages**. Understanding each is critical for both theory questions and lab viva. ### **Stage 1 — Source Code Creation** - Programmer writes `MyProgram.java` using any text editor / IDE. - This is high-level, human-readable Java code containing classes, methods, variables. ### **Stage 2 — Compilation (javac)** - The **Java compiler** (`javac`) parses, semantic-analyzes, and compiles the source. - Output: **`MyProgram.class`** file containing **bytecode**. - The `.class` file has a strict, documented binary structure defined in the **Java Virtual Machine Specification (JVMS)**. ### **Stage 3 — Class Loading** - The **ClassLoader Subsystem** (part of JVM) loads `.class` files into JVM memory. - Three default classloaders in hierarchical order: * **Bootstrap ClassLoader** — loads core Java APIs (`rt.jar`) * **Platform ClassLoader** — loads extension classes * **Application ClassLoader** — loads classes from the application's classpath ### **Stage 4 — Execution (Interpretation + JIT)** - The **Execution Engine** processes the bytecode. - **Interpreter** reads bytecode instructions one by one and executes them (slower start). - **JIT (Just-In-Time) Compiler** detects "hot" (frequently executed) bytecode blocks and compiles them to **native machine code** for that specific hardware (much faster). - This dual approach is called **HotSpot Execution** (since Java 1.2+). --- ## 2.2 Internal Structure of a `.class` File > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Topic:** The `.class` file is **NOT just bytecode**. It is a structured binary file with a fixed format. Every KTU question on this topic may ask you to list its components. | Component | Purpose | |---|---| | **Magic Number** (`0xCAFEBABE`) | Identifies the file as a valid Java class file (4 bytes) | | **Minor Version** | Compiler minor version (2 bytes) | | **Major Version** | Compiler major version, e.g., Java 17 = major 61 (2 bytes) | | **Constant Pool** | Symbolic references — string literals, class/method/field names | | **Access Flags** | Modifiers: `public`, `final`, `abstract`, `interface` | | **This Class / Super Class** | Class hierarchy references | | **Interfaces** | List of implemented interfaces | | **Fields** | Class-level variables with type descriptors | | **Methods** | Each method's name, descriptor, and **code attribute** (containing bytecode) | | **Attributes** | Source file name, line numbers, local variable table, etc. | > The actual **bytecode instructions** live inside the **Code Attribute** of each method. --- ## 2.3 The Stack-Based Execution Model > [!NOTE] > **Most frequently asked KTU question:** *"Why is Java's bytecode stack-based and not register-based?"* Java bytecode operates on a **per-method operand stack**. Each method invocation creates: - A **Frame** containing: * `locals[]` array — local variables * `operand_stack` — LIFO work stack * `method` reference and `pc` (program counter) ### **Why Stack-Based?** | Advantage | Explanation | |---|---| | **Compactness** | No register-allocation problem; instructions are smaller | | **Portability** | Stack depth is easy to verify; register allocation is hardware-specific | | **Verification** | Stack type-checking is deterministic and provable | | **Simplicity** | JVM designers can focus on correctness, not register optimization | --- ## 2.4 The Bytecode Verifier — Security Backbone > [!WARNING] > **CRITICAL FOR EXAMS:** The verifier's role is the single most-tested sub-topic. Never confuse it with a "compiler." The **Bytecode Verifier** is a component of the JVM that performs **static analysis** on bytecode **before execution** to ensure it does not violate Java's safety rules. **Four Passes of Verification:** 1. **Pass 1 — Structural Validation:** Confirms file format, magic number, version, constant pool integrity. 2. **Pass 2 — Semantic / Type Checks:** Verifies that instructions are well-typed (no `istore` of a `long`, no popping from an empty stack). 3. **Pass 3 — Data-Flow Analysis:** Checks that locals are initialized before use, types on stack match expected types, no stack overflow/underflow. 4. **Pass 4 — Access Control (in Java 7+ via StackMapTable):** Faster verification using precomputed type states. > **Without verification**, malicious code could forge pointers, access private memory, or crash the JVM. Verification is why Java applets (historically) were considered "sandboxed." --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Quick Reference Table > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — it covers 80% of Part A questions.** | # | Bytecode Concept | KTU-Ready Definition | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Opcode Size** | **1 byte (8 bits)** per instruction | | 2 | **Max Opcodes** | **256 distinct opcodes** (0x00 to 0xFF) | | 3 | **Stack Type** | **LIFO Operand Stack** (no registers) | | 4 | **Storage Format** | **`.class` file** with `CAFEBABE` magic | | 5 | **Generator** | `javac` (Java Compiler) | | 6 | **Executor** | **JVM** (Interpreter + JIT Compiler) | | 7 | **Verifier** | Checks type-safety and structural integrity | | 8 | **Platform Nature** | **Platform Independent** (WORA) | | 9 | **Average Size** | **1–2 bytes/instruction** (compact) | | 10 | **Major Versions** | Java 8 = 52, Java 11 = 55, Java 17 = 61, Java 21 = 65 | --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Android Runtime (ART/Dalvik):** Android uses its **own bytecode format** (DEX — Dalvik Executable), but the JVM bytecode model inspired it. Modern Android converts `.class` files to `.dex` via `d8`/`dx`. - **Enterprise Servers:** Banks and enterprises run Java bytecode on heterogeneous hardware (Intel servers, IBM Z mainframes, ARM cloud servers) using the **same `.class` files**. - **Cloud Microservices:** `jlink` and `jpackage` (Java 9+) bundle bytecode with custom JREs for cloud-native deployment. # 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analysis & Worked Example ## 3.1 Worked Example: Tracing a Java Method to Bytecode > [!NOTE] > **This is a typical 14-mark question in KTU exams.** We will trace the simplest possible Java program all the way to its bytecode representation. ### **Step 1 — Java Source Code** ```java public class Hello { public static void main(String[] args) { int a = 5; int b = 3; int sum = a + b; System.out.println("Sum = " + sum); } } ``` --- ### **Step 2 — Compilation Command** ```bash javac Hello.java ``` > This produces **`Hello.class`** containing the bytecode. --- ### **Step 3 — Disassemble the Bytecode** Run `javap -c -verbose Hello.class` to view bytecode. The main method's bytecode section will resemble the following. Let's interpret every line: ``` 0: iconst_5 // Push integer constant 5 onto the operand stack 1: istore_1 // Pop the top of stack and store into local variable 1 (a) 2: iconst_3 // Push integer constant 3 onto the stack 3: istore_2 // Pop and store into local variable 2 (b) 4: iload_1 // Load local variable 1 (a) onto the stack 5: iload_2 // Load local variable 2 (b) onto the stack 6: iadd // Pop two ints, add them, push result 7: istore_3 // Pop result and store into local variable 3 (sum) 8: getstatic // Push System.out onto stack 11: new // Create new StringBuilder object 14: dup // Duplicate top of stack (needed for invoking constructor) 15: invokespecial // Invoke StringBuilder.() 18: ldc // Push String constant "Sum = " 20: invokevirtual // Call StringBuilder.append(String) 23: iload_3 // Load local variable 3 (sum) onto stack 24: invokevirtual // Call StringBuilder.append(int) 27: invokevirtual // Call System.out.println(String) 30: return // Return void from main() ``` --- ### **Step 4 — Visualize the Operand Stack** Let's trace the stack state at each instruction for the addition logic: $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Initial State: stack} = [\ ], \ \text{locals} = [args,\ a=?, \ b=?, \ sum=?] \\ &\text{After } \texttt{iconst\_5}: && \text{stack} = [5] \\ &\text{After } \texttt{istore\_1}: && \text{stack} = [\ ], \ \text{locals} = [args,\ a=5,\ b=?,\ sum=?] \\ &\text{After } \texttt{iconst\_3}: && \text{stack} = [3] \\ &\text{After } \texttt{istore\_2}: && \text{stack} = [\ ], \ \text{locals} = [args,\ a=5,\ b=3,\ sum=?] \\ &\text{After } \texttt{iload\_1}: && \text{stack} = [5] \\ &\text{After } \texttt{iload\_2}: && \text{stack} = [5,\ 3] \quad (\text{5 at bottom, 3 at top}) \\ &\text{After } \texttt{iadd}: && \text{stack} = [8] \quad (\text{pops 3 and 5, pushes } 5+3=8) \\ &\text{After } \texttt{istore\_3}: && \text{stack} = [\ ], \ \text{locals} = [args,\ a=5,\ b=3,\ sum=8] \end{aligned} $$ > **Key Observation:** Notice how bytecode is **purely sequential** — the JVM has no registers, only the operand stack and the local variable array. This is fundamentally different from x86 assembly (register-based). --- ### **Step 5 — Calculate the Code Size** Total bytes used by the main method (approximate, summing opcode + operand bytes): $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Instruction Bytes} &= \underbrace{1}_{iconst\_5} + \underbrace{1}_{istore\_1} + \underbrace{1}_{iconst\_3} + \underbrace{1}_{istore\_2} + \underbrace{1}_{iload\_1} + \underbrace{1}_{iload\_2} + \underbrace{1}_{iadd} + \underbrace{1}_{istore\_3} \\ &\quad + \underbrace{3}_{getstatic} + \underbrace{3}_{new} + \underbrace{1}_{dup} + \underbrace{3}_{invokespecial} + \underbrace{2}_{ldc} + \underbrace{3}_{invokevirtual} + \underbrace{1}_{iload\_3} + \underbrace{3}_{invokevirtual} + \underbrace{3}_{invokevirtual} + \underbrace{1}_{return} \\ &= 10 + 18 = \mathbf{28\ bytes} \end{aligned} $$ > The same logic in **x86-64 native code** typically occupies 20–40 bytes — bytecode is competitive in size. --- ### **Step 6 — Mathematical Bound: Opcode Space** The maximum possible number of unique bytecode instructions: $$ N_{\text{opcodes}} = 2^{8} = 256 \ \text{distinct opcodes} $$ However, the JVM Specification defines only **~200 instructions** as of Java 21. The remaining opcode values (201–253) are reserved/unused — a deliberate design choice for future extensions. $$ \text{Defined Opcodes (Java 21)} \approx 200, \quad \text{Reserved} \approx 56 $$ # 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Java Execution Pipeline — End-to-End Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Java Source Code
Hello.java] --> B[Java Compiler
javac] B --> C[Bytecode
Hello.class
CAFEBABE format] C --> D[Class Loader
Subsystem] D --> E[Bytecode Verifier
Safety Checks] E --> F[Method Area
+ Heap Allocation] F --> G{Execution Engine} G --> H[Interpreter
Line by line] G --> I[JIT Compiler
Hotspot Detection] H --> J[Native Hardware
CPU Instructions] I --> J J --> K[Output
on Screen / File] ``` --- ## 4.2 Bytecode Verification Pipeline (4 Passes) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Hello.class file arrives] --> B[Pass 1: Structural
Magic Number Check] B --> C[Pass 2: Semantic
Instruction Type Validity] C --> D[Pass 3: Data Flow
Stack & Local Analysis] D --> E[Pass 4: StackMapTable
Access Control Validation] E --> F{All Passes OK?} F -- Yes --> G[Bytecode Approved
for Execution] F -- No --> H[VerifyError Thrown
Execution Blocked] ``` --- ## 4.3 Class File Binary Format — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph CF[".class FILE BINARY STRUCTURE"] direction TB M[Magic Number
0xCAFEBABE - 4 bytes] MV[Minor + Major Version
4 bytes total] CP[Constant Pool
All literals and references] AF[Access Flags
public, final, etc.] TC[This Class Index] SC[Super Class Index] IF[Interfaces Count + List] FI[Fields Count + Field Info] ME[Methods Count + Method Info] AS[Attributes Count + Attribute Info] end M --> MV --> CP --> AF --> TC --> SC --> IF --> FI --> ME --> AS ME -.contains.-> CO[Code Attribute
holds the actual bytecode] ``` --- ## 4.4 Stack vs. Register-Based Execution Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph STK[Stack Based - Java Bytecode] S1[Push 5] --> S2[Push 3] --> S3[ADD
pop 2 push 1] --> S4[Store to sum] end subgraph REG[Register Based - x86 Assembly] R1[mov eax, 5] --> R2[add eax, 3] --> R3[mov sum, eax] end ``` --- ## 4.5 JVM Class Loader Hierarchy ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Application ClassLoader
Loads user classpath] --> B[Platform ClassLoader
Loads extension classes] B --> C[Bootstrap ClassLoader
Loads core Java API rt.jar] C --> D[Native C Code
null parent] A --> E[Custom ClassLoaders
e.g., WebApp, Plugin] E --> A ``` # 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define Java Bytecode. List any four of its properties.** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > **Definition (1 mark):** Java Bytecode is the platform-independent, intermediate instruction set generated by the Java compiler (`javac`) from a `.java` source file. It is stored in `.class` files and executed by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM). > > **Properties (½ mark each, any four):** > 1. **Platform independent** — same `.class` file runs on any OS with a JVM. > 2. **Compact** — each instruction is approximately **1 byte (opcode)** in size. > 3. **Stack-based** — operates on a LIFO operand stack rather than CPU registers. > 4. **Type-safe** — every instruction carries type information enforced by the verifier. > 5. **Verified** — the bytecode verifier checks safety before execution. > 6. **Dynamically loadable** — classes are loaded on demand by the ClassLoader subsystem. --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **What is the role of the bytecode verifier in the JVM?** > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > The **bytecode verifier** is a JVM component that performs **static safety analysis** on bytecode **before** it is executed. Its primary roles are: > > 1. **Structural validation** — confirms the `.class` file format, magic number, and version. > 2. **Type-checking** — ensures operands on the stack and in local variables match the types expected by each instruction. > 3. **Stack/Local safety** — guarantees no stack overflow/underflow and that locals are initialized before use. > 4. **Access control** — enforces that bytecode cannot forge pointers, bypass private modifiers, or violate Java's sandbox security model. > > (1 mark for stating the definition + 1 mark for listing 2 key roles + 1 mark for the security/sandbox context.) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Instructions:** *Answer ANY ONE full question from this module. Both questions carry equal marks.* --- ### **Question B1 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` #### **(a)** Explain the complete execution pipeline of a Java program from source code to running on hardware. Discuss the role of the JIT compiler in optimizing performance. **(7 marks)** #### **(b)** Describe the internal structure of a Java `.class` file. What is the significance of the magic number `0xCAFEBABE`? **(7 marks)** > **Model Answer — Part (a) (7 marks):** > > The execution of a Java program involves the following stages: > > **Stage 1 — Source Code Creation (1 mark):** The programmer writes a `.java` file (e.g., `Hello.java`) using high-level Java constructs such as classes, methods, loops, and conditionals. > > **Stage 2 — Compilation (1 mark):** The Java compiler `javac` parses, performs semantic analysis, and compiles the source code into **bytecode** stored in a `.class` file. Bytecode is platform-independent and consists of 1-byte opcodes plus optional operands. > > **Stage 3 — Class Loading (1 mark):** The JVM's **ClassLoader Subsystem** dynamically loads `.class` files into memory. The hierarchy includes the **Bootstrap ClassLoader** (core Java APIs), **Platform ClassLoader** (extensions), and **Application ClassLoader** (user classpath). > > **Stage 4 — Bytecode Verification (1 mark):** The **Bytecode Verifier** performs four passes of safety checks (structural, semantic, data-flow, access control) to ensure the bytecode is type-safe and rule-compliant. > > **Stage 5 — Execution (2 marks):** The **Execution Engine** processes the verified bytecode using two cooperating components: > - **Interpreter:** Reads and executes bytecode instructions one at a time. This provides fast startup but slower steady-state performance. > - **JIT (Just-In-Time) Compiler:** Identifies **"hot" methods** (executed frequently, e.g., in loops) and compiles them to **native machine code** specific to the underlying hardware. The compiled native code is cached, so subsequent invocations run at full hardware speed. > > **Role of JIT in Optimization (1 mark):** The JIT compiler dramatically improves Java's runtime performance by eliminating interpretation overhead for hot code paths. It applies advanced optimizations such as **inlining**, **dead-code elimination**, **loop unrolling**, and **escape analysis**. This hybrid model combines Java's portability with performance approaching that of natively compiled languages like C++. > > --- > > **Model Answer — Part (b) (7 marks):** > > A Java `.class` file is a **strictly defined binary format** specified in the JVM Specification. It contains the following components in order: > > 1. **Magic Number (4 bytes):** The constant `0xCAFEBABE`. (1 mark) > - **Significance:** It uniquely identifies the file as a valid Java class file. If the magic number is missing or incorrect, the JVM rejects the file immediately with a `ClassFormatError`. The whimsical value reflects a Java tradition of using hexadecimal "Easter eggs" (e.g., `cafebabe`, `feedface` for older formats). > > 2. **Minor & Major Version (4 bytes):** Compiler version identifiers. (½ mark) > - e.g., Java 17 corresponds to major version 61. > > 3. **Constant Pool (variable):** A table of symbolic references — string literals, class names, method/field names, type descriptors, and numeric constants. (1 mark) > > 4. **Access Flags (2 bytes):** Modifiers like `public`, `final`, `abstract`, `interface`. (½ mark) > > 5. **This Class & Super Class (4 bytes):** Indices into the constant pool pointing to the current class and its parent class. (½ mark) > > 6. **Interfaces (variable):** Count and list of implemented interfaces. (½ mark) > > 7. **Fields (variable):** Class-level variable declarations with names, types, and modifiers. (½ mark) > > 8. **Methods (variable):** Each method's name, descriptor (return type and parameters), access flags, and the **Code Attribute** — which is where the actual **bytecode instructions** are stored. (1 mark) > > 9. **Attributes (variable):** Auxiliary information such as `SourceFile`, `LineNumberTable`, `LocalVariableTable`, and `StackMapTable`. (½ mark) > > **Conclusion (1 mark):** The `.class` file is therefore not merely a "code dump" but a richly structured metadata container. Its precise binary layout enables the JVM to perform loading, verification, and execution efficiently, and supports reflection, debugging, and runtime instrumentation. --- ### **Question B2 (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` #### **(a)** What is bytecode? Explain the bytecode execution model used by the JVM with a suitable diagram. Why is Java's execution model considered stack-based rather than register-based? **(7 marks)** #### **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the four passes of bytecode verification performed by the JVM. How does this contribute to Java's security model? **(7 marks)** > **Model Answer — Part (a) (7 marks):** > > **Definition (2 marks):** Bytecode is the intermediate, platform-neutral instruction set generated by the Java compiler (`javac`) and stored in `.class` files. Each instruction is **1 byte** long (hence "byte-code") and is designed to be executed by the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** rather than by physical hardware. > > **Bytecode Execution Model (3 marks):** > When a method is invoked, the JVM creates a **stack frame** containing three components: > 1. **Local Variables Array (`locals[]`):** Holds method parameters and local variables, indexed from 0. > 2. **Operand Stack:** A LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) stack used to hold intermediate results, arguments for method calls, and return values. > 3. **Frame Data:** Reference to the method's runtime constant pool, and the program counter (PC). > > Example: For `int c = a + b;` the JVM executes: > - `iload_0` → push local variable `a` onto operand stack > - `iload_1` → push local variable `b` onto operand stack > - `iadd` → pop two integers, push their sum > - `istore_2`→ pop the sum and store into local variable `c` > > **Diagram (already shown in Section 4.4 above — the student may reproduce a similar block diagram).** > > **Why Stack-Based and Not Register-Based? (2 marks):** > 1. **Portability & Verification:** Stack operations are easier to verify formally because the operand types are explicitly known at each point. Register allocation is hardware-specific and far harder to prove safe. > 2. **Compactness:** Stack instructions are typically smaller — they do not need to encode register operands. > 3. **Simplicity:** The JVM can be implemented on diverse hardware without complex register-allocation algorithms, making the **"Write Once, Run Anywhere"** promise achievable. > 4. **Historical context:** Stack-based designs were favored in 1990s virtual machines (Pascal's p-machine, Sun's prior `Self` VM) for these exact portability benefits. > > --- > > **Model Answer — Part (b) (7 marks):** > > The **Bytecode Verifier** is a critical JVM component that performs four sequential passes of validation before any bytecode instruction is allowed to execute. This is the cornerstone of Java's **sandboxed security model**. > > **Pass 1 — Structural/Format Verification (1 mark):** > Validates the binary `.class` file structure: the magic number `0xCAFEBABE`, version numbers, constant pool integrity, and the length of each section. Any malformed file is rejected with `VerifyError`. > > **Pass 2 — Semantic/Instruction Verification (2 marks):** > Checks that each opcode is well-formed and that its operands match expected types. For example, the verifier rejects an attempt to treat a `long` value as an object reference, or to invoke a method that does not exist. > > **Pass 3 — Data-Flow Analysis (2 marks):** > Tracks the type state of every slot in the local variable array and every slot on the operand stack across the entire method. Ensures: > - Local variables are definitely assigned before use (no uninitialized reads). > - The stack never underflows (popping from an empty stack) or overflows. > - Types match across branch targets (e.g., after an `if-else`, both paths must leave the stack in identical type states). > > **Pass 4 — StackMapTable Verification (1 mark):** > Introduced in Java 7 (Class File version 51), this pass uses precomputed type-state snapshots (the `StackMapTable` attribute) to make Pass 3 dramatically faster. The verifier cross-checks the actual bytecode control flow against the declared type states. > > **Contribution to Java's Security Model (1 mark):** > Without bytecode verification, a malicious program could: > - Forge pointers to private memory regions. > - Bypass access modifiers (`private`, `protected`). > - Cause stack overflows to corrupt memory. > > Verification ensures these attacks are **impossible at the bytecode level**, which is why Java historically supported running untrusted code (applets) in a secure sandbox. Modern modular Java continues to use verification as a baseline safety guarantee. > > **Diagram Reference:** See *Section 4.2 — Bytecode Verification Pipeline* above. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning ⚠️ > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (KTU Examiner Patterns):** > > 1. **Calling `javac` an "interpreter."** ❌ — `javac` is a **compiler**; the JVM contains the interpreter. Many students lose 1–2 marks by confusing these. > 2. **Saying "bytecode is machine code."** ❌ — Bytecode is **NOT** machine code. It is intermediate code for the JVM. Machine code runs on physical CPUs. > 3. **Confusing the verifier with the compiler.** ❌ — The verifier checks **safety**, not correctness. It does not catch logical bugs in your program; it catches *security violations*. > 4. **Omitting the JIT compiler.** ❌ — If a question asks about the execution engine, you must mention both the interpreter and the JIT compiler. Saying "the JVM interprets bytecode" is incomplete and loses a mark. > 5. **Forgetting the magic number.** ❌ — The `0xCAFEBABE` constant is a **favorite KTU question**. Memorize it. > 6. **Writing vague definitions of WORA.** ❌ — Always anchor "Write Once, Run Anywhere" to the bytecode → JVM translation process. The bytecode is the "written once" artifact, and the per-platform JVM is the "run anywhere" mechanism. > 7. **Not drawing a diagram.** ❌ — For 7-mark questions, KTU examiners *expect* a labeled diagram. A 14-mark answer without a diagram is automatically penalized 1–2 marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist ✅ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print this and revise 5 minutes before the exam.** > > ✅ Bytecode = **intermediate, 1-byte opcodes**, generated by **`javac`**, executed by the **JVM**. > ✅ Stored in **`.class` files** with magic number **`0xCAFEBABE`**. > ✅ Java execution pipeline: **Source → javac → Bytecode → ClassLoader → Verifier → Interpreter/JIT → Native Code**. > ✅ JVM has **3 default classloaders**: Bootstrap → Platform → Application (parent-delegation model). > ✅ Execution engine = **Interpreter (fast startup) + JIT Compiler (fast steady-state)**. > ✅ JIT triggers on **"hot" methods** (frequently executed), applies optimizations like inlining and dead-code elimination. > ✅ Bytecode is **stack-based** (LIFO operand stack), **not register-based** — this aids portability and verification. > ✅ The **Bytecode Verifier** performs **4 passes**: Structural → Semantic → Data-Flow → StackMapTable. > ✅ Verification makes Java **sandbox-secure**: prevents forged pointers, type confusion, and stack corruption. > ✅ Major version numbers: **Java 8 = 52, Java 11 = 55, Java 17 = 61, Java 21 = 65**. > ✅ Maximum possible opcodes: **256** (2⁸), but only ~200 are currently defined; rest are reserved. > ✅ WORA = "Write Once, Run Anywhere" — anchored to the **bytecode + per-platform JVM** architecture. > ✅ Common tools: **`javap -c`** disassembles bytecode; **`javap -verbose`** shows full metadata. > ✅ Java 9+ supports **multi-release `.class` files** (MRJAR) for backward-compatible bytecode versioning.

topics.title: Java Virtual Machine (JVM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Java Virtual Machine (JVM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** is the cornerstone of the Java platform, formally defined as an abstract computing machine (a runtime engine) that enables a computer to run Java programs (and programs written in other languages that compile to Java bytecode). It is the component responsible for loading code, verifying code, executing code, and providing the runtime environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The JVM is a **specification** that provides a runtime environment in which Java bytecode can be executed. It is a virtual machine (not a physical one) that abstracts the underlying hardware and operating system, achieving the famous Java promise: **"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)**.

The JVM performs three primary functions:
1. **Loads** the compiled `.class` files containing bytecode.
2. **Verifies** the bytecode for safety and structural integrity.
3. **Executes** the bytecode via an interpreter and/or a Just-In-Time (JIT) compiler.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The Universal Translator

Imagine the JVM as a **Universal Language Translator** stationed at an international conference.

- **The Speakers (Source Code):** Programmers from around the world each speak their native language (C++, Python, Java).
- **The Common Language (Bytecode):** All speakers first translate their speech into a single common language — **English** (this is what Java compilation does; it converts `.java` source code into universal **bytecode**).
- **The Local Audience (Operating Systems):** Each country has its own native tongue (Windows speaks "Windows-speak," Linux speaks "Linux-speak," macOS speaks "mac-speak").
- **The JVM (The Translator):** The translator listens to the common English (bytecode) and instantly translates it into the local language of whichever country the conference is being held in (the host OS).

Without the JVM, every program would have to be rewritten in every local language. **WORA** is achieved because the bytecode remains unchanged; only the JVM's "translation" differs by platform.

### 1.3 Why JVM Exists — The Core 'Why'

| Challenge | Solution Provided by JVM |
|---|---|
| Native compiled code is **platform-dependent** | JVM provides a **platform-neutral** execution environment |
| Memory management is error-prone in C/C++ | JVM provides **automatic garbage collection** |
| Programs can crash the entire system | JVM provides a **sandboxed, secure** execution boundary |
| Interpretation is slow | JVM uses **JIT compilation** to native code at runtime |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** The JVM is **not platform-independent**, but it **enables platform-independent execution**. The JVM itself must be written separately for each OS (Windows JVM, Linux JVM, Mac JVM), but the program it runs (the bytecode) is identical everywhere.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology

- **Bytecode:** Compact instruction set, **1 byte (8 bits)** per opcode, stack-based.
- **Magic Number:** Every valid `.class` file starts with the hex bytes `0xCAFEBABE` (**3405691582** in decimal).
- **JVM Specification Version (current):** **Java SE 21 (JSR 396)** — the specification is versioned independently of any Java release.
- **Stack-based architecture:** Unlike x86 (register-based), JVM uses operand stack for all arithmetic.
- **32-bit / 64-bit word size:** `int` is **32 bits**, `long` is **64 bits**, per JVM spec.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Internal Architecture of the JVM — The Three Pillars

The JVM is logically partitioned into three cooperating subsystems. The KTU examiner frequently asks students to **list and explain these three subsystems**, so memorize this hierarchy carefully.

#### Pillar 1: ClassLoader Subsystem (The Librarian)
Responsible for **loading**, **linking**, and **initializing** `.class` files on demand.

The three phases of the ClassLoader are:

- **Loading:** Reads the `.class` file from disk/network and creates a binary representation in method area memory. Uses a **delegation hierarchy**:
  1. **Bootstrap ClassLoader** (loads `rt.jar`, core Java API from `<JAVA_HOME>/jre/lib`).
  2. **Platform ClassLoader** (loads extension classes from `ext` directory, Java 9+).
  3. **Application ClassLoader** (loads classes from the classpath, i.e., user's code).
  
  > When a class is requested, the request is delegated **upward** to the parent loader first. This is the **Parent Delegation Model** — it prevents malicious code from replacing core classes like `java.lang.String`.

- **Linking:** Performs three sub-steps:
  1. **Verify** — Checks bytecode for structural correctness (no stack overflows, type safety).
  2. **Prepare** — Allocates memory for class-level static variables and assigns defaults (e.g., `int → 0`, `Object → null`).
  3. **Resolve** — Replaces symbolic references in the constant pool with direct memory references.

- **Initialization:** Executes static variable assignments and `static` blocks in textual order. This is where `static final` constants are set to their real values.

#### Pillar 2: Runtime Data Areas (The Memory Blueprint)
Five logical memory regions, divided into **per-thread** (private) and **shared** categories.

| Memory Area | Type | Purpose | Throws |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Method Area** | Shared | Stores class metadata, constant pool, static variables, method bytecode | `OutOfMemoryError` |
| **Heap Area** | Shared | Stores all objects and instance variables (GC-managed) | `OutOfMemoryError` |
| **Stack Area (JVM Stack)** | Per-Thread | One **Stack Frame** per method call; holds local variables, operand stack, frame data | `StackOverflowError` |
| **PC Register** | Per-Thread | Holds address of currently executing JVM instruction | Cannot OOM |
| **Native Method Stack** | Per-Thread | Supports native (C/C++) methods called via JNI | `StackOverflowError` |

#### Pillar 3: Execution Engine (The CPU of the JVM)
Reads bytecode from the method area and executes it. It has three components:

- **Interpreter:** Reads bytecode **line-by-line** and executes it. Simple but **slow** (each call is re-interpreted).
- **JIT (Just-In-Time) Compiler:** Detects **"hot" methods** (executed >= 10,000 times by default — known as the **C1/C2 compilation threshold**) and compiles them directly to **native machine code**. Subsequent calls run at hardware speed.
- **Garbage Collector (GC):** Automatically reclaims memory from unreachable objects in the heap. Common algorithms: **G1 (Garbage First)**, **ZGC**, **Parallel GC**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Hot Tip:** The JIT compiler is the reason Java is "fast enough" — it bridges the performance gap between interpreted languages (Python) and natively compiled languages (C++).

### 2.2 The 'How' of Platform Independence

The bytecode-to-native translation process happens in **three stages** orchestrated by the JVM:

1. Java source (`.java`) is compiled by `javac` (Java compiler) into bytecode (`.class`).
2. The `.class` file is portable — it can be copied to any machine with a compatible JVM.
3. The host machine's JVM converts the bytecode into native instructions specific to its CPU/OS.

> This is **why** Java is sometimes called a **"compiled-interpreted hybrid"** language — compile once, interpret/JIT everywhere.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Symbol / Notation | Definition | Boundary / Default Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bytecode Opcode Size | $S_{op}$ | Width of one JVM instruction | $\vert S_{op} \vert$ = **1 byte** |
| Operand Stack Word Size | $W_{os}$ | Width of values on the operand stack | $\vert W_{os} \vert$ = **32 bits** |
| Object Header Size | $H_{obj}$ | Mark word + class pointer (typical 64-bit JVM, compressed oops) | $\vert H_{obj} \vert$ ≈ **12 bytes** |
| JIT Compilation Threshold | $T_{hot}$ | Number of invocations before JIT kicks in | $T_{hot}$ = **10,000** invocations (C1), **100,000+** (C2) |
| Stack Frame Size | $F_{size}$ | Memory per active method call | $F_{size} \approx$ locals + operand stack capacity |
| Method Area Capacity | $C_{MA}$ | Configurable via `-Xmx` and `-XX:MaxMetaspaceSize` | Default Metaspace = **unbounded** (auto-grows) |
| Heap Generation Ratio | $R_{G}$ | Ratio of Young:Old generation (default) | $R_{G} \approx$ **1:2** (Young:Old) |
| Magic Number | $M_{class}$ | First 4 bytes of every valid `.class` file | $M_{class}$ = **0xCAFEBABE** |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Enterprise Servers:** JVM powers 90%+ of Fortune 500 backend systems (banking, e-commerce) due to its memory safety and GC.
- **Android Runtime (ART):** Modern Android uses a register-based VM that evolved from the **Dalvik VM**, both conceptually similar to the JVM.
- **Big Data:** Apache Spark, Hadoop, Kafka all run on the JVM, exploiting JIT optimization for streaming workloads.
- **Scientific Computing:** Libraries like **ND4J** and **Apache Commons Math** leverage JVM numerics for engineering simulations.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Execution Flow: From `HelloWorld.java` to Console Output

This derivation walks through the **complete lifecycle** of a Java program — the most common KTU long-answer topic.

**Step 0 — Source Code Input:**
Consider the canonical KTU lab program:

```java
public class HelloWorld {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Hello KTU");
    }
}
```

**Step 1 — Compilation by `javac`:**
The Java compiler (`javac`) parses, type-checks, and compiles this `.java` file into a `.class` file containing **bytecode**. The output is `HelloWorld.class`.

**Step 2 — Invocation of the JVM:**
Typing `java HelloWorld` on the command line triggers the OS to load the JVM binary (`java.exe` on Windows, `java` on Unix). The JVM is now a process running in the OS.

**Step 3 — ClassLoader Subsystem Activates:**
The JVM's **Application ClassLoader** is asked to find `HelloWorld`. It searches the classpath (`CLASSPATH` env var or `-cp` argument).

The **Parent Delegation Model** is invoked:

$$
\text{Request: HelloWorld} \;\rightarrow\; \text{AppLoader} \;\rightarrow\; \text{PlatformLoader} \;\rightarrow\; \text{BootstrapLoader}
$$

Since the Bootstrap loader handles only `rt.jar` (core API), it cannot find `HelloWorld`. The request **falls back** to the Platform loader, then to the Application loader, which finally finds and reads `HelloWorld.class` from disk.

**Step 4 — Verification of Bytecode:**
The **Bytecode Verifier** in the ClassLoader checks the file:

$$
V_{check} = \{\text{MagicNum} = 0\text{xCAFEBABE},\; \text{StackDepth OK},\; \text{TypeSafe}\}
$$

If any check fails, a `VerifyError` is thrown and execution halts.

**Step 5 — Preparation & Resolution:**
Memory is allocated in the **Method Area** for class metadata, the constant pool, and `static` fields. Symbolic references (e.g., `java/lang/System`) are resolved to direct memory addresses.

**Step 6 — Initialization:**
Static variables receive their declared values, and `static` blocks execute (in source order).

**Step 7 — Main Thread Stack Frame Creation:**
The JVM creates the **main thread** and pushes a new **Stack Frame** onto the **JVM Stack** for the `main` method. The frame contains:

- **Local Variable Array:** Slot 0 = `args` reference.
- **Operand Stack:** Empty initially, used for intermediate computations.
- **Frame Data:** Reference to constant pool, exception table.

**Step 8 — Execution Engine Activates:**
The **PC Register** points to the first bytecode instruction of `main`. The **Interpreter** reads it, decodes it, and executes it.

For the call `System.out.println("Hello KTU")`, the bytecode is roughly:

$$
\text{getstatic} \;\rightarrow\; \text{ldc "Hello KTU"} \;\rightarrow\; \text{invokevirtual println} \;\rightarrow\; \text{return}
$$

**Step 9 — JIT Hotspot Detection (Over Time):**
If `main` were a long-running loop, after $T_{hot}$ invocations the **JIT compiler** would profile it and emit optimized native code, replacing the interpreted execution with machine code.

**Step 10 — Method Return and Stack Pop:**
After `main` returns, its stack frame is popped. The main thread terminates, and the JVM shuts down.

### 3.2 Memory Address Derivation for an Object Header

For a 64-bit JVM with **Compressed OOPs** (ordinary object pointers) enabled, the object header consists of:

$$
H_{obj} = M_{mark} + K_{class}
$$

where:

$$
\vert M_{mark} \vert = 8 \text{ bytes (mark word: hash, GC age, lock state, biased thread ID)}
$$

$$
\vert K_{class} \vert = 4 \text{ bytes (compressed class pointer)}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
H_{obj} &= M_{mark} + K_{class} \\
H_{obj} &= 8 \text{ bytes} + 4 \text{ bytes} \\
H_{obj} &= 12 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation of Logic:** The mark word holds runtime metadata required for synchronization and GC. The class pointer is compressed (4 bytes instead of 8) because Java object addresses are heap-relative and fit in 32 bits when heap < 32 GB.

### 3.3 Just-In-Time Compilation Performance Gain (Analytical)

Define the average execution time per bytecode instruction:

$$
T_{avg} = p_{int} \cdot T_{int} + p_{jit} \cdot T_{jit}
$$

where:

- $p_{int}$ = proportion of execution handled by the interpreter.
- $p_{jit}$ = proportion handled by JIT-compiled native code.
- $T_{int}$ = time per interpreted instruction ≈ **100 ns**.
- $T_{jit}$ = time per JIT-compiled instruction ≈ **2 ns**.

**Step-by-Step Substitution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Without JIT: } T_{avg} &= (1.0) \cdot 100 \text{ ns} = 100 \text{ ns} \\
\text{With JIT (} p_{jit} = 0.9 \text{): } T_{avg} &= (0.1) \cdot 100 \text{ ns} + (0.9) \cdot 2 \text{ ns} \\
T_{avg} &= 10 \text{ ns} + 1.8 \text{ ns} \\
T_{avg} &= 11.8 \text{ ns}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Speedup Factor:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{jit} &= \frac{T_{avg}^{int}}{T_{avg}^{jit}} \\
S_{jit} &= \frac{100 \text{ ns}}{11.8 \text{ ns}} \\
S_{jit} &\approx 8.47 \times
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** With 90% of execution handled by JIT-compiled native code, Java achieves roughly an **8.5× speedup** over pure interpretation — explaining why JVM-based servers can match C++ performance on long-running workloads.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the JVM

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **complete JVM architecture** showing how the ClassLoader, Runtime Data Areas, and Execution Engine interact. This is the single most-asked diagram in KTU Module 1 exams.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef loader fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef memory fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef engine fill:#90EE90,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef ext fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#C71585,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#696969,color:#000

    src["Java Source Code hello.java"]:::io
    javac["Java Compiler javac"]:::ext
    bytecode["Bytecode hello.class"]:::io
    jvm["JVM Runtime Instance"]:::ext

    subgraph CL["ClassLoader Subsystem"]
        clA["Bootstrap ClassLoader rt.jar"]:::loader
        clB["Platform ClassLoader ext classes"]:::loader
        clC["Application ClassLoader classpath"]:::loader
    end

    subgraph VA["Verification and Preparation"]
        v1["Verify bytecode"]:::loader
        v2["Prepare static memory"]:::loader
        v3["Resolve symbolic refs"]:::loader
    end

    subgraph RA["Runtime Data Areas"]
        m1["Method Area class metadata"]:::memory
        m2["Heap Area all objects"]:::memory
        m3["JVM Stack per thread frames"]:::memory
        m4["PC Register"]:::memory
        m5["Native Method Stack"]:::memory
    end

    subgraph EE["Execution Engine"]
        e1["Interpreter"]:::engine
        e2["JIT Compiler native code"]:::engine
        e3["Garbage Collector heap reclaim"]:::engine
    end

    jni["JNI Native Libraries C and C plus plus"]:::ext
    os["Host Operating System"]:::io

    src --> javac
    javac --> bytecode
    bytecode --> jvm
    jvm --> CL
    CL --> VA
    VA --> RA
    RA --> EE
    EE --> jni
    jni --> os
    EE --> m2
    e1 -.hot method.- e2
```

### 4.2 Step-by-Step Reading Guide for the Diagram

1. **Top of Diagram (Source to Bytecode):** `.java` is compiled by `javac` into portable `.class` bytecode files.
2. **JVM Activation:** Running `java HelloWorld` launches a JVM instance.
3. **ClassLoader Subsystem (Yellow):** The three-tier classloader hierarchy searches for the requested class, delegating parent-first.
4. **Verification and Preparation (Yellow, lower):** The bytecode is validated, memory is allocated for static variables, and symbolic references are resolved.
5. **Runtime Data Areas (Blue):** Memory is partitioned into five regions. **Method Area** holds class metadata; **Heap** holds objects; **JVM Stack** holds per-method stack frames; **PC Register** tracks the current instruction; **Native Method Stack** supports JNI calls.
6. **Execution Engine (Green):** The Interpreter executes bytecode line-by-line. The **JIT compiler** (dotted arrow) optimizes "hot" methods to native code. The **Garbage Collector** (dotted arrow) reclaims heap memory from unreachable objects.
7. **JNI Bridge to OS (Pink/Gray):** When native methods are called, JNI bridges the JVM to underlying C/C++ libraries and the host OS.

### 4.3 Mermaid ClassLoader Delegation Sequence

This sequence diagram illustrates the **Parent Delegation Model** — frequently tested as a 7-mark question in KTU exams.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant App as Application Code
    participant ACL as Application ClassLoader
    participant PCL as Platform ClassLoader
    participant BCL as Bootstrap ClassLoader
    participant FS as File System

    App->>ACL: Request class com.example.MyClass
    ACL->>PCL: Delegate upward to parent
    PCL->>BCL: Delegate upward to parent
    BCL->>FS: Search rt.jar and boot classpath
    FS-->>BCL: Not found
    BCL-->>PCL: Cannot load, return to child
    PCL->>FS: Search ext directory
    FS-->>PCL: Not found
    PCL-->>ACL: Cannot load, return to child
    ACL->>FS: Search classpath
    FS-->>ACL: Found MyClass.class
    ACL-->>App: Return loaded Class object
```
