```mermaid
    C --> C1["Range -32768 to 32767"]
    C --> C2["Default 0"]
    C --> C3["Wrapper Short"]
    
    D --> D1["Range approx -2.15e9 to 2.15e9"]
    D --> D2["Default 0"]
    D --> D3["Wrapper Integer"]
    D --> D4["DEFAULT CHOICE for integers"]
    
    E --> E1["Range approx -9.22e18 to 9.22e18"]
    E --> E2["Default 0L"]
    E --> E3["Wrapper Long"]
    
    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D4 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#10b981,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#8b5cf6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Type Promotion Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    INPUT1["Operand A"] --> PROMOTE1{Binary Numeric Promotion Engine}
    INPUT2["Operand B"] --> PROMOTE1
    PROMOTE1 --> CHECK{Is operand smaller than int?}
    CHECK -->|Yes| TOINT["Promote to int"]
    CHECK -->|No| KEEPTYPE["Keep original type"]
    TOINT --> MATCH{Match widest operand type}
    KEEPTYPE --> MATCH
    MATCH --> RESULT["Result Type"]
    RESULT --> ASSIGN{Assignment to variable}
    ASSIGN -->|Widening| OK1["No cast needed"]
    ASSIGN -->|Narrowing| CAST["Explicit cast required"]
    
    style INPUT1 fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style INPUT2 fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style PROMOTE1 fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style CHECK fill:#a78bfa,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style MATCH fill:#a78bfa,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style RESULT fill:#10b981,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style CAST fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style OK1 fill:#10b981,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Integer Literal Processing Pipeline

```mermaid
graph TD
    SRC[Source Code Literal] --> DETECT{Detect Prefix}
    DETECT -->|Starts with 0x or 0X| HEX[Parse as Base 16]
    DETECT -->|Starts with 0b or 0B| BIN[Parse as Base 2]
    DETECT -->|Starts with 0 and more digits| OCT[Parse as Base 8]
    DETECT -->|Otherwise| DEC[Parse as Base 10]
    
    HEX --> STRIP1[Remove prefix and underscores]
    BIN --> STRIP1
    OCT --> STRIP1
    DEC --> STRIP1
    
    STRIP1 --> RANGE{Value in int range?}
    RANGE -->|Yes and no L suffix| INTTYPE[Store as int]
    RANGE -->|Yes and L suffix| LONGTYPE[Store as long]
    RANGE -->|No and no L suffix| COMPILEERR["Compile-time error: integer number too large"]
    RANGE -->|No and L suffix| LONGTYPE
    
    style SRC fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style DETECT fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style INTTYPE fill:#10b981,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style LONGTYPE fill:#10b981,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style COMPILEERR fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.4 Two's Complement Conceptual Visualization

```mermaid
graph TD
    NUM[Decimal Number] --> SIGN{Is number negative?}
    SIGN -->|No| POS[Direct binary representation]
    SIGN -->|Yes| CONV[Convert magnitude to binary]
    CONV --> INV[Invert all bits]
    INV --> ADD[Add 1 to LSB]
    ADD --> RESULT[Store as two's complement]
    POS --> STORED[Final 8-bit storage]
    RESULT --> STORED
    
    style NUM fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style SIGN fill:#a78bfa,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style POS fill:#10b981,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style CONV fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style INV fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ADD fill:#f59e0b,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style RESULT fill:#dc2626,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style STORED fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the four integer primitive data types in Java with their sizes and ranges.**

**Model Answer:**

| Type    | Size (bits) | Range                          | Default |
|---------|-------------|--------------------------------|---------|
| `byte`  | 8           | $-2^{7}$ to $2^{7}-1$          | 0       |
| `short` | 16          | $-2^{15}$ to $2^{15}-1$        | 0       |
| `int`   | 32          | $-2^{31}$ to $2^{31}-1$        | 0       |
| `long`  | 64          | $-2^{63}$ to $2^{63}-1$        | 0L      |

All four types are **signed** and use **two's complement** representation. Unlike C/C++, the size of these types is **fixed across all platforms** in Java, which ensures platform independence.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the difference between widening conversion and narrowing conversion in Java? Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

**Widening Conversion (Implicit/Auto):**
- Converting a smaller type to a larger type.
- Performed automatically by the compiler; **no data loss** occurs.
- Example: `int x = 10; long y = x;` (int is promoted to long).

**Narrowing Conversion (Explicit):**
- Converting a larger type to a smaller type.
- Requires an **explicit cast**; may result in **data loss or overflow**.
- Example: `long x = 100L; int y = (int) x;` (long is explicitly cast to int).

The order of widening is: `byte → short → int → long → float → double`.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) Explain the different ways of writing integer literals in Java with examples. Discuss the use of underscores in numeric literals. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Java supports four bases for writing integer literals:

1. **Decimal (base 10):** Digits 0–9, no prefix.
   ```java
   int dec = 123; // decimal 123
   ```

2. **Octal (base 8):** Digits 0–7, **leading zero** as prefix.
   ```java
   int oct = 0123; // octal 123 = decimal 83
   ```

3. **Hexadecimal (base 16):** Digits 0–9 and letters A–F, prefix `0x` or `0X`.
   ```java
   int hex = 0x7B; // hexadecimal 7B = decimal 123
   ```

4. **Binary (base 2):** Digits 0–1, prefix `0b` or `0B` (Java 7+).
   ```java
   int bin = 0b1111011; // binary 1111011 = decimal 123
   ```

**Suffixes:**
- No suffix → `int` type
- Suffix `L` or `l` → `long` type
   ```java
   long big = 1234567890123L;
   ```

**Underscores in Numeric Literals (Java 7+):**
Underscores can be inserted between digits in numeric literals to improve readability. They are **ignored by the compiler**.

```java
int million = 1_000_000;       // valid
long creditCard = 1234_5678_9012_3456L; // valid
int hex = 0xFF_FF_FF;          // valid
int bin = 0b1010_1010;         // valid
```

**Restrictions on underscores:**
- Cannot start or end a literal: `_123`, `123_` are invalid.
- Cannot be adjacent to the decimal point in a floating-point literal.
- Cannot be placed adjacent to the `L` suffix: `123_L` is invalid.

---

**(b) With a suitable Java program, demonstrate the concept of integer overflow and explain what happens when it occurs. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

```java
public class IntegerOverflowDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int maxInt = Integer.MAX_VALUE;  // 2^31 - 1 = 2147483647
        int minInt = Integer.MIN_VALUE;  // -2^31  = -2147483648
        
        System.out.println("Max Int  = " + maxInt);
        System.out.println("Min Int  = " + minInt);
        
        // Overflow: adding 1 to MAX_VALUE wraps to MIN_VALUE
        int overflow = maxInt + 1;
        System.out.println("MAX + 1  = " + overflow);
        
        // Underflow: subtracting 1 from MIN_VALUE wraps to MAX_VALUE
        int underflow = minInt - 1;
        System.out.println("MIN - 1  = " + underflow);
        
        // Long overflow
        long maxLong = Long.MAX_VALUE;
        System.out.println("Long MAX + 1 = " + (maxLong + 1));
        
        // Multiplication overflow
        int product = 50000 * 50000; // 2,500,000,000 > 2,147,483,647
        System.out.println("50000 * 50000 = " + product);
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Max Int  = 2147483647
Min Int  = -2147483648
MAX + 1  = -2147483648
MIN - 1  = 2147483647
Long MAX + 1 = -9223372036854775808
50000 * 50000 = -1794967296
```

**Explanation:**
Java performs integer arithmetic using **two's complement modular arithmetic**. When the result of an operation exceeds the maximum representable value, the **most significant bits are silently discarded** (wrap-around). No `OverflowException` is thrown at runtime — this is by design for performance reasons. To detect overflow, Java provides `Math.addExact()`, `Math.multiplyExact()` (Java 8+) which throw an `ArithmeticException` on overflow.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) Explain the concept of type promotion in Java expressions involving integer types. Provide a Java program to illustrate. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Type Promotion Rules:**
1. **Binary Numeric Promotion:** When two operands are involved in an arithmetic operation, both are first promoted to a common type before evaluation.
2. If either operand is `long`, the other is promoted to `long`.
3. If either operand is `float`, the other is promoted to `float`.
4. If either operand is `double`, the other is promoted to `double`.
5. **Special Rule:** `byte`, `short`, and `char` are **always promoted to `int`** before arithmetic, even in mixed operations.

```java
public class TypePromotionDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        byte a = 10;
        byte b = 20;
        
        // byte + byte is promoted to int, so result is int
        // byte c = a + b;       // COMPILE ERROR: possible lossy conversion
        int c = a + b;            // correct
        System.out.println("byte + byte = " + c);
        
        short s = 100;
        int result = a + s;       // byte promoted to int, then added
        System.out.println("byte + short = " + result);
        
        int i = 1000;
        long l = 5000L;
        long mix = i + l;         // int promoted to long
        System.out.println("int + long = " + mix);
        
        float f = 2.5f;
        double d = 3.5;
        double mix2 = f + d;      // float promoted to double
        System.out.println("float + double = " + mix2);
        
        // Compound assignment auto-casts
        byte x = 10;
        x += 5;  // equivalent to x = (byte)(x + 5) — implicit cast
        System.out.println("Compound += " + x);
    }
}
```

**Key Insight:** The compound assignment operator `+=` performs an **implicit narrowing cast**, unlike the regular `+` operator. This is why `x += 5` works for a `byte`, but `x = x + 5` does not.

---

**(b) Write a Java program to read two integers from the user and demonstrate the use of all four integer types, including valid and invalid assignments. Explain the compile-time errors. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class IntegerTypesDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        
        System.out.print("Enter a small number (for byte): ");
        byte b = sc.nextByte();
        System.out.println("byte value: " + b);
        
        System.out.print("Enter a medium number (for short): ");
        short s = sc.nextShort();
        System.out.println("short value: " + s);
        
        System.out.print("Enter a regular number (for int): ");
        int i = sc.nextInt();
        System.out.println("int value: " + i);
        
        System.out.print("Enter a large number (for long): ");
        long l = sc.nextLong();
        System.out.println("long value: " + l);
        
        // Valid widening
        int promotedInt = b + s;  // byte + short -> int
        long promotedLong = i + l; // int + long -> long
        System.out.println("Promoted int: " + promotedInt);
        System.out.println("Promoted long: " + promotedLong);
        
        // Valid explicit narrowing
        int narrowedInt = (int) l;
        byte narrowedByte = (byte) i;
        System.out.println("Narrowed int from long: " + narrowedInt);
        System.out.println("Narrowed byte from int: " + narrowedByte);
        
        // The following lines would cause COMPILE-TIME ERRORS:
        // byte err1 = 200;          // ERROR: 200 is out of byte range (-128 to 127)
        // long err2 = 100;          // OK actually, since int literal fits in long
        // int err3 = 10L;           // ERROR: cannot assign long to int without cast
        // byte err4 = b + 1;        // ERROR: b+1 is int, narrowing needs cast
        
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Compile-Time Error Explanations:**

| Code                       | Error Reason                                                              |
|----------------------------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `byte err1 = 200;`         | 200 exceeds `byte` max of 127; out of range.                             |
| `int err3 = 10L;`          | `long` literal cannot be implicitly narrowed to `int`; explicit cast required. |
| `byte err4 = b + 1;`       | `b + 1` is evaluated as `int` due to binary numeric promotion; cannot be assigned to `byte` without cast. |
| `long x = 3.14L;`          | Invalid because `3.14L` is a floating-point literal, not an integer.    |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Assuming `byte` is unsigned:** It is **signed**; range is -128 to 127, NOT 0 to 255.
> 2. **Forgetting the `L` suffix for large literals:** A literal like `10000000000` (10 billion) exceeds `int` range and causes a compile-time error. Use `10000000000L`.
> 3. **Confusing octal literals:** Writing `int x = 090;` causes a compile error because `9` is not a valid octal digit.
> 4. **Misnaming wrapper classes:** `Integer` (not `Int`), `Long` (not `Long` primitive confusion). Wrapper classes are capitalized.
> 5. **Forgetting default values:** Local variables are **not** initialized to 0; instance variables are.
> 6. **Integer division truncation:** `5 / 2` gives `2`, not `2.5` — a frequent source of bugs in KTU lab exams.
> 7. **Assuming overflow throws an exception:** It does NOT in primitive arithmetic; use `Math.addExact()` for checked arithmetic.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

✅ Java has **four signed integer primitive types**: `byte`, `short`, `int`, `long` — all using **two's complement**.

✅ **Size is fixed** in Java (unlike C/C++): 8, 16, 32, 64 bits respectively.

✅ The **range formula** is $[-2^{n-1},\ 2^{n-1}-1]$ for an $n$-bit signed integer.

✅ The **default integer type** in Java is `int`. Use the `L` suffix for `long` literals.

✅ **Literal bases:** decimal (no prefix), octal (leading `0`), hex (`0x`), binary (`0b`).

✅ **Underscores** in numeric literals (Java 7+) improve readability but cannot be at the start, end, or adjacent to suffixes.

✅ **Binary numeric promotion** automatically promotes `byte`, `short`, and `char` to `int` in expressions.

✅ **Widening** is automatic; **narrowing** requires an explicit cast and may cause data loss.

✅ **Integer overflow** silently wraps around (no exception); use `Math.addExact()` for checked arithmetic.

✅ **Wrapper classes** (`Byte`, `Short`, `Integer`, `Long`) provide object representations and support **autoboxing/unboxing**.

✅ The **Integer cache** holds values from -128 to 127 — equality checks within this range return `true` for `Integer.valueOf()` calls.

✅ **Default values**: instance/static integer variables → `0` (or `0L` for long); local variables → must be initialized manually.

✅ **Integer division** truncates toward zero: `7 / 2 == 3`, not `3.5`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Floating Point Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Floating Point Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Floating Point Types** in Java are the primitive data types used to represent real numbers (numbers containing a fractional/decimal component) that require a decimal point representation. Java provides **two** floating-point primitive types conforming to the **IEEE 754 Standard for Binary Floating-Point Arithmetic**:
> 1. `float` — Single-precision 32-bit IEEE 754 floating point.
> 2. `double` — Double-precision 64-bit IEEE 754 floating point.

In Java, floating-point types are **signed** (can represent both positive and negative values), conform to the **IEEE 754-1985** standard (also adopted as IEC 60559), and are used wherever an approximation of a real number is required — particularly in scientific, engineering, financial (with caution), and graphical computations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **scientific calculator** that stores numbers using **Scientific Notation**. For instance, the number `6,020,000,000,000,000,000,000,000` (Avogadro's number) is hard to write down, but in scientific notation, it becomes:

$$
6.022 \times 10^{23}
$$

A **floating-point type** in Java works the same way — it stores numbers as a **mantissa (significant digits)** multiplied by a **power of 2 (exponent)**. The decimal point can "float" depending on the exponent value, hence the name **floating-point**.

> [!TIP]
> Think of it like a **sliding decimal point** — just as a chameleon shifts its position, the decimal point in a floating-point number can shift left or right based on the exponent. This allows a single type to represent both **very small** and **astronomically large** numbers efficiently.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Floating-point numbers are APPROXIMATIONS, not exact values.** Just like you cannot precisely measure a coastline with a ruler, floating-point cannot precisely represent most decimal fractions (e.g., `0.1`). Always remember this when comparing floating-point values — use an **epsilon (ε) tolerance** instead of `==`.

## 1.3 KTU Syllabus Highlights

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Points KTU Examiners Love to Test:**
> - Size in bits (32 for `float`, 64 for `double`).
> - Default value (`0.0f` for `float`, `0.0d` for `double`).
> - IEEE 754 representation (sign, exponent, mantissa bits).
> - Range of values (`Float.MIN_VALUE` to `Float.MAX_VALUE`).
> - Default type for floating-point literals in Java is `double`.
> - Suffixes `f`/`F` for `float` and `d`/`D` for `double`.

## 1.4 Default Values Table

| Type | Default Value | Size (bits) | Wrapper Class |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `float` | **0.0f** | **32** | `java.lang.Float` |
| `double` | **0.0d** | **64** | `java.lang.Double` |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 IEEE 754 Representation (The Heart of Floating Point)

The **IEEE 754 Standard** defines how real numbers are stored in binary. Every floating-point number is decomposed into three components:

$$
\text{Value} = (-1)^{\text{sign}} \times 1.\text{mantissa} \times 2^{(\text{exponent} - \text{bias})}
$$

### 2.1.1 Components of IEEE 754

1. **Sign Bit (S)** — 1 bit
   - `0` represents a **positive** number.
   - `1` represents a **negative** number.

2. **Exponent (E)** — Stores the power of 2.
   - Stored in **biased form** to allow comparison of exponents as unsigned integers.
   - **Bias for `float`** = $2^{8-1} - 1 = 127$.
   - **Bias for `double`** = $2^{11-1} - 1 = 1023$.

3. **Mantissa / Significand (M)** — Stores the significant digits.
   - Implicit leading `1.` (hidden bit) for **normalized** numbers, so effectively 24 bits for `float` and 53 bits for `double`.
   - Represents the fractional part after the implicit `1.`.

## 2.2 Bit Allocation Breakdown

| Type | Total Bits | Sign Bit | Exponent Bits | Mantissa Bits | Effective Precision |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `float`  | **32**  | **1** | **8**  | **23** | **24 bits** (≈ 7 decimal digits) |
| `double` | **64**  | **1** | **11** | **52** | **53 bits** (≈ 15–17 decimal digits) |

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic: How a Float is Stored

> [!NOTE]
> **Example**: Convert `−12.375` to IEEE 754 single-precision format.

**Step 1**: Determine the **sign bit**.
- Number is negative → **S = 1**.

**Step 2**: Convert the magnitude to binary.
- Integer part: `12` = `1100` in binary.
- Fractional part: `0.375` = `0.011` in binary (since $0.375 = 0.25 + 0.125 = 2^{-2} + 2^{-3}$).
- Combined: `1100.011`.

**Step 3**: Normalize to scientific notation (binary).
- `1100.011` = `1.100011 × 2³`.

**Step 4**: Determine the **exponent** and apply bias.
- Actual exponent = `3`.
- Biased exponent = `3 + 127` = `130` = `10000010` in binary.

**Step 5**: Extract the **mantissa** (fraction after the leading `1.`).
- `1.100011` → mantissa = `.100011` → pad with zeros to 23 bits → `10001100000000000000000`.

**Step 6**: Combine all parts.

$$
\underbrace{1}_{\text{Sign}}\ \underbrace{10000010}_{\text{Exponent}}\ \underbrace{10001100000000000000000}_{\text{Mantissa}}
$$

## 2.4 Special Values in IEEE 754

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Floating-Point Values** (Frequently asked in KTU exams):

| Value | Condition | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `+0.0` | Sign=0, Exponent=0, Mantissa=0 | Positive zero |
| `-0.0` | Sign=1, Exponent=0, Mantissa=0 | Negative zero (`==` to `+0.0`) |
| `+Infinity` | Sign=0, Exponent=all 1s, Mantissa=0 | Result of `1.0/0.0` |
| `-Infinity` | Sign=1, Exponent=all 1s, Mantissa=0 | Result of `-1.0/0.0` |
| `NaN` | Exponent=all 1s, Mantissa≠0 | "Not a Number" — e.g., `0.0/0.0`, `Math.sqrt(-1)` |

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Value | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| IEEE 754 Value | $(-1)^{S} \times 1.M \times 2^{(E - \text{bias})}$ | Standard representation |
| `float` bias | $2^{7} - 1 = 127$ | Exponent offset |
| `double` bias | $2^{10} - 1 = 1023$ | Exponent offset |
| `float` MAX | $\approx 3.4028235 \times 10^{38}$ | Largest positive finite |
| `float` MIN (positive) | $\approx 1.4 \times 10^{-45}$ | Smallest positive normal |
| `double` MAX | $\approx 1.7976931348623157 \times 10^{308}$ | Largest positive finite |
| `double` MIN (positive) | $\approx 4.9 \times 10^{-324}$ | Smallest positive normal |
| ULP (Unit in Last Place) | `float`: $2^{-23}$; `double`: $2^{-52}$ | Smallest precision step |
| Epsilon comparison | $\vert a - b \vert < \varepsilon$ | Use ε ≈ $10^{-9}$ for `double` |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace**: Calculating orbital trajectories of satellites (vast ranges of magnitude).
- **Signal Processing**: Audio/Video DSP requires `float` for FFT operations.
- **Computer Graphics**: 3D rendering uses `float` for GPU vertex coordinates.
- **Scientific Simulation**: Weather models, molecular dynamics — `double` for accuracy.
- **Machine Learning**: Tensor computations rely heavily on `float32` for performance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Representing `-12.375` in IEEE 754 Single Precision

We will derive the bit-by-bit IEEE 754 representation with full mathematical justification.

### Step 1: Sign Determination

The number is **negative**, so:

$$
S = 1
$$

### Step 2: Binary Conversion of the Magnitude

**Integer part (12):** Repeated division by 2.

$$
\begin{aligned}
12 \div 2 &= 6 \quad \text{remainder } 0 \\
6 \div 2 &= 3 \quad \text{remainder } 0 \\
3 \div 2 &= 1 \quad \text{remainder } 1 \\
1 \div 2 &= 0 \quad \text{remainder } 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Reading bottom-up: `12` = `(1100)_2`.

**Fractional part (0.375):** Repeated multiplication by 2.

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.375 \times 2 &= 0.75 \quad \text{integer part } 0 \\
0.75 \times 2 &= 1.50 \quad \text{integer part } 1 \\
0.50 \times 2 &= 1.00 \quad \text{integer part } 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Reading top-down: `0.375` = `(0.011)_2`.

**Combined**: `12.375` = `(1100.011)_2`.

### Step 3: Normalization

Shift the binary point left until exactly one non-zero digit remains before the point.

$$
(1100.011)_2 = (1.100011)_2 \times 2^{3}
$$

So the **unbiased exponent** is `3`.

### Step 4: Apply Bias for Storage

Bias for `float` = `127`. Therefore:

$$
E_{\text{stored}} = 3 + 127 = 130
$$

Convert `130` to 8-bit binary:

$$
130 = 128 + 2 = 2^{7} + 2^{1} = (10000010)_2
$$

### Step 5: Extract and Pad the Mantissa

The normalized form is `1.100011`. The implicit `1.` is **hidden** (not stored). We store only the fractional part:

$$
M = .100011
$$

Pad to 23 bits total:

$$
M_{\text{stored}} = 10001100000000000000000
$$

### Step 6: Final Assembly

$$
\boxed{\underbrace{1}_{S}\ \underbrace{10000010}_{E}\ \underbrace{10001100000000000000000}_{M}}
$$

This 32-bit pattern is the IEEE 754 representation of `-12.375f`.

## 3.2 Derivation: Range of `float`

**Maximum finite value** occurs when exponent is at max (excluding special values) and mantissa is all 1s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\max} &= 254 \quad \text{(Exponent field = 11111110)} \\
\text{Actual exponent} &= 254 - 127 = 127 \\
\text{Mantissa (with hidden bit)} &= 1.111...1\ (24\ \text{ones}) \\
\text{Max value} &\approx (2 - 2^{-23}) \times 2^{127} \\
&\approx 3.4028235 \times 10^{38}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Minimum positive normal value** occurs when exponent is `1` and mantissa is all 0s.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 1 - 127 = -126 \\
\text{Value} &= 1.0 \times 2^{-126} \\
&\approx 1.175494 \times 10^{-38}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> Note that `Float.MIN_VALUE` returns the **smallest positive normal value** (≈ $1.4 \times 10^{-45}$ for subnormal range), **NOT** the most negative value. The most negative is `-Float.MAX_VALUE`. This is a common KTU exam trick question!

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Float vs Double

| Parameter | `float` | `double` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Size | **32 bits (4 bytes)** | **64 bits (8 bytes)** |
| IEEE 754 name | Single precision | Double precision |
| Sign bit | **1 bit** | **1 bit** |
| Exponent bits | **8 bits** | **11 bits** |
| Mantissa bits | **23 bits (+1 implicit)** | **52 bits (+1 implicit)** |
| Bias | **127** | **1023** |
| Decimal precision | ~**7 digits** | ~**15–17 digits** |
| Default literal type | `float` requires `f`/`F` suffix | `double` is **default** |
| Default value | `0.0f` | `0.0d` |
| Wrapper class | `java.lang.Float` | `java.lang.Double` |
| Range (positive) | ≈ $1.4 \times 10^{-45}$ to $3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | ≈ $4.9 \times 10^{-324}$ to $1.8 \times 10^{308}$ |
| Special values | `Float.POSITIVE_INFINITY`, `Float.NEGATIVE_INFINITY`, `Float.NaN` | `Double.POSITIVE_INFINITY`, etc. |

## 3.4 Java Code Demonstrations (Compilation-Ready)

### Example 1: Float and Double Declaration

```java
public class FloatingPointDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        float  f1 = 3.14159f;      // 'f' suffix REQUIRED
        float  f2 = 3.14159F;      // 'F' suffix also works
        double d1 = 3.14159;       // No suffix needed - default is double
        double d2 = 3.14159d;      // 'd' suffix optional
        
        // Scientific notation
        double avogadro = 6.022e23;       // 6.022 × 10^23
        double electronMass = 9.109e-31;  // 9.109 × 10^-31
        
        System.out.println("f1 = " + f1);
        System.out.println("d1 = " + d1);
        System.out.println("Avogadro's number = " + avogadro);
        System.out.println("Electron mass = " + electronMass);
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
f1 = 3.14159
d1 = 3.14159
Avogadro's number = 6.022E23
Electron mass = 9.109E-31
```

### Example 2: Special Values and Comparison Pitfall

```java
public class FloatingPointPitfalls {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Special values
        double inf = 1.0 / 0.0;       // → Infinity
        double negInf = -1.0 / 0.0;   // → -Infinity
        double nan = 0.0 / 0.0;       // → NaN
        
        System.out.println("Infinity : " + inf);
        System.out.println("-Infinity: " + negInf);
        System.out.println("NaN      : " + nan);
        
        // THE CLASSIC PITFALL: 0.1 + 0.2 != 0.3
        double a = 0.1, b = 0.2, c = 0.3;
        System.out.println("a + b = " + (a + b));          // 0.30000000000000004
        System.out.println("a+b == c ? " + (a + b == c));  // false !!!
        
        // CORRECT comparison using epsilon
        double epsilon = 1e-9;
        if (Math.abs((a + b) - c) < epsilon) {
            System.out.println("Equal (within epsilon)");
        }
    }
}
```

### Example 3: Wrapper Class Usage

```java
public class WrapperDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Autoboxing: primitive → wrapper
        Double dObj = 3.14159;
        Float  fObj = 2.71828f;
        
        // Unboxing: wrapper → primitive
        double d = dObj;
        float  f = fObj;
        
        // Useful constants
        System.out.println("Float  MAX_VALUE : " + Float.MAX_VALUE);
        System.out.println("Double MAX_VALUE : " + Double.MAX_VALUE);
        System.out.println("Float  MIN_VALUE : " + Float.MIN_VALUE);  // Smallest POSITIVE
        System.out.println("Double MIN_VALUE : " + Double.MIN_VALUE);
        System.out.println("Float  SIZE (bits): " + Float.SIZE);
        System.out.println("Double SIZE (bits): " + Double.SIZE);
        
        // Parsing strings
        double parsed = Double.parseDouble("123.456");
        System.out.println("Parsed: " + parsed);
    }
}
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 IEEE 754 Bit Layout Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph floatLayout["float (32 bits)"]
        sF["Sign: 1 bit"]
        eF["Exponent: 8 bits"]
        mF["Mantissa: 23 bits"]
        sF --- eF --- mF
    end

    subgraph doubleLayout["double (64 bits)"]
        sD["Sign: 1 bit"]
        eD["Exponent: 11 bits"]
        mD["Mantissa: 52 bits"]
        sD --- eD --- mD
    end

    sample["-12.375"] --> calc["Convert to Binary"]
    calc --> norm["Normalize: 1.100011 × 2^3"]
    norm --> bias["Apply Bias"]
    bias --> assemble["Assemble 32-bit Pattern"]
    assemble --> floatLayout
```

## 4.2 Floating-Point Type Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Primitive Data Types in Java] --> B[Numeric Types]
    A --> C[Boolean]
    A --> D[Character]
    B --> E[Integer Types]
    B --> F[Floating Point Types]
    E --> E1[byte - 8 bits]
    E --> E2[short - 16 bits]
    E --> E3[int - 32 bits]
    E --> E4[long - 64 bits]
    F --> F1[float - 32 bits IEEE 754]
    F --> F2[double - 64 bits IEEE 754]
    F1 --> F1a[Wrapper: java.lang.Float]
    F2 --> F2a[Wrapper: java.lang.Double]
```

## 4.3 IEEE 754 Value Categories Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Decode IEEE 754 Bits] --> checkExp{Exponent Field?}
    checkExp -->|All 0s| checkMantissaZero{Mantissa = 0?}
    checkExp -->|All 1s| checkMantissaSpecial{Mantissa = 0?}
    checkExp -->|Other values| normalized[Normalized Number]

    checkMantissaZero -->|Yes| zero[Plus or Minus Zero]
    checkMantissaZero -->|No| subnormal[Subnormal Number]

    checkMantissaSpecial -->|Yes| infinity[Plus or Minus Infinity]
    checkMantissaSpecial -->|No| nan[NaN - Not a Number]

    normalized --> formula1["Value = -1^S × 1.M × 2 power E-127"]
    subnormal --> formula2["Value = -1^S × 0.M × 2 power -126"]
```

## 4.4 Floating-Point Arithmetic Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Operand 1: float or double] --> B[Align Binary Points]
    B --> C[Add or Subtract Mantissas]
    C --> D[Normalize Result]
    D --> E[Round to Nearest]
    E --> F[Handle Overflow or Underflow]
    F --> G[Final IEEE 754 Result]
    
    H[Operand 2: float or double] --> B
```

## 4.5 Type Promotion Rules in Expressions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Java Expression Type Promotion] --> check1{Is float or double present?}
    check1 -->|Yes| promote[Promote all operands to double]
    check1 -->|No| check2{Is long present?}
    check2 -->|Yes| longPromote[Promote all to long]
    check2 -->|No| intPromote[Promote all to int]
    
    promote --> resultDouble[Result: double]
    longPromote --> resultLong[Result: long]
    intPromote --> resultInt[Result: int]
```

## 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Floating-Point Comparison Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    input[Two floating values a and b] --> decision{Equality Required?}
    decision -->|No exact match needed| epsilon[Use epsilon tolerance comparison]
    decision -->|Yes for special cases| checkSpecial{Both NaN?}
    checkSpecial -->|Yes| trueNan[Considered unequal even with equals]
    checkSpecial -->|No| checkZero{Both Plus or Minus Zero?}
    checkZero -->|Yes| trueZero[Considered equal]
    checkZero -->|No| strict[Use exact equals]

    epsilon --> formula["absolute value of a-b is less than epsilon"]
    formula --> output[Boolean Result]
    strict --> output
    trueNan --> output
    trueZero --> output
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Explain the IEEE 754 representation of floating-point numbers in Java. State the bit allocation for `float` and `double`.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

IEEE 754 is the international standard for representing floating-point numbers in binary. Each number is represented using three components:

$$
\text{Value} = (-1)^{S} \times 1.M \times 2^{(E - \text{Bias})}
$$

- **Sign (S):** 1 bit (0 = positive, 1 = negative).
- **Exponent (E):** Stored in biased form (Bias = 127 for float, 1023 for double).
- **Mantissa (M):** Stores fractional part; leading `1.` is implicit.

**Bit allocation:**

| Type | Total Bits | Sign | Exponent | Mantissa |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `float`  | 32 | 1 | 8  | 23 |
| `double` | 64 | 1 | 11 | 52 |

(3 Marks: 1 for formula, 1 for description, 1 for bit allocation table.)

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**List and explain the special values defined by IEEE 754. How does Java handle division by zero with floating-point operands?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

IEEE 754 defines the following special values:

1. **Positive Zero (+0.0) and Negative Zero (-0.0):** Sign bit differs, mantissa and exponent are zero. `+0.0 == -0.0` evaluates to `true` in Java.
2. **Positive Infinity (`POSITIVE_INFINITY`):** Result of `1.0 / 0.0`. Sign=0, exponent=all 1s, mantissa=0.
3. **Negative Infinity (`NEGATIVE_INFINITY`):** Result of `-1.0 / 0.0`. Sign=1, exponent=all 1s, mantissa=0.
4. **NaN (Not a Number):** Result of `0.0 / 0.0` or `Math.sqrt(-1.0)`. Exponent=all 1s, mantissa ≠ 0.

In Java, floating-point division by zero does **not** throw an `ArithmeticException` (unlike integer division). It returns one of the above special values gracefully.

(3 Marks: 1 for listing, 1 for explanation, 1 for Java-specific behavior.)

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Writing `float x = 3.14;` without the `f`/`F` suffix — this is a **compilation error**. Always remember: **floating-point literals default to `double`** in Java.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Comparing floats with `==` directly. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for not using an **epsilon tolerance**.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing `Float.MIN_VALUE` (smallest positive ≈ $1.4 \times 10^{-45}$) with the most negative value (which is `-Float.MAX_VALUE`).
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting that IEEE 754 representation uses **bias**, not two's complement, for exponents. Writing exponent in 2's complement form will cost you full marks.

---

### Question B1 (Choice A): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**a)** Explain the IEEE 754 standard for floating-point representation in detail. With a neat diagram, describe the bit layout of `float` and `double` data types in Java. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate the use of `float` and `double` data types. Your program should:
- Declare and initialize `float` and `double` variables.
- Display `MAX_VALUE`, `MIN_VALUE`, and `SIZE` constants for both types.
- Demonstrate the floating-point comparison pitfall and the correct epsilon-based comparison method.
**(7 Marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B1

#### Part (a) — IEEE 754 Detailed Explanation [7 Marks]

**IEEE 754 Standard:**

The Institute of Electrical and Electronics Engineers (IEEE) defined **Standard 754** in 1985 to provide a consistent method for representing real numbers in binary on computing systems. Java adopts this standard for both `float` and `double` primitive types.

**Three Components:**

1. **Sign Bit (S) — 1 bit:** Determines the sign of the number.
   - `S = 0` → positive number.
   - `S = 1` → negative number.

2. **Exponent (E):** Stored in **biased form** (not two's complement) to simplify hardware comparison.
   - **Bias for float** = $2^{7} - 1 = 127$.
   - **Bias for double** = $2^{10} - 1 = 1023$.

3. **Mantissa / Significand (M):** Stores the fractional part of the normalized number.
   - The leading `1.` is **implicit (hidden bit)** and not stored — saving one extra bit of precision.
   - Only the fractional bits after the point are stored.

**Mathematical Representation:**

$$
\text{Value} = (-1)^{S} \times (1.M) \times 2^{(E - \text{Bias})}
$$

**Bit Layout Diagram:**

| Component | `float` (32 bits) | `double` (64 bits) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sign | 1 bit | 1 bit |
| Exponent | 8 bits | 11 bits |
| Mantissa | 23 bits | 52 bits |
| **Total** | **32 bits** | **64 bits** |

**Special Encoding Rules:**

- Exponent = all zeros → **Zero or Subnormal**.
- Exponent = all ones, Mantissa = 0 → **±Infinity**.
- Exponent = all ones, Mantissa ≠ 0 → **NaN**.
- Otherwise → **Normalized number** (most values).

**Why IEEE 754?**
- Portability across hardware platforms.
- Predictable rounding behavior.
- Clear handling of edge cases (overflow → infinity, underflow → subnormal or zero).

(Marks distribution: 2 for components, 2 for formula and bias, 2 for bit layout, 1 for special encoding.)

---

#### Part (b) — Java Program Demonstration [7 Marks]

```java
public class FloatingPointTypesDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // 1. Declaration and Initialization
        float  piFloat  = 3.14159265f;   // Note the 'f' suffix
        double piDouble = 3.141592653589793;
        
        System.out.println("--- Basic Declaration ---");
        System.out.println("piFloat  = " + piFloat);
        System.out.println("piDouble = " + piDouble);
        
        // 2. Display Constants
        System.out.println("\n--- Constants ---");
        System.out.println("Float  MAX_VALUE : " + Float.MAX_VALUE);
        System.out.println("Float  MIN_VALUE : " + Float.MIN_VALUE);
        System.out.println("Float  SIZE      : " + Float.SIZE  + " bits");
        System.out.println("Double MAX_VALUE : " + Double.MAX_VALUE);
        System.out.println("Double MIN_VALUE : " + Double.MIN_VALUE);
        System.out.println("Double SIZE      : " + Double.SIZE + " bits");
        
        // 3. Floating-Point Comparison Pitfall
        System.out.println("\n--- Comparison Pitfall ---");
        double a = 0.1, b = 0.2, c = 0.3;
        double sum = a + b;
        System.out.println("0.1 + 0.2     = " + sum);
        System.out.println("Exact equal?  = " + (sum == c));   // false!
        
        // 4. Correct Epsilon Comparison
        double epsilon = 1e-9;
        boolean approxEqual = Math.abs(sum - c) < epsilon;
        System.out.println("Approximately equal (within 1e-9)? = " + approxEqual);
        
        // 5. Special Values
        System.out.println("\n--- Special Values ---");
        System.out.println("1.0 / 0.0  = " + (1.0 / 0.0));    // Infinity
        System.out.println("-1.0 / 0.0 = " + (-1.0 / 0.0));   // -Infinity
        System.out.println("0.0 / 0.0  = " + (0.0 / 0.0));    // NaN
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**

```
--- Basic Declaration ---
piFloat  = 3.1415927
piDouble = 3.141592653589793

--- Constants ---
Float  MAX_VALUE : 3.4028235E38
Float  MIN_VALUE : 1.4E-45
Float  SIZE      : 32 bits
Double MAX_VALUE : 1.7976931348623157E308
Double MIN_VALUE : 4.9E-324
Double SIZE      : 64 bits

--- Comparison Pitfall ---
0.1 + 0.2     = 0.30000000000000004
Exact equal?  = false

Approximately equal (within 1e-9)? = true

--- Special Values ---
1.0 / 0.0  = Infinity
-1.0 / 0.0 = -Infinity
0.0 / 0.0  = NaN
```

**Explanation (for examiners):**

- `piFloat` loses precision because `float` can only hold ~7 decimal digits; the value rounds to `3.1415927`.
- `Float.MIN_VALUE` returns the smallest positive subnormal value, **not** the most negative.
- `0.1 + 0.2` is **not** exactly `0.3` due to binary representation — this is the classic floating-point pitfall.
- The epsilon method (`Math.abs(sum - c) < epsilon`) is the **industry-standard** correct way to compare floating-point values.

(Marks distribution: 2 for declaration and constants, 2 for pitfall demonstration, 2 for epsilon comparison, 1 for special values.)

---

### Question B2 (Choice B): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**a)** With neat diagrams, explain how floating-point literals are represented in Java. Discuss the significance of suffixes `f`, `F`, `d`, `D`, and scientific notation (e.g., `1.5e3`). **(7 Marks)**

**b)** Write a Java program that:
- Declares variables of type `float` and `double`.
- Demonstrates automatic type promotion in mixed expressions (e.g., `float + double`, `int + double`).
- Uses wrapper classes `Float` and `Double` to perform parsing and conversion.
**(7 Marks)**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B2

#### Part (a) — Floating-Point Literals in Java [7 Marks]

**Floating-Point Literals** are numeric literals that contain a decimal point or are expressed in scientific (exponential) notation.

**Rules for Floating-Point Literals:**

1. **Default Type:** By default, every floating-point literal is of type `double` in Java.
   ```java
   double d = 3.14;      // OK - default is double
   float  f = 3.14;      // COMPILE ERROR - needs suffix
   ```

2. **Suffixes:**
   - `f` or `F` → forces the literal to be treated as `float`.
   - `d` or `D` → explicitly marks the literal as `double` (optional, since `double` is default).

3. **Scientific Notation:** Used for very large or very small numbers.
   - Format: `<mantissa>e<exponent>` or `<mantissa>E<exponent>`.
   - Example: `1.5e3` means $1.5 \times 10^{3} = 1500.0$.

**Diagrammatic Representation:**

```
        Floating-Point Literal
              |
    +---------+--------+
    |                  |
 Contains '.',     Scientific
 'e', or 'E'?      Notation?
    |                  |
    v                  v
 Has suffix?      Value = mantissa × 10^exponent
    |                  |
    +----+----+----+
         |    |
        f/F  d/D
         |    |
         v    v
       float  double  (default: double if no suffix)
```

**Examples of Literals:**

```java
double a = 3.14159;        // double (default)
float  b = 3.14159f;       // float (with suffix)
double c = 2.5e10;         // 2.5 × 10^10
float  d = 1.6E-19f;       // 1.6 × 10^-19
double e = .5;             // Valid - leading zero optional
double f = 5.;             // Valid - trailing zero optional
```

(Marks distribution: 2 for default rule, 2 for suffixes, 2 for scientific notation, 1 for valid examples.)

---

#### Part (b) — Java Program for Type Promotion and Wrappers [7 Marks]

```java
public class FloatPromotionWrapper {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // 1. Type Promotion in Mixed Expressions
        System.out.println("--- Type Promotion ---");
        float  f  = 10.5f;
        double d  = 20.75;
        int    i  = 30;
        
        // float + double → double (smaller type promoted to larger)
        double result1 = f + d;
        System.out.println("float + double = " + result1 
                         + " (type: " + ((Object)result1).getClass().getSimpleName() + ")");
        
        // int + double → double (int promoted to double)
        double result2 = i + d;
        System.out.println("int + double   = " + result2);
        
        // int + float → float (int promoted to float)
        float result3 = i + f;
        System.out.println("int + float    = " + result3);
        
        // 2. Wrapper Classes - Parsing
        System.out.println("\n--- Wrapper Class Parsing ---");
        String floatStr  = "45.67";
        String doubleStr = "1.23456789012345e10";
        
        Float  parsedFloat  = Float.parseFloat(floatStr);
        Double parsedDouble = Double.parseDouble(doubleStr);
        
        System.out.println("Parsed float  from \"" + floatStr  + "\" = " + parsedFloat);
        System.out.println("Parsed double from \"" + doubleStr + "\" = " + parsedDouble);
        
        // 3. Wrapper Classes - Conversion
        System.out.println("\n--- Wrapper Conversion ---");
        float  primitiveFloat  = parsedFloat;       // Unboxing
        double primitiveDouble = parsedDouble;     // Unboxing
        
        Double boxedDouble = 99.99;                // Autoboxing
        double unboxed     = boxedDouble;          // Unboxing
        
        System.out.println("Autoboxed Double : " + boxedDouble);
        System.out.println("Unboxed double   : " + unboxed);
        
        // 4. Useful Wrapper Methods
        System.out.println("\n--- Wrapper Utility Methods ---");
        System.out.println("Float.isNaN(0.0f/0)   = " + Float.isNaN(0.0f / 0.0f));
        System.out.println("Double.isInfinite(1/0.0)= " + Double.isInfinite(1.0 / 0.0));
        System.out.println("Float.compare(2.5f,3.0f) = " + Float.compare(2.5f, 3.0f));
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**

```
--- Type Promotion ---
float + double = 31.25 (type: Double)
int + double   = 50.75
int + float    = 40.5

--- Wrapper Class Parsing ---
Parsed float  from "45.67" = 45.67
Parsed double from "1.23456789012345e10" = 1.23456789012345E10

--- Wrapper Conversion ---
Autoboxed Double : 99.99
Unboxed double   : 99.99

--- Wrapper Utility Methods ---
Float.isNaN(0.0f/0)   = true
Double.isInfinite(1/0.0)= true
Float.compare(2.5f,3.0f) = -1
```

**Explanation:**

- **Type Promotion Rule:** In any binary arithmetic expression involving `float` and `double`, the result is always `double`. If `int` is mixed with `float`, result is `float`. If `int` is mixed with `double`, result is `double`.
- **Autoboxing:** Java automatically converts primitives to wrapper objects (e.g., `double` → `Double`).
- **Unboxing:** Automatic conversion of wrapper back to primitive.
- **Parsing:** `Float.parseFloat()` and `Double.parseDouble()` convert strings to numeric primitives.

(Marks distribution: 2 for type promotion, 2 for parsing, 2 for autoboxing/unboxing, 1 for utility methods.)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision — Floating Point Types**

- ✅ Java has **two** floating-point primitives: `float` (32-bit) and `double` (64-bit).
- ✅ Both follow the **IEEE 754 standard** (binary floating-point arithmetic).
- ✅ **Default type for literals is `double`**. Use `f`/`F` suffix for `float`.
- ✅ IEEE 754 has three components: **Sign (1 bit)**, **Exponent (biased)**, **Mantissa (with hidden leading 1)**.
- ✅ `float`: 1 + 8 + 23 bits; Bias = **127**; Precision ≈ **7 decimal digits**.
- ✅ `double`: 1 + 11 + 52 bits; Bias = **1023**; Precision ≈ **15–17 decimal digits**.
- ✅ Special values: **±0.0**, **±Infinity**, **NaN**. No exception on division by zero in floating-point.
- ✅ `Float.NaN == Float.NaN` is **false** (special NaN rule). Use `Float.isNaN()` instead.
- ✅ `Float.MIN_VALUE` = smallest **positive subnormal** value (≈ $1.4 \times 10^{-45}$), **not** most negative.
- ✅ **Never use `==` to compare floats.** Use epsilon tolerance: $\vert a - b \vert < \varepsilon$.
- ✅ Type promotion: `int + float → float`, `int/float + double → double`.
- ✅ Wrapper classes: `java.lang.Float`, `java.lang.Double` (immutable, support autoboxing).
- ✅ Useful constants: `MAX_VALUE`, `MIN_VALUE`, `SIZE`, `POSITIVE_INFINITY`, `NEGATIVE_INFINITY`, `NaN`.
- ✅ Scientific notation `1.5e3` = $1.5 \times 10^{3}$ is valid for both `float` and `double`.
- ✅ In mixed expressions, the result type follows the **largest** operand type.
- ✅ Java floating-point arithmetic is **deterministic** (no undefined behavior like C), thanks to IEEE 754 strict mode.
- ✅ Subnormal numbers (exponent = 0, mantissa ≠ 0) allow graceful underflow.
- ✅ The hidden/implicit leading `1.` bit in the mantissa gives **one extra bit of effective precision**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Characters ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the Java programming language, a **character** is the fundamental linguistic atom used to represent a single textual symbol, such as a letter, digit, punctuation mark, or any Unicode-defined glyph. Java provides two equivalent representations for characters: 1. **Primitive Type `char`** — a low-level, 16-bit unsigned integer that directly holds a Unicode code point (range **0 to 65535**). 2. **Wrapper Class `Character`** — a reference-type object defined in `java.lang.Character` that encapsulates a single `char` value and provides a rich library of utility methods for character classification, conversion, and comparison. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus (Module 2):** Students must master the distinction between the *primitive* `char` and the *object* `Character`, the underlying **Unicode** encoding scheme, the canonical set of wrapper-class methods (`isLetter`, `isDigit`, `toUpperCase`, `toLowerCase`, `compareTo`, `equals`, `charValue`), and the implicit **autoboxing/unboxing** mechanism introduced in **Java 5 (JDK 1.5)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Postbox and the Letter" Imagine a small **postbox** outside your house. Inside it, you can place a single **letter**. The postbox is the primitive container — fast, compact, and always at your door. The letter itself is a *value* (e.g., the letter 'A'). Now imagine a **smart postbox** — a small digital device that not only holds the letter but also tells you *"Is this a vowel?"*, *"Is this uppercase?"*, *"Convert it to lowercase."* This smart postbox is the `Character` wrapper class — it *wraps* the primitive letter and provides intelligent operations. > [!NOTE] > **Why two forms?** The primitive `char` exists for **performance** (stack allocation, direct arithmetic, no garbage collection overhead). The `Character` object exists for **Object-Oriented uniformity** — so characters can be used in Collections (`ArrayList`), passed via `Object` references, and processed via polymorphism. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants | Property | Value | Notes | |---|---|---| | Bit width | **16 bits (2 bytes)** | Fixed size, unlike C's 8-bit `char` | | Range | **0 to 65535** | Unsigned; wraps around at 65536 | | Encoding | **Unicode (UTF-16 code unit)** | Covers Basic Multilingual Plane (BMP) | | Default value | `'\u0000'` | The null character | | Wrapper class | `java.lang.Character` | Implicitly imported; no need for `import` | | Autoboxing threshold | **Java 5 (JDK 1.5)** | Compiler transparently bridges `char ↔ Character` | --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The `char` Primitive — Under the Hood A `char` is *not* a "character" in the human-typographic sense — it is fundamentally a **16-bit unsigned integer** that *represents* a character via the **Unicode** standard. This distinction is crucial: > [!NOTE] > **Unicode Code Point vs. UTF-16 Code Unit:** A *code point* is the abstract number assigned to a character (e.g., U+0041 for 'A'). A *code unit* is how that number is stored in memory. Java's `char` holds one **UTF-16 code unit**. Characters outside the BMP (U+10000 and above) require *two* `char` values (a surrogate pair). ### 2.2 Operational Logic — Six Pillars of Character Handling 1. **Declaration & Initialization** — Use single quotes `'A'`, Unicode escapes `'\u0041'`, or integer casts `(char) 65`. 2. **Arithmetic** — Because `char` is an unsigned integer, you can perform `++`, `--`, and arithmetic (`'A' + 1` yields the `int` 66). 3. **Wrapper Boxing** — `Character ch = 'A';` triggers **autoboxing** (primitive → object). 4. **Wrapper Unboxing** — `char c = ch;` triggers **unboxing** (object → primitive). 5. **Classification** — `Character.isLetter()`, `isDigit()`, `isWhitespace()`, `isUpperCase()`, `isLowerCase()`. 6. **Conversion** — `Character.toUpperCase()`, `toLowerCase()`, `getNumericValue()`, `forDigit()`. ### 2.3 KTU Formula & Method Cheat Sheet #### Character Escape Sequences | Escape | Meaning | ASCII/Unicode Value | |---|---|---| | `\n` | Newline (Line feed) | U+000A (10) | | `\t` | Horizontal tab | U+0009 (9) | | `\b` | Backspace | U+0008 (8) | | `\r` | Carriage return | U+000D (13) | | `\f` | Form feed | U+000C (12) | | `\\` | Backslash | U+005C (92) | | `\'` | Single quote | U+0027 (39) | | `\"` | Double quote | U+0022 (34) | | `\0` | Null character | U+0000 (0) | | `\uXXXX` | Unicode escape (4 hex digits) | Varies | #### Essential `Character` Wrapper Methods (High-Yield for KTU) | Method Signature | Returns | Purpose | |---|---|---| | `Character(char value)` | Constructor | Wraps a primitive `char` into an object | | `char charValue()` | `char` | Unwraps the contained primitive | | `static boolean isLetter(char ch)` | `true` if a letter | Unicode-aware letter test | | `static boolean isDigit(char ch)` | `true` if a digit (0–9) | Numeric test | | `static boolean isLetterOrDigit(char ch)` | `true` if letter or digit | Combined alphanumeric test | | `static boolean isWhitespace(char ch)` | `true` if whitespace | Space, tab, newline, etc. | | `static boolean isUpperCase(char ch)` | `true` if uppercase | Per Unicode casing rules | | `static boolean isLowerCase(char ch)` | `true` if lowercase | Per Unicode casing rules | | `static char toUpperCase(char ch)` | `char` | Locale-independent uppercase | | `static char toLowerCase(char ch)` | `char` | Locale-independent lowercase | | `static int getNumericValue(char ch)` | `int` | Numeric value (e.g., 'A' = 10 in hex) | | `static char forDigit(int digit, int radix)` | `char` | Converts digit to char in given base | | `int compareTo(Character another)` | `int` | Lexicographic comparison | | `boolean equals(Object obj)` | `boolean` | Value-based equality check | | `String toString()` | `String` | Returns 1-char `String` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Character handling is foundational in **lexical analyzers** (compilers), **input validators** (forms, network packets), **cryptographic ciphers** (Caesar shift: `c = (char)((ch - 'A' + shift) % 26 + 'A')`), **parsers** (JSON/XML tokenizers), and **internationalization (i18n)** layers of enterprise software. ### 2.4 Autoboxing & Unboxing Mechanics $$ \text{char} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{unboxing}]{\text{autoboxing}} \text{Character} $$ The Java compiler inserts hidden calls to `Character.valueOf(char)` and `Character.charValue()` automatically. This bridge is essential when primitives must be stored in **generic collections** (e.g., `List`), which cannot accept raw primitives due to Java's **type erasure** mechanism. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Character Classification and Conversion **Problem:** Given the character `'k'`, demonstrate (a) its Unicode code point, (b) its uppercase form, and (c) verify it is a letter. **Step-by-Step Walkthrough:** ```java public class CharacterDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { char primitiveChar = 'k'; // (a) Get Unicode code point int codePoint = (int) primitiveChar; System.out.println("Code Point: " + codePoint); // (b) Convert to uppercase char upper = Character.toUpperCase(primitiveChar); System.out.println("Uppercase: " + upper); // (c) Verify it is a letter boolean isLetter = Character.isLetter(primitiveChar); System.out.println("Is Letter? " + isLetter); } } ``` **Output Trace:** $$ \text{'k'} \xrightarrow{\text{(int) cast}} 107 $$ $$ \text{'k'} \xrightarrow{\text{toUpperCase}} \text{'K'} $$ $$ \text{Character.isLetter('k')} \rightarrow \text{true} $$ **Console Output:** ``` Code Point: 107 Uppercase: K Is Letter? true ``` **Explanation of Each Step:** 1. **Line 3:** `char primitiveChar = 'k';` — Declares a 16-bit unsigned integer holding the Unicode code unit for the letter 'k'. 2. **Line 6:** `(int) primitiveChar` — *Widening conversion* (auto-promotion would also work). Casts the `char` to a 32-bit `int` revealing its numeric value 107. 3. **Line 10:** `Character.toUpperCase(...)` — Static method on the wrapper class. Internally consults the `Character.UnicodeBlock` of 'k' (Basic Latin) and returns 'K' (U+004B). 4. **Line 14:** `Character.isLetter(...)` — Returns `true` because the character's general category is `LOWERCASE_LETTER`. ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Autoboxing in a Generic Collection **Problem:** Store the characters of the string `"JAVA"` in an `ArrayList` and unbox them for concatenation. ```java import java.util.ArrayList; public class AutoboxingDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { String word = "JAVA"; ArrayList chars = new ArrayList<>(); // Autoboxing: char → Character on insertion for (int i = 0; i < word.length(); i++) { chars.add(word.charAt(i)); // implicit Character.valueOf(...) } // Unboxing: Character → char on retrieval StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder(); for (Character chObj : chars) { sb.append(chObj); // implicit charValue() + append(char) } System.out.println("Reconstructed: " + sb); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Reconstructed: JAVA ``` **Analytical Trace:** $$ \text{'J'} \xrightarrow{\text{autobox}} \text{Character@1a2b} \xrightarrow{\text{unbox}} \text{'J'} \xrightarrow{\text{append}} \text{"J"} $$ $$ \text{'A'} \xrightarrow{\text{autobox}} \text{Character@3c4d} \xrightarrow{\text{unbox}} \text{'A'} \xrightarrow{\text{append}} \text{"JA"} $$ $$ \text{'V'} \xrightarrow{\text{autobox}} \text{Character@5e6f} \xrightarrow{\text{unbox}} \text{'V'} \xrightarrow{\text{append}} \text{"JAV"} $$ $$ \text{'A'} \xrightarrow{\text{autobox}} \text{Character@7g8h} \xrightarrow{\text{unbox}} \text{'A'} \xrightarrow{\text{append}} \text{"JAVA"} $$ ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Caesar Cipher (Engineering Application) **Problem:** Encrypt the message `"HELLO"` with a Caesar shift of 3. **Algorithm (The Formula):** $$ c_i = \big( (p_i - \text{'A'} + k) \bmod 26 \big) + \text{'A'} $$ where $p_i$ is the plaintext character, $k$ is the shift key, and $c_i$ is the ciphertext character. ```java public class CaesarCipher { public static void main(String[] args) { String plain = "HELLO"; int shift = 3; StringBuilder cipher = new StringBuilder(); for (int i = 0; i < plain.length(); i++) { char p = plain.charAt(i); char c = (char)((p - 'A' + shift) % 26 + 'A'); cipher.append(c); } System.out.println("Encrypted: " + cipher); } } ``` **Step-by-Step Trace for 'H' (ASCII 72):** $$ c = ((72 - 65 + 3) \bmod 26) + 65 $$ $$ c = (10 \bmod 26) + 65 $$ $$ c = 10 + 65 = 75 $$ $$ c = \text{chr}(75) = \text{'K'} \;\checkmark $$ **Full Encryption:** $$ \text{'H'} \rightarrow \text{'K'}, \quad \text{'E'} \rightarrow \text{'H'}, \quad \text{'L'} \rightarrow \text{'O'}, \quad \text{'L'} \rightarrow \text{'O'}, \quad \text{'O'} \rightarrow \text{'R'} $$ $$ \therefore \text{Encrypted Output: "KHOOR"} $$ ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Primitive vs. Wrapper | Aspect | `char` (Primitive) | `Character` (Wrapper) | |---|---|---| | Memory location | Stack (or heap inside object) | Heap (object) | | Size | 2 bytes | ~16 bytes (object header + field + padding) | | Default value | `'\u0000'` | `null` (for object references) | | Allowed in `switch` | ✅ Yes (since Java 7) | ❌ No (compile error) | | Can be `null` | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | | Used in generics | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (`List`) | | Methods available | None (language operators only) | Full static + instance method library | | Performance | Faster (no allocation) | Slower (GC pressure) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The most common conceptual trap is *forgetting that `Character` is an object* and trying to compare two `Character` objects with `==` instead of `.equals()`. Always use `.equals()` for value comparison on wrapper objects. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Class Hierarchy — Where `Character` Lives The `Character` class is a direct subclass of `java.lang.Object`, in contrast to numeric wrappers which extend `java.lang.Number`. ```mermaid graph TD nodeA["java.lang.Object"] --> nodeB["java.lang.Character"] nodeA --> nodeC["java.lang.Number"] nodeC --> nodeD["java.lang.Integer"] nodeC --> nodeE["java.lang.Double"] nodeC --> nodeF["java.lang.Float"] nodeC --> nodeG["java.lang.Long"] nodeC --> nodeH["java.lang.Short"] nodeC --> nodeI["java.lang.Byte"] ``` ### 4.2 Autoboxing/Unboxing Bridge Flow ```mermaid graph LR node1["char primitive
variable c"] -->|"autoboxing
Character.valueOf(c)"| node2["Character object
chObj"] node2 -->|"unboxing
chObj.charValue()"| node1 node2 -->|"List<Character>
generic collection"| node3["Heap Allocated
Object"] node1 -->|"switch statement
direct case matching"| node4["Branch Dispatch"] ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Architecture — Character Processing Pipeline ```mermaid graph TD inA["Input Source
Keyboard File Network"] --> proc1["Read Token
charAt or read"] proc1 --> proc2["Classify
isLetter isDigit isWhitespace"] proc2 --> decA{"Letter?"} decA -->|Yes| proc3["Transform
toUpperCase toLowerCase"] decA -->|No| decB{"Digit?"} decB -->|Yes| proc4["Convert
getNumericValue"] decB -->|No| proc5["Pass Through
Special Symbol"] proc3 --> out1["Output Buffer
StringBuilder"] proc4 --> out1 proc5 --> out1 ``` ### 4.4 Unicode Encoding Mapping (Conceptual) ```mermaid graph LR nodeX["Character 'A'"] --> nodeY["Code Point U+0041
Decimal 65"] nodeX --> nodeZ["UTF-16 Code Unit
Binary 00000000 01000001"] nodeX --> nodeW["Category
UPPERCASE_LETTER Lu"] ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** *Explain the difference between the primitive `char` data type and the `Character` wrapper class in Java. Why is the wrapper class needed?* **Model Answer (3 Marks — Key Points):** 1. **Primitive `char`**: 16-bit unsigned integer, default value `'\u0000'`, stored on the stack, no methods, cannot be `null`, not allowed in generic type parameters. *(1 mark)* 2. **Wrapper `Character`**: Object of `java.lang.Character` class, stored on the heap, default value `null`, provides static utility methods (`isLetter`, `toUpperCase`, etc.), and is required for use in Java Collections and Generics (e.g., `ArrayList`). *(1 mark)* 3. **Need for Wrapper**: Java generics enforce type safety only on reference types. Since primitives cannot be type parameters, wrapper classes bridge primitive and OO worlds. Autoboxing (Java 5+) makes the conversion automatic. *(1 mark)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]** *What is Unicode? How does Java's `char` type represent characters using Unicode?* **Model Answer (3 Marks — Key Points):** 1. **Unicode Definition**: A universal character encoding standard that assigns a unique numeric *code point* to every character of every written language, aiming to replace older 8-bit schemes like ASCII. *(1 mark)* 2. **Java's `char`**: A 16-bit unsigned type (range **0–65535**) that holds one **UTF-16 code unit**. Since UTF-16 is a variable-width encoding, characters outside the Basic Multilingual Plane (code points ≥ U+10000) require *two* `char` values (a surrogate pair). *(1 mark)* 3. **Practical Benefit**: A single Java program can simultaneously handle English ('A'), Hindi ('अ'), Chinese ('中'), and emoji ('😀') without changing the data type, promoting true internationalization (i18n). *(1 mark)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Students answer either **Question A OR Question B** in full. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper] **(a)** Explain the following methods of the `Character` wrapper class with suitable examples: `isLetter()`, `isDigit()`, `isWhitespace()`, `toUpperCase()`, `toLowerCase()`. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Write a Java program that reads a string and counts the number of vowels, consonants, digits, and whitespace characters using `Character` wrapper methods. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Method Explanations (7 marks — 1.4 marks each, allocate proportionally):** | Method | Signature | Returns | Example | |---|---|---|---| | `isLetter` | `static boolean isLetter(char ch)` | `true` if `ch` is a letter | `Character.isLetter('A') → true` | | `isDigit` | `static boolean isDigit(char ch)` | `true` if `ch` is a digit (0–9) | `Character.isDigit('7') → true` | | `isWhitespace` | `static boolean isWhitespace(char ch)` | `true` if `ch` is a space, tab, newline | `Character.isWhitespace(' ') → true` | | `toUpperCase` | `static char toUpperCase(char ch)` | Uppercase form of `ch` | `Character.toUpperCase('z') → 'Z'` | | `toLowerCase` | `static char toLowerCase(char ch)` | Lowercase form of `ch` | `Character.toLowerCase('Q') → 'q'` | All methods are **static** and operate on the primitive `char` directly. They use **Unicode** categories, so they work for international scripts. **(b) Java Program (7 marks):** ```java import java.util.Scanner; public class CharAnalyzer { public static void main(String[] args) { Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in); System.out.print("Enter a string: "); String input = sc.nextLine(); int vowels = 0, consonants = 0, digits = 0, spaces = 0; for (int i = 0; i < input.length(); i++) { char ch = input.charAt(i); if (Character.isDigit(ch)) { digits++; } else if (Character.isWhitespace(ch)) { spaces++; } else if (Character.isLetter(ch)) { char lower = Character.toLowerCase(ch); if (lower == 'a' || lower == 'e' || lower == 'i' || lower == 'o' || lower == 'u') { vowels++; } else { consonants++; } } } System.out.println("Vowels: " + vowels); System.out.println("Consonants: " + consonants); System.out.println("Digits: " + digits); System.out.println("Whitespace: " + spaces); } } ``` **Sample Run:** ``` Enter a string: KTU 2024 Exam Prep Vowels: 4 Consonants: 8 Digits: 4 Whitespace: 3 ``` **Mark Allocation Breakdown (7 marks):** - Correct `Character` import / Scanner usage: **1 mark** - Accurate loop traversal: **1 mark** - Correct use of `isLetter`, `isDigit`, `isWhitespace`: **2 marks** - Vowel detection logic with `toLowerCase`: **2 marks** - Proper output formatting: **1 mark** --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — [KTU University Exam – June 2023] **(a)** What is autoboxing and unboxing? Explain with an example showing how a `char` primitive and `Character` object interact in a `List`. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Write a Java program to verify whether each character in a given string is an alphabet, digit, whitespace, uppercase, or lowercase using appropriate `Character` class methods. Display the category of each character. *(7 marks)* **Model Solution:** **(a) Autoboxing and Unboxing (7 marks):** **Autoboxing** is the automatic conversion of a primitive type into its corresponding wrapper object by the Java compiler. **Unboxing** is the reverse — automatic extraction of the primitive value from a wrapper object. This feature was introduced in **Java 5 (JDK 1.5)**. **Mathematical Representation:** $$ \text{char} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{unboxing (compiler inserts charValue())}]{\text{autoboxing (compiler inserts valueOf())}} \text{Character} $$ **Example Program:** ```java import java.util.ArrayList; import java.util.List; public class BoxingDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { List list = new ArrayList<>(); // Generic requires object type // AUToboxing: char 'J' is auto-wrapped to Character object list.add('J'); list.add('A'); list.add('V'); list.add('A'); // UNBOXING: Character object auto-unwrapped to char for printing for (char c : list) { System.out.println("Char: " + c + " | Upper: " + Character.toUpperCase(c)); } } } ``` **Output:** ``` Char: J | Upper: J Char: A | Upper: A Char: V | Upper: V Char: A | Upper: A ``` **Key Points (for full 7 marks):** - Definition of autoboxing/unboxing: **1.5 marks** - Mention of Java 5 introduction: **0.5 marks** - Compiler-inserted method calls (`valueOf` / `charValue`): **1 mark** - Working code with `List`: **3 marks** - Correct output and explanation: **1 mark** **(b) Character Categorization Program (7 marks):** ```java import java.util.Scanner; public class CharCategory { public static void main(String[] args) { Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in); System.out.print("Enter text: "); String text = sc.nextLine(); System.out.printf("%-6s | %-10s | %-10s | %-10s%n", "Char", "Category", "IsUpper", "IsLower"); System.out.println("---------------------------------------------"); for (int i = 0; i < text.length(); i++) { char ch = text.charAt(i); String category; if (Character.isDigit(ch)) category = "Digit"; else if (Character.isWhitespace(ch)) category = "Whitespace"; else if (Character.isLetter(ch)) category = "Letter"; else category = "Other"; System.out.printf("%-6s | %-10s | %-10s | %-10s%n", "'" + ch + "'", category, Character.isUpperCase(ch), Character.isLowerCase(ch)); } } } ``` **Sample Run:** ``` Enter text: Java5 Char | Category | IsUpper | IsLower --------------------------------------------- 'J' | Letter | true | false 'a' | Letter | false | true 'v' | Letter | false | true 'a' | Letter | false | true '5' | Digit | false | false ``` **Mark Allocation:** - Correct nested `if-else` logic: **3 marks** - Use of `isDigit`, `isLetter`, `isWhitespace`, `isUpperCase`, `isLowerCase`: **3 marks** - Formatted output: **1 mark** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting single quotes** — Writing `char ch = A;` instead of `char ch = 'A';` (compile error). > 2. **Using `==` on `Character` objects** — This compares *references*, not values. Always use `.equals()`. > 3. **Confusing `char` with `String`** — `Character.toString(ch)` returns a 1-char `String`; do not assign it to a `char` variable. > 4. **Integer overflow in Caesar-style arithmetic** — Always cast back to `char` *after* the modular arithmetic: `(char)((p - 'A' + k) % 26 + 'A')`. > 5. **Calling non-static method on wrapper class** — Methods like `isLetter` are `static`; you cannot call them on an instance: `ch.isLetter()` is **invalid**. Use `Character.isLetter(ch)`. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — High-Yield Points for KTU 2024** - 🔹 `char` is a **16-bit unsigned** primitive in the range **0 to 65535**, representing a **Unicode UTF-16 code unit**. - 🔹 `Character` is the wrapper class in `java.lang`, encapsulating a `char` and providing static utility methods. - 🔹 `Character` is a **direct subclass of `Object`** — it does **NOT** extend `Number` (unlike numeric wrappers). - 🔹 **Autoboxing** (`char → Character`) and **unboxing** (`Character → char`) were introduced in **Java 5** and are compiler-driven. - 🔹 Escape sequences (`\n`, `\t`, `\b`, `\r`, `\\`, `\'`, `\"`, `\0`, `\uXXXX`) must be memorized for code-output questions. - 🔹 The most-tested methods are: `isLetter`, `isDigit`, `isWhitespace`, `isUpperCase`, `isLowerCase`, `toUpperCase`, `toLowerCase`, `charValue`, `compareTo`, `equals`. - 🔹 Use `.equals()` for **value comparison** of `Character` objects; never use `==` (it compares memory references). - 🔹 Generics like `List` require the **wrapper type** — primitives are not permitted as type parameters. - 🔹 `Character` can be `null`; the primitive `char` cannot. This makes `Character` preferable for optional/missing-value scenarios (e.g., database fields). - 🔹 A `char` can be used in a `switch` statement (since Java 7) along with `String` cases — a common exam twist. - 🔹 The Caesar cipher pattern $(p - \text{'A'} + k) \bmod 26 + \text{'A'}$ is a favorite KTU problem linking characters to real-world cryptography. - 🔹 Remember: `(int) 'A'` yields **65**; `Character.toString((char) 65)` yields `"A"` — bidirectional casting mastery is essential.

topics.title: Boolean
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Boolean

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Java programming language, the **Boolean** type refers to a logical data type that can hold exactly one of two possible values: **`true`** or **`false`**. Java provides the Boolean concept in two forms:

1. **Primitive boolean** — a built-in, lowercase, value-type representing a single bit of logical information.
2. **Wrapper Class `java.lang.Boolean`** — an object wrapper around the primitive `boolean`, enabling the value to participate in the Java Object hierarchy (e.g., Collections, Generics, Reflection).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):**
> The *boolean* data type represents a logical entity having two literal values: `true` and `false`. It is the only primitive type in Java whose size is **not precisely defined** by the Java Language Specification (JLS) — implementations may use 1 bit or 1 byte. The wrapper counterpart is the immutable final class `java.lang.Boolean`, which extends `java.lang.Object` and implements `java.io.Serializable` and `java.lang.Comparable<Boolean>`.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a Boolean like a **light switch on a wall**:

| Light Switch | Java Boolean |
|---|---|
| Switch is UP (ON) | `true` |
| Switch is DOWN (OFF) | `false` |
| Only two physical states possible | Only two literal values allowed |
| Cannot be "half-on" | No tri-state or partial values permitted |

A Java `boolean` is essentially a **yes/no flag** — a single binary decision unit that the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) uses to make branching decisions (`if`, `while`, `for`) and logical evaluations.

## 1.3 Key Specifications (Bolded Standard Metrics)

- **Literal values:** `true`, `false` (lowercase, both are keywords — **not** `True` or `FALSE` like in Python).
- **Size:** Implementation-defined, but **virtually always 1 byte** in modern JVMs.
- **Default value (class fields):** `false`.
- **Default value (local variables):** Compile-time error if used uninitialized.
- **Wrapper class:** `java.lang.Boolean` (public, final, extends Object).
- **Constants:** `Boolean.TRUE` and `Boolean.FALSE` (pre-instantiated immutable instances for memory efficiency).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A frequently asked distinction is *"boolean vs Boolean."* Always clarify in your answers — `boolean` is a **primitive type** (lowercase 'b'), while `Boolean` is a **reference type / wrapper class** (uppercase 'B').

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Primitive `boolean` — Operational Breakdown

The Java compiler treats `boolean` as a **distinct category** from numeric types. This is a critical design decision (unlike C/C++, where `0` is false and non-zero is true).

### Core Characteristics:
- **No Casting:** A `boolean` **cannot be cast** to or from any numeric type (`int`, `char`, etc.). This is a compile-time error.
- **No Arithmetic:** You cannot perform `+`, `-`, `*`, `/` on booleans.
- **Logical Evaluation Only:** Operations permitted are **logical operators** (`!`, `&&`, `||`, `&`, `|`, `^`) and **relational operators** that produce a boolean result (`<`, `>`, `==`, `!=`, etc.).

### Declaration Syntax:

```java
boolean isActive = true;
boolean hasPermission = false;
boolean result;          // local variable — must be initialized before use
```

## 2.2 Boolean Logical Operators — Truth Table Reference

### A. Short-Circuit Logical Operators (Most Common in KTU Exams)

| Operator | Name | Operand Type | Result Type | Evaluation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `&&` | Conditional AND | `boolean` | `boolean` | **Short-circuit**: right side skipped if left is `false` |
| `\|\|` | Conditional OR | `boolean` | `boolean` | **Short-circuit**: right side skipped if left is `true` |
| `!` | Logical NOT (unary) | `boolean` | `boolean` | Inverts the truth value |

### B. Non-Short-Circuit Bitwise Operators (Work on Booleans too)

| Operator | Name | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| `&` | Logical AND | **Always** evaluates both operands |
| `\|` | Logical OR | **Always** evaluates both operands |
| `^` | Logical XOR | `true` only if operands differ |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Truth Table Cheat Sheet

| A | B | `!A` | `A && B` | `A \|\| B` | `A ^ B` | `A & B` | `A \| B` |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| false | false | true | false | false | false | false | false |
| false | true | true | false | true | true | false | true |
| true | false | false | false | true | true | false | true |
| true | true | false | true | true | false | true | true |

## 2.4 The `Boolean` Wrapper Class — Engineering Utility

The wrapper class `java.lang.Boolean` is essential in:

- **Generic Collections** (e.g., `ArrayList<Boolean>`, `HashMap<String, Boolean>`) — generics **cannot** use primitives.
- **Reflection APIs** (e.g., `Field.getBoolean()` vs `Field.get()`).
- **Serialization** of boolean state in distributed systems.
- **Object-oriented design patterns** requiring polymorphic type wrappers.

### Static Factory Methods (Boxing):

```java
Boolean b1 = Boolean.valueOf(true);    // Preferred — avoids unnecessary object creation
Boolean b2 = Boolean.valueOf("true");  // Returns Boolean.TRUE
Boolean b3 = Boolean.valueOf("yes");   // Returns Boolean.FALSE (case-insensitive "true" only)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** `Boolean.valueOf(String)` returns `true` **only** if the input string equals `"true"` (case-insensitive). Anything else, including `"TRUE "`, `"True "` (with trailing whitespace), returns `false`. This is a **common pitfall** in KTU 2-mark questions.

## 2.5 Auto-Boxing and Auto-Unboxing (Java 5+ Feature)

| Process | Direction | Syntax Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Auto-Boxing** | primitive → wrapper | `Boolean b = true;` (compiler inserts `Boolean.valueOf(true)`) |
| **Auto-Unboxing** | wrapper → primitive | `boolean p = b;` (compiler inserts `b.booleanValue()`) |

## 2.6 Boolean in Control Flow — Real-World Engineering Utility

| Java Construct | Boolean's Role | Industrial Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| `if (condition)` | Branches execution | Sensor threshold detection in IoT |
| `while (condition)` | Loop termination | Polling in network clients |
| `for (; condition; )` | Loop control | Iterating until login success |
| Ternary `? :` | Inline conditional | `String status = (isOnline) ? "Active" : "Offline";` |
| Method return | `boolean isValid()` | Form validation in web apps |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Short-Circuit Evaluation Logic

Let us formally derive why `&&` is termed *short-circuit* AND using Boolean Algebra.

### Given Expression:

$$E = A \;\&\&\; f(B)$$
