Where `A` is a boolean variable and `f(B)` is any Java expression that returns boolean.

### Step 1: Define the Logical AND Truth Function

By the standard Boolean AND truth table:

$$A \;\land\; B = \begin{cases} \text{true} & \text{if } A = \text{true AND } B = \text{true} \\ \text{false} & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

### Step 2: Apply Identity Law of Boolean Algebra

The **Identity Law** states:

$$A \land \text{true} = A$$
$$A \land \text{false} = \text{false}$$

### Step 3: Consequence for Java's `&&`

> **If `A == false`, then `A && f(B)` MUST be `false`, regardless of `f(B)`.**

Therefore, the JVM **provably skips** the evaluation of `f(B)` when `A == false`. This optimization is:

- **Safe** (cannot alter the result)
- **Performance-enhancing** (saves CPU cycles)
- **Side-effect-sensitive** (if `f(B)` contains critical side effects like `i++`, they will be skipped — a major KTU exam trap!)

### Step 4: Proof of Equivalence Table

| A | B | A && B | Short-Circuit Saves? |
|---|---|---|---|
| false | false | false | YES — B not evaluated |
| false | true | false | YES — B not evaluated |
| true | false | false | NO — B must be evaluated |
| true | true | true | NO — B must be evaluated |

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: De Morgan's Laws (Critical for KTU)

De Morgan's Laws govern the transformation of negated compound conditions.

### Law 1: Negation of Conjunction

$$!(A \;\&\&\; B) \;\;\equiv\;\; !A \;\|\|\; !B$$

### Step-by-step Proof using Truth Table:

| A | B | A && B | !(A && B) | !A | !B | !A \|\| !B | Match? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| false | false | false | **true** | true | true | **true** | ✓ |
| false | true | false | **true** | true | false | **true** | ✓ |
| true | false | false | **true** | false | true | **true** | ✓ |
| true | true | true | **false** | false | false | **false** | ✓ |

### Law 2: Negation of Disjunction

$$!(A \;\|\|\; B) \;\;\equiv\;\; !A \;\&\&\; !B$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2-Mark Favourite:** *Convert the condition `!(x > 5 && y < 10)` into a simpler form using De Morgan's Law.*

**Solution Walkthrough:**

$$!(x > 5 \;\&\&\; y < 10) \;\;\equiv\;\; !(x > 5) \;\|\|\; !(y < 10)$$

$$= (x \le 5) \;\|\|\; (y \ge 10)$$

This is invaluable for simplifying nested `if` conditions.

## 3.3 Boxing/Unboxing Performance Derivation

For an N-element `ArrayList<Boolean>` with `add()` and `get()` operations:

**Naïve (Object construction every time):**
- Each `add(true)` allocates a new `Boolean` object on the heap.
- Garbage Collector (GC) pressure: $O(N)$ allocations.

**Optimized (`Boolean.valueOf(true)` or auto-boxing):**
- `Boolean.TRUE` and `Boolean.FALSE` are pre-cached static fields.
- All `add(true)` calls return the **same reference** to `Boolean.TRUE`.
- GC pressure: $O(1)$ allocations.

> **Real-world impact:** In a high-frequency trading system processing 1 million boolean flags/second, naïve boxing creates ~1 million short-lived objects/second — triggering frequent **Minor GC pauses** and degrading latency. Auto-boxing + `valueOf` eliminates this.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Operator Precedence (High-Yield)

| Precedence | Operators | Associativity | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (highest) | `!` (unary NOT) | Right-to-left | |
| 2 | `*`, `/`, `%` | Left-to-right | Numeric only |
| 3 | `+`, `-` | Left-to-right | Numeric only — NOT for booleans |
| 4 | `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` | Left-to-right | Returns boolean |
| 5 | `==`, `!=` | Left-to-right | **Reference equality for Boolean wrapper** |
| 6 | `&` (non-SC AND) | Left-to-right | |
| 7 | `^` (XOR) | Left-to-right | |
| 8 | `\|` (non-SC OR) | Left-to-right | |
| 9 | `&&` (SC AND) | Left-to-right | |
| 10 (lowest) | `\|\|` (SC OR) | Left-to-right | |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Boolean Type Hierarchy in Java

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["java.lang.Object"] --> B["java.lang.Boolean"]
    B --> C["implements Serializable"]
    B --> D["implements Comparable of Boolean"]
    E["primitive boolean"] --> F["Auto Boxing"]
    F --> B
    B --> G["Auto Unboxing"]
    G --> E
    H["Static Cache"] --> I["Boolean.TRUE"]
    H --> J["Boolean.FALSE"]
    B --> H
```

## 4.2 Decision Flow: Short-Circuit AND Evaluation

```mermaid
graph TD
    Start["Evaluate Expression A && B"] --> Q1{"Is A true?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> Q2{"Is B true?"}
    Q1 -- "No" --> R1["Result is false — B NOT evaluated Short circuit triggered"]
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> R2["Result is true"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> R3["Result is false"]
```

## 4.3 Boolean Lifecycle: From Primitive to Wrapper and Back

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["boolean flag = true"] --> AB["Auto Boxing Boolean obj = flag"]
    AB --> V1["JVM inserts Boolean valueOf flag"]
    V1 --> CACHE["Returns Boolean TRUE reference from cache"]
    CACHE --> USED["Used in ArrayList HashMap etc"]
    USED --> UB["Auto Unboxing boolean flag2 = obj"]
    UB --> V2["JVM inserts obj booleanValue"]
    V2 --> P2["primitive boolean flag2"]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Boolean in a Validation Engine

```mermaid
graph TD
    Input["User Form Data Input Object"] --> C1["Check isEmailValid"]
    Input --> C2["Check isPasswordStrong"]
    Input --> C3["Check isAgeAbove18"]
    C1 --> AND1["Logical AND operator"]
    C2 --> AND1
    C3 --> AND1
    AND1 --> Decision{"All three booleans true?"}
    Decision -- "Yes" --> Success["Grant Access boolean true"]
    Decision -- "No" --> Failure["Deny Access boolean false"]
    Success --> Out["Return Boolean to Controller"]
    Failure --> Out
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]
**Differentiate between the primitive data type `boolean` and the wrapper class `Boolean` in Java. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | `boolean` (primitive) | `Boolean` (wrapper class) |
|---|---|---|
| Declaration | `boolean flag = true;` | `Boolean flag = Boolean.TRUE;` |
| Memory | Stack (typically 1 byte) | Heap (object header + data) |
| Null Allowed? | **No** | **Yes** — `Boolean b = null;` is valid |
| Use in Generics | **Not allowed** | **Allowed** — `List<Boolean>` |
| Default value | `false` | `null` |
| Package | Java keyword | `java.lang` package |

*(1 mark for definition of each + 1 mark for valid syntax example and distinction point)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest Paper]
**Explain short-circuit evaluation of logical operators in Java with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Short-circuit evaluation is an optimization where the JVM **skips the evaluation of the right-hand operand** of `&&` or `||` when the result can already be determined from the left operand.

- For **`&&`**: If the left operand is `false`, the right operand is **not evaluated** (result must be `false`).
- For **`||`**: If the left operand is `true`, the right operand is **not evaluated** (result must be `true`).

**Example:**
```java
int x = 5;
if (x > 10 && ++x > 5) {  // x > 10 is FALSE, so ++x is never executed
    System.out.println("Inside");
}
System.out.println(x);  // Output: 5 (NOT 6, proving short-circuit)
```

*(1 mark definition + 1 mark rule for && and || + 1 mark example proving side-effect skip)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a)** Explain the Java Boolean wrapper class in detail. Discuss its constructors, important methods, and the constants `Boolean.TRUE` and `Boolean.FALSE`. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate **auto-boxing** and **auto-unboxing** with the boolean type. Also explain the role of `Boolean.parseBoolean()` and `Boolean.valueOf()` methods. **(7 Marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**(a) Boolean Wrapper Class Explanation (7 Marks):**

The `java.lang.Boolean` class wraps a primitive `boolean` value in an object. It is a **public final** class that extends `java.lang.Object` and implements `Serializable` and `Comparable<Boolean>`.

**Constructors:**
1. `Boolean(boolean value)` — Creates a Boolean object representing the primitive value.
   ```java
   Boolean b1 = new Boolean(true);
   ```
2. `Boolean(String s)` — Creates a Boolean object; returns `true` only if `s.equalsIgnoreCase("true")`.
   ```java
   Boolean b2 = new Boolean("TRUE");  // value is true
   Boolean b3 = new Boolean("yes");   // value is false
   ```
   > **Deprecation Note (1 mark):** Since Java 9, `new Boolean(String)` is **deprecated**. Use `Boolean.valueOf(String)` instead.

**Important Methods:**

| Method | Signature | Returns |
|---|---|---|
| `booleanValue()` | `public boolean booleanValue()` | The primitive boolean value |
| `parseBoolean()` | `public static boolean parseBoolean(String s)` | `true` if string equals "true" (case-insensitive), else `false` |
| `valueOf(boolean)` | `public static Boolean valueOf(boolean b)` | Cached Boolean.TRUE or Boolean.FALSE |
| `valueOf(String)` | `public static Boolean valueOf(String s)` | Boolean equivalent of string |
| `toString()` | `public String toString()` | "true" or "false" string |
| `equals(Object)` | `public boolean equals(Object obj)` | true if obj is Boolean with same value |
| `compareTo(Boolean)` | `public int compareTo(Boolean b)` | 0 if equal, positive if this>true, negative otherwise |
| `hashCode()` | `public int hashCode()` | 1231 for true, 1237 for false |

**Boolean.TRUE and Boolean.FALSE Constants (1 mark):**
- Public static final fields pre-instantiated at class loading time.
- Used by `valueOf()` to enable object caching — saves memory.
- All `Boolean.valueOf(true)` calls return the **same object reference**.

---

**(b) Java Program Demonstrating Auto-Boxing and Auto-Unboxing (7 Marks):**

```java
public class BooleanDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // 1. AUTO-BOXING: primitive boolean -> Boolean object
        boolean primitiveFlag = true;
        Boolean wrappedFlag = primitiveFlag;  // Compiler inserts: Boolean.valueOf(primitiveFlag)
        
        // 2. AUTO-UNBOXING: Boolean object -> primitive boolean
        Boolean objFlag = Boolean.FALSE;
        boolean primitive2 = objFlag;  // Compiler inserts: objFlag.booleanValue()
        
        // 3. Demonstrating parseBoolean() and valueOf()
        String userInput = "TRUE";
        boolean parsed = Boolean.parseBoolean(userInput);
        System.out.println("parseBoolean(\"TRUE\") = " + parsed);  // true
        
        String badInput = "yes";
        boolean parsed2 = Boolean.parseBoolean(badInput);
        System.out.println("parseBoolean(\"yes\") = " + parsed2);  // false
        
        Boolean cachedTrue = Boolean.valueOf(true);
        Boolean cachedTrue2 = Boolean.valueOf(true);
        System.out.println("Same reference? " + (cachedTrue == cachedTrue2));  // true
        
        // 4. Use in Collections (requires wrapper, not primitive)
        java.util.ArrayList<Boolean> attendance = new java.util.ArrayList<>();
        attendance.add(true);   // auto-boxing
        attendance.add(false);
        
        for (Boolean status : attendance) {
            if (status) {  // auto-unboxing inside if
                System.out.println("Present");
            }
        }
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
parseBoolean("TRUE") = true
parseBoolean("yes") = false
Same reference? true
Present
```

**Explanation of Roles (2 marks):**
- **`Boolean.parseBoolean(String)`:** Returns the **primitive** boolean — ideal when you need the value but no object overhead.
- **`Boolean.valueOf(String)` or `Boolean.valueOf(boolean)`:** Returns the **wrapper** object — ideal for collection insertion and uses the static cache for memory efficiency.
- **Auto-boxing/unboxing** (Java 5+) eliminates verbose manual calls, improving code readability.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — July 2024, Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the logical operators in Java with truth tables. Differentiate between `&&` and `&` operators. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that uses Boolean expressions to validate a login system. The program should check that (i) username is not empty, (ii) password length is at least 8, and (iii) the user is above 18 years old. Print appropriate messages using `if-else` with combined Boolean conditions. **(7 Marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**(a) Logical Operators in Java (7 Marks):**

Java provides six operators that operate on boolean operands:

**1. Logical NOT `!` (Unary):** Inverts boolean value.
```
!true  = false
!false = true
```

**2. Logical AND `&&` (Short-Circuit):**
```java
result = (A && B);
```
Returns `true` only if **both** A and B are `true`. If A is `false`, B is **not evaluated**.

| A | B | A && B |
|---|---|---|
| false | false | false |
| false | true | false |
| true | false | false |
| true | true | true |

**3. Logical OR `||` (Short-Circuit):**
```java
result = (A || B);
```
Returns `true` if **at least one** of A or B is `true`. If A is `true`, B is **not evaluated**.

**4. Non-Short-Circuit `&` and `|`:**
- `&` evaluates **both** operands always.
- `|` evaluates **both** operands always.

**Difference between `&&` and `&` (3 Marks):**

| Feature | `&&` (Conditional AND) | `&` (Bitwise/Logical AND) |
|---|---|---|
| Evaluation | Short-circuits if left is `false` | Always evaluates both operands |
| Use Case | General logical conditions | When right side has mandatory side effects |
| Performance | Faster when left is `false` | Always slower in that case |
| Bitwise on ints? | **No** | **Yes** — works on integers bit-by-bit |
| Example | `if (a > 0 && b/a > 1)` (safe — no div-by-zero) | `if (a > 0 & b/a > 1)` (risky — may divide by zero) |

**5. Logical XOR `^`:**
Returns `true` only if operands are **different**.
```java
true ^ true   = false
true ^ false  = true
false ^ true  = true
false ^ false = false
```

---

**(b) Java Login Validation Program (7 Marks):**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class LoginValidator {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        
        // Take inputs
        System.out.print("Enter username: ");
        String username = sc.nextLine();
        
        System.out.print("Enter password: ");
        String password = sc.nextLine();
        
        System.out.print("Enter age: ");
        int age = sc.nextInt();
        
        // Boolean validation flags
        boolean isUsernameValid = (username != null) && (!username.isEmpty());
        boolean isPasswordValid = (password != null) && (password.length() >= 8);
        boolean isAgeValid = (age > 18);
        
        // Combined Boolean decision using logical operators
        if (isUsernameValid && isPasswordValid && isAgeValid) {
            System.out.println("Login Successful. Welcome, " + username + "!");
        } else if (!isUsernameValid) {
            System.out.println("Error: Username cannot be empty.");
        } else if (!isPasswordValid) {
            System.out.println("Error: Password must be at least 8 characters.");
        } else {
            System.out.println("Error: Age must be above 18 to register.");
        }
        
        // Demonstrate XOR usage
        boolean isAdmin = true;
        boolean isGuest = false;
        if (isAdmin ^ isGuest) {
            System.out.println("Exactly one role assigned (XOR passed).");
        }
        
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Sample Run 1 (All Valid):**
```
Enter username: alice
Enter password: secret123
Enter age: 25
Login Successful. Welcome, alice!
Exactly one role assigned (XOR passed).
```

**Sample Run 2 (Invalid Password):**
```
Enter username: bob
Enter password: pass
Enter age: 30
Error: Password must be at least 8 characters.
```

**Code Walkthrough (1 mark):**
- Three **boolean variables** capture individual validation outcomes.
- The `if` statement uses `&&` to require **all three** to be `true` for success.
- The `else if` chain uses `!` (NOT) to identify **which** specific condition failed.
- The `^` (XOR) demonstrates that exactly one of the roles is active.
- `(username != null)` guard prevents `NullPointerException` on `.isEmpty()` — an **important defensive programming practice**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Writing `Boolean` instead of `boolean`** in primitive context (or vice-versa) — *deduct 1 mark* per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing `&` and `&&`** — students often claim they are "the same" — they are NOT for short-circuit semantics.
> 3. **Forgetting that `Boolean.parseBoolean("YES")` returns `false`** — only literal "true" (case-insensitive) returns true.
> 4. **Using `==` to compare Boolean objects** instead of `.equals()` — works only due to caching, but is **not guaranteed** in all JVMs. Prefer `.equals()`.
> 5. **Not initializing local boolean variables** — Java compiler will reject the code.
> 6. **Treating booleans like integers** (`boolean b = 1;`) — **compile error**. Java strictly forbids this unlike C/C++.
> 7. **Side-effect loss in short-circuit** — if you write `if (a > 0 && i++ > 5)`, students forget that `i++` won't execute when `a <= 0`.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Boolean in Java**

- [x] **Primitive `boolean` holds only `true` or `false`** — keywords, lowercase.
- [x] **Wrapper class is `java.lang.Boolean`** — uppercase 'B', extends Object, final.
- [x] **No casting** between `boolean` and numeric types — compile error.
- [x] **Default value of class field `boolean`** is `false`; local variables must be explicitly initialized.
- [x] **Short-circuit operators `&&` and `||`** skip right operand when result is already determined.
- [x] **Non-short-circuit `&`, `|`, `^`** always evaluate both operands — use only when side effects are required.
- [x] **`Boolean.parseBoolean(String)`** returns primitive — `true` only for "true" (case-insensitive).
- [x] **`Boolean.valueOf(boolean)`** returns cached `Boolean.TRUE` or `Boolean.FALSE` — memory efficient.
- [x] **Auto-boxing** (Java 5+): `Boolean b = true;` — implicit conversion to wrapper.
- [x] **Auto-unboxing**: `boolean p = b;` — implicit conversion to primitive.
- [x] **Use `.equals()`** to compare `Boolean` objects — not `==` (though cached instances make `==` accidentally work).
- [x] **De Morgan's Laws** — `!(A && B) ≡ !A || !B` and `!(A || B) ≡ !A && !B` — frequently tested.
- [x] **Boolean in control flow** — `if`, `while`, `for`, ternary `?:` all require boolean expressions.
- [x] **Generics require wrapper** — `List<Boolean>` valid, `List<boolean>` is a **compile error**.
- [x] **`Boolean.TRUE` and `Boolean.FALSE`** are static final cached constants.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Type Conversion and Casting
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Type Conversion and Casting

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Type Conversion** in Java refers to the process of automatically converting one primitive data type into another by the compiler without any explicit programmer intervention. **Type Casting** refers to the manual, explicit conversion of a data type into another by the programmer using the cast operator `(target_type)`.

In Java, every variable and expression has a strictly defined **data type**. When operands of different types are combined in a single expression, Java must reconcile them into a single, unified type before the operation can proceed. This reconciliation is governed by a well-defined set of rules codified under **JLS (Java Language Specification) §5.1 — Conversion Contexts** and **JLS §5.5 — Casting Contexts**.

There are **two fundamental categories** of type conversion:

| Category | Trigger | Synonym |
|---|---|---|
| **Implicit Type Conversion** | Compiler-driven, automatic | **Widening Primitive Conversion** |
| **Explicit Type Conversion** | Programmer-driven, manual | **Narrowing Primitive Conversion (Casting)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Type conversion applies to **primitive data types only** in this module. Reference type conversions (between classes, interfaces, and arrays) are covered separately under *Inheritance* and *Polymorphism* modules. Do not confuse the two in exams.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Liquid Pouring" Intuition

Imagine you have **two measuring cups** of different capacities sitting on a kitchen counter:

- 🪣 A **large 1-litre cup** (representing `double` — 64-bit)
- 🥛 A **small 250 ml cup** (representing `int` — 32-bit)

**Scenario A — Widening (Implicit Conversion):**
You pour the contents of the small cup into the large cup. The liquid **fits easily** with no spillage, no information loss, and you don't even need to think about it. This is exactly what Java does when converting a smaller-range type to a larger-range type (e.g., `int → long → float → double`).

**Scenario B — Narrowing (Explicit Casting):**
Now you try to pour a *full* 1-litre cup into a 250 ml cup. Without warning, liquid will **overflow and spill**. To avoid mess, you must *manually* confirm this action — this manual confirmation is the **cast operator**: `int small = (int) largeCupContents;`. Java may **truncate** the data, but it will not protest if you explicitly tell it to.

---

## 1.3 The Eight Java Primitive Types (Foundation Reference)

Java defines **eight primitive data types**. Memorize their sizes, ranges, and ordering — every conversion question depends on this hierarchy.

| Type | Size (bits) | Range | Wrapper Class |
|---|---|---|---|
| `byte` | 8 | −128 to 127 | `Byte` |
| `short` | 16 | −32,768 to 32,767 | `Short` |
| `int` | 32 | −2³¹ to 2³¹−1 | `Integer` |
| `long` | 64 | −2⁶³ to 2⁶³−1 | `Long` |
| `float` | 32 | IEEE 754 single-precision | `Float` |
| `double` | 64 | IEEE 754 double-precision | `Double` |
| `char` | 16 | `\u0000` to `\uFFFF` (unsigned) | `Character` |
| `boolean` | ~1 | `true` or `false` | `Boolean` |

> [!NOTE]
> The **default type for integer literals** in Java is `int`. The **default type for floating-point literals** is `double`. This is a frequent KTU exam pitfall — if you write `float x = 3.14;`, the compiler will throw a *possible lossy conversion* error.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

Type conversion questions test your understanding of:
1. **Compile-time safety** — Java is strongly typed; mismatches are caught early.
2. **Data integrity** — Understand *when* information is lost.
3. **Operator precedence in mixed expressions** — How `byte + byte = int`?
4. **Promotion rules** in arithmetic, comparison, and assignment contexts.
5. **The `boolean` exception** — `boolean` is **NOT** numerically compatible with any numeric type in Java (unlike C/C++).
</br>
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Primitive Type Conversion

### 🔹 Pillar 1: Widening Primitive Conversion (Implicit / Automatic)

**Definition:** A conversion from a type that has a *narrower* numeric range to a type that has a *wider* numeric range, performed automatically by the compiler.

**Characteristics (Bullet Logic):**
- ✅ Performed by the **compiler** at compile time.
- ✅ **Lossless** — no information, precision, or magnitude is lost.
- ✅ **Safe** — guaranteed by the JLS for the supported source–destination pairs.
- ✅ Does **not** require any special syntax.
- ✅ May involve a **change of representation** (e.g., `int → float` may lose precision, but is still classified as a widening conversion because the magnitude range is preserved).

**The Strict Widening Chain (Left-to-Right is the safe direction):**

$$
\text{byte} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double}
$$

Additionally, the `char` type follows:

$$
\text{char} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why `int → float` is widening even though precision may drop?** Because Java's widening rules care about *value-range coverage*, not bit-exact preservation. The `float` type can represent values up to ~3.4 × 10³⁸, far exceeding `int`'s maximum of ~2.1 × 10⁹. The conversion is therefore classified as widening, even though the `float` mantissa cannot hold all 32 bits of integer precision perfectly.

---

### 🔹 Pillar 2: Narrowing Primitive Conversion (Explicit / Casting)

**Definition:** A conversion from a type with a wider numeric range to a type with a narrower range, performed *manually* by the programmer using the cast operator.

**The Cast Operator Syntax:**

$$
\text{(target\_type) expression}
$$

**Characteristics (Bullet Logic):**
- ⚠️ Performed by the **programmer** (explicit).
- ⚠️ **Potentially lossy** — magnitude truncation, precision loss, or overflow may occur.
- ⚠️ **Unsafe by default** — the compiler will refuse without the cast operator.
- ⚠️ Requires the cast operator `(type)` placed *before* the value or expression.
- ⚠️ Uses **truncation toward zero** for floating-to-integer conversions (rounding is *not* applied).

**Narrowing Direction (Right-to-Left along the widening chain):**

$$
\text{double} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{byte}
$$

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Rules Sheet

### Table 2.1 — Conversion Compatibility Matrix

| Source → Destination | byte | short | int | long | float | double | char |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **byte**   | — | W | W | W | W | W | ✗* |
| **short**  | N | — | W | W | W | W | ✗* |
| **int**    | N | N | — | W | W | W | ✗* |
| **long**   | N | N | N | — | W | W | ✗* |
| **float**  | N | N | N | N | — | W | ✗* |
| **double** | N | N | N | N | N | — | ✗* |
| **char**   | N | N | W | W | W | W | — |

*Legend: W = Widening (implicit), N = Narrowing (explicit cast required), ✗* = Not a direct conversion; requires special handling.*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** `byte` and `char` are **NOT** directly compatible. You cannot implicitly convert `byte` to `char` or vice-versa, even though both are 8-bit and 16-bit respectively. An **explicit cast** is always required.

---

### Table 2.2 — Numeric Promotion Rules in Binary Expressions

> [!NOTE]
> When two operands of different types appear in a binary arithmetic operator (`+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%`), Java applies **Binary Numeric Promotion** before the operation.

| Operand A Type | Operand B Type | Promotion Result |
|---|---|---|
| `double` | any | `double` |
| `float`  | any non-double | `float` |
| `long`   | any non-float | `long` |
| `int`    | any smaller (`byte`, `short`, `char`) | `int` |

**Critical Rule:** If **either** operand is `double`, the result is `double`. Otherwise, if **either** is `float`, the result is `float`. Otherwise, if **either** is `long`, the result is `long`. **Otherwise, both operands are promoted to `int`.**

This last rule explains why `byte + byte = int` in Java.

---

### Table 2.3 — Assignment Context Rules

| Context | Behavior |
|---|---|
| Assigning `int` literal within `byte` range to `byte` | ✅ **Allowed** (compile-time constant folding) |
| Assigning `int` variable to `byte` | ❌ **Error** — narrowing cast required |
| Assigning `float` literal `3.14f` to `float` | ✅ Allowed (the `f` suffix makes it a `float`) |
| Assigning `double` literal `3.14` to `float` | ❌ Error — narrowing cast required |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding type conversion is essential in:
- **Embedded systems programming** where `int` sensor readings are cast to `byte` for transmission over low-bandwidth channels.
- **Graphics and image processing** where `float` color values (0.0–1.0) are converted to `int` (0–255) for pixel storage.
- **Database I/O** where SQL types like `BIGINT` map to Java `long`, and conversions must be lossless.
- **Financial computing** where `double` is deliberately cast to `BigDecimal` to avoid floating-point rounding errors.
</br>
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Evaluations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides **exhaustive, line-by-line analytical breakdowns** of type conversion scenarios. Every arithmetic transition, every promotion decision, and every compile-time decision is explicitly justified.

---

## 3.1 Analytical Case 1 — Implicit Widening in Arithmetic

**Problem Code:**

```java
byte a = 10;
byte b = 20;
byte c = a + b;  // Line 3
System.out.println(c);
```

### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1 — Variable declaration:**
The variables `a` and `b` are declared as `byte` and assigned compile-time constant integer literals (`10` and `20`) that fit within the `byte` range `[−128, 127]`. Java's **compile-time constant folding** allows this implicit narrowing.

**Step 2 — The `+` operator is applied:**
The `+` operator, when applied to operands of integral type smaller than `int`, triggers **Binary Numeric Promotion (JLS §5.6.2)**. Both `a` and `b` are individually promoted to `int` *before* the addition.

**Step 3 — Promotion result:**
The result of `a + b` is of type `int` (because neither operand is `long`, `float`, or `double`).

$$
\text{byte} \;\; a = 10 \;\xrightarrow{\text{promote}}\; \text{int} \;\; 10
$$

$$
\text{byte} \;\; b = 20 \;\xrightarrow{\text{promote}}\; \text{int} \;\; 20
$$

$$
10_{\text{int}} + 20_{\text{int}} = 30_{\text{int}}
$$

**Step 4 — Assignment to `byte`:**
We are now attempting to assign an `int` (value `30`) to a `byte`. This is a **narrowing** conversion, which is *not* implicitly allowed.

**Step 5 — Compile-time error:**

```
error: incompatible types: possible lossy conversion from int to byte
    byte c = a + b;
              ^
```

**Step 6 — Corrected code:**

```java
byte c = (byte)(a + b);
```

The explicit cast `(byte)` instructs the compiler that the programmer is aware of the potential truncation. Since `30` fits in `byte` range, no information is lost in this case.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Case 2 — Floating-to-Integer Truncation

**Problem Code:**

```java
double price = 19.99;
int rupees = (int) price;
System.out.println(rupees);
```

### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1 — The `price` variable holds a `double` value of `19.99`.** This requires 64 bits of IEEE 754 storage.

**Step 2 — The cast `(int)` is applied.** This is a narrowing conversion from `double` (64-bit) to `int` (32-bit).

**Step 3 — Truncation toward zero:** Java does **not** round `19.99` to `20`. Instead, it discards the fractional part:

$$
19.99_{\text{double}} \;\xrightarrow{\text{(int) cast}}\; 19_{\text{int}}
$$

**Step 4 — Output:** `19`

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Students frequently write `Math.round()` in their answer when truncation is asked. Truncation and rounding are **two different operations**. Read the question carefully.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Case 3 — Overflow in Narrowing

**Problem Code:**

```java
int large = 257;
byte small = (byte) large;
System.out.println(small);
```

### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1 — Source value:** `large = 257` (in `int`).

**Step 2 — Target range:** `byte` accepts values in `[−128, 127]`. The value `257` lies *outside* this range.

**Step 3 — Modulo reduction:** Java performs narrowing using **modular reduction** by the target type's size. For a `byte` (8 bits), the modulus is `256`:

$$
257 \bmod 256 = 1
$$

**Step 4 — Sign extension check:** The result `1` is positive, so the sign bit (bit 7) is `0`. No sign extension issue.

**Step 5 — Output:** `1`

> [!NOTE]
> If the value were `258`, then `258 mod 256 = 2`, and the output would be `2`. If the value were `200`, then `200 mod 256 = 200`, but the bit pattern of `200` interpreted as signed `byte` is `200 − 256 = −56`. **This is the most common source of KTU exam errors.**

---

## 3.4 Analytical Case 4 — Mixed-Type Expression with `double` Dominance

**Problem Code:**

```java
int quantity = 5;
double rate = 99.50;
double total = quantity * rate;
System.out.println(total);
```

### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1 — Identify operand types:** `quantity` is `int`, `rate` is `double`.

**Step 2 — Apply Binary Numeric Promotion:** Since one operand is `double`, the other is promoted to `double`.

$$
\text{int} \;\; 5 \;\xrightarrow{\text{promote}}\; \text{double} \;\; 5.0
$$

**Step 3 — Multiplication:**

$$
5.0_{\text{double}} \times 99.50_{\text{double}} = 497.5_{\text{double}}
$$

**Step 4 — Assignment:** `double = double` → implicit widening, no cast needed. **Output:** `497.5`

---

## 3.5 Analytical Case 5 — Compound Assignment Operator (Implicit Cast)

**Problem Code:**

```java
byte b = 50;
b = b * 2;     // Line 2
```

**vs.**

```java
byte b = 50;
b *= 2;        // Line 2
```

### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1 — For `b = b * 2;`:** The expression `b * 2` produces an `int` (binary promotion). Assigning `int` to `byte` requires an explicit cast. **Compilation error.**

**Step 2 — For `b *= 2;`:** Compound assignment operators (`*=`, `+=`, `-=`, `/=`, `%=`, `&=`, `|=`, `^=`, `<<=`, `>>=`, `>>>=`) contain an **implicit cast** equivalent to the operation's result type. The Java compiler internally translates `b *= 2;` to:

```java
b = (byte)(b * 2);
```

**Step 3 — Result:** The second snippet compiles successfully.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** This is a **favourite KTU theory question**. The implicit cast in compound assignment exists to preserve the type semantics of the left-hand operand. It does *not* apply to simple `=` assignment.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Case 6 — `char` Arithmetic with Integer Literals

**Problem Code:**

```java
char letter = 'A';        // ASCII 65
int next = letter + 1;
System.out.println(next);
char nextChar = (char) next;
System.out.println(nextChar);
```

### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1 — `letter` holds `'A'`, which is Unicode code point U+0041 = 65.**

**Step 2 — `letter + 1` triggers binary numeric promotion.** `char` is promoted to `int` (because `char` is smaller than `int`):

$$
65_{\text{int}} + 1_{\text{int}} = 66_{\text{int}}
$$

**Step 3 — `next` is assigned `66`. `System.out.println(next);` prints `66` (numeric).**

**Step 4 — `(char) next` casts `66` back to `char`, which is the Unicode character `'B'`.**

**Step 5 — `System.out.println(nextChar);` prints `B`.**

> [!NOTE]
> This demonstrates the *symmetric* behaviour of `char` with integers: `char` is essentially an **unsigned 16-bit integer type** in arithmetic contexts. This is unique to Java — `byte` and `short` are *signed*.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Case 7 — The `boolean` Isolation Rule

**Problem Code:**

```java
boolean flag = true;
int x = (int) flag;  // Compilation error
```

**Analysis:** Java **does not** allow any conversion between `boolean` and numeric types. This is a deliberate design decision by James Gosling to prevent C/C++-style bugs where `if (x = 0)` was a common typo for `if (x == 0)`. The `(int) true` cast is **illegal** in Java.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** A `boolean` value can NEVER be cast to any other type, and no other type can be cast to `boolean`. This question appears in nearly every KTU OOP model paper.
</br>
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Java Primitive Type Conversion Hierarchy

The following Mermaid diagram visualizes the **widening and narrowing relationships** between Java's numeric primitive types. Green arrows represent safe implicit widening conversions. Red arrows represent explicit narrowing casts.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["byte (8-bit)"]:::widening --> B["short (16-bit)"]
    B --> C["int (32-bit)"]
    C --> D["long (64-bit)"]
    D --> E["float (32-bit IEEE)"]
    E --> F["double (64-bit IEEE)"]

    A1["char (16-bit U)"]:::widening --> C

    F -.->|explicit cast| E
    E -.->|explicit cast| D
    D -.->|explicit cast| C
    C -.->|explicit cast| B
    B -.->|explicit cast| A

    C -.->|explicit cast| A1
    A1 -.->|explicit cast| A
    A1 -.->|explicit cast| B

    G["boolean (isolated)"]:::boolean
    G -.->|NO CONVERSION| A
    G -.->|NO CONVERSION| F
    G -.->|NO CONVERSION| A1

    classDef widening fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef boolean fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **green solid arrows** form the canonical widening chain: `byte → short → int → long → float → double`.
- The `char` type enters the chain at `int` (it can be widened to `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`).
- The **red dashed arrows** indicate narrowing (explicit cast required, potential data loss).
- The `boolean` node is **isolated** — no arrow connects it to any numeric type.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Binary Numeric Promotion Decision Flow

This flowchart models the **Binary Numeric Promotion** algorithm (JLS §5.6.2) that Java applies whenever two operands meet at a binary operator.

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1["Start: Two operands in binary expression"]:::start
    Q1{"Is either operand double?"}:::decision
    Q2{"Is either operand float?"}:::decision
    Q3{"Is either operand long?"}:::decision
    R1["Result is double"]:::result
    R2["Result is float"]:::result
    R3["Result is long"]:::result
    R4["Both promoted to int -> Result is int"]:::result

    S1 --> Q1
    Q1 -->|YES| R1
    Q1 -->|NO| Q2
    Q2 -->|YES| R2
    Q2 -->|NO| Q3
    Q3 -->|YES| R3
    Q3 -->|NO| R4

    classDef start fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
The decision cascade is evaluated top-to-bottom. The first `YES` answer determines the result type. If all three checks return `NO`, both operands are promoted to `int` (this is the famous `byte + byte = int` rule).

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Truncation Behaviour in Narrowing

```mermaid
graph LR
    SRC["Source Value (double/float/long/int)"]:::source
    CAST["Apply Cast Operator (byte/short/char/int)"]:::process
    TRUNC{"Fractional part?"}:::decision
    INT["Discard decimal portion -> Truncate toward zero"]:::truncate
    MOD{"Exceeds target range?"}:::decision
    FIT["Direct bit truncation -> Assign"]:::assign
    REDUCE["Modular Reduction by target size mod 2^n"]:::reduce
    SIGN{"Sign bit set?"}:::decision
    SIGNEXT["Sign extend to signed value"]:::signext
    OUT["Final narrowed value"]:::output

    SRC --> CAST
    CAST --> TRUNC
    TRUNC -->|YES| INT
    TRUNC -->|NO| MOD
    INT --> OUT
    MOD -->|NO| FIT
    MOD -->|YES| REDUCE
    REDUCE --> SIGN
    SIGN -->|YES| SIGNEXT
    SIGN -->|NO| OUT
    FIT --> OUT
    SIGNEXT --> OUT

    classDef source fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000
    classDef process fill:#e2e3e5,stroke:#383d41,color:#000
    classDef decision fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef truncate fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef assign fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef reduce fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#6c5ce7,color:#000
    classDef signext fill:#fab1a0,stroke:#d63031,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#55efc4,stroke:#00b894,color:#000
```
</br>

# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Q: Differentiate between implicit type conversion and explicit type casting in Java. Give one example of each.**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 Definition + 1 Example + 1 Difference):

> **Implicit Type Conversion (Widening):** Performed automatically by the compiler when a value of a smaller-range type is assigned to a larger-range type. No special syntax is required, and no data is lost.
>
> **Example:**
> ```java
> int x = 100;
> long y = x;   // implicit widening: int -> long
> ```
>
> **Explicit Type Casting (Narrowing):** Performed manually by the programmer using the cast operator `(target_type)`. Required when converting a larger-range type to a smaller-range type, and may cause data loss.
>
> **Example:**
> ```java
> double d = 9.78;
> int i = (int) d;   // explicit narrowing: double -> int, result = 9
> ```
>
> **Key Difference:** Implicit conversion is *compiler-driven* and *safe*, while explicit casting is *programmer-driven* and *potentially lossy*.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q: What is the result of the following expression? Explain using Java's type promotion rules.**

```java
byte a = 10;
byte b = 20;
int result = a + b;
```

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 Explanation + 1 Promotion Rule + 1 Output):

> The result of `a + b` is of type **`int`**, not `byte`. This is because of Java's **Binary Numeric Promotion Rule (JLS §5.6.2)**: when two operands of integral types smaller than `int` (`byte`, `short`, or `char`) are combined using a binary arithmetic operator, **both operands are promoted to `int` before the operation**.
>
> Even though both `a` and `b` are `byte`, the `+` operator triggers this promotion. The expression `a + b` therefore produces an `int` result with the value `30`. Assigning this to an `int` variable `result` is a valid implicit widening, and the program compiles and outputs `30`.

---

## 5.2 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has an **internal choice**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries **14 marks**, typically split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

#### **Q A (a): Explain the different types of type conversions in Java with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Answer:

Java supports two broad categories of primitive type conversion:

**1. Widening Primitive Conversion (Implicit):**

This conversion occurs automatically when a value of a smaller-range type is assigned to a larger-range type. It is **lossless** and **safe**.

$$
\text{byte} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double}
$$

```java
int x = 50;
double d = x;       // int -> double (widening)
System.out.println(d);   // Output: 50.0
```

**2. Narrowing Primitive Conversion (Explicit Casting):**

This conversion is performed manually using the cast operator `(type)`. It is **potentially lossy**.

```java
double d = 9.99;
int i = (int) d;    // double -> int (narrowing, truncation)
System.out.println(i);   // Output: 9
```

**3. Special Case — `char` to `int`:**

The `char` type participates in numeric promotion and can be implicitly widened to `int`. Its numeric value is its Unicode code point.

```java
char ch = 'Z';      // Unicode 90
int code = ch;      // char -> int (widening)
System.out.println(code);   // Output: 90
```

**4. The `boolean` Exception:**

Java does **not** permit any conversion between `boolean` and numeric types. Statements like `int x = (int) true;` produce a compile-time error.

**Summary Table of Valid Conversions:**

| Conversion Type | Direction | Implicit? | Data Loss? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Widening | `byte` → `short` → `int` → `long` → `float` → `double` | Yes | No |
| Narrowing | Reverse of above | No (cast required) | Possible |
| `char` → `int` | Widening | Yes | No |
| `boolean` ↔ numeric | **Not allowed** | — | — |

---

#### **Q A (b): What will be the output of the following Java code? Justify each conversion. (7 Marks)**

```java
public class TypeDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int a = 130;
        byte b = (byte) a;
        System.out.println("b = " + b);

        double price = 45.75;
        int rounded = (int) price;
        System.out.println("rounded = " + rounded);

        char letter = 'A';
        int ascii = letter + 5;
        System.out.println("ascii = " + ascii);

        long big = 100000L;
        float f = big;
        System.out.println("f = " + f);
    }
}
```

#### ✅ Model Answer:

**Line `byte b = (byte) a;` where `a = 130`:**

The value `130` exceeds the `byte` range `[−128, 127]`. The cast triggers **modular reduction** by `256` (byte size):

$$
130 \bmod 256 = 130
$$

But the bit pattern of `130` in 8 bits is `10000010`, where the leading `1` is the sign bit. Interpreted as a **signed** byte, this represents:

$$
130 - 256 = -126
$$

**Output:** `b = -126`

---

**Line `int rounded = (int) price;` where `price = 45.75`:**

The cast from `double` to `int` **truncates** the fractional part (no rounding is applied).

$$
45.75 \xrightarrow{\text{(int)}} 45
$$

**Output:** `rounded = 45`

---

**Line `int ascii = letter + 5;` where `letter = 'A'`:**

The character `'A'` has Unicode code point `65`. Binary numeric promotion converts `char` to `int`:

$$
65_{\text{int}} + 5_{\text{int}} = 70_{\text{int}}
$$

**Output:** `ascii = 70`

---

**Line `float f = big;` where `big = 100000L`:**

This is a widening conversion from `long` (64-bit integer) to `float` (32-bit IEEE 754). The value `100000` is exactly representable in `float`, so no precision is lost in this case. The `L` suffix explicitly marks the literal as `long`.

**Output:** `f = 100000.0`

---

**Final Combined Output:**

```
b = -126
rounded = 45
ascii = 70
f = 100000.0
```

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Internal Choice]**

#### **Q B (a): Explain the concept of type promotion in expressions. How does Java handle expressions involving mixed data types? Illustrate with an example. (7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Answer:

**Definition of Type Promotion:**

Type promotion, formally called **Binary Numeric Promotion**, is the process by which Java automatically converts operands of different primitive types to a common type before applying a binary operator. This ensures that each operator receives operands of compatible types.

**The Promotion Hierarchy (evaluated top-to-bottom):**

1. If either operand is of type `double`, the other is converted to `double`.
2. Else, if either operand is of type `float`, the other is converted to `float`.
3. Else, if either operand is of type `long`, the other is converted to `long`.
4. **Otherwise, both operands are promoted to `int`**, regardless of their original smaller types (`byte`, `short`, `char`).

**Illustration:**

```java
public class PromotionDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        byte x = 10;
        short y = 20;
        char z = 'A';     // 65
        double d = 2.5;

        // Mixed expression: byte + short + char * double
        double result = x + y * z - d;
        System.out.println(result);
    }
}
```

**Step-by-Step Evaluation:**

**Step 1:** Identify all sub-expressions:
- `y * z` → `short * char` → both promoted to `int` → `20 * 65 = 1300` (type `int`)
- `x + 1300` → `byte + int` → `x` promoted to `int` → `10 + 1300 = 1310` (type `int`)
- `1310 - d` → `int - double` → `1310` promoted to `double` → `1310.0 - 2.5 = 1307.5` (type `double`)

**Step 2:** Final result `1307.5` is assigned to `double` variable — valid implicit widening.

**Output:** `1307.5`

This example demonstrates that Java's promotion is **deterministic and follows JLS §5.6.2 strictly**, ensuring predictable results regardless of the order in which the programmer writes the expression.

---

#### **Q B (b): What is the difference between `b = b * 2` and `b *= 2` for a `byte` variable `b`? Explain with examples. (7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Answer:

**Scenario:** A `byte` variable `b` is declared as `byte b = 50;`.

**Case 1: `b = b * 2;`**

The expression `b * 2` applies binary numeric promotion. `b` (of type `byte`) is promoted to `int` before multiplication. The result `100` is of type `int`. Assigning an `int` to a `byte` requires an **explicit narrowing cast**.

```java
byte b = 50;
b = b * 2;    // COMPILE-TIME ERROR
```

**Error Message:**
```
error: incompatible types: possible lossy conversion from int to byte
    b = b * 2;
        ^
```

**Case 2: `b *= 2;`**

The compound assignment operator `*=` contains an **implicit cast** to the type of the left-hand operand. The compiler internally translates this to:

```java
b = (byte)(b * 2);
```

```java
byte b = 50;
b *= 2;      // VALID — compiles successfully
System.out.println(b);   // Output: 100
```

**Key Differences:**

| Feature | `b = b * 2` | `b *= 2` |
|---|---|---|
| Compilation | ❌ Error | ✅ Compiles |
| Implicit cast | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Result type | `int` | `byte` |
| Data loss risk | Compile error prevents assignment | Possible runtime truncation |

**Conclusion:** Compound assignment operators (`+=`, `-=`, `*=`, `/=`, `%=`, `&=`, `|=`, `^=`, `<<=`, `>>=`, `>>>=`) always include an implicit narrowing cast to the type of the left-hand variable, which is why `b *= 2` compiles while `b = b * 2` does not.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ⚠️ **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Confusing `float` and `double` literals:** Writing `float x = 3.14;` (should be `3.14f`) is a compilation error — this costs **1–2 marks** in Part A.
> 2. **Forgetting that `boolean` cannot be cast:** Students from a C/C++ background write `(int) true` — this is **illegal in Java** and demonstrates conceptual confusion. KTU examiners deduct **2–3 marks** for this.
> 3. **Thinking `int → float` is narrowing:** The widening chain is `int → long → float → double`. Many students incorrectly mark `int → float` as narrowing. This is a **high-frequency conceptual error**.
> 4. **Ignoring modular reduction in overflow:** When asked about `(byte) 300`, students answer `44` without showing the modular arithmetic. Examiners expect the **explicit step**: `300 mod 256 = 44`, then sign-check.
> 5. **Mixing up truncation and rounding:** `(int) 9.99` gives `9`, **not** `10`. Writing `10` in the answer shows incomplete understanding of the cast operator's semantics.

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use this section as a **last-minute revision checklist** before your KTU exam.

### ✅ Key Definitions
- **Widening Conversion:** Automatic, lossless, compiler-driven, from smaller to larger type.
- **Narrowing Conversion:** Explicit, potentially lossy, programmer-driven, uses `(type)` operator.
- **Binary Numeric Promotion:** Rule applied at binary operators to unify operand types.
- **Truncation:** Discarding the fractional part of a floating-point number when casting to an integer type (toward zero, *not* rounding).

### ✅ The Widening Chain (Memorize)
$$
\text{byte} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double}
$$

Plus: `char → int → long → float → double`

### ✅ Promotion Cascade (Top to Bottom Wins)
1. Any `double` → result is `double`
2. Any `float` → result is `float`
3. Any `long` → result is `long`
4. **Else → result is `int`** (this is the `byte + byte = int` rule)

### ✅ Special Rules
- 🔸 `boolean` is **completely isolated** from numeric types — no conversion in either direction.
- 🔸 Compound assignment operators (`+=`, `-=`, `*=`, etc.) include an **implicit cast** to the LHS type.
- 🔸 Compile-time constants within range can be assigned with implicit narrowing (e.g., `byte b = 100;`).
- 🔸 `int → float` is **widening** despite potential mantissa precision loss.
- 🔸 Narrowing to `byte`/`short` uses **modular reduction** (mod 256 / mod 65536) with **sign extension** interpretation.

### ✅ Default Literal Types
- Integer literals → `int` (use `L` suffix for `long`)
- Floating literals → `double` (use `f` suffix for `float`)

### ✅ One-Line Exam Mantras
- *"Small to large is automatic; large to small needs a cast."*
- *"When in doubt, Java promotes to `int`."*
- *"`boolean` never converts — that's a Java promise."*
- *"`*=` is `=` with a free cast."*

---

> 🎓 **End of Module 2 Notes — Type Conversion and Casting**

topics.title: Variables
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Variables

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Variable** in Java is a named memory location that holds a value of a specific data type, the contents of which can be modified during program execution. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Object Oriented Programming using Java, variables are the fundamental **data containers** that allow programmers to store, retrieve, and manipulate information within the object-oriented paradigm.

In Java, every variable must be declared with a **specific data type** before use, making Java a **strongly-typed (statically-typed) language**. The data type determines two critical properties:
1. The **size** of the memory allocated (in bytes)
2. The **range of values** that can be stored and the **operations** that can be performed on those values.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:**
> A variable is a *triple* `(name, type, value)` bound together by a *scope* and *lifetime* — all five attributes together define a Java variable. The KTU board examiner expects students to mention all three components when asked to "define a variable."

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **variable** like a **labeled storage box** in a warehouse:

| Storage Box Analogy | Java Variable Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The **box itself** (physical space) | The **memory location** in RAM |
| The **label** written on the box | The **variable name** (identifier) |
| The **shape/size** of the box (small, medium, large) | The **data type** (`int`, `double`, `char`) |
| The **item** placed inside the box | The **value** stored (`42`, `3.14`, `'A'`) |
| The **warehouse section** where the box sits | The **scope** of the variable |
| How **long** the box stays in the warehouse | The **lifetime** of the variable |

Just as a warehouse manager must know the **shape** of the box before placing items in it (you cannot fit a refrigerator into a shoebox), Java requires you to declare the **type** of a variable before storing any value in it. If you try to put a `double` (a large item) into an `int` box (a small item), the Java compiler will reject it — a feature called **type safety**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Metric Highlighted for Examiners:**
> The size of a Java variable is measured in **bytes**. The smallest unit is `byte` (**1 byte = 8 bits**), and common types range from `byte` (1 byte) to `double` (8 bytes) for primitives, while reference types typically occupy **4 bytes on 32-bit JVM** or **8 bytes on 64-bit JVM** (with compressed oop, often **4 bytes** on HotSpot JVM).

---

## Java's Three Primary Categories of Variables (KTU Module 2 Focus)

The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly identifies the following variable types in Module 2:

1. **Local Variables** — declared inside methods, constructors, or blocks.
2. **Instance Variables** (Non-Static Fields) — declared inside a class but outside any method, each object gets its own copy.
3. **Class Variables** (Static Fields) — declared with the `static` keyword, only one copy exists per class, shared by all objects.
4. **Parameters** (Formal Arguments) — variables that receive values during method invocation.

> [!TIP]
> **Examination Tip:** When the question says "types of variables in Java," the model answer must list **all four** (Local, Instance, Static, Parameters). Forgetting *Parameters* is a common mistake costing 1–2 marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Java Variable Declaration

A complete Java variable declaration has **three mandatory components** and **two optional components**:

```text
[access_modifier] [static] [final] data_type variable_name [= initializer];
```

| Component | Mandatory? | Purpose | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| `data_type` | **Yes** | Tells compiler the size & kind of value | `int`, `double`, `String` |
| `variable_name` | **Yes** | Identifier used to reference the value | `age`, `totalMarks` |
| `access_modifier` | No | Visibility (public, private, protected, default) | `private` |
| `static` | No | Marks it as a class variable (shared) | `static` |
| `final` | No | Makes it a constant (cannot be reassigned) | `final` |
| `= initializer` | No | Assigns an initial value at the time of declaration | `= 0`, `= "Hello"` |

### Declaration vs. Initialization vs. Assignment

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Expectation — Be Precise with Terminology:**
> - **Declaration** → Introducing the variable to the compiler (allocates the name binding). E.g., `int count;`
> - **Initialization** → The **first** assignment of a value. E.g., `count = 10;`
> - **Assignment** → Any subsequent change of value. E.g., `count = 20;`

---

## 2.2 The Four Variable Types — Detailed Classification

### A. Local Variables

**Definition:** Variables declared *inside* a method, constructor, or a `{}` block. They are created when the block is entered and **destroyed** when the block exits.

**Key Properties:**
- **Scope:** Limited to the block `{ }` in which they are declared.
- **Lifetime:** Exists only during method execution (on the **stack memory**).
- **Default Value:** **None** — must be explicitly initialized **before first use**; otherwise, compilation error.
- **Access Modifiers:** **Cannot** be applied to local variables.
- **Storage:** Stack memory (LIFO allocation).

```java
void calculate() {
    int x = 10;          // local variable — scope ends at }
    if (x > 5) {
        int y = 20;      // local to this if-block
        System.out.println(y);
    }
    // System.out.println(y); // ERROR — y out of scope
}
```

### B. Instance Variables (Non-Static Fields)

**Definition:** Variables declared *inside a class* but *outside any method, constructor, or block*. Each **object (instance)** of the class has its **own independent copy**.

**Key Properties:**
- **Scope:** Accessible from any method, constructor, or block of the class (subject to access modifiers).
- **Lifetime:** Created when the object is instantiated (`new`) and destroyed when the object is garbage-collected.
- **Default Value:** **Yes** — gets Java's default (e.g., `0` for int, `false` for boolean, `null` for objects).
- **Storage:** **Heap memory** (inside the object).
- **Access:** Through object reference, e.g., `obj.instanceVar`.

```java
class Student {
    String name;        // instance variable
    int rollNo;         // instance variable
}
```

### C. Class Variables (Static Fields)

**Definition:** Variables declared with the **`static`** keyword inside a class but outside any method. Only **one copy** exists regardless of how many objects are created.

**Key Properties:**
- **Scope:** Same as instance variable, but shared across all objects.
- **Lifetime:** Created when the class is loaded by the **ClassLoader** and destroyed when the class is unloaded.
- **Default Value:** **Yes** — same defaults as instance variables.
- **Storage:** **Method Area / Metaspace** of the JVM (part of the heap in modern JVMs).
- **Access:** Recommended through the class name: `ClassName.staticVar`.

```java
class Employee {
    static String companyName = "KTU Tech";  // one shared copy
    int empId;                                // each object has its own
}
```

### D. Parameters (Formal Arguments)

**Definition:** Variables listed in a method's or constructor's **signature** that receive values from the caller.

**Key Properties:**
- **Scope:** Local to the method body.
- **Lifetime:** Exist for the duration of the method call.
- **Default Value:** **None** — must be passed by the caller.
- **Storage:** Stack memory.

```java
int add(int a, int b) {  // a and b are parameters
    return a + b;
}
add(5, 3);  // 5 and 3 are arguments
```

---

## 2.3 The `final` Keyword — Making Variables Constant

When a variable is declared with the **`final`** keyword, it becomes a **constant** — its value cannot be reassigned after initialization.

| Position of `final` | What it makes constant | Example |
|---|---|---|
| `final` local variable | A local constant | `final int MAX = 100;` |
| `final` instance variable | A per-object constant | `final String id;` |
| `final` static variable | A true global constant | `static final double PI = 3.14159;` |
| `final` parameter | Method cannot modify the argument | `void print(final int x)` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Declaring a `final` variable **without** initialization → **compile-time error** ("variable x might not have been initialized"). Exception: **blank final instance variables** can be initialized inside a constructor.

---

## 2.4 Java Variable Naming Conventions (KTU Frequently Tested)

| Convention Type | Rule | Example | Valid? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Allowed characters | Letters, digits, `_`, `$`; cannot start with a digit | `marks_2024` | ✅ |
| Case sensitivity | `age` and `Age` are different | `int age; int Age;` | ✅ both legal |
| Reserved words | Cannot use Java keywords | `int class;` | ❌ |
| Camel case (variables) | Start lowercase, capitalize internal words | `studentName`, `totalMarks` | ✅ recommended |
| Pascal case (classes) | Start uppercase | `StudentRecord` | ✅ recommended |
| UPPER_SNAKE_CASE (constants) | All caps with underscores | `MAX_VALUE`, `PI` | ✅ recommended |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Rule / Formula | Default Value | Memory Location | Lifetime | Access |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Local Variable** | Declared inside method/block | ❌ No default | **Stack** | Method execution | Method/block only |
| 2 | **Instance Variable** | Inside class, outside method | `0 / false / null` | **Heap** (in object) | Object lifetime | Through object |
| 3 | **Static Variable** | `static` keyword inside class | `0 / false / null` | **Method Area** | Class loading to unloading | Through class name |
| 4 | **Parameter** | In method signature | ❌ No default | **Stack** | Method call | Method only |
| 5 | **Final Variable** | `final` keyword | Must be initialized at declaration or in constructor | Same as its type | Same as its type | Cannot reassign |

### Primitive Data Type Size Reference Table

| Data Type | Size | Range (Formula) | Default | Wrapper Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `byte` | **1 byte** | $-2^7$ to $2^7-1$ ($-128$ to $127$) | `0` | `Byte` |
| `short` | **2 bytes** | $-2^{15}$ to $2^{15}-1$ | `0` | `Short` |
| `int` | **4 bytes** | $-2^{31}$ to $2^{31}-1$ | `0` | `Integer` |
| `long` | **8 bytes** | $-2^{63}$ to $2^{63}-1$ | `0L` | `Long` |
| `float` | **4 bytes** | $\approx \pm 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ (7 digit precision) | `0.0f` | `Float` |
| `double` | **8 bytes** | $\approx \pm 1.8 \times 10^{308}$ (15 digit precision) | `0.0d` | `Double` |
| `char` | **2 bytes** | `'\u0000'` to `'\uffff'` | `'\u0000'` | `Character` |
| `boolean` | **1 bit (logical)** | `true` or `false` | `false` | `Boolean` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Range Formula for Signed Integer Types:**
> $$\text{Range} = -2^{n-1} \ \ \text{to} \ \ 2^{n-1} - 1$$
> where $n$ is the number of bits. For `int` ($n = 32$): $-2^{31}$ to $2^{31} - 1$.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Variables are not just a programming concept — they underpin **every** software engineering application:

- **Embedded Systems (IoT):** Sensor readings stored in `float` or `double` variables (e.g., temperature, pressure).
- **Web Applications:** User session data held in instance/static variables.
- **Machine Learning (Java-based):** Weights and biases stored in `double[]` arrays.
- **Banking Software:** Account balances stored in `BigDecimal` or `long` variables for precision.
- **Game Development:** Player position, score, and health stored in instance variables of a `Player` object.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Memory Size Derivation for `int` Type

The KTU board frequently asks: *"Why does `int` in Java occupy 4 bytes and what is its range?"* Below is the full derivation:

### Step 1 — Number of Bits

Java's `int` data type is **32 bits** wide.

$$n = 32 \ \text{bits}$$

### Step 2 — Convert Bits to Bytes

$$1 \ \text{byte} = 8 \ \text{bits}$$

$$\text{Size in bytes} = \frac{n}{8} = \frac{32}{8} = 4 \ \text{bytes}$$

### Step 3 — Java's `int` is Signed (Two's Complement Representation)

In two's complement, the leftmost bit is the **sign bit**:
- `0` → positive number
- `1` → negative number

This leaves $n-1 = 31$ bits to represent the magnitude.

### Step 4 — Maximum Positive Value

The largest positive integer is when all 31 magnitude bits are `1`:

$$\text{Max} = 2^{31} - 1$$

Evaluating the power:

$$2^{10} = 1024 \approx 10^3$$

$$2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \approx 10^6$$

$$2^{30} = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824$$

$$2^{31} = 2{,}147{,}483{,}648$$

Therefore:

$$2^{31} - 1 = 2{,}147{,}483{,}647$$

### Step 5 — Minimum (Most Negative) Value

In two's complement, the minimum value is $-2^{n-1}$:

$$\text{Min} = -2^{31} = -2{,}147{,}483{,}648$$

### Step 6 — Final Range

$$\boxed{\text{Range of } \texttt{int} = -2^{31} \ \text{to} \ 2^{31} - 1 = -2{,}147{,}483{,}648 \ \text{to} \ 2{,}147{,}483{,}647}$$

### Same Derivation Applied to `byte` ($n = 8$)

$$\text{Min} = -2^{7} = -128$$

$$\text{Max} = 2^{7} - 1 = 127$$

$$\boxed{\text{Range of } \texttt{byte} = -128 \ \text{to} \ 127}$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comprehensive Variable Comparison

The following table consolidates **all** distinguishing features of Java variables for analytical clarity:

| Attribute | Local Variable | Instance Variable | Static Variable | Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Declaration Location** | Inside method/constructor/block | Inside class, outside methods | Inside class with `static` keyword | In method/constructor signature |
| **Keyword** | None | None | `static` | None |
| **Access Modifier Allowed?** | ❌ No | ✅ Yes (`private`, `public`, etc.) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **`static` Allowed?** | ❌ No | ❌ No (that's what makes it static) | ✅ Yes (required) | ❌ No |
| **`final` Allowed?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Default Value** | ❌ No (compile error if uninitialized) | ✅ Yes (`0`/`false`/`null`) | ✅ Yes (`0`/`false`/`null`) | ❌ No (caller must pass) |
| **Memory Location** | **Stack** | **Heap** (inside object) | **Method Area / Metaspace** | **Stack** |
| **When Created** | When method/block is entered | When object is created (`new`) | When class is loaded by ClassLoader | When method is called |
| **When Destroyed** | When method/block exits | When object is garbage-collected | When class is unloaded | When method returns |
| **Number of Copies** | One per invocation | One per object | **One per class** (shared) | One per invocation |
| **How to Access** | By name (within scope) | `objRef.varName` or `this.varName` | `ClassName.varName` (recommended) | By name (within method) |
| **Example** | `int x = 5;` (in method) | `int age;` (in class) | `static int count;` (in class) | `void f(int a)` |

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Tracing a Variable's Lifetime

Consider the following Java program fragment (a typical KTU board question):

```java
class Demo {
    static int s = 100;          // (1) Static variable
    int i = 50;                  // (2) Instance variable

    void show() {
        int l = 10;              // (3) Local variable
        System.out.println(s + i + l);
    }
}
```

**Question asked in KTU:** *Identify the type of each variable and state its scope and lifetime.*

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Variable `s` (static):**
- Type: `int` (primitive, 4 bytes)
- Category: **Static / Class variable**
- Scope: Entire class `Demo` (with respect to access)
- Lifetime: From class loading until class unloading by JVM
- Number of copies: **One**, shared by all `Demo` objects
- Memory: **Method Area**

**Variable `i` (instance):**
- Type: `int` (primitive, 4 bytes)
- Category: **Instance variable (non-static field)**
- Scope: Entire class `Demo` (accessible from all instance methods)
- Lifetime: From `new Demo()` until the object is garbage-collected
- Number of copies: **One per object**
- Memory: **Heap** (inside the object)

**Variable `l` (local):**
- Type: `int` (primitive, 4 bytes)
- Category: **Local variable**
- Scope: Only inside the `show()` method body (between `{` and `}`)
- Lifetime: From the moment `show()` is called until `show()` returns
- Number of copies: **One per call** (a new `l` is created each call)
- Memory: **Stack**

**Execution result of `show()`:**

$$s + i + l = 100 + 50 + 10 = 160$$

---

## 3.4 Scope Resolution Rule (Shadowing)

When a **local variable** has the **same name** as an instance variable, the local variable **shadows** the instance variable within its scope.

```java
class Test {
    int x = 10;          // instance variable
    void display() {
        int x = 50;      // local variable shadows instance x
        System.out.println(x);       // prints 50
        System.out.println(this.x);  // prints 10 (accesses instance)
    }
}
```

**Resolution priority (highest to lowest):**

1. Local variable (innermost block)
2. Instance variable (via `this.x`)
3. Static variable (via `ClassName.x`)
4. Inherited variable (via `super.x`)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The compiler will *not* give an error for shadowing — it silently uses the local variable. Students often think `x` refers to the instance variable, but it actually refers to the local. To access the instance variable explicitly, the **`this`** keyword must be used.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Memory Architecture of Java Variables (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram shows how each variable type maps to its corresponding JVM memory region.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph JVM[JVM Runtime Memory]
        subgraph StackArea[Stack Memory - Thread Specific]
            LV["Local Variable: int x = 10"]
            PM["Parameter: int a"]
        end

        subgraph HeapArea[Heap Memory - Shared]
            subgraph Obj1[Object 1 - Demo]
                IV1["Instance Var: int i = 50"]
            end
            subgraph Obj2[Object 2 - Demo]
                IV2["Instance Var: int i = 70"]
            end
        end

        subgraph MetaArea[Method Area / Metaspace]
            SV["Static Variable: static int s = 100"]
            CLS["Class Metadata: Demo.class"]
        end
    end

    classDef stackStyle fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef heapStyle fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef metaStyle fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#8B008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class LV,PM stackStyle
    class IV1,IV2,Obj1,Obj2 heapStyle
    class SV,CLS metaStyle
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Each thread gets its **own Stack** (orange region) → local variables and parameters live here.
- All threads share the **Heap** (blue region) → objects and their instance variables are stored here. Two `Demo` objects have two separate `i` values (50 and 70).
- The **Method Area** (purple region) holds the **single shared** `s = 100`, regardless of how many objects exist.

---

## 4.2 Decision Flow — How to Classify a Variable

Use this Mermaid flowchart to determine the type of any Java variable you encounter:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Examine the variable] --> B{Is the keyword static present?}
    B -- Yes --> C{Is it declared inside a method or block?}
    B -- No --> D{Is it declared inside a method, constructor, or block?}
    C -- Yes --> E[Compilation Error: static not allowed in local scope]
    C -- No --> F[It is a STATIC CLASS VARIABLE]
    D -- Yes --> G{Is it in the method signature parentheses?}
    D -- No --> H[It is an INSTANCE VARIABLE]
    G -- Yes --> I[It is a PARAMETER Formal Argument]
    G -- No --> J[It is a LOCAL VARIABLE]

    classDef decision fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef error fill:#FF6347,stroke:#B22222,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF

    class B,D,G decision
    class F,H,I,J result
    class E error
```

**Application of the flowchart:**
- `static int counter;` in class → static keyword present (Yes), not in method (No) → **Static Variable**
- `int age;` in class → no static, not in method → **Instance Variable**
- `int x = 5;` inside `void m()` → no static, in method → check signature? No → **Local Variable**
- `int a` in `void m(int a)` → no static, in method, in signature → **Parameter**

---

## 4.3 Scope & Lifetime Timeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following diagram illustrates the **creation** and **destruction** events for each variable type during a program's execution.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Class Loaded by JVM] --> A1[Static Variable Created in Method Area]
    A1 --> A2[Static Variable Exists for Entire Program Duration]
    A2 --> A3[Program Ends or Class Unloaded: Static Destroyed]

    B0[Object Created via new] --> B1[Instance Variable Created in Heap]
    B1 --> B2[Instance Variable Exists While Object is Reachable]
    B2 --> B3[Garbage Collection: Instance Destroyed]

    C0[Method or Block Entered] --> C1[Local Variable Allocated on Stack]
    C1 --> C2[Local Variable Exists During Method Execution]
    C2 --> C3[Method Returns: Stack Frame Popped, Local Destroyed]

    D0[Method Called with Arguments] --> D1[Parameters Receive Values on Stack]
    D1 --> D2[Parameters Used Inside Method Body]
    D2 --> D3[Method Returns: Parameters Destroyed]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for the diagram:** All four lifecycles are **independent**. Statics outlive instances, instances outlive locals, and parameters are bound tightly to the call-stack frame. This is why modifying a `static` variable in one method affects **all** other methods in the program.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: Define a variable in Java. List the different types of variables in Java with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
A variable in Java is a named memory location that holds a value of a specific data type. The value can be changed during program execution. Every variable must be declared with a data type before use, making Java a strongly-typed language.

**Types (2 Marks):**
Java has four types of variables:

1. **Local Variable** — Declared inside a method or block. E.g., `int x = 10;` inside `void m(){}`
2. **Instance Variable** — Declared inside a class but outside any method. Each object gets its own copy. E.g., `int rollNo;` in class `Student`.
3. **Static Variable (Class Variable)** — Declared with `static` keyword. Only one shared copy per class. E.g., `static String collegeName;`
4. **Parameter (Formal Argument)** — Declared in method signature. E.g., `int a` in `void set(int a){}`

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: What is the difference between instance variables and static variables in Java? Explain with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Instance Variable | Static Variable |
|---|---|---|
| Keyword | No `static` | Declared with `static` |
| Copies | One per object | One per class (shared) |
| Memory | Heap (inside object) | Method Area |
| Access | Through object reference | Through class name (recommended) |
| Created | When object is instantiated | When class is loaded |
| Default | Yes (`0`/`false`/`null`) | Yes (`0`/`false`/`null`) |

**Example (1 Mark):**
```java
class Counter {
    int instanceCount = 0;          // instance — each object has its own
    static int staticCount = 0;     // static — shared by all objects
}
```
If two objects `c1` and `c2` are created, `c1.instanceCount` and `c2.instanceCount` are independent, but `c1.staticCount` and `c2.staticCount` refer to the **same** memory location.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Q: (a)** Explain the different types of variables in Java with suitable examples. Discuss their scope, lifetime, and default values. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate the use of instance variables, static variables, and local variables. Explain the output of the program. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks

The four types of variables in Java are:

**1. Local Variables (2 Marks):**
- Declared inside a method, constructor, or block `{ }`.
- **Scope:** Limited to the block in which they are declared.
- **Lifetime:** Exists only during the execution of the method/block.
- **Default Value:** **No default value** — must be initialized explicitly before use, otherwise the compiler throws an error: *"variable x might not have been initialized."*
- **Storage:** Stack memory.

```java
void display() {
    int a = 5;          // local variable
    for (int i = 0; i < 3; i++) {
        a = a + i;
    }
    System.out.println(a);  // valid
    // System.out.println(i); // ERROR — i out of scope
}
```

**2. Instance Variables (2 Marks):**
- Declared inside a class but outside any method/block.
- **Scope:** Throughout the class, subject to access modifiers.
- **Lifetime:** From object creation (`new`) until the object is garbage-collected.
- **Default Value:** Provided by Java — `0` for numeric types, `false` for boolean, `null` for reference types.
- **Storage:** Heap memory (inside the object).

```java
class Student {
    String name;     // default: null
    int marks;       // default: 0
}
```

**3. Static (Class) Variables (2 Marks):**
- Declared with the `static` modifier inside a class.
- **Scope:** Same as instance variable, but accessible without creating an object.
- **Lifetime:** From class loading by the ClassLoader until class unloading.
- **Default Value:** Same as instance variables.
- **Storage:** Method Area / Metaspace.
- **Key feature:** A single copy is shared by **all** objects of the class.

```java
class Employee {
    static String company = "KTU Pvt Ltd";
}
```

**4. Parameters (1 Mark):**
- Variables listed in a method's or constructor's signature.
- **Scope:** Local to the method body.
- **Lifetime:** Duration of the method call.
- **Default Value:** None — must be supplied by the caller.
- **Storage:** Stack memory.

```java
int square(int n) {   // n is a parameter
    return n * n;
}
```

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks

**Java Program (4 Marks):**

```java
class VariableDemo {
    // Static (class) variable — single shared copy
    static int staticCount = 0;

    // Instance variable — each object has its own copy
    int instanceCount = 0;

    // Constructor increments both
    VariableDemo() {
        staticCount++;
        instanceCount++;
    }

    void display(String label) {
        int localVar = 100;   // local variable
        System.out.println(label
            + " | staticCount = " + staticCount
            + " | instanceCount = " + instanceCount
            + " | localVar = " + localVar);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
```
