```java
                    System.out.println("Found " + target + " at [" + i + "][" + j + "]");
                    found = true;
                    break outer;   // Exits BOTH loops
                }
            }
        }
        if (!found) {
            System.out.println(target + " not found in the matrix");
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:** `Found 5 at [1][1]`

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Missing semicolon after `do-while` condition** — *very common*. Always write `} while (cond);` with the semicolon.
> 2. **Using enhanced `for` when index is required** — students try to access the index that simply does not exist in for-each. Examiners deduct 2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing `break` with `continue`** — `break` exits the loop; `continue` skips to the next iteration. Writing the wrong verb in explanation loses 1-2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting labeled `break` in nested loops** — the program still runs, but exits only the inner loop. Logic is wrong but syntactically valid. Examiners catch this and deduct heavily.
> 5. **Infinite loop due to missing update** in `while` — KTU strictly penalizes this if the program does not terminate as required.
> 6. **Off-by-one errors** in `for` loop bounds (using `<` instead of `<=`) — a perennial favorite. Always trace one extra iteration in your answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap and Important Things to Remember 📋

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Iteration & Jump Statements**

- ⭐ The `for` loop is **entry-controlled**, with three parts: **init**, **condition**, **update** — all separated by semicolons.
- ⭐ The `while` loop is **entry-controlled** with a single **condition**; the body may execute **zero** times.
- ⭐ The `do-while` loop is **exit-controlled**; the body executes **at least once**. The trailing **semicolon is mandatory**.
- ⭐ The **enhanced `for` loop** (Java 5+) works only on **arrays** and `Iterable` collections. It is **read-only** and gives **no index** access.
- ⭐ `break` → exits the loop entirely. `continue` → skips the rest of the **current iteration** only.
- ⭐ **Labeled `break` / `continue`** is the only way to control an **outer loop** from inside a **nested loop**.
- ⭐ `return` exits the **current method**, not just the loop — a subtle but important distinction.
- ⭐ Every `for` loop can be rewritten as a `while` loop, but not every `while` loop can be cleanly written as a `for` loop.
- ⭐ Closed-form formulas for common loops: $\sum_{i=1}^{n} i = \frac{n(n+1)}{2}$ and $n! = \prod_{i=1}^{n} i$.
- ⭐ Time complexity of a single loop over `n` items: **$O(n)$**; nested loop: **$O(n^2)$**.
- ⭐ In KTU exams, **always include a trace table** for any loop output question — it almost always guarantees full marks.
- ⭐ Avoid **infinite loops**: ensure the update expression in `for` / `while` eventually makes the condition `false`.
- ⭐ Remember: the `for` loop's initialization variable's scope is **limited to the loop block** in Java.
- ⭐ **Common syntax traps:** misplaced semicolons, missing braces, using `=` instead of `==` in conditions, and omitting the update expression (which is legal but creates an infinite loop).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object Oriented Programming in Java - Class Fundamentals
Declaring Objects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Declaring Objects

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In **Object Oriented Programming using Java**, **Declaring Objects** is the foundational syntactic and semantic operation by which a programmer introduces an *object reference variable* into the program's namespace and subsequently binds that reference to a memory-resident *object instance* created from a user-defined or library-defined **class** blueprint.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PBEOT404, Module 2) syllabus, an object is formally defined as a **runtime entity** that encapsulates **state (fields/instance variables)** and **behavior (methods)**. The act of *declaring* an object in Java is a **two-stage process**:
1. **Declaration of the reference variable** (compile-time step).
2. **Instantiation (memory allocation) of the object** using the `new` keyword (run-time step).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Anchor:** An *object* in Java is an instance of a class. Declaring an object therefore means **creating a reference variable** of a class type and **allocating physical memory** for the class instance via the `new` operator.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Remote Control & Television" Model

To make the concept immediately intuitive, let us use a real-world analogy:

| Real-World Element | Java Equivalent | Role |
|---|---|---|
| **Television Set (Physical Device)** | Object (Heap memory instance) | Holds actual data (channels, volume) and behavior (play, mute) |
| **Remote Control (Pointer to TV)** | Reference Variable (Stack memory) | Used to *control* the TV; it does not contain the TV itself |
| **TV Manufacturing Blueprint** | Class Definition | The template from which the TV is built |
| **Pressing the Power Button on Remote** | `.` (dot) Operator | Accesses state/behaviors of the object via the reference |
| **Buying a Remote without a TV** | Declared but uninitialized reference | `null` reference — the remote exists but points to nothing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Beginners:** A Java reference variable is **NOT** the object itself — it is merely a *handle* or *pointer* (managed, safe pointer) through which the object in memory is manipulated. This is one of the most frequently tested distinctions in KTU exams.

---

## 1.3 The Generic Declaration Syntax

The canonical Java syntax for declaring an object is shown below:

```java
ClassName objectReferenceName = new ClassName(argument_list);
```

This single line actually performs **three distinct operations** at different lifecycle stages:
1. **Declaration** — `ClassName objectReferenceName;` → tells the compiler a reference of type `ClassName` will exist.
2. **Instantiation** — `new ClassName(...)` → allocates heap memory and invokes the constructor.
3. **Binding/Assignment** — `=` → links the reference variable (stack) to the newly created object (heap).

> [!TIP]
> **Why the distinction matters in KTU exams:** Many students incorrectly say *"I created an object"* when they have only declared a reference. The **creation (instantiation)** happens ONLY at the `new` keyword. Everything before the `new` is just a reference declaration.

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Java Specifics (Bold Constants/Facts)

- **Default value of a reference variable (instance/field):** `null`.
- **Default value of a local reference variable:** **uninitialized** (compile-time error if used before assignment).
- **Memory location of the reference:** **Stack memory** (for local variables and method parameters).
- **Memory location of the object:** **Heap memory** (where all Java objects reside via Garbage-Collection-managed allocation).
- **Size of an uninitialized reference on a 64-bit JVM:** **8 bytes** (compressed oops may make it 4 bytes in some JVMs).
- **Garbage Collector eligibility:** An object becomes eligible for GC when **no live reference** points to it.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two-Stage Process — A Structured Breakdown

### Stage 1: Declaration of the Reference Variable

**What happens:** The compiler reserves a named slot in the current stack frame. The variable's *static type* is set to the class name.

```java
Student s1;          // s1 is a reference variable of type Student
Box   myBox;         // myBox is a reference variable of type Box
```

**Key characteristics at this stage:**
- The reference is **declared but not initialized** (for local variables).
- Its current value is `null` (if it is an instance field of an object) or **undefined** (if a local variable, causing compile-time error upon access).
- **No object exists in memory yet.** The variable is a placeholder.

### Stage 2: Instantiation & Initialization via `new`

**What happens:** The `new` keyword triggers a sequence of JVM-level operations:

1. **Class loading check** — JVM verifies the `ClassName` class is loaded in the Method Area.
2. **Heap memory allocation** — Sufficient contiguous bytes are reserved in the heap for all instance fields (plus object header, typically 12–16 bytes).
3. **Default initialization** — All instance fields are assigned JVM default values (`0`, `0.0`, `false`, `null`).
4. **Constructor invocation** — The appropriate constructor (matched by argument list) is executed.
5. **Return address** — A reference (memory address) to the newly created object is returned.

```java
s1 = new Student();          // object is created, reference s1 now points to it
myBox = new Box(10, 20, 30); // parameterized constructor invoked
```

### Stage 3: Combined Single-Line Form (Idiomatic Java)

The two stages are usually combined for brevity and clarity:

```java
Student s1 = new Student();
```

This is the **idiomatic KTU-expected style** for object declaration.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Exists | Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Declaration separated from `new` | Allows **polymorphic reference assignment** and **lazy initialization** | Decouples compile-time type from runtime object |
| `new` operator required (unlike C++) | Enforces **automatic memory management** | Prevents dangling pointers, memory leaks |
| Reference stored on stack | Fast access via CPU registers; auto-cleanup on method exit | Stack-allocated references are cheap (8 bytes) |
| Object stored on heap | Objects have **dynamic lifetime** (may outlive the method) | Enables shared access, GC, and persistence across scopes |
| `null` default | Provides a **safe sentinel** to detect uninitialized state | Reduces undefined behavior; forces explicit initialization |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Default / Value | Unit / Location |
|---|---|---|---|
| Declaration syntax | `ClassName refVar;` | `refVar` is uninitialized (local) or `null` (field) | Stack memory |
| Instantiation syntax | `refVar = new ClassName(args);` | Object exists in heap | Heap memory |
| Combined form | `ClassName refVar = new ClassName(args);` | Both steps combined | Mixed (Stack + Heap) |
| Reference size (64-bit JVM) | $S_{ref} = 8 \text{ bytes}$ (or 4 with compressed oops) | Fixed | Bytes |
| Object header size | $S_{header} \approx 12 \text{ to } 16 \text{ bytes}$ | Always allocated | Bytes |
| Default of `int` field | $0$ | Numeric zero | N/A |
| Default of `boolean` field | $false$ | Logical false | N/A |
| Default of `Object` reference field | $null$ | No object bound | N/A |
| Access via reference | `refVar.memberName` | Dot operator | Compile-time checked |
| GC eligibility condition | $\text{reachable}(obj) = \text{false}$ | No live references | Heap |
| Total memory per object (approx.) | $S_{total} = S_{header} + \sum S_{fields} + \text{padding}$ | Aligned to 8 bytes | Bytes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory math example:** A class with one `int` (4 bytes) and one reference (8 bytes) occupies roughly $12 \text{ (header)} + 4 + 8 = 24$ bytes, padded to the next multiple of 8 → **24 bytes** on most JVMs.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concept of *Declaring Objects* is foundational to virtually every Java-based engineering system:

- **Enterprise Java (Spring/Hibernate):** Every `@Entity` bean, controller, and service is declared and instantiated either via `new`, dependency injection, or reflection.
- **Android Development:** `Activity`, `Fragment`, `View` objects are declared, inflated, and bound to layouts.
- **Embedded & IoT (Java ME / GraalVM Native):** Sensor data objects are declared dynamically in heap memory.
- **Big Data (Hadoop/Spark):** `RDD`, `DataFrame` objects are declared with explicit class types and instantiated via factory methods.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Memory Model of a Declared Object

Let us analyze the following canonical Java program step-by-step, with explicit memory transitions.

```java
class Student {
    String name;
    int rollNo;
    Student(String n, int r) {
        name = n;
        rollNo = r;
    }
}

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1;                          // Step 1
        s1 = new Student("Arjun", 101);      // Step 2
        Student s2 = new Student("Meera", 102); // Step 3
    }
}
```

### Step 1: Declaration Only — `Student s1;`

$$
\text{Stack (main frame)} = \{ \, s1 : \text{uninitialized} \, \}
$$

$$
\text{Heap} = \emptyset \quad \text{(no object exists)}
$$

- The compiler allocates **8 bytes** on the stack for the reference `s1`.
- The variable `s1` is **uninitialized** (local variable rule in Java).
- If we attempt `System.out.println(s1.name);` here, the compiler throws:
  `error: variable s1 might not have been initialized`.

### Step 2: Instantiation — `s1 = new Student("Arjun", 101);`

The `new` operator executes the following sub-steps **in order**:

$$
\text{(a) Class load check} : \text{Is } Student.class \text{ in Method Area?}
$$

If not, the **ClassLoader subsystem** loads the `.class` bytecode file.

$$
\text{(b) Heap allocation} : \text{Reserve} \approx 12 + 4 + 4 = 20 \text{ bytes, padded to } 24 \text{ bytes.}
$$

$$
\text{(c) Default init} : name \leftarrow null, \ rollNo \leftarrow 0
$$

$$
\text{(d) Constructor exec} : name \leftarrow \text{"Arjun"}, \ rollNo \leftarrow 101
$$

$$
\text{(e) Return ref} : s1 \leftarrow \text{heap address, e.g., } 0x7A2B
$$

**Final state after Step 2:**

$$
\text{Stack} = \{ s1 : 0x7A2B \}, \quad \text{Heap} = \{ \ 0x7A2B : \{ name="Arjun", \ rollNo=101 \} \ \}
$$

### Step 3: Combined Declaration + Instantiation

$$
\text{Stack} = \{ s1 : 0x7A2B, \ s2 : 0x7B4C \}
$$

$$
\text{Heap} = \{ 0x7A2B : \{ name="Arjun", \ rollNo=101 \},\ 0x7B4C : \{ name="Meera", \ rollNo=102 \} \}
$$

### Analytical Matrix Summary

| Step | Statement | Compiler Action | JVM Runtime Action | Stack State | Heap State |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `Student s1;` | Reserves 8 bytes for `s1` | None | `s1` uninitialized | Empty |
| 2 | `s1 = new Student("Arjun", 101);` | None (already declared) | Allocates 24 bytes, default-init, calls ctor | `s1 → 0x7A2B` | Object at `0x7A2B` |
| 3 | `Student s2 = new Student("Meera", 102);` | Reserves 8 bytes for `s2` | Allocates 24 bytes, default-init, calls ctor | `s1 → 0x7A2B`, `s2 → 0x7B4C` | Two objects |

---

## 3.2 The Three Forms of Object Declaration (Derived Categories)

Java allows three syntactically distinct but semantically equivalent declaration patterns. Each is a *transformation* of the fundamental rule.

### Form 1: Separated Declaration & Instantiation

```java
ClassName ref;
ref = new ClassName(args);
```

**Equivalence:** $D_{sep} \equiv (\text{decl}) \circ (\text{instantiate})$

### Form 2: Combined Single Statement (Idiomatic)

```java
ClassName ref = new ClassName(args);
```

**Equivalence:** $D_{com} \equiv D_{sep} \ \text{(collapsed into one line)}$

### Form 3: Anonymous Object Declaration

```java
new ClassName(args).methodCall();
```

**Equivalence:** $D_{anon} \equiv \text{instantiate} \circ \text{use} \ \text{(no persistent reference)}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** *"What is the difference between `Student s = new Student();` and `new Student();`?"* — In the first, the object is **reachable** through `s`; in the second, the object is **immediately eligible for garbage collection** after the statement ends (if not stored anywhere).

---

## 3.3 Reference Assignment & Aliasing — Full Derivation

```java
Student s1 = new Student("Arjun", 101);
Student s2;
s2 = s1;                              // Reference copy (NOT object copy)
```

**Step-by-step transformation:**

$$
\text{Stack} = \{ s1 : 0x7A2B, \ s2 : \text{uninitialized} \}
$$

$$
\Downarrow \ \text{(assignment } s2 = s1\text{)} \ \Downarrow
$$

$$
\text{Stack} = \{ s1 : 0x7A2B, \ s2 : 0x7A2B \}
$$

$$
\text{Heap} = \{ 0x7A2B : \{ name="Arjun", \ rollNo=101 \} \} \quad \text{(still ONE object)}
$$

**Algebraic representation of aliasing:**

$$
\text{pointsTo}(s1) = \text{pointsTo}(s2) = 0x7A2B
$$

$$
\forall m \in \text{members} : s1.m = s2.m
$$

**Effect:** Modifying `s1.rollNo = 999;` also reflects in `s2.rollNo` — because both references point to the *same* object. This is called **aliasing** and is a critical source of bugs in OOP.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Believing `s2 = s1;` *copies* the object. It does **not**. It only copies the **reference**. To copy the object, you must use `clone()` or a copy constructor.

---

## 3.4 Null Reference — Formal Definition

$$
\text{refVar} = \text{null} \iff \text{pointsTo}(\text{refVar}) = \text{NULL\_POINTER}
$$

A `null` reference is one that does not point to any object in the heap. Accessing a member via a `null` reference throws `NullPointerException` at runtime.

```java
Student s = null;
System.out.println(s.name);   // Runtime error: NullPointerException
```

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Memory Architecture of Declared Objects (Block-Level Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how the **Stack**, **Heap**, and **Method Area** interact during object declaration and instantiation.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STK["STACK MEMORY - Main Method Frame"]
        s1["s1 : reference = 0x7A2B"]
        s2["s2 : reference = 0x7B4C"]
        s3["s3 : reference = null"]
    end

    subgraph HEP["HEAP MEMORY - Object Instances"]
        obj1["0x7A2B - Student Object"]
        n1["name = Arjun"]
        r1["rollNo = 101"]
        obj2["0x7B4C - Student Object"]
        n2["name = Meera"]
        r2["rollNo = 102"]
    end

    subgraph MA["METHOD AREA - Class Metadata"]
        cls["Student.class bytecode"]
        fld["Field descriptors: name:String, rollNo:int"]
        ctr["Constructor: Student(String, int)"]
    end

    s1 -.points to.-> obj1
    s2 -.points to.-> obj2
    obj1 --> n1
    obj1 --> r1
    obj2 --> n2
    obj2 --> r2
    cls --> fld
    cls --> ctr
```

---

## 4.2 Lifecycle Flow of an Object Declaration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["START: Source code: Student s = new Student"]
    B["STEP 1: Compiler parses statement"]
    C{"Class loaded in Method Area?"}
    D["ClassLoader loads Student.class"]
    E["STEP 2: Allocate heap memory ~24 bytes"]
    F["STEP 3: Default initialization name=null, rollNo=0"]
    G["STEP 4: Invoke constructor Student(String,int)"]
    H["STEP 5: Assign field values name=Arjun, rollNo=101"]
    I["STEP 6: Return heap address to s"]
    J["END: Object live, s bound to 0x7A2B"]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C -->|No| D
    D --> E
    C -->|Yes| E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J
```

---

## 4.3 Reference Assignment & Aliasing Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE: s2 = s1"]
        s1a["s1 -> 0x7A2B"]
        s2a["s2 = null"]
        objA["0x7A2B: Student(name=Arjun, rollNo=101)"]
    end

    subgraph AFTER["AFTER: s2 = s1"]
        s1b["s1 -> 0x7A2B"]
        s2b["s2 -> 0x7A2B"]
        objB["0x7A2B: Student(name=Arjun, rollNo=101)"]
    end

    s1a -.-> objA
    s2a -.-> objA
    s1b -.-> objB
    s2b -.-> objB
```

---

## 4.4 Declared vs Instantiated — Visual Discrimination

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Programming Statement"]
    A --> B["Student s1;"]
    A --> C["s1 = new Student();"]
    A --> D["Student s2 = new Student();"]

    B --> B1["Reference declared, no object in heap"]
    C --> C1["Object created, bound to existing reference"]
    D --> D1["Reference declared AND object created in one line"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Define an object in Java. What is the difference between declaring a reference variable and instantiating an object?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **object** in Java is a runtime instance of a class that encapsulates state (instance variables) and behavior (methods) in heap memory.

| Aspect | Declaration | Instantiation |
|---|---|---|
| Keyword/Operator | Just a type and identifier | Uses `new` keyword |
| Memory location | Stack (reference variable) | Heap (actual object) |
| When it happens | Compile time | Run time |
| Result | A reference variable (may be `null`) | A fully constructed object in heap |

**Example:**
```java
Student s;              // declaration only — s is uninitialized
s = new Student();      // instantiation — object now exists in heap
```
*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the difference with example)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: What is the role of the `new` keyword in Java? What happens if you declare a reference variable but do not use `new`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **`new` keyword** performs three essential operations:
1. **Allocates memory** in the heap for the new object.
2. **Invokes the appropriate constructor** of the class to initialize fields.
3. **Returns a reference** (memory address) to the newly created object, which is then assigned to the reference variable.

If `new` is **not used**, no object is created in memory. The reference variable remains `null` (or uninitialized if local). Attempting to access members through such a reference results in a compile-time error (uninitialized local) or a runtime **`NullPointerException`** (if it is `null`).

```java
Student s;                       // valid, but s is uninitialized
s.name = "Arjun";                // COMPILE-TIME ERROR
```
*(1 mark for `new` role, 1 mark for behavior without `new`, 1 mark for the example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the process of object creation in Java with a suitable diagram. Describe the role of stack and heap memory during object declaration.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program to demonstrate the difference between *primitive variable declaration* and *object reference declaration*. Show what happens when two references point to the same object (aliasing).

---

### **Model Answer — Question A**

#### Part (a) — Object Creation Process [7 Marks]

Object creation in Java is a **two-stage process**:

**Stage 1: Declaration of Reference Variable**
```java
Box myBox;
```
- The compiler reserves an **8-byte** slot in the **stack** memory.
- The variable `myBox` is uninitialized (local) or `null` (field).
- **No heap allocation occurs at this stage.** *(1 mark)*

**Stage 2: Instantiation using `new`**
```java
myBox = new Box();
```
The `new` operator performs the following sub-steps:
1. **Class Loading:** JVM checks Method Area for `Box.class`. If absent, the ClassLoader loads it. *(1 mark)*
2. **Memory Allocation:** A block of heap memory (approx. 24 bytes for a typical class) is reserved. *(1 mark)*
3. **Default Initialization:** All instance fields are set to JVM defaults (`0`, `false`, `null`). *(1 mark)*
4. **Constructor Invocation:** The no-arg or matching constructor is called to perform explicit initialization. *(1 mark)*
5. **Reference Return:** The heap address of the object is returned and stored in `myBox`. *(1 mark)*

**Diagram (textual representation — 1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{
\begin{array}{l}
\textbf{Stack (main method):} \\
\quad myBox \longrightarrow 0x4A2F \\[6pt]
\textbf{Heap:} \\
\quad 0x4A2F : \ \text{Box object (length=0, width=0, height=0)}
\end{array}
}
$$

The reference `myBox` (stack) **points to** the object at `0x4A2F` (heap). Access to fields/methods is performed via the `.` operator, which the JVM translates to a memory dereference through the reference.

#### Part (b) — Primitive vs Reference + Aliasing [7 Marks]

**Java Program (5 marks for code, 2 marks for explanation):**

```java
class Box {
    int length, width;

    Box(int l, int w) {
        length = l;
        width = w;
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println("Length: " + length + ", Width: " + width);
    }
}

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Primitive declaration
        int a = 10;
        int b = a;        // b is a COPY of a
        b = 20;
        System.out.println("a = " + a + ", b = " + b);  // a=10, b=20

        // Object reference declaration
        Box box1 = new Box(5, 7);
        Box box2 = box1;   // box2 is a COPY OF REFERENCE, not object
        box2.length = 99;

        System.out.print("box1: "); box1.display();
        System.out.print("box2: "); box2.display();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
a = 10, b = 20
box1: Length: 99, Width: 7
box2: Length: 99, Width: 7
```

**Explanation (2 marks):**
- For primitives (`int a = 10; int b = a;`), `b` receives an **independent copy** of the value. Changing `b` does not affect `a`.
- For references (`Box box1 = new Box(...); Box box2 = box1;`), `box2` receives a **copy of the reference**, not the object. Both `box1` and `box2` now **point to the same object** in the heap (aliasing). Therefore, modifying `box2.length` also changes `box1.length`.

---

### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is the role of constructors in object creation? Differentiate between **default constructor** and **parameterized constructor** with a Java program that creates objects using both.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **anonymous objects** in Java. When would a programmer prefer to use an anonymous object over a named reference? Provide an example demonstrating garbage collection eligibility of anonymous objects.

---

### **Model Answer — Question B**

#### Part (a) — Constructors in Object Creation [7 Marks]

A **constructor** is a special member method automatically invoked at the time of object instantiation to initialize the newly created object's state. *(1 mark)*

**Differences:**

| Aspect | Default Constructor | Parameterized Constructor |
|---|---|---|
| Arguments | Takes **no arguments** | Takes **one or more arguments** |
| When provided | Compiler-generated if no constructor is defined | Programmer-defined explicitly |
| Purpose | Initialize fields to default or fixed values | Initialize fields with caller-supplied values |
| Invocation | `new ClassName();` | `new ClassName(arg1, arg2, ...);` |

*(2 marks for the table)*

**Java Program (4 marks):**

```java
class Employee {
    String name;
    int id;
    double salary;

    // Default constructor
    Employee() {
        name = "Unknown";
        id = 0;
        salary = 0.0;
    }

    // Parameterized constructor
    Employee(String n, int i, double s) {
        name = n;
        id = i;
        salary = s;
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", ID: " + id + ", Salary: " + salary);
    }
}

public class TestEmployee {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Employee e1 = new Employee();                              // default ctor
        Employee e2 = new Employee("Arjun", 101, 55000.00);        // parameterized ctor
        e1.display();
        e2.display();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Name: Unknown, ID: 0, Salary: 0.0
Name: Arjun, ID: 101, Salary: 55000.0
```

#### Part (b) — Anonymous Objects [7 Marks]

An **anonymous object** is an object that is **instantiated but not assigned to any reference variable**. It exists only for the duration of the single statement in which it is created. *(2 marks)*

**Syntax:**
```java
new ClassName(args).methodCall();
```

**When to use anonymous objects (2 marks):**
- When the object is needed **only once** and does not need to be referenced later.
- For **chained method calls** (fluent interfaces, builders).
- For **immediate destruction** to free heap memory (eligible for GC right after the statement).

**Example Program (3 marks):**

```java
class Greeting {
    Greeting(String msg) {
        System.out.println("Constructor called: " + msg);
    }
    void sayHello() {
        System.out.println("Hello from object!");
    }

    @Override
    protected void finalize() throws Throwable {
        System.out.println("Garbage collected: anonymous object destroyed.");
    }
}

public class TestAnon {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Named object — persists in memory
        Greeting g = new Greeting("Named");
        g.sayHello();

        // Anonymous object — eligible for GC after this line
        new Greeting("Anonymous").sayHello();

        System.gc();   // request garbage collection
    }
}
```

**Output (typical):**
```
Constructor called: Named
Hello from object!
Constructor called: Anonymous
Hello from object!
Garbage collected: anonymous object destroyed.
```

The anonymous object created in `new Greeting("Anonymous").sayHello();` is **immediately eligible for garbage collection** once the statement completes, because no reference variable holds its address.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS on "Declaring Objects" questions:**
> 1. **Confusing reference with object** — Saying *"I created an object"* when only a reference is declared. **(−1 mark)**
> 2. **Ignoring the two-stage nature** — Writing the declaration and instantiation as a single opaque step instead of explaining `decl → new → assign`. **(−2 marks)**
> 3. **No memory diagram** — Part (a) of Question A specifically asks for a diagram; students who skip it lose up to **2 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing aliasing with copying** — In aliasing programs, students forget to show that *both* references print the same modified value. **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **Forgetting default values** — Not mentioning that `int → 0`, `boolean → false`, `Object ref → null`. **(−1 mark)**
> 6. **Mixing up stack and heap** — Saying the object is stored in the stack (incorrect). **(−1 mark)**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] An **object** is a runtime instance of a class; it lives in **heap memory**.
- [x] Declaring an object = **declaring a reference variable** (stack) + **instantiating with `new`** (heap).
- [x] The `new` keyword performs: class-load check → memory allocation → default init → constructor call → return reference.
- [x] A **reference variable** is *not* the object — it is a safe pointer (handle) to the object.
- [x] Default value of an uninitialized instance reference field is **`null`**; for local variables, it is **uninitialized** (compile error).
- [x] **Aliasing** occurs when two references point to the same object — `s2 = s1` does NOT copy the object.
- [x] An **anonymous object** (`new ClassName().method();`) is immediately eligible for garbage collection.
- [x] A `null` reference throws **`NullPointerException`** if a member is accessed.
- [x] Primitives are stored **directly in the stack**; object references (8 bytes) point to the heap.
- [x] Object header size ≈ **12–16 bytes**; aligned to multiples of 8 bytes.
- [x] Memory formula: $S_{total} = S_{header} + \sum S_{fields} + \text{padding}$.
- [x] KTU exam buzzwords: *"two-stage process"*, *"stack vs heap"*, *"reference vs object"*, *"aliasing"*, *"anonymous object"*.
- [x] Always prefer the **combined single-line form** for idiomatic Java: `ClassName ref = new ClassName(args);`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Object Reference
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Object Reference

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Object Reference:** In Java, an *object reference* is a typed variable that stores the **memory address** (handle) of an object allocated on the **heap memory**, rather than storing the object's data directly. The reference acts as a "remote control" or "pointer to the object" — manipulating the reference allows indirect access to the object's attributes and methods.

In Java, every class-type variable (also called a *non-primitive* or *reference type variable*) is fundamentally a **reference**. When we write:

```java
Student s1 = new Student();
```

The variable `s1` is **not** the object — it is a **reference** that *points to* the `Student` object living on the heap. The `new` operator allocates memory and returns the address, which is then assigned to `s1`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (asked frequently in KTU exams):**
> - **Primitive variables** (e.g., `int`, `float`, `char`, `boolean`) store the *actual value* directly.
> - **Reference variables** (e.g., `String`, `Student`, any class) store the *memory address* of the object.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The TV Remote Control

Imagine an object as a **Television** in your room.
- The **TV** = the object (with its data, state, and behavior).
- The **Remote Control** = the object reference.
- The **Battery inside the remote** = the memory address of the TV (you cannot read it; the remote "knows" which TV it controls).

You can have **multiple remotes** (references) controlling the **same TV** (object). Pressing a button on any remote changes the TV's state, and all other remotes will reflect that change — this is **aliasing**.

If you throw away the remote (`s1 = null;`), the TV is still there. If you throw away **all** remotes pointing to a TV, that TV becomes **garbage** and is collected by the **Garbage Collector**.

> [!NOTE]
> The `.` (dot) operator is the "button press" on the remote: `s1.name` means "use remote `s1` to access the `name` property of the TV it controls."

## 1.3 Physical Memory Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Reference size (64-bit JVM)** | **8 bytes** (compressed: **4 bytes**) | A reference variable occupies a fixed, uniform size on the stack regardless of the object's size |
| **Object size** | Variable | Depends on the fields and alignment padding |
| **Stack memory** | Per-thread | Stores reference variables and primitive locals |
| **Heap memory** | Shared across threads | Stores actual object data and instance variables |
| **`null` value** | **`0x00000000`** (or `0x0`) | Default value for uninitialized reference variables |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Lifecycle of an Object Reference

### Step 1: Declaration
```java
Student s1;     // s1 is declared, but holds null. It does NOT yet point to any object.
```

- A reference variable is **created on the stack**.
- Its initial value is **`null`** (the *null reference*).
- It does not point to any real object. Using it now causes `NullPointerException`.

### Step 2: Instantiation
```java
s1 = new Student();
```

- The `new` keyword performs three actions:
  1. Allocates memory on the **heap** for the `Student` object.
  2. Invokes a **constructor** to initialize the object.
  3. Returns the **memory address** (reference) of the new object.
- The reference `s1` is then assigned this address.

### Step 3: Access via the Reference
```java
s1.name = "Arjun";
s1.display();
```

- The `.` (dot/member access) operator dereferences the reference and accesses members of the actual object on the heap.

### Step 4: Reference Reassignment (Aliasing)
```java
Student s2 = s1;     // s2 now points to the SAME object as s1
```

- The object is **not copied**. Both `s1` and `s2` are references pointing to the same heap object.
- Changes via `s1` are visible via `s2`, and vice versa.

### Step 5: Loss of Reference → Garbage Collection
```java
s1 = null;     // Object becomes unreachable via s1, but still reachable via s2
s2 = null;     // Now BOTH references are gone → object is eligible for Garbage Collection
```

- The JVM's **Garbage Collector (GC)** reclaims heap memory once the object is **unreachable** from any live reference.

## 2.2 Why and How — The Reasoning Behind References

| Why Java uses references | How it works in practice |
|---|---|
| **Memory efficiency** — large objects (arrays, structures) are not duplicated when passed to methods | Only the address (8 bytes) is copied, not the entire object |
| **Shared mutable state** — multiple parts of a program can collaborate on the same object | Multiple reference variables point to a single object (aliasing) |
| **Dynamic memory management** — object lifetimes are not tied to variable scopes | Heap objects persist as long as some reference points to them |
| **Polymorphism support** — a parent-type reference can hold child-type objects (upcasting) | Enables dynamic method dispatch (late binding) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Reference Variable Behaviour Equivalents**
> - **Reference assignment** : `B = A` means `B` now points to the same object as `A` (not a copy).
> - **Reference comparison (`==`)** : Compares **memory addresses**, not content.
> - **Content comparison (`.equals()`)** : Compares **object state** (defined per-class).
> - **Pass-by-value-of-reference** : Java ALWAYS passes by value, but the value for objects **is the reference**.

| Concept | Syntax / Rule | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| Declaration | `ClassName ref;` | `ref` is initialized to `null` |
| Instantiation | `ref = new ClassName(args);` | Allocates heap memory, returns address |
| Combined form | `ClassName ref = new ClassName(args);` | Most common in practice |
| Aliasing | `ref2 = ref1;` | Both refer to **same** object |
| Null check | `if (ref != null) { ... }` | Avoids `NullPointerException` |
| Self-reference | `this` | Refers to the current object |
| Method parameter | `void foo(Student s) { ... }` | Parameter `s` is a **copy of the reference** (not the object) |
| Garbage Collection | `ref = null;` (when no other refs exist) | Object becomes eligible for GC |
| `==` vs `.equals()` | `==` → reference equality; `.equals()` → value equality | Critical for KTU viva and MCQs |
| Upcasting | `Animal a = new Dog();` | Parent reference, child object — enables polymorphism |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **GUI Frameworks (Swing/JavaFX)**: A `JButton` reference is passed between event listeners, controllers, and panels — all pointing to the same button so state changes (e.g., disabling) are visible everywhere.
- **Database Connection Pools**: A single `Connection` object is shared by reference across multiple service-layer methods to coordinate transactions.
- **Data Structures (LinkedList, Tree)**: Nodes are linked via references; a `next` field is literally an object reference pointing to the next node.
- **Distributed Systems (RMI, EJB)**: Java's *remote references* (RMI stubs) are essentially networked object references enabling RPC-style method invocation across JVMs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Aliasing and the "Same Object" Trap

Consider the following Java code (a classic KTU short-answer scenario):

```java
class Box {
    int length;
    Box(int l) { length = l; }
}

public class ReferenceDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Box b1 = new Box(10);   // Line A
        Box b2 = b1;            // Line B
        b2.length = 25;         // Line C
        System.out.println(b1.length);   // Line D
    }
}
```

### Exhaustive Step-by-Step Evaluation

**Line A:** `Box b1 = new Box(10);`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \texttt{new Box(10)} \text{ allocates heap memory for a } \texttt{Box} \text{ object.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Constructor sets the field } \texttt{length} = 10. \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{The address (e.g., } \texttt{0xABC}) \text{ is returned by } \texttt{new}. \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \texttt{b1} \text{ is assigned this address. Result: } \texttt{b1} \to \texttt{Box\{length=10\}}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Line B:** `Box b2 = b1;`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{The value of } \texttt{b1} \text{ (the address } \texttt{0xABC}) \text{ is copied into } \texttt{b2}. \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{No new object is created. } \texttt{b2} \text{ now also points to the same heap object.} \\
\text{Result:} \quad & \texttt{b1} \to \texttt{0xABC} \leftarrow \texttt{b2} \quad \text{(single object, two references)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Line C:** `b2.length = 25;`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \texttt{b2} \text{ is dereferenced to reach the object at } \texttt{0xABC}. \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{The field } \texttt{length} \text{ of that object is updated from } 10 \text{ to } 25. \\
\text{Result:} \quad & \text{The object now reads } \texttt{Box\{length=25\}}.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Line D:** `System.out.println(b1.length);`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \texttt{b1} \text{ is dereferenced — it points to the same object at } \texttt{0xABC}. \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{The } \texttt{length} \text{ field is read — it has been modified to } 25. \\
\text{Output:} \quad & \boxed{25}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Analytical Matrix — Tracing State

| After Line | `b1` points to | `b2` points to | Object's `length` |
|---|---|---|---|
| A | `0xABC` (new object) | `null` (uninitialized) | `10` |
| B | `0xABC` | `0xABC` (same object!) | `10` |
| C | `0xABC` | `0xABC` | `25` |
| D | `0xABC` | `0xABC` | `25` (printed) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Assignment of one reference to another does **NOT** copy the object. It copies only the *address*. The output is `25`, not `10`. This is one of the **most-asked KTU viva questions**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Pass-by-Value-of-Reference (Methods)

```java
class Number {
    int value;
    Number(int v) { value = v; }
}

public class MethodDemo {
    static void modify(Number n) { n.value = 50; }       // Modifies object
    static void reassign(Number n) { n = new Number(99); } // Reassigns local copy
    static void swap(Number a, Number b) {                // FAILS
        Number t = a; a = b; b = t;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Number x = new Number(10);
        Number y = new Number(20);

        modify(x);
        System.out.println("After modify: " + x.value);   // Q1

        reassign(x);
        System.out.println("After reassign: " + x.value); // Q2

        swap(x, y);
        System.out.println("After swap: " + x.value + "," + y.value); // Q3
    }
}
```

### Evaluation — Q1: `modify(x)` Output

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \texttt{modify} \text{ receives a copy of the reference } \texttt{x}. \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Both } \texttt{x} \text{ and the parameter } \texttt{n} \text{ point to the same object.} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \texttt{n.value = 50} \text{ updates the object on the heap.} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \texttt{x} \text{ (in main) is dereferenced — sees the modified value.} \\
\text{Output:} \quad & \boxed{50}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Evaluation — Q2: `reassign(x)` Output

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \texttt{n = new Number(99)} \text{ creates a NEW object and points } \texttt{n} \text{ to it.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{The original object (with value 50) is unchanged.} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \texttt{x} \text{ in main still points to the ORIGINAL object (value 50).} \\
\text{Output:} \quad & \boxed{50}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Evaluation — Q3: `swap(x, y)` Output

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Parameters } \texttt{a} \text{ and } \texttt{b} \text{ are local copies of the references.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Swapping them only changes the local copies, not the originals in main.} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{This is the classic Java "cannot swap using references" trap.} \\
\text{Output:} \quad & \boxed{50, 20} \quad \text{(no swap occurred)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Output of the Program

```
After modify: 50
After reassign: 50
After swap: 50,20
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly conclude that Java is "pass-by-reference" because `modify(x)` works. **It is not.** Java is **always pass-by-value** — for object types, the value *is* the reference. The method receives a *copy of the reference*, not a copy of the object.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — `null` Reference and `NullPointerException`

```java
class Car {
    String model;
    void start() { System.out.println(model + " started"); }
}

public class NullDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Car c = null;          // Line 1
        c.model = "BMW";       // Line 2  →  NullPointerException
        c.start();             // unreachable
    }
}
```

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Line 1:} \quad & \texttt{c} = \texttt{null} \quad (\text{address } \texttt{0x0}). \\
\text{Line 2:} \quad & \text{JVM attempts to dereference } \texttt{c} \text{ to set } \texttt{model}. \\
\text{Result:} \quad & \texttt{NullPointerException} \text{ is thrown at runtime (compile succeeds).} \\
\text{Defensive fix:} \quad & \texttt{if (c != null) \{ c.model = "BMW"; \} }
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Memory Layout — Stack vs Heap with References

The following **Mermaid block diagram** illustrates how primitive variables live on the stack while objects live on the heap, and how reference variables act as "wires" connecting them.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph STK["STACK MEMORY (main method)"]
        A1["b1 : Box\naddr = 0xABC"]
        A2["b2 : Box\naddr = 0xABC"]
        A3["x : int\nvalue = 25"]
    end

    subgraph HEP["HEAP MEMORY"]
        B1["Box Object @ 0xABC\nlength = 25"]
    end

    A1 -- "points to" --> B1
    A2 -- "points to" --> B1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The integer `x` holds its value `25` directly on the stack (primitive). The references `b1` and `b2` each hold the same address `0xABC`, which resolves to the single object on the heap. This is the **aliasing** effect.

---

## 4.2 Reference Assignment Flow (Aliasing Lifecycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Step 1: Box b1 = new Box(10)\nb1 → 0xABC, object.length = 10"] --> S2["Step 2: Box b2 = b1\nb2 → 0xABC (address copied)"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: b2.length = 25\nobject.length updated to 25"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Print b1.length\nb1 dereferences same object → 25"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: b1 = null\nb1 becomes null, object still alive via b2"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: b2 = null\nObject now unreachable → Garbage Collected"]
```

---

## 4.3 Pass-by-Value-of-Reference — Method Call Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as main()
    participant S as Stack (main)
    participant H as Heap
    participant MT as modify() method

    M->>S: x = new Number(10) → x points to 0xAAA
    M->>MT: modify(x) — copy of reference 0xAAA passed
    MT->>H: n.value = 50 (n and x point to same object)
    MT-->>M: returns
    M->>S: dereference x → value is now 50
    Note over M,MT: Output: 50
```

---

## 4.4 Object Reference Type Hierarchy (Reference Type vs Primitive Type)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    ROOT["Java Data Types"]
    ROOT --> P["Primitive Types"]
    ROOT --> R["Reference Types"]

    P --> P1["byte, short, int, long\nfloat, double\nchar, boolean"]
    P1 --> PNOTE["Stored BY VALUE\non the stack\ndefault numeric: 0\ndefault boolean: false"]

    R --> R1["Class types\n(e.g., String, Student)"]
    R --> R2["Interface types\n(e.g., Runnable)"]
    R --> R3["Array types\n(e.g., int[], String[][])"]
    R --> R4["Enum types"]
    R1 --> RNOTE["Stored AS REFERENCE\n(address on stack, object on heap)\ndefault: null"]
    R2 --> RNOTE
    R3 --> RNOTE
    R4 --> RNOTE
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between primitive variable and reference variable in Java. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Primitive Variable | Reference Variable |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage** | Stores the **actual value** directly | Stores the **memory address** of an object |
| **Memory location** | Usually on the **stack** | Reference on **stack**, object on **heap** |
| **Default value** | `0`, `0.0`, `false`, `'\u0000'` | **`null`** |
| **Size** | Fixed (e.g., `int` = 4 bytes) | Fixed (4 or 8 bytes — uniform) |
| **Example** | `int age = 21;` | `String name = "Kerala";` |
| **Comparison** | `==` compares values | `==` compares addresses; `.equals()` compares content |

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for tabular difference)

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is a null reference? What exception occurs when we try to use a null reference in Java?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- A **null reference** is a reference variable that does not point to any object; it holds the special value `null` (essentially the address `0x0`).
- It typically occurs when a reference is declared but not yet instantiated, or has been explicitly set to `null`.
- When a `null` reference is dereferenced (e.g., `obj.method()` or `obj.field = 5`), the JVM throws a **`NullPointerException`** at runtime.
- **Defensive check:** Always verify with `if (obj != null) { ... }` before use.
- **Example:**
  ```java
  Student s = null;
  s.display();   // throws NullPointerException
  ```

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for exception name + example, 1 mark for defensive check)

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Rule (ESE Module-wise):** *Answer any one full question from this module.* Each 14-mark question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts (a) and (b).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Aliasing, Method Calls, and Garbage Collection

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of *object reference* and *aliasing* in Java with a suitable example. What is the output of the following program? Justify your answer.

```java
class Account {
    int balance;
    Account(int b) { balance = b; }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Account a1 = new Account(1000);
        Account a2 = a1;
        a2.balance += 500;
        System.out.println("a1: " + a1.balance);
        System.out.println("a2: " + a2.balance);
    }
}
```

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Object reference definition (2 marks):** A reference variable stores the memory address of an object, not the object itself. It is a typed handle through which the object's members are accessed.

2. **Aliasing definition (2 marks):** Aliasing occurs when two or more reference variables point to the **same object** on the heap. Modifying the object through any reference is visible through all of them, because there is only one underlying object.

3. **Code execution (3 marks):**
   - `a1` is created pointing to a new `Account` object with `balance = 1000`.
   - `a2 = a1` copies the reference, so both `a1` and `a2` point to the **same** object.
   - `a2.balance += 500` updates the shared object's balance to `1500`.
   - **Output:**
     ```
     a1: 1500
     a2: 1500
     ```
   - Both print `1500` because there is only one object in memory.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain with an example how Java passes *object references* to methods. Why is it said that Java is *pass-by-value* and **not** *pass-by-reference*? What is the output of the following code?

```java
class Point {
    int x, y;
    Point(int x, int y) { this.x = x; this.y = y; }
    void move(int dx, int dy) { x += dx; y += dy; }
}

public class PassTest {
    static void tryToMove(Point p, int dx, int dy) {
        p.move(dx, dy);
        p = new Point(0, 0);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Point pt = new Point(1, 1);
        tryToMove(pt, 5, 5);
        System.out.println("pt.x = " + pt.x + ", pt.y = " + pt.y);
    }
}
```

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Passing references to methods (2 marks):** When a reference is passed as a method argument, Java passes a *copy of the reference* (i.e., the address value). The method can use this copy to mutate the original object, but reassigning the parameter to a new object affects only the local copy.

2. **Why "pass-by-value", not "pass-by-reference" (3 marks):**
   - In true *pass-by-reference* (as in C++ `&` references or Pascal `var` parameters), the parameter becomes an *alias* of the caller's variable — any reassignment inside the method would change the caller's variable.
   - In Java, the parameter is a *new local variable* holding a *copy* of the reference. Reassigning it inside the method (e.g., `p = new Point(0,0)`) does **not** affect the caller's `pt`.
   - Hence Java is strictly **pass-by-value**, where for objects the value passed is the reference itself.

3. **Code execution (2 marks):**
   - `pt = (1, 1)`.
   - `p.move(5, 5)` mutates the object: `pt` is now `(6, 6)`.
   - `p = new Point(0,0)` reassigns only the local copy of `p` — `pt` is unaffected.
   - **Output:**
     ```
     pt.x = 6, pt.y = 6
     ```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Reference Types, `this` Keyword, and Garbage Collection

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2 (Alternative Choice)]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is the `this` reference in Java? Explain its uses with a code example. How does it relate to object references?

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Definition of `this` (2 marks):** `this` is an **implicit final reference** to the current object — i.e., the object on whose behalf the method or constructor is currently executing. It is automatically available in every non-static method and constructor.

2. **Uses of `this` (3 marks):**
   - **Disambiguating instance variables from parameters** in constructors/setters:
     ```java
     class Student {
         String name;
         Student(String name) { this.name = name; }   // this.name = instance var
     }
     ```
   - **Constructor chaining** using `this(...)`:
     ```java
     Student() { this("Unknown", 0); }   // calls parameterized constructor
     Student(String n, int a) { name = n; age = a; }
     ```
   - **Passing the current object as an argument:**
     ```java
     obj.register(this);   // hand 'this' object to another method
     ```

3. **Relation to object references (2 marks):** `this` is itself a reference variable. It points to the current heap object, behaving exactly like any other reference — you can pass it, return it, or use it to chain access (e.g., `this.name`). Inside a constructor, `this` refers to the object being constructed.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain how the **Garbage Collector** determines that an object is eligible for collection, using object references. When is the `finalize()` method (deprecated in Java 9+) called? Write a program that demonstrates object becoming eligible for garbage collection.

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

1. **Eligibility rule (2 marks):** An object is **eligible for garbage collection** when no live reference variable points to it — i.e., it is *unreachable* from any thread's stack, static fields, or JNI references.

2. **Scenarios for eligibility (2 marks):**
   - Reference goes out of scope (block ends).
   - Reference is explicitly set to `null`.
   - Reference is reassigned to another object (the old object is orphaned).
   - The cyclic reference between two objects with no external reference is also eligible (Java's mark-and-sweep GC handles cycles).

3. **`finalize()` call (1 mark):** Called by the GC **at most once**, just before reclaiming the object's memory. Deprecated since Java 9; replaced by `AutoCloseable` and `Cleaner` API. Execution timing is **not guaranteed**.

4. **Program demonstrating GC eligibility (2 marks):**
   ```java
   class Employee {
       int id;
       Employee(int id) { this.id = id; }
       public void finalize() {
           System.out.println("Employee " + id + " is garbage collected");
       }
   }

   public class GCDemo {
       public static void main(String[] args) {
           Employee e1 = new Employee(101);
           e1 = null;                          // 101 is now eligible
           Employee e2 = new Employee(202);
           e2 = new Employee(303);             // 202 is now eligible
           System.gc();                        // request GC
           System.runFinalization();
       }
   }
   ```
   **Output (typical):**
   ```
   Employee 202 is garbage collected
   Employee 101 is garbage collected
   ```
   *(Order is not guaranteed — depends on GC algorithm.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Zones**
> 1. **Saying "Java is pass-by-reference"** — Instant 2-mark deduction. Correct statement: *"Java is pass-by-value, where the value is the reference."*
> 2. **Confusing `==` with `.equals()`** — `==` compares references (addresses) for objects; `.equals()` compares content. Students often claim `==` compares values — it does not for objects.
> 3. **Forgetting the default value `null`** — Uninitialized reference variables are `null`, not `undefined` (as in C/C++).
> 4. **Drawing memory diagrams incorrectly** — Stack/heap must be clearly separated; arrows must go from reference variable (stack) to object (heap), not the reverse.
> 5. **Ignoring `this` in constructors** — In a parameterized constructor, simply writing `name = name;` assigns the parameter to itself, leaving the instance variable unchanged. Always use `this.name = name;`.
> 6. **Assuming GC runs immediately** — `System.gc()` is only a *request*; the JVM may ignore it.

---

## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 🔹 **An object reference is a typed address** pointing to an object on the heap, stored as a stack variable.
- 🔹 **`new` allocates memory and returns the reference** — the variable does not contain the object.
- 🔹 **Assignment of one reference to another creates aliasing**, not a copy of the object.
- 🔹 **Java is always pass-by-value** — but for objects, the value copied is the reference itself.
- 🔹 **`==` compares addresses** for objects; **`.equals()`** compares content (when properly overridden).
- 🔹 **Default value of a reference variable is `null`**; dereferencing `null` causes `NullPointerException`.
- 🔹 **`this` is an implicit reference to the current object**, used to disambiguate and to chain constructors.
- 🔹 **An object becomes eligible for GC** when no reference points to it; `System.gc()` is only a *hint*.
- 🔹 **Cyclic references do not prevent GC** in Java (mark-and-sweep algorithm handles them).
- 🔹 **Reference type vs Primitive type** is a high-frequency KTU MCQ topic — remember the storage and default-value differences.
- 🔹 **Upcasting** (`Parent p = new Child();`) is a reference concept that enables polymorphism and dynamic dispatch.
- 🔹 **`final` on a reference** means the *reference* cannot be reassigned, but the *object's state* can still be mutated.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **method** in Java is a named, reusable block of statements that performs a specific, well-defined task. It is the Java equivalent of a *function* or *subroutine* in other programming languages, and it serves as the fundamental building block for modular, structured, and object-oriented program design. Methods are declared inside a class and operate on the data (instance variables) of that class, thereby encapsulating behavior along with the data it manipulates.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme definition, a method is a **member of a class** that defines an action an object of that class can perform, identified by its *signature* (method name + parameter list) and characterized by its *return type*, *access modifier*, and *body*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In Java, there are no standalone functions — every executable unit of logic must belong to a class in the form of a method. This is one of the key distinctions between Java and pure C/C++.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **method as a specialized worker in a factory**.

- The **factory** is your class.
- The **worker (method)** has a name, a specific job, may require raw materials (parameters), and returns a finished product (return value).
- The **manager** of the factory decides which worker to call and what materials to give them (this is the method call).
- Once the worker finishes the job, they go back to being available, but the result is handed back to the manager.

Just as a factory becomes efficient when workers are specialized and reusable, a Java program becomes clean, readable, and maintainable when logic is broken into named methods instead of being dumped into one giant `main` block.

## 1.3 Why Methods Are Essential in OOP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU syllabus lists methods as the primary mechanism to achieve three core software engineering goals:
> - **Reusability** — write once, call many times.
> - **Modularity** — divide a large problem into smaller, named sub-problems.
> - **Abstraction** — hide *how* a task is done, expose only *what* it does.

## 1.4 General Syntax of a Java Method

```java
accessModifier returnType methodName(parameterList) {
    // method body
    // local variable declarations
    // executable statements
    return value; // if returnType is not void
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> Every method **must** specify a return type. If no value is returned, the keyword **`void`** is used. A method that declares a non-`void` return type **must** return a compatible value using the `return` statement.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Java Method — Component Breakdown

A Java method declaration is composed of **six distinct components**. The KTU examiner often tests a student's ability to label each part. Let us dissect the syntax piece by piece.

### 2.1.1 Access Modifier
Defines the visibility of the method to other classes. The four valid levels (in order of increasing restriction) are:

- `public` — accessible from any class in any package.
- `protected` — accessible within the same package and by subclasses (even in other packages).
- *default* (no modifier) — package-private, accessible only within the same package.
- `private` — accessible only within the same class.

### 2.1.2 Return Type
The data type of the value the method sends back to the caller. It can be any primitive type (`int`, `double`, `char`, `boolean`, etc.) or any reference type (`String`, array, object). Use `void` when nothing is returned.

### 2.1.3 Method Name
A valid Java identifier following the naming convention: starts with a lowercase letter, uses camelCase for multi-word names (e.g., `calculateSimpleInterest`, `displayResult`). It should be a verb or verb phrase because methods represent actions.

### 2.1.4 Parameter List (Formal Parameters)
A comma-separated list of `type identifier` pairs enclosed in parentheses. The list may be empty (meaning the method takes no input). Each parameter acts as a *local variable* inside the method body, initialized by the **actual arguments** passed during the call.

### 2.1.5 Method Body
The block of statements enclosed in `{}` that defines the actual logic. All local variable declarations and executable statements live here.

### 2.1.6 Return Statement
Used to terminate the method and optionally send a value back. In a `void` method, `return;` may be used simply to exit early.

## 2.2 Types of Methods in Java

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks students to classify methods. The standard taxonomy is:

- **Predefined (Built-in) Methods** — supplied by the Java API, e.g., `Math.sqrt()`, `System.out.println()`, `String.length()`.
- **User-Defined Methods** — written by the programmer for application-specific tasks.
- **Static Methods** — belong to the class, not to an object; invoked using the class name.
- **Instance Methods** — belong to a specific object of the class; invoked using an object reference.
- **Accessor (Getter) Methods** — return the value of a private field.
- **Mutator (Setter) Methods** — modify the value of a private field.
- **Constructor** — a special method used to initialize a newly created object (no return type, name matches class name).
- **Recursive Methods** — methods that call themselves, either directly or indirectly.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| Component | Mandatory? | Allowed Values | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Access Modifier | No (defaults to package-private) | `public`, `protected`, `private`, none | Controls visibility of the method |
| Return Type | **Yes** | Any primitive, reference type, or `void` | Defines what value is sent back |
| Method Name | **Yes** | Valid Java identifier (camelCase verb) | Unique handle to invoke the method |
| Parameter List | No (may be empty) | `type name, type name ...` | Accepts input data from caller |
| Method Body | **Yes** | Any valid Java block `{ ... }` | Holds the executable logic |
| Return Statement | Required if return type $\neq$ `void` | `return value;` | Terminates method, returns value |

> [!NOTE]
> **Signature of a method** = method name + parameter list (number, type, and order of parameters). The return type is **NOT** part of the signature in Java. The signature is what the compiler uses to resolve method calls.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In real Java applications (Android apps, banking systems, IoT firmware), methods are used everywhere:
- `calculateEMI()` in a loan processing system.
- `authenticateUser()` in a login module.
- `readSensorData()` in an embedded controller.
- `compressImage()` in a media-handling application.

This makes methods the *first* unit of design the KTU examiner expects a student to master before moving to advanced OOP concepts like inheritance and polymorphism.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analysis & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Why Methods? — The Motivation Analysis

Let us first understand *why* we need methods by analyzing a problem. Suppose a KTU student must write a program to compute the **area of a circle** in three different parts of the same program, each time with a different radius. Without methods, the code is repeated three times. With methods, the logic is written once and reused. Let us quantify this.

### Step 1: Identify the repeated logic.

The formula for the area of a circle is:

$$A = \pi \cdot r^2$$

This is the same logical operation executed three times.

### Step 2: Encapsulate the logic in a named method.

```java
static double calculateArea(double radius) {
    double area = Math.PI * radius * radius;
    return area;
}
```

### Step 3: Invoke the method three times with different arguments.

```java
public class CircleDemo {
    static double calculateArea(double radius) {
        double area = Math.PI * radius * radius;
        return area;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        double a1 = calculateArea(5.0);
        double a2 = calculateArea(10.0);
        double a3 = calculateArea(15.0);

        System.out.println("Area 1 = " + a1);
        System.out.println("Area 2 = " + a2);
        System.out.println("Area 3 = " + a3);
    }
}
```

### Step 4: Trace the execution (the call flow).

- The JVM starts at `main`.
- Line `double a1 = calculateArea(5.0);` — pushes `5.0` onto the call stack and jumps to `calculateArea`.
- Inside `calculateArea`, the formal parameter `radius` is assigned the value `5.0`.
- The expression `Math.PI * radius * radius` is evaluated and stored in local variable `area`.
- The `return area;` statement sends the value back to `main` and the stack frame is popped.
- `a1` is assigned the returned value.
- The same sequence repeats for `a2` and `a3`.

## 3.2 Formal Parameter vs. Actual Argument — The Analytical Distinction

This is one of the most heavily tested KTU concepts. Let us derive the relationship.

| Term | Where it appears | What it represents | Lifetime |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Formal Parameter** | In the method declaration (signature) | A placeholder / local variable inside the method | Exists only while the method is executing |
| **Actual Argument** | In the method call (invocation) | The real value or expression passed to the method | Exists in the caller's scope |

### Worked Example: Tracing the Values

```java
static int square(int n) {     // 'n' is the formal parameter
    return n * n;
}

public static void main(String[] args) {
    int x = 4;
    int y = square(x);          // 'x' is the actual argument
    System.out.println(y);      // prints 16
}
```

### Step-by-Step Trace

1. `x` is declared and assigned the value `4` in `main`.
2. `square(x)` is called. The value of `x` (which is `4`) is copied into the formal parameter `n` of `square`.
3. Inside `square`, `n` holds `4`. The expression `n * n` evaluates to `16`.
4. `16` is returned to `main` and stored in `y`.
5. The output is `16`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial KTU Point:** In Java, arguments are passed **by value** for primitives. This means a *copy* of `x` is sent to `square`. Modifying `n` inside `square` would NOT change `x` in `main`. (For objects, the reference itself is passed by value.)

## 3.3 The `void` Return Type — A Special Case Analysis

A method declared with a return type of `void` performs an action but does not send any value back to the caller.

```java
static void greet(String name) {
    System.out.println("Hello, " + name + "!");
}
```

- The method body prints a greeting.
- The keyword `return;` is optional at the end (it is added implicitly).
- The call `greet("Anu");` is a valid *statement* (it cannot appear on the right-hand side of an assignment).

## 3.4 Method Overloading — First Glimpse (KTU Frequently Tested)

Java allows two or more methods to share the **same name** as long as their **parameter lists differ** in number, type, or order. This is called *compile-time polymorphism*.

```java
static int add(int a, int b) {
    return a + b;
}

static double add(double a, double b) {
    return a + b;
}

static int add(int a, int b, int c) {
    return a + b + c;
}
```

The compiler decides which `add` to invoke based on the arguments supplied at the call site.

> [!NOTE]
> Simply changing the return type alone is **not** sufficient to overload a method — the parameter list must also differ. This is a classic KTU exam trap.

## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Method Type | Belongs To | Invocation Syntax | Can access `this`? | Can access static members? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Static Method | The class | `ClassName.methodName(...)` | No | Yes |
| Instance Method | An object | `objectReference.methodName(...)` | Yes | Yes |
| Constructor | An object | Called automatically with `new` | Yes | Yes |
| `main` Method | The class (entry point) | Invoked by JVM | No | Yes (it is static) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Anatomy of a Java Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Method Declaration Block"] --> B["Access Modifier"]
    A --> C["Return Type"]
    A --> D["Method Name"]
    A --> E["Parameter List"]
    A --> F["Method Body"]
    A --> G["Return Statement"]
    B --> B1["public"]
    B --> B2["protected"]
    B --> B3["private"]
    B --> B4["package private default"]
    C --> C1["Primitive types int double"]
    C --> C2["Reference types String Object"]
    C --> C3["void for no return"]
    E --> E1["Empty parentheses if no input"]
    E --> E2["Comma separated type name pairs"]
    F --> F1["Local variable declarations"]
    F --> F2["Executable statements"]
    G --> G1["return value statement required if not void"]
    G --> G2["May be omitted if void"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Method Call Execution Flow (Stack-Based)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0["JVM invokes main method"] --> S1["main pushes its stack frame"]
    S1 --> S2["main calls calculateArea with argument 5"]
    S2 --> S3["calculateArea pushes its stack frame"]
    S3 --> S4["Formal parameter radius equals 5"]
    S4 --> S5["Compute area equals PI times radius squared"]
    S5 --> S6["Return statement pops the stack frame"]
    S6 --> S7["Returned value stored in variable a1"]
    S7 --> S8["main continues execution"]
    S8 --> S9["All methods finish, program ends"]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Method Components Inside a Class

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph cls["Class CircleDemo"]
        F1["Instance Variable double radius"]
        M1["Method setRadius void"]
        M2["Method calculateArea double"]
        M3["Method display void"]
        M4["Static Method main void"]
    end
    M1 --> F1
    M2 --> F1
    M3 --> F1
    M4 --> M1
    M4 --> M2
    M4 --> M3
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block diagram above illustrates how methods of a class interact with shared instance variables. This relationship between *state* (fields) and *behavior* (methods) is the essence of OOP and is one of the most commonly drawn figures in KTU theory exams.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a method in Java. List any four components of a method declaration with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer:**

A method in Java is a named block of statements declared inside a class that performs a specific task and may optionally return a value to the caller.

The four components of a method declaration are:

1. **Access Modifier** — specifies the visibility of the method (e.g., `public`, `private`).
2. **Return Type** — the data type of the value the method returns, or `void` if nothing is returned.
3. **Method Name** — a valid Java identifier that uniquely identifies the method.
4. **Parameter List** — a comma-separated list of typed parameters enclosed in parentheses; it may be empty.

---

### Question 2
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**Differentiate between formal parameters and actual arguments with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Formal Parameter | Actual Argument |
|---|---|---|
| Location | Appears in the method declaration | Appears in the method call |
| Role | Acts as a local variable inside the method | Supplies the real value at call time |
| Example | In `int square(int n)`, `n` is the formal parameter | In `int y = square(5);`, `5` is the actual argument |

When the call `square(5)` is made, the value `5` is copied into the formal parameter `n`, and the method computes $5 \times 5 = 25$ and returns it.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`

**(a)** Explain the general syntax of declaring and invoking a method in Java. Illustrate with a suitable example method that computes and returns the **simple interest** given principal, rate, and time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Java program that defines a class `Account` with an instance variable `balance`, a method `deposit(double amt)` to add money, a method `withdraw(double amt)` to deduct money only if sufficient balance exists, and a `main` method to demonstrate both operations. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

### General Syntax of Method Declaration

The general form of a Java method is:

```java
accessModifier returnType methodName(parameterList) {
    // body
    return value;
}
```

### Step 1 — Choose the access modifier
We use `public` so the method can be called from other classes in the package.

### Step 2 — Choose the return type
The formula for simple interest is:

$$SI = \frac{P \cdot R \cdot T}{100}$$

The result is a real number, so the return type is `double`.

### Step 3 — Choose the method name
A meaningful camelCase verb, e.g., `calculateSimpleInterest`.

### Step 4 — Define the parameter list
Three `double` parameters: `principal`, `rate`, `time`.

### Complete Method Definition

```java
public static double calculateSimpleInterest(double principal, double rate, double time) {
    double si = (principal * rate * time) / 100.0;
    return si;
}
```

### Step 5 — Invoke the method

```java
double interest = calculateSimpleInterest(10000.0, 8.5, 3.0);
System.out.println("Simple Interest = " + interest);
```

### Step 6 — Trace
- Actual arguments: $P = 10000$, $R = 8.5$, $T = 3$.
- Computation: $SI = \dfrac{10000 \times 8.5 \times 3}{100} = 2550.0$.
- Output: `Simple Interest = 2550.0`.

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

```java
class Account {
    double balance;

    Account(double initialBalance) {
        balance = initialBalance;
    }

    void deposit(double amt) {
        if (amt > 0) {
            balance = balance + amt;
            System.out.println("Deposited: " + amt);
        } else {
            System.out.println("Invalid deposit amount.");
        }
    }

    void withdraw(double amt) {
        if (amt > balance) {
            System.out.println("Insufficient balance. Withdrawal denied.");
        } else {
            balance = balance - amt;
            System.out.println("Withdrawn: " + amt);
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Account a1 = new Account(5000.0);
        a1.deposit(2000.0);
        a1.withdraw(1000.0);
        a1.withdraw(9000.0);
        System.out.println("Final Balance = " + a1.balance);
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Output Trace

1. `Account a1 = new Account(5000.0);` → balance becomes 5000.0.
2. `a1.deposit(2000.0);` → balance becomes 7000.0. Output: `Deposited: 2000.0`.
3. `a1.withdraw(1000.0);` → balance becomes 6000.0. Output: `Withdrawn: 1000.0`.
4. `a1.withdraw(9000.0);` → insufficient, no change. Output: `Insufficient balance. Withdrawal denied.`
5. Final balance printed: `Final Balance = 6000.0`.

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative)
`[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Modified]`

**(a)** What is method overloading in Java? State the rules that must be followed to overload a method and provide a complete program demonstrating **three overloaded versions of a method named `multiply`** (multiplying two integers, three integers, and two doubles). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the difference between **static methods** and **instance methods** in Java with code examples. When should each be used? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Part (a):**

### Definition
Method overloading is the process of defining two or more methods in the same class with the **same name** but **different parameter lists** (different number, type, or order of parameters).
