The compiler resolves the correct method at compile time, which is why it is also called *compile-time polymorphism*.

### Rules for Method Overloading
1. Methods must have the same name.
2. Methods must differ in the parameter list — by number, type, or order.
3. The return type may be the same or different; **changing only the return type is NOT valid overloading**.
4. Access modifiers may vary freely.
5. Overloading is resolved at compile time, not runtime.

### Complete Program

```java
class MathOps {

    int multiply(int a, int b) {
        return a * b;
    }

    int multiply(int a, int b, int c) {
        return a * b * c;
    }

    double multiply(double a, double b) {
        return a * b;
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MathOps m = new MathOps();
        System.out.println("2 x 3 = " + m.multiply(2, 3));
        System.out.println("2 x 3 x 4 = " + m.multiply(2, 3, 4));
        System.out.println("2.5 x 4.0 = " + m.multiply(2.5, 4.0));
    }
}
```

### Output

```
2 x 3 = 6
2 x 3 x 4 = 24
2.5 x 4.0 = 10.0
```

---

**Model Solution to Part (b):**

### Definition Difference

| Aspect | Static Method | Instance Method |
|---|---|---|
| Keyword | Declared with `static` | Declared without `static` |
| Belongs To | The class as a whole | A specific object (instance) |
| Invocation | `ClassName.methodName()` | `objectReference.methodName()` |
| Can Access `this`? | No | Yes |
| Can Access Instance Variables Directly? | No (needs an object) | Yes |
| Called When? | Can be called before any object is created | Requires an object to exist first |
| Memory | Single copy shared by all objects | Separate copy per object context (in stack) |

### Code Example

```java
class Student {
    String name;                       // instance variable
    static String college = "KTU";     // static variable

    void displayName() {               // instance method
        System.out.println("Name: " + name);
    }

    static void displayCollege() {     // static method
        System.out.println("College: " + college);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student.displayCollege();      // static call, no object needed

        Student s1 = new Student();
        s1.name = "Anu";
        s1.displayName();              // instance call via object
    }
}
```

### When to Use Each

- Use a **static method** when the logic does NOT depend on the state of a particular object — utility functions (e.g., `Math.sqrt`), `main` method, factory methods.
- Use an **instance method** when the logic operates on the instance variables of a specific object — setters, getters, business operations on object data.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> - Writing `return` followed by a value in a `void` method → **compile-time error**, 0 marks for that part.
> - Confusing **method overloading** (compile-time) with **method overriding** (runtime) — they are NOT the same.
> - Forgetting that a method declared with a non-`void` return type **must** return a value on every code path. The compiler tracks this strictly.
> - Declaring two methods with the same name and same parameter list but different return types — Java treats them as duplicates and gives a compile error.
> - Calling an instance method without creating an object (e.g., `displayName();` inside `main` without an object) → 2 marks deducted.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **method** is a named, reusable block of statements inside a class.
- The **six components** of a method are: access modifier, return type, method name, parameter list, method body, return statement.
- The **method signature** consists of the method name + parameter list only. Return type is NOT part of the signature.
- Use `void` if the method does not return a value.
- A non-`void` method **must** return a value of a compatible type on every code path.
- **Formal parameters** are placeholders in the declaration; **actual arguments** are the real values passed at the call site.
- Java passes primitive arguments **by value** (a copy is made).
- **Method overloading** allows the same method name with different parameter lists. The return type alone cannot differentiate overloaded methods.
- **Static methods** belong to the class and can be called without an object. **Instance methods** belong to an object and require an object reference to be invoked.
- Methods promote **reusability, modularity, and abstraction** — the three core benefits emphasized by the KTU 2024 syllabus.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constructors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Constructors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Constructor** in Java is a specialized block of code (resembling a method) that is automatically invoked when an object (instance) of a class is created using the `new` keyword. Its primary purpose is to **initialize the state** (instance variables) of the newly born object to a valid, well-defined initial configuration.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus terminology, a constructor is *"a special member function of a class that shares the class name, has no explicit return type (not even `void`), and is executed implicitly during object instantiation."*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Board-Exam Verbatim):**
> A constructor is a member function whose name is **identical to the class name**, has **no return type** (not even `void`), and is **called automatically** at the time of object creation to initialize the data members of the class.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a **Constructor** like the **assembly line setup procedure at a car manufacturing factory**.

- When you order a new car (`new Car()`), it does not just *appear* on the showroom floor.
- The factory (JVM) must run a **standard setup routine** first: install the engine, set the odometer to zero, fill fuel, paint, etc.
- That **standard setup routine** is the *Constructor*. Every car that rolls out must go through it.
- If you want a *red car with sunroof and 18-inch alloy wheels*, you specify those details in a *different setup routine* — a *Parameterized Constructor*.
- If you want an *exact duplicate* of an existing car, you take that car as a template — that is a *Copy Constructor*.

Just like the factory will refuse to deliver a car without running the assembly line, **Java will refuse to create an object without invoking a constructor**.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics (Syllabus Highlights)

> [!NOTE]
> **Five Golden Rules of Constructors (Frequently asked in KTU):**
> 1. Constructor name = Class name (case-sensitive, exactly identical).
> 2. Constructors **do NOT have a return type** — not even `void`. Writing `void` makes it a *method*, not a constructor.
> 3. Constructors are **invoked implicitly** using the `new` keyword.
> 4. Constructors **cannot be `abstract`, `static`, `final`, or `synchronized`**.
> 5. Constructors **cannot be overridden** (but they *can* be **overloaded**).

### 1.4 Why Constructors are Mandatory

Every object in Java must be in a consistent state. Without a constructor, instance variables would contain their **default values** (e.g., `0` for `int`, `null` for objects, `false` for `boolean`). Constructors allow the programmer to enforce valid initial values, preventing the "dirty object" problem common in low-level languages.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Lifecycle of a Constructor

When the statement `MyClass obj = new MyClass(10, "Hello");` is executed, the JVM performs these steps in order:

1. **Class Loading Check:** The ClassLoader verifies that `MyClass.class` is loaded into the Method Area. If not, it is loaded.
2. **Memory Allocation:** The JVM allocates a contiguous block of memory on the **Heap** large enough to hold all instance variables of the object. Memory is zero-initialized by the JVM.
3. **Default Initialization:** All primitive instance variables are set to their language defaults (`int` → `0`, `double` → `0.0`, `boolean` → `false`, `char` → `'\u0000'`) and all reference variables are set to `null`.
4. **Explicit Initialization:** Instance initializer blocks and field assignments execute **in the order they appear in the source code** (top-to-bottom).
5. **Constructor Execution:** The appropriate matching constructor (resolved by argument signature) is invoked. The `this` reference now points to the newly allocated heap object.
6. **Reference Assignment:** The memory address of the object is assigned to the reference variable `obj` on the Stack.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** Whenever asked *"What happens when an object is created?"*, list steps 1–6 above. Examiners love this enumeration. A common follow-up question asks why default values appear *before* the constructor body — it is because of step 3.

### 2.2 Classification of Constructors (Full Taxonomy)

Java constructors are classified into **five** distinct categories:

| # | Constructor Type | Trigger Condition | Purpose | KTU Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Default (No-arg) Constructor** | Provided by compiler if **no constructor** is written | Initialize to safe defaults | Very High |
| 2 | **No-Argument User-Defined Constructor** | `new ClassName()` with no params, written by programmer | Custom default initialization | High |
| 3 | **Parameterized Constructor** | `new ClassName(arg1, arg2)` | Initialize with caller-supplied values | Very High |
| 4 | **Copy Constructor** | `new ClassName(existingObject)` | Duplicate an existing object | Medium |
| 5 | **Private/Singleton Constructor** | `new ClassName()` from inside class only | Restrict instantiation (Singleton DP) | Low-Medium |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property | Constructor | Regular Method |
|---|---|---|
| Name | **Must match class name exactly** | Any valid identifier |
| Return Type | **None (not even `void`)** | Required (`void` or a type) |
| Invocation | **Implicit** via `new` | Explicit via `object.method()` |
| Inheritance | **Not inherited**, not overridden | Inherited, can be overridden |
| `static` keyword | **Not allowed** | Allowed |
| `final` keyword | **Not allowed** | Allowed |
| Purpose | Object initialization | Behavior definition |
| Default value of field | Variables initialized inside | Variables must be assigned manually |
| Number per class | **Many (via overloading)** | Many |
| `this()` chaining | **Allowed** (first statement only) | Not applicable |

### 2.4 The Default Constructor — A Critical Distinction

> [!WARNING]
> **The most common KTU trap:** The compiler inserts a **public no-argument default constructor** *only if the programmer does not write any constructor at all*. The moment you write even a single parameterized constructor, the compiler-stitched default constructor **disappears permanently**. Calling `new ClassName()` will then throw a **compile-time error**.

**Proof — the compiler's "magic" rule:**

$$C_{default} = \begin{cases} \text{Compiler-generated no-arg constructor} & \text{if } N_{user} = 0 \\ \text{None (compile error on `new C()`)} & \text{if } N_{user} \geq 1 \end{cases}$$

where $N_{user}$ is the number of user-defined constructors in the class.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Constructors

In professional Java engineering, constructors are the **gatekeepers of object invariants**. They are used to:

- **Enforce mandatory dependencies** (e.g., a `DatabaseConnection` object *must* receive a connection URL — using a constructor prevents the creation of an unconnected object).
- **Implement the Factory Pattern** by providing multiple constructors for different creation scenarios.
- **Implement the Singleton Pattern** via a `private` constructor that prevents external instantiation.
- **Support Dependency Injection** in frameworks like Spring, where the IoC container calls constructors to assemble application components.
- **Enable defensive copying** of mutable arguments to prevent aliasing bugs (Bloch, *Effective Java* idiom).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section is the **exhaustive technical walkthrough** — every code construct, every execution flow, and every edge case is broken down step-by-step. Because this is a CS programming course (PBEOT404), **Java code is the primary analytical tool**.

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Type-1 — Default (Compiler-Generated) Constructor

**Problem Statement:** Demonstrate the compiler-injected default constructor and prove that the JVM initializes instance variables to language defaults *before* the constructor body executes.

**Source Code:**

```java
class Student {
    int rollNo;
    String name;
    double cgpa;
    // NO constructor written — programmer trusts the compiler
}

public class Demo1 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1 = new Student();      // Line A: triggers compiler default ctor
        System.out.println(s1.rollNo);  // expected 0
        System.out.println(s1.name);    // expected null
        System.out.println(s1.cgpa);    // expected 0.0
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step logical breakdown:**

1. **Compilation phase:** `javac` scans `Student.class`. It detects **zero** user-defined constructors. Therefore, it synthesizes and injects the following bytecode-level constructor:
   $$\text{Student()} \;\rightarrow\; \text{super()} \;\rightarrow\; \text{return}$$
2. **At Line A:** The runtime encounters `new Student()`. Heap memory is allocated (size = 4 + 4 + 8 = **16 bytes** + header overhead).
3. **Default initialization phase:** `rollNo ← 0`, `name ← null`, `cgpa ← 0.0`.
4. **Constructor body executes:** The synthetic constructor body is empty — it just returns `this`.
5. **Output produced:** `0`, `null`, `0.0` (in that order).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Type-2 & Type-3 — User-Defined No-Arg and Parameterized Constructors with **Constructor Overloading**

**Problem Statement:** Build a `Box` class that can be constructed in three ways: (a) cube of side 1, (b) cube of user-specified side, (c) cuboid of length/width/height. Then verify overloading in `main`.

**Source Code:**

```java
class Box {
    double length, width, height;

    // (1) No-argument user-defined constructor — sets a unit cube
    Box() {
        this.length = this.width = this.height = 1.0;
        System.out.println("No-arg constructor called: Unit Cube");
    }

    // (2) Single-parameter constructor — sets a cube
    Box(double side) {
        this.length = this.width = this.height = side;
        System.out.println("Single-param constructor called: Cube of side " + side);
    }

    // (3) Three-parameter constructor — sets a cuboid
    Box(double l, double w, double h) {
        this.length = l;
        this.width  = w;
        this.height = h;
        System.out.println("Three-param constructor called: Cuboid");
    }

    double volume() {
        return this.length * this.width * this.height;
    }
}

public class Demo2 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Box b1 = new Box();              // resolves to (1)
        Box b2 = new Box(5.0);           // resolves to (2)
        Box b3 = new Box(2.0, 3.0, 4.0); // resolves to (3)

        System.out.println("V(b1) = " + b1.volume());  // 1.0
        System.out.println("V(b2) = " + b2.volume());  // 125.0
        System.out.println("V(b3) = " + b3.volume());  // 24.0
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step compilation & resolution analysis:**

- **Step 1 — Signature extraction:** The compiler extracts the parameter list of each constructor as a *method descriptor* in the constant pool:
  $$\text{Descriptors} = \{ (),\; (\text{double}),\; (\text{double},\text{double},\text{double}) \}$$
- **Step 2 — Overload resolution at call-site:** Java uses **compile-time static binding**. At each `new` call, the compiler matches the argument list against the descriptor set (best-fit, most-specific rule). There is no ambiguity in this example.
- **Step 3 — Constructor invocation:** The matched constructor is invoked; the three `println` messages fire in the order `b1`, `b2`, `b3`.
- **Step 4 — Volume computation:** For `b3`:
  $$V_{b3} = l \times w \times h = 2.0 \times 3.0 \times 4.0 = 24.0$$

**Final Output:**

```
No-arg constructor called: Unit Cube
Single-param constructor called: Cube of side 5.0
Three-param constructor called: Cuboid
V(b1) = 1.0
V(b2) = 125.0
V(b3) = 24.0
```

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Type-4 — Copy Constructor

**Problem Statement:** Create a `Point` class with a copy constructor and prove that mutating the original does not affect the copy (deep enough for immutable semantics).

**Source Code:**

```java
class Point {
    int x, y;

    Point(int x, int y) {       // parameterized
        this.x = x;
        this.y = y;
    }

    Point(Point other) {        // copy constructor
        this.x = other.x;
        this.y = other.y;
        System.out.println("Copy constructor invoked");
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println("(" + x + ", " + y + ")");
    }
}

public class Demo3 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Point p1 = new Point(10, 20);  // parameterized
        Point p2 = new Point(p1);      // copy
        p1.x = 999;                    // mutate original
        p1.display();                   // (999, 20)
        p2.display();                   // (10, 20)  ← unaffected
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step trace:**

1. `new Point(10, 20)` → allocates memory, calls the parameterized ctor, `p1.x = 10`, `p1.y = 20`.
2. `new Point(p1)` → the `p1` reference is passed as the argument. The compiler matches it to the `Point(Point other)` constructor.
3. Inside the copy ctor: `this.x = p1.x` (so the *new* object's `x` is set to `10`), `this.y = p1.y` (set to `20`). The two objects are now **independent** on the heap.
4. `p1.x = 999` mutates **only** `p1`. `p2` is untouched.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall — Shallow vs Deep Copy:** The above is a **shallow copy** (field-by-field). If `Point` contained a mutable field like `int[] coords = new int[2]`, both objects would *share* the same array. A true **deep copy** would need to allocate a new array inside the copy constructor. Always mention which kind of copy your code performs in the exam.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Constructor Chaining via `this()`

**Problem Statement:** Eliminate code duplication by chaining constructors using the `this(...)` call.

**Source Code:**

```java
class Employee {
    int id;
    String name;
    double salary;
    String department;

    Employee() {
        this(0, "Unknown", 0.0, "Unassigned");    // chains to 4-param ctor
        System.out.println("No-arg ctor finished");
    }

    Employee(int id) {
        this(id, "Unknown", 0.0, "Unassigned");   // chains to 4-param ctor
    }

    Employee(int id, String name) {
        this(id, name, 0.0, "Unassigned");        // chains to 4-param ctor
    }

    Employee(int id, String name, double salary, String department) {
        this.id = id;
        this.name = name;
        this.salary = salary;
        this.department = department;
        System.out.println("Master 4-param ctor executed");
    }
}
```

**Execution flow for `new Employee()`:**

$$\text{Employee()} \;\xrightarrow{\text{this}(...)}\; \text{Employee(id,name,sal,dept)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{assignments} \;\rightarrow\; \text{return} \;\rightarrow\; \text{resume Employee()} \;\rightarrow\; \text{println}$$

**Step-by-step trace of `new Employee(101, "Anu")`:**

1. Compiler sees the 2-arg call, invokes `Employee(int, String)`.
2. That constructor's **first statement** is `this(101, "Anu", 0.0, "Unassigned")`. Control jumps to the 4-param master ctor.
3. The 4-param ctor executes the four assignments, prints `"Master 4-param ctor executed"`, then returns.
4. Control returns to the 2-param ctor, which has nothing more to do, and returns.
5. The object is fully initialized.

> [!WARNING]
> **Two strict rules for `this()` chaining (Very commonly lost marks in KTU):**
> - The `this(...)` call **MUST be the very first statement** inside the constructor. Putting it on the second line is a **compile-time error**.
> - You **cannot** form a cycle: `A()` calling `this(...)` which lands in `B()` which calls `this(...)` back in `A()`. The compiler rejects infinite cycles.

### 3.5 Constructor vs Method — Side-by-Side Analytical Comparison

| Aspect | Constructor | Method |
|---|---|---|
| Declaration | `ClassName(params) { }` | `returnType methodName(params) { }` |
| Return type | **None** (no `void`) | Required |
| Name | **Same as class** | Any identifier |
| Call | `new ClassName(args)` | `object.methodName(args)` |
| Inheritance | Not inherited | Inherited |
| `final` allowed | No | Yes |
| Compiler default | Yes (if no user ctor) | No |
| Default field values | Variables auto-initialized | Caller must initialize |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Complete Taxonomy of Constructors

This block-level functional architecture diagram maps the **entire classification tree** of Java constructors, which is the most asked concept in KTU 2-mark and 7-mark questions.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["CONSTRUCTORS IN JAVA"] --> B["Compiler Generated"]
    A --> C["User Defined"]
    A --> D["Private Singleton"]

    B --> B1["Default No Arg Ctor"]
    B1 --> B1A["Injected by javac"]
    B1 --> B1B["Only if no user ctor exists"]
    B1 --> B1C["Body is empty"]

    C --> E["No Argument"]
    C --> F["Parameterized"]
    C --> G["Copy Constructor"]

    E --> E1["Custom default init"]
    F --> F1["One Parameter"]
    F --> F2["Two Parameter"]
    F --> F3["N Parameter"]
    G --> G1["Pass existing object"]
    G --> G2["Field by field copy"]
    G --> G3["Shallow vs Deep"]

    D --> D1["private ClassName"]
    D --> D2["Static getInstance"]
    D --> D3["Used in Singleton Pattern"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Object Instantiation Sequence

This sequence diagram explains the **chronological order of memory operations** when a constructor is invoked — a guaranteed KTU question.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant JVM as JVM Runtime
    participant Heap as Heap Memory
    participant Stack as Stack Frame
    participant Ctor as Constructor Body

    JVM->>Heap: 1. Allocate raw memory block
    Heap-->>JVM: Memory address returned
    JVM->>Heap: 2. Default init primitives to zero, refs to null
    JVM->>JVM: 3. Execute instance initializer blocks in order
    JVM->>Ctor: 4. Invoke matching constructor this(args)
    Ctor->>Heap: 5. Assign user supplied values to fields
    Ctor-->>JVM: 6. Return this reference
    JVM->>Stack: 7. Store object address in reference variable
    Stack-->>JVM: Object fully accessible
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Constructor Chaining via `this()`

This flow diagram illustrates how multiple constructors collaborate to eliminate code duplication — directly tied to the KTU 2024 syllabus outcome of "demonstrating constructor overloading and chaining."

```mermaid
graph LR
    C1["Employee no-arg ctor"] -- "this 0 Unknown 0.0 Unassigned" --> C4["Employee master 4 param ctor"]
    C2["Employee id only ctor"] -- "this id Unknown 0.0 Unassigned" --> C4
    C3["Employee id name ctor"] -- "this id name 0.0 Unassigned" --> C4
    C4 -- "this.id = id" --> ASS["Field Assignments Complete"]
    ASS -- "return this" --> OBJ["Fully Initialized Object on Heap"]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Constructor in Inheritance (super call)

Although Module 2 primarily focuses on single-class constructors, KTU often tests the implicit `super()` call — this diagram previews the inheritance mechanics.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Parent class Person has no user ctor"] --> B["Compiler injects Person no-arg ctor"]
    C["Child class Employee extends Person"] --> D["Employee ctor first statement is super args"]
    D --> E["Person ctor executes first"]
    E --> F["Employee field assignments execute next"]
    F --> G["Object fully constructed"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical observation:** If a parent class has only a parameterized constructor and **no default constructor**, the child class's constructor **must explicitly call `super(args)`** as its first statement, otherwise the code fails to compile. This is the #1 cause of "constructor-related compile errors" in KTU lab exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**
*Define a constructor in Java. List any **four** differences between a constructor and a method.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **constructor** is a special member of a class that has the **same name as the class**, has **no return type** (not even `void`), and is **invoked automatically** when an object is created using the `new` keyword. Its purpose is to initialize the newly created object.

| # | Constructor | Method |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Name = Class name | Any valid identifier |
| 2 | No return type | Must have a return type |
| 3 | Called implicitly via `new` | Called explicitly via dot operator |
| 4 | Not inherited / not overridden | Inherited and can be overridden |

> *(Any 4 differences: ½ mark each. Definition: 1 mark. Total = 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**
*What is a **default constructor**? Explain the situation in which the compiler does **not** provide a default constructor.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **default constructor** is a public, no-argument constructor automatically inserted by the Java compiler into a class **only if the programmer has not written any constructor**.

$$C_{default} = \begin{cases} \text{Compiler-generated no-arg} & \text{if } N_{programmer} = 0 \\ \text{NOT generated} & \text{if } N_{programmer} \geq 1 \end{cases}$$

**Situation where it is NOT provided:** If the class contains **at least one user-defined constructor** (parameterized or otherwise), the compiler stops injecting the default. Hence, calling `new ClassName()` with no arguments will result in a **compile-time error** unless the programmer has explicitly written a no-argument constructor. *(1 mark definition + 2 marks explanation = 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **constructor overloading** in Java with a suitable example. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate a **copy constructor**. Show that modifying the original object does not affect the copied object. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition (2 marks):** Constructor overloading in Java is the technique of defining **two or more constructors** within the same class, each having a **different parameter list** (different number, type, or order of parameters). It allows objects to be initialized in **multiple ways** depending on the data available at the point of creation.

**Code (4 marks):**

```java
class Rectangle {
    double length, breadth;

    // Constructor 1: No-arg (unit rectangle)
    Rectangle() {
        this.length = 1.0;
        this.breadth = 1.0;
    }

    // Constructor 2: One arg (square)
    Rectangle(double side) {
        this.length = this.breadth = side;
    }

    // Constructor 3: Two args (general rectangle)
    Rectangle(double l, double b) {
        this.length = l;
        this.breadth = b;
    }

    double area() {
        return this.length * this.breadth;
    }
}

public class OverloadDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Rectangle r1 = new Rectangle();        // calls Ctor 1
        Rectangle r2 = new Rectangle(5.0);     // calls Ctor 2
        Rectangle r3 = new Rectangle(3.0, 4.0); // calls Ctor 3
        System.out.println("Area 1: " + r1.area()); // 1.0
        System.out.println("Area 2: " + r2.area()); // 25.0
        System.out.println("Area 3: " + r3.area()); // 12.0
    }
}
```

**Explanation (1 mark):** The compiler resolves the correct constructor at compile time based on the **argument signature**. This is **static (compile-time) polymorphism** applied to constructors. Constructor overloading improves code readability and provides flexibility in object creation.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Concept (1 mark):** A copy constructor is a constructor that takes an **object of the same class** as a parameter and creates a new object with the **same field values** as the existing object.

**Code (5 marks):**

```java
class Book {
    String title;
    double price;

    // Parameterized constructor
    Book(String t, double p) {
        this.title = t;
        this.price = p;
    }

    // Copy constructor
    Book(Book other) {
        this.title = other.title;
        this.price = other.price;
    }

    void display() {
        System.out.println(title + " | Rs." + price);
    }
}

public class CopyDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Book b1 = new Book("Java Programming", 499.0);
        Book b2 = new Book(b1);     // copy constructor invoked
        b1.price = 999.0;            // mutate original
        b1.display();                // Java Programming | Rs.999.0
        b2.display();                // Java Programming | Rs.499.0  ← unaffected
    }
}
```

**Output Trace (1 mark):**
```
Java Programming | Rs.999.0
Java Programming | Rs.499.0
```
The proof that `b2` still shows the old price demonstrates the **independence of the copy** from the original.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** What is **constructor chaining**? Explain the role of the `this()` keyword with a Java program demonstrating chaining between three constructors. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Differentiate between a **constructor** and a **method** in Java. Write a program that uses a **private constructor** to implement a simple counter using a `static` variable. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition (2 marks):** Constructor chaining is the process of **calling one constructor from another constructor** within the same class using the `this()` keyword. The primary goal is to **eliminate code duplication** by centralizing the actual field assignments in a single "master" constructor.

**Rules (1 mark):**
- `this(args)` must be the **first statement** of the calling constructor.
- At least one constructor in the chain must **not** call `this()` (terminator).
- Cycles are not allowed.

**Code (4 marks):**

```java
class Time {
    int h, m, s;

    Time() {
        this(0, 0, 0);                  // chains to 3-arg
    }

    Time(int h) {
        this(h, 0, 0);                  // chains to 3-arg
    }

    Time(int h, int m) {
        this(h, m, 0);                  // chains to 3-arg
    }

    Time(int h, int m, int s) {        // master terminator
        this.h = h;
        this.m = m;
        this.s = s;
        System.out.println("Master ctor: " + h + ":" + m + ":" + s);
    }
}

public class ChainDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new Time();          // master prints 0:0:0
        new Time(10);        // master prints 10:0:0
        new Time(10, 30);    // master prints 10:30:0
        new Time(10, 30, 45);// master prints 10:30:45
    }
}
```

**Output (bonus):**
```
Master ctor: 0:0:0
Master ctor: 10:0:0
Master ctor: 10:30:0
Master ctor: 10:30:45
```

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (3 marks):** *(see SECTION_3.5 above for full table — 4 key differences are sufficient.)*

**Code with Private Constructor (4 marks):**

```java
class Counter {
    private static int count = 0;   // shared across all instances
    private int serialNo;

    // Private constructor — only callable from inside this class
    private Counter() {
        count++;
        this.serialNo = count;
    }

    // Public factory method — controlled instantiation
    public static Counter getInstance() {
        return new Counter();
    }

    public void show() {
        System.out.println("I am object #" + serialNo);
    }
}

public class PrivateCtorDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Counter c = new Counter();  ← COMPILE ERROR: constructor is private
        Counter c1 = Counter.getInstance();
        Counter c2 = Counter.getInstance();
        Counter c3 = Counter.getInstance();
        c1.show();   // I am object #1
        c2.show();   // I am object #2
        c3.show();   // I am object #3
    }
}
```

**Explanation:** The private constructor restricts external instantiation. The static factory `getInstance()` is the only legitimate path to creating objects. This pattern forms the foundation of the **Singleton Design Pattern** in Java.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on Constructor questions — the six deadly sins:**
> 1. **Adding a return type to a constructor** — writing `void Student()` makes it a *method*, NOT a constructor. The compiler silently accepts it, but it is conceptually wrong. Examiners will cut 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting that the compiler default constructor disappears** when you write *any* user constructor. Students write `new Box()` and panic when the code fails to compile. Always write a no-arg ctor explicitly if needed.
> 3. **Placing `this(...)` on the second line** of a constructor. This is a **compile-time error**. It MUST be the first statement.
> 4. **Confusing overloading with overriding.** Constructors are overloaded, never overridden. Writing `@Override` above a constructor causes a compile error.
> 5. **Missing the `super()` call** in inheritance scenarios. If the parent has no default ctor, the child MUST call `super(args)` as its first statement.
> 6. **Not specifying shallow vs deep copy** in copy constructor answers. Examiners expect at least a one-line clarification.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — `Constructors` (Module 2)**
> - ✅ A constructor is a **special initializer** with the **same name as the class** and **no return type**.
> - ✅ Constructors are **called implicitly** by `new`; they **initialize** object state.
> - ✅ The compiler inserts a **default no-arg constructor** *only if no user constructor exists*.
> - ✅ **Constructor overloading** = multiple constructors, different parameter lists → compile-time polymorphism.
> - ✅ **Copy constructor** = `ClassName(ClassName obj)` → creates a duplicate. Mention shallow/deep copy.
> - ✅ **Constructor chaining** = `this(args)` call; must be the **first statement**; cannot cycle.
> - ✅ **Private constructor** = blocks external instantiation → Singleton / factory pattern.
> - ✅ Constructors are **never inherited** and **cannot be overridden** (`final`, `static`, `abstract` not allowed).
> - ✅ Constructor vs Method: name, return type, invocation, inheritance, allowed modifiers differ.
> - ✅ In inheritance, an implicit `super()` call is inserted as the first statement of every constructor unless an explicit `super(args)` or `this(args)` is written.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: this Keyword
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** this Keyword # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In Java, **`this`** is a **reference variable** that refers to the **current object** — the instance of the class on which a method or constructor is being invoked. It is an **implicit final reference** automatically created by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) and passed (invisibly) as an argument to every instance method and constructor of a class. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PBEOT404 – Module 2):** > The `this` keyword must be understood as a **compiler-synthesized reference**, NOT a user-declared variable. It cannot be assigned a new value, and it is inaccessible inside `static` contexts (methods, blocks, or static variables) because static members do not belong to any specific object. The `this` keyword is fundamentally a **pointer-like reference** stored implicitly on the **JVM stack frame** of every non-static method call, pointing to the **heap-allocated object** that triggered the call. > [!NOTE] > **Memory Location Rule:** > - The `this` reference is stored in the **Stack Memory** (inside the method's stack frame). > - The object it points to lives in the **Heap Memory**. > - The **JVM** automatically initializes `this` before the constructor body or instance method body begins execution. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a classroom where the teacher calls out: **"The student whose roll number matches this attendance slip, please stand up!"** The phrase *"this attendance slip"* is a **stand-in for the specific slip being held right now** — a self-reference to the current slip in hand. In Java, the `this` keyword works identically. When an object calls its own method, `this` acts as a **self-pointing pronoun** analogous to the English words **"me," "myself,"** or **"the current one."** | Everyday English | Java Equivalent | |------------------|-----------------| | *I* finished the work. | `this.workStatus = "done";` | | *My own* name is Rahul. | `this.name = "Rahul";` | | Pass it to *myself*. | `method(this);` | > [!TIP] > If you ever feel confused about which object is being talked about, simply substitute the word **"current object"** wherever you see `this` in code — the meaning becomes instantly clear. ## 1.3 Where `this` Can and Cannot Be Used > [!CAUTION] > **`this` is FORBIDDEN inside:** > - `static` methods > - `static` initializer blocks > - Anywhere outside a non-static context > > Attempting to use `this` in these contexts results in the compile-time error: > **"non-static variable this cannot be referenced from a static context."** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet ## 2.1 The Six Principal Uses of `this` in Java The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests the following operational use cases. Mastering these six patterns is mandatory for full marks in any Module 2 question. ### Use 1: Differentiating Instance Variables from Local/Parameter Variables (Name Clashes) When a constructor or method parameter shares the **same name** as an instance variable, Java applies the **Shadowing Rule** — the local/parameter variable hides the instance variable. The `this` keyword breaks this shadowing and unambiguously refers to the instance variable. ### Use 2: Invoking a Constructor from Another Constructor (Constructor Chaining) Within a constructor, `this(args)` can be used to call **another overloaded constructor** of the **same class**. This is called **explicit constructor invocation** and must be the **first statement** in the calling constructor. ### Use 3: Passing the Current Object as an Argument to Another Method A method may accept an object of the same class. By passing `this`, the caller hands over a **live reference to itself**. ### Use 4: Returning the Current Object from a Method A method can return `this` to enable **method chaining** (also called *fluent interface* style), where multiple methods can be invoked sequentially on the same object in a single statement. ### Use 5: Invoking a Current Class Method (Though Implicit) Java automatically prepends `this.` to method calls, so writing `display();` inside a method is equivalent to writing `this.display();`. Explicit usage is rare but syntactically valid. ### Use 6: Used as an Argument in Constructor Calls When one constructor passes the current object as a parameter to another class's constructor — useful in composition and dependency injection patterns. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet | # | Use Case | Syntax Pattern | Mandatory Rule | Common Pitfall | |---|----------|---------------|----------------|----------------| | 1 | Instance vs. local variable disambiguation | `this.name = name;` | Parameter name must match instance variable name | Forgetting `this.` causes silent data-loss bug | | 2 | Constructor chaining | `this(arg1, arg2);` | Must be the **first statement** | Compile error if placed second | | 3 | Pass current object as argument | `obj.method(this);` | Receiving method must accept same class type | Cannot use inside `static` method | | 4 | Return current object | `return this;` | Method return type must be class name | Returns reference, not a copy | | 5 | Invoke current class method | `this.print();` | Rarely written explicitly | May cause `StackOverflowError` if recursive | | 6 | Pass `this` in constructor | `new A(this);` | Used in composition/aggregation | Circular reference risk | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Golden Rule of KTU Valuation:** > Examiners award full marks **only** when students explicitly state the **purpose** behind using `this`, not just the syntax. A line like `this.name = name;` with the explanation *"this resolves the name conflict between the local parameter `name` and the instance variable `name`"* scores higher than just writing the code. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Builder Design Pattern (Gang of Four):** Relies heavily on `return this;` for fluent object construction. - **Event Handling in JavaFX / Swing:** `this` is used to register the current object as a listener. - **JavaBeans Specification:** Setter methods universally use `this.field = field;` to disambiguate. - **Method Chaining in Stream API:** Internal stream operations use `this`-style references for fluent pipelines. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix This section provides **exhaustive, line-by-line Java programs** that demonstrate each `this` use case. Every output line is verified. ## 3.1 Use Case 1: Resolving Instance vs. Local Variable Ambiguity ```java class Student { String name; int rollNo; Student(String name, int rollNo) { this.name = name; // LHS: instance variable; RHS: local parameter this.rollNo = rollNo; // Shadowing is broken by 'this' } void display() { System.out.println("Name: " + this.name + ", Roll: " + this.rollNo); } public static void main(String[] args) { Student s = new Student("Ananya", 42); s.display(); } } ``` **Step-by-step execution trace:** | Step | Statement | Effect on Memory | |------|-----------|------------------| | 1 | `new Student("Ananya", 42)` | JVM allocates a `Student` object in **heap memory** | | 2 | `this` is initialized | `this` (in stack frame) points to the newly allocated `Student` object | | 3 | `this.name = name;` | Instance variable `name` of the object is set to `"Ananya"` | | 4 | `this.rollNo = rollNo;` | Instance variable `rollNo` is set to `42` | | 5 | `s.display();` | `display()` is called; `this` inside it points to `s` | | 6 | `println` executes | Output: `Name: Ananya, Roll: 42` | ## 3.2 Use Case 2: Constructor Chaining Using `this()` ```java class Box { int length, width, height; Box() { this(1, 1, 1); // Calls 3-arg constructor System.out.println("Default Box created."); } Box(int side) { this(side, side, side); // Calls 3-arg constructor System.out.println("Cube created with side = " + side); } Box(int length, int width, int height) { this.length = length; this.width = width; this.height = height; System.out.println("Box created: " + length + "x" + width + "x" + height); } public static void main(String[] args) { Box b1 = new Box(); System.out.println("---"); Box b2 = new Box(5); } } ``` **Expected Output:** ``` Box created: 1x1x1 Default Box created. --- Box created: 5x5x5 Cube created with side = 5 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why does the print order flow upward?** > When `Box()` is called, it executes `this(1,1,1);` *first*. The JVM suspends the no-arg constructor, runs the 3-arg constructor to completion, and **then resumes** the no-arg constructor's `println`. ## 3.3 Use Cases 3 & 4 Combined: Passing `this` and Returning `this` ```java class Calculator { int value; Calculator setValue(int v) { this.value = v; return this; // Returns current object } Calculator add(int n) { this.value = this.value + n; return this; } void print() { System.out.println("Value = " + this.value); return; } void passToHelper(Helper h) { h.receive(this); } public static void main(String[] args) { Calculator c = new Calculator(); c.setValue(10).add(5).add(20).print(); } } class Helper { void receive(Calculator c) { System.out.println("Helper received calculator with value: " + c.value); } } ``` **Expected Output:** ``` Value = 35 ``` **Analytical chain explanation:** $$ \text{Chain: } c \xrightarrow{\text{setValue(10)}} c \xrightarrow{\text{add(5)}} c \xrightarrow{\text{add(20)}} c \xrightarrow{\text{print()}} \text{Output} $$ $$ \text{Final value} = ((0 + 10) + 5) + 20 = 35 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Every method in the chain **returns the same object reference** `c` because each method returns `this`. This is the **fluent interface / method chaining** pattern. ## 3.4 The `this` Reference Equation For any non-static method invocation on object `O` of class `C`: $$ \texttt{this} \equiv \text{O} \quad \text{(for the duration of the call)} $$ Where `O` is the object reference through which the method was invoked. The JVM binds `this` to `O` at method entry and unbinds it at method return. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Memory-Level View of `this` Inside a Method Call ```mermaid flowchart TB classDef stack fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef heap fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef code fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 codeBlock["JAVA SOURCE CODE
Student s = new Student('Anu', 7);
s.display();"]:::code stackArea["STACK MEMORY
------------------------
main() frame
s --> 0xABC
------------------------
display() frame
this --> 0xABC"]:::stack heapArea["HEAP MEMORY
------------------------
0xABC : Student object
name = 'Anu'
rollNo = 7"]:::heap codeBlock --> stackArea stackArea --> heapArea ``` ## 4.2 Use-Case Classification Tree of `this` Keyword ```mermaid flowchart TD classDef root fill:#0d6efd,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef leaf fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef mid fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#fd7e14,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 A["THIS KEYWORD IN JAVA"]:::root A --> B["Inside Instance Method"]:::mid A --> C["Inside Constructor"]:::mid B --> B1["Refers to current object"]:::leaf B --> B2["Disambiguate instance vs local variable"]:::leaf B --> B3["Pass this as argument to another method"]:::leaf B --> B4["Return this for method chaining"]:::leaf C --> C1["Disambiguate instance vs parameter"]:::leaf C --> C2["Call another constructor this args"]:::leaf C --> C3["Pass this to another classes constructor"]:::leaf D["NOT ALLOWED IN"]:::root D --> D1["static method"]:::leaf D --> D2["static block"]:::leaf D --> D3["static variable initializer"]:::leaf ``` ## 4.3 Constructor Chaining Execution Flow ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant Main as main method participant B0 as Box default participant B3 as Box int int int participant Heap as Heap Memory Main->>B0: new Box() activate B0 B0->>B3: this(1,1,1) activate B3 B3->>Heap: set length=1, width=1, height=1 B3-->>B0: print Box 1x1x1 deactivate B3 B0-->>Main: print Default Box created deactivate B0 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** *What is the `this` keyword in Java? Why cannot it be used inside a `static` method?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The `this` keyword in Java is an **implicit reference variable** that refers to the **current object** — the instance on which the current method or constructor is being invoked. It is automatically created and initialized by the JVM, and it is passed invisibly to every non-static method and constructor. It cannot be used inside a `static` method because **static members belong to the class itself, not to any specific object**. Since there is no *"current object"* in a static context, the `this` reference has nothing to point to, resulting in the **compile-time error**: *"non-static variable this cannot be referenced from a static context."* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** *Explain with an example how the `this` keyword can be used to resolve the ambiguity between instance variables and parameters.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** When a parameter name in a constructor or method is **identical** to an instance variable name, the parameter *shadows* the instance variable. Using `this.variableName` explicitly refers to the instance variable, breaking the shadowing. ```java class Employee { int id; String name; Employee(int id, String name) { this.id = id; // LHS = instance var, RHS = parameter this.name = name; } } ``` Without `this`, the assignments `id = id;` and `name = name;` would assign the parameter to itself, leaving instance variables uninitialized (default values). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. KTU provides an **internal choice** — students answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** **(a)** Explain the `this` keyword in Java. List any **four** significant uses of `this` with suitable code snippets. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate **constructor chaining** using the `this()` call. Explain the rules that must be followed when using `this()` inside a constructor. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution **(a) The `this` keyword and its four uses (7 Marks):** The `this` keyword is a **reference variable** in Java that always points to the **current object** — the object on which the current method or constructor was invoked. It is auto-generated by the JVM and is available in every non-static context. **Four significant uses with code:** **1. Differentiating instance variable from local variable:** ```java class Account { double balance; Account(double balance) { this.balance = balance; // LHS: instance var, RHS: parameter } } ``` **2. Invoking current class method explicitly:** ```java void show() { System.out.println("Show called"); this.display(); } void display() { System.out.println("Display called"); } ``` **3. Passing `this` as an argument:** ```java void register(Team t) { t.addMember(this); // passes current object to Team } ``` **4. Returning `this` for method chaining:** ```java Counter increment() { this.count++; return this; } ``` **(b) Constructor chaining demonstration (7 Marks):** ```java class Rectangle { int length, width; String color; Rectangle() { this(10, 5, "Red"); // MUST be first statement System.out.println("Default Rectangle"); } Rectangle(int l, int w) { this(l, w, "Blue"); } Rectangle(int length, int width, String color) { this.length = length; this.width = width; this.color = color; System.out.println("Rectangle: " + length + "x" + width + " " + color); } public static void main(String[] args) { Rectangle r1 = new Rectangle(); System.out.println("---"); Rectangle r2 = new Rectangle(20, 30); } } ``` **Output:** ``` Rectangle: 10x5 Red Default Rectangle --- Rectangle: 20x30 Blue ``` **Rules for `this()` in constructors:** 1. `this()` must appear as the **first statement** in the constructor body. 2. Only **one** `this()` call is permitted per constructor (to prevent recursion/ambiguity). 3. The argument list must match an **existing overloaded constructor** signature. 4. `this()` and `super()` **cannot be used together** in the same constructor — they are mutually exclusive as the first statement. 5. Cyclic constructor chaining (A calls B calls A) causes a **compile-time error**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Write a Java program that uses the `this` keyword to **(i)** disambiguate instance variables, **(ii)** pass the current object to a method, and **(iii)** return the current object for method chaining. Show the output. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Differentiate between `this` and `super` keywords in Java. Can both be used in the same constructor? Justify. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution Outline **(a)** Single class `ChainingDemo` with three methods: - Constructor with parameter shadowing — uses `this.field = param`. - `register(Helper h)` — calls `h.save(this);`. - `setName(String n) { this.name = n; return this; }` enabling `obj.setName("X").setAge(20);`. **(b) Comparison table (7 marks content):** | Aspect | `this` | `super` | |--------|--------|---------| | Refers to | Current object of same class | Parent class object / members | | Used in | Non-static context only | Non-static context (instance), static for static members | | Constructor call | `this(args)` — calls same-class constructor | `super(args)` — calls parent constructor | | Variable access | `this.var` — current class instance variable | `super.var` — parent class instance variable | | Method invocation | `this.method()` | `super.method()` | | Can both appear in same constructor? | **No**, only one of `this()` or `super()` may be the first statement | **Justification:** Both `this()` and `super()` must be the **first executable statement** of a constructor. Since only one statement can occupy the first position, they are **mutually exclusive**. If neither is explicitly written, the compiler **implicitly inserts `super()`** as the first call, meaning a constructor using `this()` effectively delegates upward to the parent via the chained constructor's `super()` call. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to mention the "current object" phrasing** in the definition → loses 1 mark. > 2. **Writing `this()` as a regular method call** without saying it must be the *first statement* → loses 1–2 marks. > 3. **Confusing `this` with `super`** in tabular comparison questions → loses 2 marks. > 4. **Trying to use `this` inside a `static main`** to demonstrate examples → compile error, full code loses 2–3 marks. > 5. **Failing to show program output** in Part B (a) questions → KTU deducts 1 mark even if code is correct. > 6. **Writing `this = newObject;`** in theory answers → examiner treats as fundamental misunderstanding, heavy penalty. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before Exam:** - `this` is a **reference variable**, not a primitive; its type is the **class in which it is used**. - It is **auto-created by the JVM**; the programmer never declares it. - It refers to the **current invoking object** — the object through which a non-static method/constructor is called. - **`this` cannot be used inside `static` methods, static blocks, or static initializers.** - **Use 1:** Resolve name conflict between instance variable and parameter/local variable. - **Use 2:** Invoke another constructor of the same class via `this(args)` — must be **first statement**. - **Use 3:** Pass the current object as a method argument → enables object collaboration. - **Use 4:** Return the current object via `return this;` → enables **method chaining / fluent interface**. - `this` and `super()` **cannot both be the first statement** of a constructor — they are mutually exclusive. - Recursive constructor invocation through `this()` causes **compile-time error** (cyclic chaining). - `this` is a **`final` reference** — its value (the object it points to) **cannot be changed** once initialized. - The compiler implicitly inserts `super()` (not `this()`) as the first statement of every constructor if neither is written. - Method chaining pattern: `obj.method1().method2().method3();` works only because each method returns `this`. - `this` is **not a local variable** — it is a parameter-like reference passed by the JVM.

topics.title: Method Overloading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Method Overloading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Method Overloading (Compile-Time Polymorphism / Static Binding)**
> Method Overloading is a core feature of Object-Oriented Programming in Java that allows a class to declare **multiple methods with the same name** but with **different parameter lists** (different number of parameters, different types of parameters, or different order of parameter types) within the same class. The compiler determines which method to invoke at **compile time** based on the method signature, which is why it is also referred to as **Static Binding** or **Early Binding**.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for PBEOT404, method overloading is classified under *Compile-Time Polymorphism* and forms one of the foundational pillars of the polymorphism pillar of OOP.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Multilingual Greeting" Concept**
> Imagine you are a **universal translator** standing at the door of an international conference. When a person approaches you, you greet them based on **what language they speak**:
>
> - If they speak **English**, you say "Hello!"
> - If they speak **Spanish**, you say "¡Hola!"
> - If they speak **French**, you say "Bonjour!"
>
> The **action is the same** (greeting), and the **method name is the same** (greet), but the **internal execution differs** based on the *input type* (language). This is exactly how method overloading works in Java — the method name is reused, but the **signature** (input parameters) determines which version executes.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Method Signature** | The combination of the method's **name** and its **parameter list** (number, type, and order of parameters). The return type is NOT part of the signature. |
| **Compile-Time Polymorphism** | Polymorphism resolved by the Java compiler at compile time, before the program runs. |
| **Static Binding** | The binding of a method call to its definition happens during compilation, not at runtime. |
| **Early Binding** | Synonym for static binding — the method to invoke is determined *early* (at compile time). |
| **Type Promotion** | The automatic conversion of a smaller data type to a larger data type by the compiler when no exact match is found. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly emphasizes the distinction between **method overloading** (compile-time) and **method overriding** (runtime). This distinction is a **favourite question** in Part A (3-mark) and frequently tested in Part B (14-mark) questions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Rules for Method Overloading (KTU Board Examiner Focus)

For two methods to be considered a valid **overloaded pair**, they must satisfy ALL of the following rules:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 1: Same Method Name**
> Both methods must share the **exact same identifier name** (case-sensitive in Java).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 2: Different Parameter Lists (Mandatory)**
> The parameter list must differ in at least ONE of these three ways:
> 1. **Number of parameters** (arity)
> 2. **Type of parameters** (data type)
> 3. **Order of parameters** (sequence of types)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 3: Return Type Does NOT Matter**
> Changing only the return type while keeping the parameter list identical is **NOT** method overloading — it is a **compilation error**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 4: Access Modifiers Are Irrelevant**
> Overloaded methods can have different access modifiers (public, private, protected). The compiler does not use them for resolution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule 5: Exception Lists Are Irrelevant**
> Overloaded methods can throw different checked or unchecked exceptions. The compiler does not consider them for method resolution.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Overloading Criterion | Valid? | Example |
|---|----------------------|--------|---------|
| 1 | Different number of parameters | ✅ YES | `add(int a, int b)` vs `add(int a, int b, int c)` |
| 2 | Different types of parameters | ✅ YES | `add(int a, int b)` vs `add(double a, double b)` |
| 3 | Different order of parameter types | ✅ YES | `display(int a, String b)` vs `display(String a, int b)` |
| 4 | Only return type differs | ❌ NO (Compile Error) | `int get()` and `double get()` — **INVALID** |
| 5 | Only access modifier differs | ❌ NO (Duplicate Method) | Both become the same method — **COMPILATION ERROR** |
| 6 | Only parameter name differs | ❌ NO (Duplicate Method) | `add(int x)` vs `add(int y)` — **INVALID** |

### 2.3 Type Promotion in Method Overloading (The Silent Hero)

When the compiler cannot find an **exact match** for the method call, it promotes the argument to a **larger data type** following Java's automatic type promotion hierarchy:

```
byte  →  short  →  int  →  long  →  float  →  double
                      ↑
                    char
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Promotion Hierarchy (Bottom to Top):**
> `byte` (1 byte) → `short` (2 bytes) → `char` (2 bytes) → `int` (4 bytes) → `long` (8 bytes) → `float` (4 bytes) → `double` (8 bytes)

If no match is found even after promotion, the compiler throws an **ambiguity error**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Method overloading is heavily used in:

- **Java Standard Library:** `System.out.println()` is overloaded **10+ times** to accept `int`, `double`, `char`, `String`, `boolean`, `Object`, etc.
- **Constructors:** Every class implicitly overloads its constructor (default + parameterized).
- **Mathematical Libraries:** `Math.max(int, int)`, `Math.max(double, double)`, `Math.max(float, float)`, `Math.max(long, long)`.
- **API Design:** Improves **readability** and **API consistency** — programmers remember one method name, not ten.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Case 1: Overloading by Varying Number of Parameters (Arity)

```java
class Calculator {
    // Overload 1: Two parameters
    int add(int a, int b) {
        return a + b;
    }

    // Overload 2: Three parameters
    int add(int a, int b, int c) {
        return a + b + c;
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Calculator calc = new Calculator();
        System.out.println(calc.add(10, 20));        // Calls add(int, int)
        System.out.println(calc.add(10, 20, 30));    // Calls add(int, int, int)
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step Logical Analysis:**

| Step | Method Call | Parameter Count | Compiler Decision | Method Invoked |
|------|-------------|-----------------|-------------------|----------------|
| 1 | `calc.add(10, 20)` | 2 arguments | Exact match found for 2-parameter `add()` | `add(int a, int b)` |
| 2 | `calc.add(10, 20, 30)` | 3 arguments | Exact match found for 3-parameter `add()` | `add(int a, int b, int c)` |

### 3.2 Case 2: Overloading by Varying Type of Parameters

```java
class Printer {
    void print(int n) {
        System.out.println("Integer: " + n);
    }

    void print(double d) {
        System.out.println("Double: " + d);
    }

    void print(String s) {
        System.out.println("String: " + s);
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step Logical Analysis:**

$$
\text{Compile-time Resolution} = f(\text{arg\_type}) \rightarrow \text{select matching overload}
$$

| Step | Method Call | Argument Type | Match? | Method Selected |
|------|-------------|---------------|--------|-----------------|
| 1 | `print(42)` | `int` | ✅ Exact | `print(int n)` |
| 2 | `print(3.14)` | `double` | ✅ Exact | `print(double d)` |
| 3 | `print("Hello")` | `String` | ✅ Exact | `print(String s)` |

### 3.3 Case 3: Overloading by Varying Order of Parameters

```java
class Data {
    void show(int a, String b) {
        System.out.println("int: " + a + ", String: " + b);
    }

    void show(String a, int b) {
        System.out.println("String: " + a + ", int: " + b);
    }
}
```

**Logical Proof:**

$$
\text{Signature}_1 = \text{show}(\text{int}, \text{String})
$$

$$
\text{Signature}_2 = \text{show}(\text{String}, \text{int})
$$

$$
\text{Signature}_1 \neq \text{Signature}_2 \quad \therefore \text{Valid Overload}
$$

| Method Call | Argument Order | Method Invoked |
|-------------|----------------|----------------|
| `show(10, "Java")` | `(int, String)` | `show(int, String)` |
| `show("Java", 10)` | `(String, int)` | `show(String, int)` |

### 3.4 Case 4: Type Promotion & Ambiguity

```java
class Demo {
    void show(int a) {
        System.out.println("int method");
    }

    void show(long a) {
        System.out.println("long method");
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Demo d = new Demo();
        d.show(10);      // Exact match: int
        d.show(10L);     // Exact match: long
    }
}
```

**Logical Analysis for `d.show(10)`:**

$$
\text{Argument} = 10 \rightarrow \text{Type: int}
$$

$$
\text{Available overloads: } \text{show(int)}, \text{ show(long)}
$$

$$
\exists \text{ exact match for int} \Rightarrow \text{No promotion needed}
$$

**Result:** `int method` is invoked.

> [!WARNING]
> **Ambiguity Case:** If we also had `show(double a)`, then calling `d.show('A')` would cause the compiler to fail because `char` can be promoted to BOTH `int` and `double`, and the compiler cannot decide which to choose. This is the classic **method overloading ambiguity** scenario.

### 3.5 Comprehensive Analytical Matrix

| Overloading Feature | Compile Time? | Affects Signature? | Increases Readability? | Common in KTU Exams? |
|---------------------|---------------|---------------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| Different parameter count | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Different parameter types | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Different parameter order | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Different return type only | ❌ No | ❌ No | ❌ No | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (Trick Q) |
| Type promotion | ✅ Yes | N/A | N/A | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |
| Ambiguity error | ✅ Yes | N/A | N/A | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Method Overloading Resolution Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Method Call: add(10, 20)"] --> B{"Exact match for signature?"}
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Invoke matched method"]
    B -- "No" --> D{"Type promotion possible?"}
    D -- "Yes, single path" --> E["Promote and invoke"]
    D -- "Yes, multiple paths" --> F["AMBIGUITY ERROR"]
    D -- "No promotion fits" --> G["COMPILATION ERROR"]
    C --> H["Static Binding Complete"]
    E --> H
    F --> I["Program fails to compile"]
    G --> I
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: Compile-Time Method Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC["Source Code Phase"]
        A1["Programmer writes: obj.add(5, 10)"]
    end

    subgraph CMP["Compiler Phase"]
        B1["Lexical Analysis"]
        B2["Signature Matching Engine"]
        B3["Method Resolution Table"]
    end

    subgraph BIN["Bytecode Phase"]
        C1["invokevirtual #4 (int,int)"]
        C2["invokestatic  #5 (int,int,int)"]
    end

    subgraph RUN["Runtime Phase"]
        D1["JVM executes pre-resolved method"]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
```

### 4.3 Type Promotion Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["byte"] --> B["short"]
    B --> C["int"]
    A --> C
    D["char"] --> C
    C --> E["long"]
    E --> F["float"]
    F --> G["double"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Arrows point from a *smaller* type to the type it can be *promoted to*. For example, a `byte` can be promoted to `short`, `int`, `long`, `float`, or `double` following the arrows.

### 4.4 Overloading Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start["Two methods with same name"] --> Q1{"Parameter lists different?"}
    Q1 -- "No" --> Err1["COMPILATION ERROR: Duplicate Method"]
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> Q2{"Return type only differs?"}
    Q2 -- "Yes, and params are same" --> Err2["COMPILATION ERROR: Cannot overload by return type alone"]
    Q2 -- "No, params genuinely differ" --> Valid["VALID Method Overloading"]
    Valid --> Sub1["Differs by COUNT"]
    Valid --> Sub2["Differs by TYPE"]
    Valid --> Sub3["Differs by ORDER"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define method overloading in Java. List any two ways to achieve it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Method overloading is a mechanism in Java that allows a class to have multiple methods with the **same name** but **different parameter lists**. It is a form of **compile-time polymorphism** (static binding) where the compiler determines which method to invoke based on the method signature.
>
> **Two ways to achieve it (1.5 marks):**
> 1. **Varying the number of parameters:** e.g., `add(int, int)` and `add(int, int, int)`.
> 2. **Varying the types of parameters:** e.g., `add(int, int)` and `add(double, double)`.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**
**Why is method overloading called compile-time polymorphism? Can we achieve method overloading by changing only the return type of a method? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Why compile-time polymorphism (1.5 marks):** Method overloading is called compile-time polymorphism because the Java compiler resolves the method call at **compile time** itself by matching the argument types with the available method signatures. The binding between the method call and the method definition is fixed before the program executes, which is why it is also called **static binding** or **early binding**.
>
> **Return type only (1.5 marks):** **No**, we cannot achieve method overloading by changing only the return type while keeping the parameter list identical. This results in a **compilation error** because the method signature in Java consists only of the method name and parameter list — the return type is **not** considered part of the signature. Hence, the compiler treats both methods as duplicates.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of method overloading in Java with a suitable example program. Discuss any **three** different ways to achieve method overloading. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept Explanation (2 marks):**
Method overloading in Java allows a class to define multiple methods with the **same name** but **different parameter lists** within the same class. The compiler determines which overloaded method to execute at **compile time** based on the arguments passed. This enhances code readability and reusability.

**Java Program Demonstrating All Three Ways (3 marks):**

```java
class OverloadDemo {

    // (i) Overloading by changing NUMBER of parameters
    int sum(int a, int b) {
        return a + b;
    }

    int sum(int a, int b, int c) {
        return a + b + c;
    }

    // (ii) Overloading by changing TYPE of parameters
    double sum(double a, double b) {
        return a + b;
    }

    // (iii) Overloading by changing ORDER of parameters
    void display(int id, String name) {
        System.out.println("ID: " + id + ", Name: " + name);
    }

    void display(String name, int id) {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", ID: " + id);
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        OverloadDemo obj = new OverloadDemo();
        System.out.println("Sum of 2 ints: " + obj.sum(10, 20));
        System.out.println("Sum of 3 ints: " + obj.sum(10, 20, 30));
        System.out.println("Sum of 2 doubles: " + obj.sum(5.5, 4.5));
        obj.display(101, "Alice");
        obj.display("Bob", 102);
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Sum of 2 ints: 30
Sum of 3 ints: 60
Sum of 2 doubles: 10.0
ID: 101, Name: Alice
Name: Bob, ID: 102
```

**Discussion of the Three Ways (2 marks):**

1. **By Number of Parameters (Arity):** `sum(int, int)` vs `sum(int, int, int)` — distinguished by the count of arguments.
2. **By Type of Parameters:** `sum(int, int)` vs `sum(double, double)` — distinguished by the data type of arguments.
3. **By Order of Parameters:** `display(int, String)` vs `display(String, int)` — distinguished by the sequence of argument types.

---

**(b)** What is **type promotion** in method overloading? Explain with an example. What happens when the compiler faces **ambiguity** during method resolution? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Type Promotion Definition (2 marks):**
> Type promotion is the automatic conversion of a smaller data type argument to a larger data type by the Java compiler when no exact match is found for the method call. The compiler promotes the argument following the hierarchy: `byte → short → int → long → float → double`.

**Example Demonstrating Type Promotion (3 marks):**

```java
class PromoDemo {
    void show(int a) {
        System.out.println("int method: " + a);
    }

    void show(double a) {
        System.out.println("double method: " + a);
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        PromoDemo obj = new PromoDemo();
        obj.show(100);      // Exact int match — int method
        obj.show('A');      // char promoted to int — int method
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
int method: 100
int method: 65
```

In the second call, the character `'A'` (whose ASCII value is 65) has no exact `show(char)` overload, so the compiler promotes `char` → `int` and invokes `show(int)`.

**Ambiguity Explanation (2 marks):**
> Ambiguity occurs when the compiler finds **more than one possible match** for a method call after type promotion and cannot uniquely determine which method to invoke. This results in a **compilation error**.

**Example of Ambiguity:**
```java
class AmbiguityDemo {
    void show(int a, float b) { }
    void show(float a, int b) { }
}
// obj.show(10, 20);  // ERROR: ambiguous — int can go to float, and 20 is int for both
```

In this case, the integer `20` matches both `float` (in the first method) and `int` (in the second method), and the compiler cannot decide — leading to a **compile-time ambiguity error**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - June 2024]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **method overloading** and **method overriding** in Java. Explain why method overloading is called **compile-time polymorphism** with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Difference Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | Method Overloading | Method Overriding |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| **Definition** | Same method name, different parameter lists within the **same class** | Same method name, same parameter list, in **parent and child classes** |
| **Polymorphism Type** | Compile-time (Static) polymorphism | Runtime (Dynamic) polymorphism |
| **Binding** | Static / Early binding | Dynamic / Late binding |
| **Inheritance** | Not required | Mandatory (requires parent-child relationship) |
| **Method Signature** | Must differ | Must be identical |
| **Return Type** | Can be different | Must be same or covariant |
| **Access Modifier** | Can be different | Cannot reduce visibility |
| **Performance** | Faster (resolved at compile time) | Slower (resolved at runtime) |
| **Occurs In** | Single class | Two classes (IS-A relationship) |

**Compile-Time Polymorphism Explanation (2 marks):**
Method overloading is called **compile-time polymorphism** because the Java compiler examines the method call, matches the argument list against all available overloaded method signatures, and binds the call to the appropriate method **before** the program is run. The `.class` file generated contains direct references (via `invokevirtual` bytecode) to the specific method, leaving no decision-making work for the JVM at runtime.

**Diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC["Source Code:<br/>add(10,20)<br/>add(10,20,30)"] --> CMP["Java Compiler"]
    CMP --> R1["Resolve to add(int,int)"]
    CMP --> R2["Resolve to add(int,int,int)"]
    R1 --> BC["Bytecode: invokevirtual #4"]
    R2 --> BC2["Bytecode: invokevirtual #5"]
    BC --> JVM["JVM: No resolution needed"]
    BC2 --> JVM
```

---

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate method overloading for calculating the **area of different shapes** (circle, rectangle, square). Explain how the compiler distinguishes between the overloaded methods. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Java Program (4 marks):**

```java
class ShapeArea {

    // Area of square: side × side
    double area(double side) {
        System.out.println("Calculating area of SQUARE");
        return side * side;
    }

    // Area of rectangle: length × breadth
    double area(double length, double breadth) {
        System.out.println("Calculating area of RECTANGLE");
        return length * breadth;
    }

    // Area of circle: π × r × r
    double area(int radius) {
        System.out.println("Calculating area of CIRCLE");
        return Math.PI * radius * radius;
    }
}

public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ShapeArea shape = new ShapeArea();

        System.out.println("Square area: " + shape.area(5.0));
        System.out.println("Rectangle area: " + shape.area(4.0, 6.0));
        System.out.println("Circle area: " + shape.area(7));
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Calculating area of SQUARE
Square area: 25.0
Calculating area of RECTANGLE
Rectangle area: 24.0
Calculating area of CIRCLE
Circle area: 153.93804002589985
```

**Compiler's Resolution Logic (3 marks):**
The Java compiler distinguishes between overloaded methods using the **method signature**, which comprises the method name and the parameter list. The decision-making process is:

1. **For `shape.area(5.0)`** — The argument `5.0` is of type `double`. The compiler finds an exact match: `area(double)`. Method selected ✓
2. **For `shape.area(4.0, 6.0)`** — Two arguments of type `double`. The compiler matches `area(double, double)` based on **number** AND **type** of parameters. Method selected ✓
3. **For `shape.area(7)`** — One argument of type `int`. The compiler finds an exact match: `area(int)`. Method selected ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Even though all three methods share the name `area`, the compiler uses the **arity** (count) and **data type** of arguments to create a unique mapping. This entire resolution happens at **compile time**, which is why method overloading is a **static binding** mechanism.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Board Exam Pitfalls):**
>
