> 1. **❌ Stating that overloading is achieved by changing return type:** This is the **#1 most common error** in KTU exams. Always clarify that **return type is NOT part of the method signature** in Java.
> 2. **❌ Confusing overloading with overriding:** Overloading happens **within the same class**; overriding happens **across parent and child classes**. Mixing these two costs 3-4 marks instantly.
> 3. **❌ Forgetting that parameter NAMES don't matter:** Writing `add(int x)` vs `add(int y)` is NOT overloading — it's a duplicate method error.
> 4. **❌ Missing the type promotion explanation:** When asked "what happens when no exact match is found?", many students skip the promotion hierarchy. This is a **2-mark deduction** in 14-mark questions.
> 5. **❌ Forgetting the keyword "compile-time":** Always explicitly state that overloading is resolved at **compile time** (not runtime). The KTU examiner awards marks for this keyword.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- 📌 **Method Overloading** = Same method name + **Different parameter list** (count, type, or order).
- 📌 It is a form of **Compile-Time Polymorphism** (also called **Static Binding** or **Early Binding**).
- 📌 The **method signature** = method name + parameter list. **Return type is NOT part of the signature**.
- 📌 **You CANNOT** overload methods by changing **only** the return type or **only** the access modifier.
- 📌 **Type promotion hierarchy:** `byte → short → int → long → float → double` (with `char` → `int`).
- 📌 **Ambiguity** arises when one argument can be promoted to **multiple** matching parameter types — resulting in a **compilation error**.
- 📌 Overloading **does NOT require inheritance** — it occurs within a single class.
- 📌 The **Java compiler** (not the JVM) resolves overloaded method calls — this is the essence of static binding.
- 📌 Real-world examples: `System.out.println()`, `Math.max()`, `String.valueOf()` are all overloaded.
- 📌 **KTU Hot Tip:** Always pair the term "method overloading" with "compile-time polymorphism" in your answers — examiners look for this keyword pairing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inheritance - Super Class
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inheritance - Super Class

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Object Oriented Programming (OOP)** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus, a **Super Class** (also called a **Parent Class**, **Base Class**, or **Superclass**) is the class whose features (fields, methods, and nested types) are **inherited** by another class. The class that inherits these features is formally called the **Sub Class** (or **Derived Class** / **Child Class**).

The relationship is established in Java using the `extends` keyword:

```java
class SubClass extends SuperClass {
    // inherits all non-private members of SuperClass
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Inheritance is one of the four pillars of OOP. The super class concept is foundational for understanding **code reuse, polymorphism, method overriding, and constructor chaining** — all of which are high-weightage topics in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine a **family tree**:

- Your **grandfather** is the *super class*.
- Your **father** inherits from the grandfather (so he is a *sub class* of grandfather, and simultaneously a *super class* to you).
- **You** inherit from your father (you are a *sub class* of father).

Notice the bidirectional role — a class can be both a super class (to a child) and a sub class (to its own parent). This is exactly how Java's inheritance hierarchy works.

Another analogy: A generic blueprint of a **"Vehicle"** (which has wheels, engine, and a `start()` method) is the super class. Specific vehicles like **"Car"** or **"Bike"** are sub classes that inherit those generic features and add their own specific ones (e.g., `AC` for a car, `kickStart` for a bike).

## 1.3 The `Object` Class — The Ultimate Super Class

In Java, every class implicitly inherits from `java.lang.Object`. This means **Object** is the root of the entire class hierarchy.

> [!NOTE]
> If a class is declared without an `extends` clause, Java silently makes it a direct sub class of `Object`. Therefore, methods like `toString()`, `equals()`, `hashCode()`, and `getClass()` are available on *every* Java object — because every class has Object as its (perhaps distant) super class.

---

## 1.4 Hierarchy Visualization (Textual Overview)

$$
\text{Object} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Vehicle} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Car} \;\rightarrow\; \text{ElectricCar}
$$

In the above chain:
- `Object` is the **super class** of `Vehicle`
- `Vehicle` is the **super class** of `Car` AND a **sub class** of `Object`
- `Car` is the **super class** of `ElectricCar` AND a **sub class** of `Vehicle`

This single-directionality rule is critical: **Inheritance in Java is unidirectional** (from super class downward to sub class).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Meanings of "Super" in Java

The term "super" has **two distinct uses** that KTU examiners frequently test:

### (A) `super` as a **keyword** — to access super class members
### (B) `super()` as a **constructor call** — to invoke the parent constructor

| Construct | Purpose | Usage Context |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `super.member` | Access hidden super class field or overridden method | Anywhere in sub class |
| `super(args)` | Explicitly call a super class constructor | Must be the **first statement** in sub class constructor |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** A call to `super()` or `this()` **must be the first statement** in any constructor. You cannot have both in the same constructor — they are mutually exclusive.

## 2.2 What Does a Sub Class Inherit?

A sub class inherits the following from its super class:

- **Public** members (fields, methods, nested classes)
- **Protected** members (accessible to sub classes in any package and same-package non-sub classes)
- **Default (package-private)** members (only when both classes are in the same package)

A sub class does **NOT** inherit:

- **Private** members (they exist in memory but are inaccessible directly)
- **Constructors** (constructors are *not* members; they are chained, not inherited)
- **Final** methods can be inherited but **cannot be overridden**

## 2.3 Why Use a Super Class? (Engineering Utility)

1. **Code Reusability** — Common attributes and behaviors are defined once in the super class and reused across all sub classes.
2. **Polymorphism Foundation** — A super class reference can hold any sub class object (Liskov Substitution Principle), enabling dynamic method dispatch.
3. **Maintainability** — Changes to a common feature need to be made only in the super class, not in every sub class.
4. **Abstraction** — Super classes can be declared `abstract` to define a contract that sub classes must fulfill.
5. **Real-world Modeling** — Mirrors "is-a" relationships (a `Dog` *is-a* `Animal`; a `SavingsAccount` *is-a* `BankAccount`).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Syntax / Rule | Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Class Declaration | `class Child extends Parent` | Java supports **single inheritance only** for classes |
| Accessing super field | `super.fieldName` | Used to disambiguate hidden fields |
| Accessing super method | `super.methodName(args)` | Used inside overridden methods |
| Calling super constructor | `super();` or `super(args);` | Must be the **first line** of the sub class constructor |
| Implicit super call | Auto-inserted `super();` | Compiler adds a no-arg `super()` if no explicit call is written |
| Ultimate super class | `java.lang.Object` | All classes inherit from it transitively |
| `instanceof` check | `obj instanceof SuperClass` | Returns `true` if `obj` is a sub class instance |
| Inheritance types in Java | Single, Multilevel, Hierarchical | **No** multiple inheritance of classes (interfaces handle this) |
| Constructor chaining | sub → super → Object | Every constructor ultimately calls `Object`'s constructor |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Condition:** If the super class has **no default (no-argument) constructor** and the sub class constructor does not explicitly call `super(args)`, the compiler will throw an error: *"implicit super constructor is undefined for default constructor"*.

## 2.5 Constructor Chaining — The "Chain of Command"

When a sub class object is created, the call sequence is:

$$
\text{Object()} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Super(args)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Sub(args)}
$$

This means the super class portion of the object is constructed **first**, then the sub class portion is layered on top. This is why super class fields can be safely used inside sub class methods.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Examples & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Demonstration 1: Basic Super Class Field & Method Access

**Problem:** Demonstrate how a sub class uses `super` to access hidden members of the super class.

**Step-by-step solution:**

```java
// Step 1: Define the super class
class Animal {
    String name = "Generic Animal";

    void speak() {
        System.out.println("Animal makes a sound");
    }
}

// Step 2: Define the sub class that HIDES the field and OVERRIDES the method
class Dog extends Animal {
    String name = "Dog";

    void speak() {
        System.out.println("Dog barks");
    }

    void printDetails() {
        // Step 3: Use 'super' to explicitly access super class members
        System.out.println("Sub class name  : " + name);         // prints "Dog"
        System.out.println("Super class name: " + super.name);   // prints "Generic Animal"

        speak();         // calls Dog's speak()  (this.speak())
        super.speak();   // calls Animal's speak()
    }
}

// Step 4: Driver class
public class TestSuper {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Dog d = new Dog();
        d.printDetails();
    }
}
```

**Output Trace:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sub class name  : Dog} \\
\text{Super class name: Generic Animal} \\
\text{Dog barks} \\
\text{Animal makes a sound}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:**
- `name` (without prefix) resolves to the **current** class's variable (most-derived declaration wins).
- `super.name` forces the compiler to look one level up in the inheritance hierarchy.
- Same logic applies to `speak()` vs `super.speak()`.

---

## 3.2 Demonstration 2: Constructor Chaining via `super()`

**Problem:** Show how constructors are chained from sub class to super class.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

```java
class Vehicle {
    int wheels;

    Vehicle() {
        System.out.println("Vehicle default constructor called");
    }

    Vehicle(int w) {
        this.wheels = w;
        System.out.println("Vehicle parameterized constructor: " + w + " wheels");
    }
}

class Car extends Vehicle {
    String brand;

    Car() {
        super();   // explicit call to Vehicle's no-arg constructor
        System.out.println("Car default constructor called");
    }

    Car(int wheels, String brand) {
        super(wheels);   // explicit call to Vehicle's parameterized constructor
        this.brand = brand;
        System.out.println("Car parameterized constructor: " + brand);
    }
}

public class TestChain {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("--- Creating Car() ---");
        Car c1 = new Car();

        System.out.println("\n--- Creating Car(4, \"Honda\") ---");
        Car c2 = new Car(4, "Honda");
    }
}
```

**Output Trace (Step-by-step execution):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{--- Creating Car() ---} \\
&\text{Vehicle default constructor called} \\
&\text{Car default constructor called} \\
\\
&\text{--- Creating Car(4, "Honda") ---} \\
&\text{Vehicle parameterized constructor: 4 wheels} \\
&\text{Car parameterized constructor: Honda}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:**

When `new Car(4, "Honda")` is invoked:
1. JVM allocates memory for the entire object (super class portion + sub class portion).
2. Control jumps to `Car(int, String)` constructor.
3. The first statement `super(wheels)` is executed → control jumps to `Vehicle(int)`.
4. `Vehicle(int)` runs and finishes.
5. Control returns to `Car(int, String)`, which then assigns `this.brand = brand`.
6. The remaining `println` in `Car` executes.
7. Object reference `c2` is returned.

---

## 3.3 Demonstration 3: Multilevel Inheritance with Object as Root

```java
class Grandparent {
    Grandparent() { System.out.println("Grandparent constructed"); }
}
class Parent extends Grandparent {
    Parent() { System.out.println("Parent constructed"); }
}
class Child extends Parent {
    Child() { System.out.println("Child constructed"); }
}

public class MultiLevel {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Child c = new Child();
    }
}
```

**Output (proves transitive inheritance from Object):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Grandparent constructed} \\
&\text{Parent constructed} \\
&\text{Child constructed}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Implicit chain (what actually happens behind the scenes):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Child()} \;\xrightarrow{\text{implicit super()}}\; \text{Parent()} \;\xrightarrow{\text{implicit super()}}\; \text{Grandparent()} \;\xrightarrow{\text{implicit super()}}\; \text{Object()}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Even though we never wrote `extends Object` in `Grandparent`, the JVM automatically chains up to `Object`. This proves that **the Object class is the universal super class** in Java.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common KTU Scenarios

| Scenario | Compiler Behavior | Solution |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Super class has only parameterized constructor; sub class has none | **Compile error** (no implicit `super()` matches) | Add explicit `super(args)` as the first line of sub class constructor |
| Sub class calls `super()` *not* as the first statement | **Compile error** | Always place `super()` on line 1 of the constructor |
| Sub class field shadows super class field of the same name | Compiler binds to the sub class field | Use `super.fieldName` to access super class field |
| Sub class overrides a super class method and wants to *extend* it | Override + call `super.method()` | Inside the overridden method, `super.method()` invokes parent logic |
| `private` member in super class | **Not inherited** (not directly accessible) | Provide a `public`/`protected` getter in super class |
| Cyclic inheritance (`A extends B` and `B extends A`) | **Compile error** | Java forbids cyclic class inheritance |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Class Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class ObjectClass {
        <<super class>>
        +String toString()
        +boolean equals(Object o)
        +int hashCode()
        +Class getClass()
    }
    class VehicleClass {
        <<super class>>
        #int wheels
        #String fuel
        +start()
        +stop()
    }
    class CarClass {
        <<sub class>>
        -String brand
        +start()
        +honk()
    }
    class ElectricCarClass {
        <<sub class>>
        -int batteryCapacity
        +charge()
    }
    class BikeClass {
        <<sub class>>
        -boolean hasGear
        +kickStart()
    }

    ObjectClass <|-- VehicleClass
    VehicleClass <|-- CarClass
    VehicleClass <|-- BikeClass
    CarClass <|-- ElectricCarClass
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Arrows point **from sub class to super class** (open triangle head `<|--` denotes inheritance/realization).
- `ObjectClass` is the apex super class.
- `VehicleClass` is simultaneously a sub class of `Object` and a super class of `Car`, `Bike`.
- `ElectricCarClass` demonstrates **multilevel inheritance** (Object → Vehicle → Car → ElectricCar).

---

## 4.2 Constructor Chaining Flow Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Main as main()
    participant Child as Child()
    participant Parent as Parent()
    participant Grand as Grandparent()
    participant Obj as Object()

    Main->>Child: new Child()
    activate Child
    Child->>Parent: super()  [implicit]
    activate Parent
    Parent->>Grand: super()  [implicit]
    activate Grand
    Grand->>Obj: super()  [implicit]
    activate Obj
    Obj-->>Grand: initialized
    deactivate Obj
    Grand-->>Parent: initialized
    deactivate Grand
    Parent-->>Child: initialized
    deactivate Parent
    Child-->>Main: object returned
    deactivate Child
```

**Interpretation:** The sequence diagram shows the **downward call** (sub → super → ... → Object) and the **upward return** (each constructor finishes and returns control to its caller). Memory for the full object is allocated upfront, but initialization happens top-down.

---

## 4.3 Super Keyword Usage Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Java 'super' Keyword"] --> B["super.member"]
    A --> C["super(args)"]
    A --> D["super.method()"]

    B --> B1["Access hidden field"]
    B --> B2["Resolve name conflict"]

    C --> C1["Must be first statement"]
    C --> C2["Implicit no-arg if omitted"]
    C --> C3["Chains up to Object()"]

    D --> D1["Call overridden method"]
    D --> D2["Extend parent behavior"]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style B fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
    style C fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,color:#000
```

This block-level architecture summarizes the **three functional uses** of the `super` keyword, all of which are tested individually in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What is a super class in Java? How is it different from a sub class?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **super class** (also known as a parent or base class) is a class whose properties and methods are inherited by another class. It is declared using the `extends` keyword in the sub class.

$$
\text{class SubClass } \mathbf{extends} \text{ SuperClass } \{ \; \ldots \; \}
$$

**Differences:**

| Super Class | Sub Class |
| :--- | :--- |
| The class being inherited **from** | The class that **inherits** |
| Also called parent/base class | Also called child/derived class |
| Generalized features | Specialized features |
| Cannot access sub class-specific members | Can access inherited (non-private) super class members |

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the contrast table)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the use of the `super` keyword with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `super` keyword in Java is a reference variable used to refer to the **immediate parent class object**. It has three primary uses:

1. **`super.variable`** — To access the super class field when it is hidden by a sub class field.
2. **`super.method()`** — To invoke the super class version of an overridden method.
3. **`super(args)`** — To explicitly call a super class constructor (must be the first statement).

**Example:**

```java
class A { int x = 10; }
class B extends A {
    int x = 20;
    void show() { System.out.println(super.x); }   // prints 10
}
```

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing uses, 1 mark for example)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting `super()` as the first line** → Common deduction of **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing `super` with `this`** — `this` refers to the current object; `super` refers to the parent portion. Mixing them loses **1–2 marks**.
> 3. **Not showing the output trace** — KTU examiners award **2 marks** specifically for the correct execution/output. Always dry-run your code.
> 4. **Assuming multiple inheritance works with classes** — It does not. Use interfaces. This is a favorite trick question.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Style]*

**(a)** Explain the concept of inheritance and super class in Java with suitable examples. Discuss the different types of inheritance supported by Java. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Inheritance is an OOP mechanism that allows a new class (sub class) to acquire the properties and behaviors of an existing class (super class). It promotes **code reuse** and establishes an **"is-a"** relationship.

**Java Syntax:**

```java
class SuperClass {
    // fields and methods
}
class SubClass extends SuperClass {
    // inherits + adds new features
}
```

**Types of inheritance in Java:**

1. **Single Inheritance** — One sub class inherits from one super class.
   `class B extends A`

2. **Multilevel Inheritance** — Chain of inheritance (A → B → C).

3. **Hierarchical Inheritance** — Multiple sub classes inherit from one super class (A → B, A → C).

4. **Hybrid Inheritance** — Combination of two or more types (achieved using **interfaces**, not classes).

5. **Multiple Inheritance** — One class inherits from multiple parents. **Not supported with classes** in Java to avoid the *Diamond Problem*; achieved through interfaces.

**Example demonstrating hierarchical inheritance:**

```java
class Shape {       // Super class
    void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing shape"); }
}
class Circle extends Shape {
    void area() { System.out.println("Area = pi * r * r"); }
}
class Square extends Shape {
    void area() { System.out.println("Area = side * side"); }
}
```

*(3 marks concept + 2 marks types + 2 marks example)*

---

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate the use of the `super` keyword to call the super class constructor and to access the super class method when it has been overridden in the sub class. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
// Super class
class Employee {
    String name;
    double salary;

    Employee(String name, double salary) {
        this.name = name;
        this.salary = salary;
        System.out.println("Employee constructor called");
    }

    void displayDetails() {
        System.out.println("Name: " + name + ", Salary: " + salary);
    }
}

// Sub class
class Manager extends Employee {
    String department;

    Manager(String name, double salary, String dept) {
        super(name, salary);   // calls Employee(String, double)
        this.department = dept;
        System.out.println("Manager constructor called");
    }

    @Override
    void displayDetails() {
        super.displayDetails();   // calls super class version
        System.out.println("Department: " + department);
        System.out.println("--- End of Manager details ---");
    }
}

// Driver
public class TestManager {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Manager m = new Manager("Alice", 75000, "IT");
        System.out.println();
        m.displayDetails();
    }
}
```

**Output:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Employee constructor called} \\
&\text{Manager constructor called} \\
\\
&\text{Name: Alice, Salary: 75000.0} \\
&\text{Department: IT} \\
&\text{--- End of Manager details ---}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation (for full marks):**
- The `super(name, salary)` call invokes the `Employee` constructor, demonstrating constructor chaining. **(2 marks)**
- `super.displayDetails()` inside the overridden method calls the parent version, demonstrating method access via super. **(2 marks)**
- The output trace proves the order of execution. **(2 marks)**
- Proper class design and syntax. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[Alternative Choice]*

**(a)** What is the role of the `Object` class as the super class of all classes in Java? List any **four** methods inherited by every class from `Object`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The `java.lang.Object` class is the **root of the class hierarchy** in Java. Every class, either directly or transitively, is a sub class of `Object`. If a class does not explicitly extend any other class, it implicitly extends `Object`.

This design ensures that:
- All objects have a common set of basic methods.
- Polymorphism works universally (any reference can be cast to `Object`).
- Garbage collection, synchronization primitives, and reflection APIs have a consistent contract.

**Four methods inherited from `Object`:**

| # | Method | Signature | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `toString()` | `String toString()` | Returns a string representation of the object |
| 2 | `equals()` | `boolean equals(Object obj)` | Compares two objects for equality |
| 3 | `hashCode()` | `int hashCode()` | Returns the hash code (used in hashing collections) |
| 4 | `getClass()` | `Class<?> getClass()` | Returns the runtime class of the object |

**Example:**

```java
class MyClass { }   // implicitly extends Object

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyClass obj = new MyClass();
        System.out.println(obj.getClass().getName());   // Demo$MyClass or similar
        System.out.println(obj.toString());              // MyClass@hashcode
        System.out.println(obj.hashCode());              // some integer
    }
}
```

*(2 marks role + 3 marks methods table + 2 marks example)*

---

**(b)** Explain **constructor chaining** in inheritance with a suitable Java program. What happens if the super class has only a parameterized constructor and the sub class does not call it explicitly? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept:**
Constructor chaining is the mechanism by which a sub class constructor **implicitly or explicitly** calls a constructor of its super class, which in turn may call its own super class constructor, all the way up to `Object()`.

This ensures that the entire inheritance chain of an object is fully initialized before the sub class constructor body executes.

**The Rule:**
- If a sub class constructor **does not** explicitly call `super(...)` or `this(...)`, the compiler **automatically inserts** `super();` (a call to the no-argument constructor of the super class) as the first statement.
- This implicit call **fails** if the super class does not have a no-argument constructor available.

**Demonstration Program:**

```java
class Account {
    String accountHolder;
    Account(String name) {     // ONLY parameterized constructor
        this.accountHolder = name;
        System.out.println("Account constructor: " + name);
    }
}

class SavingsAccount extends Account {
    double balance;
    SavingsAccount(String name, double bal) {
        super(name);           // MUST call super(name) explicitly
        this.balance = bal;
        System.out.println("SavingsAccount constructor: balance = " + bal);
    }
}

public class TestAccount {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SavingsAccount sa = new SavingsAccount("John", 5000);
    }
}
```

**Output:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Account constructor: John} \\
&\text{SavingsAccount constructor: balance = 5000.0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**What if we remove `super(name)`?**

```java
SavingsAccount(String name, double bal) {
    // no super() call → compiler tries to insert super(); implicitly
    this.balance = bal;
}
```

**Result:** **Compile-time error!**
*"constructor Account in class Account cannot be applied to given types; required: String, found: no arguments"*

The compiler cannot insert `super()` because the only available `Account` constructor requires a `String` argument.

*(2 marks concept + 3 marks program + 2 marks error explanation)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Must Remember for KTU ESE:**

- ✅ A **super class** is the class being inherited **from**; a **sub class** is the class that inherits.
- ✅ Use the `extends` keyword to establish inheritance in Java.
- ✅ Java supports **single, multilevel, and hierarchical** inheritance for classes; **multiple and hybrid** inheritance require **interfaces**.
- ✅ The `super` keyword has **three** uses: `super.field`, `super.method()`, and `super(args)`.
- ✅ A call to `super()` or `this()` **must** be the first statement of a constructor; they are mutually exclusive.
- ✅ If no explicit `super()` call is provided, the compiler **implicitly inserts** a no-arg `super()`.
- ✅ **Private** members of a super class are **not** inherited; **constructors** are not inherited (only chained).
- ✅ Every class in Java **transitively** inherits from `java.lang.Object`.
- ✅ **Constructor chaining** order: `Object()` → `Super()` → `Sub()` (top-down initialization).
- ✅ Method overriding combined with `super.method()` is a common KTU question pattern.
- ✅ Cyclic inheritance (`A extends B`, `B extends A`) is **illegal** in Java.
- ✅ The `final` keyword on a method **prevents overriding** in the sub class.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sub Class
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sub Class

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Sub Class** in Java is a class that is derived from another class (called the **Super Class** or **Parent Class**) using the `extends` keyword. The subclass inherits the accessible fields and methods of its superclass, enabling the implementation of the **"IS-A" relationship** and supporting the OOP principle of **Inheritance**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim):**
> A subclass is a class that inherits properties and behavior (fields and methods) from its parent class. It represents an **"IS-A"** specialization and forms the structural backbone of inheritance hierarchies in Java.

In Java syntax, the relationship is declared as:

$$
\text{class SubClassName extends SuperClassName} \{ \ldots \}
$$

The `extends` keyword establishes a unidirectional inheritance link. Since Java does **not support multiple inheritance** between classes, a subclass can extend **exactly one** direct superclass.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Mapping for KTU Exams:**
> - Subclass = **Child Class** = **Derived Class** = **Extended Class**
> - Superclass = **Parent Class** = **Base Class**

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **family tree**:
- Your **grandfather** is the Super Class.
- Your **father**, who inherits traits (name, eye color, mannerisms) from the grandfather, is the **Sub Class**.
- You, who inherit traits from your father (who himself inherited from the grandfather), are a **Sub Class of a Sub Class** (multi-level inheritance).

Another everyday analogy: think of a **generic "Vehicle"** as a superclass. It has properties like `speed`, `fuelType` and methods like `start()`, `stop()`. Now consider a **"Car"** — it IS-A Vehicle, but it adds more specific properties like `numberOfDoors` and methods like `playHorn()`. So `Car` is a subclass of `Vehicle`.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exams:** **"IS-A Test"** — If `SubClass IS-A SuperClass` makes logical sense, the inheritance is correctly designed. Example: *Dog IS-A Animal ✓*, *Chair IS-A Animal ✗*.

---

### 1.3 Fundamental Properties of a Sub Class

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Characteristics (Frequently tested in KTU Part A):**
> 1. **Inheritance of Members:** A subclass inherits all `public` and `protected` members of its superclass directly. `private` members are NOT inherited but can be accessed via public/protected accessor methods.
> 2. **Addition of New Members:** A subclass can define its own new fields and methods, extending the functionality.
> 3. **Method Overriding:** A subclass can provide a specific implementation of a method already defined in the superclass (runtime polymorphism).
> 4. **Single Inheritance Restriction:** A Java class can extend **only one** class at a time.
> 5. **Constructor Inheritance:** Constructors are **NOT inherited**, but the superclass constructor is invoked using `super(...)` from the subclass constructor.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The lifecycle and execution of a subclass can be broken down as follows:

**Step 1 — Declaration via `extends`**
The compiler establishes an inheritance link at compile time. All non-private members of the superclass become part of the subclass's memory layout.

**Step 2 — Object Instantiation**
When `new SubClass()` is called:
1. Memory is allocated in the heap.
2. The superclass's instance variables are initialized (superclass constructor executes first).
3. The subclass's instance variables are initialized.
4. The subclass constructor body executes.

**Step 3 — Member Resolution**
When a method is invoked on the subclass object, the JVM uses the **dynamic dispatch** mechanism (Method Table / vtable) to resolve which method implementation to call (the superclass version or the overridden subclass version).

**Step 4 — Access Control Check**
The Java compiler enforces access modifiers (`public`, `protected`, `default`, `private`) at compile time. Subclass access to superclass members depends on the modifier, not on runtime behavior.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Rule / Formula** | **Example / Notes** |
|---|---|---|
| Declaration Syntax | `class Child extends Parent` | `class Dog extends Animal` |
| Inheritable Members | `public`, `protected`, and default (package-private) | `private` members are NOT inherited |
| Single Inheritance Limit | A class extends **at most 1** superclass | Java disallows multiple class inheritance |
| `super` Keyword | Refers to immediate superclass | `super.method()`, `super(...)` |
| Constructor Invocation | First statement of subclass constructor must be `super(...)` or `this(...)` | Else compiler inserts `super()` |
| Method Overriding | Same signature, covariant return, access level ≥ parent | `@Override` annotation recommended |
| IS-A Relationship | SubClass **IS-A** SuperClass | `Dog IS-A Animal` → valid |
| Object Class | Every class implicitly extends `java.lang.Object` | Top-most superclass |
| Multi-level Chain | A → B → C → D | Transitive inheritance |
| Access Modifier Expansion in Override | Can broaden access (e.g., `protected` → `public`) | Cannot narrow it |
| Static Method Hiding | Static methods are HIDDEN, not overridden | Compile-time resolution |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In real-world software engineering, subclasses form the foundation of:
- **Framework Design (Spring, Hibernate):** Base classes like `AbstractController` are extended by user-defined controllers.
- **GUI Toolkits (JavaFX, Swing):** `JFrame` is a subclass of `Window`, and user UIs subclass `JFrame`.
- **JDBC API:** User code subclasses `AbstractClass` implementations provided by the JDBC driver.
- **Design Patterns:** Template Method, Factory, and Strategy patterns heavily rely on subclassing.

> [!NOTE]
> The principle **"Favor Composition over Inheritance"** (from *Effective Java*) is a modern best practice — but for KTU exams, focus on class-based inheritance as the primary OOP reuse mechanism.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough: Complete Subclass Inheritance Flow

Let us trace through a complete subclass scenario step by step.

**Source Code:**

```java
class SuperClass {
    int x = 10;
    SuperClass() { System.out.println("Super constructor"); }
    void display() { System.out.println("Super display: " + x); }
}

class SubClass extends SuperClass {
    int y = 20;
    SubClass() { System.out.println("Sub constructor"); }
    @Override
    void display() { System.out.println("Sub display: " + x + ", " + y); }
    void subOnly() { System.out.println("Sub-only method"); }
}
```

**Execution Analysis when `new SubClass();` is invoked:**

$$
\text{Step A: Memory Allocation}
$$

$$
\text{Heap Object Layout} = \{ \underbrace{x = 10}_{\text{from SuperClass}}, \underbrace{y = 20}_{\text{from SubClass}} \}
$$

The JVM allocates a single object containing both superclass and subclass fields.

$$
\text{Step B: Constructor Chaining}
$$

The very first action inside the subclass constructor (whether written explicitly or auto-inserted by the compiler) is a call to `super()`. This is mandated by the JLS (Java Language Specification).

$$
\text{Execution Order} \Rightarrow \text{SuperClass()} \rightarrow \text{SubClass()}
$$

Output sequence:
```
Super constructor
Sub constructor
```

$$
\text{Step C: Method Resolution (Dynamic Dispatch)}
$$

When `obj.display()` is invoked on a `SubClass` reference, the JVM consults the **vtable (virtual method table)**:

$$
\text{vtable lookup} \Rightarrow \text{display()} \rightarrow \text{SubClass.display()}
$$

The overridden method executes, not the superclass version. Output: `Sub display: 10, 20`.

$$
\text{Step D: Direct Member Access}
$$

The `x` field (declared in superclass) is accessed directly by the subclass `display()` method because `x` is package-private/default (same package) — equivalent to inherited access. If `x` were `private`, it would NOT be accessible here, and a compiler error would occur.

$$
\text{Step E: Type Casting Check}
$$

A reference of type `SuperClass` can hold a `SubClass` object (upcasting is implicit and always safe):

$$
\text{SuperClass ref} = \text{new SubClass()}; \quad \text{(Upcasting — Implicit)}
$$

But the reverse (downcasting) requires an explicit cast and may throw `ClassCastException` at runtime:

$$
\text{SubClass ref} = (\text{SubClass}) \text{new SuperClass()}; \quad \text{(Downcasting — Runtime Error)}
$$

---

### 3.2 Inheritance Accessibility Matrix

The following table is **HIGH-YIELD** for KTU exams. Memorize it.

| **Super Class Member Modifier** | **Same Package Subclass** | **Different Package Subclass** | **Non-subclass (other package)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| `public` | ✓ Accessible | ✓ Accessible | ✓ Accessible |
| `protected` | ✓ Accessible | ✓ Accessible | ✗ Not Accessible |
| Default (no modifier) | ✓ Accessible | ✗ Not Accessible | ✗ Not Accessible |
| `private` | ✗ Not Accessible | ✗ Not Accessible | ✗ Not Accessible |

> [!TIP]
> **`protected` is the "inheritance-friend" access modifier.** It grants access to subclasses even outside the package — this is precisely why KTU examiners love asking questions about `protected`.

---

### 3.3 The `super` Keyword — Usage Patterns

The `super` keyword in a subclass has three primary uses:

$$
\text{Use 1: super.variable} \rightarrow \text{Access superclass field hidden by subclass field}
$$

$$
\text{Use 2: super.method()} \rightarrow \text{Invoke superclass method overridden in subclass}
$$

$$
\text{Use 3: super(args)} \rightarrow \text{Invoke superclass constructor (must be first line)}
$$

If neither `super(...)` nor `this(...)` is written as the first line of a subclass constructor, the compiler **automatically inserts** a call to the **no-argument constructor** of the superclass. If the superclass lacks a no-arg constructor, a compile-time error occurs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Inheritance Hierarchy Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction LR
    class Object {
        +getClass()
        +equals(Object)
        +hashCode()
        +toString()
    }
    class Animal {
        #name: String
        +eat()
        +sleep()
    }
    class Dog {
        -breed: String
        +bark()
        +eat()
    }
    class Puppy {
        -motherName: String
        +weep()
    }
    Object <|-- Animal : extends
    Animal <|-- Dog : extends
    Dog <|-- Puppy : extends
```

**Interpretation:** `Puppy` is a 3-level deep subclass. It transitively inherits from `Animal` and `Object`. The overridden `eat()` in `Dog` hides `Animal.eat()`.

---

### 4.2 Object Creation & Constructor Chaining Flow (Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Client calls new SubClass] --> B[JVM allocates heap memory for combined object]
    B --> C[Step 1: Initialize superclass fields to defaults]
    C --> D[Step 2: Execute superclass constructor body]
    D --> E[Step 3: Initialize subclass fields to defaults]
    E --> F[Step 4: Execute subclass constructor body]
    F --> G[Return reference to client]
    G --> H[Client invokes obj.method]
    H --> I[JVM performs vtable lookup]
    I --> J{Is method overridden?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Execute subclass version]
    J -- No --> L[Execute superclass version]
```

---

### 4.3 Access Modifier Inheritance Visualization

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph A[Super Class Package com.example.base]
        SC[SuperClass with members: m_public, m_protected, m_default, m_private]
    end
    subgraph B[Sub Class Package com.example.derived]
        SUBC[SubClass extends SuperClass]
    end
    subgraph C[Other Package com.example.other]
        OC[UnrelatedClass]
    end
    SC -- public OK --> SUBC
    SC -- protected OK --> SUBC
    SC -- default NOT OK --> SUBC
    SC -- private NOT OK --> SUBC
    SC -. public only .-> OC
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**Define a subclass in Java. Explain how it differs from a superclass with an example.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A subclass is a class that inherits fields and methods from another class using the `extends` keyword. The class being inherited from is called the superclass.

**Difference Table:**

| **Sub Class** | **Super Class** |
|---|---|
| Derived / Child class | Base / Parent class |
| Uses `extends` keyword to inherit | Is being inherited from |
| Has access to superclass's public/protected members | Defines members to be inherited |
| Can add its own new members | Cannot access subclass-specific members |

**Example:** `class Car extends Vehicle` — `Car` (subclass) inherits from `Vehicle` (superclass).

*(Distribution: 1 mark definition + 1 mark difference table + 1 mark example)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Repeated Pattern]**

**What is the role of the `super` keyword in a subclass? List its three uses.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `super` keyword in Java refers to the immediate superclass of the current class and is used to access superclass members.

**Three Uses:**
1. `super.variable` — Accesses the superclass's field when the subclass has hidden it.
2. `super.method()` — Calls the superclass's version of an overridden method.
3. `super(args)` — Invokes a specific superclass constructor; must be the **first statement** in the subclass constructor.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of a subclass in Java with a suitable example. Discuss the different types of members that can be inherited by a subclass from its superclass. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate method overriding using a superclass `Shape` with a method `area()` and subclasses `Circle` and `Rectangle` that override `area()` to provide specific implementations. Also demonstrate the use of the `super` keyword to call the superclass method. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

A subclass is a class that is derived from another class (its superclass) using the `extends` keyword. It inherits the accessible members of the superclass and can add its own or modify inherited behavior.

**Types of Members and Their Inheritance:**

| **Member Type** | **Inherited by Subclass?** |
|---|---|
| `public` fields and methods | ✓ Yes |
| `protected` fields and methods | ✓ Yes |
| Default (package-private) fields/methods | ✓ Yes (only if same package) |
| `private` fields and methods | ✗ No (but accessible via public/protected getters) |
| Constructors | ✗ No (NOT inherited, but `super()` is invoked) |
| Static methods | ✓ Inherited (but hidden if redefined, not overridden) |
| Final methods | ✓ Inherited but cannot be overridden |
| Abstract methods | ✓ Inherited; subclass must implement or itself be abstract |

**Example:**
```java
class Employee {
    String name;
    double salary;
    Employee(String n, double s) { name = n; salary = s; }
    double calculateBonus() { return salary * 0.10; }
}

class Manager extends Employee {
    int teamSize;
    Manager(String n, double s, int t) {
        super(n, s);
        teamSize = t;
    }
    @Override
    double calculateBonus() { return salary * 0.20 + teamSize * 500; }
}
```
Here, `Manager` is a subclass that inherits `name`, `salary` from `Employee`, overrides `calculateBonus()`, and adds `teamSize`.

*(Distribution: 2 marks concept + 3 marks inheritance table + 2 marks example)*

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

```java
class Shape {
    void area() {
        System.out.println("Area of shape is undefined.");
    }
    void display() {
        System.out.println("This is a geometric shape.");
    }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    double radius;
    Circle(double r) { radius = r; }

    @Override
    void area() {
        double result = Math.PI * radius * radius;
        System.out.println("Area of Circle = " + result);
        super.display();   // Calling superclass method using 'super'
    }
}

class Rectangle extends Shape {
    double length, breadth;
    Rectangle(double l, double b) { length = l; breadth = b; }

    @Override
    void area() {
        double result = length * breadth;
        System.out.println("Area of Rectangle = " + result);
        super.display();
    }
}

public class ShapeDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s;
        s = new Circle(5.0);
        s.area();
        s = new Rectangle(4.0, 6.0);
        s.area();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Area of Circle = 78.53981633974483
This is a geometric shape.
Area of Rectangle = 24.0
This is a geometric shape.
```

**Explanation of Key Concepts Demonstrated:**
- **`extends` keyword:** Used to declare `Circle` and `Rectangle` as subclasses of `Shape`.
- **Method Overriding:** `area()` is overridden in both subclasses with different formulas.
- **`super.display()`:** Demonstrates calling the superclass method from within the overridden subclass method.
- **Dynamic Polymorphism:** The `Shape s` reference holds different subclass objects at runtime, and the correct overridden `area()` is invoked via vtable lookup.

*(Distribution: 3 marks correct code + 2 marks output + 2 marks explanation)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the constructor execution flow during subclass instantiation in Java. What is the role of the `super()` call? What happens if the superclass lacks a no-argument constructor? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a Java program with a superclass `Account` (account number, balance) and subclass `SavingsAccount` (interest rate) that overrides a method `withdraw()` to enforce minimum balance. Demonstrate with appropriate code and explain how access modifiers affect inheritance. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Constructor Execution Flow:**

When `new SubClass()` is executed, the following sequence occurs:

1. **JVM allocates memory** in the heap for the entire object (superclass fields + subclass fields).
2. **Superclass instance variables** are initialized to default values.
3. **Superclass constructor** is invoked via the implicit or explicit `super()` call. This must be the **first statement** in the subclass constructor.
4. **Subclass instance variables** are initialized.
5. **Subclass constructor body** executes.

**Role of `super()`:**
- It ensures that the superclass portion of the object is properly initialized before the subclass adds its own initialization.
- It establishes the **IS-A** relationship at object construction time.
- If not written explicitly, the compiler auto-inserts `super()` (no-arg call).

**What if the superclass lacks a no-argument constructor?**

If the superclass defines only parameterized constructors (and no default constructor), and the subclass constructor does **not explicitly** invoke a parameterized `super(args)` call, then a **compile-time error** occurs:

```
error: constructor SuperClass in class SuperClass cannot be applied to given types;
    required: int
    found: no arguments
```

The solution is for the subclass to explicitly call `super(value)` as its first statement, passing the required arguments.

**Example:**
```java
class SuperClass {
    SuperClass(int x) { System.out.println("Super: " + x); }
}

class SubClass extends SuperClass {
    SubClass() {
        super(100);   // MUST explicitly pass 100; compiler error otherwise
        System.out.println("Sub constructor");
    }
}
```

*(Distribution: 3 marks flow + 2 marks role of super + 2 marks error scenario)*

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

```java
class Account {
    protected String accNo;
    protected double balance;

    public Account(String accNo, double balance) {
        this.accNo = accNo;
        this.balance = balance;
    }

    public void withdraw(double amount) {
        if (amount <= balance) {
            balance -= amount;
            System.out.println("Withdrawn: " + amount + ", New Balance: " + balance);
        } else {
            System.out.println("Insufficient funds in Account.");
        }
    }

    public void display() {
        System.out.println("AccNo: " + accNo + ", Balance: " + balance);
    }
}

class SavingsAccount extends Account {
    private double interestRate;
    private static final double MIN_BALANCE = 1000.0;

    public SavingsAccount(String accNo, double balance, double rate) {
        super(accNo, balance);
        this.interestRate = rate;
    }

    @Override
    public void withdraw(double amount) {
        if (balance - amount >= MIN_BALANCE) {
            super.withdraw(amount);
        } else {
            System.out.println("Withdrawal denied! Minimum balance of " + MIN_BALANCE + " must be maintained.");
        }
    }

    public void computeInterest() {
        double interest = balance * interestRate / 100;
        System.out.println("Interest earned: " + interest);
    }
}

public class BankDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SavingsAccount sa = new SavingsAccount("SB101", 5000, 4.5);
        sa.display();
        sa.withdraw(3500);    // Allowed
        sa.withdraw(2000);    // Denied (would drop below minimum)
        sa.computeInterest();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
AccNo: SB101, Balance: 5000.0
Withdrawn: 3500.0, New Balance: 1500.0
Withdrawal denied! Minimum balance of 1000.0 must be maintained.
Interest earned: 67.5
```

**Explanation of Access Modifiers in This Code:**
- The fields `accNo` and `balance` are declared `protected` in `Account`, which allows direct access from the subclass `SavingsAccount` (even if it were in a different package).
- The subclass uses `super.withdraw()` to invoke the superclass's version, which performs the basic balance check.
- The overridden `withdraw()` adds the additional minimum-balance constraint — a typical KTU exam scenario for demonstrating polymorphism via inheritance.

*(Distribution: 3 marks code + 2 marks output + 2 marks access modifier explanation)*

---

### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `super()` call** when the superclass has only a parameterized constructor → compile-time error (2-3 marks lost).
> 2. **Confusing method hiding with method overriding** for static methods. KTU examiners specifically test this. Static methods are **hidden**, not overridden.
> 3. **Claiming `private` members are inherited** — they are NOT inherited but the class still "contains" them in memory. Access must go through public/protected getters.
> 4. **Narrowing access in overriding** (e.g., changing `public` to `private`) → compile-time error. Only **broadening** is allowed.
> 5. **Not knowing that `Object` is the implicit superclass** of every Java class. KTU loves asking: *"What is the superclass of every class in Java?"*
> 6. **Missing the `@Override` annotation** in exam answers. While not strictly required, mentioning it shows depth and earns evaluator goodwill.

---

### 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**
> - ✅ A **subclass** uses `extends` to inherit from exactly **one** superclass.
> - ✅ The relationship is **IS-A** (subclass IS-A superclass).
> - ✅ **Inherited members:** `public`, `protected`, package-private (same package only). **NOT inherited:** `private` members, constructors.
> - ✅ The **`super` keyword** has 3 uses: `super.field`, `super.method()`, `super(args)`.
> - ✅ **Constructor chaining:** Superclass constructor ALWAYS runs before subclass constructor.
> - ✅ If not explicitly written, the compiler inserts `super()` (no-arg call).
> - ✅ **Method overriding** requires the same signature and covariant return; cannot narrow access.
> - ✅ **Static methods** are HIDDEN, not overridden (compile-time binding).
> - ✅ **Every class implicitly extends `java.lang.Object`** unless it extends another class.
> - ✅ **Upcasting** (subclass → superclass) is implicit; **Downcasting** requires explicit cast.
> - ✅ **Multi-level inheritance** is allowed (A → B → C); **Multiple inheritance** between classes is NOT allowed in Java.
> - ✅ Use **`@Override`** annotation for clarity and compile-time safety.

**🎯 Likely KTU 2024 Question Patterns:**
1. Define subclass with example (3 marks).
2. Explain `super` keyword with all 3 uses (3 marks).
3. Write a program demonstrating inheritance + method overriding (7 marks).
4. Discuss constructor execution order in inheritance (7 marks).
5. Explain access modifier visibility from subclass perspective (table-based 7-mark question).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method Overriding - super Keyword
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Method Overriding - super Keyword

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Method Overriding** is a feature in Java that allows a **subclass (child class)** to provide a **specific implementation** of a method that is **already defined** in its **superclass (parent class)**. The overridden method in the subclass must have the **exact same signature** (name, parameter list, and return type) as the method in the parent class. It is the primary mechanism that enables **Runtime Polymorphism (Dynamic Method Dispatch)** in Java.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The `super` Keyword** in Java is a reference variable that is used to refer to the **immediate parent class object**. It serves three primary purposes: (1) invoking the parent class **method** that has been overridden, (2) invoking the parent class **constructor**, and (3) accessing the parent class **instance variable** when name collision occurs.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

#### 🏠 Real-World Analogy: "The Family Business"

Imagine a father who owns a bakery and has a signature recipe for a cake. His son, who grew up watching his father, decides to take over the business. The son **inherits** the bakery (parent class) but chooses to **modify the cake recipe** to add his own twist (e.g., extra chocolate). 

- The **original method** (`bakeCake()`) belongs to the father.
- The **son overrides** the method with his own version.
- If a customer asks the bakery for a cake, **depending on whether the father or the son is currently running the counter**, the customer gets the corresponding version of the recipe. This is **runtime decision-making** — exactly what Java does!
- The `super` keyword is like the son calling out to his father: *"Hey Dad, can you show me your original recipe first?"* — i.e., `super.bakeCake()` invokes the parent's version even when the son has overridden it.

---

### 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary (Bold Constants & Metrics)

- **Method Signature**: Name of the method + parameter list (NOT the return type).
- **@Override Annotation**: A compiler-time safety check annotation (since **Java 1.5**).
- **Dynamic Method Dispatch**: Mechanism by which a call to an overridden method is resolved **at runtime** rather than compile time.
- **Covariant Return Type**: Since **Java 5**, the return type of the overridden method can be a **subtype** of the return type declared in the parent method.
- **Upcasting**: Referring to a child object through a parent reference — essential for runtime polymorphism.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip**: When asked to "define method overriding" in a 3-mark question, always mention the three key pillars: **(i) Inheritance, (ii) Same Signature, (iii) Different Implementation**. Examiners specifically look for these!

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic of Method Overriding

**Step 1: Establish Inheritance**
- Method overriding is **only possible** when there is an `extends` (class) or `implements` (interface) relationship. Without inheritance, the concept of overriding does not exist.

**Step 2: Method Declaration Must Match**
- The method name, parameter types, and order must be **identical** to the parent class method.
- The return type must be the same or a **covariant subtype**.

**Step 3: Implementation Differs**
- The body (`{}`) of the method in the child class provides a **specialized behavior** that is contextually appropriate for the subclass.

**Step 4: Binding Happens at Runtime**
- The JVM uses the **actual object type** (not the reference type) to decide which method to invoke. This is the heart of **dynamic polymorphism**.

**Step 5: Static/Final/Private Methods Cannot Be Overridden**
- `static` methods are **hidden**, not overridden (they belong to the class, not the object).
- `final` methods are explicitly disallowed from being overridden.
- `private` methods are not visible to the subclass, so they cannot be overridden.

---

### 2.2 Rules for Method Overriding (KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

| # | Rule | Engineering Reality |
|---|------|---------------------|
| 1 | Method name & parameter list must be **identical** | This is the **signature contract** |
| 2 | Return type must be the same or a **covariant subtype** (since Java 5) | Allows `Object` → `String` returns in subclass |
| 3 | Access modifier in subclass **cannot be more restrictive** | `public` → `private` is illegal; `protected` → `public` is legal |
| 4 | **Cannot override `static` methods** (only hide them) | Static binding is class-level, not object-level |
| 5 | **Cannot override `final` methods** | `final` enforces immutability of behavior |
| 6 | **Cannot override `private` methods** | Private = invisible outside the class |
| 7 | **Cannot override constructors** | Constructors are not members of the class hierarchy in the same sense |
| 8 | Subclass can use **`@Override`** annotation | Compiler verifies that overriding actually occurred |
| 9 | **Exception handling**: Subclass can throw the **same, fewer, or narrower** checked exceptions | Broadening exceptions violates the Liskov Substitution Principle |
| 10 | **Abstract methods MUST be overridden** in the first concrete subclass | Otherwise, the subclass must also be declared `abstract` |

---

### 2.3 The `super` Keyword — Three Use Cases

| Use Case | Syntax | Purpose |
|----------|--------|---------|
| Call parent class method | `super.methodName()` | Invoke the overridden method of the parent |
| Call parent class constructor | `super()` or `super(args)` | Invoke the parent's constructor explicitly |
| Access parent class variable | `super.variableName` | Resolve name collision between parent and child instance variables |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Rule**: `super` must always be the **first statement** in a constructor if used. Also, you **cannot** use `super` inside a `static` method, because `super` refers to an instance of the parent class.

---

### 2.4 Comparison Table: `super` vs `this`

| Feature | `this` keyword | `super` keyword |
|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| Refers to | Current class instance | Immediate parent class instance |
| Calls current class method | `this.methodName()` | ❌ Not used |
| Calls parent class method | ❌ Not used | `super.methodName()` |
| Calls current class constructor | `this()` or `this(args)` | ❌ Not used |
| Calls parent class constructor | ❌ Not used | `super()` or `super(args)` |
| Access current class variable | `this.variable` | ❌ Not used |
| Access parent class variable | ❌ Not used | `super.variable` |
| Position in constructor | First line | First line |
| Allowed in static context? | ❌ No | ❌ No |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Java Code Walkthrough — Method Overriding

Below is a **fully annotated**, publication-grade Java program demonstrating method overriding and the `super` keyword. Every line is explained.

```java
// ============================================================
// PARENT CLASS
// ============================================================
class Animal {
    String name = "Generic Animal";  // Parent's instance variable

    // Method to be overridden
    void speak() {
        System.out.println("Animal makes a generic sound.");
    }

    // Method to demonstrate super.methodName() invocation
    void eat() {
        System.out.println("Animal eats generic food.");
    }

    // Constructor
    Animal() {
        System.out.println("Animal constructor called.");
    }
}

// ============================================================
// CHILD CLASS
// ============================================================
class Dog extends Animal {
    String name = "Dog";  // Child's instance variable (shadows parent's)

    // OVERRIDDEN METHOD - same signature as parent's speak()
    @Override
    void speak() {
        System.out.println("Dog barks: Woof! Woof!");
    }

    // Method that uses super to call parent's overridden method
    void demonstrateSuper() {
        super.eat();           // Calls Animal's eat() method
        super.speak();         // Calls Animal's speak() method (the overridden one!)
        System.out.println("Parent name via super: " + super.name);
        System.out.println("Child name via this:  " + this.name);
    }

    // Constructor using super() to invoke parent constructor
    Dog() {
        super();               // Calls Animal() constructor (must be first line)
        System.out.println("Dog constructor called.");
    }
}

// ============================================================
// DRIVER CLASS
// ============================================================
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 1: Direct instantiation
        Dog d = new Dog();
        d.speak();             // Calls Dog's overridden speak()
        d.demonstrateSuper();  // Demonstrates super keyword

        // Step 2: Runtime Polymorphism (Upcasting)
        Animal a = new Dog();  // Parent reference, Child object
        a.speak();             // JVM decides at runtime → calls Dog's speak()!
    }
}
```

#### Output Trace Explanation (Step-by-Step)

| Step | Statement | Output | Explanation |
|------|-----------|--------|-------------|
| 1 | `new Dog()` invokes `Dog()` | `Animal constructor called.` | `super()` in `Dog()` calls `Animal()` first |
| 2 | Continues in `Dog()` | `Dog constructor called.` | Then the rest of `Dog()` constructor body runs |
| 3 | `d.speak()` | `Dog barks: Woof! Woof!` | Dog's overridden `speak()` is invoked |
| 4 | `d.demonstrateSuper()` → `super.eat()` | `Animal eats generic food.` | `super` reaches into `Animal` and calls `eat()` |
| 5 | `d.demonstrateSuper()` → `super.speak()` | `Animal makes a generic sound.` | `super.speak()` invokes the **parent's** overridden method |
| 6 | `super.name` | `Generic Animal` | Resolves shadowing — accesses parent's `name` |
| 7 | `this.name` | `Dog` | Resolves to child's own `name` |
| 8 | `Animal a = new Dog();` then `a.speak()` | `Dog barks: Woof! Woof!` | **Dynamic Method Dispatch** — JVM uses object's actual type |

---

### 3.2 Mathematical/Logical Derivation: Dynamic Method Dispatch Resolution

Consider a hierarchy and a polymorphic reference:

$$
\text{Reference: Parent } p = \text{new Child}();
$$

When `p.method()` is called, the JVM performs the following resolution at **runtime**:

$$
\text{Method Invoked} = \begin{cases}
\text{Child's overridden method} & \text{if Child overrides } method() \\
\text{Parent's method} & \text{if Child does NOT override } method()
\end{cases}
$$

#### Step-by-step JVM Resolution Logic

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Step 1: Compiler verifies: } \texttt{method()} \text{ exists in reference type (Parent).} \\
&\text{Step 2: At runtime, JVM checks the actual object's class (Child).} \\
&\text{Step 3: JVM searches Child's vtable (method table) for } \texttt{method()}. \\
&\text{Step 4a: If found} \rightarrow \text{invoke Child's method.} \\
&\text{Step 4b: If not found} \rightarrow \text{walk up the inheritance chain to Parent and invoke Parent's method.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this called "dynamic"?** Because the decision of *which* method to invoke is made at **runtime**, based on the actual object type — not at **compile time** based on the reference type. This is why method overriding is the cornerstone of **Runtime Polymorphism** in Java.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Overloading vs Overriding (Frequently Asked in KTU)

| Feature | Method Overloading | Method Overriding |
|---------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Purpose | Adds **multiple behaviors** with the **same name** | Changes **existing behavior** in a subclass |
| Where? | Within the **same class** | Between **parent and child classes** |
| Signature | Must be **different** (params) | Must be **same** |
| Return type | Can be **different** | Must be **same or covariant** |
| Binding | **Compile-time** (Static) | **Run-time** (Dynamic) |
| Polymorphism type | **Compile-time** polymorphism | **Runtime** polymorphism |
| Access modifier | Can be **any** | Cannot be **more restrictive** in subclass |
| `static` methods? | Can be **overloaded** | **Cannot** be overridden (only hidden) |
| `private` methods? | Can be **overloaded** | **Cannot** be overridden |
| `final` methods? | Can be **overloaded** | **Cannot** be overridden |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Class Hierarchy Diagram (Inheritance & Overriding)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    class Animal {
        +String name
        +void speak()
        +void eat()
        +Animal()
    }
    class Dog {
        +String name
        +void speak()
        +void demonstrateSuper()
        +Dog()
    }
    class Puppy {
        +String name
        +void speak()
        +Puppy()
    }
    Animal <|-- Dog
    Dog <|-- Puppy
    note for Animal "PARENT CLASS\nDefines base behavior"
    note for Dog "OVERRIDES speak()\nUses super to access parent"
    note for Puppy "OVERRIDES speak() AGAIN\nMulti-level overriding chain"
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Dynamic Method Dispatch Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Code: Animal a = new Dog] --> B{a.speak called}
    B --> C{JVM: Check object's ACTUAL type}
    C --> D[Object is Dog]
    D --> E{Dog overrides speak?}
    E -->|YES| F[Invoke Dog.speak]
    E -->|NO| G[Walk up to Animal and invoke Animal.speak]
    F --> H[Output: Dog barks Woof Woof]
    G --> I[Output: Animal makes generic sound]
    style A fill:#FFE4B5
    style F fill:#90EE90
    style G fill:#FFB6C1
    style H fill:#90EE90
    style I fill:#FFB6C1
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: `super` Keyword in Action

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant M as main method
    participant D as Dog object
    participant A as Animal parent
    M->>D: new Dog
    D->>A: super() constructor call
    A-->>D: Animal initialized
    D-->>M: Dog object created
    M->>D: d.demonstrateSuper
    D->>A: super.eat()
    A-->>D: Returns Animal eats generic food
    D->>A: super.speak() invoke overridden parent method
    A-->>D: Returns Animal makes generic sound
    D-->>M: Both super calls complete
```

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: `super` Keyword — Three Functional Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ParentClass[Parent Class: Animal]
        P1[parentMethod]
        P2[parentVariable]
        P3[parentConstructor]
    end
    subgraph ChildClass[Child Class: Dog]
        C1[overriddenMethod]
        C2[childVariable]
        C3[childConstructor]
    end
    C1 -->|super.parentMethod| P1
    C2 -->|super.parentVariable| P2
    C3 -->|super args| P3
    style ParentClass fill:#E0F7FA
    style ChildClass fill:#FFF9C4
    style P1 fill:#B2EBF2
    style P2 fill:#B2EBF2
    style P3 fill:#B2EBF2
    style C1 fill:#FFF59D
    style C2 fill:#FFF59D
    style C3 fill:#FFF59D
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023] Define method overriding in Java. List any four rules that must be followed while overriding a method.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Method overriding is a feature in Java where a subclass provides a specific implementation of a method that is already defined in its superclass. The method must have the same name, same parameter list, and the same or covariant return type. It is used to achieve runtime polymorphism.

**Rules (2 marks — any four):**
1. The method name and parameter list must be **identical** to the parent class method.
2. The return type must be the **same or a covariant subtype** of the parent's return type.
3. The access modifier in the subclass **cannot be more restrictive** than the one in the parent class.
4. **`final`**, **`static`**, and **`private`** methods **cannot be overridden**.
5. The overriding method can throw the **same, fewer, or narrower** checked exceptions.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] What is the use of the `super` keyword in Java? Explain any two uses with examples.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The `super` keyword in Java is a reference variable that refers to the **immediate parent class object**. It is used for three primary purposes:

**Use 1 — Invoking parent class method (1 mark):**
```java
class Parent {
    void show() { System.out.println("Parent show"); }
}
class Child extends Parent {
    void show() {
        super.show();  // Calls Parent's show()
        System.out.println("Child show");
    }
}
```

**Use 2 — Invoking parent class constructor (1 mark):**
```java
class Child extends Parent {
    Child() {
        super();   // Must be first line; calls Parent's no-arg constructor
        System.out.println("Child constructor");
    }
}
```

**Use 3 — Accessing parent class variable (Bonus):**
```java
System.out.println(super.name);   // When child's 'name' shadows parent's 'name'
```

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, every Part B question has an **internal choice**. You must attempt **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are provided below.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Q. (a)** Explain method overriding in Java with a suitable example. Discuss the rules of method overriding. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Method overriding is the mechanism in Java by which a subclass redefines a method inherited from its superclass, providing a more specific implementation. The method in the subclass must have the same signature as in the parent class.

**Example (3 marks):**
```java
class Vehicle {
    void run() {
        System.out.println("Vehicle is running");
    }
}
class Car extends Vehicle {
    @Override
    void run() {
        System.out.println("Car is running safely at 100 km/h");
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Vehicle v = new Car();   // Upcasting
        v.run();                  // Dynamic dispatch → calls Car's run()
    }
}
```
**Output:** `Car is running safely at 100 km/h`

**Rules (2 marks — list any four):**
1. Same name and parameter list.
2. Return type must be the same or covariant.
3. Access level cannot be reduced.
4. `static`, `final`, and `private` methods cannot be overridden.
5. Cannot throw broader checked exceptions.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the use of the `super` keyword in Java with an example. How is it different from the `this` keyword? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Use of `super` (4 marks):**

The `super` keyword refers to the immediate parent class and is used in three ways:

```java
class Employee {
    double salary = 50000;
    Employee() {
        System.out.println("Employee constructor");
    }
    void work() {
        System.out.println("Employee works 8 hours");
    }
}
class Manager extends Employee {
    double salary = 100000;
    Manager() {
        super();   // Calls Employee constructor
        System.out.println("Manager constructor");
    }
    void work() {
        super.work();   // Calls parent's work()
        System.out.println("Manager works 10 hours");
        System.out.println("Employee salary: " + super.salary);
        System.out.println("Manager salary: " + this.salary);
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new Manager().work();
    }
}
```

**Difference between `super` and `this` (3 marks — any three):**

| Feature | `this` | `super` |
|---------|--------|---------|
| Refers to | Current class instance | Parent class instance |
| Calls | Current class method/constructor | Parent class method/constructor |
| Variable access | `this.variable` | `super.variable` |
| Static method usage | ❌ Not allowed | ❌ Not allowed |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**Q. (a)** What is runtime polymorphism in Java? Explain how method overriding enables runtime polymorphism with an example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Runtime polymorphism (also called dynamic method dispatch) is the process by which a call to an overridden method is resolved at runtime based on the actual object type, rather than the reference type. It is achieved in Java through method overriding.

**Mechanism (2 marks):**
1. A parent reference variable is used to refer to a child class object (upcasting).
2. When an overridden method is called, the JVM determines at runtime which version (parent or child) to execute by inspecting the object's actual class.

**Example (3 marks):**
```java
class Shape {
    void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing a shape"); }
}
class Circle extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing a circle"); }
}
class Square extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing a square"); }
}
public class TestPolymorphism {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s;
        s = new Circle();
        s.draw();    // Runtime decision → Circle's draw()
        s = new Square();
        s.draw();    // Runtime decision → Square's draw()
    }
}
```
**Output:**
```
Drawing a circle
Drawing a square
```

---

**Q. (b)** Explain the different cases where the `super` keyword is used with a suitable Java program. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The `super` keyword is used in three distinct scenarios. Comprehensive program (7 marks):

```java
// Case 1: super to call parent class METHOD
// Case 2: super to call parent class CONSTRUCTOR
// Case 3: super to access parent class VARIABLE

class Vehicle {
    int maxSpeed = 120;  // Parent variable
    Vehicle() {
        System.out.println("Vehicle class constructor");
    }
    void display() {
        System.out.println("Vehicle max speed: " + maxSpeed + " km/h");
    }
}

class Bike extends Vehicle {
    int maxSpeed = 180;  // Child variable (shadows parent)

    Bike() {
        super();   // CASE 2: calls Vehicle() constructor — must be first line
        System.out.println("Bike class constructor");
    }

    @Override
    void display() {
        super.display();  // CASE 1: calls Vehicle's overridden display()
        System.out.println("Bike max speed: " + maxSpeed + " km/h");
    }

    void showSpeeds() {
        System.out.println("Parent maxSpeed (via super): " + super.maxSpeed);  // CASE 3
        System.out.println("Child maxSpeed  (via this):  " + this.maxSpeed);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Bike b = new Bike();
        b.display();
        b.showSpeeds();
    }
}
```

**Output Trace:**
```
Vehicle class constructor
Bike class constructor
Vehicle max speed: 120 km/h
Bike max speed: 180 km/h
Parent maxSpeed (via super): 120
Child maxSpeed  (via this):  180
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks**
> 
> 1. **Confusing Overloading with Overriding** — Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if a student mixes up these two concepts. Always state: *overloading = same class, different params (compile-time); overriding = parent-child, same signature (runtime).*
> 
> 2. **Forgetting `@Override` annotation** — While not mandatory, omitting it loses a **0.5 to 1 mark** in code-based questions where best practices are tested.
> 
> 3. **Wrong `super()` placement** — Writing `super()` as the **second line** in a constructor is a **compilation error**. It MUST be the first statement. Examiners will deduct full marks for the constructor sub-question.
> 
> 4. **Using `super` in a `static` context** — Writing `super.method()` inside a `static` method is illegal. Students often do this in error — full marks lost.
> 
> 5. **Thinking `static` methods can be overridden** — They CANNOT. They can only be **hidden** (a subtle but important distinction that examiners love to test).
> 
> 6. **Forgetting to mention Dynamic Method Dispatch** — When explaining method overriding, **always** mention that the JVM uses the object's actual type (not the reference type) at runtime.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Method overriding requires an **inheritance relationship** (`extends` or `implements`).
- ✅ The **method signature** (name + parameters) must be **identical**; the return type must be the same or a **covariant subtype**.
- ✅ Overriding enables **Runtime Polymorphism (Dynamic Method Dispatch)**.
- ✅ **`static`, `final`, and `private` methods CANNOT be overridden**.
- ✅ The access modifier in the subclass **cannot be more restrictive** than the parent's.
