- ✅ The overriding method **cannot throw broader checked exceptions**.
- ✅ **`@Override` annotation** is a compile-time safety check; always use it.
- ✅ **`super` keyword** has three uses: invoke parent **method**, invoke parent **constructor**, access parent **variable**.
- ✅ `super()` must be the **first statement** in a constructor if used.
- ✅ `super` **cannot** be used inside a **`static` method** or **static block**.
- ✅ Use `super.method()` to explicitly call the parent's overridden method from within the child class.
- ✅ Difference: `this` refers to the **current** class; `super` refers to the **parent** class.
- ✅ Method **overloading** = compile-time polymorphism; method **overriding** = runtime polymorphism.
- ✅ Constructors are **not inherited** and **cannot be overridden** (they can only be chained via `super()`).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Input/Output - I/O Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Input/Output - I/O Basics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Java Programming Language**, **Input/Output (I/O)** refers to the mechanism by which a program exchanges data with **external sources** (such as the keyboard, files, network sockets, or other programs) and **external destinations** (such as the monitor/console, files, or network endpoints). The Java I/O system is built upon the unified abstraction of a **Stream**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Official KTU Definition (aligned with Herbert Schildt & Eckstein's *Java I/O*):**
> A **Stream** in Java is a logical conduit that represents either a source from which data can be read (**Input Stream / Reader**) or a destination to which data can be written (**Output Stream / Writer**). All I/O operations in `java.io` and `java.nio` packages ultimately revolve around manipulating streams.

Java distinguishes between **two fundamental stream categories**:

| Stream Type | Unit of Data | Root Abstract Class | Package |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Byte Streams** | 8-bit bytes (`byte`) | `InputStream` / `OutputStream` | `java.io` |
| **Character Streams** | 16-bit Unicode chars (`char`) | `Reader` / `Writer` | `java.io` |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Module 2 introduces I/O basics, standard streams (`System.in`, `System.out`, `System.err`), reading from the keyboard using the **`Scanner`** class, and reading/writing files using the core byte and character stream classes.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

Think of a Java stream like a **water pipeline** connecting your program to the outside world:

* 🚰 **Your program** = The kitchen tap (consumer/producer of water)
* 📥 **InputStream / Reader** = A pipe that brings water *into* the kitchen (read operation)
* 📤 **OutputStream / Writer** = A drain pipe that takes water *out* of the kitchen (write operation)
* 📄 **File, Keyboard, Monitor, Network Socket** = The water source or the sink at the other end of the pipe

Just like a plumber doesn't care where the water *ultimately* came from (well, river, tank), your Java code doesn't care whether the stream is attached to a file, the keyboard, or a network — **it only reads from or writes to the stream**. This **abstraction** is what makes Java I/O beautifully uniform.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for Exam:**
> *"**B**yte streams handle **B**inary, **C**haracter streams handle **C**haracters (Text)."* — **B → Binary, C → Character/Text**

---

## 1.3 Why Java I/O is Designed This Way

The designers of Java chose the **Decorator Pattern** for I/O classes so that functionality (buffering, filtering, data conversion) can be *layered* on top of a basic stream dynamically — without modifying the original class hierarchy.

```
Basic FileInputStream
        ↓  (wrap for performance)
   BufferedInputStream
        ↓  (wrap for primitive data)
       DataInputStream
```

This gives you **composition over inheritance**, a hallmark of clean OOP design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Java Streams

Streams are classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

### Axis 1: Direction
* **Input Stream / Reader** → Source → Program
* **Output Stream / Writer** → Program → Destination

### Axis 2: Data Unit
* **Byte Streams** → Raw binary data (images, audio, PDFs, executables)
* **Character Streams** → Text data (Unicode-aware — handles internationalization correctly)

### Axis 3: Connection Type
* **Node Streams** → Directly connected to a source/sink (e.g., `FileInputStream`, `FileReader`)
* **Filter/Processing Streams** → Wrap another stream to add functionality (e.g., `BufferedInputStream`, `DataInputStream`)

---

## 2.2 The `java.io` Class Hierarchy (Exam-Critical Tree)

```
                       java.lang.Object
                              │
            ┌─────────────────┴─────────────────┐
            │                                   │
      InputStream (abstract)              OutputStream (abstract)
      ┌──────┬──────┬────────┐            ┌──────┬──────┬────────┐
      │      │      │        │            │      │      │        │
 FileInputStream  ByteArray  Filter   FileOutputStream ByteArray Filter
                  InputStream InputStream              OutputStream OutputStream
                                │                                   │
                          BufferedInputStream                BufferedOutputStream
                                │                                   │
                          DataInputStream                  DataOutputStream

                       Reader (abstract)              Writer (abstract)
                       ┌──────┬────────┐              ┌──────┬────────┐
                       │      │        │              │      │        │
                  FileReader  BufferedReader    FileWriter  BufferedWriter
                  InputStreamReader           OutputStreamWriter
                       CharArrayReader         PrintWriter
                       StringReader
```

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Method Signature Cheat Sheet 📋

### A. Root Abstract Classes — Method Signatures (Must Memorize)

| Class | Method | Return Type | Purpose |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| `InputStream` | `int read()` | `int` (0–255, or `-1` at EOF) | Reads **one byte** |
| `InputStream` | `int read(byte[] b)` | `int` (bytes read, or `-1`) | Reads into buffer |
| `InputStream` | `void close()` | `void` | Releases system resources |
| `OutputStream` | `void write(int b)` | `void` | Writes **one byte** |
| `OutputStream` | `void write(byte[] b)` | `void` | Writes entire buffer |
| `OutputStream` | `void flush()` | `void` | Forces buffered bytes out |
| `OutputStream` | `void close()` | `void` | Closes the stream |
| `Reader` | `int read()` | `int` (0–65535, or `-1` at EOF) | Reads **one char** |
| `Reader` | `int read(char[] cbuf)` | `int` | Reads into char buffer |
| `Reader` | `void close()` | `void` | Closes the reader |
| `Writer` | `void write(int c)` | `void` | Writes **one char** |
| `Writer` | `void write(String str)` | `void` | Writes a string |
| `Writer` | `void flush()` | `void` | Flushes the buffer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Fact:**
> All `read()` methods return an **`int`**, not a `byte` or `char`. The value **`-1`** universally signals **End-Of-File (EOF)**. Using `byte` would fail because `-1` cast to `byte` is indistinguishable from a valid value!

### B. The `Scanner` Class (Module 2 Favourite)

| Method | Returns | Reads |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `nextInt()` | `int` | Next token as integer |
| `nextDouble()` | `double` | Next token as double |
| `nextLine()` | `String` | Whole line (incl. spaces) |
| `next()` | `String` | Next whitespace-delimited token |
| `hasNext()` | `boolean` | True if more tokens exist |
| `close()` | `void` | Closes the scanner |

### C. Standard Streams (Pre-opened Objects)

| Stream | Type | Default Device | Purpose |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| `System.in` | `InputStream` (byte) | **Keyboard** | Standard input |
| `System.out` | `PrintStream` | **Monitor** | Standard output |
| `System.err` | `PrintStream` | **Monitor** | Standard error |

---

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Java I/O

* 📂 **File Processing Systems** — Reading/writing databases, configs, logs.
* 🌐 **Network Programming** — Sockets are wrapped around stream classes (`Socket.getInputStream()`).
* 🎮 **Game Development** — Saving/loading player state (serialization).
* 📊 **Data Analytics Pipelines** — Parsing CSV, JSON, XML files.
* 🏥 **Medical/Industrial Systems** — Real-time sensor data streams.
* 💾 **Backup Utilities** — Binary copy of files via byte streams.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Fact:** Java's I/O model is so successful that many modern languages (C#, Kotlin, Scala) borrow the same stream abstraction. The Decorator Pattern used here is a textbook Gang-of-Four pattern.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs

> Since this is a **Java programming topic**, the "derivations" here are replaced by **exhaustive code walkthroughs**, **method-flow traces**, and **decision matrices** — all standard for KTU Java examinations.

---

## 3.1 Walkthrough 1: Reading a File Using `FileInputStream` (Byte Stream)

### Code:
```java
import java.io.FileInputStream;
import java.io.IOException;

public class ReadBytes {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException {
        FileInputStream fis = new FileInputStream("data.txt");
        int ch;
        while ((ch = fis.read()) != -1) {
            System.out.print((char) ch);
        }
        fis.close();
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace:

**Step 1 →** JVM calls `main(String[] args)`.

**Step 2 →** `new FileInputStream("data.txt")` opens the file `data.txt` for **byte-level reading**.

> If the file does **not exist**, a **`FileNotFoundException`** (a subclass of `IOException`) is thrown.

**Step 3 →** The `while` loop invokes `fis.read()`:

* Reads the **next byte** from the file.
* Returns an `int` in the range **0 to 255**.
* If EOF is reached, returns **`-1`**.

**Step 4 →** The cast `(char) ch` converts the byte value to a Unicode character for printing.

**Step 5 →** Loop continues until `read()` returns `-1`.

**Step 6 →** `fis.close()` releases the OS file handle. **Mandatory** for preventing resource leaks.

### Why the construct `while ((ch = fis.read()) != -1)`?

Because in Java you can **assign** and **compare** in the same expression, and the parentheses `(ch = fis.read())` are required because `!=` has higher precedence than `=`. This is the **canonical idiomatic pattern** for stream reading — examiners love asking this!

---

## 3.2 Walkthrough 2: Writing to a File Using `FileWriter` (Character Stream)

### Code:
```java
import java.io.FileWriter;
import java.io.IOException;

public class WriteText {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException {
        FileWriter fw = new FileWriter("output.txt");
        fw.write("Hello, KTU!\n");
        fw.write("Java I/O is fun.");
        fw.close();
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Trace:

**Step 1 →** `new FileWriter("output.txt")` creates/overwrites `output.txt` in the current working directory.

> [!WARNING]
> **`FileWriter` truncates (overwrites) by default!** Use `new FileWriter("output.txt", true)` for **append mode** — common exam pitfall!

**Step 2 →** `fw.write("Hello, KTU!\n")` writes 12 characters (including `\n`).

**Step 3 →** `fw.write("Java I/O is fun.")` appends 15 more characters.

**Step 4 →** `fw.close()` flushes any buffered characters and closes the file.

### Resource-Handling Decision Matrix

| Method | Auto-Close? | Risk of Data Loss | Boilerplate |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| `close()` at end | Yes (if no exception) | Yes (if exception mid-flow) | Minimal |
| `try-catch-finally` | Guaranteed | Low | Verbose |
| **try-with-resources** | **Guaranteed (Java 7+)** | **Very Low** | **Concise** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Favourite — try-with-resources:**
> ```java
> try (FileWriter fw = new FileWriter("output.txt")) {
>     fw.write("Safe writing!");
> } // close() auto-called here
> ```

---

## 3.3 Walkthrough 3: Reading Keyboard Input with `Scanner`

### Code:
```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class KeyboardInput {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter your name: ");
        String name = sc.nextLine();
        System.out.print("Enter age: ");
        int age = sc.nextInt();
        System.out.println("Hello " + name + ", age " + age);
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Trace:

**Step 1 →** `new Scanner(System.in)` wraps the standard input stream (`System.in`) so we can read tokens easily.

**Step 2 →** `sc.nextLine()` reads characters **until a newline** is encountered.

**Step 3 →** `sc.nextInt()` parses the next whitespace-delimited token as an `int`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Famous KTU Pitfall — the `nextLine()` trap:**
> After `nextInt()`, the newline character is left in the buffer. The *next* `nextLine()` call returns an **empty string**. Fix: consume the leftover newline with an extra `sc.nextLine()`.

### Algorithm Flow for `Scanner.nextInt()`:

$$
\text{Input Buffer: } \overbrace{\text{"42\n"}}^{\text{user types}}
$$

$$
\text{Step A: Skip whitespace} \rightarrow \text{cursor at '4'}
$$

$$
\text{Step B: Accumulate digits while isDigit} \rightarrow \text{"42"}
$$

$$
\text{Step C: Parse to int} \rightarrow 42
$$

$$
\text{Step D: '\textbackslash n' remains in buffer}
$$

---

## 3.4 Walkthrough 4: Copying a File Using Buffering

### Code:
```java
import java.io.*;

public class FileCopy {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException {
        FileInputStream fis = new FileInputStream("source.jpg");
        FileOutputStream fos = new FileOutputStream("dest.jpg");
        BufferedInputStream bis = new BufferedInputStream(fis);
        BufferedOutputStream bos = new BufferedOutputStream(fos);
        int b;
        while ((b = bis.read()) != -1) {
            bos.write(b);
        }
        bos.close();
        bis.close();
    }
}
```

### Why Buffer?

Without buffering, **every** `read()` triggers a **system call** to the disk — extremely slow!

With an 8 KB buffer, one disk read fetches 8192 bytes into memory, and subsequent `read()` calls are served from RAM (a million times faster).

### Performance Equation:

$$
T_{\text{unbuffered}} = n \times t_{\text{syscall}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{buffered}} \approx \left\lceil \frac{n}{B} \right\rceil \times t_{\text{syscall}}
$$

Where:
* $n$ = total bytes to read
* $B$ = buffer size (default **8192 bytes** in Java)
* $t_{\text{syscall}}$ = time per system call

> For $n = 1{,}000{,}000$ bytes and $B = 8192$, the speedup factor is approximately **$\frac{n}{n/B} = B = 8192\times$** — massive!

---

## 3.5 Decision Matrix: Which Stream Class to Use?

| Scenario | Recommended Class | Why |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Reading text files | `FileReader` / `BufferedReader` | Handles Unicode chars |
| Reading binary files (images, PDFs) | `FileInputStream` / `BufferedInputStream` | Byte-faithful, no encoding issues |
| Writing text to console | `System.out.println()` (uses `PrintStream`) | Built-in, formatted |
| Reading primitive types from binary file | `DataInputStream` | `readInt()`, `readDouble()`, etc. |
| Reading user input (simple) | `Scanner` | Easy parsing, locale-aware |
| User input requiring secure password | `Console.readPassword()` | Hides input on screen |
| Writing objects to file | `ObjectOutputStream` | Serialization support |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Java I/O Class Hierarchy (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    objA[java.lang.Object]
    objA --> isA[InputStream]
    objA --> osA[OutputStream]
    objA --> rdA[Reader]
    objA --> wrA[Writer]

    isA --> fis[FileInputStream]
    isA --> bis[BufferedInputStream]
    isA --> dis[DataInputStream]
    isA --> bais[ByteArrayInputStream]

    osA --> fos[FileOutputStream]
    osA --> bos[BufferedOutputStream]
    osA --> dos[DataOutputStream]
    osA --> baos[ByteArrayOutputStream]

    rdA --> fr[FileReader]
    rdA --> br[BufferedReader]
    rdA --> isr[InputStreamReader]
    rdA --> sr[StringReader]

    wrA --> fw[FileWriter]
    wrA --> bw[BufferedWriter]
    wrA --> osw[OutputStreamWriter]
    wrA --> pw[PrintWriter]

    bis -. wraps .-> fis
    br -. wraps .-> fr
    br -. wraps .-> isr
    isr -. wraps .-> fis

    classDef root fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef byteS fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    classDef charS fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    class objA root
    class isA,osA,fis,fos,bis,bos,dis,dos,bais,baos byteS
    class rdA,wrA,fr,fw,br,bw,isr,osw,sr,pw charS
```

---

## 4.2 Data Flow During a Typical File Read Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Physical File on Disk] -->|byte stream| B[FileInputStream Node Stream]
    B -->|wrapped by| C[BufferedInputStream Filter Stream]
    C -->|read method| D[Java Application JVM]
    D -->|process data| E[Console Output via System.out]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000
    style B fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style E fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Byte Stream vs Character Stream — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[What kind of data?] --> q1{Is the data text?}
    q1 -->|Yes, Unicode text| q2{Need buffering?}
    q1 -->|No, binary| bytePath[Use Byte Streams]

    q2 -->|Yes| charBuf[FileReader + BufferedReader]
    q2 -->|No| charDir[FileReader directly]

    bytePath --> q3{Need buffering?}
    q3 -->|Yes| byteBuf[FileInputStream + BufferedInputStream]
    q3 -->|No| byteDir[FileInputStream directly]

    style start fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style charBuf fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style charDir fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000
    style byteBuf fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style byteDir fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#000
    style bytePath fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
```

---

## 4.4 The Decorator Pattern in Action (Layered Stream Wrapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L0[Raw Source: data.txt on disk] --> L1[Layer 1: FileInputStream]
    L1 --> L2[Layer 2: BufferedInputStream - adds 8KB buffer]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: DataInputStream - reads primitives like readInt]
    L3 --> APP[Your Java Application]

    L1 -.->|byte by byte slow| X1[Slow but simple]
    L2 -.->|block reads fast| X2[Performance boost]
    L3 -.->|typed reads| X3[Convenience]

    style L0 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000
    style L1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style L2 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style L3 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style APP fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Differentiate between Byte Streams and Character Streams in Java.** *(3 marks)*
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Byte Streams | Character Streams |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Root Classes | `InputStream`, `OutputStream` | `Reader`, `Writer` |
| Unit of data | 8-bit byte | 16-bit Unicode character |
| Suitable for | Binary data (images, audio, video) | Text data |
| Encoding | No automatic Unicode conversion | Automatically handles Unicode |
| Example Classes | `FileInputStream`, `FileOutputStream` | `FileReader`, `FileWriter` |

Byte streams operate directly on raw bytes without any encoding/decoding, while character streams automatically translate bytes to characters using the platform's default character encoding (e.g., UTF-8). For internationalized text, character streams are preferred as they prevent corruption of non-ASCII characters.

---

### **Q2. What is the role of `System.in`, `System.out`, and `System.err` in Java?** *(3 marks)*
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Java pre-opens three standard streams accessible via static fields of the `java.lang.System` class:

* **`System.in`** — An object of type `InputStream` (byte stream) connected to the **keyboard** by default. Used to read user input. Typically wrapped with `Scanner` for convenient parsing.
* **`System.out`** — An object of type `PrintStream` connected to the **monitor**. Used to print normal output via `print()` and `println()`.
* **`System.err`** — Another `PrintStream` connected to the **monitor**, used specifically for printing **error messages**. Can be redirected separately from `System.out`, which is useful for separating logs from errors.

Example:
```java
System.out.println("Normal message");
System.err.println("Error message");
```

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has an **internal choice** — answer EITHER Option A OR Option B. Both options below are independent 14-mark problems with sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of **streams** in Java. With a neat diagram, describe the classification of streams based on direction and data type. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java program to **read a text file** named `"input.txt"` using `FileReader` and display its contents on the console. Use appropriate exception handling. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a):

**Concept of Streams:**
A stream in Java is a logical channel representing a flow of data between a program and an external device (file, keyboard, network). It acts as an abstraction, hiding the physical details of the source/sink.

**Classification:**

1. **Based on Direction:**
   * **Input Stream** — flows from source to program (read operations)
   * **Output Stream** — flows from program to destination (write operations)

2. **Based on Data Type:**
   * **Byte Streams** (root: `InputStream`/`OutputStream`) — handle 8-bit bytes; used for binary data
   * **Character Streams** (root: `Reader`/`Writer`) — handle 16-bit chars; used for text

**Diagram:**

```
                       Stream
                          │
        ┌─────────────────┼─────────────────┐
        │                 │                 │
   Byte Stream      Character Stream   Direction
   (8-bit)           (16-bit)         (Input/Output)
        │                 │                 │
   ┌────┴────┐       ┌────┴────┐       ┌────┴────┐
   │         │       │         │       │         │
Input    Output   Reader   Writer   Source→  Program→
Stream   Stream                     (Read)   (Write)
```

**(7 marks breakdown: Concept 2 + Direction 2 + Data type 2 + Diagram 1)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b):

```java
import java.io.FileReader;
import java.io.IOException;

public class DisplayFile {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        FileReader fr = null;
        try {
            fr = new FileReader("input.txt");
            int ch;
            while ((ch = fr.read()) != -1) {
                System.out.print((char) ch);
            }
        } catch (IOException e) {
            System.err.println("Error reading file: " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            try {
                if (fr != null) fr.close();
            } catch (IOException e) {
                System.err.println("Error closing file: " + e.getMessage());
            }
        }
    }
}
```

**Explanation:**
* `FileReader` opens `input.txt` for character-based reading.
* The `while` loop calls `read()` repeatedly; it returns `-1` at EOF.
* The `try-catch-finally` ensures the file is closed even if an exception occurs.
* The `(char) ch` cast converts the int to a printable character.

**Better (modern) version using try-with-resources:**
```java
try (FileReader fr = new FileReader("input.txt")) {
    int ch;
    while ((ch = fr.read()) != -1) {
        System.out.print((char) ch);
    }
} catch (IOException e) {
    System.err.println("Error: " + e.getMessage());
}
```

**(7 marks breakdown: Code 4 + Explanation 2 + Exception handling 1)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What are the **standard streams** in Java? Explain `System.in`, `System.out`, and `System.err` with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write a Java program that uses the **`Scanner` class** to read a student's name (String), roll number (int), and CGPA (float) from the keyboard and displays them in a formatted manner. *(7 marks)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a):

Java provides three pre-defined standard streams as static final fields of the `java.lang.System` class:

1. **`System.in`** — Standard input stream, type `InputStream`, usually pointing to the keyboard. Reads raw bytes, typically wrapped by `Scanner` or `BufferedReader` for usability.
   ```java
   Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
   int n = sc.nextInt();
   ```

2. **`System.out`** — Standard output stream, type `PrintStream`, pointing to the monitor. Supports `print()`, `println()`, and `printf()` for formatted output.
   ```java
   System.out.println("Hello, World!");
   System.out.printf("Value = %.2f%n", 3.14159);
   ```

3. **`System.err`** — Standard error stream, type `PrintStream`, also pointing to the monitor but reserved for error/diagnostic messages. Can be redirected independently of `System.out`.
   ```java
   System.err.println("Error: File not found");
   ```

**Key Property:** All three can be **redirected** using methods like `System.setIn()`, `System.setOut()`, and `System.setErr()`, allowing I/O from/to files instead of keyboard/monitor — useful for testing.

**(7 marks breakdown: 3 streams × 2 marks each + redirection property 1 mark)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b):

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class StudentDetails {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        
        System.out.print("Enter name: ");
        String name = sc.nextLine();
        
        System.out.print("Enter roll number: ");
        int roll = sc.nextInt();
        
        System.out.print("Enter CGPA: ");
        float cgpa = sc.nextFloat();
        
        System.out.println("\n--- Student Details ---");
        System.out.printf("Name    : %s%n", name);
        System.out.printf("Roll No : %d%n", roll);
        System.out.printf("CGPA    : %.2f%n", cgpa);
        
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Sample Output:**
```
Enter name: Aswin
Enter roll number: 42
Enter CGPA: 8.75

--- Student Details ---
Name    : Aswin
Roll No : 42
CGPA    : 8.75
```

**Explanation:**
* `Scanner` wraps `System.in` to provide convenient parsing.
* `nextLine()` reads a full line (including spaces).
* `nextInt()` and `nextFloat()` parse numeric tokens.
* `printf()` with format specifiers (`%s`, `%d`, `%.2f`) gives formatted output.
* `sc.close()` releases the underlying stream.

**(7 marks breakdown: Correct imports 1, Scanner creation 1, three reads 1.5, formatted output 2, exception consideration 0.5, code quality/comments 1)**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in I/O questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to close streams** — Examiner deducts **1 mark** if `close()` is missing. In KTU 2024, they prefer `try-with-resources` but accept `finally`.
> 2. **Mixing byte and character streams incorrectly** — e.g., wrapping `FileReader` around `FileOutputStream` is nonsense. Examiner will **deduct 2 marks**.
> 3. **Using `byte` instead of `int` for `read()` return** — `int ch = fis.read();` is **mandatory**, otherwise `EOF` check fails. Lose **1 mark**.
> 4. **Ignoring checked `IOException`** — Either declare `throws` or wrap in `try-catch`. Missing both costs **1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Not handling the `nextLine()`-after-`nextInt()` buffer issue** — Often tested! Either consume the leftover newline or explain the issue.
> 6. **Confusing `FileWriter` overwrite vs append** — Default is **overwrite**. Use `new FileWriter(file, true)` to append.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

* ✅ A **stream** = logical conduit for data flow; **direction-agnostic abstraction**.
* ✅ **Byte streams** = `InputStream`/`OutputStream`; for **binary** data.
* ✅ **Character streams** = `Reader`/`Writer`; for **Unicode text**.
* ✅ All `read()` methods return **`int`**; **`-1` means EOF**.
* ✅ **Node streams** connect directly to source/sink; **Filter streams** wrap other streams.
* ✅ **`BufferedInputStream`/`BufferedReader`** boost performance via 8 KB default buffer.
* ✅ **Standard streams:** `System.in` (keyboard), `System.out` (monitor), `System.err` (monitor, for errors).
* ✅ **`Scanner`** wraps `System.in` for token-based parsing (`nextInt`, `nextLine`, etc.).
* ✅ Always **close streams** — preferably via **try-with-resources** (Java 7+).
* ✅ **`IOException`** is the **checked** superclass of all I/O exceptions — must be handled.
* ✅ **`FileWriter`** overwrites by default; pass `true` for append mode.
* ✅ Java I/O is built on the **Decorator Design Pattern** — functionality layered via wrapping.
* ✅ For **binary primitive data**, use `DataInputStream`/`DataOutputStream`.
* ✅ **`Console.readPassword()`** is the secure way to read passwords (no echo on screen).

---

> **End of Module 2 — Input/Output Basics Notes** ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reading Console Input
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reading Console Input

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Console Input in Java** refers to the mechanism by which a running Java application captures data typed by the user through the **Standard Input Stream (`System.in`)** during program execution. The `System.in` object is an instance of `java.io.InputStream` connected to the keyboard (or piped process) and must be wrapped in a higher-level reader class to parse primitive data types and Strings in a structured, type-safe manner.

In the KTU 2024 OOP-Java syllabus (Module 2: Java Fundamentals & I/O), the standard library provides **three primary classes** to accomplish this task:

1. **`java.util.Scanner`** – The modern, token-based, regex-driven parser (recommended for beginners & KTU labs).
2. **`java.io.BufferedReader`** + **`java.io.InputStreamReader`** – The traditional, performance-oriented, character-stream approach.
3. **`java.io.Console`** – The secure, password-masking console reader introduced in Java 1.6.

> [!NOTE]
> The term **"console"** in Java is a *logical abstraction*. It is not the terminal emulator itself, but a Java-side object that communicates with the underlying OS stream (stdin, fd 0 on Unix, CONIN$ on Windows).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Coffee Shop

Imagine you walk into a coffee shop and shout your order ("Latte, extra hot, no sugar") at the barista standing behind the counter.

| Real-World Element | Java Equivalent | Role |
|---|---|---|
| The customer shouting the order | The user typing on the keyboard | Source of data |
| The air carrying your voice | **`System.in`** (raw byte stream) | Unstructured transport |
| The barista's ear | **`InputStreamReader`** | Converts noise → words |
| The barista's notepad | **`BufferedReader`** | Stores whole phrases |
| The POS system that types out the order | **`Scanner`** | Parses typed tokens into typed fields |
| The locked ordering screen | **`Console`** (with `readPassword()`) | Secure input channel |

The raw keyboard bytes (the "noise") cannot be used directly by your Java program — Java must wrap, decode, buffer, and parse them before they become a usable `int`, `double`, or `String`.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

Reading Console Input is a **gateway skill**: it underpins every KTU lab viva, every "write a program to compute…" Part B question, and every Part A definition question in Module 2. Examiners frequently test:

- The **correct import statement** (`java.util.Scanner` vs `java.io.*`).
- The **constructor invocation** (`new Scanner(System.in)`).
- The **method names** and the data type each returns (`nextInt()`, `nextDouble()`, `nextLine()`).
- The notorious **buffer-flush problem** when mixing `nextLine()` with `nextInt()`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Module 2 explicitly lists *"Reading input using `Scanner` class and `BufferedReader` class"* as a mandatory topic. Expect at least **one 7-mark sub-question** in the End Semester Exam (ESE) and **guaranteed lab-viva questions** on this exact topic.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield API Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Architecture of Java Console Input

Java console input follows a strict **three-tier architectural pattern**, much like the ISO OSI model. Each tier wraps the one below it:

**Tier 0 — Raw Byte Stream**
$$\text{Keyboard} \xrightarrow{\text{bytes (ASCII/UTF-8)}} \texttt{System.in} \;\; (\texttt{InputStream})$$

**Tier 1 — Character Stream Bridge**
$$\texttt{InputStreamReader} \;:\; \text{bytes} \rightarrow \text{Unicode chars} \; (\text{decoding via default charset})$$

**Tier 2 — Buffered / Tokenized Stream**
$$\texttt{BufferedReader} \;(\text{line-based}) \;\;\text{or}\;\; \texttt{Scanner} \;(\text{token-based, regex-split)}$$

Each tier **decorates** the lower tier, adding capability without modifying it. This is the classic **Decorator Design Pattern** in action — a guaranteed high-yield OOP concept.

## 2.2 Why `System.in` Cannot Be Used Directly

`System.in` is declared as:
```java
public static final InputStream in;
```

It is **raw** — it returns one byte at a time (a value between `0` and `255`). If you try to read an `int` or a `String` directly, you would need to manually handle:
- Encoding (UTF-8 vs UTF-16 vs ASCII)
- Whitespace separators
- End-of-line markers (`\r`, `\n`, `\r\n`)
- Buffering (reading byte-by-byte is glacially slow)

The high-level classes absorb all of this complexity, which is why KTU questions emphasize them.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield API Cheat Sheet

### 2.3.1 `java.util.Scanner` — The Recommended Approach

| Method Signature | Returns | Reads Up To | Throws |
|---|---|---|---|
| `String next()` | `String` | Next whitespace-delimited token | `NoSuchElementException`, `IllegalStateException` |
| `String nextLine()` | `String` | Next line (until `\n` consumed) | `NoSuchElementException` |
| `int nextInt()` | `int` | Next token as a base-10 integer | `InputMismatchException` |
| `long nextLong()` | `long` | Next token as a base-10 long | `InputMismatchException` |
| `float nextFloat()` | `float` | Next token as a 32-bit IEEE-754 float | `InputMismatchException` |
| `double nextDouble()` | `double` | Next token as a 64-bit IEEE-754 double | `InputMismatchException` |
| `boolean nextBoolean()` | `boolean` | `"true"` or `"false"` | `InputMismatchException` |
| `byte nextByte()` | `byte` | Next token as a signed byte | `InputMismatchException` |
| `BigInteger nextBigInteger()` | `BigInteger` | Arbitrary-precision integer | `InputMismatchException` |
| `BigDecimal nextBigDecimal()` | `BigDecimal` | Arbitrary-precision decimal | `InputMismatchException` |
| `boolean hasNext()` | `boolean` | Test if another token exists | — |
| `boolean hasNextInt()` | `boolean` | Test if next token is a valid `int` | — |
| `boolean hasNextDouble()` | `boolean` | Test if next token is a valid `double` | — |
| `Scanner useDelimiter(String)` | `Scanner` | Set custom regex separator | — |
| `void close()` | `void` | Release underlying stream | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Delimiter rule:** The default delimiter is whitespace matching the regex `\p{javaWhitespace}+`. The scanner buffers tokens and never reaches end-of-stream until the underlying `System.in` is closed (typically at program exit).

### 2.3.2 `java.io.BufferedReader` — The Performance Workhorse

| Method Signature | Returns | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `BufferedReader(Reader in, int sz)` | constructor | Wraps a `Reader` with an 8192-char buffer |
| `String readLine()` | `String` or `null` | Reads one line; returns `null` on EOF |
| `Stream<String> lines()` | `Stream<String>` | Returns a stream of all lines (Java 8+) |
| `void close()` | `void` | Flushes and closes |

**Required Companion Class:**

| Method Signature | Returns | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `InputStreamReader(InputStream in)` | constructor | Default charset decode (usually UTF-8 on KTU lab Linux) |
| `InputStreamReader(InputStream in, String charset)` | constructor | Explicit charset decode |

### 2.3.3 `java.io.Console` — The Secure Reader

| Method Signature | Returns | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `Console readLine()` | `String` | Reads one visible line |
| `Console readLine(String fmt, Object... args)` | `String` | Reads one line with a formatted prompt |
| `char[] readPassword()` | `char[]` | Reads invisible password (returns `char[]` not `String` so it can be overwritten) |
| `char[] readPassword(String fmt, Object... args)` | `char[]` | Formatted prompt + invisible input |
| `Console format(String fmt, Object... args)` | `Console` | Outputs formatted text (Fluent API) |
| `Console printf(String fmt, Object... args)` | `Console` | Identical to `format` |
| `Reader reader()` | `Reader` | Returns a `Reader` for raw access |
| `PrintWriter writer()` | `PrintWriter` | Returns a `Writer` for raw access |

> [!WARNING]
> **`Console` returns `null` in two cases:** (1) when the JVM has no interactive console (e.g., launched from a background script with redirected I/O), and (2) when the program is launched via certain IDEs in non-interactive mode. KTU students commonly forget the null-check and receive a `NullPointerException`.

## 2.4 When To Use What — Decision Matrix

| Scenario | Recommended Class | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| KTU lab programs with mixed `int`, `double`, `String` input | **`Scanner`** | Direct typed-method parsing (`nextInt()`), short code, fewer try/catch blocks |
| Reading entire lines containing spaces (e.g., `"John Doe"`) | **`BufferedReader.readLine()`** | `Scanner.next()` splits on whitespace; `nextLine()` consumes the leftover `\n` |
| High-performance log / batch file processing | **`BufferedReader`** with explicit buffer size | Avoids tokenization overhead; ~10× faster than `Scanner` in tight loops |
| Password input / secure prompts | **`Console.readPassword()`** | Echoes nothing to terminal; returns `char[]` for memory wiping |
| Reading from a **file** in KTU practicals | **`Scanner(File)`** or **`BufferedReader(FileReader)`** | Both accept alternate sources besides `System.in` |

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Industry

- **Banking & fintech** kiosks use `Console.readPassword()` for PIN entry so the digits never sit in immutable `String` intern-pool memory where they cannot be erased.
- **Server-side ETL** pipelines use `BufferedReader` to ingest **millions of lines per second** from stdin or sockets.
- **REPL tools** (JShell, Groovy, Kotlin REPL) are built on top of `Scanner` because of its convenience methods.
- **Command-line utilities** like `git`, `curl`, and `ffmpeg` rely on the same `System.in` / `stdout` / `stderr` trinity to compose Unix pipelines (`cat file.txt  $\vert$  java MyApp`).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Implementations & Analytical Walkthroughs

This section transitions from theory to **fully worked-out executable Java programs** corresponding to the four canonical patterns KTU examiners test.

---

## 3.1 Pattern 1 — `Scanner` (The Workhorse)

### 3.1.1 The "Hello World" of Console Input

**Problem:** Read a student's name (String) and three marks (double), then print the name and the average.

```java
// File: StudentAverage.java
import java.util.Scanner;                          // Step 1: import the Scanner class

public class StudentAverage {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 2: Instantiate Scanner wrapped around System.in
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);

        // Step 3: Prompt the user (optional but professional)
        System.out.print("Enter student name: ");
        String name = sc.nextLine();               // Reads the entire line including spaces

        System.out.print("Enter mark 1: ");
        double m1 = sc.nextDouble();               // Reads next whitespace-delimited token as double

        System.out.print("Enter mark 2: ");
        double m2 = sc.nextDouble();

        System.out.print("Enter mark 3: ");
        double m3 = sc.nextDouble();

        // Step 4: Compute
        double avg = (m1 + m2 + m3) / 3.0;

        // Step 5: Output
        System.out.printf("%nAverage mark for %s = %.2f%n", name, avg);

        // Step 6: Release the underlying stream (best practice)
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step trace** (assuming input `Anu Mary\n78.5 88.0 91.5\n`):

| Line | `sc` internal buffer state | Variable value |
|---|---|---|
| After `nextLine()` | consumes `Anu Mary\n` → buffer empty | `name = "Anu Mary"` |
| After `nextDouble()` | consumes `78.5 ` → buffer holds `88.0 91.5\n` | `m1 = 78.5` |
| After `nextDouble()` | consumes `88.0 ` → buffer holds `91.5\n` | `m2 = 88.0` |
| After `nextDouble()` | consumes `91.5\n` → buffer empty | `m3 = 91.5` |
| Compute | — | `avg = (78.5+88.0+91.5)/3.0 = 86.0` |
| Output | — | `Average mark for Anu Mary = 86.00` |

---

### 3.1.2 The Famous Buffer-Flush Bug — A KTU Classic

**Problem:** Read an integer `n`, then read `n` names on separate lines.

**The WRONG code (what students write):**
```java
Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
System.out.print("How many names? ");
int n = sc.nextInt();                              // Consumes "3\n"  →  leaves "\n" in buffer!

for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    System.out.print("Name " + (i + 1) + ": ");
    String name = sc.nextLine();                   // BUG: reads leftover "\n" → empty string!
    System.out.println("You entered: " + name);
}
sc.close();
```

**Input:**
```
3
Anu
Balu
Chitra
```

**Expected output:**
```
You entered: Anu
You entered: Balu
You entered: Chitra
```

**Actual buggy output:**
```
You entered: 
You entered: Anu
You entered: Balu
```

**The FIX — three valid strategies:**

**Fix A — Drain the leftover newline with an extra `nextLine()`:**
```java
int n = sc.nextInt();
sc.nextLine();                                     // Consume the orphan "\n"
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    String name = sc.nextLine();                   // Now reads correctly
    System.out.println("You entered: " + name);
}
```

**Fix B — Parse the integer manually with `nextLine()` + `Integer.parseInt()`:**
```java
int n = Integer.parseInt(sc.nextLine());           // Whole line consumed; no leftover
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
    String name = sc.nextLine();
    System.out.println("You entered: " + name);
}
```

**Fix C — Use `next()` instead of `nextInt()` then convert:**
```java
int n = Integer.parseInt(sc.next());               // next() leaves ONLY the trailing space
sc.nextLine();                                     // Drain remaining newline
for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) { … }
```

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners report that **roughly 1 in 3** submissions for any input-then-string question contains this buffer-flush bug. Memorize Fix B — it is the most robust.

---

## 3.2 Pattern 2 — `BufferedReader` + `InputStreamReader`

### 3.2.1 The Performance-Critical Pattern

```java
// File: EmpPayroll.java
import java.io.BufferedReader;
import java.io.IOException;
import java.io.InputStreamReader;

public class EmpPayroll {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException {
        // Step 1: Wrap System.in in InputStreamReader (byte→char), then in BufferedReader (buffer+line API)
        BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));

        System.out.print("Enter employee ID: ");
        int id = Integer.parseInt(br.readLine().trim());

        System.out.print("Enter employee name: ");
        String name = br.readLine();

        System.out.print("Enter basic pay: ");
        double basic = Double.parseDouble(br.readLine().trim());

        double da  = 0.40 * basic;
        double hra = 0.20 * basic;
        double net = basic + da + hra;

        System.out.printf("%nEmployee : %s (ID %d)%n", name, id);
        System.out.printf("Net pay  : %.2f%n", net);

        br.close();
    }
}
```

**Why `.trim()` matters for numeric parsing:**

$$\text{Input line} = \texttt{"5000"}\phantom{xxxxx}\texttt{""}\phantom{xxxxx}\texttt{""}\phantom{xxxxx}\texttt{""}$$

The characters read by `readLine()` include a trailing `\r\n` on Windows (and only `\n` on Linux). `Integer.parseInt("5000\r\n")` throws `NumberFormatException`. Hence we **always trim** before parsing numeric `readLine()` output.

**Notice the differences from `Scanner`:**

| Aspect | `Scanner` | `BufferedReader` |
|---|---|---|
| Number parsing | `int x = sc.nextInt();` (built-in) | `int x = Integer.parseInt(br.readLine().trim());` |
| Line containing spaces | `sc.next()` would split it | `br.readLine()` keeps it intact by design |
| Checked exception | Unchecked | `IOException` must be declared or caught |
| Speed for large input | Slower (regex tokenizer) | ~10× faster (line-buffered) |
| Default delimiter | Whitespace regex | Newline character |

---

## 3.3 Pattern 3 — `Console` for Secure Input

```java
// File: SecureLogin.java
import java.io.Console;

public class SecureLogin {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Console c = System.console();               // Returns null if no interactive console

        if (c == null) {
            System.err.println("No interactive console available. Exiting.");
            System.exit(1);
        }

        String username = c.readLine("Username: ");
        char[] password = c.readPassword("Password: ");

        // Wipe the password from memory after use (best security practice)
        boolean ok = authenticate(username, password);
        for (int i = 0; i < password.length; i++) {
            password[i] = '\0';                     // Overwrite with null characters
        }

        System.out.println(ok ? "Login successful." : "Login failed.");
    }

    private static boolean authenticate(String u, char[] p) {
        // Demo only — never hardcode credentials
        return "admin".equals(u) && "java123".equals(new String(p));
    }
}
```

**Walkthrough:**

1. `System.console()` retrieves the unique `Console` object associated with the JVM.
2. The null-check is mandatory — IDEs that launch without a true TTY (like Eclipse's default console on some platforms) will yield `null`.
3. `readPassword()` echoes nothing to the terminal. The return type is `char[]`, not `String`, so the JVM cannot pool the password in the string-literal pool where it would be immortal.
4. The `for` loop overwrites each `char` cell with `'\0'`, erasing the cleartext password from heap memory before garbage collection eventually reclaims the array.

---

## 3.4 Pattern 4 — Command-Line Arguments (Bonus Pattern)

Although strictly not "console input", KTU examiners bundle it with this topic.

```java
// File: SumArgs.java
public class SumArgs {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // args is a String[] — values arrive pre-typed from the OS shell
        int sum = 0;
        for (String s : args) {
            sum += Integer.parseInt(s);
        }
        System.out.println("Sum = " + sum);
    }
}
```

**Invocation:**
```
$ java SumArgs 10 20 30
Sum = 60
```

Inside the JVM, `args` is populated by the class launcher from `argv` passed to `main(String[] argv)`. There is no `Scanner` or `BufferedReader` involved — the values were already on the OS command line *before* the JVM started.

---

## 3.5 Comparison Table — When To Use Each (Exam-Ready Summary)

| Property | `Scanner` | `BufferedReader` | `Console` | `args[]` |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Package | `java.util` | `java.io` | `java.io` | (language built-in) |
| Synchronized? | No | Yes | No | N/A |
| Reads spaces in tokens? | No (use `nextLine`) | Yes (`readLine`) | Yes | Yes |
| Password masking? | No | No | Yes | N/A |
| Can read from String source? | Yes (`Scanner(String)`) | No | No | N/A |
| Can read from File? | Yes (`Scanner(File)`) | Yes (`FileReader`) | No | No |
| Throws checked exception? | No | Yes (`IOException`) | Yes (`IOError`) | Yes (`NumberFormatException`) |
| Recommended for | Mixed-type small input | Large / line-oriented input | Secure prompts | Pre-supplied parameters |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Decorator Stack — How Input Layers Compose

This Mermaid graph shows the **layered architecture** of console-input classes, mirroring the Decorator Design Pattern. Every lower node "decorates" or wraps the one above it.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[App1: main method] --> B[App2: reads data]
    B --> C[App3: parses data]
    C --> D1[Scanner class util pkg]
    C --> D2[BufferedReader class io pkg]
    C --> D3[Console class io pkg]
    D1 --> E1[InputStreamReader utility]
    D2 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[System.in static field]
    F1 --> G1[OS stdin file descriptor 0]
    G1 --> H1[Hardware keyboard]

    style A fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style B fill:#FFE6B3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C fill:#FFEFCC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style D1 fill:#C7E9FF,stroke:#005A8E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style D2 fill:#C7E9FF,stroke:#005A8E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style D3 fill:#C7E9FF,stroke:#005A8E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style E1 fill:#A6D8A6,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style F1 fill:#FFB6B6,stroke:#7F0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style G1 fill:#D8B6FF,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style H1 fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **`Console`** is a special case — it bypasses the chain and talks to the JVM directly via JNI, which is why it can mask input. It is intentionally omitted from the wrap-chain above to keep the diagram accurate.

---

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Which Reader Should I Pick?

This flowchart guides the student through the decision tree KTU examiners expect them to internalize.

```mermaid
graph TD
    start([Start: I need keyboard input]) --> q1{Is input a password?}
    q1 -- Yes --> p1[Use Console readPassword]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Is it mixed types int double String?}
    q2 -- Yes --> p2[Use Scanner]
    q2 -- No --> q3{Reading large file or million lines?}
    q3 -- Yes --> p3[Use BufferedReader]
    q3 -- No --> q4{Need spaces inside a token?}
    q4 -- Yes --> p4[Use BufferedReader or Scanner nextLine]
    q4 -- No --> p5[Use Scanner next]
```

---

## 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Internal Call Sequence for `sc.nextInt()`

This sequence diagram traces what happens *under the hood* when `Scanner.nextInt()` is invoked, exposing the layered machinery a top-level API call triggers.

```mermaid
graph TD
    s1[Step 1: Developer calls sc nextInt] --> s2[Step 2: Scanner scans tokens using delimiter regex]
    s2 --> s3[Step 3: Finds next whitespace delimited token]
    s3 --> s4[Step 4: Calls Integer parseInt on the token]
    s4 --> s5[Step 5: If valid returns int value]
    s4 --> s6[Step 6: If not valid throws InputMismatchException]
    s2 --> s7[Step 7: If no token remains scanner blocks waiting for more System in bytes]
    s7 --> s8[Step 8: System in reads raw bytes from OS stdin]
    s8 --> s9[Step 9: Charset decoder converts bytes to chars default UTF8]
    s9 --> s3

    style s5 fill:#A6D8A6,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    style s9 fill:#D8B6FF,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Buffer-Flush Bug — Visual Map

This flow map illustrates the exact path of the orphan newline character.

```mermaid
graph TD
    b1[User types 3 and hits Enter] --> b2[System in sends bytes 51 48 51 10]
    b2 --> b3[Scanner nextInt reads 51 48 51 forming 3]
    b3 --> b4[Scanner stops at the whitespace 10 leaving it in the buffer]
    b4 --> b5[Developer calls sc nextLine]
    b5 --> b6[Scanner returns immediately with empty string consuming only the 10]
    b6 --> b7[BUG: name appears blank on screen]
    b7 --> b8[FIX: call one extra sc nextLine BEFORE the loop OR use Integer parseInt sc nextLine]
```
