# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer / Definition)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between `Scanner` class and `BufferedReader` class in Java for reading console input.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `Scanner` | `BufferedReader` |
|---|---|---|
| Package | `java.util` | `java.io` |
| Token parsing | Built-in (`nextInt()`, `nextDouble()`) | Requires manual `Integer.parseInt()`, etc. |
| Speed | Slower (regex tokenizer) | Faster (plain char buffer) |
| Synchronized | No | Yes |
| Reads spaces in token | No (`next()`); `nextLine()` reads them | Yes (`readLine()` always keeps spaces) |
| Exception | Unchecked `InputMismatchException` | Checked `IOException` |

`Scanner` is best for short mixed-type input; `BufferedReader` is preferred for large line-oriented input or when spaces inside a token must be preserved.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of `System.in` in Java? Why is it wrapped inside another class?**

**Model Answer:**

`System.in` is a **static, final** object of type `java.io.InputStream` that represents the standard input stream (typically the keyboard) connected to the Java program. It is a *raw byte stream* — it can only deliver one byte at a time, requiring manual encoding, whitespace handling, and buffering.

It is wrapped inside classes like `Scanner` or `BufferedReader` to:

1. Convert bytes to characters (via `InputStreamReader`).
2. Buffer input for efficiency (via `BufferedReader`).
3. Tokenize and parse typed values (`Scanner`).
4. Provide convenient typed methods such as `nextInt()` or `readLine()`.

Without wrapping, the developer would have to manually implement all of the above.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Full Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, every 14-mark Part B question allows the student to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both are provided below for practice.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Write a Java program to read `n` student records (roll number, name, and three marks) from the console using the `Scanner` class and display the student details along with the total and average. Use an appropriate class to encapsulate the data.  [7 Marks]**

**(b) Explain with code how the `BufferedReader` class can be used to read a line of text containing spaces. Compare its performance with the `Scanner` class for large inputs.  [7 Marks]**

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) — Scanner with Student Class

```java
// File: StudentRecordApp.java
import java.util.Scanner;

class Student {
    private int rollNo;
    private String name;
    private double m1, m2, m3;

    public Student(int rollNo, String name, double m1, double m2, double m3) {
        this.rollNo = rollNo;
        this.name   = name;
        this.m1 = m1; this.m2 = m2; this.m3 = m3;
    }

    public double total()  { return m1 + m2 + m3; }
    public double average() { return total() / 3.0; }

    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return String.format(
            "Roll: %d  Name: %-10s  Total: %.1f  Average: %.2f",
            rollNo, name, total(), average()
        );
    }
}

public class StudentRecordApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);

        System.out.print("Enter number of students: ");
        int n = Integer.parseInt(sc.nextLine().trim());   // Avoid buffer-flush bug

        Student[] arr = new Student[n];

        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            System.out.println("\n--- Student " + (i + 1) + " ---");

            System.out.print("Roll number: ");
            int r = Integer.parseInt(sc.nextLine().trim());

            System.out.print("Name: ");
            String nm = sc.nextLine();

            System.out.print("Mark 1: ");
            double a = Double.parseDouble(sc.nextLine().trim());

            System.out.print("Mark 2: ");
            double b = Double.parseDouble(sc.nextLine().trim());

            System.out.print("Mark 3: ");
            double c = Double.parseDouble(sc.nextLine().trim());

            arr[i] = new Student(r, nm, a, b, c);
        }

        System.out.println("\n--- Results ---");
        for (Student s : arr) {
            System.out.println(s);
        }

        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Key concepts demonstrated:**
- Encapsulation via the `Student` class (private fields, public methods, `toString()`).
- Use of `Integer.parseInt(sc.nextLine().trim())` to avoid the buffer-flush bug.
- Array of objects (`Student[] arr`).
- Enhanced `for` loop for output.

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) — BufferedReader for Lines with Spaces

```java
// File: AddressBook.java
import java.io.BufferedReader;
import java.io.IOException;
import java.io.InputStreamReader;

public class AddressBook {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException {
        BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));

        System.out.print("Enter your full address: ");
        String address = br.readLine();     // Captures the entire line, spaces intact

        System.out.print("Enter your city: ");
        String city = br.readLine();

        System.out.println("\nYou live at:");
        System.out.println(address);
        System.out.println(city);

        br.close();
    }
}
```

**Sample interaction:**
```
Enter your full address: Flat 4B, Green Valley Apartments
Enter your city: Kochi

You live at:
Flat 4B, Green Valley Apartments
Kochi
```

**Performance Comparison for Large Inputs:**

| Metric | `Scanner` | `BufferedReader` |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Regex tokenization + internal `Pattern` matching | Plain `char[]` line buffer |
| 1 million lines on a 4-core CPU | ~3.2 s | ~0.35 s |
| Memory footprint | Higher (regex state, lookahead) | Lower (single buffer) |
| Best used when | Mixed types, small programs | Pure line-oriented bulk read |

`Scanner` is therefore **10× slower** in micro-benchmarks because every call to `next()` re-evaluates the delimiter regex against the buffer. `BufferedReader` simply scans for `'\n'` in a `char[]` — an O(n) memchr-style operation.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Explain the `Console` class in Java. Write a Java program that reads a username and password securely using `Console.readPassword()` and verifies the credentials.  [7 Marks]**

**(b) With a suitable example, explain the buffer-flush problem that occurs when `nextInt()` is followed by `nextLine()` in Java. How do you resolve it?  [7 Marks]**

#### MODEL SOLUTION (a) — Console Class for Secure Login

```java
// File: SecureConsole.java
import java.io.Console;

public class SecureConsole {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Console c = System.console();
        if (c == null) {
            System.err.println("No interactive console. Run from terminal.");
            return;
        }

        String user = c.readLine("Username: ");
        char[] pass = c.readPassword("Password: ");

        // Pretend "database" lookup
        boolean valid = "kerala".equals(user)
                     && "ktu2024".equals(new String(pass));

        // Wipe password from memory
        for (int i = 0; i < pass.length; i++) pass[i] = '\0';

        System.out.println(valid ? "Login OK" : "Login FAILED");
    }
}
```

**Explanation of `Console`:**

- `System.console()` returns a singleton `Console` object, or `null` if the JVM has no terminal (e.g., when run via Eclipse without a TTY redirect).
- `readPassword()` disables terminal echo so the keystrokes are not visible.
- It returns a `char[]` rather than a `String` so the application can overwrite the contents after use, preventing the password from lingering in the immutable string pool.
- `Console` is available only from Java 1.6 onwards.

#### MODEL SOLUTION (b) — Buffer-Flush Problem Explained

**The buggy code:**
```java
Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
int roll = sc.nextInt();          // reads "101" but leaves "\n" in buffer
String name = sc.nextLine();      // returns "" because it consumes only "\n"
```

**Why it happens:** `nextInt()` reads digits until a non-digit (the `\n`) is encountered, but it does **not** consume that delimiter. The very next `nextLine()` immediately consumes the leftover `\n` and returns an empty string.

**Three correct resolutions (any one is accepted in KTU):**

**Resolution 1 — Drain the newline:**
```java
int roll = sc.nextInt();
sc.nextLine();                    // consume the orphan newline
String name = sc.nextLine();
```

**Resolution 2 — Use `nextLine()` + manual parse:**
```java
int roll = Integer.parseInt(sc.nextLine());
String name = sc.nextLine();      // no leftover newline, so it works
```

**Resolution 3 — Replace `nextInt()` with `next()` + manual parse:**
```java
int roll = Integer.parseInt(sc.next());
sc.nextLine();                    // drain remaining newline
String name = sc.nextLine();
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common mark-deduction pattern is when a student writes the *correct code* for Resolution 1 but **fails to explain WHY the bug occurs** — i.e., the orphan newline theory. A 7-mark answer must include both the **conceptual explanation** and the **working fix**. Marks are split roughly 3 for explanation + 4 for working code.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Reading Console Input**

- [x] `System.in` is a **static final `InputStream`** — it delivers raw bytes, never characters.
- [x] `Scanner` lives in **`java.util`**; `BufferedReader`, `InputStreamReader`, and `Console` live in **`java.io`**.
- [x] The three-layer decorator chain is **`System.in`  →  `InputStreamReader`  →  `BufferedReader`  (or `Scanner`)**.
- [x] `Scanner.nextInt()`, `nextDouble()`, `nextFloat()`, etc. throw **`InputMismatchException`** (unchecked) on invalid input.
- [x] `BufferedReader.readLine()` throws **`IOException`** (checked) — must be caught or declared.
- [x] `Console.console()` returns **`null`** when no TTY is attached — **always null-check** before use.
- [x] `Console.readPassword()` returns a **`char[]`** — overwrite with `'\0'` after use to wipe from memory.
- [x] Mixing `nextInt()` and `nextLine()` causes the **orphan-newline bug** — fix it with `Integer.parseInt(sc.nextLine())` or an extra `sc.nextLine()` drain.
- [x] `Scanner.next()` **splits on whitespace**; `nextLine()` reads until `\n` — choose carefully when input contains spaces.
- [x] `BufferedReader` is **~10× faster** than `Scanner` for large line-oriented input.
- [x] Closing a `Scanner` also closes `System.in` — close only the **outermost** reader.
- [x] Command-line arguments arrive in `String[] args` — they are pre-supplied before the JVM starts; no input class is required.

> **End of Module 2 — Reading Console Input Notes**
> *(Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 outcome-based education framework)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Writing Console Output
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Writing Console Output # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the Java programming language, **Console Output** refers to the process of sending textual data from a running program to the **Standard Output Stream (stdout)**, which is typically displayed on the user's terminal (Command Prompt, PowerShell, or IDE Console). The mechanism is anchored on the `java.lang.System` class, which exposes a `public static final PrintStream out` field. Any invocation such as `System.out.println(...)` ultimately delegates the rendering of characters to an underlying `java.io.PrintStream` object connected to the host operating system's standard output file descriptor. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** > Students must understand the three primary console output methods — `print()`, `println()`, and `printf()` — along with the use of **escape sequences** and the **concatenation operator (`+`)** for building dynamic output strings. Mastery of `printf()` format specifiers is a high-yield area for ESE questions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Newsroom Broadcaster" Model Imagine your Java program is a **newsroom**: - **`System`** is the **Broadcasting Authority** — the central authority that controls all standard I/O channels. - **`out`** is the **specific microphone** assigned for broadcasting news to the public (the console). - **`println()` / `print()` / `printf()`** are the **announcers** who actually speak into the microphone. - **The Console (Terminal)** is the **public television** on which the audience sees the broadcast. When you write `System.out.println("Hello");`, you are essentially picking up the "public microphone" (`System.out`) and asking the announcer (`println`) to deliver the message `Hello` followed by a new line, which the television (console) then displays. > [!NOTE] > **Why `System` is `final` and `out` is `static`?** > - `final` → The `System` class cannot be subclassed or instantiated (its constructor is `private`). > - `static` → The `out` field belongs to the class itself, so you do not need to create a `System` object to access it. This is why `System.out` works without `new System()`. ## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Console Output | Method | Declaration | Behavior | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `print()` | `void print(String s)` | Outputs the string **without** advancing the cursor to a new line. | | `println()` | `void println(String s)` | Outputs the string and then advances the cursor to the **beginning of the next line** (appends a line separator). | | `printf()` | `PrintStream printf(String format, Object... args)` | Outputs a **formatted** string using C-style format specifiers. | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Units / Conventions**: > - **Line Separator on Windows**: `\r\n` (Carriage Return + Line Feed) > - **Line Separator on Linux/Mac**: `\n` (Line Feed) > - **Java's `println()`** uses the platform-independent `System.lineSeparator()` automatically, so code is **portable**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Internal Resolution Chain of `System.out.println()` To truly master console output, you must understand what happens behind the dot (`.`). The expression `System.out.println("KTU")` is resolved by the Java compiler in **three stages**: 1. **Stage 1 — The `System` Class Lookup:** The compiler searches the `java.lang` package (auto-imported) for a class named `System`. It finds the class definition. 2. **Stage 2 — The Static Field `out`:** Inside `System`, the compiler locates the public static field `out` of type `java.io.PrintStream`. Because it is `static`, no object instance is required. 3. **Stage 3 — The Instance Method `println()`:** The compiler then invokes the `println(String x)` method on the `PrintStream` object referenced by `out`. This method internally calls `String.valueOf(x)`, writes the resulting string, and then writes the line separator. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The `PrintStream` Class (in `java.io`):** > This is the actual workhorse. It provides all the overloaded versions of `print()`, `println()`, and `printf()`. It does **not throw checked IOException** (it uses an internal `boolean` flag `trouble` to track errors) — this is a deliberate design choice to keep simple console output code clean of try-catch blocks. ## 2.2 Escape Sequences — The "Invisible Characters" Escape sequences are two-character combinations beginning with a backslash (`\`) that represent characters which cannot be typed directly from the keyboard. They are essential for formatting console output. | Escape Sequence | Meaning | ASCII Code | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `\n` | New Line (Line Feed) | **10** | | `\t` | Horizontal Tab | **9** | | `\r` | Carriage Return | **13** | | `\\` | Backslash (literal) | **92** | | `\"` | Double Quote (literal) | **34** | | `\'` | Single Quote (literal) | **39** | | `\b` | Backspace | **8** | | `\f` | Form Feed | **12** | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Pitfall:** Students often write `System.out.println("Hello"World");` which is a **compilation error** because `"` ends the string prematurely. The correct form is `System.out.println("Hello\"World");`. This is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction if missed in ESE. ## 2.3 The `printf()` Method and Format Specifiers The `printf()` method is borrowed from the C language's `printf()` function. It allows precise control over the appearance of output using **format specifiers**. ### General Syntax of a Format Specifier $$ \%\left[\text{argument\_index}\$\right]\left[\text{flags}\right]\left[\text{width}\right]\left[.\text{precision}\right]\text{conversion} $$ ### KTU High-Yield Format Specifier Cheat Sheet | Specifier | Data Type Accepted | Description | Example | Output | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `%d` | `int`, `long`, `short`, `byte` | Decimal integer | `printf("%d", 42)` | `42` | | `%f` | `float`, `double` | Decimal floating-point | `printf("%.2f", 3.14159)` | `3.14` | | `%s` | `String` or any object | String representation | `printf("%s", "Java")` | `Java` | | `%c` | `char` | Single character | `printf("%c", 'A')` | `A` | | `%b` | `boolean` | Boolean value | `printf("%b", true)` | `true` | | `%n` | None | Platform-independent newline | `printf("Line%n")` | New line | | `%x` or `%X` | Integer | Hexadecimal (lower/upper) | `printf("%X", 255)` | `FF` | | `%o` | Integer | Octal | `printf("%o", 8)` | `10` | | `%e` or `%E` | Floating-point | Scientific notation | `printf("%e", 1234.5)` | `1.234500e+03` | ### Flags and Width Modifiers | Flag | Effect | Example | Output | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `-` | Left-justify within width | `printf("%-10d|", 42)` | `42 ` | | `+` | Force sign display | `printf("%+d", 42)` | `+42` | | `0` | Pad with leading zeros | `printf("%05d", 42)` | `00042` | | `,` | Group separator (thousands) | `printf("%,d", 1000000)` | `1,000,000` | ## 2.4 The `+` Concatenation Operator In Java, the `+` operator has a special **overloaded meaning** when **at least one operand is a `String`**: it performs **string concatenation** rather than arithmetic addition. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Left-to-Right Evaluation Rule (Critical for KTU):** > Java evaluates `+` strictly from **left to right**. If a `String` appears anywhere in the chain, all subsequent operands are converted to strings. **Example:** `System.out.println("Sum = " + 10 + 20);` - Step 1: `"Sum = " + 10` → `"Sum = 10"` - Step 2: `"Sum = 10" + 20` → `"Sum = 1020"` (NOT `30`!) To get the arithmetic sum, force evaluation with parentheses: `System.out.println("Sum = " + (10 + 20));` → `Sum = 30`. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Logging Frameworks:** Production-grade systems (SLF4J, Log4j) wrap `System.out` to inject timestamps, thread IDs, and severity levels. - **Scientific Computing:** `printf("%.6f", result)` is the standard for publishing floating-point experimental data with controlled precision. - **Embedded Systems / IoT:** Java ME uses the same `System.out` abstraction to send diagnostics over serial console ports. - **Financial Applications:** The `%,.2f` specifier is universally used to format currency (e.g., `$1,234.56`). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Code Analysis: `print()` vs `println()` ### Example Program ```java public class OutputDemo1 { public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.print("Welcome to "); System.out.print("KTU 2024 Scheme"); System.out.println(" B.Tech"); System.out.println("Module 2: Console Output"); } } ``` ### Line-by-Line Evaluation | Line of Code | Action | Cursor Position After | Console Content So Far | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `System.out.print("Welcome to ");` | Writes the string. **No newline emitted.** | End of `"Welcome to "` on line 1. | `Welcome to ` | | `System.out.print("KTU 2024 Scheme");` | Writes the next string right after the previous one. | End of `"KTU 2024 Scheme"` on line 1. | `Welcome to KTU 2024 Scheme` | | `System.out.println(" B.Tech");` | Writes `" B.Tech"` and then **emits a line separator**. | Beginning of line 2. | `Welcome to KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech` (line 1) | | `System.out.println("Module 2: Console Output");` | Writes the string and emits a line separator. | Beginning of line 3. | `Module 2: Console Output` (line 2) | ### Final Console Output ``` Welcome to KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Module 2: Console Output ``` ## 3.2 Exhaustive Code Analysis: `printf()` with Format Specifiers ### Example Program ```java public class OutputDemo2 { public static void main(String[] args) { String studentName = "Ananya"; int rollNo = 42; double cgpa = 9.8765; char grade = 'A'; System.out.printf("Name: %s | Roll: %d | CGPA: %.2f | Grade: %c%n", studentName, rollNo, cgpa, grade); System.out.printf("Hex of %d is %X%n", rollNo, rollNo); System.out.printf("Padded: [%05d]%n", rollNo); System.out.printf("Currency: %,.2f INR%n", 1234567.891); } } ``` ### Step-by-Step Resolution **Line 1 — `printf("Name: %s | Roll: %d | CGPA: %.2f | Grade: %c%n", ...)`** - `%s` → consumes `studentName` → `Ananya` - `%d` → consumes `rollNo` → `42` - `%.2f` → consumes `cgpa`, rounds to **2 decimal places** → `9.88` (rounded from 9.8765 using banker's rounding rules in Java) - `%c` → consumes `grade` → `A` - `%n` → emits a platform-independent newline. **Line 2 — `printf("Hex of %d is %X%n", rollNo, rollNo)`** - `%d` → `42` - `%X` → converts `42` to uppercase hexadecimal → `2A` (since 42 = 2×16 + 10 = 0x2A) **Line 3 — `printf("Padded: [%05d]%n", rollNo)`** - `%05d` → prints the integer in a field of **width 5**, padded with leading `0`s → `00042` **Line 4 — `printf("Currency: %,.2f INR%n", 1234567.891)`** - `%,.2f` → prints the double with **thousand separators** and 2 decimal places → `1,234,567.89` ### Final Console Output ``` Name: Ananya | Roll: 42 | CGPA: 9.88 | Grade: A Hex of 42 is 2A Padded: [00042] Currency: 1,234,567.89 INR ``` ## 3.3 Exhaustive Analysis: Escape Sequences in Action ### Example Program ```java public class OutputDemo3 { public static void main(String[] args) { System.out.println("Item\tPrice\tQty"); System.out.println("----\t-----\t---"); System.out.println("Pen\tRs.10\t2"); System.out.println("Book\tRs.50\t1"); System.out.println("File saved at: C:\\Users\\Ananya\\Documents\\result.txt"); System.out.println("She said, \"Java is fun!\""); } } ``` ### Step-by-Step Resolution | Source Code Token | Rendered Output | Reason | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `\t` | A horizontal tab gap (≈ 8 spaces) | Tab character ASCII 9 is emitted. | | `\\` | A single `\` | First `\` escapes the second. | | `\"` | A single `"` | Backslash escapes the quote. | | `\n` | A new line | Line Feed ASCII 10 is emitted. | ### Final Console Output ``` Item Price Qty ---- ----- --- Pen Rs.10 2 Book Rs.50 1 File saved at: C:\Users\Ananya\Documents\result.txt She said, "Java is fun!" ``` ## 3.4 Exhaustive Analysis: The `+` Concatenation Trap ### Example Program ```java public class OutputDemo4 { public static void main(String[] args) { int a = 10, b = 20; System.out.println("a + b = " + a + b); System.out.println("a + b = " + (a + b)); } } ``` ### Step-by-Step Resolution **Statement 1:** `"a + b = " + a + b` - **Step A:** `"a + b = " + a` → `a` is an `int`, but since left operand is `String`, it is converted via `String.valueOf(10)` → `"a + b = 10"`. - **Step B:** `"a + b = 10" + b` → Left operand is now `String`, so `b=20` is also converted via `String.valueOf(20)` → `"a + b = 1020"`. **Statement 2:** `"a + b = " + (a + b)` - **Step A:** Parentheses force the inner arithmetic first → `(a + b)` → `30` (an `int`). - **Step B:** `"a + b = " + 30` → `"a + b = 30"`. ### Final Console Output ``` a + b = 1020 a + b = 30 ``` ## 3.5 Mathematical Reference: Total Output Width Calculation For a `printf` specifier of the form `%-W.Pf`, the total number of characters consumed (including the sign, decimal point, and digits) is governed by the formula: $$ \text{Total Width} = W \quad \text{(minimum field width, padded with spaces if content is shorter)} $$ The **precision** `P` for `%f` controls the number of digits **after the decimal point**. For `%s`, it controls the **maximum number of characters** printed from the string. **Example:** `printf("[%-10.3s]", "HELLO");` - `P=3` → only the first 3 characters of `"HELLO"` → `"HEL"` - `W=10` → field width is 10; `"HEL"` is left-justified with 7 trailing spaces. - Final output: `[HEL ]` # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: The `System.out.println()` Resolution Chain ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Java Source Code:
System.out.println('Hello')"] --> B["Stage 1: Class Lookup
Compiler finds java.lang.System"] B --> C["Stage 2: Static Field Access
System.out is a public static final PrintStream"] C --> D["Stage 3: Method Dispatch
println(String) is invoked on PrintStream object"] D --> E["PrintStream internal logic:
1. Convert arg to String via String.valueOf()
2. Write bytes to underlying OutputStream
3. Flush the line separator"] E --> F["Operating System stdout
(fd 1 on Unix / handle on Windows)"] F --> G["Console / Terminal
Displays: Hello"] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Method Family of `PrintStream` ```mermaid flowchart LR A["PrintStream Class
(java.io)"] --> B["print() methods
No newline"] A --> C["println() methods
Appends line separator"] A --> D["printf() methods
C-style formatting"] A --> E["format() methods
Same as printf"] A --> F["append() methods
Implements Appendable"] B --> G["Overloaded for:
String, int, long,
float, double,
char, boolean, Object"] C --> G D --> H["Format Specifiers:
%d, %f, %s, %c,
%b, %n, %x, %o"] ``` ## 4.3 Flowchart: Decision Logic for Choosing the Right Method ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Need to write to console?"] --> Q1{"Is formatted
output required?"} Q1 -- "Yes" --> P1["Use printf()
with format specifiers"] Q1 -- "No" --> Q2{"Need a new line
after the output?"} Q2 -- "Yes" --> P2["Use println()"] Q2 -- "No" --> P3["Use print()"] P1 --> End["Output rendered on console"] P2 --> End P3 --> End ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks each) ### Question A1 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]** Explain the difference between `print()` and `println()` methods in Java. How is each method associated with the `System` class? **Model Answer:** The `print()` method outputs a string to the console **without** appending a line separator, so the cursor remains on the same line after execution. The `println()` method outputs a string and then **appends a platform-specific line separator** (`\n` on Unix, `\r\n` on Windows), moving the cursor to the start of the next line. Both methods are instance methods of the `java.io.PrintStream` class. They are accessed through the `public static final` field `System.out`, which is a `PrintStream` object pre-created by the JVM and connected to the standard output stream. Because `out` is `static`, no `System` object needs to be instantiated. **Marks distribution: Definition (1) + System class linkage (1).** ### Question A2 **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]** What are escape sequences in Java? List any four commonly used escape sequences with their meanings. **Model Answer:** Escape sequences are two-character combinations starting with a backslash (`\`) used to represent characters that cannot be typed directly from the keyboard inside a string literal. **Four common escape sequences:** - `\n` — New line (Line Feed, ASCII 10) - `\t` — Horizontal Tab (ASCII 9) - `\\` — Backslash literal (ASCII 92) - `\"` — Double Quote literal (ASCII 34) **Marks distribution: Definition (1) + Four listed sequences (1).** --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question A (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model, Module 2 — 14 Marks]** **(a)** Explain the `printf()` method in Java with its general syntax. List any **six** format specifiers used with `printf()` and describe each with an example. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a Java program that demonstrates the use of `print()`, `println()`, escape sequences (`\n`, `\t`, `\\`, `\"`), and `printf()` with at least three format specifiers. Show the expected output. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): The `printf()` method is defined in `java.io.PrintStream` and is used to write a **formatted** string to the console using C-style format specifiers. It is declared as: `PrintStream printf(String format, Object... args)`. **General Syntax of a Format Specifier:** $$ \%\left[\text{argument\_index}\$\right]\left[\text{flags}\right]\left[\text{width}\right]\left[.\text{precision}\right]\text{conversion} $$ **Six Format Specifiers:** | Specifier | Meaning | Example Statement | Output | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | `%d` | Decimal integer | `printf("%d", 25);` | `25` | | `%f` | Floating-point decimal | `printf("%.3f", 3.14159);` | `3.142` | | `%s` | String | `printf("%s", "KTU");` | `KTU` | | `%c` | Character | `printf("%c", 'Z');` | `Z` | | `%b` | Boolean | `printf("%b", false);` | `false` | | `%n` | Platform-independent newline | `printf("Line1%nLine2");` | Two lines | **Marks distribution: Syntax explanation (2) + Six specifiers in table (3) + Examples (2).** #### Model Solution for (b): ```java public class ConsoleOutputDemo { public static void main(String[] args) { // Demonstration of print() vs println() System.out.print("Hello"); System.out.print(" World"); System.out.println("!"); // newline emitted here // Demonstration of escape sequences System.out.println("Subject\tMarks\tGrade"); System.out.println("------\t-----\t-----"); System.out.println("Maths\t95\tA"); System.out.println("Java\t88\tB+"); // Path requires escaped backslashes System.out.println("Path: C:\\Program Files\\Java\\jdk"); // Quotes inside a string System.out.println("He said, \"Java is portable!\""); // Demonstration of printf() with three specifiers String name = "Rahul"; int age = 21; double marks = 87.4567; System.out.printf("Name: %s | Age: %d | Marks: %.2f%n", name, age, marks); } } ``` **Expected Output:** ``` Hello World! Subject Marks Grade ------ ----- ----- Maths 95 A Java 88 B+ Path: C:\Program Files\Java\jdk He said, "Java is portable!" Name: Rahul | Age: 21 | Marks: 87.46 ``` **Marks distribution: Correct use of print/println (2) + Correct escape sequences (2) + Correct printf with three specifiers (2) + Output accuracy (1).** --- ### Question B (Choice 2) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model, Module 2 — 14 Marks]** **(a)** Explain the `System` class and its `out` field. How is `System.out.println()` internally resolved in Java? Describe the role of the `PrintStream` class. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Write a Java program that prints a **formatted bill receipt** for a customer containing: customer name (string), item code (integer), quantity (integer), unit price (float with 2 decimals), and total amount (float with 2 decimals, computed as quantity × unit price). Use `printf()` with appropriate format specifiers, padding, and separators. Show the expected output. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): The `System` class is a `final` class defined in the `java.lang` package. It cannot be instantiated (its constructor is `private`) and cannot be subclassed (declared `final`). It provides **static** facilities for standard input, standard output, and error streams, among other system-level operations. The `out` field is declared as: `public static final PrintStream out;` It is a `PrintStream` object that is **pre-instantiated** by the JVM during class initialization and is connected to the host operating system's standard output stream (file descriptor 1 on Unix-like systems). Because it is `static`, it is accessed using the class name itself: `System.out`, without needing any object. **Internal Resolution of `System.out.println("Hello")`:** 1. **Class lookup:** The compiler locates `java.lang.System` (auto-imported). 2. **Static field access:** It accesses the `static` field `out` of type `PrintStream`. 3. **Method invocation:** The overloaded `println(String x)` instance method is called on the `PrintStream` object. 4. **Internal execution:** `PrintStream` converts the argument to a string (via `String.valueOf`), writes its bytes to the underlying buffered `OutputStream`, then writes the line separator obtained from `System.lineSeparator()`. 5. **Flush:** The bytes are eventually flushed to the OS, which renders them on the console. **Role of `PrintStream`:** It is the actual class that does the work — it provides all overloaded `print()`, `println()`, `printf()`, and `format()` methods. It is designed to **never throw a checked `IOException`**; instead, it sets an internal `trouble` flag, keeping console-output code free of mandatory try-catch blocks. **Marks distribution: System class explanation (2) + out field explanation (1) + Resolution chain (3) + PrintStream role (1).** #### Model Solution for (b): ```java public class BillReceipt { public static void main(String[] args) { String customerName = "Ananya Krishnan"; int itemCode = 1042; int quantity = 3; double unitPrice = 249.5; double totalAmount = quantity * unitPrice; System.out.println("============================================"); System.out.printf(" KTU BOOK STORE RECEIPT%n"); System.out.println("============================================"); System.out.printf("Customer : %s%n", customerName); System.out.printf("Item Code : %d%n", itemCode); System.out.printf("Quantity : %d%n", quantity); System.out.printf("Unit Price: Rs. %.2f%n", unitPrice); System.out.println("--------------------------------------------"); System.out.printf("TOTAL : Rs. %,.2f%n", totalAmount); System.out.println("============================================"); System.out.printf("Thank you, %s! Visit again.%n", customerName); } } ``` **Expected Output:** ``` ============================================ KTU BOOK STORE RECEIPT ============================================ Customer : Ananya Krishnan Item Code : 1042 Quantity : 3 Unit Price: Rs. 249.50 -------------------------------------------- TOTAL : Rs. 748.50 ============================================ Thank you, Ananya Krishnan! Visit again. ``` **Marks distribution: Correct variable declarations (1) + Correct printf syntax (3) + Use of padding / thousand separator / 2-decimal precision (2) + Accurate output (1).** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Concatenation Trap:** Writing `"Sum = " + a + b` instead of `"Sum = " + (a + b)` is the **#1 reason students lose 1 mark** in Part A numeric-output questions. Always remember **left-to-right evaluation**. > 2. **Missing `;` in `printf`:** A single missing semicolon at the end of a `printf` statement costs 1 full mark. KTU examiners will NOT grant partial credit for syntax errors. > 3. **Wrong Escape for Paths:** Writing `C:\Users\Ananya` instead of `C:\\Users\\Ananya` produces a compilation error with several "illegal escape character" messages. Always double-escape backslashes. > 4. **Mixing `%d` with `double`:** Passing a `double` to `%d` throws `java.util.IllegalFormatConversionException` at runtime. KTU examiners test this in Part B. > 5. **Forgetting `%n`:** Using `\n` inside `printf` works on most systems but is **not strictly portable**; `%n` is the KTU-recommended choice. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **`System.out`** is a `public static final PrintStream` field — accessible without instantiation. - **`print()`** writes without a newline; **`println()`** writes and appends a line separator; **`printf()`** writes a formatted string. - **`PrintStream`** (in `java.io`) is the workhorse class; it never throws checked `IOException`. - **Escape sequences** must be used for special characters: `\n`, `\t`, `\r`, `\\`, `\"`, `\'`, `\b`, `\f`. - **Format specifiers** in `printf()`: `%d` (int), `%f` (float/double), `%s` (String), `%c` (char), `%b` (boolean), `%n` (newline), `%x` (hex), `%o` (octal). - **Flags**: `-` (left-justify), `+` (force sign), `0` (zero-pad), `,` (thousands separator). - **Precision `.P`** in `%f` controls decimal digits; in `%s` it limits printed characters. - **Concatenation `+`** binds left-to-right; use parentheses to force arithmetic before string conversion. - **`%n` is preferred over `\n`** inside `printf()` for true platform independence. - **Common KTU marks-losers:** unescaped quotes, missing parentheses in concatenation, mixing data types with wrong specifiers, and forgetting semicolons. - **Real-world equivalents:** `System.out.printf("%,.2f", value)` is the standard for currency formatting; `System.out.printf("%-20s", name)` is the standard for column-aligned text output in CLI tools.

topics.title: Packages - Defining Package
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Packages - Defining Package

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

> [!NOTE]
> **Package:** A Java *package* is a **namespace** that organises a set of related **classes**, **interfaces**, **enumerations**, and **sub-packages** into a single logical unit. Conceptually, it is a **folding mechanism** for grouping semantically related types and for **preventing naming conflicts** in large software systems.

In the context of *defining a package*, the act of **defining a package** means **declaring a Java source file (or a collection of source files) to belong to a specific package** by placing a `package` statement as the **first non-comment, non-whitespace line** of the file. This declaration binds the type's **fully qualified name (FQN)** to a logical directory hierarchy on the file system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a Java package as a **labelled filing cabinet in a large office**:

| Office Analogy | Java Mapping |
|---|---|
| The **office building** | The **Java Development Kit (JDK)** library |
| A **floor** in the building (e.g., 4th floor – "Accounts") | A **package** (e.g., `com.company.accounts`) |
| A **drawer** within a floor | A **sub-package** (e.g., `com.company.accounts.payroll`) |
| A **single file folder** in a drawer | A **class/interface** file (`.java` / `.class`) |
| The **sticker label** on the folder | The **package declaration** (`package …;`) |
| The **room number + drawer + folder** address | The **fully qualified name** (`com.company.accounts.payroll.Salary`) |

If two employees (classes) have the same first name, disambiguation is achieved by specifying the floor (package). **Packages exist for two principal reasons:**
1. **Namespace Management** – Avoids class name collisions.
2. **Access Control** – Enforces encapsulation at the module level using package-private (default) visibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The `package` statement, if present, **must be the first executable statement** in a `.java` file. Only **comments and whitespace** are allowed before it. Violating this rule is a **compilation error** worth **2 marks** in the ESE.

## 1.3 Standard Package Metrics & Terminology

- **Fully Qualified Name (FQN):** `packagename.ClassName` (e.g., `java.util.Scanner`).
- **Package Naming Convention:** All **lowercase**, hierarchical, reverse-domain style (e.g., `in.ac.ktu.cse.oop.lab3`).
- **Default (Unnamed) Package:** A `.java` file with **no `package` statement** belongs to the *unnamed default package* — only valid for small standalone programs.
- **Sealed Packages (Java 17+):** Packages whose contents are restricted to specific sub-packages and modules. *(Beyond KTU 2024 scope — mentioned only for awareness.)*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the `package` Statement

The general syntactic template for **defining a package** is:

$$ \texttt{package \ [<package\_name>];} $$

Where:
- `package` is a **reserved keyword** in the Java language.
- `[<package_name>]` is a **single, dot-separated, hierarchical identifier** (e.g., `com.ktu.pbeot404.module3`).
- The statement is **terminated by a semicolon `;`**.

### 2.1.1 Why a Single Line Controls the Whole File

When the Java compiler (`javac`) encounters a `package` statement, it performs three binding operations:

1. **Namespace Binding:** The compiled `.class` file's FQN becomes `<package_name>.<ClassName>`.
2. **File-System Binding:** The compiler expects the `.class` output to reside in a directory tree mirroring the package hierarchy.
3. **Access-Binding:** The class becomes a *member* of that package, which determines:
   - Default (package-private) access for its members.
   - The set of other types it can `package-private` reference without imports.

## 2.2 Categories of Packages

Java provides two broad categories, both examinable under KTU Module 3:

| # | Category | Definition | Examples | Who Defines? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Built-in (Implicit / API) Packages** | Pre-defined libraries shipped with the JDK | `java.lang`, `java.util`, `java.io`, `java.net`, `java.awt`, `javax.swing` | Oracle / OpenJDK |
| 2 | **User-defined (Explicit) Packages** | Packages created by programmers using the `package` statement | `mypack`, `college.students.cse`, `com.acme.inventory` | The programmer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`java.lang` is auto-imported** — classes like `System`, `String`, `Math`, `Object` are available without an explicit `import` statement.

## 2.3 Naming Convention Rules (KTU High-Yield)

- All **lowercase letters** — to avoid clash with class names.
- Use the **reverse Internet domain** of the organisation to ensure global uniqueness.
- **Hierarchical levels** separated by `.` (dot) — each level corresponds to a **sub-directory**.
- Java keywords and reserved words **must not** be used (e.g., `package.new` is **illegal** because `new` is a keyword).

## 2.4 Directory Structure: The Physical Realisation

A package `college.students.cse` **must** physically map to the directory tree:

```
project_root/
└── college/
    └── students/
        └── cse/
            ├── StudentRecord.class
            └── ResultCalculator.class
```

This 1-to-1 mapping between **package name ↔ directory path** is enforced by the Java compiler and the JVM ClassLoader.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Syntax / Rule | Constraint / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Package Declaration | `package name1.name2;` | Must be **first statement** in file |
| 2 | Single-package-per-file | One file → one package | Multiple classes allowed in one file but **all share the same package** |
| 3 | FQN Format | `packagename.ClassName` | Used when no `import` is provided |
| 4 | Directory Mapping | Every `.` in package name = one directory level | Enforced by `javac` and JVM |
| 5 | Compile with Destination | `javac -d . MyClass.java` | `-d .` creates directories automatically |
| 6 | Run a Packaged Class | `java packagename.MyClass` | JVM uses CLASSPATH / classpath |
| 7 | Default Package Access | `class` member with no modifier | Visible **only within the same package** |
| 8 | `import` Single Class | `import packagename.ClassName;` | Avoids FQN repetition |
| 9 | `import` Whole Package | `import packagename.*;` | Imports only **types**, not sub-packages |
| 10 | Static Import | `import static packagename.ClassName.member;` | For constants / static methods (Java 5+) |
| 11 | Auto-imported Package | `java.lang` | Always available |
| 12 | Unnamed Package | No `package` statement | Classes in default package cannot be imported |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microservices & Modular Design:** Each microservice maps to a top-level package.
- **Library Development:** Distributed JARs (e.g., Apache Commons) use packages to namespace their APIs.
- **Android SDK:** Every Android API class is namespaced under `android.*` or `androidx.*`.
- **Enterprise Java (Jakarta EE):** The entire servlet/JPA stack is organised via packages such as `jakarta.servlet.*` and `jakarta.persistence.*`.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Examples & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since the topic is **conceptual + procedural**, the "derivation" here refers to the **step-by-step mechanical procedure** for defining, compiling, and using a user-defined package — analogous to a mathematical derivation in rigour.

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Defining a Simple Single-Level Package

### Step 1 — Create the source directory
Open a terminal and create a project folder:
```
mkdir C:\JavaProjects\Module3
cd C:\JavaProjects\Module3
mkdir mypack
```

### Step 2 — Write the source file
Using any text editor, create `C:\JavaProjects\Module3\mypack\Demo.java`:

```java
package mypack;   // <-- THIS LINE DEFINES THE PACKAGE

public class Demo {
    public void showMessage() {
        System.out.println("Hello from package mypack!");
    }
}
```

**Line-by-line reasoning:**
- `package mypack;` — Binds the class `Demo` to the namespace `mypack`.
- `public class Demo` — Must be `public` if accessed from outside the package.
- `public void showMessage()` — Public method, callable across packages.

### Step 3 — Compile with directory creation
From `C:\JavaProjects\Module3`, run:
```
javac -d . mypack\Demo.java
```
The `-d .` flag tells the compiler to **place the generated `.class` file in a directory structure mirroring the package name, relative to the current directory** (`.`). The compiler auto-creates the `mypack/` directory and writes `Demo.class` inside it.

### Step 4 — Verify directory layout
```
C:\JavaProjects\Module3\
└── mypack\
    └── Demo.class   (generated)
```

### Step 5 — Use the class from another package
Create `C:\JavaProjects\Module3\TestPackage.java`:

```java
import mypack.Demo;   // imports the specific class

public class TestPackage {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Demo obj = new Demo();
        obj.showMessage();
    }
}
```

### Step 6 — Compile and run
```
javac TestPackage.java
java TestPackage
```

**Output:**
```
Hello from package mypack!
```

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Hierarchical (Multi-Level) Package

Suppose we want package: `college.department.cse.semester4`

### Step 1 — Create nested directories
```
mkdir -p college\department\cse\semester4
```

### Step 2 — Write the source
File: `college\department\cse\semester4\Student.java`

```java
package college.department.cse.semester4;

public class Student {
    private String name;
    private int rollNo;

    public Student(String name, int rollNo) {
        this.name = name;
        this.rollNo = rollNo;
    }

    public void display() {
        System.out.println("Name  : " + name);
        System.out.println("RollNo: " + rollNo);
    }
}
```

### Step 3 — Compile
```
javac -d . college\department\cse\semester4\Student.java
```

### Step 4 — Access from a client program
```java
import college.department.cse.semester4.Student;

public class StudentApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Student s1 = new Student("Ananya", 47);
        s1.display();
    }
}
```

### Step 5 — Output
```
Name  : Ananya
RollNo: 47
```

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Access Modifiers vs. Package Scope

| Modifier | Same Class | Same Package (Sub-class) | Same Package (Non-Sub-class) | Different Package (Sub-class) | Different Package (Non-Sub-class) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `private` | ✔ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ | ✘ |
| *default* (package-private) | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✘ | ✘ |
| `protected` | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✘ |
| `public` | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ | ✔ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When you **define a package** and place classes inside it, those classes automatically gain **package-private** access to each other — a powerful encapsulation primitive frequently tested in KTU Module 3.

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Default (Package-Private) Access Within a Package

**File 1 — `mypack/Helper.java`**
```java
package mypack;

class Helper {                    // note: no 'public' → package-private
    void greet() {               // package-private method
        System.out.println("Greetings from Helper");
    }
}
```

**File 2 — `mypack/MainApp.java`**
```java
package mypack;

public class MainApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Helper h = new Helper();  // ✔ Accessible: same package
        h.greet();                // ✔ Accessible: same package
    }
}
```

**Compilation & Run:**
```
javac -d . mypack\Helper.java mypack\MainApp.java
java mypack.MainApp
```

**Output:**
```
Greetings from Helper
```

> [!WARNING]
> If `Helper` is declared **package-private** (no `public` keyword), then a class in **another package** cannot import it. Attempting `import mypack.Helper;` from outside `mypack` causes:
> `error: Helper is not public in mypack; cannot be accessed from outside package`

## 3.5 Worked Example 4: Fully Qualified Name (FQN) Usage Without `import`

```java
public class NoImportDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // No import statement — using FQN directly
        mypack.Demo obj = new mypack.Demo();
        obj.showMessage();
    }
}
```

**Use case:** When **two imported classes have the same simple name** from different packages, KTU board papers often require resolving the ambiguity via FQN. Example:
```java
import java.util.Date;
import java.sql.Date;     // CONFLICT — compile error

public class Conflict {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        java.util.Date d1 = new java.util.Date();  // FQN disambiguates
        java.sql.Date  d2 = new java.sql.Date(0);
    }
}
```

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram: Lifecycle of Defining & Using a Package

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Programmer writes .java file] --> B[First line: package statement]
    B --> C{Is package statement valid?}
    C -- No --> X[Compilation Error: package must be first]
    C -- Yes --> D[Compile with javac -d .]
    D --> E[Auto-create directory tree]
    E --> F[Generate .class file in correct folder]
    F --> G[Another file imports the class]
    G --> H[Compile client .java]
    H --> I[Run with java command]
    I --> J[JVM ClassLoader locates class via CLASSPATH]
    J --> K[Program executes successfully]
```

## 4.2 Diagram: Hierarchical Package → Directory Mapping

    Root[Project Root: JavaProjects/Module3]
    Root --> P1[mypack/]
    Root --> P2[college/]
    Root --> P3[default unnamed package: TestPackage.java]

    P1 --> D1[Demo.class]
    P1 --> H1[Helper.class]
    P1 --> M1[MainApp.class]

    P2 --> Dept[department/]
    Dept --> Cse[cse/]

```

```mermaid

    end

    end

```

## 4.4 Diagram: Compile-Run Workflow with CLASSPATH

    JC->>FS: Create mypack/ directory
    JC->>FS: Write mypack/Demo.class
    Dev->>JC: javac TestPackage.java

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**What is a package in Java? Why is it used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A package in Java is a **namespace** that organises a set of related **classes, interfaces, and sub-packages** into a single logical unit. It is declared using the `package` statement as the first line of a `.java` file.

Packages are used for:
1. **Preventing naming conflicts** — two classes can share a simple name if they live in different packages.
2. **Logical grouping** of related types (e.g., all I/O classes under `java.io`).
3. **Access control** — default (package-private) visibility restricts members to the same package.
4. **Easier maintenance and reusability** of large codebases.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four uses.)*

---

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**Explain the role of the `import` statement. Differentiate between `import packagename.ClassName;` and `import packagename.*;`.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The `import` statement makes types from another package **directly accessible by their simple name** in the current file, avoiding repetitive use of fully qualified names. It is placed **after** the `package` statement and **before** the class declaration.

| Form | Effect |
|---|---|
| `import packagename.ClassName;` | Imports **only** `ClassName`. Smaller namespace pollution. |
| `import packagename.*;` | Imports **all public types** declared **directly** in `packagename`. Does **not** recursively import sub-packages. |

Note: `java.lang.*` is **implicitly** imported; no explicit statement is required for its classes.

*(Award 1 mark for role, 2 marks for comparison table.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[INTERNAL CHOICE — Answer ANY ONE of the following]**

---

### Question B-A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` — **14 Marks**

**(a)** Explain the concept of packages in Java. Discuss the advantages of using packages. **【7 marks】**

**(b)** Write a Java program to create a user-defined package `shapes` containing a class `Circle` with a method `area(double r)` that returns the area. Write another program that imports and uses this class. **【7 marks】**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**Definition (2 marks):** A Java package is a **grouping mechanism** for related classes and interfaces, defined via the `package` keyword. It serves as a *namespace* and a *visibility boundary*.

**Key Rules (2 marks):**
- The `package` declaration must be the **first** statement (after comments) in the source file.
- The physical directory structure must mirror the package hierarchy.
- Classes with default (no) access are visible only within the package.
- The compiler flag `-d` is used to control the output directory.

**Advantages (3 marks):**
1. **Namespace management** — prevents name collisions.
2. **Encapsulation at package level** — package-private members hide implementation details.
3. **Logical organisation** — improves code readability and maintainability.
4. **Reusability** — packaged libraries (JARs) can be distributed.
5. **Access protection** — restricts which classes from outside the package may extend or instantiate package members.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Create directory `shapes/`.**

**Step 2: File `shapes/Circle.java`:**
```java
package shapes;                          // Defines package 'shapes'

public class Circle {                    // public → accessible across packages
    public double area(double r) {       // public method
        return Math.PI * r * r;
    }
}
```

**Step 3: Compile:**
```
javac -d . shapes/Circle.java
```

**Step 4: File `TestShapes.java` (in default package):**
```java
import shapes.Circle;                    // Imports class from 'shapes' package

public class TestShapes {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Circle c = new Circle();
        double result = c.area(5.0);
        System.out.println("Area of Circle (r=5.0) = " + result);
    }
}
```

**Step 5: Compile & Run:**
```
javac TestShapes.java
java TestShapes
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Area of Circle (r=5.0) = 78.53981633974483
```

**Mark distribution (7 marks):**
- Correct `package shapes;` declaration: **1 mark**
- `public class Circle` with valid `area` method: **2 marks**
- Correct `import` in client file: **1 mark**
- Correct object instantiation & invocation: **1 mark**
- Correct compilation/run commands & output: **2 marks**

---

### Question B-B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` — **14 Marks** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Differentiate between **built-in** and **user-defined** packages. Give two examples of each. **【7 marks】**

**(b)** Write a Java program demonstrating a **hierarchical package** `college.department.cse` containing a class `Faculty` with members `name` and `id`, and a method `display()`. Demonstrate its use with a separate driver class. **【7 marks】**

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

| # | Built-in Packages | User-defined Packages |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Pre-defined by Java / JDK | Created by the programmer using `package` statement |
| 2 | Shipped with `jrt-fs.jar` (Java 9+) | Stored in user projects / distributed as JARs |
| 3 | Auto-imported: `java.lang` (others need `import`) | Must be explicitly imported or accessed via FQN |
| 4 | Example: `java.util`, `java.io`, `java.net`, `java.sql`, `java.awt` | Example: `mypack`, `college.students.cse`, `shapes` |
| 5 | Source code is **read-only** and part of JDK | Source code is **writable** and owned by developer |

**Examples (2 marks):**
- Built-in: `java.util.ArrayList`, `java.io.File`
- User-defined: `mypack.Demo`, `shapes.Circle`

*(Award 3 marks for table content, 2 marks for valid examples, 2 marks for explanatory precision.)*

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Directory creation**
```
mkdir -p college\department\cse
```

**Step 2: File `college\department\cse\Faculty.java`**
```java
package college.department.cse;          // Defines hierarchical package

public class Faculty {
    public String name;
    public int id;

    public Faculty(String name, int id) {
        this.name = name;
        this.id   = id;
    }

    public void display() {
        System.out.println("Faculty Name : " + name);
        System.out.println("Faculty ID   : " + id);
    }
}
```

**Step 3: Compile:**
```
javac -d . college\department\cse\Faculty.java
```

**Step 4: File `FacultyApp.java` (in default package)**
```java
import college.department.cse.Faculty;

public class FacultyApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Faculty f = new Faculty("Dr. Meena Pillai", 1042);
        f.display();
    }
}
```

**Step 5: Compile & Run**
```
javac FacultyApp.java
java FacultyApp
```

**Expected Output**
```
Faculty Name : Dr. Meena Pillai
Faculty ID   : 1042
```

**Mark distribution (7 marks):**
- Correct directory hierarchy creation: **1 mark**
- `package college.department.cse;` line: **1 mark**
- `Faculty` class with two members + constructor: **2 marks**
- Correct `display()` method: **1 mark**
- Driver class with proper `import` and output: **2 marks**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Module 3 – Packages)**
> 1. **Missing `-d` flag during compilation** — Students often run `javac Demo.java` which **fails to create the package directory** → 2 marks lost.
> 2. **Running `java Demo` instead of `java mypack.Demo`** — JVM cannot find the class → 1 mark lost.
> 3. **Placing `package` statement *after* `import` or `class`** — Compilation error → 2 marks lost.
> 4. **Forgetting to make class `public`** — Other packages cannot import it → 1 mark lost.
> 5. **Confusing sub-package import (`*`)** — `import java.util.*;` does **not** import `java.util.concurrent.*`. The asterisk is **non-recursive** → 2 marks lost.
> 6. **Mismatch between package name and directory name** (e.g., `package mypack;` inside `myfiles/`) → Compiler fails to locate source → 2 marks lost.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Defining a Package**

- [x] **Keyword:** `package` is a reserved Java keyword used solely to declare a package.
- [x] **Position Rule:** Must be the **first executable statement**; only comments/whitespace may precede it.
- [x] **Syntax:** `package <dotted.identifier.path>;`
- [x] **One Package per Source Compilation Unit:** All top-level types in one file share a single `package` declaration.
- [x] **Naming Convention:** All **lowercase**, reverse-domain style (e.g., `in.ac.ktu.cse.pbeot404`).
- [x] **Directory Mirroring:** Every dot `.` in the package name corresponds to **one nested sub-directory**.
- [x] **Compile Flag:** Use `javac -d . FileName.java` to auto-generate the package directory tree.
- [x] **Run Command:** Use `java packagename.ClassName` (FQN), never the simple class name.
- [x] **Auto-Import:** `java.lang.*` is **implicitly** imported.
- [x] **`import` Forms:** (i) Specific class import, (ii) On-demand (`.*`) import — **non-recursive**, (iii) Static import.
- [x] **FQN Disambiguation:** Used when two imports collide (e.g., `java.util.Date` vs `java.sql.Date`).
- [x] **Default (Unnamed) Package:** A file with no `package` statement; cannot be imported from named packages.
- [x] **Package-Private Access:** Default modifier → visible **only** to classes in the **same package**.
- [x] **CLASSPATH:** Environment variable (or `-cp` flag) used by the JVM ClassLoader to locate packaged classes at runtime.
- [x] **JAR Distribution:** Packaged classes are commonly archived into `.jar` files using `jar` tool for distribution.
- [x] **Modular Note (Java 9+):** Beyond KTU 2024 scope, but packages may be encapsulated inside modules via `module-info.java`.

---
