<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
**Define checked exceptions in Java. List any four examples with their typical scenarios.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Checked exceptions are exceptions that are verified at compile-time in Java. They are subclasses of `java.lang.Exception` but not subclasses of `java.lang.RuntimeException`. The compiler forces the programmer to either handle them using `try-catch` or declare them using the `throws` keyword.

**Examples with Scenarios (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **`IOException`** — Raised when an input-output operation fails, e.g., reading from a network socket.
2. **`FileNotFoundException`** — Raised when a specified file path does not exist, e.g., opening `"config.txt"` that was deleted.
3. **`SQLException`** — Raised when a database query fails, e.g., invalid SQL syntax or broken JDBC connection.
4. **`ClassNotFoundException`** — Raised when JVM cannot find a required `.class` file at runtime, e.g., missing library JAR.
5. **`ParseException`** — Raised when text fails to conform to an expected format, e.g., parsing `"31-13-2025"` as a date.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**
**Differentiate between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Checked Exception | Unchecked Exception |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Verification Time | Compile-time | Runtime |
| Parent Class | `Exception` (not `RuntimeException`) | `RuntimeException` |
| Handling | Mandatory (try-catch or throws) | Optional |
| Examples | `IOException`, `SQLException` | `NullPointerException`, `ArithmeticException` |
| Cause | External conditions (I/O, DB) | Programming logic errors |

Checked exceptions represent **predictable external failures** (e.g., network disconnection), while unchecked exceptions usually indicate **bugs in the code logic** (e.g., dividing by zero).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Pattern Note:** Each Part B question contains sub-parts **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**. Two alternatives are provided — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### 📘 QUESTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Q. A (a)** Explain the concept of checked exceptions in Java. Discuss how they are handled using `try-catch` and `throws` with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (2 Marks):** Checked exceptions are exceptions checked by the compiler at compile-time. They represent recoverable conditions external to the program (file systems, databases, networks). If not handled, the program will not compile.

**try-catch Handling (3 Marks):**

```java
import java.io.*;
public class FileReadDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            FileReader fr = new FileReader("input.txt");
            int c = fr.read();
            System.out.println((char) c);
            fr.close();
        } catch (FileNotFoundException e) {
            System.out.println("File not found: " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (IOException e) {
            System.out.println("I/O Error: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**throws Declaration (2 Marks):**

```java
import java.io.*;
public class FileLoader {
    public void readFile(String path) throws IOException {
        FileReader fr = new FileReader(path);
        fr.read();
        fr.close();
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException {
        new FileLoader().readFile("input.txt");
    }
}
```

---

**Q. A (b)** Write a Java program to create a custom checked exception class `InvalidAgeException` and demonstrate its use in validating voter registration age (age must be ≥ 18). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Custom Exception Class (2 Marks):**

```java
// Custom checked exception — extends Exception, NOT RuntimeException
public class InvalidAgeException extends Exception {
    private int age;

    public InvalidAgeException(int age) {
        super("Invalid age: " + age + ". Voter must be at least 18 years old.");
        this.age = age;
    }

    public int getAge() { return age; }
}
```

**Step 2 — Voter Class (2 Marks):**

```java
public class Voter {
    public void register(int age) throws InvalidAgeException {
        if (age < 18) {
            throw new InvalidAgeException(age);
        }
        System.out.println("Voter registered successfully at age " + age);
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — Application (2 Marks):**

```java
public class VoterApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Voter voter = new Voter();
        try {
            voter.register(15);   // Will throw InvalidAgeException
        } catch (InvalidAgeException e) {
            System.out.println("Registration Failed: " + e.getMessage());
            System.out.println("Provided Age: " + e.getAge());
        }
        System.out.println("Program continues safely...");
    }
}
```

**Step 4 — Expected Output (1 Mark):**
```
Registration Failed: Invalid age: 15. Voter must be at least 18 years old.
Provided Age: 15
Program continues safely...
```

---

### 📗 QUESTION B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Retest Pattern]`

**Q. B (a)** Explain the Java exception class hierarchy with a neat diagram. Why are checked exceptions checked at compile-time? Justify with an example. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Hierarchy Explanation (3 Marks):**

```
java.lang.Throwable
        |
        +-- java.lang.Exception  (Checked)
        |       |-- IOException
        |       |-- SQLException
        |       |-- ClassNotFoundException
        |       |-- ParseException
        |       |
        |       +-- java.lang.RuntimeException (Unchecked)
        |               |-- ArithmeticException
        |               |-- NullPointerException
        |               |-- IndexOutOfBoundsException
        |
        +-- java.lang.Error
                |-- OutOfMemoryError
                |-- StackOverflowError
```

**Why Compile-Time Checking? (2 Marks):**

Compile-time checking exists because checked exceptions represent **external conditions** that a robust application must anticipate — failing to compile ensures that developers handle these conditions explicitly *before* deployment, reducing production crashes.

**Justification Example (2 Marks):**

```java
import java.io.FileReader;

public class JustifyDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        FileReader r = new FileReader("data.txt");   // COMPILE ERROR
    }
}
```

The above code produces the compile-time error:
```
unhandled exception: java.io.FileNotFoundException
```

This forces the programmer to add a `try-catch` or `throws` declaration, ensuring the application handles the missing file gracefully.

---

**Q. B (b)** Differentiate between `throw` and `throws` keywords in Java. Write a program that demonstrates both keywords using checked exception handling for a banking withdrawal scenario. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Difference Table (3 Marks):**

| Feature | `throw` | `throws` |
|---------|---------|----------|
| Purpose | Used to **explicitly throw** an exception | Used to **declare** exceptions a method may throw |
| Location | Inside method body | In method signature |
| Number of Exceptions | Throws **one** exception at a time | Can declare **multiple** exceptions |
| Syntax | `throw new Exception()` | `void method() throws Exception` |

**Program Demonstration (4 Marks):**

```java
// Custom checked exception
class LowBalanceException extends Exception {
    public LowBalanceException(String msg) { super(msg); }
}

// Bank class using throws in signature
class Bank {
    private double balance = 1000;

    public void withdraw(double amount) throws LowBalanceException { // throws
        if (amount > balance) {
            throw new LowBalanceException("Balance too low! Available: " + balance); // throw
        }
        balance -= amount;
        System.out.println("Withdrawal successful. Remaining balance: " + balance);
    }
}

public class BankApp {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Bank b = new Bank();
        try {
            b.withdraw(5000);   // Will throw
        } catch (LowBalanceException e) {
            System.out.println("Transaction Failed: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Transaction Failed: Balance too low! Available: 1000.0
```

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Confusing `throw` and `throws`** — `throw` is a *statement* used inside method body; `throws` is a *declaration* in method signature. Examiners strictly check this distinction.
> 2. **Extending `RuntimeException` instead of `Exception`** when creating custom checked exceptions — this silently turns it into an unchecked exception, losing the compile-time enforcement (lose 2-3 marks).
> 3. **Catching parent class before child class** — causes unreachable catch block compile error.
> 4. **Forgetting `throws` clause** on overridden methods when parent throws a checked exception (violates the checked exception contract rule from inheritance).
> 5. **Empty catch blocks** — always provide a meaningful message using `e.getMessage()` or `e.printStackTrace()`.

---

### 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ Checked exceptions are **compile-time verified** by the Java compiler.
- ✅ They extend `java.lang.Exception` **directly** (NOT `RuntimeException`).
- ✅ They MUST be either **caught** (`try-catch`) or **declared** (`throws`).
- ✅ Common examples: `IOException`, `FileNotFoundException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException`, `ParseException`, `InterruptedException`.
- ✅ The compiler enforces this rule only for checked exceptions — unchecked exceptions and errors are exempt.
- ✅ The `throws` keyword can declare **multiple** exceptions separated by commas.
- ✅ A subclass overriding a method **cannot throw broader or new checked exceptions** than the parent.
- ✅ The `finally` block **always executes**, even when an exception is thrown or caught.
- ✅ Use **try-with-resources** (Java 7+) for automatic resource closure with `AutoCloseable` objects.
- ✅ Custom checked exceptions must extend `Exception` (not `RuntimeException`) to remain checked.
- ✅ The `throw` keyword is followed by an **instance**; `throws` is followed by a **class name**.
- ✅ Catch blocks must be ordered from **most specific** (subclass) to **most general** (parent class).
- ✅ KTU loves comparison questions: *checked vs unchecked*, *throw vs throws*, *final vs finally vs finalize*.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Unchecked Exceptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Unchecked Exceptions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Java programming language, **Unchecked Exceptions** are a specific category of exceptions that are *not verified by the Java compiler at compile-time*. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PBEOT404, Module 3 — Exception Handling), unchecked exceptions are formally defined as:

> *All exceptions that are direct or indirect subclasses of `java.lang.RuntimeException` are classified as Unchecked Exceptions. The compiler does not mandate that they be either caught (using a `try-catch` block) or declared (using the `throws` keyword) in the method signature.*

These exceptions arise during the **runtime phase** of a program, typically as a consequence of **programming logic errors, invalid input data, or API misuse**, rather than external environmental conditions. They signal flaws in the application's internal logic that ideally should have been prevented by the programmer through defensive coding techniques and input validation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Unchecked exceptions are formally documented in the Java Language Specification (JLS, Section 11.2). They contrast with **Checked Exceptions** (subclasses of `Exception` other than `RuntimeException`), which the compiler *does* enforce handling for.

---

## 1.2 The Java Exception Class Hierarchy (Top-Level View)

The position of unchecked exceptions in the broader Java Throwable hierarchy is critical for examination purposes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{java.lang.Throwable} \\
\quad \hookrightarrow \text{java.lang.Error} \quad \text{(Unrecoverable, unchecked)} \\
\quad \hookrightarrow \text{java.lang.Exception} \\
\quad \quad \hookrightarrow \text{Checked Exceptions (e.g., IOException, SQLException)} \\
\quad \quad \hookrightarrow \text{java.lang.RuntimeException} \\
\quad \quad \quad \hookrightarrow \text{Unchecked Exceptions (e.g., ArithmeticException, NullPointerException)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The pivotal class is **`java.lang.RuntimeException`**. Every exception class that inherits directly or transitively from `RuntimeException` is, by Java's language definition, an **unchecked exception**.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Speed Bump" vs. "Open Manhole" Metaphor**

Imagine a city road. There are two kinds of hazards:

1.  **Speed Bumps (Checked Exceptions):** The city council (Java compiler) has placed clearly painted, bright yellow speed bumps at known risky intersections. The city *requires* every car (method) to either slow down (catch the exception) or display a sign saying "This road has bumps" (`throws` declaration). You cannot ignore them.

2.  **Open Manholes (Unchecked Exceptions):** These are unmarked, hidden dangers in the road — a manhole left open by a careless construction worker, or a wrong turn into a one-way street. No prior warning sign exists. You only discover them when you actually drive over them (at runtime). The city does not pre-mark every single possible mistake; instead, it expects you, the *driver (programmer)*, to drive carefully, look at the road (validate inputs), and use the GPS (defensive coding).

**Unchecked exceptions = Open Manholes.** They represent **programmer mistakes** that the compiler cannot foresee, and they are caught only when the program is *actually executing* and encounters the flawed logic.

---

## 1.4 Key Characteristics (Bulleted Summary)

- **Compile-Time Transparency:** The Java compiler (`javac`) performs no syntactic verification — the code compiles cleanly even without any `try-catch` or `throws` clause.
- **Runtime Detection:** The Java Virtual Machine (JVM) throws the exception object only when the offending line is executed.
- **Indicate Programming Bugs:** They almost always signal a logic flaw (e.g., dividing by zero, accessing `null`, indexing an array out of bounds).
- **Optional Handling:** Handling is at the discretion of the programmer, not a compiler mandate.
- **Unrecoverable in Nature:** They typically cannot be meaningfully recovered from in a production environment; the program usually terminates with a stack trace.
- **Inherited from `RuntimeException`:** This is the *defining* technical criterion for classification.
- **Common Package:** Most are defined in `java.lang` and are available without imports.

---

## 1.5 Standard Metrics & Properties

| Property | Value/Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Defining Superclass** | `java.lang.RuntimeException` |
| **Grandparent Class** | `java.lang.Exception` |
| **Root Class** | `java.lang.Throwable` |
| **Compile-time check** | **None** (skipped by `javac`) |
| **Default Handler** | JVM default exception handler (prints stack trace, terminates) |
| **Recommended handling** | Fix the root cause, or use defensive validation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Operational Logic — Why and How

To fully understand unchecked exceptions, we must examine the **"Why"** (language design rationale) and **"How"** (compiler and JVM behavior) in detail.

### 2.1.1 The "Why" — Language Design Rationale

Java's original designers (Gosling, Joy, Steele, et al.) introduced the checked/unchecked dichotomy to enforce a balance between **safety** and **developer freedom**:

-   **Checked exceptions** were designed for *recoverable*, *external* conditions (file not found, network failure) where the API consumer is *forced* to acknowledge them.
-   **Unchecked exceptions** were designed for *programming errors* that are, in principle, *preventable* by the developer. The compiler stays out of the way because:
    1.  Forcing `try-catch` around every potential `NullPointerException` would clutter code unacceptably.
    2.  The vast majority of runtime exceptions stem from bugs that must be *fixed at the source*, not handled at the call site.
    3.  Pre-checking every array index and divisor at runtime would impose a severe performance penalty.

### 2.1.2 The "How" — Compiler and JVM Mechanics

The operational flow can be summarized in five steps:

1.  **Compilation Phase (`javac`):** The compiler parses the source code and verifies syntax. For methods that may throw checked exceptions, it checks whether the exception is either caught or declared. For unchecked exceptions (subclasses of `RuntimeException`), this check is **bypassed entirely**. The `.class` file is generated.
2.  **Class Loading Phase:** The `.class` file is loaded into the JVM by the class loader. No exception handling verification occurs here for unchecked types.
3.  **Execution Phase (Bytecode Interpretation):** When a specific bytecode instruction executes (e.g., `idiv`, `aaload`, `getfield`), the JVM hardware-emulated environment monitors for *exceptional conditions* in the operand stack.
4.  **Exception Instantiation:** Upon detecting a fault (e.g., divisor is zero), the JVM creates a new instance of the corresponding exception class (e.g., `new java.lang.ArithmeticException("/ by zero")`), populates its stack trace, and performs an **implicit `athrow`**.
5.  **Propagation:** If the current method does not contain a matching `catch` block, the exception object propagates up the call stack (stack unwinding). If no method catches it, the JVM's default uncaught exception handler activates, prints the stack trace to `System.err`, and terminates the thread.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Classification Cheat Sheet

The following table contains the **canonical taxonomy** of unchecked exceptions that KTU examiners expect students to recognize and explain. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` for textual "or" to preserve table integrity.

| Exception Class | Trigger Condition | Common Scenario |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `ArithmeticException` | Integer division by zero (`/` or `%`) | `int x = 10 / 0;` |
| `NullPointerException` | Accessing a method/field on a `null` reference | `String s = null; s.length();` |
| `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | Array index $\lt 0$ or $\geq$ length | `arr[10]` on a 5-element array |
| `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` | Invalid index in `String.charAt()` | `str.charAt(100)` |
| `IndexOutOfBoundsException` | Parent class for index errors in lists | `list.get(99)` on a small list |
| `NumberFormatException` | Invalid string-to-number conversion | `Integer.parseInt("abc")` |
| `ClassCastException` | Invalid type cast of an object reference | `(Integer) obj` where `obj` is a `String` |
| `IllegalArgumentException` | Method receives an argument outside valid range | `Thread.sleep(-100)` |
| `IllegalStateException` | Method invoked at an illegal time/state | Using an iterator after `remove()` |
| `UnsupportedOperationException` | Operation not supported by the collection | Modifying an immutable list |
| `SecurityException` | Security manager denies an operation | Disallowed file access |
| `ConcurrentModificationException` | Collection modified while iterating | Modifying an `ArrayList` in a `for-each` loop |

---

## 2.3 Decision Matrix: Checked vs. Unchecked (High-Yield)

A frequent KTU question asks students to distinguish checked from unchecked exceptions. The decision matrix below encapsulates the precise rules:

| Criterion | Checked Exception | Unchecked Exception |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Superclass** | Direct subclass of `Exception` (not `RuntimeException`) | Direct or indirect subclass of `RuntimeException` |
| **Compiler Check** | Mandatory (catch or declare) | None |
| **Typical Cause** | External resources (I/O, DB, network) | Programming logic errors |
| **Recoverable?** | Often yes (e.g., retry network call) | Usually no (fix the bug) |
| **Examples** | `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException` | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException` |
| **Best Practice** | Handle at appropriate layer | Prevent via validation and defensive coding |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In professional software engineering (especially enterprise Java systems built with Spring, Jakarta EE, or Android), unchecked exceptions form the backbone of **fail-fast development philosophies**:

-   **Defensive Programming:** Programmers wrap critical inputs with `Objects.requireNonNull()` and explicit `if-else` checks precisely to *prevent* unchecked exceptions from reaching end-users.
-   **Assertion of Invariants:** Custom unchecked exceptions (e.g., extending `IllegalStateException`) are thrown to signal that a method's preconditions have been violated, surfacing bugs early.
-   **Global Error Handling:** Frameworks like Spring Boot use `@ControllerAdvice` and `@ExceptionHandler` to centrally catch unchecked exceptions and translate them into meaningful HTTP status codes (e.g., `NullPointerException` → 500 Internal Server Error).
-   **Logging and Observability:** Tools like Sentry, Datadog, and ELK Stack track unchecked exceptions in production to alert developers of latent bugs in deployed code.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Code Walkthroughs

## 3.1 The Classification Algorithm — Is an Exception Checked or Unchecked?

A KTU-favorite question is: *"Given an exception class name, determine whether it is checked or unchecked."* The algorithm below provides a deterministic, step-by-step derivation that examiners reward highly.

### 3.1.1 The Decision Algorithm (Written Out)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Let } C \text{ be the candidate exception class.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Check if } C \text{ is a subclass of } \texttt{java.lang.Throwable}. \\
& \text{If NO} \rightarrow \text{It is not an exception; classification terminates.} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Check if } C \text{ is a subclass of } \texttt{java.lang.Error}. \\
& \text{If YES} \rightarrow \text{It is an Error; not an Exception (orthogonal category).} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Check if } C \text{ is a subclass of } \texttt{java.lang.RuntimeException}. \\
& \text{If YES} \rightarrow \textbf{Unchecked Exception}. \quad \text{Stop.} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Check if } C \text{ is a subclass of } \texttt{java.lang.Exception}. \\
& \text{If YES (and not RuntimeException)} \rightarrow \textbf{Checked Exception}. \quad \text{Stop.} \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{If none of the above match, consult JLS §11.2; default classification: Unchecked.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.2 Worked Example: Classifying `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad & C = \texttt{java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException} \\
\text{Step 1:} \quad & C \text{ is a public class in } \texttt{java.lang} \text{ package.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & C \text{ extends } \texttt{java.lang.IndexOutOfBoundsException}. \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \texttt{IndexOutOfBoundsException} \text{ extends } \texttt{java.lang.RuntimeException}. \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \texttt{RuntimeException} \text{ extends } \texttt{java.lang.Exception}. \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \texttt{Exception} \text{ extends } \texttt{java.lang.Throwable}. \\
\text{Conclusion:} \quad & C \text{ is a transitive subclass of } \texttt{RuntimeException}. \\
& \therefore C \text{ is an } \textbf{Unchecked Exception}. \quad \blacksquare
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Code → Exception Mapping

The matrix below demonstrates concrete Java code snippets and the exact unchecked exception each one triggers. This is the *single most testable* form of knowledge for KTU Module 3.

### 3.2.1 ArithmeticException Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Source Code:} \quad & \texttt{int numerator = 10;} \\
& \texttt{int denominator = 0;} \\
& \texttt{int result = numerator / denominator;} \quad \text{(Bytecode: \texttt{idiv})} \\
\text{JVM Action:} \quad & \text{During execution of the \texttt{idiv} instruction,} \\
& \text{the JVM detects that the divisor operand on the} \\
& \text{stack is 0.} \\
\text{Result:} \quad & \text{JVM instantiates } \texttt{ArithmeticException("/ by zero")} \\
& \text{and performs an implicit \texttt{athrow} opcode.} \\
\text{Output:} \quad & \texttt{Exception in thread "main" java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Crucial Detail:** `ArithmeticException` is thrown *only* for integer division. Floating-point division by zero (e.g., `10.0 / 0`) yields `Infinity` or `NaN` in IEEE 754 arithmetic and does **not** throw an exception.

### 3.2.2 NullPointerException Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Source Code:} \quad & \texttt{String name = null;} \\
& \texttt{int length = name.length();} \\
\text{JVM Action:} \quad & \text{The \texttt{invokevirtual} bytecode for \texttt{length()}} \\
& \text{verifies that the objectref on the stack is non-null.} \\
& \text{It finds the objectref is \texttt{null}.} \\
\text{Result:} \quad & \text{JVM instantiates } \texttt{NullPointerException} \\
& \text{and throws it at the offending \texttt{invokevirtual} instruction.} \\
\text{Output:} \quad & \texttt{Exception in thread "main" java.lang.NullPointerException} \\
& \texttt{at MyClass.main(MyClass.java:5)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2.3 ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Source Code:} \quad & \texttt{int[] scores = new int[3];} \quad \text{(valid indices: 0, 1, 2)} \\
& \texttt{int value = scores[5];} \\
\text{JVM Action:} \quad & \text{The \texttt{iaload} bytecode checks if the index} \\
& \text{(5) is } \geq \text{ arraylength (3).} \\
& \text{The check fails.} \\
\text{Result:} \quad & \text{JVM instantiates } \texttt{ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException(5)} \\
& \text{and performs an \texttt{athrow}.} \\
\text{Output:} \quad & \texttt{Exception in thread "main" java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: Index 5 out of bounds for length 3}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Java Program — Demonstrating Multiple Unchecked Exceptions

Below is a complete, compilable Java program that demonstrates the runtime behavior of several unchecked exceptions. Each segment is annotated with the exact line number where the exception originates.

```java
public class UncheckedDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {

        // ---- Case 1: ArithmeticException ----
        try {
            int a = 10;
            int b = 0;
            int c = a / b;   // Line 7: triggers ArithmeticException
            System.out.println("Result: " + c);
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught ArithmeticException: " + e.getMessage());
        }

        // ---- Case 2: NullPointerException ----
        try {
            String text = null;
            int len = text.length();   // Line 16: triggers NullPointerException
            System.out.println("Length: " + len);
        } catch (NullPointerException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught NullPointerException");
        }

        // ---- Case 3: ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException ----
        try {
            int[] arr = {1, 2, 3};
            int x = arr[10];   // Line 24: triggers ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
            System.out.println("Element: " + x);
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: " + e.getMessage());
        }

        // ---- Case 4: NumberFormatException ----
        try {
            int n = Integer.parseInt("Hello123");   // Line 31
            System.out.println("Parsed: " + n);
        } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught NumberFormatException");
        }

        // ---- Case 5: ClassCastException ----
        try {
            Object obj = "I am a String";
            Integer num = (Integer) obj;   // Line 38
            System.out.println("Casted: " + num);
        } catch (ClassCastException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught ClassCastException: " + e.getMessage());
        }

        System.out.println("Program continued after all catches — no abnormal termination.");
    }
}
```

### 3.3.1 Expected Output Trace

```
Caught ArithmeticException: / by zero
Caught NullPointerException
Caught ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: Index 10 out of bounds for length 3
Caught NumberFormatException
Caught ClassCastException: Cannot cast String to Integer
Program continued after all catches — no abnormal termination.
```

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Explanation of the Trace

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Iteration 1:} \quad & \text{JVM executes line 7. The \texttt{idiv} opcode detects zero divisor.} \\
& \text{An \texttt{ArithmeticException} is thrown. The \texttt{catch} block at line 8} \\
& \text{intercepts it, prints the message, and control flows to line 14.} \\
\text{Iteration 2:} \quad & \text{At line 16, the \texttt{invokevirtual} opcode for \texttt{length()}} \\
& \text{detects a \texttt{null} objectref. A \texttt{NullPointerException} is thrown,} \\
& \text{caught at line 17, and handled gracefully.} \\
\text{Iteration 3-5:} \quad & \text{Same mechanism: each \texttt{try} block's offending line triggers its} \\
& \text{corresponding unchecked exception, which is caught by the matching} \\
& \text{\texttt{catch} clause, allowing the program to continue normally.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** This program demonstrates the *optional* nature of catching unchecked exceptions. The code would still compile and run even if all five `try-catch` blocks were removed — but it would terminate abruptly at the first exception. This is precisely the "unchecked" behavior the compiler does not enforce.

---

## 3.4 Custom Unchecked Exception — Engineering Pattern

In real-world Java projects, developers often create their own unchecked exceptions to signal domain-specific errors. The derivation of a custom class is as follows:

```java
// Step 1: Define the custom unchecked exception
public class InsufficientBalanceException extends RuntimeException {
    public InsufficientBalanceException(String message) {
        super(message);
    }
}

// Step 2: Use it in a banking context
class BankAccount {
    private double balance = 500.0;

    public void withdraw(double amount) {
        if (amount > balance) {
            throw new InsufficientBalanceException(
                "Attempted to withdraw " + amount + " but balance is only " + balance
            );
        }
        balance -= amount;
        System.out.println("Withdrawal successful. New balance: " + balance);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        BankAccount acc = new BankAccount();
        acc.withdraw(1000.0);  // No try-catch required — compiles cleanly
    }
}
```

### 3.4.1 Why Extend `RuntimeException`?

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Design Decision:} \quad & \text{By extending } \texttt{RuntimeException}, \text{ our custom class} \\
& \texttt{InsufficientBalanceException} \text{ becomes unchecked.} \\
\text{Consequence:} \quad & \text{Methods like } \texttt{withdraw()} \text{ are NOT required to declare} \\
& \text{the exception in a \texttt{throws} clause.} \\
\text{Consequence:} \quad & \text{Callers of } \texttt{withdraw()} \text{ are NOT forced to wrap the call} \\
& \text{in a \texttt{try-catch} block.} \\
\text{Trade-off:} \quad & \text{Greater API flexibility, but call sites must be aware} \\
& \text{of the possibility of failure through documentation.}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 4.2 Runtime Exception Propagation — Flow Block Diagram

The diagram below illustrates how an unhandled unchecked exception propagates up the JVM call stack until either a matching `catch` block is found or the JVM default handler terminates the thread.

```mermaid
graph TD
    mainA["main method"]
    methodA["methodA calls methodB"]
    methodB["methodB calls methodC"]
    methodC["methodC triggers ArithmeticException"]
    throwA["JVM creates exception object and performs athrow"]
    searchA["Unwind call stack frame by frame"]
    catchA["Matching catch block found in methodA"]
    handledA["Handle exception and continue execution"]
    defaultA["No catch found. JVM default handler activates"]
    terminateA["Print stack trace to System.err and terminate thread"]

    mainA --> methodA
    methodA --> methodB
    methodB --> methodC
    methodC --> throwA
    throwA --> searchA
    searchA --> catchA
    catchA --> handledA
    searchA --> defaultA
    defaultA --> terminateA

    style mainA fill:#4682b4,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style methodA fill:#4682b4,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style methodB fill:#4682b4,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style methodC fill:#dc143c,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style throwA fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style searchA fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style catchA fill:#32cd32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style handledA fill:#32cd32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style defaultA fill:#8b0000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style terminateA fill:#8b0000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Try-Catch-Finally Architecture for Unchecked Exceptions

The following functional block diagram maps the architectural flow of a `try-catch-finally` construct when handling an unchecked exception. This is a common diagram-based question in KTU exams.

```mermaid
graph TD
    entryA["ENTRY: try block begins execution"]
    bodyA["Execute statements inside try"]
    checkA["Did an unchecked exception occur?"]
    catchA["Locate matching catch block"]
    handlerA["Execute catch block code"]
    finallyA["Execute finally block ALWAYS"]
    normalA["Continue with rest of method"]
    skipA["Skip remaining catch blocks"]
    exitA["Method returns or terminates"]

    entryA --> bodyA
    bodyA --> checkA
    checkA -->|No Exception| normalA
    checkA -->|Exception Thrown| catchA
    catchA -->|Match Found| handlerA
    catchA -->|No Match| skipA
    handlerA --> finallyA
    normalA --> finallyA
    skipA --> finallyA
    finallyA --> exitA

    style entryA fill:#4682b4,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style bodyA fill:#4682b4,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style checkA fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style catchA fill:#ff8c00,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style handlerA fill:#32cd32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style finallyA fill:#9932cc,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style normalA fill:#32cd32,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style skipA fill:#8b0000,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    style exitA fill:#696969,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The `finally` block executes **regardless** of whether an unchecked exception was thrown or not. This is critical for resource cleanup (closing files, releasing database connections).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Question)]**
**Define unchecked exceptions in Java. How are they different from checked exceptions?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Unchecked exceptions in Java are exceptions that the compiler does not verify at compile-time. They are direct or indirect subclasses of `java.lang.RuntimeException`. The compiler does not force the programmer to either catch them using a `try-catch` block or declare them in a method's `throws` clause. They typically occur at runtime due to programming logic errors such as dividing by zero, accessing a `null` reference, or indexing an array beyond its bounds. In contrast, checked exceptions are subclasses of `Exception` (but not `RuntimeException`) and are verified by the compiler, which mandates that they be either caught or declared. Examples of unchecked exceptions include `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, and `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, while checked exceptions include `IOException` and `SQLException`.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Question)]**
**List any five common unchecked exceptions in Java and state one scenario that triggers each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Tabular Format Expected):**

| Sl. No. | Unchecked Exception | Triggering Scenario |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `ArithmeticException` | Performing integer division by zero, e.g., `int x = 10 / 0;` |
| 2 | `NullPointerException` | Invoking a method on a `null` reference, e.g., `s.length()` where `s == null` |
| 3 | `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | Accessing an array at an invalid index, e.g., `arr[10]` on a 3-element array |
| 4 | `NumberFormatException` | Converting a non-numeric string to a number, e.g., `Integer.parseInt("abc")` |
| 5 | `ClassCastException` | Casting an object to an incompatible type, e.g., `(Integer) "Hello"` |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**
**(a)** Explain the Java exception class hierarchy with a neat diagram. Distinguish clearly between checked and unchecked exceptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that demonstrates the occurrence of at least four different unchecked exceptions and handles each one using a separate `catch` block. Provide the expected output. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

##### Part (a) — Java Exception Class Hierarchy

The Java exception class hierarchy is rooted at `java.lang.Throwable`, which has two direct subclasses:

1.  **`java.lang.Error`**: Represents serious, unrecoverable conditions (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`) that an application should not attempt to catch. These are unchecked.

2.  **`java.lang.Exception`**: Represents conditions that a reasonable application might want to catch. It has two main branches:

    -   **Checked Exceptions**: Direct subclasses of `Exception` other than `RuntimeException`. The compiler enforces handling. Examples: `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException`.

    -   **Unchecked Exceptions**: Subclasses of `RuntimeException`. The compiler does not enforce handling. Examples: `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, `NumberFormatException`, `ClassCastException`.

**Key Differences Tabulated:**

| Aspect | Checked Exception | Unchecked Exception |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Superclass | `Exception` (not `RuntimeException`) | `RuntimeException` (direct or indirect) |
| Compile-time check | Yes (catch or declare) | No |
| Typical cause | External resources, I/O | Programming logic errors |
| Recoverability | Often recoverable | Usually not recoverable |
| Example | `IOException` | `ArithmeticException` |

*(Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1 for the visual hierarchy.)*

---

##### Part (b) — Java Program Demonstrating Four Unchecked Exceptions

```java
public class UncheckedExceptionDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {

        // 1. ArithmeticException
        try {
            int a = 50, b = 0;
            int c = a / b;
            System.out.println(c);
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("1. Caught ArithmeticException: " + e.getMessage());
        }

        // 2. NullPointerException
        try {
            String str = null;
            System.out.println(str.toUpperCase());
        } catch (NullPointerException e) {
            System.out.println("2. Caught NullPointerException: Cannot invoke method on null");
        }

        // 3. ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
        try {
            int[] numbers = {10, 20, 30};
            System.out.println(numbers[5]);
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("3. Caught ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: " + e.getMessage());
        }

        // 4. NumberFormatException
        try {
            int n = Integer.parseInt("NotANumber");
            System.out.println(n);
        } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
            System.out.println("4. Caught NumberFormatException: Invalid input format");
        }

        System.out.println("Program completed normally.");
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**

```
1. Caught ArithmeticException: / by zero
2. Caught NullPointerException: Cannot invoke method on null
3. Caught ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: Index 5 out of bounds for length 3
4. Caught NumberFormatException: Invalid input format
Program completed normally.
```

**Explanation:** Each `try` block contains code that triggers a specific unchecked exception. The corresponding `catch` block intercepts the exception, prints a custom message, and allows the program to continue. The `finally` concept is not required here but could be added for completeness.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3 Alternative]**
**(a)** What is an unchecked exception? Explain with examples how the Java Virtual Machine handles an uncaught unchecked exception during program execution. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to create a custom unchecked exception class called `InvalidAgeException`. Demonstrate its use in a method that validates the age of a voter. Show that the compiler does not require the caller to handle this exception. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

##### Part (a) — Unchecked Exceptions and JVM Handling

An **unchecked exception** is an exception belonging to the `java.lang.RuntimeException` hierarchy. The compiler performs no verification, and such exceptions surface only at runtime. Common examples include `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, and `ClassCastException`.

**JVM Handling Mechanism for an Uncaught Unchecked Exception:**

1.  **Detection:** The JVM monitors bytecode instructions during execution. For example, the `idiv` instruction checks for a zero divisor; `invokevirtual` checks for a non-null objectref.

2.  **Instantiation:** Upon detecting a fault, the JVM creates a new instance of the relevant exception class (e.g., `new ArithmeticException("/ by zero")`) and populates its stack trace by capturing the current call stack.

3.  **Throwing:** The JVM performs an implicit `athrow` bytecode operation, which initiates stack unwinding.

4.  **Stack Unwinding:** The exception object propagates upward through the call stack. The JVM pops each method's stack frame, checking for a matching `catch` block.

5.  **Default Handler:** If no `catch` block matches any frame in the thread's call stack, control reaches the JVM's default uncaught exception handler, which prints the stack trace to `System.err` and terminates the thread abnormally.

**Example:** In `int x = 10 / 0;`, when `idiv` executes with a zero divisor, the JVM throws `ArithmeticException`. If uncaught, the output is:
```
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero
    at MyClass.main(MyClass.java:3)
```

---

##### Part (b) — Custom Unchecked Exception: `InvalidAgeException`

```java
// Step 1: Define the custom unchecked exception
class InvalidAgeException extends RuntimeException {
    public InvalidAgeException(String message) {
        super(message);
    }
}

// Step 2: Use it in a voter registration context
public class VoterValidator {
    public static void registerVoter(String name, int age) {
        if (age < 18) {
            throw new InvalidAgeException(
                "Voter " + name + " is underage. Age " + age + " is below the legal voting age of 18."
            );
        }
        System.out.println("Voter " + name + " registered successfully.");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Call without try-catch — compiles cleanly because the exception is unchecked
        registerVoter("Arjun", 25);   // Valid: registers successfully
        registerVoter("Kiran", 15);   // Invalid: throws InvalidAgeException at runtime
        System.out.println("End of program");
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**

```
Voter Arjun registered successfully.
Exception in thread "main" InvalidAgeException: Voter Kiran is underage. Age 15 is below the legal voting age of 18.
    at VoterValidator.registerVoter(VoterValidator.java:5)
    at VoterValidator.main(VoterValidator.java:17)
```

**Explanation of the Compiler Behavior:**

-   The `registerVoter` method throws `InvalidAgeException` but does **not** declare it in a `throws` clause.
-   The `main` method calls `registerVoter` without any `try-catch` block.
-   Despite this, the program **compiles successfully** because `InvalidAgeException` extends `RuntimeException`, making it an unchecked exception.
-   At runtime, when `age = 15` is passed, the exception is thrown and propagates up to the JVM default handler, which prints the stack trace and terminates the thread.

This demonstrates the core principle: **the compiler never forces handling of unchecked exceptions**, even for custom user-defined ones.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls — Read Carefully Before Your Exam:**

1.  **Confusing `Error` with `Exception` (–2 Marks typical):** `Error` and `Exception` are both subclasses of `Throwable`, but they are *separate categories*. Do not list `OutOfMemoryError` as an unchecked *exception*; it is an *error*. Say "unchecked throwable" if you want to be safe, but be precise.

2.  **Saying "RuntimeException" instead of "subclass of RuntimeException" (–1 Mark):** The technically correct statement is: *"An unchecked exception is any class that is a direct or indirect subclass of `java.lang.RuntimeException`."* `RuntimeException` itself is technically not an unchecked exception — it is the *parent class* of all unchecked exceptions.

3.  **Forgetting the `try-catch` block in code questions (–2 to –3 Marks):** When asked to *demonstrate* an unchecked exception, students often write only the offending line without any `try-catch` or explanation. Examiners want to see the *complete program flow* — including how the exception is thrown, caught, and handled.

4.  **Claiming `ArithmeticException` is thrown for `10.0 / 0` (–2 Marks):** This is a classic trap question. Floating-point division by zero in Java follows IEEE 754 rules and produces `Infinity` or `NaN`, not an exception. Only *integer* division by zero throws `ArithmeticException`.

5.  **Forgetting the `throws` clause requirement distinction (–3 Marks):** When the question asks "Why does the compiler not require a `throws` clause for `NullPointerException`?", the answer must explicitly mention that it extends `RuntimeException`, which is unchecked. A vague answer like "because it's a runtime error" is insufficient.

6.  **Not using the `finally` block when required (–2 Marks):** If the question asks to demonstrate *resource cleanup*, you must include a `finally` block. Examiners specifically test whether students understand that `finally` runs even when an unchecked exception occurs.

7.  **Mixing up `NumberFormatException` with `ParseException` (–1 Mark):** `NumberFormatException` is a subclass of `IllegalArgumentException` and is *unchecked*. `ParseException` (from `java.text`) is a checked exception. Do not confuse them.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Pin This Before the Exam:**

-   ✅ **Defining Criterion:** Unchecked exceptions = direct or indirect subclasses of `java.lang.RuntimeException`.
-   ✅ **Compiler Behavior:** `javac` performs **no verification** of unchecked exceptions — no `try-catch` or `throws` mandate.
-   ✅ **JVM Behavior:** The JVM detects, instantiates, and throws the exception object at runtime via implicit `athrow`.
-   ✅ **Root Hierarchy:** `Throwable` → `Exception` → `RuntimeException` → [specific unchecked classes].
-   ✅ **Top 10 Unchecked Exceptions to Memorize:** `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException`, `IndexOutOfBoundsException`, `NumberFormatException`, `ClassCastException`, `IllegalArgumentException`, `IllegalStateException`, `UnsupportedOperationException`.
-   ✅ **Errors are NOT Exceptions:** `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError` extend `Error`, not `Exception`. They are technically unchecked but are classified separately.
-   ✅ **Floating-Point Division by Zero Does NOT Throw:** Only integer division by zero triggers `ArithmeticException`. `10.0 / 0` returns `Infinity`.
-   ✅ **Custom Unchecked Exceptions:** To create one, extend `RuntimeException` (not `Exception` directly).
-   ✅ **`try-catch-finally` is Optional for Unchecked:** You *can* catch them, but the compiler does not *force* you to.
-   ✅ **`finally` Always Executes:** Whether or not an unchecked exception is thrown, the `finally` block runs.
-   ✅ **Best Practice:** Prefer *prevention* (input validation, `Objects.requireNonNull`, range checks) over *handling* unchecked exceptions. They signal bugs — fix the bug, not the symptom.
-   ✅ **Killer Distinction:** Checked = "the external world failed me" (handle it). Unchecked = "I, the programmer, made a mistake" (fix the code).
-   ✅ **One-Line Exam Answer:** *"Unchecked exceptions in Java are runtime exceptions that are subclasses of `java.lang.RuntimeException` and are not checked at compile time."*

---

**End of Module 3 — Unchecked Exceptions Notes**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: try Block and catch Clause
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** try Block and catch Clause

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Java programming language (as prescribed by the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PBEOT404), **exception handling** is a robust mechanism that allows a program to detect, intercept, and recover from runtime anomalies (called *exceptions*) without abruptly terminating the JVM execution thread. The two foundational pillars of this mechanism are the **`try` block** and the **`catch` clause**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Standard):**
> - **try block:** A *guarded region* of code, demarcated by the `try` keyword, within which an exception (a runtime disruption) may potentially be thrown. If an exception is thrown anywhere inside this block, normal sequential execution is abandoned, and control is transferred to the matching `catch` handler.
> - **catch clause:** A *parameterized exception handler* introduced by the `catch` keyword. It catches (i.e., accepts) an exception object thrown from its associated `try` block whose type matches the catch parameter's declared type (or is a subtype of it).

**Generalized Syntax (Java SE Specification):**
```java
try {
    // Guarded / Monitored Code Block
    // Statements that may throw an exception
} catch (ExceptionType1 identifier1) {
    // Recovery / Reporting logic for ExceptionType1
} catch (ExceptionType2 identifier2) {
    // Recovery / Reporting logic for ExceptionType2
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology for KTU Board Exams:**
> - **Guarded Region:** The body of the `try` block.
> - **Exception Object:** An instance of `java.lang.Throwable` (or its subclass) that carries information (type, message, stack trace) about the abnormal condition.
> - **Handler / Recovery Code:** The body of the `catch` clause.
> - **Propagation:** The process by which an unhandled exception travels up the method call stack until a matching handler is found.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

Imagine you are a **net-banking customer performing a fund transfer of ₹10,000**:

1. **The `try` block** is like the **airport-style security scanning area** at the entrance of the airport. You, your luggage, and your boarding pass enter this zone knowing that a problem (e.g., a prohibited item) *might* be detected. Everything inside this zone is being **monitored**.
2. If the scanner detects a problem, it does not let the issue silently pass. It **raises an alarm** and diverts the passenger to a specific security officer.
3. **The `catch` clause** is that **security officer** standing right outside the scanner. The officer is trained to handle a *specific type* of problem (e.g., a knife vs. a liquid). They take the problem off your hands, deal with it (confiscate the item), and let you continue your journey safely.
4. If **no problem** is detected, you walk straight through to the gate — the `catch` clause is **never invoked**.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** **T**ry = **T**unnel (everything inside is monitored). **C**atch = **C**age that traps the exception. Without a `catch` (or `finally`) after a `try`, the Java compiler raises the error: *"'try' without 'catch' or 'finally'."*

---

## 1.3 The Three Execution Paths (Foundation of the Entire Topic)

When a `try-catch` construct is executed, exactly one of three paths will be taken by the JVM:

| Path | Condition | Result |
|---|---|---|
| **Path A – Happy Path** | No exception is thrown in the `try` block. | All statements in `try` execute. All `catch` clauses are **skipped**. Program continues normally. |
| **Path B – Caught Path** | An exception is thrown in the `try` block AND a `catch` clause matches. | Remaining `try` statements are skipped. The matching `catch` executes. Program continues. |
| **Path C – Uncaught Path** | An exception is thrown but no `catch` matches. | The exception is **rethrown / propagated** up the call stack. If unhandled, the JVM terminates the current thread. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often believe the `catch` clause executes *only when* an exception occurs. This is wrong. The `catch` clause is **defined unconditionally** — it is just **bypassed** when no exception occurs. This distinction is a frequent board exam question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the `try` Block

The `try` block is a **compound statement** that syntactically delimits a region of code. Its rules are non-negotiable for KTU exams:

- **Rule 1 (Scope):** A variable declared *inside* the `try` block is **not visible** outside it (block scope).
- **Rule 2 (Nesting):** `try` blocks can be **nested** inside another `try`, `catch`, or `finally` block.
- **Rule 3 (Tracking Point):** Once an exception is thrown inside a `try` block, **all statements *after* the throwing point are skipped**, regardless of whether they could have caused the same error.
- **Rule 4 (Resources):** In modern Java (Java 7+), a `try` block can be followed by parentheses for *try-with-resources* — but that is a separate sub-topic and is not the same as the standard `try-catch`.

## 2.2 Anatomy of the `catch` Clause

The `catch` clause formally has the structure: `catch (ExceptionType identifier) { ... }`.

- **ExceptionType:** A class type that must be `Throwable` or a subclass. The JVM checks this type at runtime using `instanceof`.
- **Identifier:** The parameter name (e.g., `e`, `ex`, `err`) that binds to the actual exception object thrown. This object gives access to:
  - `e.getMessage()` — Returns a human-readable description.
  - `e.toString()` — Returns the type plus the message.
  - `e.printStackTrace()` — Prints the full stack trace to `System.err`.
- **Multi-Catch (Java 7+):** A single `catch` can catch multiple unrelated types using the pipe syntax: `catch (IOException | SQLException e) { ... }`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Catch Parameter is Effectively `final`:** Although you can mark it explicitly with `final`, the catch parameter is treated as effectively final — you cannot reassign it inside the `catch` body. The Java compiler enforces this.

## 2.3 The Matching Algorithm (How the JVM Selects a Catch)

When an exception of object `ex` is thrown, the JVM scans the `catch` clauses **in the order they appear** in the source code:

1. The first `catch` whose declared parameter type is a **superclass of (or same as)** the runtime type of `ex` is selected.
2. **The remaining `catch` clauses are skipped**, even if they would also match.
3. If no `catch` matches, the exception propagates to the enclosing method.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Cardinal Rule of Order:** `catch` clauses for **more specific (subclass)** exceptions MUST appear **BEFORE** `catch` clauses for **more general (superclass)** exceptions. Otherwise, the compiler reports: *"Unreachable catch block for X. It is already handled by an earlier catch block."*

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Rule / Property | Formal Statement | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Try-Catch Pairing | A `try` block MUST be followed by at least one `catch` or a `finally`. | `try { } catch (E e) { }` ✅ |
| 2 | Single Active Handler | When an exception is thrown, **exactly one** `catch` (at most) executes. | First match wins. |
| 3 | Skip on Throw | Statements after the throwing statement inside `try` are **not executed**. | `try { a(); b(); }` if `a()` throws, `b()` is skipped. |
| 4 | Variable Scope | Local variables declared in `try` are **not visible** in `catch`. | `try { int x=5; } catch(E e){ x; }` ❌ Compile error. |
| 5 | Catch Order | Subclass exceptions must precede superclass exceptions. | `catch(IOException)` before `catch(Exception)`. |
| 6 | Polymorphic Match | A `catch` of type `Exception` catches **all checked + unchecked** exceptions except `Throwable` subclasses like `Error`. | `catch(Exception e)` is the broadest legal catch. |
| 7 | Cannot Catch `Error` | `Error` (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`) is a subclass of `Throwable` but conventionally NOT caught. | `catch(Error e)` compiles but is bad practice. |
| 8 | Final Parameter | The catch parameter is effectively `final`. | `catch(IOException e){ e = null; }` ❌ Compile error. |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry-grade Java systems, the `try-catch` construct is the backbone of:

- **Banking Software:** Catching `ArithmeticException` during division operations on monetary values.
- **Network Applications:** Catching `IOException` and `SocketTimeoutException` for resilient REST API clients (used in Spring Boot microservices).
- **Database Systems (JDBC):** Catching `SQLException` to roll back transactions and log errors without crashing the application server.
- **IoT and Embedded Java:** Catching `NullPointerException` in sensor data pipelines to prevent device reboots.
- **Web Servers (Tomcat, Jetty):** Catching `Throwable` at the top of request threads so that one bad request does not take down the entire server.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Application Question Tip:** When asked *"Where is try-catch used in real systems?"*, always tie your answer to **transactional rollback in JDBC** — it is the most cited industry example in the KTU model answer key.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analysis & Execution Trace Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Code Walkthrough (The "Hello, try-catch" Canonical Example)

The following Java program is a **canonical KTU lab question**. Every step of execution is dissected.

```java
public class TryCatchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int[] marks = { 85, 90, 78 };
        int totalStudents = 0;
        
        try {
            System.out.println("Step 1: Entering try block.");
            totalStudents = 3;
            System.out.println("Step 2: Total students = " + totalStudents);
            
            // The next statement WILL throw ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
            System.out.println("Step 3: Accessing marks[5] = " + marks[5]);
            
            System.out.println("Step 4: This line will NOT execute.");
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("CAUGHT! Exception type: " + e.getClass().getName());
            System.out.println("CAUGHT! Message: " + e.getMessage());
        }
        
        System.out.println("Step 5: Program continues normally after try-catch.");
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace (Output Reconstruction)

| Line | Action | State of Program |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `int[] marks = {85, 90, 78};` | Array of length **3** is allocated. Valid indices: **0, 1, 2**. |
| 2 | `int totalStudents = 0;` | Local variable initialized to **0**. |
| 3 | JVM enters the `try` block. | A new *exception monitoring* scope is established. |
| 4 | Prints *"Step 1: Entering try block."* | Output: `Step 1: Entering try block.` |
| 5 | `totalStudents = 3;` | `totalStudents` is now **3**. |
| 6 | Prints *"Step 2: Total students = 3"*. | Output: `Step 2: Total students = 3` |
| 7 | Evaluates `marks[5]`. | Index **5** is outside the valid range **[0..2]**. |
| 8 | JVM constructs an `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` object and **throws** it. | Normal flow is interrupted. |
| 9 | *"Step 4"* statement is **skipped** (Rule 3 from cheat sheet). | Not printed. |
| 10 | JVM scans `catch` clauses. Finds a match: `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`. | Control transfers to that `catch`. |
| 11 | Prints the exception class name and message. | Output includes `java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` and `Index 5 out of bounds for length 3`. |
| 12 | JVM exits the `try-catch` block. | Monitoring scope is closed. |
| 13 | Prints *"Step 5: Program continues normally..."*. | Output: `Step 5: Program continues normally after try-catch.` |

### Final Console Output
```
Step 1: Entering try block.
Step 2: Total students = 3
CAUGHT! Exception type: java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
CAUGHT! Message: Index 5 out of bounds for length 3
Step 5: Program continues normally after try-catch.
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Step 9:** The line `"Step 4: This line will NOT execute."` is **physically present in the code** but **never printed**. This proves the rule that *statements after the throwing point are skipped*. KTU examiners frequently award marks for this observation.

---

## 3.2 Multi-Catch Execution Trace (Subtler Case)

```java
public class MultiCatchDemo {
    static void perform(int choice) {
        try {
            if (choice == 1) {
                int result = 10 / 0;                    // ArithmeticException
            } else if (choice == 2) {
                String s = null;
                System.out.println(s.length());         // NullPointerException
            } else {
                int[] a = new int[2];
                a[5] = 99;                              // ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
            }
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Handler A: Arithmetic → " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (NullPointerException e) {
            System.out.println("Handler B: NullPointer → " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Handler C: Array → " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (RuntimeException e) {
            System.out.println("Handler D: Generic runtime → " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        perform(1);
        perform(2);
        perform(3);
        perform(4);   // Will it reach Handler D?
    }
}
```

### Analytical Trace

- `perform(1)` → `10/0` throws `ArithmeticException` → Matches **Handler A**. Output: `Handler A: Arithmetic → / by zero`.
- `perform(2)` → Null deref throws `NullPointerException` → Matches **Handler B**. Output: `Handler B: NullPointer → null`.
- `perform(3)` → Bad array index throws `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` (subclass of `IndexOutOfBoundsException`, subclass of `RuntimeException`) → Matches **Handler C**. Output: `Handler C: Array → Index 5 out of bounds for length 2`.
- `perform(4)` → `if (choice == 4)` does nothing, falls to the `else` block → throws `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` → Matches **Handler C again**. **Handler D is never reached** because C already caught it.

> [!WARNING]
> **Counter-Intuitive Result:** `Handler D` is **unreachable for the inputs given**. The compiler does NOT flag this as an error because a subclass *could* theoretically be added (e.g., a future `ClassCastException`). The compiler only flags **unreachable catch blocks for explicit, declared exceptions**, not for runtime exceptions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Control Flow Inside try-catch

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Method Entry]) --> enterTry[Enter try block]
    enterTry --> stmt1[Execute Statement 1]
    stmt1 --> check1{Statement 2 throws?}
    check1 -- No --> stmt2[Execute Statement 2]
    stmt2 --> check2{Statement 3 throws?}
    check2 -- No --> stmt3[Execute Statement N]
    stmt3 --> exitTry[Exit try normally]
    exitTry --> skipCatch([All catch clauses SKIPPED])
    skipCatch --> continueA([Continue after try-catch])
    check1 -- Yes, Type T1 --> scanCatches[JVM scans catch clauses in order]
    check2 -- Yes, Type T2 --> scanCatches
    scanCatches --> matchQ{First catch where T is instanceof ParamType?}
    matchQ -- Yes, Handler H --> runH[Execute Handler H body]
    runH --> afterH([Continue after try-catch])
    matchQ -- No match --> propagate[Propagate exception to caller]
    propagate --> callerQ{Caller has matching handler?}
    callerQ -- Yes --> runH2[Caller's handler runs]
    callerQ -- No --> jvmTerminate[JVM terminates thread; prints stack trace]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: try-catch Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GUARDED["Guarded Region (try block)"]
        S1[Statement A]
        S2[Statement B - may throw]
        S3[Statement C - skipped if B throws]
    end
    
    subgraph HANDLERS["Exception Handlers (catch clauses)"]
        H1["catch (SubException e1)"]
        H2["catch (ParentException e2)"]
        H3["catch (Exception e3)"]
    end
    
    S1 --> S2
    S2 -->|If throws| SCAN{Exception type?}
    S2 -->|If succeeds| S3
    SCAN -->|Type matches H1| H1
    SCAN -->|Type matches H2 only| H2
    SCAN -->|Type matches H3 only| H3
    SCAN -->|No match| PROP[Propagate to enclosing scope]
    
    H1 --> AFTER[Continue normal execution]
    H2 --> AFTER
    H3 --> AFTER
    PROP --> AFTER
    S3 --> AFTER
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Class Hierarchy: Java Exception Classes (Most Tested in KTU)

```mermaid
classDiagram
    direction BT
    class Throwable {
        <<abstract>>
        +getMessage() String
        +printStackTrace() void
    }
    class Error {
        <<unchecked>>
        OutOfMemoryError
        StackOverflowError
    }
    class Exception {
        <<checked-or-unchecked>>
    }
    class RuntimeException {
        <<unchecked>>
    }
    class IOException {
        <<checked>>
    }
    class SQLException {
        <<checked>>
    }
    class ArithmeticException {
        <<unchecked>>
        Divide by zero
    }
    class NullPointerException {
        <<unchecked>>
        Null object access
    }
    class ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException {
        <<unchecked>>
        Bad array index
    }
    class NumberFormatException {
        <<unchecked>>
        Bad parseInt input
    }
    class ClassNotFoundException {
        <<checked>>
    }
    
    Throwable <|-- Error
    Throwable <|-- Exception
    Exception <|-- IOException
    Exception <|-- SQLException
    Exception <|-- ClassNotFoundException
    Exception <|-- RuntimeException
    RuntimeException <|-- ArithmeticException
    RuntimeException <|-- NullPointerException
    RuntimeException <|-- ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
    RuntimeException <|-- NumberFormatException
    ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException <|-- IndexOutOfBoundsException
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Note for KTU Students:** The arrows point from subclass → superclass (UML inheritance). `RuntimeException` is the parent of *all four* most common unchecked exceptions tested in KTU 2024 Scheme exams: `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, and `NumberFormatException`.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions (Direct Definitions)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between a `try` block and a `catch` clause in Java.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | try block | catch clause |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Defines a *guarded region* of code that is *monitored* for exceptions. | Defines the *handler* that *intercepts and processes* an exception. |
| **Keyword** | `try` | `catch` |
| **Syntax** | `try { statements; }` | `catch (ExceptionType identifier) { statements; }` |
| **Number** | Exactly one `try` per handler chain (may be nested). | One or more `catch` clauses may follow a single `try`. |
| **Execution** | Always executed (entered). | Executed **only** if the exception type matches. |
| **Variable** | Block scope for local variables. | Catch parameter is effectively `final`. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** A *2-mark* question will *not* award full marks for a one-line answer. You must present at least **three** points of difference. Many students write only the syntax and lose the third point.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What happens if an exception is thrown inside a `try` block but no matching `catch` clause is found?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. The remaining statements inside the `try` block **after the throwing point are skipped** (not executed).
2. The exception object is **propagated to the enclosing method** (or constructor) via the JVM runtime stack.
3. If the calling method also has no matching `catch`, the exception continues to propagate up the call stack. If it reaches the `main` method unhandled, the **JVM terminates the current thread**, prints the exception's **stack trace** on `System.err`, and the program **abnormally terminates** with a non-zero exit code.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic:** *No catch → Propagate → Caller → Caller → main → JVM crash.*

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern Reminder:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with internal choice. Typically split as (a) **7 marks** + (b) **7 marks**. You may attempt EITHER Option A OR Option B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**a)** Explain the `try-catch` mechanism in Java with its general syntax. Discuss what happens when an exception is *not* caught by any `catch` clause. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Write a Java program that reads two integers from the user and performs division. Use a `try-catch` block to handle the case where the divisor is zero. The program should also handle the case where the user enters non-integer input. Display appropriate messages for each case. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

```java
// General syntax
try {
    // guarded code
} catch (ExceptionType1 e1) {
    // handler for type 1
} catch (ExceptionType2 e2) {
    // handler for type 2
} catch (Exception e3) {
    // generic handler
}
```

**Explanation (mark distribution):**

- **(2 marks)** Definition of `try` as a *guarded/monitored* region using the `try` keyword.
- **(2 marks)** Definition of `catch` as a *handler* that matches the thrown exception's type and executes recovery code.
- **(2 marks)** Behavior on no-match: remaining `try` statements are skipped; the exception *propagates* up the call stack.
- **(1 mark)** Terminal consequence: uncaught exception in `main` → JVM prints stack trace and program terminates abnormally.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;
import java.util.InputMismatchException;

public class SafeDivision {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        
        try {
            System.out.print("Enter numerator: ");
            int num = sc.nextInt();
            
            System.out.print("Enter denominator: ");
            int den = sc.nextInt();
            
            int result = num / den;   // may throw ArithmeticException
            System.out.println("Result = " + result);
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Error: Division by zero is not allowed.");
        } catch (InputMismatchException e) {
            System.out.println("Error: Please enter valid integers only.");
        } catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("Unexpected error: " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            sc.close();
            System.out.println("Scanner closed.");
        }
    }
}
```

**Mark distribution:**

- **(2 marks)** Correct use of `Scanner.nextInt()` to read integers.
- **(2 marks)** Correct `try` block structure with division operation.
- **(2 marks)** Two distinct `catch` clauses for the two specified exceptions.
- **(1 mark)** Appropriate user-friendly error message in each catch.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often write `catch (Exception e)` as a single generic catch and lose **2 marks** because the question explicitly asks for *handling both cases separately*. Always prefer multiple specific catches unless told otherwise.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**a)** Explain the rules that must be followed when using multiple `catch` blocks in Java. What happens if the order of the `catch` clauses is incorrect? Illustrate with an example. **(7 marks)**

**b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate the use of a `try-catch` block to handle `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` and `NumberFormatException`. The program should accept a string array of numbers, parse them to integers, and compute the sum. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Rules for multiple catch blocks (mark distribution):**

- **(2 marks)** The most specific exception class (subclass) must be caught *first*, followed by more general (superclass) classes.
- **(2 marks)** The JVM matches the **first applicable** `catch` in the order written; subsequent matching catches are skipped.
- **(2 marks)** The catch parameter is effectively `final` — cannot be reassigned within the catch body.
- **(1 mark)** A single catch can catch multiple unrelated exception types using the `|` operator (multi-catch, Java 7+).

**Illustration of incorrect order (compile-time error):**

```java
try {
    FileReader fr = new FileReader("data.txt");
} catch (Exception e) {                  // ← This is OK (broad)
    System.out.println("General error.");
} catch (FileNotFoundException e) {       // ← COMPILE ERROR!
    System.out.println("File missing.");
}
```

**Compiler error produced:**
```
error: exception FileNotFoundException has already been caught
```
Because `FileNotFoundException` is a *subclass* of `Exception`, the first catch already handles it. The second catch is **unreachable**.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

```java
public class StringSumDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String[] numbers = { "10", "20", "thirty", "40", "50x" };
        int sum = 0;
        int parsedCount = 0;
        
        for (int i = 0; i < numbers.length; i++) {
            try {
                int n = Integer.parseInt(numbers[i]);
                sum += n;
                parsedCount++;
                System.out.println("Parsed " + numbers[i] + " → " + n);
            } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
                System.out.println("Skipping invalid entry at index " + i
                                   + ": " + numbers[i]);
            } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
                // Defensive, though loop bound prevents this
                System.out.println("Index error: " + e.getMessage());
            }
        }
        
        System.out.println("Total parsed values: " + parsedCount);
        System.out.println("Sum = " + sum);
    }
}
```

**Expected Output:**
```
Parsed 10 → 10
Parsed 20 → 20
Skipping invalid entry at index 2: thirty
Parsed 40 → 40
Skipping invalid entry at index 4: 50x
Total parsed values: 3
Sum = 70
```

**Mark distribution:**

- **(2 marks)** Use of `Integer.parseInt()` inside `try`.
- **(2 marks)** Two `catch` clauses: `NumberFormatException` and `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`.
- **(2 marks)** Loop iterates correctly without terminating on the first exception (graceful recovery).
- **(1 mark)** Program computes the sum of valid entries only.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-Mark Question Trap:** Students sometimes place `catch (Exception e)` FIRST. The compiler will then reject all subsequent `catch` clauses as **unreachable**, causing the program to **fail to compile** — and you will receive **zero marks** for the entire coding sub-part. Always re-read your catch order before submission.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (Print & Keep)**

- ✅ A `try` block MUST be followed by at least one `catch` or a `finally` block.
- ✅ When an exception is thrown, **only the matching catch executes**; all others are skipped.
- ✅ Statements in `try` *after* the throwing point are **not executed** (skipped).
- ✅ The catch parameter is **effectively final** — you cannot reassign it.
- ✅ **Catch order matters:** Subclass before Superclass. Otherwise, *"unreachable catch block"* compile error.
- ✅ A `catch (Exception e)` is the **broadest legal catch**; it catches everything except `Error` and other `Throwable` subtypes.
- ✅ If no `catch` matches, the exception **propagates up the call stack** to the caller.
- ✅ A `try` block CAN be nested inside another `try`, `catch`, or `finally`.
- ✅ Local variables declared inside `try` are **NOT visible** outside the block (block scope).
- ✅ `getMessage()`, `toString()`, and `printStackTrace()` are the three essential methods on the catch parameter.
- ✅ **Java 7 multi-catch:** `catch (IOException | SQLException e)` — single handler for multiple unrelated types.
- ✅ `Error` (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`) is conventionally NOT caught.
- ✅ `try` without `catch` is allowed **only if** it is followed by `finally` (or is a try-with-resources).
- ✅ The JVM is **polymorphic** when matching: a `catch (RuntimeException e)` will catch an `ArithmeticException` because the latter is a subtype.
- ✅ For KTU 2024 Scheme, the four most-tested unchecked exceptions are: **`ArithmeticException`**, **`NullPointerException`**, **`ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`**, **`NumberFormatException`**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiple catch Clauses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
