> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multiple catch Clauses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In Java's exception handling framework, **Multiple catch Clauses** refer to the mechanism of associating a single `try` block with several `catch` blocks, where each `catch` block is dedicated to handling a specific type of exception. The Java Virtual Machine (JVM) examines the `catch` blocks sequentially (top-to-bottom) and executes the **first matching handler** whose parameter type is compatible with the thrown exception object. This construct enables **type-specific, granular, and robust error recovery** within Object-Oriented Programs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 — Exception Handling):**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly lists *"Multiple catch clauses (finally, throw, throws, try-catch)"* under the exception handling unit. Students are expected to know the **syntax, the rule of ordering from specific to general, and the multi-catch (Java 7+) syntax** as direct 3-mark and 14-mark exam topics.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a hospital's **emergency triage desk**. Patients arrive with different ailments — a heart attack, a broken bone, or a mild fever. The hospital does **not** treat every patient with the same protocol. Instead:
- A **cardiology specialist** handles heart attacks.
- An **orthopedic surgeon** handles fractures.
- A **general physician** handles mild fevers.

The `try` block is the **triage desk** (where the patient arrives), and each `catch` block is a **specialist** trained for one specific problem. The desk routes the patient to the **first available specialist** whose expertise matches. The `finally` block is the **discharge counter** — *every* patient goes through it, regardless of which doctor treated them.

If a **fever patient** were sent to the **heart specialist first**, the patient would be **mis-diagnosed or unreachable** — this is exactly why **specific exceptions must be caught before general ones** in Java.

## 1.3 Quick Syntax Snapshot

```java
try {
    // Protected code: may throw one of several exceptions
} catch (ArithmeticException e) {
    // Handler 1: handles division by zero
} catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
    // Handler 2: handles invalid array index
} catch (Exception e) {
    // Handler 3: catch-all for any other exception
} finally {
    // Always executes — cleanup code
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **general-to-specific** rule in the syllabus is the **single most-tested concept** in KTU exams on this topic. The compilation error *"Unreachable catch block"* is the most common pitfall.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Rules — The "Why" Behind Each Step

1. **Sequential Matching (Top-Down Search)**
   The JVM walks through `catch` blocks in the order they are written. The **first** `catch` whose declared parameter type **is the same as, or a supertype of**, the thrown exception is selected. All subsequent `catch` blocks are skipped for that exception event.

2. **Specificity Rule (Subclass-Before-Superclass)**
   Because matching is sequential, a more **specific (subclass)** exception type **must appear before** any of its **general (superclass)** types. Otherwise, the subclass handler becomes **unreachable code**, and the compiler raises the error:
   `error: exception java.io.IOException has already been caught`

3. **Type-Compatibility Binding**
   The thrown exception object is implicitly **upcast** (polymorphically) to the parameter type of the first matching `catch`. This is a direct application of **runtime polymorphism** — one of the CO2 outcomes of the course.

4. **Multi-Catch Syntax (Java 7+ Feature)**
   A single `catch` block can catch **multiple unrelated exception types** using the pipe (`\vert`) operator. This **reduces code duplication** when the handling logic is identical for several exception types.

5. **Execution Guarantee of `finally`**
   The optional `finally` block is executed **after** the `try` (or matching `catch`) finishes, **regardless of whether an exception was thrown, caught, or even propagated further**. It is used for releasing resources (closing files, sockets, database connections).

6. **Single Matching Handler**
   **Only one** `catch` block executes per exception event. There is no "fall-through" to the next `catch` — execution transfers to the `finally` block (if present) or continues after the entire `try-catch-finally` construct.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Rule / Construct | Syntax Pattern | Key Constraint |
|---|------------------|----------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Basic Multi-catch** | `catch (TypeA e) { } catch (TypeB e) { }` | Order: specific → general |
| 2 | **Multi-catch (Java 7+)** | `catch (TypeA \vert TypeB e) { }` | Types must **not** be in a parent-child relation |
| 3 | **Catch-all** | `catch (Exception e) { }` | Must be the **last** `catch` block |
| 4 | **Finally Guarantee** | `finally { /* cleanup */ }` | Executes **always** — even on `System.exit()` is the only exception |
| 5 | **Re-throw** | `throw e;` inside a `catch` | The method must declare `throws` if re-throwing a checked exception |
| 6 | **Rethrow with Improved Type Checking (Java 7+)** | `catch (final IOException e) { ... throw e; }` | Compiler analyzes what is actually thrown |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Multiple catch clauses form the backbone of **resilient enterprise systems**:

- **Banking Software**: A fund-transfer module may face `NetworkException` (retry), `InsufficientFundsException` (notify user), `InvalidAccountException` (block account) — each requiring a **distinct business workflow**.
- **IoT / Embedded Systems**: Sensor-reading code may throw `TimeoutException`, `IOException`, or `NumberFormatException` — each logged differently for **diagnostic telemetry**.
- **Web Servers (Tomcat, Spring)**: Catch blocks differentiate between **recoverable** (DB connection lost → retry pool) and **non-recoverable** (auth token expired → redirect to login) errors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Connection to KTU Course Outcomes (CO):** This topic directly maps to **CO3 — Implement robust Java programs using exception handling mechanisms** of the PBEOT404 syllabus. Marks are typically awarded for **correct ordering, multi-catch syntax, and proper `finally` usage** in 14-marker questions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section provides an **exhaustive, line-by-line walkthrough** of the program flow when a `try` block with multiple `catch` clauses encounters different exception scenarios.

## 3.1 Analytical Trace — Working Example

Consider the following Java program (the canonical KTU exam example):

```java
public class MultiCatchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            int a = Integer.parseInt(args[0]);
            int b = Integer.parseInt(args[1]);
            int c = a / b;
            System.out.println("Result = " + c);
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Cannot divide by zero.");
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Please supply two numbers.");
        } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
            System.out.println("Inputs must be integers.");
        } catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("Some other error: " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Finally block executed.");
        }
        System.out.println("Program continues normally.");
    }
}
```

### 3.2 Case-by-Case Execution Trace

**Case 1:** `java MultiCatchDemo 10 2` (Normal flow)

The execution proceeds step-by-step as follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{JVM enters the `try` block.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \texttt{args[0]} = \text{"10"} \rightarrow \texttt{a = 10}. \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \texttt{args[1]} = \text{"2"} \rightarrow \texttt{b = 2}. \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \texttt{c = 10 / 2 = 5}. \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Prints } \texttt{"Result = 5"}. \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{No exception → all `catch` blocks skipped.} \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \text{`finally` block executes → prints "Finally block executed".} \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad & \text{Execution continues → prints "Program continues normally".}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case 2:** `java MultiCatchDemo 10 0` (Division by zero)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{JVM enters the `try` block.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \texttt{a = 10}, \texttt{b = 0}. \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \texttt{a / b} \rightarrow \text{JVM throws an } \texttt{ArithmeticException}. \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Control transfers out of `try`.} \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{JVM checks `catch(ArithmeticException e)`} \rightarrow \textbf{MATCH (1st handler)}.\\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Prints "Cannot divide by zero."} \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \text{All subsequent `catch` blocks are SKIPPED.} \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad & \text{`finally` executes → "Finally block executed".} \\
\text{Step 9:} \quad & \text{"Program continues normally."}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case 3:** `java MultiCatchDemo 10` (Missing argument)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{JVM enters the `try` block.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \texttt{args[0]} = \text{"10"} \rightarrow \texttt{a = 10}. \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \texttt{args[1]} \text{ does not exist → } \texttt{ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException} \text{ thrown.}\\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{JVM checks `catch(ArithmeticException e)`} \rightarrow \textbf{NO match}. \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{JVM checks `catch(ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e)`} \rightarrow \textbf{MATCH}. \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Prints "Please supply two numbers."} \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \text{`finally` executes.} \\
\text{Step 8:} \quad & \text{Program continues normally.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case 4:** `java MultiCatchDemo ten five` (Non-numeric input)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{JVM enters the `try` block.} \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \texttt{Integer.parseInt("ten")} \rightarrow \texttt{NumberFormatException}. \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{JVM checks `catch(ArithmeticException e)`} \rightarrow \textbf{NO match}. \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{JVM checks `catch(ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e)`} \rightarrow \textbf{NO match}. \\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{JVM checks `catch(NumberFormatException e)`} \rightarrow \textbf{MATCH}. \\
\text{Step 6:} \quad & \text{Prints "Inputs must be integers."} \\
\text{Step 7:} \quad & \text{`finally` executes.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Case 5:** `java MultiCatchDemo` (Empty arguments) — Demonstrates the **catch-all**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \texttt{args[0]} \text{ does not exist → } \texttt{ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException}. \\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{First two specific `catch` blocks: NO match for this scenario if reordered incorrectly.} \\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{If `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` is not caught specifically,} \\
& \text{it propagates to the general } \texttt{catch (Exception e)} \text{ handler.} \\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Prints "Some other error: ...".}\\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{`finally` executes.}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Multi-Catch Equivalence Analysis

The multi-catch syntax introduced in Java 7:

```java
catch (IOException | SQLException e) { ... }
```

is **semantically equivalent** to the verbose form:

```java
catch (IOException e) { ... }
catch (SQLException e) { ... }
```

**However**, the multi-catch has these subtle advantages:

- The variable `e` is effectively `final` inside the multi-catch block — you cannot reassign it.
- The bytecode generated is more compact (a single exception table entry).
- Improves **maintainability** when the same recovery logic applies to multiple exception families.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Expected Answer Depth:** For a 14-mark question, the model answer should include **at least 3 distinct input scenarios**, an **exception hierarchy diagram**, the **rule of ordering**, and a **multi-catch Java 7 syntax snippet**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Exception Decision Flow

| Exception Type | When Triggered | Hierarchy Level | Subclass of | Typical Handler Action |
|----------------|----------------|-----------------|-------------|------------------------|
| `ArithmeticException` | `x / 0` | Leaf | `RuntimeException` | Print error, continue |
| `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | `arr[5]` on size-3 array | Leaf | `RuntimeException` | Re-prompt input |
| `NumberFormatException` | `parseInt("abc")` | Leaf | `IllegalArgumentException` | Show valid format |
| `NullPointerException` | `null.method()` | Leaf | `RuntimeException` | Initialize object |
| `IOException` | File not found | Intermediate | `Exception` | Retry or close stream |
| `SQLException` | DB query fail | Intermediate | `Exception` | Rollback transaction |
| `Exception` | Catch-all | Top | `Throwable` | Generic logging |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Java Exception Class Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    throwableA["Throwable"]
    errorA["Error"]
    outofmemA["OutOfMemoryError"]
    stackoverA["StackOverflowError"]
    exceptionA["Exception"]
    ioexA["IOException"]
    sqlexA["SQLException"]
    runtimeA["RuntimeException"]
    arithA["ArithmeticException"]
    nullptrA["NullPointerException"]
    arridxA["ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException"]
    numfmtA["NumberFormatException"]
    illegalargA["IllegalArgumentException"]

    throwableA --> errorA
    throwableA --> exceptionA
    errorA --> outofmemA
    errorA --> stackoverA
    exceptionA --> ioexA
    exceptionA --> sqlexA
    exceptionA --> runtimeA
    runtimeA --> arithA
    runtimeA --> nullptrA
    runtimeA --> arridxA
    runtimeA --> illegalargA
    illegalargA --> numfmtA
```

## 4.2 Multiple Catch Block — Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Program enters try block"] --> execA["Execute statements in try"]
    execA --> checkA{"Exception thrown?"}
    checkA -- "No" --> finallyA["Execute finally block"]
    checkA -- "Yes" --> match1{"Matches catch1 TypeA?"}
    match1 -- "Yes" --> handler1["Execute catch1 handler"]
    match1 -- "No" --> match2{"Matches catch2 TypeB?"}
    match2 -- "Yes" --> handler2["Execute catch2 handler"]
    match2 -- "No" --> match3{"Matches catch3 TypeC?"}
    match3 -- "Yes" --> handler3["Execute catch3 handler"]
    match3 -- "No" --> matchGeneral{"Matches catchAll Exception?"}
    matchGeneral -- "Yes" --> handlerGeneral["Execute catchAll handler"]
    matchGeneral -- "No" --> propagateA["Propagate up the call stack"]
    handler1 --> finallyA
    handler2 --> finallyA
    handler3 --> finallyA
    handlerGeneral --> finallyA
    finallyA --> continueA["Continue after try-catch-finally"]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Multi-Catch vs Separate Catch

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SCENARIO_A["Scenario A: Separate catch blocks"]
        tryA["try block"] --> arithCatch["catch ArithmeticException"]
        arithCatch --> arrCatch["catch ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException"]
        arrCatch --> nullCatch["catch NullPointerException"]
    end

    subgraph SCENARIO_B["Scenario B: Multi-catch Java 7 plus"]
        tryB["try block"] --> multiCatch["catch ArithmeticException OR ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException OR NullPointerException"]
    end

    SCENARIO_A --> finallyCommon["Common finally block cleanup"]
    SCENARIO_B --> finallyCommon
```

## 4.4 Specificity Ordering — Why Specific First?

```mermaid
graph TD
    orderStart["Declare catch blocks in this order"] --> orderStep1["1. Most specific subclass e.g. NumberFormatException"]
    orderStep1 --> orderStep2["2. Intermediate subclass e.g. IllegalArgumentException"]
    orderStep2 --> orderStep3["3. General parent e.g. RuntimeException"]
    orderStep3 --> orderStep4["4. Top catch-all e.g. Exception"]
    orderStep4 --> orderFinal["If order is reversed, compile error: Unreachable catch block"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Cue for KTU Exams:** Always draw the exception hierarchy with `Throwable` at the root and `Exception → RuntimeException` as the main branch in board examinations. The most commonly asked 7-mark sub-part is *"Draw the exception hierarchy in Java and explain the significance of multiple catch blocks."*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**What are multiple catch clauses in Java? Why is the order of catch blocks important?**

**Model Answer:**

Multiple catch clauses refer to the use of more than one `catch` block after a single `try` block, where each `catch` handles a different type of exception. The JVM examines the catch blocks in the order they appear and selects the first one whose declared exception type matches (or is a supertype of) the thrown exception.

The **order is critical** because a more general (parent) exception class, if written before a more specific (child) class, would catch the exception first, making the specific handler **unreachable code**. This causes a **compile-time error: "exception X has already been caught"**. Therefore, catch blocks must always be ordered from **most specific to most general**.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**Explain the multi-catch feature introduced in Java 7 with an example.**

**Model Answer:**

The **multi-catch** feature, introduced in Java 7, allows a single `catch` block to handle **multiple unrelated exception types** using the pipe (`\vert`) operator. This eliminates code duplication when the recovery logic is identical for several exception types.

**Syntax:**

```java
try {
    // code
} catch (IOException | SQLException e) {
    System.out.println("Database or file error: " + e.getMessage());
}
```

**Key points:**
- The exception variable `e` is **implicitly final** and cannot be reassigned.
- The caught types must **not have a parent-child relationship** (e.g., you cannot write `catch (Exception \vert RuntimeException e)`).
- It improves code readability and reduces maintenance overhead.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain multiple catch clauses in Java with proper syntax. Write a Java program that demonstrates a `try` block with at least **three** different `catch` blocks to handle `ArithmeticException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, and `NumberFormatException`.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the rules and best practices that must be followed while writing multiple catch clauses. What happens if we place a superclass `catch` before a subclass `catch`?

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Syntax of Multiple Catch Clauses:**

```java
try {
    // code that may throw exceptions
} catch (SubclassExceptionType1 e1) {
    // handler 1
} catch (SubclassExceptionType2 e2) {
    // handler 2
} catch (SuperclassExceptionType e) {
    // catch-all handler
} finally {
    // optional cleanup
}
```

**Complete Java Program:**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class MultiCatchExample {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        try {
            System.out.print("Enter numerator: ");
            int a = Integer.parseInt(sc.nextLine());
            System.out.print("Enter denominator: ");
            int b = Integer.parseInt(sc.nextLine());
            int[] arr = new int[a];
            int result = a / b;
            arr[a] = result;          // May throw ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
            System.out.println("Result = " + result);
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Error: Division by zero is not allowed.");
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Error: Array index out of bounds.");
        } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
            System.out.println("Error: Please enter valid integers only.");
        } catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("Generic error: " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Operation completed — finally block executed.");
            sc.close();
        }
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Program Flow:**

- If the user enters a non-numeric value, `NumberFormatException` is caught by the third handler.
- If the denominator is `0`, the first handler (`ArithmeticException`) fires.
- If `a` is larger than the array size, the second handler fires.
- The generic `Exception` handler acts as a safety net for any other unforeseen issue.
- The `finally` block ensures the `Scanner` is closed and a confirmation message is printed.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Rules and Best Practices for Multiple Catch Clauses:**

1. **Order from specific to general** — Subclass exception types must appear before their parent class types.
2. **No duplicate exception types** — The same exception type cannot appear in two `catch` blocks of the same `try`.
3. **Multi-catch only for unrelated types** — In Java 7+ `catch (A \vert B e)`, the types `A` and `B` must not be in the same inheritance hierarchy.
4. **The exception variable is final in multi-catch** — Reassignment is not permitted.
5. **The catch-all (`Exception` or `Throwable`) must be the last block** — Placing it earlier renders all subsequent blocks unreachable.
6. **Do not catch `Throwable` or `Error` in normal application code** — `Error` represents JVM-level fatal conditions (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`) that should not be recovered by application logic.
7. **Log or re-throw, do not swallow** — Every `catch` block should either log the exception, re-throw it, or take corrective action. Empty `catch` blocks are a code-smell.
8. **Resource cleanup belongs in `finally`** — Use it to release file handles, database connections, and network sockets.

**What happens if superclass is placed before subclass?**

Placing a superclass `catch` before a subclass `catch` causes a **compile-time error**:

```
error: exception java.lang.ArithmeticException has already been caught
```

This is because the superclass handler would already match the subclass exception, making the subclass block **unreachable code**, which Java prohibits.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the **exception class hierarchy** in Java starting from `Throwable`. Explain the difference between **checked** and **unchecked** exceptions with examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program that uses the **multi-catch syntax (Java 7+)** to handle `IOException` and `SQLException` in a single `catch` block, and explain its advantages.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Exception Hierarchy Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    thr["java.lang.Throwable"]
    err["java.lang.Error"]
    exc["java.lang.Exception"]
    oom["OutOfMemoryError"]
    sof["StackOverflowError"]
    ioex["IOException"]
    sqlex["SQLException"]
    cnfe["ClassNotFoundException"]
    runtime["RuntimeException"]
    arith["ArithmeticException"]
    npe["NullPointerException"]
    aiobe["ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException"]
    nfe["NumberFormatException"]
    iae["IllegalArgumentException"]

    thr --> err
    thr --> exc
    err --> oom
    err --> sof
    exc --> ioex
    exc --> sqlex
    exc --> cnfe
    exc --> runtime
    runtime --> arith
    runtime --> npe
    runtime --> aiobe
    runtime --> iae
    iae --> nfe
```

**Checked vs Unchecked Exceptions:**

| Feature | Checked Exceptions | Unchecked Exceptions |
|---------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **Parent Class** | Direct subclass of `Exception` (not `RuntimeException`) | Subclass of `RuntimeException` |
| **Compile-time Check** | Yes — compiler enforces handling | No — handling is optional |
| **Must Declare with `throws`** | Yes | No |
| **Examples** | `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException` | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, `NumberFormatException` |
| **Typical Cause** | External resources (file, network, DB) | Programming bugs (logic errors, null refs) |
| **Recovery** | Mandatory handling at compile time | Optional; usually indicate a bug to fix |

**Example Demonstrating Both:**

```java
import java.io.*;

public class CheckedVsUnchecked {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            FileReader fr = new FileReader("data.txt");  // Checked: FileNotFoundException (subclass of IOException)
            int x = 10 / 0;                              // Unchecked: ArithmeticException
        } catch (FileNotFoundException e) {              // Checked — compiler enforces
            System.out.println("File missing: " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {                // Unchecked — handling optional
            System.out.println("Math error: divide by zero.");
        }
    }
}
```

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Java Program with Multi-Catch Syntax:**

```java
import java.io.*;
import java.sql.*;

public class MultiCatchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            // Simulated operations that may throw different checked exceptions
            readFile();
            connectDatabase();
        } catch (IOException | SQLException e) {     // Multi-catch (Java 7+)
            System.out.println("Operation failed: " + e.getMessage());
            e.printStackTrace();
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Cleanup performed in finally block.");
        }
    }

    static void readFile() throws IOException {
        throw new IOException("File could not be read.");
    }

    static void connectDatabase() throws SQLException {
        throw new SQLException("Database connection refused.");
    }
}
```

**Explanation and Advantages of Multi-Catch:**

1. **Reduced Code Duplication** — The same recovery logic (logging + printing) is shared between `IOException` and `SQLException` without writing two separate blocks.
2. **Improved Readability** — The intent ("handle any I/O or database error the same way") is conveyed in a single line.
3. **Easier Maintenance** — If the handling logic changes, only one block needs editing, not two.
4. **Implicitly Final Variable** — The caught variable `e` cannot be reassigned within the block, encouraging immutability and preventing accidental misuse.
5. **Bytecode Efficiency** — The Java compiler emits a single exception handler entry for the multi-catch, making the resulting `.class` file marginally smaller.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Ordering Rule Violation (3-4 marks deducted):** Writing `catch (Exception e)` BEFORE `catch (ArithmeticException e)` is the most common mistake. The compiler error "exception has already been caught" is the direct proof. Always remember: **subclass first, parent last**.
> 2. **Forgetting `finally` semantics (2 marks deducted):** Many students wrongly state that `finally` executes "only when an exception is caught". It executes **always**, even when no exception occurs. The only exception is an abnormal JVM termination via `System.exit()` or a JVM crash.
> 3. **Confusing `throw` and `throws` (1-2 marks deducted):** `throw` is used to *actually throw* an exception object; `throws` is used in a method signature to *declare* that the method may pass a checked exception to its caller.
> 4. **Writing `catch (Throwable t)` in normal code (1 mark deducted):** This catches `Error` instances (like `OutOfMemoryError`) which should never be handled by application code.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3: Multiple Catch Clauses**

- ✅ A single `try` block can have **multiple `catch` blocks**, each catching a **different exception type**.
- ✅ The JVM matches the first compatible `catch` in **top-down** order — only **one** `catch` executes per exception event.
- ✅ **Specific (subclass) exceptions must be listed BEFORE** general (superclass) ones — otherwise, *"unreachable code"* compile error.
- ✅ Java 7 introduced **multi-catch syntax**: `catch (TypeA \vert TypeB e)` for unrelated exception types.
- ✅ The variable `e` in a multi-catch block is **implicitly final** and cannot be reassigned.
- ✅ The **catch-all** `catch (Exception e)` (or `Throwable t`) must always be the **last** `catch` block.
- ✅ The `finally` block executes **always** — used for resource cleanup (closing files, releasing connections).
- ✅ Multiple catch blocks are an application of **runtime polymorphism** — the thrown exception is upcast to the parameter type of the matched handler.
- ✅ A `catch` block may **re-throw** the exception using `throw e;` to propagate it further up the call stack.
- ✅ Do **not** catch `java.lang.Error` subclasses in application code — they indicate fatal JVM conditions.
- ✅ **Checked exceptions** (subclass of `Exception`, not `RuntimeException`) must be either caught or declared with `throws` — enforced at compile time.
- ✅ **Unchecked exceptions** (subclass of `RuntimeException`) are programming bugs — handling is optional.
- ✅ KTU exam focus areas: (1) ordering rule, (2) `finally` semantics, (3) multi-catch syntax, (4) difference between `throw` and `throws`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nested try Statements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nested try Statements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme – PBEOT404, Module 3)**
> A **Nested try Statement** in Java is an exception-handling construct in which one `try` block (along with its associated `catch` and/or `finally` blocks) is placed **lexically inside the body of another `try` block**. This forms a hierarchical exception-handling architecture that enables programmers to isolate and recover from distinct categories of runtime anomalies at different levels of granularity within the same method.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme specification for *Object Oriented Programming using Java (PBEOT404)*, nested `try` constructs belong to the **Exception Handling** sub-module of Module 3, and they directly support the course outcome:

> **CO3:** *Apply object-oriented concepts such as inheritance, polymorphism, exception handling, and multithreading to develop robust Java programs.*

The Java Language Specification (JLS, §14.20) permits **arbitrary depth of nesting** of `try` statements, including the nesting of `try` blocks inside the `catch` and `finally` blocks of the enclosing `try`.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Layered Hospital Triage System**
> Imagine a **multi-specialty hospital**:
> - The **outer reception** (outer `try`) handles *general* complaints — fever, mild pain, cold.
> - Within the **General Medicine ward** (inner `try`), doctors sometimes encounter a *more specific* problem — say, a suspected cardiac arrest — which needs a **specialist cardiologist** (inner `catch`).
> - If the **cardiologist's equipment fails** (an even deeper exception inside the inner `catch`), the patient is escalated back to the **Emergency ICU** (outer `catch` of the outer `try`).
> - The **billing desk** (the outermost `finally`) runs no matter what — whether the patient was treated, referred, or left the hospital.

In the same way, Java's nested `try` lets you catch a **specific** exception at the **most local** level possible, while still preserving a **broader safety net** at an outer level for anything that escapes the inner handlers.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Highlighted in Bold)

- **try block** – the guarded region where exceptions are monitored.
- **catch block** – the handler that executes when a matching exception type is thrown.
- **finally block** – the cleanup region that always executes.
- **Exception propagation** – the upward movement of an unhandled exception from inner `try` to outer `try`.
- **Inner try** – the lexically enclosed `try` block.
- **Outer try** – the enclosing `try` block.
- **Exception shadowing** – a situation where an inner `catch` masks a more general exception of the same type that the outer `catch` would otherwise have caught.
- **Stack unwinding** – the JVM process of deactivating stack frames while searching for a matching handler.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — How Nested `try` Actually Executes

The execution semantics of a nested `try` follow a strict, deterministic set of rules encoded in the **JVM's exception dispatch algorithm**:

1. **Lexical entry** — Execution enters the outer `try` block first.
2. **Sequential execution** — Statements inside the outer `try` are executed in order.
3. **Inner `try` activation** — When the flow of control reaches an inner `try`, a *new exception monitor scope* is pushed onto the JVM's handler stack.
4. **Exception detection** — If an exception is thrown:
   - The **innermost matching** `catch` (by exact type or supertype) handles it.
   - If no matching `catch` exists at the inner level, the exception is **rethrown implicitly** and the search continues in the **next enclosing** `try`.
5. **`finally` execution** — A `finally` block associated with the `try` whose scope is being exited is executed **before** the exception continues to propagate outward.
6. **Outer handler resolution** — If no inner `catch` (in any nested level) handles the exception, control propagates to the outer `try`'s `catch` blocks.
7. **Unhandled escalation** — If even the outermost `catch` cannot handle it, the exception escapes the method and is delegated up the **method call stack** to the caller.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use nested `try` at all?** The KTU examiner expects you to know that nested `try` is used when **different statements inside the same method can throw entirely different categories of exceptions** that require *different recovery strategies*. Using a single flat `try` would force all handlers to live at the same level, reducing clarity.

## 2.2 KTU Cheat Sheet — Key Rules at a Glance

| # | Rule | Code/Concept Snippet | Consequence |
|---|------|----------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Inner `catch` handles the exception first | `catch(ArithmeticException e)` inside outer `catch(Exception e)` | Inner handler wins for that exact type |
| 2 | Inner `try` may be inside `catch` of outer | `catch(...) { try{...} catch(...){...} }` | Re-entry into guarded code after recovery |
| 3 | Inner `try` may be inside `finally` of outer | `finally { try{...} catch(...){...} }` | Cleanup-of-cleanup pattern |
| 4 | `finally` always runs (except `System.exit`) | `try{...} finally{...}` | Resource release is guaranteed |
| 5 | Uncaught inner exception propagates outward | `throw new IOException();` with no inner `catch` | Search continues in outer `catch` chain |
| 6 | A more specific `catch` must appear BEFORE a more general one | `catch(IOException) then catch(Exception)` | Compile-time error otherwise |
| 7 | `try` cannot stand alone; it needs `catch` and/or `finally` | `try{ }` alone is a **compile-time error** | Always pair it up |

## 2.3 Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Nested `try` is the **cornerstone of robust file and network I/O handling** in real-world Java systems. Common industry use cases include:
> - **JDBC code** that opens a `Connection`, then inside opens a `Statement`, then inside fetches a `ResultSet` — each level can fail differently.
> - **Socket programming** where the outer `try` handles `IOException` for the socket, and the inner `try` handles `NumberFormatException` while parsing the response.
> - **Recursive parsers** (compilers, XML parsers) where each recursive descent opens its own guarded context.

## 2.4 Boundary Conditions & Common Pitfalls

- A `try` block **must be accompanied by at least one `catch` or one `finally`**.
- A `catch` with a *more derived* exception type must come **before** a *less derived* one — the Java compiler enforces this.
- A variable declared inside a `try` block is **out of scope** outside that `try` block.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Trace

## 3.1 Canonical Syntax Skeleton (Textual Derivation)

The formal syntactic grammar of a nested `try` can be written as:

$$
\text{TryStmt} \; ::= \; \texttt{try} \; \text{Block} \; (\text{CatchClause})^* \; (\text{FinallyClause})?
$$

$$
\text{CatchClause} \; ::= \; \texttt{catch} \; ( \; \text{FormalParameter} \; ) \; \text{Block}
$$

$$
\text{FinallyClause} \; ::= \; \texttt{finally} \; \text{Block}
$$

$$
\text{Block} \; ::= \; \{\; \text{BlockStmt}^* \; ( \; \text{TryStmt} \; \vert \; \text{other} \; )^* \;\}
$$

The recursive nature of $\text{Block} \to \text{TryStmt}$ is what **formally permits nesting** — there is no syntactic limit on depth in Java.

## 3.2 Executable Trace — Worked Example #1 (Classic Two-Level Nesting)

```java
public class NestedTryDemo1 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {                                  // OUTER try
            System.out.println("Outer try: entered");
            int a = 10, b = 0;
            try {                              // INNER try
                System.out.println("Inner try: dividing");
                int c = a / b;                 // throws ArithmeticException
                System.out.println("Inner try: result = " + c);
            } catch (ArithmeticException e) {  // INNER catch
                System.out.println("Inner catch: " + e.getMessage());
            } finally {
                System.out.println("Inner finally: executed");
            }
            System.out.println("Outer try: continuing after inner block");
        } catch (Exception e) {                // OUTER catch
            System.out.println("Outer catch: " + e);
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Outer finally: executed");
        }
    }
}
```

### Step-by-step trace (KTU Board style)

| Step | Statement / Event | Output | State of JVM Handler Stack |
|-----:|-------------------|--------|----------------------------|
| 1 | Enter `main` | – | empty |
| 2 | Enter outer `try` | `Outer try: entered` | `[outer]` |
| 3 | `a=10, b=0` | – | `[outer]` |
| 4 | Enter inner `try` | `Inner try: dividing` | `[outer, inner]` |
| 5 | Evaluate `a / b` → throws `ArithmeticException` | – | exception raised in `inner` |
| 6 | JVM searches `inner` handler list | – | `ArithmeticException` matches inner `catch` |
| 7 | Execute inner `catch` body | `Inner catch: / by zero` | `[outer, inner(handling)]` |
| 8 | Exit inner `try` (handled) | – | pop inner scope |
| 9 | Execute inner `finally` | `Inner finally: executed` | – |
| 10 | Continue after inner block | `Outer try: continuing after inner block` | `[outer]` |
| 11 | Outer `try` ends normally | – | – |
| 12 | Outer `catch` is **skipped** | – | – |
| 13 | Execute outer `finally` | `Outer finally: executed` | – |
| 14 | Method returns | – | – |

**Final Console Output (in order):**

```
Outer try: entered
Inner try: dividing
Inner catch: / by zero
Inner finally: executed
Outer try: continuing after inner block
Outer finally: executed
```

## 3.3 Worked Example #2 — Inner `try` Inside Outer `catch`

```java
public class NestedTryDemo2 {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            int[] arr = new int[5];
            arr[10] = 50;          // throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Outer catch: array issue → " + e.getMessage());
            try {
                String s = null;
                System.out.println(s.length());   // throws NullPointerException
            } catch (NullPointerException npe) {
                System.out.println("Inner catch (inside outer catch): " + npe.getMessage());
            } finally {
                System.out.println("Inner finally (inside outer catch)");
            }
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Outer finally");
        }
    }
}
```

### Analytical Trace

| Step | Event | Output |
|-----:|-------|--------|
| 1 | Outer `try` entered | – |
| 2 | `arr[10]=50` throws `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | – |
| 3 | Outer `catch` matched | `Outer catch: array issue → Index 10 out of bounds for length 5` |
| 4 | Inside outer `catch`, **inner `try` opened** | – |
| 5 | `s.length()` throws `NullPointerException` | – |
| 6 | Inner `catch` matched | `Inner catch (inside outer catch): Cannot invoke "String.length()" because "s" is null` |
| 7 | Inner `finally` runs | `Inner finally (inside outer catch)` |
| 8 | Outer `catch` body finishes normally | – |
| 9 | Outer `finally` runs | `Outer finally` |

**Key takeaway for the KTU board:**
> An inner `try` placed *inside* a `catch` block is the **canonical way to perform recovery or rollback logic** after handling an outer failure.

## 3.4 Worked Example #3 — Exception Propagation (Inner Unhandled → Outer Handles)

```java
public class NestedTryDemo3 {
    static void innerMethod() throws Exception {
        try {
            throw new Exception("Originated deep inside");
        } catch (RuntimeException re) {            // does NOT match Exception
            System.out.println("Inner catch (Runtime): not reached");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            innerMethod();
        } catch (Exception e) {
            System.out.println("Outer catch: " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Outer finally");
        }
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step derivation:**

- Step 1 — `innerMethod` is invoked from inside the outer `try`.
- Step 2 — The inner `try` throws `new Exception("...")`.
- Step 3 — The JVM inspects the inner `catch`. Its parameter type is `RuntimeException`. Since `Exception` is **not a subclass** of `RuntimeException`, the handler does **not** match.
- Step 4 — The exception **propagates outward** past the inner `try` and exits `innerMethod`.
- Step 5 — The call site is inside the outer `try`, so the JVM re-inspects handlers. The outer `catch (Exception e)` matches.
- Step 6 — Outer `catch` runs, prints message, then `finally` runs.

**Console Output:**

```
Outer catch: Originated deep inside
Outer finally
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap Question:** "Will the inner `catch (RuntimeException re)` block execute?" → **No**, because `java.lang.Exception` is **not** a `RuntimeException`. The student must trace the **inheritance hierarchy** carefully.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Exception Search Algorithm in Nested `try`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Enter outer try block] --> B[Execute statements in outer try]
    B --> C{Encounter inner try block?}
    C -- No --> D{Any statement throws exception?}
    C -- Yes --> E[Enter inner try block]
    E --> F[Execute statements in inner try]
    F --> G{Exception thrown in inner try?}
    G -- No --> H[Inner try ends normally]
    H --> I[Execute inner finally if present]
    I --> J[Continue in outer try]
    G -- Yes --> K{Does inner catch match type?}
    K -- Yes --> L[Execute inner catch block]
    L --> M[Execute inner finally]
    M --> J
    K -- No --> N[Propagate exception outward]
    N --> O[Execute inner finally if present]
    O --> P{Does outer catch match type?}
    P -- Yes --> Q[Execute outer catch block]
    P -- No --> R[Propagate to calling method]
    D -- No --> S[Outer try ends normally]
    S --> T[Execute outer finally]
    Q --> T
    T --> U[Method returns]
    R --> V[Caller method searches for handler]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Layered Architecture of Nested `try`

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1[Outer Scope Method Level]
        direction TB
        OT[Outer try block]
        OF[Outer finally block]
        OC1[Outer catch Exception]
    end

    subgraph L2[Inner Scope Statement Level]
        direction TB
        IT[Inner try block]
        IF[Inner finally block]
        IC1[Inner catch ArithmeticException]
        IC2[Inner catch NullPointerException]
    end

    subgraph L3[Deepest Scope Recovery Level]
        direction TB
        IT2[Try inside catch]
        IC3[Inner catch IOException]
    end

    OT --> IT
    IT --> IC1
    IT --> IC2
    IT --> IF
    OC1 --> IT2
    IT2 --> IC3
    OT --> OF
```

## 4.3 Diagram Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> The first diagram (flowchart) is a **runtime execution model** — it shows the *decision-making path* the JVM follows when an exception is thrown. The second diagram (block architecture) is a **static lexical model** — it shows the *source-code nesting structure* of the program. KTU examiners frequently test whether the student can distinguish these two views.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **Define a nested `try` statement in Java. Give one situation where it is preferred over a flat `try-catch`.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** A nested `try` statement is a `try` block placed inside the body of another `try` block (or inside its `catch`/`finally`).
- **(1 mark)** It allows hierarchical exception handling where different statements can throw different categories of exceptions requiring different recovery actions.
- **(1 mark)** *Example situation:* In a JDBC program, the outer `try` handles `SQLException` for the database connection, while the inner `try` handles `ArithmeticException` or `NumberFormatException` while parsing query results.

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **What happens if an exception is thrown inside an inner `try` block and there is no matching `catch` at the inner level? Explain with a suitable scenario.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **(1 mark)** The exception is **not handled** at the inner level; it propagates outward.
- **(1 mark)** The `finally` block of the inner `try` (if present) executes first, and then the JVM searches the next enclosing `try` for a matching `catch`.
- **(1 mark)** *Scenario:* If an inner `try` throws `java.io.IOException` but only catches `ArithmeticException`, the `IOException` is rethrown to the outer `try`, where an `IOException` handler (or a `Exception` superclass handler) can catch it.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT** declare a variable in an inner `try` and try to use it outside — it is a **scope error**, the most common deduction.
> - **Do NOT** place a more general `catch` BEFORE a more specific one — this is a **compile-time error** and costs 2 full marks.
> - **Do NOT** write `try { }` alone without `catch` or `finally` — the compiler will reject it.
> - Always mention the **output order of `finally` blocks** — they run in *reverse nesting order relative to propagation* and *innermost-first on normal exit*.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **nested `try` statements** in Java with a suitable example. Discuss the rules governing the placement of `catch` blocks when `try` statements are nested.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program that demonstrates a `try` block inside the `catch` block of an outer `try`. Show the output and explain the flow of control.

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

- **(2 marks)** **Definition:** A nested `try` is a `try` block inside another `try` (or its `catch`/`finally`). The Java Language Specification permits arbitrary nesting depth because the `Block` production rule is recursive over `TryStmt`.

- **(2 marks)** **Why nesting is useful:** It enables the programmer to scope exception handling to the **smallest logical region** of code. For example, in array-processing code, the outer `try` can guard the entire loop, while the inner `try` guards a single array-access statement. This gives precise control over recovery.

- **(3 marks)** **Rules for nested `catch` placement:**
  1. A `catch` whose parameter is a **subclass** must come **before** one whose parameter is a **superclass** — otherwise compile-time error `exception java.lang.X has already been caught`.
  2. An inner `catch` is searched **before** an outer `catch` for the same exception type.
  3. If no `catch` in any nesting level matches, the exception propagates to the **caller method**.
  4. A `finally` block is executed regardless of whether the matching `catch` is at the same level or a different level.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Complete Java Program (7 marks):**

```java
public class Q5bOuterCatch {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            int[] a = new int[3];
            a[5] = 100;                          // (1) throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Outer catch: index error → " + e.getMessage());

            try {                                // (2) nested try INSIDE outer catch
                String s = "KTU";
                int n = Integer.parseInt(s);     // (3) throws NumberFormatException
            } catch (NumberFormatException nfe) {
                System.out.println("Inner catch: cannot parse → " + nfe.getMessage());
            } finally {
                System.out.println("Inner finally (recovery cleanup done)");
            }
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Outer finally (program terminating cleanly)");
        }
    }
}
```

**Predicted Output:**

```
Outer catch: index error → Index 5 out of bounds for length 3
Inner catch: cannot parse → For input string: "KTU"
Inner finally (recovery cleanup done)
Outer finally (program terminating cleanly)
```

**Flow-of-control explanation (4 sub-points, allocate 1–2 marks each):**

1. Outer `try` executes the statement `a[5]=100`, which throws `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`.
2. The JVM finds the outer `catch` (parameter type matches exactly) and begins executing its body.
3. Inside that body, the inner `try` runs `Integer.parseInt("KTU")`, which throws `NumberFormatException`.
4. The JVM finds the inner `catch` (exact type match) and prints the message.
5. The inner `finally` runs *before* control returns out of the inner `try`.
6. Control returns to the outer `catch` body, which then terminates normally.
7. The outer `finally` runs last, ensuring program-level cleanup.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **nested `try` statements** and **multiple `catch` blocks** in a single `try`. Provide a Java program that demonstrates the use of nested `try` where the **outer `try` handles `Exception` and the inner `try` handles `ArithmeticException`**. Predict the output.

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is **exception propagation** in the context of nested `try`? Write a Java program where an exception thrown in the **innermost** `try` is **not caught** at the inner level but is caught at the **outer** `try`. Show the output.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — Comparison Table (4 marks) + Code (3 marks)

| Aspect | Nested `try` | Multiple `catch` (single `try`) |
|--------|--------------|---------------------------------|
| Structure | `try { try { … } catch(…) {…} } catch(…) {…}` | `try { … } catch(A) {…} catch(B) {…}` |
| Granularity | Different **regions** of code get different handlers | A **single** region is guarded by multiple handlers |
| Use case | When different statements have different failure modes | When several different exception types can arise from the **same** statement |
| Handler search | Innermost matching `catch` wins | First matching `catch` in declared order wins |
| Lexical flexibility | Allows `try` inside `catch` or `finally` | Not allowed |

**Java Program:**

```java
public class Q5bNested {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {                                          // OUTER
            int x = 100, y = 0;
            try {                                      // INNER
                int z = x / y;                         // ArithmeticException
            } catch (ArithmeticException ae) {
                System.out.println("Inner catch (Arithmetic): " + ae.getMessage());
            }
            String s = null;
            System.out.println(s.length());            // NullPointerException
        } catch (Exception e) {                        // OUTER (generic)
            System.out.println("Outer catch (Exception): " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Outer finally");
        }
    }
}
```

**Predicted Output:**

```
Inner catch (Arithmetic): / by zero
Outer catch (Exception): Cannot invoke "String.length()" because "s" is null
Outer finally
```

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — Exception Propagation (3 marks concept + 4 marks code)

**Concept (3 marks):**
- **Exception propagation** is the automatic, JVM-driven process by which an unhandled exception travels from the throwing statement up the call stack and the lexical nesting structure until a matching `catch` is found.
- In nested `try`, when an inner `try` does **not** contain a matching `catch` (or its `catch` is for a different type), the exception escapes the inner block, its `finally` (if any) runs, and the search continues outward.

**Java Program:**

```java
public class Q5bPropagation {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {                                          // OUTER try
            System.out.println("Outer try: entered");
            try {                                      // INNER try
                System.out.println("Inner try: about to throw");
                throw new NumberFormatException("Bad number deep inside");
            } catch (ArithmeticException ae) {         // Wrong type!
                System.out.println("Inner catch (Arith): NOT REACHED");
            }
            // unreachable ↓ in this run
            System.out.println("Inner try: ended normally (skipped)");
        } catch (NumberFormatException nfe) {          // OUTER catch
            System.out.println("Outer catch: " + nfe.getMessage());
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Outer finally");
        }
    }
}
```

**Predicted Output:**

```
Outer try: entered
Inner try: about to throw
Outer catch: Bad number deep inside
Outer finally
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the inner `catch` skipped?** Because `NumberFormatException` is **not** a subclass of `ArithmeticException` — they are *siblings* under `IllegalArgumentException → RuntimeException`. The inner handler list does not contain a match, so the exception propagates to the outer level.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Points — Must Memorize for KTU**

- ✅ A **nested `try`** = a `try` block lexically inside another `try` (or its `catch`/`finally`).
- ✅ Java allows **unlimited nesting depth** — the `Block` grammar rule is recursive.
- ✅ The **innermost** matching `catch` always wins; outer handlers act as a *safety net*.
- ✅ `finally` blocks execute **even when an exception is propagating** outward — they run *before* the next outer handler is consulted.
- ✅ A `try` block **must** be paired with at least one `catch` **or** one `finally` — never alone.
- ✅ The **specific** exception `catch` must come **before** the **general** one — else compile error.
- ✅ Variables declared inside a `try` are **not visible** outside that block (scope rule).
- ✅ If no `catch` anywhere matches, the exception **escapes the method** to its caller.
- ✅ Nested `try` is most often used to **scope different recovery actions** to logically distinct sub-operations inside a single method.
- ✅ The JVM uses a **handler table** at compile time to determine the *nearest matching catch* — search is *static (lexical)*, not dynamic.
- ✅ `System.exit(0)` is the only normal-path way to **skip** a `finally` block.
- ✅ For the KTU 14-mark question, the examiner expects: **(i)** definition, **(ii)** syntax/skeleton, **(iii)** complete working code, **(iv)** predicted output, **(v)** flow-of-control explanation with at least 4–5 numbered steps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: throw, throws and finally
Multithreaded Programming - Thread Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Multithreaded Programming - Thread Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multithreaded Programming** is a programming paradigm that allows a single process to execute **multiple threads** concurrently, where each thread represents an independent path of execution within the same program. A **Thread** is the smallest unit of CPU execution, lightweight compared to a process, and shares the process's memory space (heap) while maintaining its own **stack**, **program counter (PC)**, and **register context**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for Java, the **Thread Model** refers to the Java Virtual Machine's (JVM) abstraction that allows a Java program to spawn, schedule, synchronize, and terminate multiple concurrent threads of control. The model is built upon two foundational constructs:

- The **`java.lang.Thread` class** — represents a thread of execution.
- The **`java.lang.Runnable` functional interface** — provides the `run()` method contract that thread bodies must implement.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Definition**: According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, the Java Thread Model is built on the principle that *"a thread is a thread of execution in a program. The Java Virtual Machine allows an application to have multiple threads of execution running concurrently."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine a **restaurant kitchen**:

- **Single-Threaded Program** = **One chef** cooking all orders one after another. While the chef chops vegetables, no soup is being stirred. The kitchen is "idle" most of the time.
- **Multithreaded Program** = **A team of chefs** in the same kitchen, each handling a different dish simultaneously. One chef boils pasta, another grills meat, another plates desserts — **all sharing the same pantry (memory) and stove (CPU scheduling)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Software Examples**:
> - A **web browser** uses one thread to render HTML, another to load images, another to run JavaScript, and another to handle user clicks.
> - **MS Word** autosaves in one thread while you type in another.
> - **A music player** streams audio in one thread while updating the UI animation in another.

### 1.3 Why Multithreading Matters — Core Advantages

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Advantages (High-Yield for KTU 2-Mark Questions)**:
> 1. **Better CPU Utilization** — Idle CPU time is minimized by overlapping I/O and computation.
> 2. **Responsiveness** — UI remains interactive while background tasks execute.
> 3. **Resource Sharing** — Threads share heap memory, avoiding costly Inter-Process Communication (IPC).
> 4. **Simpler Program Structure** — Concurrent activities can be modeled naturally as separate threads.
> 5. **Scalability** — On multi-core systems, threads can execute **truly in parallel**.

### 1.4 Process vs Thread — The Core Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Distinction Table (Frequently Asked in University Exams)**:

| Property | Process | Thread |
|----------|---------|--------|
| Memory | Own isolated address space | Shares heap with other threads |
| Creation Cost | **Heavy** (system call, ~1 MB stack) | **Lightweight** (JVM-managed, ~512 KB stack) |
| Communication | IPC (pipes, sockets, shared memory) | Direct shared variable access |
| Context Switch | Expensive | Cheap |
| Crash Impact | Isolated | One thread crash can kill the whole process |
| Java Creation | `ProcessBuilder` | `new Thread()` or `Runnable` |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Java Thread Model — Architectural Breakdown

The Java Thread Model is fundamentally a **M:N hybrid model** mapped onto the underlying operating system. The JVM provides a thread abstraction where each Java thread is bound to one **native OS thread** (on modern HotSpot JVMs using the **1:1 threading model**).

**Structural Layers (Top to Bottom):**
1. **Application Layer** — Your Java code calls `new Thread()` or implements `Runnable`.
2. **Java Thread API Layer** — `java.lang.Thread`, `Object` (wait/notify), `java.util.concurrent`.
3. **JVM Layer** — Thread scheduler, monitor/lock implementation, stack management.
4. **OS Native Thread Layer** — The OS kernel schedules native threads onto CPU cores.

### 2.2 The Six Thread States (Java 17+ Specification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Favourite Diagram Question**: Draw and explain the Thread State Diagram.

A Java thread exists in exactly **one** of these six states at any given moment:

| State | Description | Entry Trigger | Exit Trigger |
|-------|-------------|---------------|--------------|
| **NEW** | Thread object created, `start()` not yet called | `new Thread()` | `start()` invoked |
| **RUNNABLE** | Ready to run or currently running on CPU | `start()` called | Scheduler preempts / enters blocked state |
| **BLOCKED** | Waiting to acquire a **monitor lock** | `synchronized` entry | Lock acquired |
| **WAITING** | Indefinitely waiting for another thread's action | `wait()`, `join()` (no timeout) | `notify()`, `notifyAll()`, target thread terminates |
| **TIMED_WAITING** | Waiting with a time limit | `sleep(ms)`, `wait(ms)`, `join(ms)` | Timeout expires / `notify()` |
| **TERMINATED** | `run()` method has completed or threw uncaught exception | `run()` returns | (Final state) |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Performance Metrics for Multithreading

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Formula for Theory Exams**: Amdahl's Law for Speedup

The theoretical maximum speedup achievable by parallelizing a program across **N** threads:

$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$$

Where:
- $S(N)$ = Speedup factor (how many times faster the parallel version runs)
- $P$ = Proportion of the program that **can be parallelized** (0 ≤ P ≤ 1)
- $N$ = Number of threads / processors
- $(1 - P)$ = Inherently **serial** portion of the program

**Derived: Maximum Theoretical Speedup (as N → ∞):**

$$S_{\max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} S(N) = \frac{1}{1 - P}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineer's Insight**: If only **90%** of a program is parallelizable (P = 0.9), then no matter how many CPU cores you throw at it, the **maximum possible speedup is 10x**. The serial 10% becomes the bottleneck. This is why multithreading alone cannot solve all performance problems.

**Memory Model Formula (Conceptual):**

$$\text{Per-Thread Memory} = \text{Stack Size} + \text{PC} + \text{Registers} \approx 512 \text{ KB} - 1 \text{ MB}$$

$$\text{Shared Memory (Heap)} = \text{Shared among all threads of the process}$$

**Concurrency Efficiency:**

$$E(N) = \frac{S(N)}{N} = \frac{1}{N(1 - P) + P}$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Multithreading Use Case |
|--------|------------------------|
| **Web Servers** (Tomcat, Netty) | Each HTTP request handled by a separate thread from a thread pool |
| **Game Engines** (Unity, Unreal) | Render thread, physics thread, AI thread, audio thread |
| **High-Frequency Trading** | Order matching, market data ingestion, risk calculation in parallel |
| **Scientific Computing** | Matrix multiplication, Monte Carlo simulations parallelized across cores |
| **Android UI** | **Main thread** for UI, **background threads** for network/file I/O |
| **Compilers** (javac) | Lexing, parsing, semantic analysis, code generation on separate threads |

### 2.5 Two Ways to Create a Thread in Java (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Method 1: Extending the `Thread` class**
> - Inherit from `java.lang.Thread`.
> - Override the `public void run()` method.
> - Call `start()` to spawn a new call stack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Method 2: Implementing the `Runnable` interface**
> - Implement `public void run()`.
> - Pass the `Runnable` object to `new Thread(runnable)`.
> - **Preferred approach** because Java does not support multiple class inheritance.

| Aspect | Extending `Thread` | Implementing `Runnable` |
|--------|---------------------|--------------------------|
| Inheritance | Occupies the only inheritance slot | Frees class to extend another |
| Object Reusability | Each thread tied to one object | One `Runnable` shared by many threads |
| OOP Purity | Less pure (inherits for code reuse) | More pure (uses composition) |
| Recommended | Legacy / simple demos | **Industry / KTU preferred** |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Amdahl's Law for Threading Speedup (Full Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Context**: A KTU student writes a program where **75%** of the code can be parallelized using 4 threads. Compute the speedup and efficiency.

**Step 1: Identify the parameters.**

$$P = 0.75, \quad N = 4, \quad (1 - P) = 0.25$$

**Step 2: Substitute into Amdahl's Law.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(4) &= \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}} \\[6pt]
S(4) &= \frac{1}{0.25 + \dfrac{0.75}{4}} \\[6pt]
S(4) &= \frac{1}{0.25 + 0.1875} \\[6pt]
S(4) &= \frac{1}{0.4375} \\[6pt]
S(4) &= 2.2857
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the maximum theoretical speedup (N → ∞).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_{\max} &= \frac{1}{1 - P} \\[6pt]
S_{\max} &= \frac{1}{0.25} \\[6pt]
S_{\max} &= 4.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate the parallel efficiency.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E(4) &= \frac{S(4)}{N} = \frac{2.2857}{4} \\[6pt]
E(4) &= 0.5714 = 57.14\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Engineering Interpretation.**

> [!TIP]
> Going from 4 threads to infinite threads only improves speedup from **2.29x to 4.0x** — less than **2x** improvement despite infinite hardware. The serial 25% portion is the **fundamental bottleneck**. To exceed 4x, the developer must refactor the serial portion.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Step-by-Step Java Thread Creation (Runnable Approach)

> [!NOTE]
> **Complete Model Code for KTU Lab Exam (Frequently Asked)**

**Step 1: Implement the `Runnable` interface.**

```java
class MyTask implements Runnable {
    private String threadName;
    
    public MyTask(String name) {
        this.threadName = name;
    }
    
    @Override
    public void run() {
        // THIS is the thread body
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(threadName + " - Count: " + i);
            try {
                Thread.sleep(500);  // Pause for 500ms
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println(threadName + " interrupted.");
            }
        }
        System.out.println(threadName + " exiting.");
    }
}
```

**Step 2: Instantiate and launch the threads in `main()`.**

```java
public class ThreadDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyTask task1 = new MyTask("Thread-A");
        MyTask task2 = new MyTask("Thread-B");
        
        Thread t1 = new Thread(task1);  // Wrap Runnable in a Thread
        Thread t2 = new Thread(task2);
        
        t1.start();  // Calls run() in a NEW call stack
        t2.start();  // Calls run() in a NEW call stack
        
        // CRITICAL: Calling run() directly would execute
        // sequentially on the main thread (NOT multithreaded).
        
        System.out.println("Main thread finished launching.");
    }
}
```

**Step 3: Trace execution step-by-step.**

| Step | Action | Active Threads | State Transitions |
|------|--------|----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | `new MyTask("Thread-A")` | None | Object created (no thread) |
| 2 | `new Thread(task1)` | None | Thread object in **NEW** state |
| 3 | `t1.start()` | t1 enters **RUNNABLE** pool | NEW → RUNNABLE |
| 4 | `t2.start()` | t1, t2 in **RUNNABLE** pool | NEW → RUNNABLE |
| 5 | JVM scheduler picks t1 | t1 runs, t2 waits | RUNNABLE → RUNNING (implicit) |
| 6 | `Thread.sleep(500)` in t1 | t1 in **TIMED_WAITING**, t2 gets CPU | RUNNING → TIMED_WAITING |
| 7 | Sleep expires | t1 returns to pool | TIMED_WAITING → RUNNABLE |
| 8 | Loop completes, `run()` returns | t1 in **TERMINATED** | RUNNING → TERMINATED |
| 9 | t2 follows same lifecycle | — | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Distinction (Frequently Confused by Students)**:
> - `t1.run()` → **Sequential** call, executes in the **current** thread (main).
> - `t1.start()` → **Concurrent** call, spawns a **new** thread that calls `run()`.

### 3.3 Key Thread Methods — Analytical Reference

| Method | Purpose | Affects Which Thread? | Throws? |
|--------|---------|------------------------|---------|
| `start()` | Spawns new thread, calls `run()` | Creates a new thread | No |
| `run()` | Thread body (entry point) | The calling thread | No |
| `sleep(ms)` | Pause current thread for ms ms | Current thread | `InterruptedException` |
| `join()` | Wait for target thread to die | Current thread (blocks) | `InterruptedException` |
| `yield()` | Hint to scheduler to give up CPU | Current thread | No |
| `interrupt()` | Send interrupt signal | Target thread | — |
| `isAlive()` | Check if thread has not terminated | — | No |
| `setPriority(int)` | Set priority (1–10) | Target thread | `IllegalArgumentException` |
| `setDaemon(boolean)` | Mark as daemon thread | Target thread | — |

**Priority Constants in `Thread` class:**

$$
\text{MIN\_PRIORITY} = 1, \quad \text{NORM\_PRIORITY} = 5, \quad \text{MAX\_PRIORITY} = 10
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Java Thread Lifecycle — State Transition Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the single most important diagram for KTU Module 3 (asked almost every year).**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW : new Thread()
    NEW --> RUNNABLE : start()
    RUNNABLE --> BLOCKED : synchronized lock contention
    BLOCKED --> RUNNABLE : lock acquired
    RUNNABLE --> WAITING : wait() / join() / park()
    WAITING --> RUNNABLE : notify() / notifyAll() / target ends
    RUNNABLE --> TIMED_WAITING : sleep(ms) / wait(ms) / join(ms)
    TIMED_WAITING --> RUNNABLE : timeout expires / notify()
    RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : run() returns / uncaught exception
    TERMINATED --> [*]
```

**Read this as:** A thread begins in **NEW**, moves to **RUNNABLE** when `start()` is called, and may oscillate among **RUNNABLE**, **BLOCKED**, **WAITING**, and **TIMED_WAITING** based on synchronization and scheduler decisions, finally reaching **TERMINATED** when its `run()` method completes.

---

### 4.2 Two Approaches to Thread Creation — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ApproachA["APPROACH A: Extending Thread Class"]
        A1["class MyThread extends Thread"] --> A2["Override public void run()"]
        A2 --> A3["MyThread t = new MyThread()"]
        A3 --> A4["t.start()"]
    end
    
    subgraph ApproachB["APPROACH B: Implementing Runnable"]
        B1["class MyTask implements Runnable"] --> B2["Implement public void run()"]
        B2 --> B3["Thread t = new Thread(new MyTask())"]
        B3 --> B4["t.start()"]
    end
    
    A4 --> Result["OS Schedules New Thread"]
    B4 --> Result
    
    Result --> JVM["JVM Thread Scheduler"]
    JVM --> CPU["CPU Core Execution"]
```

---

### 4.3 Process vs Thread Memory Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Process1["PROCESS 1 (Heavy, Isolated)"]
        P1Heap["Private Heap Memory"]
        P1Code["Code Segment"]
        P1Data["Data Segment"]
    end
    
    subgraph Process2["PROCESS 2 (Java Application)"]
        P2Code["Code Segment"]
        P2Data["Data Segment"]
        P2Heap["Shared Heap Memory"]
        
        subgraph T1["Thread 1"]
            T1Stack["Stack: 512 KB"]
            T1PC["PC + Registers"]
        end
        
        subgraph T2["Thread 2"]
            T2Stack["Stack: 512 KB"]
            T2PC["PC + Registers"]
        end
        
        subgraph T3["Main Thread"]
            T3Stack["Stack: 512 KB"]
            T3PC["PC + Registers"]
        end
    end
    
    P1Heap -.IPC.-> P2Heap
    P2Heap --- T1Stack
    P2Heap --- T2Stack
    P2Heap --- T3Stack
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight**: All three threads (T1, T2, Main) inside Process 2 **share the same heap** but have **independent stacks**. This is why local variables are thread-safe, but instance/static variables are **not**.

---

### 4.4 Thread Priority and Scheduling Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OS["OS-Level Scheduler"]
        HP["High-Priority Queue (8-10)"]
        NP["Normal-Priority Queue (5-7)"]
        LP["Low-Priority Queue (1-4)"]
    end
    
    CPU["Single CPU Core"] --> HP
    HP --> NP
    NP --> LP
    LP --> Done["Thread Blocked / Terminated"]
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Note for KTU Exams**: Java thread priorities are **hints to the OS scheduler, not guarantees**. On Windows, priority is roughly respected. On Linux/macOS, the JVM priority often maps to `nice` values, and **starvation can occur** if low-priority threads never get CPU time.

---

### 4.5 Daemon vs User Thread — Lifecycle Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Main["JVM starts (main is User Thread)"]
    Main --> U1["User Thread 1 (e.g., HTTP handler)"]
    Main --> U2["User Thread 2 (e.g., business logic)"]
    Main --> D1["Daemon Thread (e.g., GC, AutoSaver)"]
    Main --> D2["Daemon Thread (e.g., log flusher)"]
    
    U1 --> AliveCheck{"All User Threads Dead?"}
    U2 --> AliveCheck
    D1 --> AliveCheck
    D2 --> AliveCheck
    
    AliveCheck -->|Yes| Kill["JVM kills all Daemon Threads and exits"]
    AliveCheck -->|No| Continue["JVM continues running"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU-Favoured Question)**: A **Daemon thread** is a low-priority background thread (e.g., Garbage Collector) that does **not prevent the JVM from exiting**. The JVM terminates itself when **only daemon threads remain alive**. Set via `t.setDaemon(true)` **before** `t.start()`.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a process and a thread. Mention any two advantages of multithreading.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Process | Thread |
|-----------|---------|--------|
| Definition | Independent program in execution | Lightweight sub-unit of a process |
| Memory | Own isolated address space | Shares heap memory of the parent process |
| Creation overhead | High (system call) | Low (JVM managed) |
| Communication | IPC (pipes, sockets) | Shared variables |

**Two advantages of multithreading (2 marks):**
1. **Better CPU utilization** — Idle time during I/O can be used to run other threads.
2. **Responsiveness** — Long-running tasks (e.g., file download) do not freeze the UI; they run in a background thread.
3. *Optional*: Resource sharing via shared heap; cheaper context switching.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Explain the lifecycle of a thread in Java with a neat state diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Java thread passes through the following six states:

1. **NEW** — Thread object is created using `new Thread()`, but `start()` is not yet called.
2. **RUNNABLE** — After `start()` is invoked, the thread is ready and waiting in the scheduler's pool (or currently running).
3. **BLOCKED** — Thread is waiting to acquire a monitor lock (e.g., waiting to enter a `synchronized` block).
4. **WAITING** — Thread is waiting indefinitely for another thread's action (e.g., via `wait()`, `join()` without timeout).
5. **TIMED_WAITING** — Thread is waiting for a specified time (e.g., `Thread.sleep(1000)`, `wait(1000)`).
6. **TERMINATED** — Thread has finished execution (`run()` method returned or threw uncaught exception). This is a **final state**.

> The state diagram (as shown in SECTION 4.1) summarizes all valid transitions.

---

### 📕 PART B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a) Explain the Java Thread Model in detail. Discuss the two ways of creating threads in Java with suitable examples. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Java Thread Model (3 marks):**

The Java Thread Model is a programming abstraction provided by the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** that enables a Java program to execute **multiple threads concurrently** within a single process. The model defines:

- A thread as an independent path of execution.
- **Shared memory model** — all threads of a process share the **heap** (object memory) and **method area** (class metadata).
- **Private per-thread memory** — each thread has its own **stack**, **program counter (PC)**, and **local registers**.
- **Native thread mapping** — on modern HotSpot JVMs, each Java thread maps **1:1** to an OS native thread (the **1:1 threading model**).
- **Pre-emptive scheduling** — the OS scheduler decides which runnable thread gets the CPU.

**2. Two Ways to Create Threads (4 marks):**

> **Method 1: Extending the `Thread` class**

```java
class MyThread extends Thread {
    public void run() {
        System.out.println("Thread is running: " + getName());
    }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyThread t = new MyThread();
        t.start();   // Creates new call stack and invokes run()
    }
}
```

> **Method 2: Implementing the `Runnable` interface** *(Preferred)*

```java
class MyRunnable implements Runnable {
    public void run() {
        System.out.println("Runnable thread: " + Thread.currentThread().getName());
    }
}

public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Thread t = new Thread(new MyRunnable());
        t.start();
    }
}
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Justification for preferring Method 2** (1 mark): Implementing `Runnable` is preferred because Java does not support multiple class inheritance. By implementing `Runnable`, the class is free to extend another class. Additionally, a single `Runnable` instance can be passed to **multiple `Thread` objects**, promoting object reuse.

---

**(b) With a neat state diagram, describe the various states in the lifecycle of a thread. Write a Java program that creates two threads, where one thread prints even numbers and the other prints odd numbers from 1 to 20. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Thread State Diagram (3 marks):**
