Refer to the diagram shown in **SECTION 4.1**. The six states are: **NEW → RUNNABLE ↔ BLOCKED / WAITING / TIMED_WAITING → TERMINATED**.

**2. Java Program (4 marks):**

```java
class NumberPrinter implements Runnable {
    private boolean printEven;
    
    public NumberPrinter(boolean printEven) {
        this.printEven = printEven;
    }
    
    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 20; i++) {
            if (printEven && i % 2 == 0) {
                System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + ": " + i);
            } else if (!printEven && i % 2 != 0) {
                System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + ": " + i);
            }
            try {
                Thread.sleep(100);  // Slow down to allow interleaving
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println("Interrupted.");
            }
        }
    }
}

public class EvenOddDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Thread even = new Thread(new NumberPrinter(true),  "Even-Thread");
        Thread odd  = new Thread(new NumberPrinter(false), "Odd-Thread");
        
        even.start();
        odd.start();
    }
}
```

**Explanation (1 mark):** Two threads share the heap. The `NumberPrinter` is instantiated twice — one with `printEven = true`, another with `false`. Both threads are started and the scheduler interleaves their output, producing an unpredictable but valid sequence of even and odd numbers between 1 and 20.

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #1**:
> Students often call `run()` instead of `start()`. This results in **sequential execution** on the main thread — the program loses all multithreaded behaviour. **Always use `start()`** to spawn a new thread.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain Amdahl's Law for parallel speedup. A program spends 80% of its time in parallelizable code. Calculate the maximum speedup achievable using 8 threads. Also compute the efficiency. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Amdahl's Law — Theory (3 marks):**

Amdahl's Law, formulated by **Gene Amdahl in 1967**, gives the theoretical speedup of a program when only a fraction `P` of it is parallelized across `N` processors/threads:

$$S(N) = \frac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$$

The law illustrates a fundamental limitation: the **serial portion** $(1 - P)$ of the program acts as a bottleneck. Even with infinite processors, the maximum speedup is bounded by:

$$S_{\max} = \frac{1}{1 - P}$$

**2. Numerical Calculation (4 marks):**

Given: $P = 0.80$, $N = 8$, $(1 - P) = 0.20$.

**Speedup with 8 threads:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(8) &= \frac{1}{(1 - 0.80) + \dfrac{0.80}{8}} \\[6pt]
S(8) &= \frac{1}{0.20 + 0.10} \\[6pt]
S(8) &= \frac{1}{0.30} \\[6pt]
S(8) &= 3.333
\end{aligned}
$$

**Maximum theoretical speedup (N → ∞):**

$$S_{\max} = \frac{1}{0.20} = 5.0$$

**Parallel efficiency with 8 threads:**

$$E(8) = \frac{S(8)}{N} = \frac{3.333}{8} = 0.4166 = 41.67\%$$

**Engineering Interpretation (1 mark):** Even with 8 threads, we only get **3.33x** speedup, not 8x. The 20% serial portion caps the maximum theoretical speedup at 5x. To go faster, the serial part of the program must be optimized or parallelized.

---

**(b) Write a Java program that demonstrates the use of `Thread` class methods: `start()`, `run()`, `sleep()`, `join()`, `setPriority()`, and `getName()`. Use two threads named "Worker-1" and "Worker-2". (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
public class ThreadMethodsDemo extends Thread {
    
    public ThreadMethodsDemo(String name) {
        super(name);  // Set thread name via Thread(String name) constructor
    }
    
    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " [Priority=" + getPriority() + "] - Step " + i);
            try {
                Thread.sleep(400);  // Pause for 400 ms
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println(getName() + " was interrupted.");
            }
        }
        System.out.println(getName() + " has finished.");
    }
    
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ThreadMethodsDemo t1 = new ThreadMethodsDemo("Worker-1");
        ThreadMethodsDemo t2 = new ThreadMethodsDemo("Worker-2");
        
        // Setting priorities BEFORE starting
        t1.setPriority(Thread.MIN_PRIORITY);   // 1
        t2.setPriority(Thread.MAX_PRIORITY);   // 10
        
        System.out.println("Starting threads...");
        t1.start();   // Spawns new call stack
        t2.start();
        
        try {
            t1.join();  // main waits for t1 to terminate
            t2.join();  // main waits for t2 to terminate
        } catch (InterruptedException e) {
            System.out.println("Main thread interrupted.");
        }
        
        System.out.println("Both workers finished. Main exits.");
    }
}
```

**Explanation of method usage (3 marks):**

| Method | Usage in Program | Purpose |
|--------|------------------|---------|
| `start()` | `t1.start()` | Spawns a new thread; **must** be used to enable multithreading |
| `run()` | Overridden in subclass | Contains the thread body executed by the new thread |
| `sleep(400)` | Inside `run()` loop | Pauses current thread for 400 ms (moves to TIMED_WAITING) |
| `join()` | In `main()` | `main` blocks until t1/t2 finish; guarantees orderly termination |
| `setPriority(int)` | `t1.setPriority(MIN_PRIORITY)` | Suggests scheduler preference; t2 gets higher CPU preference |
| `getName()` | In `run()` print statement | Returns the thread's name set via the constructor |

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall #2**:
> Students frequently call `setPriority()` **after** `start()`. While not strictly illegal, it is **best practice** to set priority *before* starting the thread, and KTU expects you to mention this. Additionally, priority is a **hint only** — never a guarantee of scheduling order.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 3 — Multithreaded Programming: Thread Model | KTU 2024 High-Yield Cheat Sheet**

**🔑 Definitions (Must Memorize Verbatim):**
- ✅ **Thread**: Smallest unit of execution; lightweight sub-process sharing the parent's memory.
- ✅ **Multithreading**: Concurrent execution of multiple threads within a single program.
- ✅ **Process**: Independent program with its own memory space.
- ✅ **Daemon Thread**: Background thread (e.g., GC) that does not block JVM shutdown.

**🔑 Two Thread Creation Methods:**
- ✅ **Method 1**: `extends Thread` → override `run()` → call `start()`.
- ✅ **Method 2**: `implements Runnable` → implement `run()` → wrap in `new Thread(r)` → call `start()`.
- ✅ **Preferred**: Method 2 (saves inheritance slot, allows object reuse).

**🔑 Six Thread States:**
- ✅ **NEW** → created, not started.
- ✅ **RUNNABLE** → ready or running.
- ✅ **BLOCKED** → waiting for monitor lock.
- ✅ **WAITING** → indefinite wait (`wait()`, `join()` no timeout).
- ✅ **TIMED_WAITING** → timed wait (`sleep(ms)`, `wait(ms)`).
- ✅ **TERMINATED** → `run()` finished (final state).

**🔑 Critical `Thread` Methods:**
- ✅ `start()` — **spawns new thread**; calling `run()` directly does NOT multithread.
- ✅ `sleep(ms)` — pauses current thread; static method.
- ✅ `join()` — current thread waits for target thread to die.
- ✅ `yield()` — hint to scheduler; may be ignored.
- ✅ `setPriority(int)` — value 1 (MIN) to 10 (MAX); default 5 (NORM).
- ✅ `setDaemon(true)` — must be called **before** `start()`.

**🔑 Amdahl's Law (Mandatory Formula):**
- ✅ $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - P) + \dfrac{P}{N}}$
- ✅ $S_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{1 - P}$ (as N → ∞)
- ✅ $E(N) = \dfrac{S(N)}{N}$

**🔑 Common Viva / Short-Answer Questions:**
- ✅ Difference between `start()` and `run()`?
- ✅ Difference between `wait()` and `sleep()`?
- ✅ Difference between `wait()` and `join()`?
- ✅ Why is `Runnable` preferred over extending `Thread`?
- ✅ What is a daemon thread? Give an example.
- ✅ Can we start a thread twice? *(Answer: No — throws `IllegalThreadStateException`)*

**🔑 Pitfalls to Avoid in KTU Exams:**
- ❌ Don't call `run()` instead of `start()`.
- ❌ Don't claim thread priority guarantees execution order.
- ❌ Don't forget that threads share **heap** but have **private stacks**.
- ❌ Don't call `setDaemon(true)` after `start()`.
- ❌ Don't claim multithreading gives linear speedup (use Amdahl's Law).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Creating Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — CREATING THREADS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> A **Thread** in Java is the smallest unit of execution within a program (process). It is an independent path of execution that shares the same memory space (heap) of the process but maintains its own **stack**, **program counter**, and **local variables**. Java provides built-in, first-class support for multithreading through the `java.lang.Thread` class and the `java.lang.Runnable` interface.

### 🔑 Conceptual Analogy — "The Kitchen Restaurant"

Imagine a busy restaurant kitchen preparing 100 orders:

- **Single-Threaded Program** = One chef cooking one order at a time. Order 5 waits until orders 1–4 are done. Slow! 🐢
- **Multi-Threaded Program** = Five chefs (threads) working **simultaneously** in the **same kitchen** (shared memory). Each chef has their own cutting board (stack), but they all share the fridge, stove, and pantry (heap). Orders get cooked in parallel! 🚀

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does KTU emphasize this topic?**
> Multithreading is the **backbone of modern Java applications** — from server-side Spring Boot request handling, Android UI rendering, to real-time data processing. In your KTU exam, you will be tested on **how** to create a thread (syntax), **why** we prefer one method over the other (theory), and **what** happens internally (lifecycle).

### 🏛️ The Two Official Ways to Create a Thread (KTU 2024 Scheme)

The Java API offers **exactly two** sanctioned pathways for thread creation. Both ultimately depend on the `Thread` class, but the **entry point** differs.

| # | Method | Entry Point | Extends / Implements | Recommended? |
|---|--------|-------------|----------------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Extending `Thread` class** | Override `public void run()` | `extends Thread` | ⚠️ Limited |
| 2 | **Implementing `Runnable` interface** | Implement `public void run()` | `implements Runnable` | ✅ **Industry Standard** |

> [!TIP]
> **Golden Rule (from Effective Java, Bloch):** *"Favor composition over inheritance."* Because Java does not support multiple class inheritance, if you extend `Thread`, your class **cannot** extend any other class. This is why KTU examiners **love** asking why `Runnable` is preferred!

### 📂 Package & Class Location

- `java.lang.Thread` — the concrete thread class
- `java.lang.Runnable` — the functional interface (single method: `void run()`)
- No imports are needed because both reside in `java.lang` (auto-imported).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard metric (Java spec):** Thread priorities are integers in the range **1 (MIN_PRIORITY)** to **10 (MAX_PRIORITY)**, with **5 (NORM_PRIORITY)** as the default. The JVM *may* map these to the underlying OS scheduler (e.g., Windows, Linux), but **priority does not guarantee execution order** — it is purely a *hint* to the scheduler.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 🧠 The "Why" Behind Thread Creation

Every Java program starts with **at least one thread** — the `main` thread, which the JVM spawns automatically to execute your `main(String[] args)` method. When you create additional threads, you essentially ask the JVM to allocate **a new stack frame and program counter** for each thread, all pointing into the same heap memory.

### 🔬 Method 1 — Extending the `Thread` Class

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Declare a subclass of `java.lang.Thread`.
2. Override the `public void run()` method — this is the **entry point** of the new thread.
3. Instantiate your subclass: `MyThread t = new MyThread();`
4. Invoke `t.start();` — **NOT** `t.run()`! `start()` allocates a fresh stack and calls `run()` on the *new* thread. Calling `run()` directly executes on the *current* (caller's) thread — a classic KTU trick question! 🎯

**Code Skeleton:**
```java
class MyThread extends Thread {
    @Override
    public void run() {
        // code to execute in new thread
        System.out.println("Running in: " + Thread.currentThread().getName());
    }
}

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyThread t1 = new MyThread();
        t1.start();   // ✅ creates new thread
        // t1.run();  // ❌ would run on main thread (common mistake)
    }
}
```

**Constructor Variants of `Thread` (KTU-Favorite):**
- `Thread()`
- `Thread(String name)`
- `Thread(Runnable target)`
- `Thread(Runnable target, String name)`
- `Thread(ThreadGroup group, Runnable target)`
- `Thread(ThreadGroup group, String name)`
- `Thread(ThreadGroup group, Runnable target, String name)`
- `Thread(ThreadGroup group, Runnable target, String name, long stackSize)`

### 🔬 Method 2 — Implementing the `Runnable` Interface

**Operational Logic Steps:**
1. Declare a class that `implements Runnable`.
2. Provide an implementation for `public void run()`.
3. Create a `Thread` object, **passing your `Runnable` instance** to its constructor.
4. Call `t.start()` on the `Thread` object.

**Code Skeleton:**
```java
class MyTask implements Runnable {
    @Override
    public void run() {
        System.out.println("Running in: " + Thread.currentThread().getName());
    }
}

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Thread t = new Thread(new MyTask());
        t.start();
    }
}
```

**Lambda Shortcut (Java 8+):** Since `Runnable` is a *functional interface*, you can use a lambda:
```java
Thread t = new Thread(() -> System.out.println("Hello from lambda thread!"));
t.start();
```

### 📊 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — `Thread` Class Methods

| Method | Signature | Purpose | Static? |
|--------|-----------|---------|---------|
| `start()` | `void start()` | Spawns new thread, calls `run()` | No |
| `run()` | `void run()` | Entry point logic | No |
| `currentThread()` | `static Thread currentThread()` | Returns reference to currently executing thread | ✅ Yes |
| `getName()` / `setName()` | `String` / `void` | Read / set thread name | No |
| `getId()` | `long getId()` | Unique thread ID assigned by JVM | No |
| `getPriority()` / `setPriority(int)` | `int` / `void` | Read / set priority (1–10) | No |
| `isAlive()` | `boolean` | Tests if thread is still running | No |
| `sleep(long ms)` | `static void sleep(long)` | Pauses current thread for *at least* ms milliseconds | ✅ Yes |
| `join()` | `void join()` | Caller waits for this thread to die | No |
| `yield()` | `static void yield()` | Hints scheduler to give other threads a chance | ✅ Yes |
| `interrupt()` | `void interrupt()` | Sends interrupt signal to thread | No |
| `isDaemon()` / `setDaemon(boolean)` | `boolean` / `void` | Daemon thread flag (background services) | No |
| `getState()` | `Thread.State` | Returns one of 6 states (Java 5+) | No |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **`start()` vs `run()` — THE KTU Classic Trap:**
> - `t.start()` → creates a **new call stack**; `run()` executes on the **new thread**. JVM calls `run()` internally.
> - `t.run()` → executes `run()` on the **current thread (main)**; no new thread is created.
> - Calling `start()` **twice** on the same `Thread` object throws `IllegalThreadStateException`. 🚨

### 🌀 Thread Lifecycle States (Java `Thread.State` enum — 6 states)

1. **NEW** — `Thread` object created, `start()` not yet called.
2. **RUNNABLE** — `start()` invoked; thread is ready/actively running on the JVM.
3. **BLOCKED** — Waiting to acquire a monitor lock (synchronized block entry).
4. **WAITING** — `wait()`, `join()`, `LockSupport.park()` — indefinitely until notified.
5. **TIMED_WAITING** — `sleep(ms)`, `wait(ms)`, `join(ms)` — for a specified time.
6. **TERMINATED** — `run()` has completed; thread cannot be restarted.

### ⚖️ `Thread` vs `Runnable` — Decision Matrix

| Criterion | `extends Thread` | `implements Runnable` |
|-----------|------------------|------------------------|
| Multiple inheritance | ❌ Blocked (already extends Thread) | ✅ Class can extend another class |
| Object reusability | ❌ Each task = new Thread object | ✅ One `Runnable` can be passed to many `Thread` objects |
| Inheritance design | Tight coupling | Loose coupling (separation of task from thread) |
| Lambda compatibility | ❌ Cannot use lambda directly | ✅ Single-method interface, lambda-friendly |
| KTU recommendation | Use only when overriding `Thread` behavior (e.g., priority logic) | ✅ **Preferred for 90% of cases** |

### 🌍 Real-World Utility
- **Web servers (Tomcat, Jetty):** Each HTTP request handled in its own thread from a thread pool.
- **Android UI:** The main thread (UI thread) must never be blocked — background work goes to worker threads.
- **Real-time trading systems:** Order matching, price feeds, and analytics run in concurrent threads.
- **Game engines:** Rendering, physics, AI, and audio run in separate threads.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 🧪 Analytical Matrix — Constructing a Thread: Both Methods Compared

Below is a complete, exhaustive analytical walkthrough of how a thread is *actually* constructed and scheduled. The Java language specification guarantees the following sequence when `start()` is invoked.

#### **Step 1 — Allocate the `Thread` object in the heap**
$$\text{heap} \leftarrow \text{allocate}(Thread, \text{sizeOf(Thread instance)})$$

**Explanation:** Calling `new MyThread()` or `new Thread(runnable)` allocates memory for the object on the **shared heap**, with all instance fields (e.g., `name`, `priority`, `target`) initialized to default values. At this moment, the thread is in the **NEW** state.

#### **Step 2 — Configure thread properties (optional)**
$$\text{thread.name} \leftarrow \text{user-supplied string} \quad \text{or} \quad \text{default: "Thread-"} \oplus \text{auto-increment integer}$$

$$\text{thread.priority} \leftarrow \begin{cases} 5 & \text{(default NORM\_PRIORITY)} \\ 1 \le p \le 10 & \text{(if setPriority called, else unchanged)} \end{cases}$$

**Explanation:** Property setters like `setName()`, `setPriority()`, and `setDaemon()` only mutate the object fields. The thread has **not** started yet — it is still in NEW state.

#### **Step 3 — Invoke `t.start()` — the critical transition**

**Mathematical/Logical Model:**
$$t.\text{start}() \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{state}(t) \;\leftarrow\; \text{RUNNABLE}$$

Internally, `start()` performs three sub-operations:
1. **State check:** $\text{if } \text{state}(t) \neq \text{NEW } \Rightarrow \text{throw IllegalThreadStateException}$
2. **JVM-level registration:** Registers the new thread with the JVM thread manager.
3. **Native `start0()` call:** Invokes the platform-dependent method (written in C/C++) to spawn an OS thread, which will then invoke `t.run()` once the scheduler picks it up.

#### **Step 4 — Scheduler picks the thread → `run()` executes**

When the OS scheduler allocates CPU to the new thread:
$$t.\text{run}() \;\Longrightarrow\; \text{new stack frame pushed onto thread's private call stack}$$

**Explanation:** A *new call stack* is allocated, separate from `main`'s stack. All local variables, method arguments, and return addresses are stored here. This is the physical reason why threads are "independent paths of execution."

#### **Step 5 — `run()` completes → state transition to TERMINATED**
$$\text{state}(t) \;\leftarrow\; \text{TERMINATED}$$

The thread's stack is deallocated, and the `Thread` object remains in the heap (now an ordinary garbage-collectible object).

---

### 📐 Worked Example — Predicting Output (KTU-Style)

**Question:** What is the output of the following program? Justify.

```java
class MyThread extends Thread {
    public MyThread(String name) { super(name); }
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++)
            System.out.println(getName() + " : " + i);
    }
}
public class Test {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyThread t1 = new MyThread("Alpha");
        MyThread t2 = new MyThread("Beta");
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
        System.out.println("Main done");
    }
}
```

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

| Step | Thread | Action | State |
|------|--------|--------|-------|
| 1 | main | `new MyThread("Alpha")` | t1 in NEW |
| 2 | main | `new MyThread("Beta")` | t2 in NEW |
| 3 | main | `t1.start()` | t1 → RUNNABLE, main continues |
| 4 | main | `t2.start()` | t2 → RUNNABLE, main continues |
| 5 | main | prints `"Main done"` | — |
| 6 | scheduler | t1 may print `Alpha : 1`, `Alpha : 2`, `Alpha : 3` | RUNNABLE |
| 7 | scheduler | t2 may print `Beta : 1`, `Beta : 2`, `Beta : 3` | RUNNABLE |

**Key KTU Insight:** The output **order is non-deterministic** (interleaving depends on JVM/OS scheduler). The only guarantee is that `main done` will appear *eventually* (possibly first, possibly last). This is why you should **never write exam answers assuming a fixed order** unless `join()` is used.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **If the question had used `t1.run(); t2.run();`** — the output would be deterministic: `Alpha : 1, Alpha : 2, Alpha : 3, Beta : 1, Beta : 2, Beta : 3, Main done`, all on the **main thread** (single-threaded execution). This is a *favourite* KTU trick question! 🎯

---

### 🧮 Lifecycle Transition Equation (Conceptual)

For a thread lifecycle, the state transitions can be modeled as a finite-state automaton:

$$\text{State}(t) \;\in\; \{\text{NEW}, \text{RUNNABLE}, \text{BLOCKED}, \text{WAITING}, \text{TIMED\_WAITING}, \text{TERMINATED}\}$$

**Transitions:**
- $\text{NEW} \xrightarrow{\text{start()}} \text{RUNNABLE}$
- $\text{RUNNABLE} \xrightarrow{\text{acquire lock fail}} \text{BLOCKED}$
- $\text{RUNNABLE} \xrightarrow{\text{wait() / join() / park()}} \text{WAITING}$
- $\text{RUNNABLE} \xrightarrow{\text{sleep(ms) / wait(ms) / join(ms)}} \text{TIMED\_WAITING}$
- $\text{WAITING / TIMED\_WAITING} \xrightarrow{\text{notify() / timeout / interrupt}} \text{RUNNABLE}$
- $\text{BLOCKED} \xrightarrow{\text{lock acquired}} \text{RUNNABLE}$
- $\text{RUNNABLE} \xrightarrow{\text{run() returns}} \text{TERMINATED}$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 🗺️ Diagram 1 — Two Ways to Create Threads (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Create Thread in Java] --> B{Choose Strategy}
    B -->|Strategy 1| C[Declare class extends Thread]
    B -->|Strategy 2| D[Declare class implements Runnable]

    C --> E[Override public void run method]
    D --> F[Implement public void run method]

    E --> G[Create instance: MyThread obj = new MyThread]
    F --> H[Create Runnable target: MyTask task = new MyTask]
    H --> I[Wrap in Thread: Thread t = new Thread task]

    G --> J[Call obj.start]
    I --> J

    J --> K{start method}
    K --> L[Allocate new call stack in JVM]
    L --> M[Invoke run method on NEW thread]
    M --> N[Thread executes concurrently with main]
    N --> O[run method returns]
    O --> P[State changes to TERMINATED]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style K fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P fill:#D3D3D3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 🗺️ Diagram 2 — Thread Lifecycle State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW : new Thread created
    NEW --> RUNNABLE : start method called
    RUNNABLE --> BLOCKED : waiting for monitor lock
    BLOCKED --> RUNNABLE : lock acquired
    RUNNABLE --> WAITING : wait or join or park called
    WAITING --> RUNNABLE : notified or unparked
    RUNNABLE --> TIMED_WAITING : sleep or wait timeout or join timeout
    TIMED_WAITING --> RUNNABLE : timeout expires
    RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : run method returns
    TERMINATED --> [*]
```

### 🗺️ Diagram 3 — Memory Architecture: Single-Thread vs Multi-Thread

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ST[Single Threaded Program]
        ST1[Heap - Shared Objects] --- ST2[Stack - main thread only]
    end

    subgraph MT[Multi Threaded Program]
        MT1[Heap - Shared Objects - all threads see this]
        MT2[Stack - main thread]
        MT3[Stack - thread t1]
        MT4[Stack - thread t2]
        MT1 -.shared.-> MT2
        MT1 -.shared.-> MT3
        MT1 -.shared.-> MT4
    end

    style ST fill:#FFE4E1
    style MT fill:#E0FFFF
    style ST1 fill:#FFA07A
    style ST2 fill:#FFA07A
    style MT1 fill:#FFA07A
    style MT2 fill:#98FB98
    style MT3 fill:#98FB98
    style MT4 fill:#98FB98
```

### 🗺️ Diagram 4 — `start()` vs `run()` Call Stack Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[What happens when you call t.start] --> R1[JVM allocates NEW call stack]
    R1 --> S1[OS thread spawned]
    S1 --> T1[run method executes on NEW stack]
    T1 --> U1[Output: separate thread name]

    Q2[What happens when you call t.run] --> R2[No new stack allocated]
    R2 --> S2[run method executes on CALLERS stack]
    S2 --> T2[Output: main thread name]
    T2 --> U2[Program remains single threaded]

    style Q1 fill:#90EE90
    style Q2 fill:#FFB6C1
    style U1 fill:#90EE90
    style U2 fill:#FFB6C1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Explain the two ways of creating a thread in Java. State which is preferred and why.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Way 1 — Extending the `Thread` class:** Define a subclass of `java.lang.Thread` and override its `run()` method. The new thread is started by calling `start()` on an instance of this subclass.

**Way 2 — Implementing the `Runnable` interface:** Define a class that implements `java.lang.Runnable` and provides a `run()` method. Create a `Thread` object by passing the `Runnable` instance to the `Thread` constructor, then call `start()` on the `Thread` object.

**Preferred method:** Implementing `Runnable` is preferred because (i) Java does not support multiple inheritance, so extending `Thread` prevents the class from extending any other useful class; (ii) it separates the *task* (what to do) from the *thread* (the worker), giving better object-oriented design; (iii) the same `Runnable` instance can be reused across multiple `Thread` objects.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between `start()` and `run()` methods of the `Thread` class.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `start()` | `run()` |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| Thread creation | ✅ Creates a new thread with its own call stack | ❌ Does **not** create a new thread |
| Where executed | On the **new** thread | On the **current** (caller's) thread |
| Internal call | Invokes `start0()` (native) → scheduler picks up → calls `run()` | Directly executes the method body |
| Re-invocation | Cannot be called twice on the same object (throws `IllegalThreadStateException`) | Can be called any number of times |
| Purpose | Used to begin concurrent execution | Used as the *entry point* for thread logic |

Calling `run()` directly defeats the entire purpose of multithreading — the code runs sequentially on the main thread.

---

### 📝 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **🛑 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Writing `t.run()` instead of `t.start()`** — instant 2-mark deduction; examiner assumes you don't understand thread creation.
> 2. **Forgetting `@Override` annotation** — minor, but in KTU scripts, omitting it may cost you 0.5 mark.
> 3. **Confusing constructor signatures** — `Thread(Runnable)` vs `Thread(String)` is a common trap; the compiler distinguishes them, but students mix up the order of arguments.
> 4. **Not explaining *why* `Runnable` is preferred** — KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes design rationale, not just syntax.
> 5. **Writing code without indentation or comments** — examiner-friendly formatting earns you "grace marks."

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the two ways of creating threads in Java with suitable code examples. Compare them in terms of inheritance, reusability, and design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that creates two threads, where one thread prints even numbers from 1 to 20 and the other prints odd numbers from 1 to 20. Use the `Runnable` interface. Explain the expected output behavior. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Two Ways of Creating Threads (7 marks):**

**Method 1: Extending `Thread` class**

```java
class MyThread extends Thread {
    private String message;
    public MyThread(String msg) { this.message = msg; }
    
    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() 
                               + " : " + message + " " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(100); } 
            catch (InterruptedException e) { }
        }
    }
}
```

**Method 2: Implementing `Runnable` interface**

```java
class MyTask implements Runnable {
    private String message;
    public MyTask(String msg) { this.message = msg; }
    
    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() 
                               + " : " + message + " " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(100); } 
            catch (InterruptedException e) { }
        }
    }
}
```

**Comparison Table (for the 3 sub-marks):**

| Criterion | Extends Thread | Implements Runnable |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|
| Inheritance | Locks the class — cannot extend anything else | Class is free to extend another class |
| Object design | Task and thread tightly coupled | Task and thread loosely coupled |
| Reusability | One instance = one thread | Same `Runnable` can be passed to many `Thread` objects |
| Best for | Overriding `Thread`-specific behavior | Most general-purpose threading |

---

**(b) — Program for Even/Odd Threads (7 marks):**

```java
class EvenPrinter implements Runnable {
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 2; i <= 20; i += 2) {
            System.out.println("Even : " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(50); }
            catch (InterruptedException e) { }
        }
    }
}

class OddPrinter implements Runnable {
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 20; i += 2) {
            System.out.println("Odd  : " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(50); }
            catch (InterruptedException e) { }
        }
    }
}

public class EvenOddDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Thread t1 = new Thread(new EvenPrinter(), "EvenThread");
        Thread t2 = new Thread(new OddPrinter(), "OddThread");
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
    }
}
```

**Expected Output Behavior (2 marks):**
The output will be **interleaved** in a non-deterministic order, e.g.:

```
Odd  : 1
Even : 2
Odd  : 3
Even : 4
...
```

The exact interleaving depends on the JVM thread scheduler and the OS. There is **no guarantee** of strict alternation unless synchronization is added (e.g., using `wait()` and `notify()` — a Module 4 topic).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the lifecycle of a thread in Java. Illustrate the different states a thread passes through with a neat diagram. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate thread creation by extending the `Thread` class. The thread should display the multiplication table of 5 with a delay of 200 ms between successive numbers. Also display the name of the thread using `currentThread()`. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Thread Lifecycle (7 marks):**

A thread in Java passes through **six states** defined in `Thread.State` enum:

1. **NEW** — Thread object is created but `start()` has not been called.
2. **RUNNABLE** — After `start()` is called, the thread is ready to run or running.
3. **BLOCKED** — Thread is waiting to acquire a monitor lock (entering a `synchronized` block).
4. **WAITING** — Thread is waiting indefinitely (e.g., via `wait()`, `join()`, `park()`).
5. **TIMED_WAITING** — Thread is waiting for a specified time (e.g., `sleep(200)`, `wait(500)`).
6. **TERMINATED** — `run()` has finished executing; thread cannot be restarted.

**Diagram (as previously shown in Section 4)** — refer to the state machine diagram above. *(Examiner tip: hand-drawing the diagram in the answer sheet is fine; you do not need to print it.)*

**Key transition triggers:**
- `start()` moves NEW → RUNNABLE
- `sleep(ms)`, `wait(ms)`, `join(ms)` move RUNNABLE → TIMED_WAITING
- `wait()`, `join()`, `park()` move RUNNABLE → WAITING
- Completing `run()` moves any state → TERMINATED

---

**(b) — Multiplication Table Program (7 marks):**

```java
class TableThread extends Thread {
    public TableThread(String name) {
        super(name); // sets thread name
    }
    
    @Override
    public void run() {
        System.out.println("Thread Name: " 
                           + Thread.currentThread().getName());
        for (int i = 1; i <= 10; i++) {
            System.out.println("5 x " + i + " = " + (5 * i));
            try {
                Thread.sleep(200); // 200 ms delay
            } catch (InterruptedException e) {
                System.out.println("Thread interrupted: " 
                                   + e.getMessage());
            }
        }
    }
}

public class TableDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        TableThread t = new TableThread("Five-Table-Thread");
        t.start();
        System.out.println("Main thread continues...");
    }
}
```

**Sample Output:**
```
Main thread continues...
Thread Name: Five-Table-Thread
5 x 1 = 5
5 x 2 = 10
5 x 3 = 15
...
5 x 10 = 50
```

The line `Main thread continues...` may appear **before** or **after** the thread's output lines because `start()` returns immediately to `main` while the new thread runs concurrently.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔑 Top 10 Things You MUST Memorize for KTU 2024 Exam:**

1. ✅ **Two ways to create threads:** `extends Thread` and `implements Runnable`.
2. ✅ **`Runnable` is preferred** because of multiple-inheritance restriction and loose coupling.
3. ✅ **Always call `start()`, NEVER `run()`** for multithreading — calling `run()` executes on the current thread.
4. ✅ **`start()` cannot be called twice** on the same `Thread` object — `IllegalThreadStateException`.
5. ✅ **`Runnable` is a functional interface** (single abstract method `run()`) — supports lambda expressions.
6. ✅ **Thread priority range:** 1 (MIN) to 10 (MAX), default = 5 (NORM). Priority is a *hint*, not a guarantee.
7. ✅ **`Thread.currentThread()` is a static method** that returns the currently executing thread reference.
8. ✅ **Six lifecycle states:** NEW, RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING, TERMINATED.
9. ✅ **Threads share heap memory** but each has its **own stack** — this is why local variables are thread-safe.
10. ✅ **`Thread` constructors accepting `Runnable`** allow you to pass any task to a thread — the standard pattern.

> [!TIP]
> **📚 One-Line Mantra for Viva:**
> *"A thread is an independent path of execution sharing the heap but owning its own stack — and in Java, you create one by either extending `Thread` or implementing `Runnable`, always calling `start()` to launch it."*

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 Final KTU 2024 Scheme Reminder:**
> - Always write **complete, compilable Java code** in your answer sheets.
> - **Explain the `why`**, not just the `how` — KTU 2024 scheme uses OBE (Outcome-Based Education), and part-marks are awarded for rationale.
> - For 14-mark questions, **include a diagram or comparison table** wherever possible — it elevates your answer from "average" to "examiner's favorite."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Creating Multiple Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Creating Multiple Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Multiple Threads** in Java refer to the concurrent execution of two or more independent paths of control within a single program, where each thread represents a separate flow of execution that can run **simultaneously (or in a time-sliced manner on a single core)** to perform distinct tasks.

A **Thread** in Java is the smallest unit of dispatched execution within a process. In the KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned, Outcome-Based Education framework), the standard `java.lang` package provides two canonical pathways to create multiple threads:

1. **By Extending the `Thread` Class** — Inheritance-based approach.
2. **By Implementing the `Runnable` Interface** — Composition-based (preferred) approach.

The `main()` method itself runs on a primary thread (called the **Main Thread**) created automatically by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM). Every additional thread must be explicitly instantiated, started via the `start()` method, and its execution path is defined by overriding the `run()` method.

| Property | Value (KTU Standard) |
|---|---|
| Base Package | `java.lang` |
| Default JVM Threads | Main Thread, Garbage Collector, Finalizer |
| Lifecycle Methods | `start()`, `run()`, `sleep()`, `join()`, `yield()` |
| Min Priority Value | **1** (`MIN_PRIORITY`) |
| Default Priority (Main) | **5** (`NORM_PRIORITY`) |
| Max Priority Value | **10** (`MAX_PRIORITY`) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen:**
> Imagine a single restaurant kitchen with **one chef (Single-Threaded Program)**. The chef takes one order, cooks it, plates it, and serves it — *then* takes the next. This is slow.
>
> Now imagine **multiple chefs (Multiple Threads)** working in the same kitchen. Each chef independently takes an order, cooks it, and plates it. They share the same kitchen resources (ovens, pans — analogous to shared heap memory), but each has their own cutting board and recipe (analogous to the **call stack** and **local variables**).
>
> The **Head Chef (Main Thread)** starts the shift, and may delegate tasks to other chefs (user threads). This is precisely what "Creating Multiple Threads" achieves in Java — multiple independent execution flows sharing the same JVM process memory.

> [!TIP]
> **Why do we need multiple threads?**
> To perform **time-consuming or I/O-bound operations** (file reading, network calls, animations) without **freezing** the main program. This is the foundation of responsive GUIs, servers handling thousands of clients, and parallel computation.

---

## 1.3 The Two Foundational Approaches — A Synopsis

| Approach | Mechanism | Key Method | Inheritance Used? | Multiple Inheritance Allowed? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Extending `Thread` | Subclass `java.lang.Thread` | Override `public void run()` | Yes (extends Thread) | **No** (Java does not support MI) |
| Implementing `Runnable` | Implement `java.lang.Runnable` | Override `public void run()` | No (uses composition) | **Yes** (class can extend another + implement Runnable) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Approach 1 — Extending the `Thread` Class

In this approach, a class **inherits** from `java.lang.Thread` and overrides its `run()` method. The `run()` method contains the code that constitutes the new thread's task.

### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Step 1:** Declare a class that extends the predefined `Thread` class.
  - *Why:* To inherit thread-management capabilities (`start()`, `sleep()`, `getName()`, etc.).
- **Step 2:** Override the `public void run()` method in the subclass.
  - *Why:* The `run()` method is the **entry point** for the new thread — exactly like `main()` is the entry point for the program.
- **Step 3:** Create an object of the subclass.
  - *Why:* The object is the actual thread instance.
- **Step 4:** Call `start()` on the object.
  - *Why:* `start()` allocates a new call stack, registers the thread with the thread scheduler, and then invokes `run()`. **Calling `run()` directly does NOT create a new thread** — it executes on the calling thread.

### 2.1.2 Canonical Code Structure (Template)

```java
class MyThread extends Thread {
    @Override
    public void run() {
        // Code executed by the new thread
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " - Count: " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(500); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
        }
    }
}

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyThread t1 = new MyThread();
        MyThread t2 = new MyThread();
        t1.start();   // Creates new call stack, invokes run()
        t2.start();   // Creates another new call stack
    }
}
```

> [!CAUTION]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Calling `t1.run()` instead of `t1.start()` will execute `run()` on the **main thread** (no new thread is created). The output will be sequential, defeating the purpose of multithreading.

---

## 2.2 Approach 2 — Implementing the `Runnable` Interface

In this approach, the class **implements** the functional interface `java.lang.Runnable` (which has a single abstract method `run()`). A `Thread` object is then constructed by passing the `Runnable` instance to the `Thread` constructor.

### 2.2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

- **Step 1:** Declare a class that implements `Runnable`.
  - *Why:* To define a task without inheriting from `Thread`. This decouples "task definition" from "thread management."
- **Step 2:** Override `public void run()`.
- **Step 3:** Create an instance of the class implementing `Runnable`.
- **Step 4:** Pass that instance to the `Thread` constructor: `Thread t = new Thread(runnableObj);`
- **Step 5:** Call `t.start()`.

### 2.2.2 Canonical Code Structure (Template)

```java
class MyRunnable implements Runnable {
    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(Thread.currentThread().getName() + " - " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(500); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
        }
    }
}

public class Demo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        MyRunnable r = new MyRunnable();
        Thread t1 = new Thread(r, "Worker-1");
        Thread t2 = new Thread(r, "Worker-2");
        t1.start();
        t2.start();
    }
}
```

---

## 2.3 Comparison: `Thread` vs `Runnable` — KTU High-Yield Table

| Feature | Extending `Thread` | Implementing `Runnable` |
|---|---|---|
| Inheritance | Inherits from `Thread` | Implements interface |
| Multiple Inheritance | Not possible (class already extends `Thread`) | Possible (class can extend another + implement `Runnable`) |
| Reusability of Task | Each object gets its own `run()` | Same `Runnable` object can be passed to multiple `Thread` objects (task sharing) |
| Object Orientation | Violates "favor composition over inheritance" | Adheres to OOP best practices |
| Access to Thread methods | Direct (`getName()`, `setPriority()`) | Indirect (`Thread.currentThread().getName()`) |
| **KTU Recommendation** | Acceptable | **Preferred (Best Practice)** |

---

## 2.4 The `Thread` Constructors Used in This Module

| Constructor | Description |
|---|---|
| `Thread()` | Creates a thread with default name (`Thread-N`) |
| `Thread(String name)` | Creates a thread with the given name |
| `Thread(Runnable r)` | Creates a thread that executes `r.run()` |
| `Thread(Runnable r, String name)` | Creates a named thread executing `r.run()` |
| `Thread(Runnable r, String name, long stackSize)` | Allows custom stack size (advanced) |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula / Method-Call Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since multithreading is a programming paradigm (not a numerical subject), the "formulas" are actually **method invocations and their effects**.

| Method Call | Effect | Returns |
|---|---|---|
| `t.start()` | Creates new call stack, invokes `run()` asynchronously | `void` |
| `t.run()` | Executes `run()` on the **current** thread (no new thread) | `void` |
| `Thread.sleep(ms)` | Pauses **current** thread for `ms` milliseconds (static) | `void` |
| `t.getName()` | Returns thread's name | `String` |
| `t.setName(n)` | Sets thread's name | `void` |
| `t.getId()` | Returns unique thread ID assigned by JVM | `long` |
| `t.getPriority()` | Returns priority (1–10) | `int` |
| `t.setPriority(p)` | Sets priority (must be 1–10) | `void` |
| `Thread.currentThread()` | Returns reference to currently executing thread | `Thread` |
| `t.isAlive()` | Returns `true` if thread has started and not yet died | `boolean` |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Web Servers (Tomcat, Spring Boot):** Each client request handled by a separate thread.
- **Android UI:** Main thread renders UI; background threads perform network/db operations.
- **Game Development:** Rendering thread, physics thread, AI thread running concurrently.
- **Data Pipelines:** Producer-consumer patterns using multiple threads.
- **Real-time systems:** Sensor reading + processing + display in parallel.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: `start()` vs `run()` — The Lifecycle Transition

In this section, we exhaustively trace what happens internally when each method is invoked — the **definitive** KTU board-exam proof of why `start()` creates a new thread but `run()` does not.

### 3.1.1 Case A: Calling `t.run()` Directly

**Step 1.** The `main` thread is currently executing (created by JVM, priority 5, name "main").

**Step 2.** Execution reaches the line `t1.run();` in the `main` method.

**Step 3.** The JVM treats this as an **ordinary method call**, identical to calling any custom method.

**Step 4.** A new stack frame for `run()` is **pushed onto the main thread's call stack** (NOT a new stack).

**Step 5.** The `run()` method executes to completion, then the stack frame is popped.

**Step 6.** Control returns to `main`, which proceeds to `t2.run();`. The same thing happens.

**Result:** Output is **strictly sequential**. No new thread is born.

### 3.1.2 Case B: Calling `t.start()`

**Step 1.** The `main` thread is currently executing.

**Step 2.** Execution reaches `t1.start();`.

**Step 3.** The JVM's `start()` method performs the following operations (this is the canonical JVM behavior):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 3a:} \quad & \text{Check threadStatus} \neq \text{NEW} \Rightarrow \text{throw IllegalThreadStateException} \\
\text{Step 3b:} \quad & \text{Allocate a fresh, private Java Virtual Machine stack} \\
\text{Step 3c:} \quad & \text{Register the new thread with the OS thread scheduler} \\
\text{Step 3d:} \quad & \text{Set threadStatus} = \text{RUNNABLE} \\
\text{Step 3e:} \quad & \text{Invoke the native } start0() \text{ method (JVM → OS)} \\
\text{Step 3f:} \quad & \text{OS creates a new native thread} \\
\text{Step 3g:} \quad & \text{The new thread's entry point is the overridden } run() \text{ method}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4.** The new thread begins executing `run()` on its **own call stack**, concurrently with `main`.

**Result:** Output is **interleaved / non-deterministic**, demonstrating true multithreading.

### 3.1.3 Comparative Summary Table

| Aspect | `t.run()` | `t.start()` |
|---|---|---|
| New thread created? | **No** | **Yes** |
| New call stack? | **No** (uses caller's stack) | **Yes** (fresh stack allocated) |
| Execution mode | Synchronous (blocks caller) | Asynchronous (caller continues) |
| Output order | Deterministic, sequential | Non-deterministic, interleaved |
| If called twice on same `t`? | Allowed (just a method) | **Throws `IllegalThreadStateException`** |
| `isAlive()` after call | Depends on caller | `true` while `run()` is executing |

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Designing Multiple Threads for a Real KTU-Style Problem

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Pattern):**
> Create a Java program that launches **three** threads. Each thread prints numbers from 1 to 5 with its thread name. The main thread should also print a message before and after starting them.

### 3.2.1 Solution Using `Runnable` (Preferred Method)

**Step 1: Define the task as a `Runnable` class.**

```java
class NumberPrinter implements Runnable {
    @Override
    public void run() {
        Thread t = Thread.currentThread();
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(t.getName() + " prints: " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(200); }
            catch (InterruptedException e) { System.out.println(e); }
        }
    }
}
```

**Step 2: Wire it into a driver class with three threads.**

```java
public class MultiThreadDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Main thread starting...");

        NumberPrinter task = new NumberPrinter();   // shared task object

        Thread t1 = new Thread(task, "Thread-A");
        Thread t2 = new Thread(task, "Thread-B");
        Thread t3 = new Thread(task, "Thread-C");

        t1.start();
        t2.start();
        t3.start();

        System.out.println("Main thread finished launching threads.");
    }
}
```

**Step 3: Trace the output (sample non-deterministic run).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Main thread starting...} \\
\text{Thread-A prints: 1} \\
\text{Thread-C prints: 1} \\
\text{Thread-B prints: 1} \\
\text{Thread-A prints: 2} \\
\text{Thread-C prints: 2} \\
\text{Thread-B prints: 2} \\
\vdots \\
\text{Thread-X prints: 5} \\
\vdots \\
\text{Main thread finished launching threads.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The `try-catch` around `Thread.sleep()` is **mandatory** because `sleep()` throws the **checked exception** `InterruptedException`. This is a frequent KTU exam question: *"Why is `try-catch` required around `sleep()`?"*

---

### 3.2.2 Solution Using `Thread` Class (Alternative Method)

```java
class NumberThread extends Thread {
    public NumberThread(String name) { super(name); }

    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " prints: " + i);
            try { sleep(200); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
        }
    }
}

public class MultiThreadDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        NumberThread t1 = new NumberThread("Thread-A");
        NumberThread t2 = new NumberThread("Thread-B");
        NumberThread t3 = new NumberThread("Thread-C");
        t1.start(); t2.start(); t3.start();
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** The `super(name)` call invokes `Thread(String name)` constructor, naming the thread. If omitted, threads are auto-named `Thread-0`, `Thread-1`, `Thread-2`.

---

## 3.3 Why `Runnable` is the KTU-Preferred Approach — A Proof

**Scenario:** Suppose your class needs to extend another class (e.g., `class MyFrame extends JFrame`), and you also want it to be a thread.

- Using **Thread** approach: **Impossible** (Java does not support multiple class inheritance). You would have to refactor your entire class hierarchy.
- Using **Runnable** approach: **Perfectly fine.**

```java
class MyFrame extends JFrame implements Runnable {
    // Can extend JFrame AND implement Runnable simultaneously
    @Override
    public void run() { /* ... */ }
}
```

This proof is the standard KTU 2024 board-exam justification for the preference.

---

## 3.4 The `isAlive()` Verification — Empirical Proof of Concurrency

The following code empirically proves that multiple threads are running concurrently:

```java
class Worker extends Thread {
    public Worker(String n) { super(n); }
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " alive=" + isAlive());
            try { sleep(300); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
        }
    }
}

public class Verify {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        Worker w1 = new Worker("W1");
        Worker w2 = new Worker("W2");
        w1.start();
        w2.start();
        System.out.println("W1 alive (after start): " + w1.isAlive());
        w1.join();   // wait for w1 to finish
        System.out.println("W1 alive (after join): " + w1.isAlive());
    }
}
```

**Expected Observation:**
- Before `w1.join()`: `isAlive()` returns `true` (thread running).
- After `w1.join()`: `isAlive()` returns `false` (thread has terminated).

This provides **empirical, runtime proof** that a new thread was indeed created.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — Two Pathways for Creating Multiple Threads (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Start - JVM Launches Main Thread] --> B{Need Multiple Threads?}
    B -->|Yes| C{Choose Creation Strategy}
    C -->|Approach 1| D[Extend java.lang.Thread class]
    C -->|Approach 2| E[Implement java.lang.Runnable interface]
    D --> D1[Override public void run method]
    D1 --> D2[Instantiate subclass object t1, t2, t3]
    D2 --> D3[Invoke t1.start, t2.start, t3.start]
    E --> E1[Implement public void run method]
    E1 --> E2[Instantiate Runnable task object]
    E2 --> E3[Wrap in Thread: new Thread task name]
    E3 --> E4[Invoke t1.start, t2.start, t3.start]
    D3 --> F[JVM Allocates Separate Call Stacks]
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[OS Thread Scheduler Dispatches Threads]
    G --> H[Concurrent Execution of run methods]
    H --> I[Thread Termination - Stack Deallocated]
```

---

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Lifecycle of a Thread (State Transition Flow)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> NEW : Thread object created
    NEW --> RUNNABLE : start method called
    RUNNABLE --> BLOCKED : Waiting for monitor lock
    BLOCKED --> RUNNABLE : Lock acquired
    RUNNABLE --> WAITING : wait, join without timeout
    WAITING --> RUNNABLE : notify, notifyAll, or join completion
    RUNNABLE --> TIMED_WAITING : sleep ms, wait ms, join ms
    TIMED_WAITING --> RUNNABLE : Timeout elapsed
    RUNNABLE --> TERMINATED : run method returns normally or throws
    TERMINATED --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** A thread is **born** in the `NEW` state. Calling `start()` transitions it to `RUNNABLE` (eligible for CPU). It can move to `BLOCKED`, `WAITING`, or `TIMED_WAITING` based on synchronization, and finally reaches `TERMINATED` when `run()` completes. A terminated thread **cannot be restarted** — calling `start()` again throws `IllegalThreadStateException`.

---

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — Memory & Stack Architecture of Multiple Threads

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph proc [JVM Process Memory]
        subgraph heap [Shared Heap Memory]
            obj1[Thread Object t1]
            obj2[Thread Object t2]
            shared[Shared Runnable Task Object]
        end
        subgraph stack1 [Stack of Thread 1]
            f1[Frame run method]
            lv1[Local Variables of t1]
        end
        subgraph stack2 [Stack of Thread 2]
            f2[Frame run method]
            lv2[Local Variables of t2]
        end
        subgraph stackMain [Stack of Main Thread]
            fm[Frame main method]
        end
    end
    obj1 -.-> f1
    obj2 -.-> f2
    shared -.-> f1
    shared -.-> f2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** All threads in a JVM share the **same heap** (so they can communicate via shared objects), but each thread has its **own private call stack** (so local variables are thread-safe by default). This is why instance fields can cause race conditions, but local variables cannot.

---

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — `start()` vs `run()` — Execution Path Comparison

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MT as Main Thread
    participant JVM as JVM / OS Scheduler
    participant NT1 as New Thread 1
    participant NT2 as New Thread 2

    Note over MT: t1.start called
    MT->>JVM: Request new thread
    JVM->>NT1: Allocate stack and start
    Note over MT: t2.start called
    MT->>JVM: Request new thread
    JVM->>NT2: Allocate stack and start
    NT1->>NT1: Execute run method
    NT2->>NT2: Execute run method
    MT->>MT: Continue with main method body
    Note over NT1,NT2: Concurrent interleaved execution
    NT1-->>JVM: run returns - TERMINATED
    NT2-->>JVM: run returns - TERMINATED
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Sequential `run()` path:** If `t1.run()` is called instead, **no JVM intervention** occurs. `run()` simply executes inline on the main thread's existing stack, blocking `main` until completion. There is no concurrency.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks Equivalent)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Exam 2024]`

**Differentiate between calling `start()` and `run()` methods on a Thread object. What happens if `start()` is called twice on the same thread object? [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | `start()` | `run()` |
|---|---|---|
| Thread Creation | Registers a new thread with the thread scheduler; allocates a fresh call stack. | Treated as a normal method call; no new thread is created. |
| Execution | Asynchronous — caller continues immediately. | Synchronous — caller blocks until `run()` returns. |
| Invocation | Can be called **only once** per thread object. | Can be called any number of times. |

**If `start()` is called twice:** The JVM checks the internal thread state. On the second call, the state is no longer `NEW`, so the JVM throws `java.lang.IllegalThreadStateException`, a `RuntimeException`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students write *"calling `run()` directly creates a new thread"* — this is **wrong** and costs 1 mark immediately. Memorize the distinction.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Series Exam 1]`

**Explain why implementing the `Runnable` interface is preferred over extending the `Thread` class for creating multiple threads in Java. [3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Multiple Inheritance:** Java does not support extending multiple classes. If a class already extends another (e.g., `JFrame`, `Applet`), it cannot also extend `Thread`. However, it can implement `Runnable` alongside any superclass.
2. **Reusability of the Task:** A single `Runnable` instance can be passed to multiple `Thread` constructors, allowing the same task to be executed by many threads with shared state. With the `Thread` approach, each thread subclass instance carries its own `run()` definition.
3. **Separation of Concerns:** `Runnable` separates the *task* (what to do) from the *thread* (how to run it), aligning with the OO principle of *favor composition over inheritance*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (ESE Module — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Module 3]`

**A) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program to create three threads using the `Runnable` interface. Each thread should print its name and a counter from 1 to 5. Also, show the main thread printing a message before and after starting all three threads.

**B) [7 Marks]** Explain the constructors of the `Thread` class used for creating threads. How does `Thread.currentThread()` help in retrieving the current thread's information inside a `run()` method implemented via `Runnable`?

#### Solution A — Complete Java Program

**Step 1: Define the `Runnable` task.**

```java
class PrintTask implements Runnable {
    @Override
    public void run() {
        Thread t = Thread.currentThread();
        for (int i = 1; i <= 5; i++) {
            System.out.println(t.getName() + " - Counter: " + i);
            try { Thread.sleep(300); }
            catch (InterruptedException e) { System.out.println("Interrupted: " + e); }
        }
    }
}
```

**Step 2: Create the driver class with the main thread and three worker threads.**

```java
public class ThreeThreadsDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("[" + Thread.currentThread().getName() + "] Starting...");

        PrintTask task = new PrintTask();
        Thread t1 = new Thread(task, "Worker-1");
        Thread t2 = new Thread(task, "Worker-2");
        Thread t3 = new Thread(task, "Worker-3");

        t1.start();
        t2.start();
        t3.start();

        System.out.println("[" + Thread.currentThread().getName() + "] All threads launched.");
    }
}
```

**Step 3: Explanation of each block (Valuation Points).**

- `Thread.currentThread()` is used inside `run()` because the class doesn't extend `Thread`, so `this` refers to the `Runnable` task, not the executing thread. `currentThread()` is a static method returning the actual `Thread` reference executing the code.
- The `try-catch` around `sleep()` is mandatory because `sleep()` declares `throws InterruptedException` (a checked exception).
- The output is **non-deterministic**; each run will interleave the worker outputs differently.

---

#### Solution B — Thread Constructors & `currentThread()`

**The relevant `Thread` constructors for this module:**

| Constructor | Usage in Solution A |
|---|---|
| `Thread(Runnable target)` | `new Thread(task)` — anonymous thread named `Thread-0` etc. |
| `Thread(Runnable target, String name)` | `new Thread(task, "Worker-1")` — named thread |

**`Thread.currentThread()` explained:**

It is a static method in the `Thread` class with the signature:

$$
\text{public static Thread currentThread()}
$$

It returns a reference to the thread that is currently executing the call. When the `run()` method is invoked by the thread scheduler (after `start()`), the **executing thread** is the newly created worker thread (e.g., `Worker-1`). So:

```java
Thread t = Thread.currentThread();
t.getName();   // returns "Worker-1"
t.getId();     // returns unique long ID
t.getPriority(); // returns 5 (default)
```

This is the **only** way to get thread info when using the `Runnable` approach, because the class itself is not a `Thread` subclass.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam]`

**A) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program to create multiple threads by extending the `Thread` class. The program should demonstrate the difference between calling `start()` and `run()` on the same thread object.

**B) [7 Marks]** Discuss the life cycle of a thread with the help of a state diagram. Mention at least four states with their causes.

#### Solution A — `Thread` Class with `start()` vs `run()` Demonstration

**Step 1: Create the thread subclass.**

```java
class DemoThread extends Thread {
    public DemoThread(String name) { super(name); }

    @Override
    public void run() {
        for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
            System.out.println(getName() + " executing run() - " + i);
            try { sleep(400); } catch (InterruptedException e) {}
        }
    }
}
```

**Step 2: Driver class demonstrating the difference.**

```java
public class StartVsRun {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws InterruptedException {
        DemoThread t1 = new DemoThread("MyThread-1");

        System.out.println("--- Calling run() directly ---");
        t1.run();   // executes on main thread; main blocks until done

        System.out.println("--- Calling start() ---");
        t1.start(); // creates a new thread

        // Uncommenting the next line throws IllegalThreadStateException
        // t1.start();  // second call on same object

        for (int i = 1; i <= 2; i++) {
            System.out.println("Main thread working - " + i);
            Thread.sleep(300);
        }
    }
}
```

**Step 3: Observation & Explanation.**

- During `t1.run()`: Output is sequential and labeled with "MyThread-1", but no actual new thread is created. `main` waits.
- During `t1.start()`: A new thread is created. Its output interleaves with the main thread's "Main thread working" output.
- A second `t1.start()` call on the same object throws `IllegalThreadStateException`.

---

#### Solution B — Thread Life Cycle States

The Java thread has **six** states as defined in `java.lang.Thread.State` enum:

| State | Description | Triggering Event |
|---|---|---|
| **NEW** | Thread object created, `start()` not yet called. | `new MyThread()` |
| **RUNNABLE** | Thread is ready to run, waiting for CPU. | `start()` called |
| **BLOCKED** | Waiting to acquire a monitor lock (synchronization). | Entering a `synchronized` block held by another thread |
| **WAITING** | Waiting indefinitely for another thread's action. | `wait()`, `join()` (no timeout) |
| **TIMED_WAITING** | Waiting for a specified time. | `sleep(ms)`, `wait(ms)`, `join(ms)` |
| **TERMINATED** | `run()` has finished execution. | Normal return or uncaught exception |

**State Diagram (textual representation):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{NEW} &\xrightarrow{\text{start()}} \text{RUNNABLE} \\
\text{RUNNABLE} &\xrightarrow{\text{acquire lock}} \text{BLOCKED} \xrightarrow{\text{lock obtained}} \text{RUNNABLE} \\
\text{RUNNABLE} &\xrightarrow{\text{wait() / join()}} \text{WAITING} \xrightarrow{\text{notify / join end}} \text{RUNNABLE} \\
\text{RUNNABLE} &\xrightarrow{\text{sleep() / wait(timeout)}} \text{TIMED\_WAITING} \xrightarrow{\text{timeout}} \text{RUNNABLE} \\
\text{RUNNABLE} &\xrightarrow{\text{run() returns}} \text{TERMINATED}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common mistake is confusing **`BLOCKED`** with **`WAITING`**. `BLOCKED` is specifically for monitor-lock contention (synchronization). `WAITING` is for explicit `wait()`/`join()` calls. Confusing them costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3: Creating Multiple Threads**

- ✅ A **thread** is the smallest unit of execution within a process; multiple threads share the **heap** but have **separate stacks**.
- ✅ There are **two** ways to create threads in Java:
  1. **Extending `java.lang.Thread`** (inheritance-based).
  2. **Implementing `java.lang.Runnable`** (composition-based, **KTU-preferred**).
- ✅ The `run()` method is the **entry point** of a new thread, analogous to `main()` for the program.
- ✅ **Always call `start()`**, never `run()` directly, to spawn a new thread. `run()` called directly is just a normal method invocation.
- ✅ Calling `start()` **twice** on the same `Thread` object throws `IllegalThreadStateException`.
- ✅ The `main` method runs on the **Main Thread**, automatically created by the JVM with priority **5** (`NORM_PRIORITY`).
- ✅ Use `Thread.currentThread()` inside a `Runnable.run()` to obtain the executing thread's reference (since `this` refers to the `Runnable` object, not the `Thread`).
- ✅ `Thread.sleep(ms)` is a **static** method that pauses the **currently executing** thread and **throws checked** `InterruptedException`, which **must** be handled via `try-catch` or `throws`.
- ✅ The `Runnable` approach is preferred because:
  1. Java does not support multiple class inheritance.
  2. A single `Runnable` can be reused across multiple `Thread` objects.
  3. It separates *task* from *thread* (OO best practice).
- ✅ Thread states: **NEW → RUNNABLE → (BLOCKED | WAITING | TIMED_WAITING) → TERMINATED**.
- ✅ `getName()`, `setName()`, `getId()`, `getPriority()`, `setPriority()`, `isAlive()` are key `Thread` class methods.
- ✅ Priority range: **1 (MIN) to 10 (MAX)**; default is **5 (NORM)**. Priority is a **hint** to the scheduler, not a guarantee.
- ✅ The output of a multithreaded program is **non-deterministic** (interleaved) because the scheduler decides execution order.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Tip (KTU Board Pattern):** When asked to "create multiple threads" in a 14-mark question, the KTU model answer key expects:
> 1. The complete program (≈ 5–6 marks).
> 2. The constructors used (≈ 2 marks).
> 3. The `start()` vs `run()` distinction (≈ 2 marks).
> 4. The output/explanation (≈ 2 marks).
> 5. Justification for preferring `Runnable` (≈ 2 marks).
>
> Cover all five to secure full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thread Synchronization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Thread Synchronization # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Thread Synchronization** is the mechanism that ensures that **two or more concurrent threads** do not simultaneously access a shared resource (critical section) in a way that produces inconsistent or erroneous results. In Java, synchronization is built around the concept of **monitors** and the intrinsic lock (also called the *monitor lock* or *mutex*) associated with every object. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PBEOT404 – Module 3), thread synchronization is taught under the umbrella of *Multithreading and Concurrency Control*. It addresses the fundamental problem of **race conditions**, which occur when the output of a program depends on the unpredictable timing of thread execution. ### Key Terminology (Mandatory for KTU Viva & Theory) | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Race Condition** | A bug where the program's outcome depends on the non-deterministic interleaving of threads | | **Critical Section** | The segment of code that accesses shared variables or shared resources | | **Monitor** | A synchronization construct that allows only one thread to execute a protected region at a time | | **Intrinsic Lock** | A built-in lock acquired implicitly by a thread when it enters a `synchronized` method/block | | **Mutual Exclusion** | The property that at most one thread can be inside the critical section at any given instant | | **Liveness** | The property that a program continues to make progress (no deadlocks, starvation, livelocks) | ## 1.2 The Real-World Analogy — The "Bank Account Locker" Imagine a **joint bank locker** that is shared between two people, **Ram** and **Shyam**. The locker room has: 1. **One physical key** (the *intrinsic lock*). 2. **One locker** (the *shared resource*). When Ram enters the locker room, he takes the key. While Ram is inside: - **Shyam cannot enter** — he must wait outside. - When Ram finishes, he **returns the key** so Shyam can go in next. This single-key, single-room design enforces **mutual exclusion** — exactly what Java's `synchronized` keyword achieves for shared objects. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Synchronization in Java is **not** about synchronizing *threads* per se; it is about synchronizing **access to objects**. Every Java object has **one intrinsic lock**, and a thread must acquire that lock before it can execute any `synchronized` instance method on that object. ## 1.3 Why Do We Need Synchronization? Consider a shared counter `balance = 1000` accessed by two threads, each trying to deposit `100`. Without synchronization, the following interleaving can occur: $$\text{Thread T1: reads } balance = 1000$$ $$\text{Thread T2: reads } balance = 1000$$ $$\text{Thread T1: writes } balance = 1100$$ $$\text{Thread T2: writes } balance = 1100 \;\; (\text{incorrect — should be } 1200)$$ This **lost-update problem** is precisely what synchronization prevents. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Java Synchronization ### Pillar 1 — The `synchronized` Keyword The `synchronized` keyword can be applied at **two granularities**: **A. Synchronized Instance Method** ```java class SharedResource { synchronized void printData(String name) { // critical section for(int i=1; i<=3; i++) { System.out.println(name + " - " + i); try { Thread.sleep(400); } catch(Exception e){} } } } ``` When a thread invokes a `synchronized` instance method, it **automatically acquires the intrinsic lock of the object** on which the method is called. Other threads attempting to call **any** `synchronized` instance method on the **same object** will block until the lock is released. **B. Synchronized Block (Fine-Grained Locking)** ```java void printData(String name) { synchronized(this) { // critical section } } ``` This gives you explicit control over the **scope of the lock** and **which object provides the lock**. It is preferred when only a small part of the method needs protection, improving **concurrency throughput**. **C. Synchronized Static Method** ```java class SharedResource { static synchronized void printData(String name) { // critical section } } ``` A `synchronized static` method acquires the lock on the **Class object** (i.e., `SharedResource.class`), not on any instance. This protects static variables shared across all instances. ### Pillar 2 — The `wait()` / `notify()` / `notifyAll()` Mechanism These three methods (defined in `java.lang.Object`) implement **inter-thread communication**. They can only be called from within a `synchronized` context. | Method | Effect | |---|---| | `wait()` | Releases the lock and puts the thread into the **waiting pool** | | `notify()` | Wakes up **one** randomly chosen thread waiting on the same object's monitor | | `notifyAll()` | Wakes up **all** threads waiting on the same object's monitor | ## 2.2 Lifecycle Transitions Induced by Synchronization A normal thread transitions through: **NEW → RUNNABLE → BLOCKED/WAITING → TERMINATED**. Synchronization introduces two new waiting states: 1. **BLOCKED** — Thread is waiting to acquire the intrinsic lock (e.g., trying to enter a `synchronized` block). 2. **WAITING** — Thread has explicitly called `wait()` and is awaiting `notify()`/`notifyAll()`. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Frequently Asked Distinction:** > - **BLOCKED** → waiting for an **intrinsic monitor lock** (passive queue managed by JVM). > - **WAITING** → invoked `wait()` (active wait in the object's wait set). > A blocked thread becomes runnable as soon as the lock is released; a waiting thread requires an explicit `notify()`. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Concept Cheat Sheet | Concept | Rule / Property | Engineering Utility | |---|---|---| | Mutual Exclusion | At most **1** thread inside `synchronized` region per object lock | Database transactions, banking, embedded sensor reads | | Reentrancy | Java intrinsic locks are **reentrant** — same thread can re-acquire its own lock | Recursive methods calling other `synchronized` methods | | Happens-Before | All actions inside a `synchronized` block happen-before any subsequent thread acquiring the same lock | Guarantees **memory visibility** across cores | | Fairness | Java intrinsic locks are **not** fair (no FIFO guarantee) | Use `ReentrantLock(true)` for fairness | | Starvation | Threads waiting for `notify()` may starve if `notify()` is misused | Always prefer `notifyAll()` over `notify()` in complex logic | | Deadlock | Two threads each hold one lock and wait for the other | Always acquire locks in a **fixed global order** | ## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility Thread synchronization is the **backbone** of every concurrent system: - **Banking software** — atomic balance updates. - **Operating systems** — kernel-level mutexes for device drivers. - **Web servers (Tomcat, Spring)** — synchronizing `HttpSession` data. - **Real-time embedded systems** — protecting shared sensor buffers. - **Database engines** — row-level locking is a generalized form of synchronization. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivational Walkthrough — From Race Condition to a Synchronized Fix ### Step 1: The Unsynchronized Version (Demonstrating the Bug) ```java class Table { void printTable(int n) { // NOT synchronized for(int i=1; i<=5; i++) { System.out.println(n + " x " + i + " = " + (n*i)); try { Thread.sleep(200); } catch(Exception e){} } } } class MyThread extends Thread { Table t; int n; MyThread(Table t, int n){ this.t=t; this.n=n; } public void run(){ t.printTable(n); } } public class Demo { public static void main(String[] args) { Table obj = new Table(); // SHARED object MyThread t1 = new MyThread(obj, 5); MyThread t2 = new MyThread(obj, 100); t1.start(); t2.start(); } } ``` **Output (interleaved, incorrect):** ``` 5 x 1 = 5 100 x 1 = 100 5 x 2 = 10 100 x 2 = 200 ... (mixed output) ``` ### Step 2: Logical Analysis — Why Is Output Mixed? Each thread calls `printTable()`. Without synchronization: $$\text{ThreadState}(t_1) = \text{RUNNABLE}, \quad \text{ThreadState}(t_2) = \text{RUNNABLE}$$ $$\text{CPU Scheduler interleaves: } t_1 \rightarrow t_2 \rightarrow t_1 \rightarrow t_2 \rightarrow \dots$$ The `sleep(200)` inside the loop is a **yielding point** that gives the scheduler a chance to switch. Because both threads share the same `Table obj`, they race on the `System.out.println()` call. ### Step 3: The Mathematical Model of the Race Let $T_1$ and $T_2$ be two transactions on shared variable $x$: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Without synchronization:} \quad & \text{read}_{T_1}(x) \rightarrow \text{read}_{T_2}(x) \rightarrow \text{write}_{T_1}(x) \rightarrow \text{write}_{T_2}(x) \\ \text{With synchronization:} \quad & \text{lock}(obj) \rightarrow \text{read}(x) \rightarrow \text{write}(x) \rightarrow \text{unlock}(obj) \\ & \text{(Other thread BLOCKED until unlock)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4: The Synchronized Fix ```java class Table { synchronized void printTable(int n) { // <-- keyword added for(int i=1; i<=5; i++) { System.out.println(n + " x " + i + " = " + (n*i)); try { Thread.sleep(200); } catch(Exception e){} } } } ``` Now the JVM enforces: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Lock}(obj) &\rightarrow \text{execute printTable} \rightarrow \text{Unlock}(obj) \\ &\Rightarrow \text{Second thread BLOCKED until first releases} \end{aligned} $$ **Output (correct, sequential):** ``` 5 x 1 = 5 5 x 2 = 10 5 x 3 = 15 5 x 4 = 20 5 x 5 = 25 100 x 1 = 100 100 x 2 = 200 ...
