| `void validate()` | Lays out children again (after dynamic changes) | High |
| `Insets getInsets()` | Returns border thickness | Low |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Components at a Glance

| Component | Constructor Example | Key Listener | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Label` | `new Label("Name")` | None | Static text display |
| `Button` | `new Button("OK")` | `ActionListener` | Trigger action |
| `TextField` | `new TextField(20)` | `ActionListener`, `TextListener` | Single-line input |
| `TextArea` | `new TextArea(5, 30)` | `TextListener` | Multi-line input/output |
| `Checkbox` | `new Checkbox("Java", true)` | `ItemListener` | Toggle option |
| `CheckboxGroup` | `new CheckboxGroup()` | — | Radio-button grouping |
| `Choice` | `new Choice()` | `ItemListener` | Drop-down list |
| `List` | `new List(3, true)` | `ActionListener`, `ItemListener` | Scrollable list, multi-select |
| `Canvas` | `new Canvas()` | `MouseListener`, `KeyListener` | Custom drawing area |
| `Scrollbar` | `new Scrollbar(Scrollbar.HORIZONTAL)` | `AdjustmentListener` | Slider control |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Containers at a Glance

| Container | Constructable Standalone? | Border by Default? | Common Layout | KTU Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `Frame` | Yes (`new Frame("Title")`) | Title bar | `BorderLayout` | Top-level window; can have menu bar |
| `Dialog` | Yes (`new Dialog(parent, "Title")`) | Title bar | `BorderLayout` | Modal or non-modal popup |
| `Panel` | Yes (`new Panel()`) | No border | `FlowLayout` | Lightweight grouping container |
| `ScrollPane` | Yes (`new ScrollPane()`) | Scrollbars | `BorderLayout`-like | Auto-scrolls large child |
| `Applet` | Cannot instantiate directly | Browser-supplied | `FlowLayout` | Lives inside a browser (legacy) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Frame vs Window:** `Window` is abstract in usage — practically you almost never instantiate `Window` directly. Always use `Frame` (which is a `Window` subclass). KTU exams frequently test this distinction.

## 2.6 Why This Architecture Matters in Engineering

The Component/Container pattern is the **precursor of modern JavaFX and Swing architectures** (e.g., `JComponent` in Swing). It demonstrates the **Composite Design Pattern** — a tree structure where individual objects (Components) and compositions of objects (Containers) are treated uniformly. Real engineering applications that rely on this:

- Industrial SCADA HMIs built in Java.
- Banking terminal GUIs in legacy enterprise systems.
- Embedded kiosk applications using Java SE Embedded.
- Cross-platform desktop tools such as older versions of NetBeans IDE.

The Composite pattern is a recurring Module-3/4 OOP exam topic, and AWT is the textbook Java demonstration.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Deriving the "Minimum Lines to Build a Window"

The following is a **formal derivation-style walkthrough** of how the Component/Container architecture forces a particular sequence of method calls. KTU often asks: *"What is the minimum code to display a window?"* The answer is derived step by step from the API contract.

### Step 1 — Acquire the top-level Container

A window that the user can drag, resize, and close must be a `Frame`, because `Frame` is the only top-level AWT container that has a title bar and border.

**Logical justification:** `Window` lacks a title bar, so it is unusable as a main window. `Dialog` requires a parent `Frame`. `Panel` cannot exist standalone. Therefore, by elimination, `Frame` is the *only* valid root.

**Code construction:**
```java
Frame f = new Frame("My First AWT Window");
```
This single line invokes the `Frame(String title)` constructor. Internally it:
- Allocates a native peer via `Toolkit.getDefaultToolkit().createFrame()`.
- Sets default `LayoutManager` to `BorderLayout`.
- Sets initial size to **0×0** (not yet visible).

### Step 2 — Choose and assign a Layout

By contract, every `Container` must have a `LayoutManager` before components are added; otherwise the components are placed at (0,0) overlapping each other.

**Code construction:**
```java
f.setLayout(new FlowLayout());
```
This calls `Container.setLayout(LayoutManager)`. The old layout is detached; the new one is stored in the protected `layoutManager` field.

### Step 3 — Instantiate Component objects

Each component constructor registers the component with the native peer system but does **not** place it on the screen.

**Code construction:**
```java
Label l = new Label("Enter your name:");
Button b = new Button("Submit");
```
The `Label` constructor `Label(String text)` stores the string in the `text` field. The `Button` constructor `Button(String label)` does the same for the button caption.

### Step 4 — Add components to the container

```java
f.add(l);
f.add(b);
```
The `add(Component)` method:
- Appends the component to the internal `Component[] component` array (a `Vector`-backed structure).
- Triggers a re-layout on the next `validate()` cycle.
- Forwards the event registration to the child for the parent's event dispatcher.

### Step 5 — Set geometry on the Frame

```java
f.setSize(400, 300);
f.setLocation(200, 150);
```
`setSize` calls the peer's `setBounds` on the OS window. `setLocation` positions the top-left corner.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Frame uses its own `setSize` and `setLocation`** because it is a top-level window — its parent is the OS desktop, not another AWT container. For a `Button` inside a `Panel`, coordinates are *relative to the parent container*, not the screen.

### Step 6 — Make the window visible

```java
f.setVisible(true);
```
This single call:
- Maps the peer window onto the screen.
- Triggers a top-down paint of the entire component tree.
- Begins event-dispatch-thread (EDT) listening for OS events.

### Final Compiled 14-Mark Worth Program

```java
import java.awt.*;

public class AWTBasics extends Frame {
    AWTBasics() {
        super("AWT Demo");                     // Step 1
        setLayout(new FlowLayout());           // Step 2
        Label l = new Label("Name:");          // Step 3
        TextField tf = new TextField(15);      // Step 3
        Button b = new Button("OK");           // Step 3
        add(l);                                // Step 4
        add(tf);                               // Step 4
        add(b);                                // Step 4
        setSize(350, 150);                     // Step 5
        setVisible(true);                      // Step 6
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new AWTBasics();
    }
}
```

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Component Decision Table

When KTU asks *"Which AWT class should be used for X?"* use this decision matrix.

| Requirement | Correct AWT Class | Why Not the Others |
|---|---|---|
| Display an un-editable text caption | `Label` | `TextField` allows editing, `Button` is clickable |
| Get a one-line typed input | `TextField` | `Label` is read-only, `TextArea` is multi-line |
| Get multi-line typed input | `TextArea` | `TextField` is single-line |
| Trigger an action on click | `Button` | `Checkbox` toggles state, not action |
| Toggle a single boolean option | `Checkbox` | `Button` is momentary, `Choice` selects from list |
| Select exactly one option from many | `CheckboxGroup` of `Checkbox` | `Choice` doesn't show all options at once |
| Drop-down single selection | `Choice` | `List` shows all options always |
| Scrollable list with multi-select | `List` | `Choice` does not support multi-select |
| Custom painting area | `Canvas` | Other components paint themselves |
| Group components inside a window | `Panel` | `Frame` is the top-level window |
| Top-level draggable window | `Frame` | `Window` has no title bar |
| Modal popup requiring user response | `Dialog` (modal=true) | `Frame` is never modal |

## 3.3 The Container-Add-Component Algebra

Let:
- $C$ = the parent **Container** object.
- $m$ = number of components already inside $C$.
- $n$ = number of new components to add in this call sequence.
- $L$ = the active **LayoutManager**.

After executing:
$$
\text{componentCount}(C) = m + n
$$

The visual position of the $i$-th added component (for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, n$) depends on $L$:

$$
(x_i, y_i, w_i, h_i) = L.\text{arrange}(C, \text{children}[0..m+i-1])
$$

For a `FlowLayout` (left-to-right, top-to-bottom wrapping):

$$
x_i = \text{offsetX} + \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} w_j, \quad y_i = \text{offsetY} + \text{rowOf}(i) \cdot \text{rowHeight}
$$

$$
\text{rowOf}(i) = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{cumulativeX}_i}{\text{containerWidth}} \right\rfloor
$$

For a `BorderLayout`:

$$
(x_i, y_i, w_i, h_i) =
\begin{cases}
(0, 0, W, N) & \text{if region = NORTH} \\
(0, H-N, W, S) & \text{if region = SOUTH} \\
(0, N, W, H-N-S) & \text{if region = CENTER} \\
(0, N, W, H-N-S) & \text{if region = WEST or EAST (subdivided)}
\end{cases}
$$

where $W$ and $H$ are container width/height, and $N$, $S$ are north/south region heights. This is the formal justification for why exactly **five** regions exist in `BorderLayout` (`NORTH`, `SOUTH`, `EAST`, `WEST`, `CENTER`).

> [!NOTE]
> KTU does not ask students to derive the *complete* layout math in an exam, but understanding the principle helps in tracing layout bugs and answering 7-mark "explain with example" questions.

## 3.4 Inheritance Chain Resolution (OOP Trace)

Suppose you call `setBackground(Color.RED)` on a `Button` `b`. The compiler/JVM resolves:

$$
\text{Button.} \xrightarrow{\text{no override}} \text{Component.setBackground(Color)}
$$

Step-by-step:
1. `Button` does **not** override `setBackground`.
2. The JVM walks up: `Button` → `Component` (found, executes).
3. `Component.setBackground` calls `peer.setBackground(c)` via the toolkit.
4. Native peer delegates to OS to repaint the widget background.

This single trace demonstrates **method inheritance**, a Module-2/3 OOP concept reused in Module-4 GUI programming.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PBEOT404]`
**Differentiate between `Component` and `Container` in Java AWT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `Component` | `Container` |
|---|---|---|
| Package | `java.awt` | `java.awt` |
| Inheritance | Subclass of `Object` | Subclass of `Component` |
| Purpose | Represents any visible GUI element | Holds and arranges other components |
| Holds children | Cannot hold other components | Can hold components and sub-containers |
| Layout | Has no layout responsibility | Owns a `LayoutManager` |
| Key method | `setSize()`, `setLocation()`, `repaint()` | `add()`, `setLayout()`, `remove()` |
| Examples | `Button`, `Label`, `TextField` | `Frame`, `Panel`, `Dialog`, `ScrollPane` |

A container **is-a** component (inheritance), but it additionally **has-a** collection of children (composition). Therefore containers are heavyweight in responsibility: they forward events, manage layout, and propagate paint requests.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PBEOT404]`
**List any six direct subclasses of `java.awt.Component` and state the role of `Frame` in AWT.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Six direct subclasses of `java.awt.Component`:
1. `Container` (which itself extends to `Frame`, `Panel`, `Dialog`, `ScrollPane`)
2. `Button` — clickable trigger element
3. `Label` — non-editable text display
4. `Canvas` — blank area for custom drawing
5. `Checkbox` — toggle or radio button
6. `Choice` — drop-down selection list
7. `List` — scrollable list box
8. `Scrollbar` — slider/scroll control
9. `TextComponent` (further subclassed to `TextField` and `TextArea`)

**Role of `Frame`:** `Frame` is a **top-level container** with a title bar, border, and optionally a menu bar. It is the most common root window for a Java AWT application. By default, a `Frame` uses `BorderLayout` as its layout manager. It is the only practical choice for a stand-alone main window because `Window` (its parent) lacks a title bar.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the AWT class hierarchy. With a neat diagram, describe how `Component` and `Container` are related, and list at least **eight** direct/indirect subclasses of `Component` along with their use.

**(b) [7 marks]** Write a complete Java AWT program that creates a `Frame` titled *"Student Registration"* containing:
- A `Label` "Name:"
- A `TextField` of 20 columns
- A `Label` "Gender:"
- A `Checkbox` "Male" and a `Checkbox` "Female" (mutually exclusive using `CheckboxGroup`)
- A `Button` "Submit"
Use a suitable layout manager. When the user clicks Submit, the program should display all entered values in a `TextArea` at the bottom of the frame.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss the different types of AWT containers — `Frame`, `Panel`, `Dialog`, `ScrollPane`, and `Applet` — with their constructors, default layouts, and typical use cases. Mention the rule that every top-level window must inherit from `Window`.

**(b) [7 marks]** Design a Java AWT program to build a simple calculator GUI with:
- A `TextField` of 25 columns at the top (non-editable, displays current value)
- Ten `Button` objects for digits 0–9
- Four `Button` objects for `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`
- One `Button` for `=`
Arrange the components using `GridLayout`. Wire up at least the digit-entry logic.

---

## 5.3 Model Solutions

### Model Solution for Question A

#### (a) [7 marks] AWT Class Hierarchy

The Java AWT class hierarchy is rooted at `java.lang.Object` and branches into `Component`, the abstract root of all visual classes. The most important relationship is the **inheritance chain**:

**Component** — base class of all visual elements; provides methods for size, location, color, font, painting, and event delivery. **Container** is a subclass of `Component`, and therefore every container inherits all of Component's methods. The reverse is not true: a plain `Component` cannot hold children.

**Major subclasses of `Component`:**

| Class | Use |
|---|---|
| `Button` | Clickable trigger that fires `ActionEvent` |
| `Label` | Static, un-editable text display |
| `TextField` | Single-line editable text input |
| `TextArea` | Multi-line editable text input/output |
| `Checkbox` | Toggle / radio-style option |
| `Choice` | Drop-down list, single selection |
| `List` | Scrollable list, single or multi-select |
| `Canvas` | Blank area for custom painting |
| `Scrollbar` | Slider / scroll control |
| `Container` | Holds other components; owns a `LayoutManager` |

**Container hierarchy:**

`Container` → `Window` → `Frame`, `Dialog`
`Container` → `Panel` → `Applet`
`Container` → `ScrollPane`

(Include the Mermaid diagram from Section 4.1 as the visual.)

**Key inheritance rule:** *A container is a component, but a component is not necessarily a container.* This is the textbook OOP "is-a" relationship.

#### (b) [7 marks] Student Registration Program

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class StudentRegistration extends Frame
        implements ActionListener {

    Label lblName, lblGender, lblOutput;
    TextField tfName;
    Checkbox chkMale, chkFemale;
    CheckboxGroup cbgGender;
    Button btnSubmit;
    TextArea taOutput;

    StudentRegistration() {
        super("Student Registration");
        setLayout(new BorderLayout());

        // North: heading
        lblOutput = new Label("Fill the form and click Submit");
        add(lblOutput, BorderLayout.NORTH);

        // Center: form using a Panel with GridLayout
        Panel form = new Panel(new GridLayout(3, 2, 5, 5));
        lblName = new Label("Name:");
        tfName  = new TextField(20);
        lblGender = new Label("Gender:");
        cbgGender = new CheckboxGroup();
        chkMale   = new Checkbox("Male", cbgGender, true);
        chkFemale = new Checkbox("Female", cbgGender, false);
        form.add(lblName);
        form.add(tfName);
        form.add(lblGender);
        Panel genderPanel = new Panel(new FlowLayout());
        genderPanel.add(chkMale);
        genderPanel.add(chkFemale);
        form.add(genderPanel);
        form.add(new Label(""));                 // filler
        btnSubmit = new Button("Submit");
        form.add(btnSubmit);
        add(form, BorderLayout.CENTER);

        // South: output TextArea
        taOutput = new TextArea(5, 40);
        taOutput.setEditable(false);
        add(taOutput, BorderLayout.SOUTH);

        btnSubmit.addActionListener(this);

        setSize(420, 320);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String name = tfName.getText();
        Checkbox selected = cbgGender.getSelectedCheckbox();
        String gender = (selected == null) ? "Not specified" : selected.getLabel();
        taOutput.setText("--- Registration Details ---\n"
                       + "Name   : " + name + "\n"
                       + "Gender : " + gender + "\n");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new StudentRegistration();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of design choices (allocate the 7 marks):**
- `BorderLayout` on the `Frame` (1 mark).
- `GridLayout(3,2)` on the central form panel to align label-field pairs (1 mark).
- `CheckboxGroup` enforces mutual exclusion of Male/Female (1 mark).
- `addActionListener(this)` registers the handler (1 mark).
- `actionPerformed` extracts name and gender and updates `TextArea` (2 marks).
- Proper use of `setSize` and `setVisible(true)` (1 mark).

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### (a) [7 marks] AWT Containers

| Container | Constructors | Default Layout | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| `Frame` | `Frame()`, `Frame(String title)` | `BorderLayout` | Top-level main window with title bar |
| `Dialog` | `Dialog(Frame parent)`, `Dialog(Frame parent, String title)`, `Dialog(Frame parent, String title, boolean modal)` | `BorderLayout` | Pop-up requiring user response (modal) or informational (non-modal) |
| `Panel` | `Panel()`, `Panel(LayoutManager)` | `FlowLayout` | Lightweight grouping of components inside a Frame or another Panel |
| `ScrollPane` | `ScrollPane()`, `ScrollPane(int scrollbarDisplayPolicy)` | Internal | Hosts a single (large) child and provides automatic scrollbars |
| `Applet` | No public constructor (instantiated by browser/JVM) | `FlowLayout` | Embeds Java program inside a web page (legacy) |

**The "Window-based" rule (2 marks):** Every top-level window in AWT must ultimately extend `Window`. The two direct subclasses of `Window` are `Frame` and `Dialog`. Therefore, every visible, movable, top-level AWT window is either a `Frame` or a `Dialog`. This is why `Frame` is preferred for the main window and `Dialog` is preferred for pop-ups.

`Panel` is **not** a `Window` subclass and therefore cannot be displayed standalone. It must be added to a `Frame`, `Dialog`, `Applet`, or another `Panel` (which in turn is inside one of these).

#### (b) [7 marks] Calculator GUI

```java
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class CalculatorAWT extends Frame implements ActionListener {
    TextField tfDisplay;
    Button[] btnDigits = new Button[10];
    Button btnAdd, btnSub, btnMul, btnDiv, btnEq;
    String current = "0";
    double operand = 0;
    char operator = ' ';

    CalculatorAWT() {
        super("AWT Calculator");
        setLayout(new BorderLayout());

        // North: display
        tfDisplay = new TextField(25);
        tfDisplay.setText("0");
        tfDisplay.setEditable(false);
        tfDisplay.setBackground(Color.WHITE);
        add(tfDisplay, BorderLayout.NORTH);

        // Center: buttons in GridLayout
        Panel p = new Panel(new GridLayout(4, 4, 5, 5));
        String[] labels = {
            "7","8","9","/",
            "4","5","6","*",
            "1","2","3","-",
            "0","=","+","C"
        };
        for (String lab : labels) {
            Button b = new Button(lab);
            b.addActionListener(this);
            p.add(b);
        }
        add(p, BorderLayout.CENTER);

        setSize(300, 350);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String cmd = e.getActionCommand();
        if (cmd.charAt(0) >= '0' && cmd.charAt(0) <= '9') {
            if (current.equals("0")) current = cmd;
            else current += cmd;
            tfDisplay.setText(current);
        } else if (cmd.equals("+") || cmd.equals("-")
                || cmd.equals("*") || cmd.equals("/")) {
            operand = Double.parseDouble(current);
            operator = cmd.charAt(0);
            current = "0";
        } else if (cmd.equals("=")) {
            double rhs = Double.parseDouble(current);
            double result;
            switch (operator) {
                case '+': result = operand + rhs; break;
                case '-': result = operand - rhs; break;
                case '*': result = operand * rhs; break;
                case '/': result = operand / rhs; break;
                default : result = rhs;
            }
            tfDisplay.setText("" + result);
            current = "0";
        } else if (cmd.equals("C")) {
            current = "0"; operand = 0; operator = ' ';
            tfDisplay.setText("0");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new CalculatorAWT();
    }
}
```

**Mark allocation for (b):**
- Correct `Frame` construction with title (1 mark).
- `TextField` at NORTH, `GridLayout(4,4)` Panel at CENTER (1 mark).
- Sixteen `Button` objects created and added (1 mark).
- `addActionListener(this)` for every button (1 mark).
- `actionPerformed` handles digits, operators, `=`, and `C` (2 marks).
- Correct compilation/visible output demonstration (1 mark).

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting `setVisible(true)`** — the program compiles but nothing appears. Examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 2. **Calling `setSize` on a `Button` inside a `FlowLayout`** — layout overrides the size, demonstrating that the student misunderstands layout-manager authority. Deduct 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting to import `java.awt.event.*`** when using `ActionListener` / `ActionEvent`. Compilation error; partial credit at examiner's discretion (usually 0 for the listener part).
> 4. **Confusing `TextField` with `TextArea`** — writing `new TextField(5, 30)` is invalid (no such constructor). Exam trap.
> 5. **Not closing the `Frame` properly** — although not explicitly asked, the examiner may dock a mark if `WindowListener` is partially implemented and broken.
> 6. **Treating `Frame` as a subclass of `Panel`** — it is not. `Frame` extends `Window`, which extends `Container`, which extends `Component`. Mark deducted for every wrong level in the hierarchy.
> 7. **Using Swing (`JFrame`, `JButton`) instead of AWT** when the question explicitly says AWT. KTU Module 4 is on AWT. Marks are halved for Swing answers.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Component** = abstract root of all visual AWT classes. Provides `setSize`, `setLocation`, `setBounds`, `setBackground`, `setForeground`, `setFont`, `setEnabled`, `setVisible`, `repaint`.
- **Container** = a `Component` that can hold other components. Provides `add`, `remove`, `setLayout`, `getLayout`, `removeAll`, `validate`, `getInsets`.
- **Inheritance chain:** `Object` → `Component` → `Container` → `Window` → `Frame`/`Dialog`. A `Container` *is-a* `Component`.
- **Top-level windows** must be `Window` subclasses — practically either `Frame` (main window) or `Dialog` (popup).
- **Panel** is a non-window container — must live inside a `Frame`, `Dialog`, or `Applet`. Default layout: `FlowLayout`.
- **Frame** default layout: `BorderLayout`. Has a title bar and can host a menu bar.
- **Layout managers** are mandatory for any container; without one, components pile up at (0,0).
- **Leaf components** (Button, Label, TextField, TextArea, Checkbox, Choice, List, Canvas, Scrollbar) **cannot** contain children.
- **TextField and TextArea** are subclasses of `TextComponent`, not direct subclasses of `Component`.
- **CheckboxGroup** is *not* a visual component — it is a logical grouping used to make `Checkbox` instances behave like radio buttons.
- **The standard order to build a window is:** construct Frame → `setLayout` → create components → `add` components → `setSize`/`setLocation` → `setVisible(true)`.
- **Inheritance + polymorphism** mean that a single `setBackground(Color)` call works uniformly on a `Button`, `Label`, `Panel`, or `Frame` because all inherit from `Component`.
- **KTU Module 4 emphasis:** understand hierarchy diagrams, write small GUI programs using `Frame`, `Panel`, and at least three different components, and use one of the layout managers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swing Packages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swing Packages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Java Swing** is a set of class libraries built on top of the Java Abstract Window Toolkit (**AWT**) API, providing a robust, lightweight, and platform-independent graphical user interface (**GUI**) toolkit for the Java SE platform. Swing is an official part of the **Java Foundation Classes (JFC)** and resides primarily in the `javax.swing` package hierarchy.

The **Swing Packages** refer collectively to the cluster of classes, interfaces, and supporting utilities distributed across several packages under the `javax.swing` namespace. The primary packages that constitute the Swing library are:

- **`javax.swing`** → Core Swing component classes (JButton, JLabel, JFrame, JPanel, etc.)
- **`javax.swing.event`** → Event-specific listener interfaces and event classes
- **`javax.swing.border`** → Classes that draw specialized borders around Swing components
- **`javax.swing.plaf`** → Pluggable Look-And-Feel support classes
- **`javax.swing.tree`** → Classes supporting tree-structured data display
- **`javax.swing.table`** → Classes supporting tabular data display
- **`javax.swing.text`** → Classes supporting styled document components
- **`javax.swing.undo`** → Classes supporting undo/redo functionality

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction from AWT**: Every AWT component is a *heavyweight* peer-based component that relies on the underlying operating system's GUI. Swing components are mostly *lightweight* (written entirely in Java without native OS peers), which means they render identically across platforms. The prefix **"J"** is reserved for all Swing top-level and intermediate classes (e.g., `JButton` vs `Button`, `JFrame` vs `Frame`).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition First

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy**: Think of **AWT as a set of basic LEGO blocks** — solid, dependable, but plain and constrained to whatever shapes the manufacturer originally carved. **Swing is like an advanced, modular LEGO Technic set** built on top of those original blocks. You can still use the old bricks, but now you have articulating joints, custom skins (Look-and-Feel), transparent panels, and components you can paint any color. Swing components *paint themselves onto canvases* drawn by heavyweight AWT containers, giving Java programmers near-total visual and behavioral control.

In simpler words: When a Java program creates a `JFrame`, it asks the OS for a real window. Inside that window, every `JButton`, `JLabel`, or `JTextField` is **drawn by Java code onto a canvas** — they aren't real buttons of the operating system. This is why Swing is called *lightweight*.

### 1.3 Key Constants and Metrics

- **Lightweight vs Heavyweight Ratio**: Approximately **95%** of `javax.swing` classes are lightweight; only the top-level containers (`JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow`, `JApplet`) are heavyweight because they need direct OS window-manager interaction.
- **Default Look and Feel Constant**: `UIManager.getSystemLookAndFeelClassName()` returns the platform-specific L&F class name as a `String`.
- **MVC Architecture**: Swing components follow the **Model–View–Controller (MVC)** design pattern, decoupling data (Model) from rendering (View) and input handling (Controller).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Swing Class Hierarchy — Layered Architecture

The Swing library is engineered in a strict, four-tiered inheritance tree. Every component occupies exactly one tier:

| Tier | Category | Purpose | Example Classes |
|:----:|:---------|:--------|:----------------|
| **1** | **Top-Level Containers** | Real OS windows; entry points for any Swing GUI | `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow`, `JApplet` |
| **2** | **General-Purpose Containers** (Intermediate) | Hold and group other components using a LayoutManager | `JPanel`, `JScrollPane`, `JSplitPane`, `JTabbedPane`, `JToolBar` |
| **3** | **Special-Purpose Containers** | Pre-configured containers for specific UI patterns | `JInternalFrame`, `JLayeredPane`, `JRootPane` |
| **4** | **Atomic Components** | Leaf-level controls that interact with the user | `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JComboBox`, `JList`, `JTable`, `JTree`, `JMenu`, `JSlider`, `JProgressBar` |

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step — Operational Logic

1. **Top-Level containers are heavyweight** because they map 1-to-1 with OS windows. You *cannot* add one top-level container directly inside another — they must be top-level entries of the application.
2. **Intermediate containers organize components** with a `LayoutManager`. A `JFrame` itself uses a `BorderLayout` by default; you typically add a `JPanel` (with `FlowLayout` or `GridLayout`) into the frame's content pane.
3. **Atomic components are the visible controls**. They cannot contain other components (a `JButton` cannot hold a `JLabel` directly inside it).

### 2.3 Layout Managers — The Geometry Engine

Every container has a layout manager that dictates **how its children are sized and positioned**. Changing the layout changes the geometry instantly without manual pixel math.

| Layout Manager | Arrangement Strategy | Typical Use Case |
|:---------------|:--------------------|:----------------|
| **`FlowLayout`** | Left-to-right, wraps to next line | Toolbars, button rows |
| **`BorderLayout`** | 5 regions: NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST, CENTER | Main window skeletons |
| **`GridLayout`** | Equal-sized rectangular grid | Calculator keypads |
| **`BoxLayout`** | Single-axis (X or Y) stacking | Vertical/horizontal menus |
| **`GridBagLayout`** | Most flexible — fine-grained cell constraints | Complex forms, IDEs |
| **`CardLayout`** | Stack of cards, one visible at a time | Wizard dialogs |
| **`GroupLayout`** | Hierarchical groups (horizontal/vertical) | NetBeans-generated GUIs |
| **`SpringLayout`** | Spring-like constraints between edges | Resizable form layouts |
| **`null`** | Absolute pixel positioning | Custom-drawn canvases |

> [!NOTE]
> **Default Layouts**: `JFrame` → `BorderLayout`; `JPanel` → `FlowLayout`; `JDialog` → `BorderLayout`.

### 2.4 Event Delegation Model in Swing

Swing uses the same **Event Delegation Model** as AWT but with extended event types defined under `javax.swing.event`:

$$\text{Event Source} \xrightarrow{\text{fires}} \text{Event Object} \xrightarrow{\text{dispatched to}} \text{Event Listener} \xrightarrow{\text{invokes}} \text{Handler Method}$$

- **Event Source**: The component that generates the event (e.g., clicked `JButton`).
- **Event Object**: Carries information (e.g., `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`, `KeyEvent`).
- **Event Listener Interface**: Declares handler methods (e.g., `ActionListener`).
- **Registration**: `source.addListener(listenerInstance);`

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet — Key Methods

| Method Signature | Purpose |
|:-----------------|:--------|
| `JFrame.setDefaultCloseOperation(int op)` | Defines window-close behavior (e.g., `EXIT_ON_CLOSE`) |
| `JFrame.setContentPane(Container c)` | Replaces the default content pane |
| `Container.setLayout(LayoutManager mgr)` | Assigns a layout strategy |
| `Container.add(Component comp, Object constraints)` | Adds a component with optional layout hints |
| `Component.setSize(int w, int h)` | Sets explicit pixel dimensions |
| `Component.setBounds(int x, int y, int w, int h)` | Sets position and size |
| `JButton.addActionListener(ActionListener l)` | Registers a click handler |
| `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(String className)` | Switches Look-and-Feel at runtime |

> [!WARNING]
> A frequently tested point: `setSize()` is ineffective on a `JFrame` until you call `setLayout(null)` OR pack the components using `frame.pack()` after adding them. Calling `setSize()` alone on a frame using `BorderLayout` is overridden.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Swing is foundational in **enterprise engineering tools**, **SCADA/HMI panels**, **embedded device configuration dashboards**, and **legacy banking GUIs**. Its **pluggable Look-and-Feel** allows the *same* code base to render as Windows-style, Motif-style, or the cross-platform **Metal** L&F — a critical feature for distributed engineering teams using mixed operating systems.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Anatomy of a Minimal Swing Application — Step-by-Step Construction

Below is the *complete, line-by-line logical construction* of a Swing program. Every step is annotated so a first-time reader can follow the engineering reasoning.

**Step 1 — Import the packages.** Every Swing class lives in `javax.swing.*`. AWT classes are still needed for event types and colors.

```java
import javax.swing.*;        // Core Swing components (JFrame, JButton, JLabel)
import java.awt.*;           // AWT classes (BorderLayout, FlowLayout, Color, Dimension)
import java.awt.event.*;     // AWT event interfaces (ActionListener, ActionEvent)
```

**Step 2 — Make the class implement an event listener interface.** We declare that this class *promises* to handle `ActionEvent`s.

```java
public class SwingDemo implements ActionListener {
```

**Step 3 — Declare the GUI components as instance fields.** This lets every method (especially the listener callback) reference them.

```java
    JFrame frame;
    JButton clickButton;
    JLabel messageLabel;
    int clickCount = 0;
```

**Step 4 — Build the constructor.** Inside, we instantiate each component, configure it, attach listeners, add components to containers, and finally make the frame visible.

```java
    SwingDemo() {
        frame = new JFrame("KTU Swing Demonstration");
        frame.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        frame.setSize(420, 200);
        frame.setLayout(new FlowLayout());

        messageLabel = new JLabel("Button not clicked yet.");
        messageLabel.setFont(new Font("Arial", Font.BOLD, 16));

        clickButton = new JButton("Click Me!");
        clickButton.addActionListener(this);   // 'this' is the ActionListener

        frame.add(messageLabel);
        frame.add(clickButton);

        frame.setVisible(true);
    }
```

**Step 5 — Implement the event-handler method.** The compiler *forces* this method because `ActionListener` declares it abstract.

```java
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        clickCount++;
        messageLabel.setText("Button clicked " + clickCount + " times.");
    }
```

**Step 6 — Entry point on the Event Dispatch Thread (EDT).** Best practice requires GUI creation on the EDT for thread-safety.

```java
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(new Runnable() {
            public void run() {
                new SwingDemo();
            }
        });
    }
}
```

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Why Each Line Exists

| Line of Code | Engineering Reason |
|:-------------|:-------------------|
| `implements ActionListener` | Forces the class to declare `actionPerformed()`, satisfying the callback contract. |
| `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)` | Tells the JVM to terminate when the user closes the window — without this, the window closes but the JVM keeps running. |
| `setLayout(new FlowLayout())` | Overrides the default `BorderLayout` so components stack left-to-right. |
| `addActionListener(this)` | Registers the *current object* as the recipient of button-click events. |
| `frame.setVisible(true)` | Components exist in memory before this call but remain invisible. Setting visible triggers the Swing painting pipeline. |
| `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(...)` | Schedules GUI creation on the Event Dispatch Thread, preventing race conditions in concurrent repainting. |

### 3.3 Mathematical Model of the Event Dispatch Thread

The EDT obeys a strict *single-threaded queue* model. For any GUI event *eᵢ* enqueued at time *tᵢ*:

$$ \text{Order of dispatch} = e_1, e_2, e_3, \ldots, e_n \quad \text{where} \quad t_1 \leq t_2 \leq t_3 \leq \ldots \leq t_n $$

If the handler for event *eᵢ* takes time *Δtᵢ*, the next event *eᵢ₊₁* cannot begin dispatching until:

$$ t_{\text{dispatch}}(e_{i+1}) \geq t_{\text{dispatch}}(e_i) + \Delta t_i $$

This guarantees **no two GUI events ever execute concurrently** — a property that simplifies Swing threading enormously compared to AWT.

### 3.4 Layout Manager — Worked Example (GridLayout)

Suppose we are building a calculator with **16 buttons** (digits 0–9, operators +, −, ×, ÷, =, and Clear). We want a **4×4 grid**.

```java
JPanel calcPanel = new JPanel();
calcPanel.setLayout(new GridLayout(4, 4, 5, 5));   // 4 rows, 4 cols, 5px gaps
String[] labels = {"7","8","9","/",
                   "4","5","6","*",
                   "1","2","3","-",
                   "C","0","=","+"};
for (String lbl : labels) {
    calcPanel.add(new JButton(lbl));
}
```

**Logical breakdown**:
- `GridLayout(4, 4, 5, 5)` declares 4 rows, 4 columns, 5-pixel horizontal gap, 5-pixel vertical gap.
- Every cell has identical width = (panel_width − 3×5) / 4 and identical height = (panel_height − 3×5) / 4.
- The `for-each` loop iterates through 16 labels and pushes one button per cell — order matters because `GridLayout` fills row-major.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Swing Class Hierarchy (Inheritance Map)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[java.lang.Object] --> B[java.awt.Component]
    B --> C[java.awt.Container]
    C --> D[java.awt.Window]
    D --> E[java.awt.Frame]
    E --> F[JFrame]
    D --> G[JDialog]
    C --> H[JPanel]
    H --> I[JScrollPane]
    H --> J[JTabbedPane]
    B --> K[JComponent]
    K --> L[JButton]
    K --> M[JLabel]
    K --> N[JTextField]
    K --> O[JTextArea]
    K --> P[JCheckBox]
    K --> Q[JRadioButton]
    K --> R[JComboBox]
    K --> S[JList]
    K --> T[JTable]
    K --> U[JTree]
    K --> V[JMenuBar]
    K --> W[JMenu]
    K --> X[JMenuItem]
    K --> Y[JSlider]
    K --> Z[JProgressBar]
```

**Reading the diagram**: `JFrame` is a `Frame` is a `Window` is a `Container` is a `Component` is an `Object`. `JComponent` is the abstract base class for **every lightweight Swing component** except the top-level containers.

### 4.2 Package Structure & Class Mapping (Block-Level Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
graph LR
    pkgSwing[javax.swing] --> frame[JFrame]
    pkgSwing --> panel[JPanel]
    pkgSwing --> button[JButton]
    pkgSwing --> label[JLabel]
    pkgSwing --> combo[JComboBox]
    pkgSwing --> list[JList]
    pkgSwing --> table[JTable]
    pkgSwing --> umgr[UIManager]

    pkgEvent[javax.swing.event] --> chEvt[ChangeEvent]
    pkgEvent --> listEvt[ListSelectionEvent]
    pkgEvent --> menuEvt[MenuEvent]
    pkgEvent --> tblEvt[TableModelEvent]

    pkgBorder[javax.swing.border] --> brd1[LineBorder]
    pkgBorder --> brd2[EtchedBorder]
    pkgBorder --> brd3[TitledBorder]
    pkgBorder --> brd4[EmptyBorder]

    pkgPlaf[javax.swing.plaf] --> metal[MetalLookAndFeel]
    pkgPlaf --> motif[MotifLookAndFeel]
    pkgPlaf --> win[WindowsLookAndFeel]

    pkgTable[javax.swing.table] --> dtm[DefaultTableModel]
    pkgTable --> tc[TableColumn]

    pkgTree[javax.swing.tree] --> dtn[DefaultTreeModel]
    pkgTree --> tn[TreeNode]
```

### 4.3 Event Delegation Flow Diagram

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant User
    participant Button as JButton
    participant Listener as ActionListener
    participant Handler as actionPerformed()

    User->>Button: Mouse click on button surface
    Button->>Button: Detect event type (ActionEvent)
    Button->>Listener: dispatchEvent(ActionEvent e)
    Listener->>Handler: invoke actionPerformed(e)
    Handler-->>User: GUI updates label text
    Handler-->>Button: Optionally toggle button state
```

### 4.4 Layout Manager Strategy Selection Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Need to arrange components?] --> B{How many components?}
    B -- 1 to 5 linear --> C[FlowLayout]
    B -- 5 regions --> D[BorderLayout]
    B -- Equal grid --> E[GridLayout]
    B -- Variable sizes --> F[GridBagLayout]
    B -- Tabs --> G[JTabbedPane]
    B -- Wizard slides --> H[CardLayout]
    B -- Pannable area --> I[JScrollPane]
    B -- Custom drawing --> J[null layout]
```

### 4.5 Top-Level Container Internal Structure (JFrame Anatomy)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[JFrame Window] --> B[Root Pane]
    B --> C[Glass Pane]
    B --> D[Layered Pane]
    D --> E[Content Pane]
    D --> F[Menu Bar]
    E --> G[User Components]
    E --> H[JPanels and Buttons]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Glass Pane** sits invisibly on top of everything and is used for intercepting mouse events over the entire window. The **Layered Pane** holds the Content Pane and Menu Bar at different depths. The **Content Pane** is where you actually `add()` your components.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**List any three differences between AWT and Swing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| # | AWT | Swing |
|:-:|:----|:------|
| 1 | Components are *heavyweight* (rely on OS peers). | Components are mostly *lightweight* (drawn by Java). |
| 2 | Limited set of components; no `JTable`, `JTree`, `JTabbedPane`. | Rich component library including tables, trees, tabbed panes. |
| 3 | Platform-dependent look and feel. | Pluggable Look-and-Feel (`Metal`, `Motif`, `System`). |
| 4 | Class names do **not** start with `J`. | All Swing classes are prefixed with `J`. |
| 5 | Components cannot have transparent backgrounds. | Supports transparency and custom painting. |

*(Any 3 points = 3 marks. Award 1 mark per valid difference.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of `UIManager` in Swing? Write the syntax to set the system Look-and-Feel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

`UIManager` is a Swing utility class that allows Java applications to **query and replace the current Look-and-Feel (L&F)** — the visual appearance of every Swing component. It provides methods such as `setLookAndFeel(String className)` and `getSystemLookAndFeelClassName()`.

**Syntax**:

```java
try {
    UIManager.setLookAndFeel(UIManager.getSystemLookAndFeelClassName());
} catch (Exception e) {
    System.out.println("L&F not supported: " + e.getMessage());
}
```

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for correct syntax. Full marks only if try-catch is included.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End Semester Examinations require **internal choice** within each module. Below are **Question A** and **Question B**, each with sub-parts (a) of 7 marks and (b) of 7 marks. Either question can appear on the exam paper.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU Model Paper — 2024 Scheme]*

**(a)** Explain the Swing class hierarchy. Differentiate between Top-Level containers, General-Purpose containers, and Atomic components with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program to create a simple **Login Form** having two `JLabel`s ("Username", "Password"), two `JTextField`s, a `JButton` ("Submit"), and display a `JOptionPane` confirming login when the button is clicked. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The Swing class hierarchy is rooted in `java.lang.Object` and descends through AWT classes before branching into Swing-specific lightweight and heavyweight components. **Top-Level Containers** (`JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow`, `JApplet`) are heavyweight — each maps to an OS window and forms the entry point of a Swing application. **General-Purpose Containers** (`JPanel`, `JScrollPane`, `JSplitPane`, `JTabbedPane`, `JToolBar`) are lightweight intermediate containers used to group and organize other components using a `LayoutManager`. **Atomic Components** (`JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, `JComboBox`, `JList`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`) are leaf-level controls that interact with the user but cannot contain other components.

*Mark split: 2 marks hierarchy explanation, 3 marks categories with examples, 2 marks any comparison diagram.*

**Model Solution (b):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class LoginForm implements ActionListener {
    JFrame f;
    JTextField userField;
    JPasswordField passField;
    JButton submitBtn;

    LoginForm() {
        f = new JFrame("Login");
        f.setSize(320, 180);
        f.setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        f.setLayout(new GridLayout(3, 2, 10, 10));

        f.add(new JLabel("Username:"));
        userField = new JTextField();
        f.add(userField);

        f.add(new JLabel("Password:"));
        passField = new JPasswordField();
        f.add(passField);

        submitBtn = new JButton("Submit");
        submitBtn.addActionListener(this);
        f.add(submitBtn);

        f.setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String user = userField.getText();
        JOptionPane.showMessageDialog(f, "Welcome, " + user + "!");
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(LoginForm::new);
    }
}
```

*Mark split: 2 marks frame setup, 2 marks components added with layout, 1 mark listener registration, 2 marks `actionPerformed` implementation with `JOptionPane`.*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — June 2023]*

**(a)** Explain the **Event Delegation Model** used in Swing with a neat diagram. List any four Swing-specific event listener interfaces from `javax.swing.event`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program demonstrating the use of `BorderLayout` with five components placed in NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST, and CENTER regions. The CENTER component should change its color when the button in NORTH is clicked. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The **Event Delegation Model** decouples event producers from event consumers. A component (the *event source*) does not handle its own events; instead, it maintains a list of registered *listener* objects and dispatches each `EventObject` to them when the event occurs. The listener implements a handler interface (e.g., `ActionListener.actionPerformed`). This pattern allows multiple listeners per source, dynamic registration, and clean separation of concerns.

**Swing-specific listeners** in `javax.swing.event`:

1. `ChangeListener` — handles state changes in sliders, tabs, spinners.
2. `ListSelectionListener` — reacts to row selection in `JList`.
3. `TreeSelectionListener` — reacts to node selection in `JTree`.
4. `TableModelListener` — reacts to row/column changes in `JTable`.
5. `MenuListener` — reacts to menu opening/closing.
6. `CaretListener` — tracks text-caret position changes.

*Mark split: 3 marks model explanation + diagram, 2 marks diagram, 2 marks for any 4 Swing-specific listeners.*

**Model Solution (b):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class BorderDemo extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JPanel centerPanel;
    JButton colorBtn;

    BorderDemo() {
        setTitle("BorderLayout Demo");
        setSize(400, 300);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new BorderLayout(10, 10));

        add(new JButton("NORTH Button"), BorderLayout.NORTH);
        add(new JButton("SOUTH Button"), BorderLayout.SOUTH);
        add(new JButton("WEST Button"), BorderLayout.WEST);
        add(new JButton("EAST Button"), BorderLayout.EAST);

        centerPanel = new JPanel();
        centerPanel.setBackground(Color.YELLOW);
        add(centerPanel, BorderLayout.CENTER);

        colorBtn = new JButton("Change Center Color");
        colorBtn.addActionListener(this);
        add(colorBtn, BorderLayout.NORTH);   // replaces earlier NORTH label

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        centerPanel.setBackground(new Color(
            (int)(Math.random() * 255),
            (int)(Math.random() * 255),
            (int)(Math.random() * 255)
        ));
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(BorderDemo::new);
    }
}
```

*Mark split: 2 marks `BorderLayout` setup, 2 marks all 5 regions used correctly, 1 mark listener registration, 2 marks `actionPerformed` producing random RGB color.*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students most commonly lose marks in Swing questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting `setDefaultCloseOperation(EXIT_ON_CLOSE)`** → window closes but JVM keeps running; examiner deducts 1 mark.
> 2. **Calling `setSize()` on a `JFrame` after adding components without `frame.pack()`** → layout overrides size; examiner deducts 1 mark for "non-functional UI".
> 3. **Using `AWT Button` instead of `JButton`** → entire answer treated as AWT, not Swing; up to 50% marks deducted.
> 4. **Listener implementation syntax errors** — students often write `addActionListner` (typo). Examiners are strict on spelling.
> 5. **Mixing up `add(comp)` vs `add(comp, BorderLayout.NORTH)`** — without the constraint, only the CENTER region is populated.
> 6. **Not importing `javax.swing.*` separately from `java.awt.*`** — using `Label` instead of `JLabel` is a recurring mistake.

### 5.4 Topic Recap — High-Density Revision Checklist

- **Core Packages**: `javax.swing`, `javax.swing.event`, `javax.swing.border`, `javax.swing.plaf`, `javax.swing.table`, `javax.swing.tree`, `javax.swing.text`, `javax.swing.undo`.
- **Top-Level Containers**: `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow`, `JApplet` — heavyweight.
- **Intermediate Containers**: `JPanel`, `JScrollPane`, `JSplitPane`, `JTabbedPane`, `JToolBar`.
- **Atomic Components**: `JButton`, `JLabel`, `JTextField`, `JTextArea`, `JCheckBox`, `JRadioButton`, `JComboBox`, `JList`, `JTable`, `JTree`, `JSlider`, `JMenu`, `JMenuBar`, `JMenuItem`.
- **Hierarchy root**: `java.lang.Object` → `Component` → `Container` → `Window` → `Frame` → `JFrame`.
- **Lightweight base class**: `JComponent` (abstract) — parent of all lightweight Swing components.
- **Default Layouts**: `JFrame` → `BorderLayout`; `JPanel` → `FlowLayout`.
- **Event Delegation Model**: Source → Event Object → Listener → Handler.
- **Look-and-Feel**: `UIManager.setLookAndFeel(String className)`.
- **EDT Best Practice**: Wrap GUI creation in `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(Runnable r)`.
- **MVC Pattern**: Most Swing components separate data (Model), display (View), and input (Controller).
- **Key Suffix**: Every Swing class starts with **`J`** — examiners explicitly look for this prefix.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Swing Layout Managers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Swing Layout Managers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Layout Manager** in Java Swing (and AWT) is an object that implements the `LayoutManager` (or its sub-interface `LayoutManager2`) interface defined in `java.awt`. It dictates the **size**, **position**, and **spatial arrangement** of components placed inside a `Container` (such as `JFrame`, `JPanel`, `JDialog`, or `JApplet`). The layout manager dynamically negotiates with each component by querying its **preferred**, **minimum**, and **maximum** sizes via the `Component.getPreferredSize()`, `getMinimumSize()`, and `getMaximumSize()` methods, and then arranges the children according to its specific positioning policy.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Layout Managers are the **Strategy Design Pattern** in action: the container *delegates* the layout decision to a pluggable strategy object, allowing the *same container* to be visually restructured merely by swapping the strategy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> "Swing components, event handling, layout managers (BorderLayout, FlowLayout, GridLayout, GridBagLayout, BoxLayout), menus, dialog boxes."

The pre-defined layout classes that students must master are:
1. `java.awt.BorderLayout`
2. `java.awt.FlowLayout`
3. `java.awt.GridLayout`
4. `java.awt.GridBagLayout`
5. `javax.swing.BoxLayout`
6. `java.awt.CardLayout`

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition: The "Smart Furniture Arranger"**
> Imagine you are moving into a new hostel room. You tell your roommate, *"Arrange the bed, table, and bookshelf in a way that looks good."* Your roommate has a **strategy**:
> - **BorderLayout** is like putting the bookshelf against the North wall, the bed against the South wall, and the table in the center — fixed compass directions.
> - **FlowLayout** is like laying items on a shelf, one after another from left to right, and when the shelf is full, continuing on the next row.
> - **GridLayout** is like a tic-tac-toe board — an exact number of equal-sized cells.
> - **BoxLayout** is like stacking items vertically in a single tall column, or a single horizontal row.
> - **CardLayout** is like a deck of playing cards — only the top card is visible at any time.

The **Layout Manager** is that smart roommate. Instead of you manually calculating `setBounds(50, 80, 200, 150)` for every button (which would break the moment the window is resized), the layout manager **negotiates the geometry automatically** based on the container's current size.

## 1.3 Why Are Layout Managers Mandatory?

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Platform Independence** | Pixel coordinates that look right on Windows break on macOS/Linux. Layout managers adapt to font metrics and look-and-feel. |
| **Dynamic Resizing** | When the user drags the window corner, components must reflow correctly. Hand-coded coordinates are static. |
| **Maintainability** | Adding/removing a component does not require recalculating pixel offsets. |
| **Internationalization** | German labels are longer than English ones. Layout managers expand containers to fit. |

> [!TIP]
> The `setLayout(null)` call **disables** the layout manager. While you can then call `component.setBounds(x, y, w, h)`, this is **strongly discouraged** in KTU-graded assignments unless the question explicitly asks for it (e.g., "design a calculator with absolute positioning").

## 1.4 The `LayoutManager` Interface — Contract

```java
public interface LayoutManager {
    void addLayoutComponent(String name, Component comp);
    void removeLayoutComponent(Component comp);
    Dimension preferredLayoutSize(Container parent);
    Dimension minimumLayoutSize(Container parent);
    void layoutContainer(Container parent);
}
```

The sub-interface `LayoutManager2` extends it to support **constraints-based** layouts (like `BorderLayout.NORTH` or `GridBagConstraints`).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Phase Layout Negotiation Protocol

Whenever a container is validated (via `validate()` or implicitly after `pack()` / `setVisible(true)`), the layout manager executes this 4-phase protocol:

1. **Trigger Phase** — `Container.validate()` is called, usually automatically.
2. **Query Phase** — The manager calls `preferredLayoutSize(parent)` and `minimumLayoutSize(parent)` on **every child** to learn its dimensions.
3. **Compute Phase** — The manager uses its internal algorithm to compute the final `(x, y, width, height)` for each child, constrained by the parent container's bounds.
4. **Apply Phase** — The manager calls `child.setBounds(x, y, w, h)` on each component.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for KTU:** `setBounds()` is invoked by the **layout manager**, not by the programmer. If you call `setBounds()` *after* `setVisible(true)`, your values will be **overwritten** by the manager on the next validation cycle.

## 2.2 Comprehensive Layout Manager Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Reference Table — Memorize for ESE**

| Layout Manager | Class Name | Cell Structure | Component Count | Resize Behavior | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `BorderLayout` | `java.awt.BorderLayout` | 5 regions (N, S, E, W, CENTER) | Up to 5 (or 5 groups) | Center expands; N/S stretch horizontally; E/W stretch vertically | App shells, dialogs |
| `FlowLayout` | `java.awt.FlowLayout` | Single row that wraps | Unlimited | Components stay at preferred size; rows reflow | Button bars, toolbars |
| `GridLayout` | `java.awt.GridLayout` | $r \times c$ equal cells | Exactly $r \times c$ | All cells expand uniformly to fill container | Keypads, tic-tac-toe |
| `GridBagLayout` | `java.awt.GridBagLayout` | Variable cell grid | Unlimited | Highly flexible; cells can span multiple grid cells | Professional forms |
| `BoxLayout` | `javax.swing.BoxLayout` | Single row OR single column | Unlimited | Components retain preferred size; uses glue/struts | Vertical menus, toolbars |
| `CardLayout` | `java.awt.CardLayout` | A "stack" of panels | Unlimited | Only the "top" card is visible | Wizards, tabbed interfaces (pre-JTabbedPane) |

## 2.3 Core Mathematical Sizing Relationships

Let $W_c$ and $H_c$ denote the container's width and height, and let $n$ denote the number of components.

### 2.3.1 BorderLayout Equations

The Center region receives all remaining space after the side regions are placed:

$$W_{\text{center}} = W_c - W_{\text{east}} - W_{\text{west}}$$

$$H_{\text{center}} = H_c - H_{\text{north}} - H_{\text{south}}$$

The North and South regions span the full container width:

$$W_{\text{north}} = W_{\text{south}} = W_c$$

The East and West regions occupy the remaining height after North and South:

$$H_{\text{east}} = H_{\text{west}} = H_c - H_{\text{north}} - H_{\text{south}}$$

### 2.3.2 GridLayout Equations

With $r$ rows and $c$ columns, the cell dimensions are:

$$w_{\text{cell}} = \frac{W_c - (\text{horizontal gap}) \cdot (c + 1)}{c}$$

$$h_{\text{cell}} = \frac{H_c - (\text{vertical gap}) \cdot (r + 1)}{r}$$

The **horizontal gap** (default $0$ px) is set via `new GridLayout(r, c, hgap, vgap)`.

### 2.3.3 FlowLayout Equations

A row break occurs when the cumulative preferred width exceeds the container width:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i + (k+1) \cdot \text{hgap} > W_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{break before component } k+1$$

The **alignment** is one of `FlowLayout.LEFT` (default), `CENTER`, or `RIGHT`.

### 2.3.4 GridBagLayout — The Constraint Tuple

Each component is associated with a `GridBagConstraints` object specifying a 12-field constraint tuple. The most important fields are:

| Field | Type | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `gridx`, `gridy` | `int` | Grid coordinates of the cell's top-left corner (use `GridBagConstraints.RELATIVE` for "next"). |
| `gridwidth`, `gridheight` | `int` | Number of cells the component spans. Use `REMAINDER` (last in row/col) or `RELATIVE` (next-to-last). |
| `fill` | `int` | `NONE`, `HORIZONTAL`, `VERTICAL`, or `BOTH` — how the component fills its display area. |
| `weightx`, `weighty` | `double` | How extra space is distributed. **Crucial**: defaults are $0.0$, which means the component does **not** grow. |
| `anchor` | `int` | Where the component sits within its display area (`CENTER`, `NORTHWEST`, etc.). |
| `insets` | `Insets` | External padding around the cell (top, left, bottom, right). |
| `ipadx`, `ipady` | `int` | Internal padding added to the component's preferred size. |

### 2.3.5 BoxLayout Sizing

`BoxLayout` honors three special **invisible components** that act like springs:

- **`Glue`** — Expands to fill all available space.
- **`Strut`** — Fixed-size invisible spacer.
- **`Rigid Area`** — Fixed-size invisible spacer in *both* dimensions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **GUI Development** | NetBeans, IntelliJ, Eclipse all use `GridBagLayout` for their property sheets and form designers. |
| **Embedded Systems** | Touch-screen UIs on medical devices rely on layout managers to render consistently across $480 \times 800$ and $1080 \times 1920$ panels. |
| **Web-Desktop Hybrid** | JavaFX inherits this same strategy pattern; the conceptual model transfers directly. |
| **KTU Labs** | The OOP-Java lab cycles 6–9 require you to construct a **Calculator** or **Login Form** — both demand `GridBagLayout` or nested `BorderLayout`+`GridLayout`. |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 BorderLayout — Full Derivation Walkthrough

### 3.1.1 Code Skeleton

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class BorderLayoutDemo extends JFrame {
    public BorderLayoutDemo() {
        setTitle("BorderLayout Demo");
        setSize(400, 300);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new BorderLayout(5, 5));   // 5px horizontal & vertical gap

        add(new JButton("NORTH"), BorderLayout.NORTH);
        add(new JButton("SOUTH"), BorderLayout.SOUTH);
        add(new JButton("EAST"),  BorderLayout.EAST);
        add(new JButton("WEST"),  BorderLayout.WEST);
        add(new JButton("CENTER"),BorderLayout.CENTER);

        setVisible(true);
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new BorderLayoutDemo();
    }
}
```

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Layout Derivation

Suppose the user resizes the window to $W_c = 400$ px and $H_c = 300$ px, and each button's preferred width/height is $w_p = 80$, $h_p = 30$.

**Step 1** — Place the North region at the top, spanning the full container width:

$$W_{\text{north}} = W_c = 400 \text{ px}$$

**Step 2** — Place the South region at the bottom, also spanning the full container width:

$$W_{\text{south}} = W_c = 400 \text{ px}$$

**Step 3** — Compute the remaining vertical space between North and South:

$$H_{\text{middle}} = H_c - H_{\text{north}} - H_{\text{south}} = 300 - 30 - 30 = 240 \text{ px}$$

**Step 4** — Place East and West regions within that middle band, each using the full remaining height:

$$H_{\text{east}} = H_{\text{west}} = H_{\text{middle}} = 240 \text{ px}$$

**Step 5** — Compute the Center width after East and West are placed:

$$W_{\text{center}} = W_c - W_{\text{east}} - W_{\text{west}} = 400 - 80 - 80 = 240 \text{ px}$$

**Step 6** — Final coordinate table:

| Region | x | y | width | height |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| NORTH | 0 | 0 | 400 | 30 |
| SOUTH | 0 | 270 | 400 | 30 |
| WEST | 0 | 30 | 80 | 240 |
| EAST | 320 | 30 | 80 | 240 |
| CENTER | 80 | 30 | 240 | 240 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** `BorderLayout` silently **overwrites** components added with the same region. The second component added to `NORTH` replaces the first. To place multiple components in one region, nest a `JPanel` with its own layout inside the region.

## 3.2 GridLayout — Step-by-Step Derivation

```java
JPanel p = new JPanel(new GridLayout(3, 3, 2, 2));
for (int i = 1; i <= 9; i++) p.add(new JButton(String.valueOf(i)));
```

For a $3 \times 3$ grid in a $W_c = 300, H_c = 300$ container with `hgap = vgap = 2`:

$$w_{\text{cell}} = \frac{300 - 2 \cdot (3 + 1)}{3} = \frac{300 - 8}{3} = \frac{292}{3} \approx 97.33 \text{ px}$$

$$h_{\text{cell}} = \frac{300 - 2 \cdot (3 + 1)}{3} = 97.33 \text{ px}$$

The button at cell $(i, j)$ (zero-indexed) has its top-left corner at:

$$x_{i,j} = 2 + j \cdot (97.33 + 2) = 2 + 99.33 \cdot j$$

$$y_{i,j} = 2 + i \cdot (97.33 + 2) = 2 + 99.33 \cdot i$$

## 3.3 GridBagLayout — Full Worked Example (Login Form)

This is the **most frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU papers. Let us construct a typical login form.

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class LoginForm extends JFrame {
    public LoginForm() {
        setTitle("Login");
        setLayout(new GridBagLayout());
        GridBagConstraints gbc = new GridBagConstraints();
        gbc.insets = new Insets(5, 5, 5, 5);
        gbc.anchor = GridBagConstraints.WEST;

        // Row 0: Username label
        gbc.gridx = 0; gbc.gridy = 0;
        gbc.weightx = 0;   // label does not stretch
        add(new JLabel("Username:"), gbc);

        // Row 0: Username field
        gbc.gridx = 1; gbc.gridy = 0;
        gbc.weightx = 1.0; // field stretches horizontally
        gbc.fill = GridBagConstraints.HORIZONTAL;
        JTextField user = new JTextField(15);
        add(user, gbc);

        // Row 1: Password label
        gbc.gridx = 0; gbc.gridy = 1;
        gbc.weightx = 0;
        gbc.fill = GridBagConstraints.NONE;
        add(new JLabel("Password:"), gbc);

        // Row 1: Password field
        gbc.gridx = 1; gbc.gridy = 1;
        gbc.weightx = 1.0;
        gbc.fill = GridBagConstraints.HORIZONTAL;
        JPasswordField pass = new JPasswordField(15);
        add(pass, gbc);

        // Row 2: Login button spanning both columns
        gbc.gridx = 0; gbc.gridy = 2;
        gbc.gridwidth = 2;        // spans two columns
        gbc.weightx = 0;
        gbc.anchor = GridBagConstraints.CENTER;
        add(new JButton("Login"), gbc);

        pack();
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setVisible(true);
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new LoginForm();
    }
}
```

### 3.3.1 Logical Derivation of the `weightx` / `weighty` Distribution

Suppose after placing the labels and fields, $200$ extra pixels of horizontal space remain. The fields have `weightx = 1.0` and the labels have `weightx = 0.0`.

**Step 1** — Sum the weights along the row:

$$\sum w_i = 0 + 1.0 + 0 + 1.0 = 2.0$$

**Step 2** — Compute each field's share:

$$W_{\text{field, extra}} = \frac{1.0}{2.0} \times 200 = 100 \text{ px}$$

**Step 3** — Final field width:

$$W_{\text{field}} = W_{\text{preferred}} + W_{\text{extra}} = 100 + 100 = 200 \text{ px}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Why `weightx = 0.0` is the silent killer:** If you forget to set `weightx > 0` on a field, the layout manager will compute the grid using **only preferred sizes** and leave the extra $200$ px unallocated in the **center of the window**. This is the #1 reason students' "stretching" forms fail.

## 3.4 BoxLayout — Vertical Toolbar

```java
JPanel toolbar = new JPanel();
toolbar.setLayout(new BoxLayout(toolbar, BoxLayout.Y_AXIS));
toolbar.add(new JButton("New"));
toolbar.add(Box.createVerticalStrut(10));    // 10px invisible spacer
toolbar.add(new JButton("Open"));
toolbar.add(Box.createVerticalGlue());        // absorbs all remaining space
toolbar.add(new JButton("Exit"));
```

**Derivation of vertical glue behavior:**

If the toolbar height is $H_t = 400$ and the three buttons consume $90$ px in total, the **glue** receives:

$$H_{\text{glue}} = H_t - \sum H_{\text{buttons}} - H_{\text{strut}} = 400 - 90 - 10 = 300 \text{ px}$$

## 3.5 CardLayout — Wizard Pattern

```java
JPanel cards = new JPanel(new CardLayout());
cards.add(panel1, "Step1");
cards.add(panel2, "Step2");
((CardLayout) cards.getLayout()).show(cards, "Step2");
```

Only **one** card is visible at a time; the others have $\{(x, y, w, h)\}$ set to $(0, 0, W_c, H_c)$ but are stacked in z-order above/below the visible one.

## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Property | BorderLayout | FlowLayout | GridLayout | GridBagLayout | BoxLayout |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Honors preferred size? | Yes (sides) | Yes | Stretches to cell | Configurable | Yes |
| Allows overlapping? | No | No | No | No | No |
| Container must use `setLayout()`? | Yes (default for `JFrame` content pane) | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Default container? | `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow` content panes | `JPanel` | — | — | — |
| Gap parameter? | `BorderLayout(hgap, vgap)` | `FlowLayout(align, hgap, vgap)` | `GridLayout(r, c, hgap, vgap)` | Via `Insets` per cell | `Strut` / `Glue` |
| Constraint object? | Region string | Alignment int | (r, c) in constructor | `GridBagConstraints` | Axis constant |
| Resize elasticity | High (center grows) | Low (reflows) | High (uniform) | Maximum | Medium |

## 4.2 FlowLayout Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Container resized] --> B[LayoutManager.layoutContainer invoked]
    B --> C[Iterate components in order]
    C --> D{Does current row exceed container width?}
    D -- No --> E[Place at next x position]
    E --> F{More components?}
    F -- Yes --> C
    F -- No --> G[Done]
    D -- Yes --> H[Start new row: x = hgap, y = y + rowHeight]
    H --> E
```
