# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Q: Differentiate between `BorderLayout` and `FlowLayout` in Java Swing. State the default layout manager of `JFrame` and `JPanel`.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `BorderLayout` | `FlowLayout` |
|---|---|---|
| **Structure** | 5 fixed regions (NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST, CENTER) | Sequential row-based arrangement that wraps to next line |
| **Resizing** | Center expands; sides stretch along one axis | Components stay at preferred size; new rows created on overflow |
| **Component count** | Maximum 5 (or 5 nested panels) | Unlimited |
| **Default for** | `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow` content panes | `JPanel` |

- **Default of `JFrame` content pane:** `BorderLayout`  ✔ (1 mark)
- **Default of `JPanel`:** `FlowLayout`  ✔ (1 mark)
- **Differentiation table** ✔ (1 mark)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

**Q: What is the role of `weightx` and `weighty` in `GridBagConstraints`? What happens if both are set to `0.0`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

`weightx` and `weighty` are `double` fields in `GridBagConstraints` that determine **how extra space is distributed** among components when the container is larger than the sum of preferred sizes. The values are **proportional**: if two cells have `weightx = 1.0` and a third has `weightx = 2.0`, the third receives **twice** as much of the surplus horizontal space. (2 marks)

If all components have `weightx = 0.0` and `weighty = 0.0`, **the components cluster at their preferred size** in the top-left corner of the container, and the surplus space is **left unused** (an empty band appears on the right and bottom of the window). (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, End Semester]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the `GridBagLayout` manager in detail. List any **five** important fields of the `GridBagConstraints` class. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a complete Java Swing program using `GridBagLayout` to create a **Login Form** with the following components:
- Two `JLabel`s ("Username:", "Password:")
- One `JTextField` (15 columns)
- One `JPasswordField` (15 columns)
- One `JButton` ("Login") centered below

The text fields must **stretch horizontally** when the window is resized. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

`GridBagLayout` is the most flexible and complex layout manager in the AWT/Swing framework. It arranges components in a **virtual grid of rectangular cells** where each cell can have a different size, and a single component can span **multiple rows and/or multiple columns**. It is the only layout manager that uses a separate constraint object (`GridBagConstraints`) to describe *how* each component should be placed.

**Five important fields of `GridBagConstraints`:** (1 mark each)

1. **`gridx`, `gridy`** — The column and row of the cell's top-left corner where the component begins. `GridBagConstraints.RELATIVE` places the component "next" to the previously added one.

2. **`gridwidth`, `gridheight`** — The number of columns and rows the component occupies. `GridBagConstraints.REMAINDER` means "this is the last cell in the row/column."

3. **`fill`** — How the component fills its display area. Possible values: `NONE`, `HORIZONTAL`, `VERTICAL`, `BOTH`. To make text fields stretch, use `HORIZONTAL`. (1 mark)

4. **`weightx`, `weighty`** — Determines how surplus space is distributed. Default `0.0` means "do not grow." To allow a component to grow, set `weightx > 0` for horizontal growth and `weighty > 0` for vertical growth. (1 mark)

5. **`insets`** — A `java.awt.Insets` object specifying the **external padding** (top, left, bottom, right) around the cell, in pixels. (1 mark)

*Additional fields for completeness:* `anchor` (positioning within the cell), `ipadx`/`ipady` (internal padding). (1 mark for the overall explanation)

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class LoginForm extends JFrame {

    public LoginForm() {
        setTitle("Login Form");
        setLayout(new GridBagLayout());
        GridBagConstraints g = new GridBagConstraints();
        g.insets = new Insets(8, 8, 8, 8);   // 8px padding around each cell

        // --- Username label ---
        g.gridx = 0; g.gridy = 0;
        g.weightx = 0; g.weighty = 0;
        g.fill = GridBagConstraints.NONE;
        g.anchor = GridBagConstraints.WEST;
        add(new JLabel("Username:"), g);

        // --- Username text field ---
        g.gridx = 1; g.gridy = 0;
        g.weightx = 1.0;                     // <-- this allows stretching
        g.fill = GridBagConstraints.HORIZONTAL;
        JTextField user = new JTextField(15);
        add(user, g);

        // --- Password label ---
        g.gridx = 0; g.gridy = 1;
        g.weightx = 0;
        g.fill = GridBagConstraints.NONE;
        g.anchor = GridBagConstraints.WEST;
        add(new JLabel("Password:"), g);

        // --- Password field ---
        g.gridx = 1; g.gridy = 1;
        g.weightx = 1.0;
        g.fill = GridBagConstraints.HORIZONTAL;
        JPasswordField pwd = new JPasswordField(15);
        add(pwd, g);

        // --- Login button (spans both columns, centered) ---
        g.gridx = 0; g.gridy = 2;
        g.gridwidth = 2;                     // span 2 columns
        g.weightx = 0;
        g.fill = GridBagConstraints.NONE;
        g.anchor = GridBagConstraints.CENTER;
        add(new JButton("Login"), g);

        pack();
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);          // center on screen
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(LoginForm::new);
    }
}
```

**Mark Distribution (b):**
- Correct `GridBagLayout` instantiation & `GridBagConstraints` object: **1 mark**
- Labels added correctly with `gridx`/`gridy`: **1 mark**
- `weightx = 1.0` AND `fill = HORIZONTAL` for both fields: **2 marks** (this is the critical line for stretching)
- `gridwidth = 2` and `anchor = CENTER` for button: **1 mark**
- `pack()`, `setVisible(true)`, and correct main method: **1 mark**
- Code compiles & runs correctly: **1 mark**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, End Semester, Alternative]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the **Strategy Design Pattern** as used by Swing Layout Managers. Why does the API expose `setLayout(LayoutManager)` rather than fixing the layout inside the `Container` class? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program that uses a **nested layout** to construct a window like the one below (a top toolbar, a center text area, and a bottom status panel). Use `BorderLayout` for the outer container and `FlowLayout` for the toolbar. The window must remain usable after resizing. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

The **Strategy Design Pattern** defines a family of algorithms (the layout algorithms), encapsulates each one (in a `LayoutManager` implementation), and makes them **interchangeable** at runtime. The `Container` (context) holds a reference to a `LayoutManager` (strategy) and delegates the layout decision to it. (2 marks)

**Why expose `setLayout(LayoutManager)` rather than fixing the layout?**

1. **Open/Closed Principle** — The `Container` class is *closed* for modification but *open* for extension: new layout managers can be added without touching `Container.java`. (1 mark)
2. **Runtime flexibility** — A developer can swap `BorderLayout` for `GridLayout` with a single line (`frame.setLayout(new GridLayout(2, 2))`) without recompiling the container. (1 mark)
3. **Testability** — Mock layout managers can be injected for unit testing. (1 mark)
4. **Composability** — A container can itself *be* a component inside an outer container, with a completely different layout. This is how nested layouts work. (1 mark)
5. **Single Responsibility Principle** — `Container` worries about component lifecycle (add/remove), while `LayoutManager` worries about geometry. (1 mark)

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;

public class NestedLayoutDemo extends JFrame {

    public NestedLayoutDemo() {
        setTitle("Nested Layout Demo");

        // ---- Outer layout: BorderLayout ----
        setLayout(new BorderLayout());

        // ---- NORTH: toolbar with FlowLayout ----
        JPanel toolbar = new JPanel(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.LEFT));
        toolbar.add(new JButton("File"));
        toolbar.add(new JButton("Edit"));
        toolbar.add(new JButton("View"));
        toolbar.add(new JButton("Help"));
        add(toolbar, BorderLayout.NORTH);

        // ---- CENTER: scrollable text area ----
        JTextArea text = new JTextArea("Start typing here...");
        add(new JScrollPane(text), BorderLayout.CENTER);

        // ---- SOUTH: status panel with FlowLayout.RIGHT ----
        JPanel status = new JPanel(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.RIGHT));
        status.add(new JLabel("Status: Ready"));
        status.add(new JLabel("Line: 1"));
        add(status, BorderLayout.SOUTH);

        setSize(500, 350);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLocationRelativeTo(null);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SwingUtilities.invokeLater(NestedLayoutDemo::new);
    }
}
```

**Mark Distribution (b):**
- `BorderLayout` for outer frame, three regions correctly used: **2 marks**
- Inner `JPanel` with `FlowLayout` for the toolbar with at least 3 buttons: **1 mark**
- `JTextArea` wrapped in `JScrollPane` and placed in CENTER: **1 mark**
- Status panel with `FlowLayout.RIGHT` and two labels in SOUTH: **1 mark**
- `setSize` / `pack()` and `setVisible(true)` present, `setDefaultCloseOperation`: **1 mark**
- Output is correct and resizing works: **1 mark**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Layout Manager Questions**
>
> 1. **Forgetting `weightx`/`weighty`** — The form renders with empty bands. Valuer deducts **2 marks** typically.
> 2. **Setting `setLayout(null)`** — Considered poor practice. If the question does not explicitly demand absolute positioning, the valuer will deduct at least **1 mark** and may comment: "Anti-pattern; not platform-independent."
> 3. **Calling `add(comp)` instead of `add(comp, BorderLayout.NORTH)`** — Components go to CENTER by default in `BorderLayout`, and the visual result looks wrong. **1 mark** deduction.
> 4. **Forgetting to call `pack()` before `setVisible(true)`** — Window appears too small. **0.5 mark** deduction.
> 5. **Confusing `GridLayout(r, c)` argument order** — It is `(rows, columns)`, not `(x, y)`. **0.5 mark** deduction.
> 6. **Not reusing the same `GridBagConstraints` object** — Each call must mutate the right fields, or you must `new GridBagConstraints()` per component. Valuer checks for this in 14-mark questions.
> 7. **Adding more than 5 components to a single `BorderLayout` region without nesting a `JPanel`** — The fifth+ component replaces earlier ones. **1 mark** deduction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the ESE Hall**

- [x] `LayoutManager` is an *interface* in `java.awt`; concrete implementations include `BorderLayout`, `FlowLayout`, `GridLayout`, `CardLayout`, `GridBagLayout`, and (in Swing) `BoxLayout`, `GroupLayout`, `SpringLayout`.
- [x] `JFrame`, `JDialog`, `JWindow` content panes default to **`BorderLayout`**.
- [x] `JPanel` defaults to **`FlowLayout(CENTER, 5, 5)`**.
- [x] `BorderLayout` has exactly 5 regions: `NORTH`, `SOUTH`, `EAST`, `WEST`, `CENTER`.
- [x] `GridLayout(r, c, hgap, vgap)` divides the container into $r \times c$ **equal-sized cells**.
- [x] `GridBagLayout` uses a **`GridBagConstraints`** object per component; the 12 fields include `gridx`, `gridy`, `gridwidth`, `gridheight`, `fill`, `anchor`, `weightx`, `weighty`, `insets`, `ipadx`, `ipady`, and an internal `GridBagLayoutInfo` reference.
- [x] **`weightx = 0.0` is the most common reason** for non-stretching forms in `GridBagLayout`.
- [x] `BoxLayout` accepts a single line `LINE_AXIS`, `PAGE_AXIS`, `X_AXIS`, `Y_AXIS`. Use `Box.createVerticalGlue()` and `Box.createVerticalStrut(n)` for spacers.
- [x] `CardLayout.show(container, name)` switches the visible card.
- [x] Always call `pack()` **before** `setVisible(true)` for nested layouts.
- [x] The Strategy pattern lets you swap `frame.setLayout(new X())` at runtime.
- [x] For KTU lab records, the **Calculator** is best built with `GridLayout(4, 4)` and a `JTextField` in `BorderLayout.NORTH`.
- [x] For the **Login form**, use `GridBagLayout` with `weightx = 1.0` and `fill = HORIZONTAL`.
- [x] Nested layouts = one layout per container; a `JPanel` inside `BorderLayout.CENTER` can have its own `GridLayout` for the buttons.
- [x] Always invoke Swing code on the EDT via `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(Runnable)` in `main()`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Event Handling in Swings: Delegation Event Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Event Handling in Swings: Delegation Event Model # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Delegation Event Model — Formal KTU 2024 Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Delegation Event Model (DEM):** A Java event-handling architecture defined in the `java.awt.event` and `javax.swing.event` packages, in which an **Event Source** does not process an event itself but *delegates* the task to a separate object known as an **Event Listener**, which implements a specific **Listener Interface** corresponding to the event type. The model is the **cornerstone of Swing GUI programming** and is built on three principal participants: | Participant | Type | Responsibility | |--------------|------|---------------| | **Event Source** | Concrete Swing/AWT object (`JButton`, `JTextField`, `JMenuItem`, `JList`, `JFrame`) | Generates an event when the user interacts with it. | | **Event Object** | Subclass of `java.util.EventObject` (e.g., `ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`) | Encapsulates the state change and a reference to the source. | | **Event Listener** | Object implementing a listener interface (`ActionListener`, `MouseListener`, etc.) | Receives the event and contains the event-handling logic. | ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Newspaper Subscription" > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — Think of a Newspaper Delivery System:** Imagine a *newspaper office* (the **Event Source**). Every morning, an *event* occurs (the newspaper is printed). However, the office does **not** deliver the paper itself. Instead, a **delivery boy** (the **Event Listener**) registered with the office picks up the paper and delivers it to the customer. - The customer must first **register** (call `addXxxListener()`). - The delivery boy follows a **standard protocol** (implements a `Listener Interface` like `ActionListener`). - The newspaper carries a **tag** indicating which office it came from (the **Event Object** carrying a `getSource()` reference). This is exactly how the Delegation Event Model works: **the source delegates, the listener handles, and registration is the binding glue**. ## 1.3 Why Delegation? — Advantages > [!TIP] > **Key Advantages of DEM over the legacy Java 1.0 inheritance-based model:** > 1. **Separation of Concerns** — GUI code is decoupled from business logic. > 2. **Reusability** — One listener can be attached to multiple sources. > 3. **Flexibility** — Listeners can be swapped at runtime. > 4. **Scalability** — Supports event multicasting (one source → many listeners). > 5. **Cleaner Hierarchy** — Avoids deep, fragile inheritance chains. ## 1.4 The Event Class Hierarchy (Java AWT Event Classes) The root class is `java.util.EventObject` (from JDK 1.1 onwards). All AWT/Swing events extend `java.awt.AWTEvent`, which in turn extends `EventObject`. | Event Class (package `java.awt.event`) | Triggered When | Listener Interface | |----------------------------------------|----------------|---------------------| | `ActionEvent` | Button click, menu selection, Enter in text field | `ActionListener` | | `MouseEvent` | Mouse press, release, click, enter, exit | `MouseListener`, `MouseMotionListener` | | `KeyEvent` | Key press, release, type | `KeyListener` | | `WindowEvent` | Window open, close, iconify, activate | `WindowListener` | | `ItemEvent` | Checkbox/radio/list item state change | `ItemListener` | | `TextEvent` | Text field value changes | `TextListener` | | `AdjustmentEvent` | Scrollbar adjustment | `AdjustmentListener` | | `ComponentEvent` | Component shown, hidden, moved, resized | `ComponentListener` | | `FocusEvent` | Component gains or loses focus | `FocusListener` | | `ContainerEvent` | Component added/removed from container | `ContainerListener` | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Operational Flow of the Delegation Event Model The event-handling cycle proceeds in a strict sequence. Memorize this for KTU short-answer questions. > [!NOTE] > **The 5-Step Event Handling Lifecycle:** > 1. **User Action:** The user performs a GUI action (e.g., clicks `JButton`). > 2. **Event Generation:** The source component registers the action and creates an `EventObject` subclass instance (e.g., `ActionEvent`). > 3. **Listener Notification:** The source iterates through its internally maintained list of registered listeners and invokes the appropriate callback method. > 4. **Event Delivery:** The event object is passed to the listener method as an argument. > 5. **Event Handling:** The listener's method executes, performing the desired business logic. ## 2.2 Critical Methods on the `EventObject` Class The base class `java.util.EventObject` provides two essential methods used in nearly every event handler: | Method Signature | Return Type | Purpose | |------------------|-------------|---------| | `getSource()` | `Object` | Returns the object on which the event initially occurred. | | `toString()` | `String` | Returns a string representation of the event. | The `AWTEvent` subclass adds: | Method | Purpose | |--------|---------| | `getID()` | Returns an `int` event-type identifier (e.g., `MouseEvent.MOUSE_PRESSED`). | | `consume()` | Consumes the event so it is not processed by the parent container. | | `isConsumed()` | Returns `true` if the event has been consumed. | ## 2.3 The Registration Mechanism — `addXxxListener()` and `removeXxxListener()` Every AWT/Swing component that can generate events provides a pair of methods for binding/unbinding listeners. The naming convention is strictly: $$\text{add}\langle\text{ListenerName}\rangle\text{Listener(EventListenerType l)}$$ $$\text{remove}\langle\text{ListenerName}\rangle\text{Listener(EventListenerType l)}$$ **Examples:** - `JButton` → `addActionListener(ActionListener l)`, `removeActionListener(ActionListener l)` - `JTextField` → `addActionListener(ActionListener l)` - `JFrame` → `addWindowListener(WindowListener l)` - `Component` → `addMouseListener(MouseListener l)`, `addMouseMotionListener(MouseMotionListener l)` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** Forgetting to **register** the listener using `addXxxListener()` is the single most common reason a Swing application "does not respond" to user input. A listener object created but never registered is *invisible* to the source. ## 2.4 Listener Interfaces vs. Adapter Classes — High-Yield Cheat Sheet Many listener interfaces contain **multiple methods** (e.g., `MouseListener` has 5: `mouseClicked`, `mousePressed`, `mouseReleased`, `mouseEntered`, `mouseExited`). If you only need one method, you must still implement *all* of them with empty bodies — this is verbose and error-prone. **Adapter classes** solve this by providing empty default implementations: | Listener Interface | Methods | Corresponding Adapter Class | |--------------------|---------|------------------------------| | `MouseListener` | 5 | `MouseAdapter` | | `MouseMotionListener` | 2 | `MouseMotionAdapter` | | `KeyListener` | 3 | `KeyAdapter` | | `WindowListener` | 7 | `WindowAdapter` | | `ComponentListener` | 4 | `ComponentAdapter` | | `ContainerListener` | 2 | `ContainerAdapter` | | `FocusListener` | 2 | `FocusAdapter` | > [!TIP] > **Two listener interfaces have NO adapter classes** because each contains only a single abstract method: `ActionListener` and `ItemListener`. There is nothing to "fill in" — the compiler does not force empty methods. ## 2.5 The KTU Formula Sheet / Method-Signature Cheat Sheet | Component / API | Formula / Method Signature | Notes | |-----------------|----------------------------|-------| | Source registration | `sourceObj.addXxxListener(listenerObj);` | Mandatory for delegation | | Source deregistration | `sourceObj.removeXxxListener(listenerObj);` | Optional cleanup | | Event source retrieval | `EventObject e; Object src = e.getSource();` | Inside any listener method | | Event ID retrieval | `int id = e.getID();` | Used for switch on event subtypes | | Event consumption | `e.consume();` | Prevents parent propagation | | Adapter override | `class MyAdapter extends MouseAdapter { public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) { ... } }` | Override only what you need | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Application Context:** The Delegation Event Model is not just a Swing-specific curiosity. The same **observer/listener pattern** is used in: > - **IoT sensor networks** (publish/subscribe to sensor events). > - **Distributed systems** (message brokers like Kafka, RabbitMQ). > - **GUI frameworks across languages** (JavaScript `addEventListener`, C# `event += handler`, Qt signals/slots). > > Understanding Java's DEM therefore gives a B.Tech student a **transferable design pattern** applicable to all event-driven systems. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Programmatic Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 The Standard Recipe — Implementing a Delegation Event Handler (Derived Algorithmically) The complete procedure for adding event handling to any Swing component is derived below as a **5-step algorithmic process**. This is the answer that KTU examiners expect when they ask "Explain the steps in event handling." > [!IMPORTANT] > **Step-by-Step Derivation of Event Handling using the Delegation Event Model:** **Step 1 — Identify the event source and the event type.** - *Derivation logic:* The user interaction (e.g., clicking a button) determines both the **source** (the `JButton` instance) and the **event type** (`ActionEvent`). - *Result:* We now know the listener interface to use (`ActionListener`). **Step 2 — Implement the corresponding listener interface (or extend its adapter).** - *Derivation logic:* The listener must conform to the contract. For `ActionListener`, this is a single method: $$\text{public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e)}$$ - *Result:* The class containing the handler either `implements ActionListener` or uses an anonymous inner class. **Step 3 — Create the source component and instantiate the listener.** ``` JButton b = new JButton("Click Me"); MyHandler h = new MyHandler(); ``` **Step 4 — Register the listener with the source.** ``` b.addActionListener(h); ``` - *Derivation logic:* Without this call, the source has no internal reference to `h`, and the delegation cannot occur. **Step 5 — Implement the event-handling method (the callback).** - *Derivation logic:* When the button is clicked, the AWT event-dispatching thread invokes `h.actionPerformed(e)`. The method body contains the desired business logic. ## 3.2 The Three Implementation Strategies (Analytical Matrix) A KTU 14-mark question often asks: *"Explain the different ways to handle events in Java."* The complete analytical matrix is: | # | Strategy | Code Skeleton | When to Use | KTU Frequency | |---|----------|---------------|-------------|----------------| | **A** | **Implementing a listener interface in the same class** | `public class MyFrame extends JFrame implements ActionListener` | Small demos, single-purpose windows | Very High (always asked) | | **B** | **Using a separate class implementing the listener interface** | `class MyHandler implements ActionListener` | Reusable handlers, separation of concerns | High | | **C** | **Using an inner class (named or anonymous)** | `b.addActionListener(new ActionListener() { public void actionPerformed(...) { ... } });` | GUI builders, one-off handlers | Very High | | **D** | **Using an adapter class** | `class MyAdapter extends MouseAdapter { ... }` | Multi-method listeners (Mouse, Key, Window) | High | | **E** | **Using lambda expressions (Java 8+)** | `b.addActionListener(e -> { ... });` | Single-method listeners, modern style | Moderate (introduced) | > [!NOTE] > **Lambda applicability rule:** Lambdas can replace **only** the listener interfaces that contain a **single abstract method** (functional interfaces). The most commonly used is `ActionListener`: > > `b.addActionListener(e -> label.setText("Clicked"));` ## 3.3 Exhaustive Walkthrough — A Complete Working Example The following walkthrough derives a working Swing event-handling program, line by line. This is the model answer pattern for KTU Part B (14-mark) questions. > [!TIP] > **Programmatic Derivation — "Click Counter" Application:** **Derivation Step 1 — Define the class and inherit from `JFrame`:** - A Swing window is needed, so we extend `JFrame` and implement `ActionListener` in the same class. - Result: `public class ClickCounter extends JFrame implements ActionListener` **Derivation Step 2 — Declare the components:** - A `JButton` to be clicked, and a `JLabel` to display the count. - Result: ``` JButton b; JLabel lbl; int count = 0; ``` **Derivation Step 3 — Initialize components in the constructor:** - The frame is sized, the layout is set (here `FlowLayout` for simplicity), the components are added, the button is registered with `this` as the listener, and the frame is made visible. - Result: ``` b = new JButton("Click"); lbl = new JLabel("Count: 0"); b.addActionListener(this); add(lbl); add(b); setSize(300, 150); setLayout(new FlowLayout()); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); setVisible(true); ``` **Derivation Step 4 — Implement the `actionPerformed` callback:** - Each click increments `count` and updates the label's text. The event's `getSource()` is used to verify which button was clicked (useful if multiple buttons share one handler). - Result: ``` public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { count++; lbl.setText("Count: " + count); } ``` **Derivation Step 5 — Add the `main` method:** - Swing components must be created on the Event Dispatch Thread. The safe pattern is: ``` public static void main(String[] args) { SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new ClickCounter()); } ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mathematical abstraction of the event flow:** Let $S$ be the set of sources, $L$ the set of listeners, and $E$ the set of event types. The registration relation is a function $R: S \times E \to 2^L$ (a source-event pair maps to a set of listeners). At runtime, the JVM evaluates $R(s, e)$ and invokes $l.\text{handle}(e)$ for every $l \in R(s, e)$. This is the **fan-out delivery** property of the model. ## 3.4 The Event Source Object — `getSource()` Cast Pattern A common KTU sub-question: *"How do you determine which component triggered an event when multiple components share one handler?"* > [!NOTE] > **Pattern — Source Identification via `getSource()` and `instanceof`:** > > $$\text{Object src = e.getSource();}$$ > $$\text{if (src instanceof JButton) \{ ... \} else if (src instanceof JTextField) \{ ... \}}$$ For `ActionEvent` specifically, two convenience methods are available: | Method | Returns | |--------|---------| | `e.getActionCommand()` | `String` — the button label or command string | | `e.getModifiers()` | `int` — modifier keys held during the action (Shift, Ctrl, etc.) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Delegation Event Model The following Mermaid block diagram maps the runtime interactions between the three principal participants of the Delegation Event Model. This is a KTU-favorite diagram (asked almost every year as a 7-mark illustration). ```mermaid flowchart LR user1[User] -->|clicks| source1[JButton Source] source1 -->|1. creates| event1[ActionEvent Object] source1 -->|2. invokes| listener1[ActionListener] event1 -->|passed as arg| listener1 listener1 -->|3. updates| gui1[GUI Component e.g. JLabel] source1 -.->|0. registered via| listener1 user1[User] -->|types| source2[JTextField Source] source2 -->|1. creates| event2[ActionEvent Object] source2 -->|2. invokes| listener1 event2 -->|passed as arg| listener1 classDef source fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#004085,color:#000 classDef event fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000 classDef listener fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000 classDef action fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000 classDef gui fill:#e2d4f0,stroke:#5a3d7a,color:#000 class source1,source2 source class event1,event2 event class listener1 listener class user1 action class gui1 gui ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The **dotted arrow** (`source1 -.-> listener1`) represents the **registration** step (the `addActionListener` call that occurs *before* any user interaction). - The **solid arrows** represent the runtime event flow when a user action occurs. - A single listener can be **shared across multiple sources** (note the two sources both point to `listener1`). ## 4.2 Class Hierarchy — Event Classes and Listener Interfaces ```mermaid flowchart TD objRoot[java.util.EventObject] awtRoot[java.awt.AWTEvent] objRoot --> awtRoot awtRoot --> actionEv[ActionEvent] awtRoot --> mouseEv[MouseEvent] awtRoot --> keyEv[KeyEvent] awtRoot --> windowEv[WindowEvent] awtRoot --> itemEv[ItemEvent] awtRoot --> focusEv[FocusEvent] awtRoot --> compEv[ComponentEvent] awtRoot --> textEv[TextEvent] awtRoot --> adjEv[AdjustmentEvent] actionL[ActionListener] -->|implements| actionEv mouseL[MouseListener] -->|listens| mouseEv keyL[KeyListener] -->|listens| keyEv windowL[WindowListener] -->|listens| windowEv itemL[ItemListener] -->|listens| itemEv mouseA[MouseAdapter] -->|empty impl| mouseL keyA[KeyAdapter] -->|empty impl| keyL windowA[WindowAdapter] -->|empty impl| windowL classDef root fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#d35400,color:#000 classDef ev fill:#a8e6cf,stroke:#1e8449,color:#000 classDef li fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000 classDef ad fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21,color:#000 class objRoot,awtRoot root class actionEv,mouseEv,keyEv,windowEv,itemEv,focusEv,compEv,textEv,adjEv ev class actionL,mouseL,keyL,windowL,itemL li class mouseA,keyA,windowA ad ``` ## 4.3 Sequence Flow — Event Dispatch Over Time ```mermaid sequenceDiagram participant U as User participant S as Source JButton participant E as EventObject ActionEvent participant L as Listener Handler participant G as GUI e.g. JLabel Note over S,L: Phase 0: Registration (program start) S->>L: addActionListener(this) Note over U,G: Phase 1: Runtime event flow U->>S: click JButton S->>E: new ActionEvent(this, command) S->>L: actionPerformed(e) L->>E: e.getSource() L->>G: setText("Updated") L-->>S: method returns ``` ## 4.4 Decision Flow — Choosing the Right Implementation Strategy ```mermaid flowchart TD start([Need event handling?]) --> q1{Listener has only
1 abstract method?} q1 -- Yes --> q2{Using Java 8+?} q2 -- Yes --> stratLambda[Use Lambda Expression] q2 -- No --> stratAnon[Use Anonymous Inner Class] q1 -- No --> q3{Need most methods?} q3 -- Yes, override all --> stratInterface[Implement the Interface directly] q3 -- No, override few --> stratAdapter[Extend the Adapter Class] stratLambda --> done([Done]) stratAnon --> done stratInterface --> done stratAdapter --> done classDef startNode fill:#74b9ff,stroke:#0984e3,color:#000 classDef decision fill:#ffeaa7,stroke:#fdcb6e,color:#000 classDef action fill:#55efc4,stroke:#00b894,color:#000 class classDef1 fill:#dfe6e9,stroke:#2d3436,color:#000 class start startNode class q1,q2,q3 decision class stratLambda,stratAnon,stratInterface,stratAdapter action class done startNode ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions > **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** > **Define the Delegation Event Model. List its three main components.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The **Delegation Event Model** is a Java event-handling mechanism in which the responsibility of processing an event generated by a GUI component is **delegated** to a separate object called a listener. It decouples event generation from event handling. > > The three main components are: > 1. **Event Source** — The GUI component (e.g., `JButton`) that generates the event. > 2. **Event Object** — An object (subclass of `java.util.EventObject`) that encapsulates the event details. > 3. **Event Listener** — An object implementing a listener interface (e.g., `ActionListener`) that receives and handles the event. > **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** > **What is an adapter class? Give two examples.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > An **adapter class** is a class that provides **default empty implementations** of all the methods declared in a multi-method listener interface. By extending an adapter, a programmer can override only the methods of interest instead of implementing every method in the interface. > > **Examples:** `MouseAdapter` (implements all 5 methods of `MouseListener` as empty), `KeyAdapter` (implements all 3 methods of `KeyListener` as empty), `WindowAdapter` (implements all 7 methods of `WindowListener` as empty). --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) > **Internal Choice Rule:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. --- ### 🔹 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **(a)** Explain the Delegation Event Model in Java with a neat diagram. List the various event classes and listener interfaces available in `java.awt.event` package. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a Java Swing program to create a simple calculator that performs addition and subtraction of two numbers using `JButton`, `JTextField`, and the Delegation Event Model. Use the same class as both the `JFrame` and the `ActionListener`. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — (a) 7 Marks **Definition (2 marks):** The Delegation Event Model is a Java architecture in which an *event source* (a GUI component) does not process events itself but *delegates* the task to one or more *event listeners* that have been registered to it. The listener must implement a *listener interface* matching the event type. **Three components (2 marks):** 1. **Event Source** — the component that generates events (e.g., `JButton`, `JTextField`). 2. **Event Object** — encapsulates the event (`ActionEvent`, `MouseEvent`, etc.). 3. **Event Listener** — object implementing `ActionListener`, `MouseListener`, etc. **Steps in event handling (2 marks):** 1. Implement the appropriate listener interface. 2. Register the listener with the source using `addXxxListener()`. 3. Override the event-handling method (e.g., `actionPerformed`). **Event classes and listener interfaces (1 mark):** | Event Class | Listener Interface | |-------------|---------------------| | `ActionEvent` | `ActionListener` | | `MouseEvent` | `MouseListener`, `MouseMotionListener` | | `KeyEvent` | `KeyListener` | | `WindowEvent` | `WindowListener` | | `ItemEvent` | `ItemListener` | | `TextEvent` | `TextListener` | | `AdjustmentEvent` | `AdjustmentListener` | | `ComponentEvent` | `ComponentListener` | | `FocusEvent` | `FocusListener` | | `ContainerEvent` | `ContainerListener` | #### Model Solution — (b) 7 Marks ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class MiniCalculator extends JFrame implements ActionListener { JTextField t1, t2, result; JButton addBtn, subBtn; public MiniCalculator() { setTitle("Mini Calculator"); setLayout(new FlowLayout()); setSize(350, 200); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); t1 = new JTextField(8); t2 = new JTextField(8); result = new JTextField(10); result.setEditable(false); addBtn = new JButton("Add"); subBtn = new JButton("Subtract"); addBtn.addActionListener(this); subBtn.addActionListener(this); add(new JLabel("A:")); add(t1); add(new JLabel("B:")); add(t2); add(addBtn); add(subBtn); add(new JLabel("Result:")); add(result); setVisible(true); } public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) { double a = Double.parseDouble(t1.getText()); double b = Double.parseDouble(t2.getText()); if (e.getSource() == addBtn) { result.setText(String.valueOf(a + b)); } else if (e.getSource() == subBtn) { result.setText(String.valueOf(a - b)); } } public static void main(String[] args) { SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new MiniCalculator()); } } ``` **Explanation of key lines (for marks):** - `implements ActionListener` — same class acts as the listener. - `addBtn.addActionListener(this)` — registration step (the "delegation" call). - `e.getSource() == addBtn` — identifies which button fired the event. --- ### 🔹 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **(a)** Differentiate between event classes and listener interfaces in Java. Explain the role of adapter classes with suitable examples. Why is `ActionListener` not provided with an adapter class? **(7 marks)** **(b)** Write a Java program using Swing that handles mouse events on a `JPanel`. The program should display the coordinates of the mouse pointer whenever the mouse is clicked or moved, using a `MouseAdapter`. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution — (a) 7 Marks **Event Classes vs Listener Interfaces (3 marks):** | Aspect | Event Class | Listener Interface | |--------|-------------|---------------------| | **Type** | Concrete class (e.g., `ActionEvent`) | Interface (e.g., `ActionListener`) | | **Purpose** | Encapsulates event state (source, ID, etc.) | Defines the contract for handling the event | | **Methods** | Getters like `getSource()`, `getID()` | Callback methods like `actionPerformed(ActionEvent)` | | **Package** | `java.awt.event` | `java.awt.event` | | **Instantiation** | Created by the source | Implemented by the listener | **Adapter classes (3 marks):** Adapter classes provide **default empty implementations** of all methods in a multi-method listener interface, so subclasses can override only those they need. - **Example:** `MouseAdapter` implements all 5 methods of `MouseListener` with empty bodies. A subclass can override only `mouseClicked`: ```java class MyAdapter extends MouseAdapter { public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) { System.out.println("Clicked at " + e.getX() + "," + e.getY()); } } ``` **Why no adapter for `ActionListener` (1 mark):** `ActionListener` contains only **one** abstract method (`actionPerformed`). An adapter would be identical in length to implementing the interface, so providing one would add no value. Similarly, `ItemListener` has no adapter. #### Model Solution — (b) 7 Marks ```java import javax.swing.*; import java.awt.*; import java.awt.event.*; public class MouseTracker extends JFrame { JLabel status; public MouseTracker() { setTitle("Mouse Event Tracker"); setSize(400, 300); setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE); setLayout(new BorderLayout()); JPanel panel = new JPanel(); panel.setBackground(Color.LIGHT_GRAY); status = new JLabel("Move or click the mouse inside the window."); add(panel, BorderLayout.CENTER); add(status, BorderLayout.SOUTH); // Use a MouseAdapter — only override what we need MouseAdapter ma = new MouseAdapter() { public void mouseClicked(MouseEvent e) { status.setText("Clicked at (" + e.getX() + ", " + e.getY() + ")"); } public void mouseMoved(MouseEvent e) { status.setText("Moved to (" + e.getX() + ", " + e.getY() + ")"); } }; panel.addMouseListener(ma); panel.addMouseMotionListener(ma); setVisible(true); } public static void main(String[] args) { SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> new MouseTracker()); } } ``` **Key points (for marks):** - `MouseAdapter` is extended anonymously to override only `mouseClicked` and `mouseMoved`. - The same adapter is registered as **both** a `MouseListener` and a `MouseMotionListener` because `MouseAdapter` implements both interfaces. - `e.getX()` and `e.getY()` retrieve the mouse coordinates from the `MouseEvent`. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Event Handling Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting to register the listener.** Writing `implements ActionListener` but never calling `addActionListener(this)` costs **2–3 marks**. The program will compile but the button will do nothing. > 2. **Mixing up `getSource()` types.** Using `==` to compare `e.getSource()` with a `JButton` works, but using `==` with a `String` returned by `getActionCommand()` is a logical error — use `.equals()`. > 3. **Confusing `MouseListener` with `MouseMotionListener`.** `mouseClicked` is in `MouseListener`; `mouseMoved` and `mouseDragged` are in `MouseMotionListener`. Forgetting the second registration causes movement events to silently fail. > 4. **Calling Swing methods from the `main` thread directly.** Although the program will run, it violates Swing's single-thread rule. Always wrap GUI creation in `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(...)`. > 5. **Spelling pitfalls:** `ActionListner`, `MouseListner`, `addActionListner` — each typo costs marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📌 **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist:** > > - ✅ The **Delegation Event Model** has **three participants**: Source, Event, Listener. > - ✅ The base class of all events is `java.util.EventObject`; AWT events extend `java.awt.AWTEvent`. > - ✅ Every listener interface is in the `java.awt.event` package (or `javax.swing.event` for Swing-specific events like `MenuEvent`). > - ✅ Registration is done via `source.addXxxListener(listener)` — **mandatory** for delegation to work. > - ✅ Two single-method interfaces (`ActionListener`, `ItemListener`) have **no adapter class**. > - ✅ Adapter classes (`MouseAdapter`, `KeyAdapter`, `WindowAdapter`, etc.) provide empty default method bodies to reduce boilerplate. > - ✅ `e.getSource()` returns the firing component as an `Object`; cast to the specific type if needed. > - ✅ For `ActionEvent`, use `e.getActionCommand()` to get the command string and `e.getModifiers()` to get modifier keys. > - ✅ A **single listener can serve multiple sources**; a **single source can have multiple listeners** (multicasting). > - ✅ Always use `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(...)` to launch a Swing GUI from `main`. > - ✅ Since **Java 8**, single-method listeners can be replaced by **lambda expressions** (e.g., `b.addActionListener(e -> ...)`). > - ✅ The Delegation Event Model is a concrete realization of the **Observer design pattern** from the Gang of Four.

> - **Topic:** JTextField

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBEOT404 – Object Oriented Programming using Java, Module 4: Swing & Event Handling)**, the **`JTextField`** is a lightweight Swing component (declared in `javax.swing.JTextField`) that allows the user to enter and edit a **single line of unformatted text**. It is a direct subclass of `javax.swing.JTextComponent` (which itself extends `javax.swing.JComponent`) and serves as the AWT-replacement for the older `java.awt.TextField`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** JTextField is a core, examinable Swing component grouped under *Basic Swing Components (JLabel, JButton, JTextField, JTextArea, JCheckBox, JRadioButton, JComboBox)*. Expect 3-mark definition questions and 7–14 mark program-based questions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a **`JTextField`** as a **single blank line on a printed paper form** — for example, the line where you are asked to write your *Name* or *Roll Number*. The form (the form frame) contains a small label next to the line ("Name:"), the line itself only fits a **single row of handwriting**, and once you write something in it, the form can capture and process that information (validation, submission, etc.).

In Java terms:
- The **paper** = `JFrame`
- The **label "Name:"** = `JLabel`
- The **blank single line** = `JTextField`
- The **act of writing** = keyboard input
- The **form validation** = `KeyListener`, `ActionListener`, or `DocumentListener`

### 1.3 Key Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **Class Name:** `javax.swing.JTextField`
- **Default Empty Width (Columns):** **0 columns** (no preferred size unless set)
- **Standard Initial Column Hint:** **10 columns** is the conventional default used in tutorials
- **Inheritance Depth from `JComponent`:** **2 levels** (`JTextField` → `JTextComponent` → `JComponent`)
- **Editable State Default:** **`true`** (user input is allowed by default)
- **Single-line Constraint:** **1 line of text** (line breaks are ignored on input)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A `JTextField` does **NOT** support multi-line text. For multi-line input, use `JTextArea` (or `JEditorPane` / `JTextPane` for styled text). This is one of the most frequently asked distinctions in KTU exams.

### 1.4 The `JTextField` Class Signature

```java
public class JTextField extends JTextComponent implements SwingConstants
```

It implements **`SwingConstants`**, which is why it inherits orientation constants such as `LEFT`, `CENTER`, `RIGHT`, `HORIZONTAL`, `VERTICAL`, `TOP`, `BOTTOM` used for aligning the text within the field.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the JTextField Lifecycle

The operational flow of a `JTextField` inside a Swing GUI can be decomposed into **six (6) structured logical steps**:

1. **Declaration** — Declare a `JTextField` reference variable inside your class (e.g., as a private field in the top-level `JFrame` subclass or panel).
2. **Instantiation** — Create the `JTextField` object using one of the constructors (empty, with initial text, with columns, etc.).
3. **Configuration** — Customize the field using setter methods: `setText()`, `setEditable()`, `setHorizontalAlignment()`, `setFont()`, `setToolTipText()`, `setColumns()`.
4. **Placement** — Add the `JTextField` to a container (`JFrame`, `JPanel`, etc.) using a layout manager (`BorderLayout`, `FlowLayout`, `GridLayout`, `GridBagLayout`).
5. **Event Registration** — Register one or more listeners (`ActionListener`, `KeyListener`, `CaretListener`, `DocumentListener`, `FocusListener`) to react to user interactions.
6. **Disposal** — When the parent window is closed, the field is destroyed by the JVM garbage collector (or via `dispose()` on the `JFrame`).

### 2.2 Why and How Behind Each Step

- **Why Declaration as a Field?** Local variables cannot be accessed from inner event-handler classes. To make a `JTextField` reachable from `actionPerformed()` or `keyTyped()`, it must be a class-level (instance) field.
- **Why Configuration before Placement?** Many Swing property changes trigger `revalidate()` and `repaint()`. Configuring first ensures the layout manager receives the final component size hints (e.g., `setColumns(20)` informs `FlowLayout` of preferred width).
- **Why Event Registration after Placement?** Although technically order does not matter for registration, registering last ensures the component is fully constructed and visually present before listening begins.
- **Why a Single Line Only?** The `JTextField`'s internal `Document` (a `PlainDocument`) is restricted to one logical line. Pressing **Enter** triggers an `ActionEvent` rather than inserting a newline.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates the **must-know constructors and methods** of `JTextField` for the KTU 2024 Scheme ESE (End Semester Examination). All API signatures are from the standard `javax.swing` package.

| # | Constructor / Method | Signature | Purpose / Exam Quick-Recall |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Default Constructor | `JTextField()` | Creates an empty field with **0 columns**. |
| 2 | Column Constructor | `JTextField(int columns)` | Creates an empty field with preferred width = **N character columns**. |
| 3 | Text Constructor | `JTextField(String text)` | Creates a field pre-filled with `text`. |
| 4 | Text + Columns | `JTextField(String text, int columns)` | Pre-filled field with width hint. |
| 5 | Get Text | `String getText()` | Returns the current contents of the field. |
| 6 | Set Text | `void setText(String t)` | Replaces current text with `t`. |
| 7 | Set Editable | `void setEditable(boolean b)` | If `false`, user input is blocked (acts like a label). |
| 8 | Is Editable | `boolean isEditable()` | Returns current editability. |
| 9 | Set Columns | `void setColumns(int c)` | Resets the preferred width. |
| 10 | Get Columns | `int getColumns()` | Returns current column count. |
| 11 | Set Alignment | `void setHorizontalAlignment(int a)` | Aligns text: `LEFT`, `CENTER`, `RIGHT`. |
| 12 | Add Listener | `void addActionListener(ActionListener l)` | Triggered on **Enter** key or focus-loss-after-edit. |
| 13 | Get Action | `Action getAction()` | Returns bound `Action` object (advanced). |
| 14 | Set Action | `void setAction(Action a)` | Binds a Swing `Action` to the field. |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World

`JTextField` is the **backbone of nearly every desktop data-entry form** — login screens, search bars, registration forms, calculator displays, IP/port input dialogs, configuration panels, IDE text-input boxes, etc. In enterprise Java (Swing, JavaFX, JSP/Servlets backed by JavaBeans), `JTextField` is the standard primitive for capturing **short, structured textual data** such as usernames, IDs, phone numbers, and numeric input (after `Integer.parseInt` / `Double.parseDouble` conversion).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Constructor

Given a design requirement, derive (choose) the correct constructor:

$$
\text{Requirement} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Constructor Selected}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Empty field, default size} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{new JTextField()} \\
\text{Empty field, fixed width} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{new JTextField(20)} \\
\text{Pre-filled field, default size} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{new JTextField("Hello")} \\
\text{Pre-filled field, fixed width} &\;\longrightarrow\; \text{new JTextField("Hello", 20)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic Explained:**

1. **Step 1 — Identify Content Need:** Does the field need an *initial value* (e.g., a placeholder default like `"0"` in a calculator)? If **yes**, choose a constructor that takes a `String`. If **no**, pick a no-arg or `int`-arg constructor.
2. **Step 2 — Identify Layout Need:** Will the field be inside a layout manager that does not respect the field's preferred size (e.g., `BorderLayout.CENTER` stretches it)? If **no**, call `setColumns(int)` to enforce a visible width.
3. **Step 3 — Map to API:** Combine the two decisions into the matching constructor from the **Cheat Sheet** table above.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Walkthrough: Building a "Login" Mini-Application with JTextField

Below is a **complete, line-by-line explained** KTU-style program. Every line is annotated with its purpose so the student understands *why* it is there — not just *what* it does.

```java
import javax.swing.*;           // Step A: Import the entire Swing package (JTextField, JFrame, JButton, JLabel)
import java.awt.*;              // Step B: Import AWT for BorderLayout and FlowLayout constants
import java.awt.event.*;        // Step C: Import AWT event package for ActionListener, ActionEvent

public class LoginDemo extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    // Step 1: Declaration of GUI components as instance fields
    //   (so the event handler below can reference them)
    private JLabel userLabel, passLabel, messageLabel;
    private JTextField userField;       // <-- The JTextField under study (single-line username input)
    private JPasswordField passField;   // companion component (password masking)
    private JButton loginButton, resetButton;

    // Step 2: Constructor — builds and assembles the GUI
    public LoginDemo() {
        setTitle("KTU Login Demo");
        setSize(420, 220);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 10, 15));

        // Step 3: Instantiation & configuration of the JTextField
        userLabel   = new JLabel("Username:");
        userField   = new JTextField(15);        // 15-character-wide single-line input
        // Optional configuration:
        userField.setToolTipText("Enter your KTU register number");
        userField.setHorizontalAlignment(JTextField.LEFT);

        passLabel   = new JLabel("Password:");
        passField   = new JPasswordField(15);

        loginButton = new JButton("Login");
        resetButton = new JButton("Reset");

        // Step 4: Event registration
        loginButton.addActionListener(this);
        resetButton.addActionListener(this);

        // Step 5: Placement — adding components to the JFrame
        add(userLabel);
        add(userField);                         // <-- JTextField added to container
        add(passLabel);
        add(passField);
        add(loginButton);
        add(resetButton);
        add(messageLabel = new JLabel(" "));    // blank status line

        setVisible(true);                       // Step 6: Display the GUI
    }

    // Step 7: Event-handler callback
    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent ae) {
        String command = ae.getActionCommand();
        if (command.equals("Login")) {
            String username = userField.getText();   // READING from JTextField
            if (username.isEmpty()) {
                messageLabel.setText("Username cannot be empty!");
            } else {
                messageLabel.setText("Welcome, " + username + "!");
            }
        } else if (command.equals("Reset")) {
            userField.setText("");               // WRITING to JTextField (clearing it)
            passField.setText("");
            messageLabel.setText(" ");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new LoginDemo();   // Launches the Swing GUI on the Event Dispatch Thread (NOT recommended
                           // for production, but accepted in KTU 2024 Scheme lab exams).
    }
}
```

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Logical Trace

**Trace — User clicks "Login" with username field containing `"alice"`:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Event fires} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{ActionEvent dispatched to actionPerformed} \\
\text{ae.getActionCommand()} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{"Login"} \\
\text{command.equals("Login")} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{true} \\
\text{username = userField.getText()} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{"alice"} \\
\text{username.isEmpty()} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{false} \\
\text{messageLabel.setText(...)} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{"Welcome, alice!"} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trace — User clicks "Reset":**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{ae.getActionCommand()} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{"Reset"} \\
\text{command.equals("Reset")} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{true} \\
\text{userField.setText("")} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{clears the JTextField visually} \\
\text{passField.setText("")} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{clears the password field} \\
\text{messageLabel.setText(" ")} &\;\rightarrow\; \text{erases the previous status} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Important Constraint Equations (LaTeX-Isolated)

The width of a `JTextField` (in pixels) given its column count is approximately governed by the Swing Look-and-Feel's font metrics:

$$
W_{\text{preferred}} \;\approx\; C \cdot W_{\text{char}} \;+\; 2 \cdot P_{\text{inset}}
$$

Where:

- $C$ = number of columns set via `setColumns(int)`
- $W_{\text{char}}$ = width of one 'M' (em-width) in the current font
- $P_{\text{inset}}$ = the platform-specific horizontal padding (typically **2–4 px**)

> [!NOTE]
> This is an *approximation* used by `getPreferredSize()`. The actual rendered width may differ slightly across platforms (Metal, Nimbus, Windows L&F).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Class Hierarchy of JTextField (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["java.lang.Object"] --> B["java.awt.Component"]
    B --> C["java.awt.Container"]
    C --> D["javax.swing.JComponent"]
    D --> E["javax.swing.text.JTextComponent"]
    E --> F["javax.swing.JTextField"]
    F --> G["javax.swing.JPasswordField"]

    classDef base fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef mid  fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef leaf fill:#5b9bd5,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    class A,B base
    class C,D,E mid
    class F,G leaf
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- `JTextField` inherits all painting, accessibility, and UI-delegate machinery from `JComponent`.
- `JPasswordField` (a closely related sub-class) inherits everything from `JTextField` and overrides the `echoChar` behavior to mask input — a frequent 3-mark question.

### 4.2 Event-Handling Architecture for JTextField

```mermaid

    Field -->|Key Pressed| KeyEvt["KeyEvent"]

    KeyEvt --> KListen["KeyListener"]

    CListen --> Logic

    Logic --> Update["Update other components or model"]

    classDef evt fill:#c00000,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff
    classDef lst fill:#2e75b6,stroke:#ffffff,color:#ffffff

```

```mermaid

    subgraph Frame["JFrame - LoginDemo"]
        direction LR
        L1["JLabel Username"] --> TF["JTextField userField"]
        L2["JLabel Password"] --> PF["JPasswordField passField"]

        B2["JButton Reset"]
        ML["JLabel messageLabel"]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 3 Marks]**
*Define `JTextField` in Java Swing. List any two constructors of `JTextField` with their signatures.*

**Model Answer (≈ 3-mark valuation key):**

`JTextField` is a lightweight Swing component defined in the `javax.swing` package that allows the user to enter and edit a **single line of unformatted text**. It is a direct subclass of `JTextComponent`, which in turn extends `JComponent`.

Two commonly used constructors:

- `JTextField()` — Creates an empty text field with no preferred width.
- `JTextField(int columns)` — Creates an empty text field with a preferred width of `columns` character widths.

*(Full 3 marks: 1 mark for class/package, 1 mark for single-line property, 1 mark for the two constructors.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4, 3 Marks]**
*Explain the difference between `JTextField` and `JTextArea` in Java Swing.*

**Model Answer (≈ 3-mark valuation key):**

| Aspect | JTextField | JTextArea |
|---|---|---|
| Allowed text | **Single line only** | **Multiple lines** |
| Internal Document | `PlainDocument` (single-line restricted) | `PlainDocument` (multi-line allowed) |
| Typical use | Short input (name, ID, search query) | Long input (comments, description, address) |
| Line wrapping | Not applicable | Supported via `setLineWrap(true)` |
| Parent class | `JTextComponent` | `JTextComponent` |
| Pressing Enter | Triggers `ActionEvent` | Inserts a newline character |

*(Full 3 marks: 1 mark each for any three valid differences.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer / Program Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[Internal Choice Pattern]** — KTU ESE convention: Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Both questions below are mapped to **Module 4** and test the JTextField concept along with Event Handling.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — JTextField + ActionListener

**(a)** Explain the constructors and important methods of `JTextField` with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program to create a form with two `JTextField`s (for *Name* and *Age*) and a `JButton` labeled *"Display"*. When the button is clicked, display the entered name and age in a `JLabel` below the button. Use `ActionListener`. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Constructors of `JTextField` (3 marks for enumeration, 2 marks for method descriptions, 2 marks for examples):**

The four primary constructors are:

- `JTextField()` — Creates an empty text field.
- `JTextField(String text)` — Creates a field pre-filled with the given text.
- `JTextField(int columns)` — Creates an empty field with a preferred width in character columns.
- `JTextField(String text, int columns)` — Creates a pre-filled field with the given width.

Important methods:

- `String getText()` — Returns the current contents.
- `void setText(String t)` — Sets the contents.
- `void setEditable(boolean b)` — Enables or disables editing.
- `void setColumns(int c)` — Changes the preferred width.
- `void setHorizontalAlignment(int align)` — Aligns text left, center, or right.
- `void addActionListener(ActionListener l)` — Registers a listener that fires when the user presses **Enter** inside the field.

**Example usage:**

```java
JTextField tf = new JTextField("Enter name", 20);
tf.setEditable(true);
tf.setHorizontalAlignment(JTextField.LEFT);
String value = tf.getText();
```

**(b) Complete Java Program (7 marks — distribution: 2 for layout, 2 for listener logic, 1 for proper imports, 2 for correct compilation logic):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class DisplayForm extends JFrame implements ActionListener {
    JTextField nameField, ageField;
    JLabel resultLabel;

    public DisplayForm() {
        setTitle("Display Form");
        setSize(350, 200);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 10, 15));

        add(new JLabel("Name:"));
        nameField = new JTextField(15);
        add(nameField);

        add(new JLabel("Age:"));
        ageField = new JTextField(5);
        add(ageField);

        JButton displayBtn = new JButton("Display");
        displayBtn.addActionListener(this);
        add(displayBtn);

        resultLabel = new JLabel("Result will appear here");
        add(resultLabel);

        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void actionPerformed(ActionEvent e) {
        String name = nameField.getText();
        String ageText = ageField.getText();
        try {
            int age = Integer.parseInt(ageText);
            resultLabel.setText("Name: " + name + "  |  Age: " + age);
        } catch (NumberFormatException ex) {
            resultLabel.setText("Invalid age! Please enter a number.");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new DisplayForm();
    }
}
```

**Logic explanation:**

1. The `JTextField` `nameField` and `ageField` are created with widths of 15 and 5 columns respectively (justifying the `JTextField(int)` constructor).
2. The `JButton` is registered with an `ActionListener`.
3. On click, `getText()` extracts the raw strings from both `JTextField`s.
4. `Integer.parseInt` converts the age safely; exception handling prevents crashes on invalid input.
5. The `JLabel` is updated using `setText()`.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — JTextField + KeyListener (Real-time filtering)

**(a)** Differentiate between `ActionListener`, `KeyListener`, and `DocumentListener` when used with a `JTextField`. Give one suitable use-case for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java Swing program using `JTextField` and `KeyListener` to create a real-time **uppercase converter**: as the user types in a `JTextField`, the characters should be displayed in **UPPERCASE** in a second (read-only) `JTextField`. Use `KeyListener` interface. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Differences (7 marks — 1 mark per row, 1 mark for examples/use-cases):**

| Listener | Triggered When | Use-Case |
|---|---|---|
| `ActionListener` | User presses **Enter** inside the field (or programmatic `postActionEvent()`). | Submitting a search query. |
| `KeyListener` | Any key is **pressed, released, or typed** (3 callbacks: `keyPressed`, `keyReleased`, `keyTyped`). | Real-time validation, character filtering, live formatting. |
| `DocumentListener` | The field's underlying `Document` **changes** (insert, remove, or update text). | Cross-component syncing, undo/redo, complex validation. |

**Use-case example for `KeyListener`:** Preventing digits in a name field by listening to `keyTyped()` and consuming digit key events via `e.consume()`.

**(b) Complete Java Program (7 marks — 2 for KeyListener implementation, 2 for real-time logic, 1 for setEditable(false), 1 for proper output, 1 for clean code):**

```java
import javax.swing.*;
import java.awt.*;
import java.awt.event.*;

public class UppercaseConverter extends JFrame implements KeyListener {
    JTextField inputField, outputField;

    public UppercaseConverter() {
        setTitle("Real-time Uppercase Converter");
        setSize(400, 120);
        setDefaultCloseOperation(JFrame.EXIT_ON_CLOSE);
        setLayout(new FlowLayout(FlowLayout.CENTER, 10, 15));

        add(new JLabel("Type here:"));
        inputField = new JTextField(15);
        add(inputField);

        add(new JLabel("UPPERCASE:"));
        outputField = new JTextField(15);
        outputField.setEditable(false);   // read-only mirror
        add(outputField);

        inputField.addKeyListener(this);
        setVisible(true);
    }

    public void keyTyped(KeyEvent e) {
        // After the character is inserted into inputField, mirror it in uppercase
        outputField.setText(inputField.getText().toUpperCase());
    }
    public void keyPressed(KeyEvent e) { /* not used */ }
    public void keyReleased(KeyEvent e) { /* not used */ }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        new UppercaseConverter();
    }
}
```

**Logic explanation:**

1. The `inputField` is the editable `JTextField`; the `outputField` is configured as read-only via `setEditable(false)`.
2. The class implements `KeyListener` and registers itself on the `inputField`.
3. On every `keyTyped` event (after the character is inserted), the current text of `inputField` is fetched, converted to uppercase using `String.toUpperCase()`, and pushed into `outputField`.
4. The two unused methods (`keyPressed`, `keyReleased`) are kept as empty implementations because the interface requires all three.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls (JTextField-specific):**
> 1. **Confusing `JTextField` with `JTextArea`** — Students often use `JTextField` for multi-line input. Loss: up to **2 marks** per question.
> 2. **Forgetting `setEditable(false)` on read-only mirror fields** — This is a common deduction in GUI programs requiring output display.
> 3. **Implementing `KeyListener` but not registering it** — Calling `addKeyListener(this)` is **mandatory**; the methods won't fire otherwise. Loss: up to **3 marks**.
> 4. **Using `KeyEvent.getKeyChar()` instead of reading from `JTextField.getText()`** — Always read from the component's current state (`getText()`) inside `keyTyped` to capture the *post-insert* value.
> 5. **Missing `implements ActionListener` / `implements KeyListener`** declaration on the class — Without this declaration, the code will not compile. Instant zero.
> 6. **Not handling `NumberFormatException`** in numeric input parsing — Deducted in the **Display Form** problem.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Package:** `javax.swing`
- **Class signature:** `public class JTextField extends JTextComponent`
- **Single-line only** — pressing Enter triggers `ActionEvent`, not a newline.
- **Four constructors:** `()`, `(int columns)`, `(String text)`, `(String text, int columns)`.
- **Key methods to memorize:** `getText()`, `setText(String)`, `setEditable(boolean)`, `setColumns(int)`, `setHorizontalAlignment(int)`, `addActionListener(ActionListener)`.
- **Default `columns` = 0**; conventional default in tutorials = **10**.
- **Listener pairings (high-yield for KTU):**
  - `ActionListener` → Enter key
  - `KeyListener` → real-time keystroke handling
  - `CaretListener` → cursor position changes
  - `DocumentListener` → text-content changes (insert/remove)
- **Read-only trick:** Use `setEditable(false)` to disable user input (visual mirror fields).
- **Inheritance chain:** `Object` → `Component` → `Container` → `JComponent` → `JTextComponent` → `JTextField` → `JPasswordField`.
- **Alignment constants inherited from `SwingConstants`:** `LEFT`, `CENTER`, `RIGHT`.
- **Always declare GUI components as instance fields** (not local variables) so inner-class event handlers can access them.
- **Swing L&F pitfall:** Column width is platform/font-dependent — never hardcode pixel widths.
- **Best practice (advanced, not in syllabus but helpful):** In production, wrap GUI creation inside `SwingUtilities.invokeLater(() -> { ... })` to ensure thread-safety on the Event Dispatch Thread (EDT).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Java Database Connectivity (JDBC) - JDBC Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBEOT404 - OBJECT ORIENTED PROGRAMMING USING JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Java Database Connectivity (JDBC) - JDBC Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Java Database Connectivity (JDBC)** is a Java API (Application Programming Interface) that enables Java applications to interact with a wide range of relational databases. It provides a standard set of classes and interfaces written in the Java programming language, which act as an abstraction layer between the Java application logic and the underlying database engine.

**JDBC Architecture** refers to the layered, modular structural design of the JDBC framework that defines how the Java application, the JDBC API, the JDBC Driver Manager, and the database-specific JDBC drivers communicate with each other to execute SQL statements and retrieve results in a vendor-neutral manner.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PBEOT404 - Module 4):**
> Students must be able to *explain the JDBC architecture*, identify its *four major components*, distinguish between the *two-tier and three-tier* processing models, and enumerate the *four types of JDBC drivers*. The architecture is the single most important concept under this module and carries the highest weightage in university examinations.

### The Four Pillars of JDBC Architecture (As per `java.sql` Package)

The JDBC specification officially defines **four** primary architectural components. Memorizing these four is non-negotiable for KTU exams:

1. **JDBC API** — The set of Java classes and interfaces (`java.sql` and `javax.sql`) that the application uses to send SQL statements and retrieve results.
2. **JDBC Driver Manager** — The class `java.sql.DriverManager` that loads the appropriate database driver and provides connection services to the application.
3. **JDBC Test Suite** — A set of test cases used to verify that a JDBC driver complies with the JDBC specification.
4. **JDBC-ODBC Bridge** — A legacy driver that translates JDBC calls into ODBC calls (deprecated since JDK 8 and removed in JDK 9+).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Making JDBC Immediately Intuitive

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Universal Power Adapter" Analogy**
> Imagine you have a brand-new Japanese laptop that needs a Japanese 3-pin plug to charge, but you are travelling across **Germany, India, UK, and the USA** — all of which use completely different wall socket shapes. You cannot redesign the laptop for every country. Instead, you buy a **Universal Travel Adapter**: a single device that has one standard input (your laptop's plug) and many interchangeable output pins (one for each country). Your laptop "talks" to the adapter in one standard way; the adapter handles the country-specific conversion.
>
> **In this analogy:**
> - The **laptop** = Your Java application.
> - The **Universal Adapter** = The JDBC API (the standard, vendor-neutral interface).
> - The **country-specific pin heads** = The JDBC drivers (one for Oracle, one for MySQL, one for PostgreSQL, etc.).
> - The **Driver Manager** = The socket on the adapter that holds the correct pin-head in place.
> - The **wall socket** = The actual relational database.

The fundamental insight is this: **your Java code never changes** when you switch databases. Only the driver (the "pin-head") changes. This is the entire philosophical foundation of JDBC Architecture.

### 1.3 Key Metrics, Standards, and Constants

- **Default Port for Oracle DB**: **1521**
- **Default Port for MySQL**: **3306**
- **Default Port for PostgreSQL**: **5432**
- **Default Port for Microsoft SQL Server**: **1433**
- **JDBC URL Format Prefix**: `jdbc:<subprotocol>:<subname>` (e.g., `jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/schoolDB`)
- **Latest Stable JDBC Specification**: **JDBC 4.3** (included in **Java SE 9+**)
- **Package Locations**: `java.sql` (core) and `javax.sql` (extended, used in connection pooling)

---

## 1.4 Why JDBC Architecture Matters in Real Engineering

Every enterprise-grade Java application — from banking systems to e-commerce platforms to hospital management systems — relies on JDBC as the **foundational data-access layer**. Understanding the architecture is not merely academic; it is the prerequisite for mastering **Hibernate**, **Spring Data JPA**, and **MyBatis**, all of which sit *on top of* JDBC.
